```mermaid
    C2 --> D2
    C3 --> D3
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define a petrochemical complex. What are its two structural pillars?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A petrochemical complex is an integrated industrial cluster of process plants that converts hydrocarbon feedstocks (crude oil, natural gas, coal) into value-added organic chemicals and polymers through sequential processing.

The two structural pillars are:
1. **Olefinic Core** (from Steam Cracker) — Ethylene, Propylene, Butadiene.
2. **Aromatic Core** (from Aromatics Complex) — Benzene, Toluene, Xylenes (BTX).

All Tier-2 intermediates and Tier-3 end products are derived from these two pillars. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for pillars)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2023]**
**Distinguish between Tier-1, Tier-2, and Tier-3 petrochemicals with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Tier | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Tier-1 (Primary)** | Basic building blocks obtained directly from steam cracking or reforming | Ethylene, Benzene |
| **Tier-2 (Intermediates)** | Chemicals derived from Tier-1 by further reaction | Ethylene glycol, Styrene, Caprolactam |
| **Tier-3 (End Products)** | Finished marketable products (mostly polymers) | Polyethylene, PET, Nylon-6 |

*(1 mark per row)*

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **structure of a naphtha-based petrochemical complex**. Discuss the role of the steam cracker and the aromatics complex as the two primary pillars. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A naphtha steam cracker processes **1,500,000 t/yr of naphtha**. Given the following weight yield distribution: Ethylene 30%, Propylene 16%, Butadiene 4.5%, Pyrolysis gasoline 24%, Fuel oil 5%, others 20.5%. Calculate the **annual production** of ethylene, propylene, and butadiene. Also compute the **P/E ratio** and comment on cracker severity. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram (4 marks) + Explanation (3 marks):**

Students should draw a clear hierarchical flow diagram (similar to the one in Section 4.2) showing:
- **Feedstock:** Naphtha → Steam Cracker (800–900 °C, steam dilution).
- **Cracked gas** → Olefins Recovery → Polymer-grade C₂=, C₃=, C₄=.
- **Pyrolysis gasoline + Reformate** → Aromatics Complex → BTX.
- **Tier-2** plants: EO/EG, EDC/VCM, Styrene, Phenol, PTA.
- **Tier-3** plants: PE, PP, PVC, PS, PET, SBR, Nylon.
- **Central Utilities** serving all plants.

**Role of Steam Cracker:** It is the *heart* of the complex. It thermally decomposes hydrocarbons into the **olefinic core** (ethylene, propylene, butadiene). It operates at 800–900 °C with steam (to lower partial pressure and prevent coking). It supplies the **maximum value stream** of the complex.

**Role of Aromatics Complex:** It is the *second pillar*. It uses **extractive distillation** (e.g., Sulfolane process) to recover pure BTX from pyrolysis gasoline and catalytic reformate. It supplies benzene, toluene, and xylenes for the entire aromatic-based chain (caprolactam, PTA, MDI, etc.).

*(Full marks for diagram clarity, arrows correctly labelled, and explanation tying pillars to downstream)*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Mass Balance Formula (1 mark)**

$$m_i = F_{\text{feed}} \times Y_i$$

**Step 2: Ethylene (2 marks)**

$$m_{C_2H_4} = 1{,}500{,}000 \times 0.30 = 450{,}000 \text{ t/yr}$$

**Step 3: Propylene (2 marks)**

$$m_{C_3H_6} = 1{,}500{,}000 \times 0.16 = 240{,}000 \text{ t/yr}$$

**Step 4: Butadiene (1 mark)**

$$m_{C_4H_6} = 1{,}500{,}000 \times 0.045 = 67{,}500 \text{ t/yr}$$

**Step 5: P/E Ratio and Severity Comment (1 mark)**

$$\text{P/E} = \frac{0.16}{0.30} = 0.533$$

A P/E ratio of ~0.53 indicates a **medium-severity naphtha cracker**. High-severity crackers have P/E ≈ 0.3–0.4 (more ethylene); low-severity crackers have P/E ≈ 0.7–0.8 (more propylene).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**(a)** Describe the **feedstock sources** for petrochemical complexes. Compare naphtha-based and ethane-based steam cracking in terms of product yield and downstream structure. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **refinery–petrochemical integration** with examples. How does this integration improve the overall economics of the complex? *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Feedstock Sources (3 marks):**
- **Crude Oil / Naphtha:** Paraffinic liquid, C₅–C₁₂, used in steam crackers.
- **Natural Gas:** Methane + ethane/propane; ethane is an excellent cracker feed.
- **Coal:** Solid; gasified to syngas → methanol → olefins (MTO).
- **Biomass:** Sugars, lignocellulose; bio-ethanol → bio-ethylene.

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Naphtha Cracker | Ethane Cracker |
|---|---|---|
| Ethylene yield (wt%) | ~30% | ~80% |
| Propylene yield (wt%) | ~16% | ~2% |
| Butadiene yield | ~4.5% | ~1% |
| P/E ratio | ~0.5 | ~0.03 |
| Steam dilution | High | Low |
| Coke formation | Moderate | Minimal |
| Downstream structure | **Broad** (diversified) | **Narrow** (ethylene-only) |
| Co-product valorization | Complex | Simple |
| Examples | European crackers (Reliance, Sinopec) | USA (Chevron Phillips, Qatar) |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Naphtha crackers yield a *diversified* downstream product slate (PE, PP, PVC, SBR), while ethane crackers are optimized for *polyethylene dominance* and are more carbon-efficient.

**(b) Refinery–Petrochemical Integration (4 marks + 3 marks):**

**Concept:** The structural coupling of a crude oil refinery with a petrochemical complex, where streams and utilities are interchanged.

**Examples of Integration:**
- **Naphtha routing:** Naphtha from refinery → Steam Cracker (instead of gasoline pool).
- **FCC off-gas:** Light olefins (C₃=, C₄=) from Fluid Catalytic Cracker → Polymer-grade recovery.
- **Catalytic Reformer:** Provides high-purity reformate (rich in aromatics) → Aromatics Complex.
- **Hydrogen from cracker:** Used in refinery hydrodesulfurization (HDS) and hydrocracker.
- **Steam/power sharing:** Single cogeneration plant serves both.
- **Sulfur recovery:** Shared in a single Claus unit.

**Economic Benefits (3 marks):**
1. **Value addition:** Crude molecules are upgraded 3–10× in value.
2. **Energy efficiency:** Heat integration reduces fuel consumption by 15–20%.
3. **Risk diversification:** Refinery margins and petrochemical margins often move in opposite directions.
4. **Reduced carbon footprint:** Shared utilities, less flaring, more efficient logistics.
5. **Examples:** Reliance Jamnagar (India), Yanbu (Saudi Arabia), Singapore Petrochemical Complex.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Drawing the wrong diagram:** Students often draw a refinery diagram, not a *petrochemical* complex. Make sure the **Steam Cracker** and **Aromatics Complex** are clearly marked as the two pillars.
> 2. **Mixing Tier-1 with Tier-3:** Writing "PE, PP" as primary petrochemicals loses 1 mark. Always classify them.
> 3. **Skipping the P/E ratio calculation:** A numerical question that does NOT compute P/E and comment on severity loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Not closing the mass balance:** If you compute 2 yields, always verify $\sum Y_i = 1.0$ or write a check.
> 5. **Forgetting utilities:** A complex without utilities is a collection of plants, not a *complex*. Always include a Central Utilities block in the diagram.
> 6. **Confusing "cracker" with "reformer":** A *cracker* produces **olefins**; a *reformer* produces **aromatics + hydrogen**. This is a frequent KTU trap question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **Petrochemical Complex** is a 3-tier integrated network: **Feedstock → Tier-1 → Tier-2 → Tier-3**.
- ✅ The **two structural pillars** are the **Steam Cracker** (olefins) and the **Aromatics Complex** (BTX).
- ✅ **Steam Cracker** conditions: **800–900 °C**, steam dilution, low residence time — produces cracked gas (C₁–C₄ olefins) + pyrolysis gasoline + fuel oil + H₂.
- ✅ **Olefinic core:** Ethylene → PE/EO/EG/PVC/Styrene; Propylene → PP/AN/PO; Butadiene → SBR/ABS.
- ✅ **Aromatic core (BTX):** Benzene → Phenol/Caprolactam/Aniline; Toluene → TDI/Benzene; Xylenes → PTA/o-PA.
- ✅ **P/E ratio:** Naphtha ≈ 0.5 (medium severity), Ethane ≈ 0.03, Low-severity naphtha ≈ 0.8.
- ✅ **Weight yield** of ethylene from naphtha ≈ **30%**; propylene ≈ **16%**; butadiene ≈ **4.5%**.
- ✅ **Integration advantages:** Material economy, energy recovery, utility sharing, logistics, risk diversification.
- ✅ **Value addition** increases 5–20× from Tier-1 to Tier-3.
- ✅ Modern complex = **Refinery + Petrochemical + Downstream Polymerization** (e.g., Jamnagar, Yanbu, Jurong).
- ✅ **Mass balance must close:** $\sum Y_i = 1.0$ in any yield calculation.
- ✅ The **heart** of the complex is the Steam Cracker; the **backbone** is the Aromatics Complex; the **nervous system** is the Central Utilities.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Profile of Petrochemicals and Their End Products
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT741 - PETROCHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Profile of Petrochemicals and Their End Products ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Petrochemicals** are a distinct group of chemical substances manufactured on an industrial scale using **hydrocarbon feedstocks** derived primarily from crude oil (petroleum) and natural gas. They serve as the fundamental chemical "building blocks" (or olefinic/aromatic intermediates) from which thousands of downstream consumer and industrial end-products are synthesized. The term **petrochemical** itself is a portmanteau derived from **"petroleum"** + **"chemical"**, signifying chemicals extracted or synthesized from fossil-fuel hydrocarbons. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PECHT741, petrochemicals are broadly classified by their molecular generation distance from the parent feedstock. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** A typical petrochemical value chain transforms a barrel of crude oil (≈159 liters) into products ranging from fertilizers and plastics to pharmaceuticals and synthetic textiles. The petrochemical industry is therefore considered a **strategic backbone industry** for the manufacturing sector of any modern economy. ### 1.2 Classification of Petrochemicals (KTU 2024 Scheme Framework) | Classification Tier | Definition | Examples | |---|---|---| | **First-Generation Petrochemicals** | Directly obtained from primary hydrocarbon feedstocks via cracking/reforming | Ethylene, Propylene, Butadiene, Benzene, Toluene, Xylene, Synthesis gas (CO + H₂) | | **Second-Generation Petrochemicals** | Produced by reacting/transforming first-generation intermediates | Ethylene oxide, Ethylene glycol, Vinyl chloride, Styrene, Phenol, Acrylonitrile, Caprolactam | | **Third-Generation Petrochemicals** | Finished polymers/synthetics manufactured from second-generation monomers | Polyethylene (PE), PVC, Polystyrene, Polyester, Nylon-6, Synthetic rubber, Detergents | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Tree of Petroleum" Imagine a giant **banyan tree**: - **Roots** = Crude oil and natural gas (the hydrocarbon feedstocks) - **Trunk** = First-generation petrochemicals (ethylene, propylene, benzene — the strong central building blocks) - **Branches** = Second-generation intermediates (vinyl chloride, styrene, ethylene glycol — the offshoots) - **Leaves & Fruits** = Third-generation end products (PVC pipes, polyester shirts, plastic bottles, synthetic rubber tyres — what humans finally consume) Just as a banyan tree produces oxygen, shade, fruits, and wood from the same root system, the petroleum tree produces plastics, fertilizers, paints, detergents, drugs, and synthetic fibers from a single hydrocarbon source. ### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Barrel of crude oil:** **158.987 liters ≈ 0.159 m³** - **Global petrochemical share of oil consumption:** roughly **14%–18%** of total barrel - **Global petrochemical share of natural gas consumption:** roughly **8%–10%** - **Primary C1–C4 olefins:** Ethylene (C₂H₄), Propylene (C₃H₆), 1,3-Butadiene (C₄H₆) - **Primary BTX aromatics:** Benzene (C₆H₆), Toluene (C₇H₈), Xylene (C₈H₁₀) > [!IMPORTANT] > The **three primary pillars** of the petrochemical industry are universally accepted as: > 1. **Olefins** (ethylene + propylene + butadiene) > 2. **Aromatics** (BTX — benzene, toluene, xylene) > 3. **Synthesis gas** (syn-gas: CO + H₂) ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Theoretical Framework of the Petrochemical Value Chain The petrochemical industry operates on a strict **hierarchical tier system** known as the **"Petrochemical Tree"** or **"Hydrocarbon Ladder."** Each tier adds economic value exponentially. **Tier 1 — Feedstock Preparation** - Crude oil undergoes **atmospheric distillation** and **vacuum distillation** in oil refineries. - Naphtha (C₅–C₁₁), natural gas liquids (NGLs), ethane, propane, and methane are separated as petrochemical feedstocks. - Additionally, natural gas (mostly CH₄) is cracked to yield synthesis gas. **Tier 2 — First-Generation Petrochemicals (Olefins & Aromatics)** - Produced via two principal technologies: - **Steam Cracking (Pyrolysis):** Thermal decomposition of saturated hydrocarbons in the presence of steam at **750–900 °C** to produce olefins (ethylene, propylene, butadiene) and pyrolysis gasoline (aromatics). - **Catalytic Reforming:** Dehydrocyclization of naphthenes to aromatics over **Pt/Al₂O₃** catalysts at **450–520 °C**. **Tier 3 — Second-Generation Intermediates** - First-generation olefins are converted using **selective catalysis, oxidation, halogenation, and alkylation**: - Ethylene + O₂ → Ethylene oxide (Ag catalyst) - Ethylene + Cl₂ → 1,2-Dichloroethane → Vinyl chloride - Benzene + Ethylene → Ethylbenzene → Styrene (dehydrogenation) - Propylene + Ammonia + O₂ → Acrylonitrile (Sohio process) **Tier 4 — Third-Generation End Products (Polymers & Synthetics)** - Polymerization reactions convert monomers into macromolecules: - Vinyl chloride → PVC (polyvinyl chloride) - Ethylene → LDPE/HDPE/LLDPE - Styrene → Polystyrene (PS) - Caprolactam → Nylon-6 - Adipic acid + Hexamethylenediamine → Nylon-6,6 ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula & Reaction Sheet > [!NOTE] > Below is the **master reference table** of all critical reactions and formulas for this topic. No vertical bars `|` are used inside table cells to ensure correct markdown rendering. | Reaction / Concept | Balanced Equation / Relationship | Catalyst / Conditions | |---|---|---| | Steam Cracking (Ethane → Ethylene) | C₂H₆ → C₂H₄ + H₂ | Steam, 800–900 °C | | Steam Cracking (Naphtha → Mixed Olefins) | CₙH₂ₙ₊₂ → CₘH₂ₘ + Cₙ₋ₘH₂ₙ₋ₘ₊₂ | 750–900 °C | | Ethylene to Ethylene Oxide | 2 C₂H₄ + O₂ → 2 C₂H₄O | Ag catalyst, 250 °C | | Ethylene to Vinyl Chloride | C₂H₄ + Cl₂ → C₂H₄Cl₂ → C₂H₃Cl + HCl | 400 °C, then pyrolysis | | Propylene to Acrylonitrile (Sohio) | C₃H₆ + NH₃ + 1.5 O₂ → C₃H₃N + 3 H₂O | Bi-Mo oxide, 450 °C | | Benzene to Ethylbenzene | C₆H₆ + C₂H₄ → C₆H₅C₂H₅ | AlCl₃ or ZSM-5 | | Ethylbenzene to Styrene | C₆H₅C₂H₅ → C₆H₅CH=CH₂ + H₂ | Fe₂O₃-Cr₂O₃-K₂O, 600 °C | | Steam Reforming to Syn-gas | CH₄ + H₂O → CO + 3 H₂ | Ni/Al₂O₃, 800–900 °C | | Water-gas shift | CO + H₂O → CO₂ + H₂ | Fe₃O₄-Cr₂O₃, 400 °C | | Methanol synthesis | CO + 2 H₂ → CH₃OH | Cu-ZnO-Al₂O₃, 250 °C, 50 bar | | Polymerization (Free radical, ethylene to LDPE) | n CH₂=CH₂ → (-CH₂-CH₂-)$_{n}$ | Peroxide initiator, 1000–2000 bar, 200 °C | | Ziegler-Natta (HDPE) | n CH₂=CH₂ → (-CH₂-CH₂-)$_{n}$ | TiCl₄ + Al(C₂H₅)₃, low pressure | ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Ethylene** is the **single largest organic chemical produced globally** (over **200 million tonnes/year**) and feeds into roughly **60% of all petrochemical end-products**. - **BTX aromatics** are critical for dyes, explosives (TNT from toluene), polyurethanes (from benzene), and polyester fibers (from p-xylene via PTA/DMT route). - **Synthesis gas** is the gateway to **C1 chemistry** — producing methanol, ammonia (Haber-Bosch), Fischer-Tropsch fuels, and formaldehyde. - **India's petrochemical hubs:** Reliance Jamnagar, ONGC Mangalore, BPCL Kochi, Haldia Petrochemicals, Indian Oil Panipat — together feeding India's ₹10+ lakh crore chemical industry. ### 2.4 Why & How — Logic Layer - **Why do we crack naphtha instead of just burning it?** Because cracking converts low-value saturated hydrocarbons into **reactive olefins** whose unsaturated C=C bonds are the gateway to virtually all synthetic chemistry. - **Why is steam added during cracking?** Steam **dilutes hydrocarbons**, reduces coke formation, increases olefin yield, and provides process heat. - **How does the "value chain multiplier" work?** Every step up the ladder multiplies value roughly **5×–10×**: crude oil → naphtha → ethylene → ethylene oxide → PET bottle resin. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Mathematical Derivation — Olefins Yield from Steam Cracking When a feedstock of general formula **CₙH₂ₙ₊₂** is subjected to steam cracking, the **mass balance** follows conservation principles. Let us derive the theoretical maximum olefin yield. **Assumptions:** - Steam cracking is approximated as a free-radical β-scission. - For a pure paraffin CₙH₂ₙ₊₂, each molecule yields (n − 1) moles of H₂ during full dehydrogenation. **Step 1 — Total dehydrogenation reaction:** $$\text{C}_{n}\text{H}_{2n+2} \longrightarrow n\,\text{C} + (n+1)\,\text{H}_{2}$$ **Step 2 — Partial dehydrogenation to a single olefin (general case):** $$\text{C}_{n}\text{H}_{2n+2} \longrightarrow \text{C}_{m}\text{H}_{2m} + \text{C}_{n-m}\text{H}_{2(n-m)+2}$$ **Step 3 — Mass-balance verification (carbon atoms):** $$\text{LHS carbons} = n$$ $$\text{RHS carbons} = m + (n - m) = n \;\checkmark$$ **Step 4 — Hydrogen balance:** $$\text{LHS hydrogens} = 2n + 2$$ $$\text{RHS hydrogens} = 2m + 2(n - m) + 2 = 2n + 2 \;\checkmark$$ **Step 5 — Theoretical olefin weight fraction:** For **ethane** ($n = 2$) cracked to ethylene ($m = 2$): $$\text{Molar mass of ethane} = 30 \;\text{g/mol}$$ $$\text{Molar mass of ethylene} = 28 \;\text{g/mol}$$ $$\text{Mass yield of ethylene} = \frac{28 \times 1}{30 \times 1} \times 100\% \approx 93.3\%$$ For **propane** ($n = 3$) cracked to propylene ($m = 3$): $$\text{Mass yield of propylene} = \frac{42}{44} \times 100\% \approx 95.4\%$$ For **naphtha** (assumed C₇H₁₆, $n = 7$), cracking to mixed olefins yields roughly: $$\text{Ethylene yield} \approx 30\%,\quad \text{Propylene yield} \approx 16\%$$ ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — End-Product to Feedstock Mapping The following analytical matrix traces each major third-generation end product **backwards** through its synthesis route to the originating first-generation petrochemical. This is the single most important **"mapping table"** for KTU 2024 exam answers. | End Product | Monomer / Intermediate | Second-Generation | First-Generation | Parent Feedstock | |---|---|---|---|---| | Polyethylene (PE) bags, films | Ethylene → polymerized | Ethylene | Ethylene | Naphtha / Ethane | | Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC) pipes | Vinyl chloride polymerized | Vinyl chloride | Ethylene + Chlorine | Naphtha + Brine | | Polystyrene (PS) cups | Styrene polymerized | Styrene | Benzene + Ethylene | Naphtha | | PET bottles | Terephthalic acid + Ethylene glycol | PTA, MEG | p-Xylene, Ethylene | Naphtha | | Nylon-6 ropes | Caprolactam polymerized | Caprolactam | Benzene | Naphtha | | Synthetic rubber (SBR) tyres | Styrene + Butadiene | Styrene, 1,3-Butadiene | Benzene, Ethylene, Butene | Naphtha | | Polyacrylonitrile (PAN) fibers | Acrylonitrile polymerized | Acrylonitrile | Propylene + Ammonia | Naphtha | | Urea-formaldehyde resins | Urea + Formaldehyde | Formaldehyde | Methanol (from Syn-gas) | Natural gas | | Detergents (LABSA) | Linear alkylbenzene sulfonate | LAB | Benzene + C₁₀–C₁₄ olefin | Kerosene | | Ammonia fertilizer | NH₃ from N₂ + H₂ | — | Synthesis gas H₂ | Natural gas | ### 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Numerical — Cracking Yield Problem > [!NOTE] > **Worked Example:** A steam cracker processes **10,000 kg/hr** of ethane (C₂H₆). Conversion is **60% per pass**, with ethylene selectivity of **85%** (rest is methane, hydrogen, coke). Calculate: > (a) Ethylene produced per hour > (b) Unconverted ethane > (c) Theoretical CO₂ emitted annually if all ethylene is combusted (assume 8000 operating hours) **Part (a) — Ethylene produced per hour:** $$\text{Ethane reacted} = 10000 \times 0.60 = 6000 \;\text{kg/hr}$$ $$\text{Moles of ethane reacted} = \frac{6000}{30} = 200 \;\text{kmol/hr}$$ $$\text{Stoichiometry: C}_2\text{H}_6 \rightarrow \text{C}_2\text{H}_4 + \text{H}_2 \quad (1:1 \text{ molar ratio})$$ $$\text{Molar mass of ethylene} = 28 \;\text{g/mol}$$ $$\text{Theoretical C}_2\text{H}_4 = 200 \times 28 = 5600 \;\text{kg/hr}$$ $$\text{Actual C}_2\text{H}_4 \text{ (after 85\% selectivity)} = 5600 \times 0.85 = 4760 \;\text{kg/hr}$$ **Part (b) — Unconverted ethane:** $$\text{Unconverted ethane} = 10000 - 6000 = 4000 \;\text{kg/hr}$$ **Part (c) — Annual CO₂ from ethylene combustion:** $$\text{Combustion: C}_2\text{H}_4 + 3\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\,\text{CO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ $$\text{Moles of C}_2\text{H}_4 \text{ per hour} = \frac{4760}{28} = 170 \;\text{kmol/hr}$$ $$\text{Moles of CO}_2 \text{ per hour} = 2 \times 170 = 340 \;\text{kmol/hr}$$ $$\text{Molar mass of CO}_2 = 44 \;\text{g/mol}$$ $$\text{Mass of CO}_2 \text{ per hour} = 340 \times 44 = 14960 \;\text{kg/hr}$$ $$\text{Annual CO}_2 = 14960 \times 8000 = 1.1968 \times 10^8 \;\text{kg} = 1.197 \times 10^5 \;\text{tonnes}$$ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 The Petrochemical Value-Chain Flow Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A[CRUDE OIL and NATURAL GAS] --> B[REFINERY: Atmospheric and Vacuum Distillation] B --> C1[NAPHTHA C5 to C11] B --> C2[ETHANE and PROPANE NGL] B --> C3[METHANE CH4] C1 --> D1[STEAM CRACKING 750 to 900 C] C2 --> D1 C1 --> D2[CATALYTIC REFORMING 450 to 520 C] D1 --> E1[FIRST GEN OLEFINS: Ethylene, Propylene, Butadiene] D2 --> E2[FIRST GEN AROMATICS: BTX] C3 --> D3[STEAM REFORMING to SYN GAS CO plus H2] D3 --> E3[SYNTHESIS GAS: Methanol, Ammonia, Fischer Tropsch] E1 --> F[SECOND GEN INTERMEDIATES: EO, EG, VCM, Styrene, AN] E2 --> F F --> G[THIRD GEN END PRODUCTS: PE, PVC, PS, PET, Nylon, Rubber] E3 --> H[C1 CHEMICALS: Fertilizers, Formaldehyde, Fuels] G --> I[CONSUMER GOODS: Bottles, Pipes, Tyres, Textiles, Paints] H --> I ``` ### 4.2 Petrochemical Generation Hierarchy Block Architecture ```mermaid graph TB L1[TIER 0: FEEDSTOCKS
Crude Oil, Natural Gas] L2[TIER 1: FIRST GENERATION
Olefins, BTX, Syn-gas] L3[TIER 2: SECOND GENERATION
EO, EG, VCM, Styrene, Caprolactam] L4[TIER 3: THIRD GENERATION
Polymers, Fibers, Elastomers, Detergents] L5[CONSUMER SECTOR
Packaging, Auto, Agriculture, Pharma, Construction] L1 --> L2 L2 --> L3 L3 --> L4 L4 --> L5 style L1 fill:#f9d71c,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style L2 fill:#ff9f43,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style L3 fill:#ee5253,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style L4 fill:#341f97,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style L5 fill:#10ac84,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff ``` ### 4.3 End-Product Sectoral Distribution (Block-Level Schematic) ```mermaid graph LR P[PETROCHEMICAL BUILDING BLOCKS
Ethylene, Propylene, BTX, Syn-gas] P --> S1[PLASTICS SECTOR 38 percent] P --> S2[SYNTHETIC FIBERS 18 percent] P --> S3[ELASTOMERS 12 percent] P --> S4[SURFACTANTS and DETERGENTS 10 percent] P --> S5[FERTILIZERS and C1 CHEMICALS 15 percent] P --> S6[OTHERS Dyes, Paints, Pharma 7 percent] S1 --> SS1[PE, PP, PVC, PS, PET] S2 --> SS2[Nylon-6, Nylon-66, Polyester, Acrylic] S3 --> SS3[SBR, PBR, IIR, EPDM] S4 --> SS4[LABSA, Ethylene Oxide Derivatives] S5 --> SS5[Urea, Ammonia, Methanol] style P fill:#5f27cd,stroke:#fff,color:#fff ``` > [!NOTE] > The percentage figures above are **indicative global sectoral splits** based on typical industry data. They may be cited in KTU answers as approximate distributions. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** *Define the term "petrochemicals" and classify them into first, second, and third-generation products with two examples each.* **Model Answer (10 lines):** Petrochemicals are chemicals derived from petroleum (crude oil) and natural gas. **First-generation** petrochemicals are produced directly from feedstocks by cracking/reforming (e.g., ethylene from naphtha, benzene from reformate). **Second-generation** petrochemicals are synthesized from first-generation intermediates (e.g., ethylene oxide from ethylene, styrene from benzene + ethylene). **Third-generation** petrochemicals are finished polymers and synthetics obtained by polymerizing second-generation monomers (e.g., polyethylene from ethylene, PVC from vinyl chloride). The hierarchy is feedstock → first-gen → second-gen → third-gen → consumer products. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *List the three primary pillars of the petrochemical industry and mention their most important end product.* **Model Answer (8 lines):** The three primary pillars are: 1. **Olefins** (ethylene, propylene, butadiene) → most important end product: **Polyethylene (PE)** 2. **Aromatics** (benzene, toluene, xylene — BTX) → most important end product: **Polystyrene / Polyester (PET)** 3. **Synthesis gas** (CO + H₂) → most important end product: **Ammonia (NH₃) for fertilizers** --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > (i) Students often **skip the classification table** and directly jump to products — losing 2 marks. > (ii) Do **not confuse "feedstock" with "petrochemical."** Crude oil is a feedstock; ethylene is a petrochemical. > (iii) Always mention **specific reactions with catalyst + temperature** for full marks (e.g., not just "ethylene is produced" but "ethylene is produced by steam cracking of naphtha at 800–900 °C"). > (iv) For 14-mark questions, the **value-chain diagram is mandatory** — examiners reward visual clarity. --- #### **Part B — Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]** **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat flow diagram, describe the **petrochemical value chain** starting from crude oil to third-generation end products. Clearly distinguish each generation. **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **steam cracking process** for the production of ethylene and propylene from naphtha. Include reaction conditions and product distribution. **Model Solution:** **(a) Petrochemical Value Chain (7 marks):** The petrochemical value chain is a hierarchical four-tier system: **Tier 0 — Feedstock:** Crude oil (after atmospheric and vacuum distillation gives naphtha, kerosene, gas oil, residue) and natural gas (methane, ethane, propane, butane) act as raw feedstocks. **Tier 1 — First-Generation Petrochemicals:** Steam cracking of naphtha/ethane at 800–900 °C yields olefins (ethylene, propylene, 1,3-butadiene). Catalytic reforming of naphtha at 450–520 °C over Pt/Al₂O₃ gives BTX aromatics. Steam reforming of methane gives synthesis gas. **Tier 2 — Second-Generation Petrochemicals:** First-gen olefins undergo selective oxidation, halogenation, and alkylation to give ethylene oxide, ethylene glycol, vinyl chloride, styrene, acrylonitrile, caprolactam, etc. **Tier 3 — Third-Generation End Products:** Polymerization and polycondensation of second-gen monomers produce polyethylene, PVC, polystyrene, PET, nylon-6, SBR rubber, polyacrylonitrile, etc. **Diagram (as shown in Section 4.1 above) carries 2 marks alone.** **(b) Steam Cracking Process (7 marks):** Steam cracking (also called pyrolysis) is the **thermal decomposition of saturated hydrocarbons** in the presence of steam to produce olefins. Naphtha (a C₅–C₁₁ hydrocarbon mixture) is vaporized, mixed with steam (steam-to-hydrocarbon ratio ≈ 0.4–0.6 by weight), and passed through **radiant coils** in a fired furnace at **750–900 °C** with a **residence time of 0.1–1 second**. The reactions follow free-radical β-scission: $$\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2} \longrightarrow \text{C}_m\text{H}_{2m} + \text{C}_{n-m}\text{H}_{2(n-m)+2}$$ **Key process conditions:** - Furnace outlet temperature: 800–900 °C - Steam-to-oil ratio: 0.4–0.6 wt/wt - Residence time: 0.1–1 s (short to maximize olefins) - Pressure: near-atmospheric (~1 bar) **Product distribution (per 1000 kg naphtha):** - Ethylene: ~300 kg - Propylene: ~160 kg - 1,3-Butadiene: ~50 kg - Pyrolysis gasoline (BTX): ~150 kg - Fuel oil: ~120 kg - Methane + H₂: ~150 kg - Other byproducts: balance The cracked gas is rapidly **quenched** in transfer-line exchangers (TLEs) to stop secondary reactions, then compressed and fractionated in distillation columns to separate ethylene, propylene, and butadiene. --- #### **Part B — Question B (14 Marks — Internal Choice Alternative)** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 — Alternative Set]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **classification of petrochemicals** based on their origin and end use. Give at least five end products with their corresponding monomers. **(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **production of synthesis gas** by steam reforming of methane. Write down its major downstream products with reactions. **Model Solution:** **(a) Classification of Petrochemicals (7 marks):** Petrochemicals are classified into three categories based on their position in the value chain: 1. **First-Generation Petrochemicals** — Direct products of cracking/reforming from crude oil fractions: - Olefins: Ethylene (C₂H₄), Propylene (C₃H₆), 1,3-Butadiene (C₄H₆) - Aromatics: Benzene (C₆H₆), Toluene (C₇H₈), Xylenes (C₈H₁₀) - Syn-gas: CO + H₂ 2. **Second-Generation Petrochemicals** — Intermediates from first-gen: - Ethylene oxide, Ethylene glycol, Vinyl chloride, Styrene, Acrylonitrile, Caprolactam, Phenol 3. **Third-Generation Petrochemicals** — Finished products: - **Polyethylene (PE)** → monomer: ethylene - **Polyvinyl chloride (PVC)** → monomer: vinyl chloride - **Polystyrene (PS)** → monomer: styrene - **PET (polyester)** → monomers: terephthalic acid + ethylene glycol - **Nylon-6** → monomer: caprolactam - **SBR rubber** → monomers: styrene + 1,3-butadiene These end products find applications in packaging (PE, PET), construction (PVC pipes), automotive (SBR tyres), textiles (nylon, polyester), and electronics (PS insulators). **(b) Production of Synthesis Gas (7 marks):** Synthesis gas (syn-gas) is a mixture of **CO and H₂** produced industrially by steam reforming of natural gas (methane). **Primary Reaction — Steam Reforming:** $$\text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{CO} + 3\,\text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H = +206 \;\text{kJ/mol}$$ **Secondary Reaction — Water-Gas Shift:** $$\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H = -41 \;\text{kJ/mol}$$ **Process conditions:** - Catalyst: **Nickel on Al₂O₃ support** with potassium promoter - Temperature: **800–900 °C** - Pressure: **15–30 bar** - Steam-to-methane ratio: 2.5–3.5 **Major downstream products of syn-gas:** 1. **Methanol synthesis** (Cu-ZnO-Al₂O₃ catalyst, 250 °C, 50 bar): $$\text{CO} + 2\,\text{H}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{OH}$$ 2. **Ammonia synthesis** (Haber-Bosch, Fe catalyst, 450 °C, 200 bar): $$\text{N}_2 + 3\,\text{H}_2 \longrightarrow 2\,\text{NH}_3$$ 3. **Fischer-Tropsch synthesis** (Co or Fe catalyst, 200–350 °C): $$\text{nCO} + (2n+1)\text{H}_2 \longrightarrow \text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2} + n\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ 4. **Formaldehyde** (Ag or Fe-Mo catalyst): $$\text{CH}_3\text{OH} + 0.5\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{HCHO} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$ These C1 chemicals are the foundation of fertilizers (urea from NH₃), paints, plastics, and synthetic fuels. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam:** - **Definition:** Petrochemicals = chemicals from petroleum + natural gas feedstocks - **Three pillars:** Olefins, Aromatics (BTX), Synthesis gas (CO + H₂) - **Three generations:** First (from feedstock) → Second (intermediates) → Third (finished polymers/synthetics) - **Steam cracking:** Naphtha + steam → olefins at **750–900 °C**, **0.1–1 s residence time** - **Catalytic reforming:** Naphtha → BTX aromatics at **450–520 °C** over **Pt/Al₂O₃** - **Largest petrochemical globally:** **Ethylene** (≈200 million tonnes/year) - **PVC monomer:** Vinyl chloride (from ethylene + Cl₂) - **PET monomers:** Terephthalic acid + Mono-ethylene glycol (MEG) - **Nylon-6 monomer:** Caprolactam (from benzene + phenol route) - **Syn-gas production:** Steam reforming CH₄ + H₂O → CO + 3H₂ over **Ni/Al₂O₃** at **800–900 °C** - **Ammonia (Haber-Bosch):** N₂ + 3H₂ → 2NH₃, Fe catalyst, 450 °C, 200 bar - **Methanol synthesis:** CO + 2H₂ → CH₃OH, Cu-ZnO-Al₂O₃, 250 °C, 50 bar - **Ethylene oxide:** 2 C₂H₄ + O₂ → 2 C₂H₄O, Ag catalyst, 250 °C - **Styrene:** Benzene + Ethylene → Ethylbenzene → Styrene + H₂ (dehydrogenation) - **Acrylonitrile (Sohio process):** C₃H₆ + NH₃ + 1.5 O₂ → C₃H₃N + 3 H₂O, Bi-Mo oxide, 450 °C - **Always cite:** Reaction + Catalyst + Temperature + Pressure + Conversion/Selectivity (where applicable) - **Draw the value-chain diagram** in every 14-mark answer for extra clarity marks

topics.title: Introduction to Polymers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT741 - PETROCHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Polymers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **polymer** (from the Greek words *poly* meaning "many" and *meros* meaning "parts") is a high-molecular-weight macromolecule composed of a large number of small repeating structural units called **monomers**, which are joined together by covalent chemical bonds through a process known as **polymerization**.

The formal IUPAC-aligned definition accepted in the KTU 2024 Petrochemical Technology syllabus states:

> A polymer is a substance composed of macromolecules characterized by the **multiple repetition** of one or more species of atoms or groups of atoms (constitutional repeating units) linked to one another in amounts sufficient to provide a set of properties that do not vary markedly with the addition or removal of one or a few constitutional repeating units.

Mathematically, the simplest representation of a polymer is:

$$[M]_n \xrightarrow{\text{Polymerization}} -(M-M-M-\cdots-M)_n-$$

where:
- $M$ = monomer unit
- $n$ = **degree of polymerization (DP)**
- $M_n$ = total molecular weight of the polymer

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Polymers form the foundational backbone of the petrochemical industry because the majority of commercial polymers (polyethylene, polypropylene, PVC, polystyrene, PET, etc.) are derived directly from **petrochemical feedstocks** — primarily monomers obtained via the **steam cracking of naphtha and natural gas liquids**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **train**. Each train is made up of many similar **coaches** linked together by couplings. A polymer is exactly that — a "molecular train" where:

- The **coaches** = monomers (small molecules)
- The **couplings** = covalent bonds formed during polymerization
- The **entire train** = the polymer macromolecule

If the train has only 2–3 coaches, it is just a small molecule (like ethylene, $C_2H_4$). But when the same coach is repeated **hundreds, thousands, or even millions of times**, we get a polymer (like polyethylene, with $n$ in the range $10^3$ to $10^6$).

Another intuitive analogy: think of polymer chemistry as **stringing beads on a necklace**. Each bead is a monomer, and the entire necklace (with thousands of beads) is the polymer. This is why polymers are also called **macromolecules** ("macro" = large, "molecule" = small chemical unit repeated many times).

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range / Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Degree of Polymerization | $DP$ or $n$ | $10^2$ to $10^6$ | Dimensionless |
| Molecular weight of polymer | $M_n$ | $10^4$ to $10^7$ | g/mol (Da) |
| Repeating unit molar mass | $M_0$ | 28 (for $CH_2$) to 200+ | g/mol |
| Avogadro's Number | $N_A$ | $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ | mol$^{-1}$ |
| Boltzmann Constant | $k_B$ | $1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ | J/K |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Classification:** A molecule is generally accepted as a "polymer" only when $n \geq 100$ (i.e., molecular weight $> 1000$ g/mol). Below this, it is typically classified as an **oligomer**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Hierarchy of a Polymer

A polymer's structure can be visualized at **four hierarchical levels**, each of which governs its macroscopic properties:

1. **Primary Structure** — The linear sequence of monomer units and the exact chemical identity of each repeating unit.
2. **Secondary Structure** — Local conformation (e.g., helical, planar zigzag) arising from short-range rotations around backbone bonds.
3. **Tertiary Structure** — Three-dimensional folding and arrangement of the chain in space.
4. **Quaternary Structure** — Aggregation/interaction of multiple polymer chains (crystallites, amorphous domains).

## 2.2 Classification of Polymers (KTU High-Yield Topic)

Polymers are classified along **multiple orthogonal axes**. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the following classifications:

### (A) Based on Origin
- **Natural Polymers** — Found in nature (cellulose, starch, proteins, natural rubber, silk, DNA).
- **Synthetic Polymers** — Man-made via polymerization (polyethylene, nylon, PVC, Bakelite).
- **Semi-Synthetic** — Modified natural polymers (celluloid, rayon, cellulose acetate).

### (B) Based on Thermal Response (Critical for Processing)
- **Thermoplastics** — Linear/branched chains, soften on heating, harden on cooling, **reprocessable** (PE, PP, PS, PVC, PET).
- **Thermosetting Plastics (Thermosets)** — Form a 3D crosslinked network on heating, **cannot be remolded** (Bakelite, melamine, epoxy, phenol-formaldehyde).
- **Elastomers** — Weakly crosslinked, exhibit high elastic recovery (natural rubber, silicone, polyurethane).

### (C) Based on Polymerization Mechanism
- **Addition (Chain-Growth) Polymers** — No byproduct; monomers add sequentially via free-radical/ionic/coordinate mechanisms (PE, PP, PS, PVC).
- **Condensation (Step-Growth) Polymers** — Small molecules (often $H_2O$, $HCl$, $CH_3OH$) are eliminated (Nylon-6,6, PET, Bakelite).

### (D) Based on Chain Architecture
- **Linear** — Straight chain (HDPE, PVC).
- **Branched** — Side chains off main backbone (LDPE).
- **Crosslinked** — Covalent bridges between chains (vulcanized rubber, thermosets).
- **Network** — Fully interconnected 3D network (epoxy).

### (E) Based on Monomer Type
- **Homopolymer** — Single type of monomer (Polyethylene from ethylene).
- **Copolymer** — Two or more monomers (SBR from styrene + butadiene).
   - Types: Random, Alternating, Block, Graft.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the table below — every formula appears directly in KTU ESE numerical questions.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $DP = \dfrac{M_n}{M_0}$ | Degree of Polymerization | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $\overline{M_n} = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i}$ | Number-Average Molecular Weight | g/mol |
| 3 | $\overline{M_w} = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i^{\,2}}{\sum N_i M_i} = \dfrac{\sum w_i M_i}{\sum w_i}$ | Weight-Average Molecular Weight | g/mol |
| 4 | $PDI = \dfrac{\overline{M_w}}{\overline{M_n}}$ | Polydispersity Index | Dimensionless ($\geq 1$) |
| 5 | $n_i = \dfrac{N_i}{\sum N_i}$ | Number Fraction of Species $i$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $w_i = \dfrac{N_i M_i}{\sum N_i M_i}$ | Weight Fraction of Species $i$ | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $[\eta] = K M^{\,a}$ | Mark-Houwink-Sakurada Equation (Intrinsic Viscosity) | dL/g |
| 8 | $\tau = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Shear Stress (for polymer melt rheology) | Pa |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The Polydispersity Index (PDI) determines the **processability** of a polymer. Monodisperse polymers (PDI = 1, only ideal/laboratory) are theoretically uniform; commercial polymers have PDI between 1.5 and 50. A narrow PDI gives better mechanical strength, while a broad PDI improves melt flow during extrusion/injection molding.

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind Molecular Weight Averaging

A polymer sample is **never composed of chains of identical length**. It is a mixture of chains of varying $n$. Therefore, two different averaging methods give different values:

- **$\overline{M_n}$ (Number-Average)** — Weighted by the *number* of molecules of each size. Sensitive to small molecules.
- **$\overline{M_w}$ (Weight-Average)** — Weighted by the *mass* contribution of each size. Sensitive to large molecules.

Always: $\overline{M_w} \geq \overline{M_n}$, so $PDI \geq 1$ (equality holds only for a perfectly monodisperse sample).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Number-Average Molecular Weight ($\overline{M_n}$)

**Starting Premise:** Consider a polymer sample containing $N$ total molecules distributed among $i = 1, 2, 3, \ldots, k$ species, where the $i^{\text{th}}$ species has $N_i$ molecules, each of molar mass $M_i$.

**Step 1 — Define the total mass of species $i$:**
The total mass of all chains belonging to species $i$ is given by the product of the number of chains and the mass of each chain.

$$m_i = N_i \times M_i$$

**Step 2 — Define the total number of molecules in the sample:**
This is simply the sum of the populations of all species in the sample.

$$N_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} N_i$$

**Step 3 — Define the total mass of the sample:**
This is the sum of the masses of each species' molecular population.

$$m_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} N_i M_i$$

**Step 4 — Construct the number-average:**
The number-average molecular weight is the **arithmetic mean** of the molar masses, weighted by the number of molecules in each species. It is the total mass divided by the total number of molecules.

$$\overline{M_n} = \frac{m_{total}}{N_{total}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} N_i M_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{k} N_i}$$

This is the standard textbook definition of the number-average molecular weight.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Weight-Average Molecular Weight ($\overline{M_w}$)

**Starting Premise:** To get a mass-weighted average, each species must be weighted by its **total mass contribution** to the sample, not by the count of its molecules.

**Step 1 — Express the weight (mass) fraction of species $i$:**
The weight fraction is the ratio of the total mass of species $i$ to the total mass of the entire sample.

$$w_i = \frac{N_i M_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{k} N_i M_i}$$

**Step 2 — Confirm normalization of weight fractions:**
By construction, the weight fractions must sum to unity, since they are a partition of the total mass into fractional contributions. We verify this:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{N_i M_i}{\sum N_i M_i} = \frac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i M_i} = 1$$

**Step 3 — Construct the weight-average:**
The weight-average is the weighted arithmetic mean of molar masses, where the weighting coefficients are the weight fractions. This biases the average toward the heavier species.

$$\overline{M_w} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i M_i = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \left( \frac{N_i M_i}{\sum N_j M_j} \right) M_i$$

**Step 4 — Simplify to canonical textbook form:**
The denominator $\sum N_j M_j$ is a constant with respect to the summation index, so it can be factored out to give the standard form found in all polymer science textbooks.

$$\overline{M_w} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} N_i M_i^{\,2}}{\sum_{i=1}^{k} N_i M_i}$$

This completes the derivation. The appearance of $M_i^2$ in the numerator is precisely why larger molecules contribute disproportionately, making $\overline{M_w} \geq \overline{M_n}$.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A polymer sample consists of three monodisperse fractions. The data is tabulated below:

| Species ($i$) | $N_i$ (number of molecules) | $M_i$ (g/mol) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 100 | 10,000 |
| 2 | 50 | 50,000 |
| 3 | 10 | 100,000 |

**Calculate $\overline{M_n}$, $\overline{M_w}$, and PDI.**

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute required summations:**

$$\sum N_i = 100 + 50 + 10 = 160$$

$$\sum N_i M_i = (100)(10{,}000) + (50)(50{,}000) + (10)(100{,}000) = 1{,}000{,}000 + 2{,}500{,}000 + 1{,}000{,}000 = 4{,}500{,}000$$

$$\sum N_i M_i^{\,2} = (100)(10{,}000)^2 + (50)(50{,}000)^2 + (10)(100{,}000)^2$$
$$= (100)(1 \times 10^{8}) + (50)(25 \times 10^{8}) + (10)(1 \times 10^{10})$$
$$= 1 \times 10^{10} + 12.5 \times 10^{10} + 10 \times 10^{10} = 23.5 \times 10^{10}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\overline{M_n}$:**

$$\overline{M_n} = \frac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i} = \frac{4{,}500{,}000}{160} = 28{,}125 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\overline{M_w}$:**

$$\overline{M_w} = \frac{\sum N_i M_i^{\,2}}{\sum N_i M_i} = \frac{23.5 \times 10^{10}}{4.5 \times 10^{6}} = 52{,}222.22 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 4 — Compute PDI:**

$$PDI = \frac{\overline{M_w}}{\overline{M_n}} = \frac{52{,}222.22}{28{,}125} = 1.857$$

**Interpretation:** Since $PDI > 1$, the polymer is polydisperse (typical of commercial polymers). The weight-average is higher than the number-average, confirming the presence of heavier chains.

## 3.4 Derivation 3: Degree of Polymerization (DP) from Molecular Weight

The DP is the average number of monomer units in a polymer chain. For a homopolymer where every repeating unit has identical molar mass $M_0$:

**Step 1 — Start from definition:** The total molar mass of a chain equals the molar mass of one repeating unit multiplied by the number of units.

$$M_{chain} = n \times M_0$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $n$:** Dividing both sides by $M_0$ yields the number of repeating units in a single chain.

$$n = \frac{M_{chain}}{M_0}$$

**Step 3 — Average over the sample:** For a polydisperse sample, replace $M_{chain}$ with the number-average molecular weight $\overline{M_n}$ to get the average degree of polymerization.

$$DP = \frac{\overline{M_n}}{M_0}$$

For polyethylene, $M_0 = 28$ g/mol ($CH_2$ group). A commercial HDPE with $\overline{M_n} = 2.8 \times 10^5$ g/mol therefore has:

$$DP = \frac{2.8 \times 10^5}{28} = 10{,}000 \text{ repeating units per chain}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Hierarchical Classification of Polymers

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Polymerization Process Architecture

```mermaid

    feedA[Naphtha Feedstock]
    feedB[Natural Gas NGL]

    feedA --> procA
```
