
## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Molecular Weight Distribution Concept

```mermaid
graph TD
    samp[Polymer Sample]
    dist[Distribution of Chain Lengths]
    shortChains[Short Chains low M]
    medChains[Medium Chains]
    longChains[Long Chains high M]

    MnBar[Number Average Mn biased toward short chains]
    MwBar[Weight Average Mw biased toward long chains]
    PDIval[PDI Mw over Mn greater than 1]

    samp --> dist
    dist --> shortChains
    dist --> medChains
    dist --> longChains

    shortChains --> MnBar
    longChains --> MwBar
    MnBar --> PDIval
    MwBar --> PDIval
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "Degree of Polymerization" (DP). Derive the relationship between DP and number-average molecular weight.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Degree of Polymerization (DP)** is defined as the average number of monomer (repeating) units present in a single polymer chain. It is a dimensionless quantity that characterizes the average chain length in a polymer sample.

**Derivation:**
For a single polymer chain made up of $n$ repeating units, each of molar mass $M_0$, the total molar mass of the chain is given by:

$$M_{chain} = n \times M_0$$

For a polydisperse sample, we replace the single chain mass with the number-average molecular weight $\overline{M_n}$ and $n$ with $DP$:

$$\overline{M_n} = DP \times M_0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad DP = \frac{\overline{M_n}}{M_0}$$

For example, for polyethylene ($M_0 = 28$ g/mol), a sample with $\overline{M_n} = 1.4 \times 10^5$ g/mol has $DP = 5000$.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between addition and condensation polymerization with two examples each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Addition Polymerization | Condensation Polymerization |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Chain-growth; monomers add sequentially to an active chain end | Step-growth; any two species with complementary functionality can react |
| Byproduct | **No** small molecule eliminated | Small molecule (water, HCl, methanol) eliminated |
| Initiator | Required (radical, ionic, or coordination) | Generally not required; driven by functionality and heat |
| Repeat unit | Same molecular formula as monomer | Loss of atoms due to byproduct elimination |
| Examples | Polyethylene (PE), Polypropylene (PP), Polystyrene (PS), PVC | Nylon-6,6, PET, Bakelite, Polycarbonate |
| Monomer functionality | Unsaturated (e.g., $CH_2=CH-R$) | Bifunctional (e.g., $HOOC-R-COOH$ and $HO-R'-OH$) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define a polymer. Classify polymers with examples based on (i) thermal response, and (ii) chain architecture. Draw neat sketches of each type of chain architecture.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A polymer sample has the following composition:

| Species | $N_i$ | $M_i$ (g/mol) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 30 | 20,000 |
| 2 | 40 | 50,000 |
| 3 | 20 | 80,000 |
| 4 | 10 | 1,00,000 |

Calculate $\overline{M_n}$, $\overline{M_w}$, and PDI. Comment on the polydispersity.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 Marks]**

**Definition:** A polymer is a high-molecular-weight macromolecule composed of a large number of repeating structural units (monomers) linked together by covalent bonds. (1 Mark)

**Classification based on thermal response (3 Marks):**

1. **Thermoplastics** — Linear or branched chains, soften on heating, harden on cooling, reprocessable. Examples: Polyethylene, PVC, PET, Polystyrene.

2. **Thermosetting Plastics (Thermosets)** — Form 3D crosslinked network on heating, cannot be remolded. Examples: Bakelite, Epoxy, Melamine.

3. **Elastomers** — Weakly crosslinked, exhibit large elastic deformation. Examples: Natural rubber, silicone rubber.

**Classification based on chain architecture (3 Marks):**

1. **Linear** — Straight unbranched chains (e.g., HDPE).
2. **Branched** — Long side chains off main backbone (e.g., LDPE).
3. **Crosslinked** — Covalent bridges connecting chains (e.g., vulcanized rubber).
4. **Network** — Fully 3D interconnected network (e.g., Bakelite, epoxy).

*(Sketches of each architecture should be drawn as straight, branched, crosslinked, and 3D network structures.)*

**(b) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Compute the summation terms:**

$$\sum N_i = 30 + 40 + 20 + 10 = 100$$

$$\sum N_i M_i = (30)(20{,}000) + (40)(50{,}000) + (20)(80{,}000) + (10)(1{,}00{,}000)$$
$$= 6{,}00{,}000 + 20{,}00{,}000 + 16{,}00{,}000 + 10{,}00{,}000 = 52{,}00{,}000 \text{ g/mol}$$

$$\sum N_i M_i^{\,2} = (30)(20{,}000)^2 + (40)(50{,}000)^2 + (20)(80{,}000)^2 + (10)(1{,}00{,}000)^2$$
$$= (30)(4 \times 10^{8}) + (40)(25 \times 10^{8}) + (20)(64 \times 10^{8}) + (10)(1 \times 10^{10})$$
$$= 12 \times 10^{8} + 100 \times 10^{8} + 128 \times 10^{8} + 100 \times 10^{8} = 340 \times 10^{8}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate $\overline{M_n}$:**

$$\overline{M_n} = \frac{52{,}00{,}000}{100} = 52{,}000 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate $\overline{M_w}$:**

$$\overline{M_w} = \frac{340 \times 10^{8}}{52 \times 10^{5}} = \frac{3.4 \times 10^{10}}{5.2 \times 10^{6}} = 65{,}384.6 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate PDI:**

$$PDI = \frac{65{,}384.6}{52{,}000} = 1.257$$

**Step 5 — Comment:**
Since $PDI = 1.257$, the polymer is **moderately polydisperse**. Commercial polymers typically exhibit PDI between 1.5 and 5; this sample is **narrowly polydisperse** and hence will have good mechanical strength but may be slightly difficult to process via melt extrusion.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat flow diagram, explain the petrochemical route to the production of polyethylene starting from naphtha. List any two industrial grades of polyethylene with their distinguishing properties.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of Polydispersity Index (PDI). A polymer sample has $\overline{M_n} = 40{,}000$ g/mol and $PDI = 2.5$. Calculate $\overline{M_w}$ and the degree of polymerization (assume $M_0 = 28$ g/mol for polyethylene).

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 Marks]**

**Petrochemical Route to Polyethylene from Naphtha (5 Marks for flow diagram + explanation):**

The process flow is:
$$\text{Naphtha} \xrightarrow{\text{Steam Cracking (750-900°C)}} \text{Cracked Gas (mixed olefins)} \xrightarrow{\text{Fractional Distillation}} \text{Ethylene} \xrightarrow{\text{Polymerization}} \text{Polyethylene}$$

**Step 1 — Steam Cracking:** Naphtha (a mixture of hydrocarbons boiling 30-200°C) is fed to a steam cracker with steam at very high temperatures (750-900°C) and short residence time. This breaks C-C bonds, producing a mixture of olefins (ethylene, propylene, butadiene) and aromatics.

**Step 2 — Quenching & Compression:** The cracked gas is rapidly quenched to stop secondary reactions, then compressed.

**Step 3 — Fractional Distillation:** Ethylene is separated as a high-purity stream (polymer-grade, >99.9%).

**Step 4 — Polymerization:** Polymer-grade ethylene is polymerized via:
- **High-pressure process (1000-3000 atm, free-radical):** produces **LDPE** (Low-Density Polyethylene).
- **Ziegler-Natta / Phillips catalyst (low pressure, coordination polymerization):** produces **HDPE** (High-Density Polyethylene).
- **Metallocene catalysts:** produces **LLDPE** (Linear LDPE) and **mLLDPE**.

**Two Industrial Grades with Distinguishing Properties (2 Marks):**

1. **LDPE (Low-Density Polyethylene):** Highly branched chains, density 0.910-0.925 g/cm³, soft, flexible, transparent; used for plastic bags, squeeze bottles, film packaging.

2. **HDPE (High-Density Polyethylene):** Linear chains, density 0.941-0.965 g/cm³, stiff, strong, opaque; used for rigid containers, pipes, toys, fuel tanks.

**(b) [7 Marks]**

**Polydispersity Index (PDI) Explanation (3 Marks):**

The Polydispersity Index is the ratio of the weight-average molecular weight to the number-average molecular weight:

$$PDI = \frac{\overline{M_w}}{\overline{M_n}}$$

- $PDI = 1$: Perfectly monodisperse (theoretical/laboratory only — e.g., proteins, polypeptides).
- $PDI > 1$: Polydisperse; all synthetic polymers are polydisperse.
- $PDI$ indicates the **breadth of the molecular weight distribution**: a low PDI (1.0-1.2) implies narrow distribution (good mechanical properties), while a high PDI (>3) implies broad distribution (easier melt processing).

**Calculation (4 Marks):**

Given: $\overline{M_n} = 40{,}000$ g/mol, $PDI = 2.5$, $M_0 = 28$ g/mol.

**Step 1 — Calculate $\overline{M_w}$:**

$$\overline{M_w} = PDI \times \overline{M_n} = 2.5 \times 40{,}000 = 1{,}00{,}000 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Degree of Polymerization:**

$$DP = \frac{\overline{M_n}}{M_0} = \frac{40{,}000}{28} = 1428.57 \approx 1429 \text{ repeating units}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to use $M_i^2$** in the numerator for $\overline{M_w}$ — many students erroneously write $\overline{M_w} = \frac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i}$, which is the formula for $\overline{M_n}$. This single error typically costs **3-4 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing $DP$ with $n$** — $DP$ is an average over the sample, not the chain length of a single molecule.
> 3. **In unit problems**, always express $M_i$ in consistent units (g/mol). Mixing g/kg is a frequent mark-loser.
> 4. **Not commenting on PDI** — examiners award a half-mark specifically for the qualitative interpretation of $PDI$ (narrow vs. broad distribution, implication on processability).
> 5. **Forgetting the byproduct** when describing condensation polymerization (always state $H_2O$ or $HCl$).
> 6. **Confusing thermoset vs. thermoplastic** — thermosets DO NOT soften on reheating (they decompose). This distinction is a classic KTU short-answer question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Polymer** = many (poly) units (meros); high MW macromolecule from monomer repetition.
- A molecule with $n \geq 100$ is a polymer; below is an oligomer.
- **Degree of Polymerization:** $DP = \dfrac{\overline{M_n}}{M_0}$ — dimensionless, ~$10^2$ to $10^6$.
- **Two molecular weight averages are mandatory:**
  - $\overline{M_n}$ (number-average) = $\dfrac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i}$ — sensitive to small chains.
  - $\overline{M_w}$ (weight-average) = $\dfrac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i}$ — sensitive to large chains.
- **PDI = $\overline{M_w}/\overline{M_n} \geq 1$** — equals 1 only for monodisperse samples.
- **Three thermal classes:** Thermoplastic (reprocessable), Thermoset (irreversible 3D network), Elastomer (elastic recovery).
- **Two polymerization mechanisms:** Addition (chain-growth, no byproduct) vs. Condensation (step-growth, eliminates small molecule).
- **Chain architectures:** Linear, Branched, Crosslinked, Network.
- **Copolymers** have four arrangements: Random, Alternating, Block, Graft.
- **Petrochemical origin:** Most commercial polymers come from ethylene, propylene, butadiene, styrene, vinyl chloride — all derived from **steam cracking of naphtha/NGL**.
- **Mark-Houwink equation** $[\eta] = K M^a$ relates intrinsic viscosity to molecular weight — used in viscometric MW determination.
- **Two key industrial polyethylenes:** LDPE (branched, free-radical, high pressure) vs. HDPE (linear, Ziegler-Natta, low pressure).
- A higher PDI = broader distribution = easier melt processing but lower mechanical strength.
- The repeating unit of polyethylene is $-(CH_2-CH_2)_n-$ with $M_0 = 28$ g/mol.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Elastomers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT741 - PETROCHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Elastomers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Elastomers** are a class of viscoelastic **polymeric materials** that exhibit **large elastic strain** (typically $\geq 200\%$) under relatively low applied stress and recover their original dimensions upon removal of the stress. The name is derived from *elastic polymer*. They are amorphous polymers with **glass transition temperature ($T_g$)** well below their service temperature, possess weak intermolecular forces, and are rendered elastic through the introduction of a controlled network of chemical crosslinks (vulcanization) that prevents permanent plastic flow.

In petrochemical technology, elastomers form the downstream value-added chain emerging from C4/C5 olefins, butadiene, styrene, isoprene, acrylonitrile, isobutylene, and chloroprene — making them a strategic product family of the modern petrochemical complex.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine a bowl of cooked spaghetti left loose on a plate — the strands slide over each other and never return to their original tangled shape once you lift them. Now imagine the same spaghetti but with a few hundred rubber bands wrapped around the bundle at random points. When you lift the bundle, the bands pull it back toward its original coiled shape.

- The **spaghetti strands** represent **polymer chains** (long, tangled molecules).
- The **rubber bands** represent **crosslinks** (chemical bridges formed during vulcanization).
- The behaviour of returning to original shape is **elasticity**.

This is precisely what an elastomer is: **flexible polymer chains held together by sparse, springy crosslinks** that allow stretch and recoil without flow.

## 1.3 Key KTU-Syllabus Terminology

| Term | Meaning in KTU Context |
|---|---|
| **Elastomer** | Elastic polymer; low modulus, high elongation |
| **Vulcanization** | Crosslinking of rubber using sulfur/peroxide/metal oxides |
| **Cure** | The process of establishing the crosslink network |
| **Compounding** | Mixing rubber with additives to achieve desired properties |
| **Tensile Strength** | Maximum stress before breaking; units: MPa |
| **Elongation at Break** | % strain at fracture |
| **Hardness (Shore A)** | Resistance to indentation; standard elastomer scale |
| **Glass Transition Temp $T_g$** | Temperature below which elastomer becomes rigid/glassy |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric for an Engineering Elastomer:**
> - Ultimate Tensile Strength: **15–30 MPa**
> - Elongation at Break: **300–800 %**
> - Hardness: **30–90 Shore A**
> - Density: **0.9–1.5 g/cm³**
> - Operating $T_g$ range for general purpose rubbers: **−70 °C to −30 °C**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Classification of Elastomers (KTU Bloom Level: Remember / Understand)

Elastomers are classified on **two KTU-standard axes**:

### A. By Origin
1. **Natural Rubber (NR)** — obtained as latex from *Hevea brasiliensis*; chemically **cis-1,4-polyisoprene**.
2. **Synthetic Rubbers (SR)** — petrochemically manufactured.

### B. By Thermal Behaviour / Usage
- **General Purpose (Unsaturated backbone):** NR, SBR, BR, IR
- **Special Purpose / Oil-Resistant (Polar backbone):** NBR, CR, ACM, HNBR
- **Saturated / Heat-Resistant (Backbone resists oxidation):** EPDM, IIR, CSM, FKM
- **Thermoplastic Elastomers (TPE):** SBS, SEBS, TPU, TPV — processed like plastics, behave like rubber.

## 2.2 The 'Why & How' of Elasticity — Structural Logic

Elastomeric elasticity is **entropic** in origin (not energetic as in metals):

1. **Amorphous polymer chains** above $T_g$ are in continuous micro-Brownian motion, coiling randomly.
2. On stretching, chains are **uncoiled** → conformational entropy drops.
3. **Crosslinks** prevent chain slippage (no permanent flow).
4. On release, thermal motion drives chains back to maximum entropy state → **snap-back**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Rubber elasticity is governed by entropy, $F \approx -T \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_T$, which is why rubber gets *stiffer* when heated (counter-intuitive for metals).

## 2.3 Vulcanization — The Heart of Elastomer Engineering

**Vulcanization** is the heat-induced process of forming 3D crosslinks between polymer chains. With **sulfur**, the chemistry proceeds via:

$$
\text{R–H} + \text{S}_8 \xrightarrow{\text{Heat, Accelerator, ZnO}} \text{R–S}_x\text{–R}
$$

Where:
- **R–H** = allylic hydrogen on polymer chain
- **S$_x$** = polysulfidic bridge (mono-, di-, poly-sulfide)
- **Accelerator** (MBT, CBS, TBBS) shortens cure time
- **Activator** (ZnO + stearic acid) activates accelerators
- **Activator System** + **Antioxidant** + **Plasticizer** + **Filler (Carbon Black)** + **Sulfur** = total compounding recipe.

### Crosslink Types in Vulcanized Rubber
- **Monosulfidic (C–S–C):** heat-stable, less flexible
- **Disulfidic (C–S–S–C):** balanced
- **Polysulfidic (C–S$_x$–C, $x \geq 3$):** flexible, less thermally stable

### Other Cure Systems (Important for KTU)
| System | Used For | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Sulfur** | NR, SBR, BR | Polysulfidic bridges |
| **Peroxide (DCP)** | EPDM, HNBR, Silicone | C–C crosslinks, heat resistant |
| **Metal Oxide (ZnO)** | CR (Neoprene) | Ether-bridge via Cl abstraction |
| **Urethanes** | Millable PU rubber | Urethane bridges |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = F/A_0$ | Engineering Stress | MPa |
| 2 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}$ | Engineering Strain | dimensionless |
| 3 | $E = \sigma / \varepsilon$ | Young's Modulus (initial slope) | MPa |
| 4 | $n = \rho / \rho_{water}$ | Specific Gravity (rubber: 0.9–1.5) | — |
| 5 | $T_g \approx (2/3)\,T_m$ | Beaman–Boyer rule (for amorphous polymers) | K |
| 6 | $\text{MC} = (W_0 - W_f)/W_0 \times 100\%$ | **Moisture Content** (latex) | % |
| 7 | $\text{VR} = (W_f - W_f')/W_f \times 100\%$ | **Volatile Rubber** loss | % |
| 8 | $\text{ACN content} = \dfrac{m_{N}}{m_{NBR}}\times 100\%$ | Acrylonitrile content of NBR | wt % |
| 9 | $H = \dfrac{F}{A_{indent}}$ | Shore A Hardness conversion | Shore A |
| 10 | $\eta_{Mooney} = f(t, T)$ | Mooney Viscosity (processability index) | MU |

> [!NOTE]
> **Avoid `|` symbols** in solution writing: write $\vert T \vert$ or $\mid T \mid$, never raw `|T|` as it breaks markdown.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Tyres** (SBR + BR + NR blends) — ~70 % of world rubber consumption.
- **Conveyor belts, hoses, gaskets** — industrial elastomer goods.
- **Vibration dampers, O-rings** — automotive & aerospace sealing.
- **Medical devices** (silicone, latex gloves) — biocompatibility.
- **Oilfield applications** — NBR/HNBR seals resistant to hydrocarbons.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Sulfur Vulcanization Stoichiometry (KTU-Calculable Problem)

**Statement:** During vulcanization of Natural Rubber (polyisoprene), the reaction consumes sulfur in proportion to the crosslink density. If 1.0 kg of NR (repeat unit C$_5$H$_8$, $M_0 = 68.12$ g/mol) consumes 2.5 g of sulfur (S, $M_S = 32.06$ g/mol) to form mono-sulfidic bridges, calculate:
1. Number of moles of repeat units
2. Number of moles of sulfur
3. Average number of sulfur atoms per repeat unit
4. Effective crosslink density if every sulfur forms one bridge between two chains.

### Step 1 — Moles of repeat unit
$$
n_{RU} = \frac{m_{NR}}{M_0} = \frac{1000 \text{ g}}{68.12 \text{ g/mol}}
$$
$$
n_{RU} = 14.68 \text{ mol}
$$

### Step 2 — Moles of Sulfur
$$
n_{S} = \frac{m_{S}}{M_S} = \frac{2.5 \text{ g}}{32.06 \text{ g/mol}}
$$
$$
n_{S} = 0.0780 \text{ mol}
$$

### Step 3 — Sulfur per repeat unit
$$
\eta_{S/RU} = \frac{n_S}{n_{RU}} = \frac{0.0780}{14.68}
$$
$$
\eta_{S/RU} = 5.31 \times 10^{-3}
$$
i.e. approximately **1 sulfur atom per ~188 repeat units**, typical for efficient vulcanization.

### Step 4 — Crosslink density
Since each mono-sulfidic bridge consumes **one S atom** linking two chains:
$$
\nu = n_S = 0.0780 \text{ mol} \;\; (\text{total bridges in 1 kg NR})
$$

In per unit volume, using rubber density $\rho_{NR} \approx 0.913$ g/cm³:
$$
V = \frac{1000}{0.913} \text{ cm}^3 = 1095.3 \text{ cm}^3 = 1.0953 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3
$$
$$
\nu_v = \frac{0.0780}{1.0953 \times 10^{-3}} = 71.2 \text{ mol/m}^3
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry target crosslink density:** $50$–$150$ mol/m³ for general purpose rubber. Our 71.2 mol/m³ corresponds to an *efficiently vulcanized, soft compound* (low durometer).

## 3.2 Derivation: Entropic Elastic Force (Conceptual, KTU Theory)

For a single ideal polymer chain of $N$ freely-jointed segments, the entropic spring constant:
$$
k_{chain} = \frac{3 k_B T}{N b^2}
$$

For a network of $\nu$ chains per unit volume:
$$
E \approx 3 \nu k_B T
$$

This is the **kinetic theory of rubber elasticity** and explains:
- Modulus increases with **temperature** (opposite of metals)
- Modulus depends **only on crosslink density**, not on chemical structure of the chain.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Two-Mark Sub-Question Favourite)

| Property | NR | SBR | BR | NBR | EPDM | CR | IIR |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $T_g$ (°C) | −72 | −50 | −110 | −30 | −55 | −45 | −70 |
| Tensile (MPa) | 25 | 20 | 18 | 18 | 15 | 24 | 18 |
| Elongation (%) | 600 | 500 | 500 | 500 | 400 | 600 | 700 |
| Oil Resistance | Poor | Poor | Poor | Excellent | Poor | Good | Fair |
| Heat Resistance | Fair | Fair | Fair | Good | Excellent | Good | Excellent |
| Ozone Resistance | Poor | Poor | Poor | Fair | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent |
| Main Use | Tyres | Tyres | Tyre sidewall | Seals | Roofing | Hoses | Inner tubes |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Classification Flowchart (KTU Visual Aid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Elastomers] --> A1[Natural Rubber NR]
    A0 --> A2[Synthetic Rubbers SR]
    A0 --> A3[Thermoplastic Elastomers TPE]

    A1 --> A1a[Cis-1,4-polyisoprene from Hevea latex]

    A2 --> B1[General Purpose]
    A2 --> B2[Oil Resistant]
    A2 --> B3[Heat and Weather Resistant]
    A2 --> B4[Specialty]

    B1 --> B1a[SBR Styrene Butadiene]
    B1 --> B1b[BR Polybutadiene]
    B1 --> B1c[IR Polyisoprene]

    B2 --> B2a[NBR Nitrile Butadiene]
    B2 --> B2b[CR Neoprene]
    B2 --> B2c[ACM Acrylic]

    B3 --> B3a[EPDM]
    B3 --> B3b[IIR Butyl]
    B3 --> B3c[CSM Hypalon]
    B3 --> B3d[FKM Viton]

    B4 --> B4a[Silicone Q]
    B4 --> B4b[PU Polyurethane]
    B4 --> B4c[Fluorocarbon]

    A3 --> C1[SBS SEBS]
    A3 --> C2[TPU]
    A3 --> C3[TPV]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Rubber Compounding & Vulcanization Plant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Raw Polymer NR SBR BR] --> M1[Banbury Mixer]
    R2[Filler Carbon Black Silica] --> M1
    R3[Plasticizer Oil] --> M1
    R4[Antioxidant TMQ] --> M1
    R5[Accelerator CBS TBBS] --> M2[Two Roll Mill]
    R6[Activator ZnO Stearic Acid] --> M2
    R7[Sulfur / Peroxide] --> M2
    M1 --> M2
    M2 --> X1[Extrusion / Calendaring]
    X1 --> X2[Moulding]
    X2 --> X3[Vulcanization 140 to 180 deg C]
    X3 --> X4[Finished Elastomer Product]
    X4 --> X5[QC Testing Tensile Hardness Ageing]
```

## 4.3 Crosslink Architecture Schematic (Textual Mermaid Representation)

```mermaid
graph TD
    CHAIN1((Polymer chain 1)) --- LINK1[ S bridge ]
    CHAIN2((Polymer chain 2)) --- LINK1
    CHAIN2 --- LINK2[ S bridge ]
    CHAIN3((Polymer chain 3)) --- LINK2
    CHAIN3 --- LINK3[ S bridge ]
    CHAIN1 --- LINK3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the schematic:** Each circle represents a long polymer chain; each labelled box represents a sulfur crosslink. The resulting 3D network is what gives an elastomer its **elastic recovery** and **dimensional stability**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question]**
**Define an elastomer. How is it distinguished from a thermoplastic and a thermoset?**

**Model Answer:**
An **elastomer** is a polymer that can be stretched to at least twice its original length and returns to its original shape upon release of stress, due to a sparse 3D crosslink network above its $T_g$.

- **vs Thermoplastic:** Thermoplastics are linear/branched, *melt and flow* on heating (no crosslinks); elastomers have *permanent crosslinks* and cannot be remelted (except TPE which combines both behaviours).
- **vs Thermoset:** Thermosets have a **dense** crosslink network giving rigidity; elastomers have a **sparse** network giving flexibility and high elongation.

*(Word limit: 80–100; 3 marks — 1 each for definition and two distinctions.)*

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is vulcanization? Name the additives used in a typical sulfur-vulcanization recipe.**

**Model Answer:**
**Vulcanization** is the chemical process of forming crosslinks between polymer chains of rubber using sulfur (or other curatives) at elevated temperature, converting soft, tacky raw rubber into a strong, elastic, dimensionally stable material.

A typical sulfur-vulcanization recipe contains:
1. **Polymer** (NR/SBR)
2. **Sulfur** (curative, 1.5–3 phr)
3. **Accelerator** (CBS/MBT/TBBS — 0.5–1.5 phr)
4. **Activator** (ZnO 3–5 phr + stearic acid 1–2 phr)
5. **Filler** (Carbon black 30–60 phr)
6. **Plasticizer/Process oil** (5–15 phr)
7. **Antioxidant/Antiozonant** (TMQ, IPPD — 1–2 phr)

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for additives.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout]**
> 
> > [!WARNING]
> > **Common places students lose marks:**
> > 1. Confusing *vulcanization* with *compounding* — vulcanization is **crosslinking**; compounding is **mixing ingredients** BEFORE vulcanization.
> > 2. Writing "sulfur crosslinks" without specifying the **poly-sulfidic** type (mono/di/poly).
> > 3. Forgetting to mention **accelerator + activator system** when describing vulcanization recipes — these are MANDATORY for sulfur cure to function.
> > 4. Mixing up **NR (cis-1,4-polyisoprene)** and **Gutta-percha (trans-1,4-polyisoprene)** — they are stereoisomers with very different properties.
> > 5. In problems, not converting **phr** (parts per hundred rubber) properly into weight fractions.

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the help of a neat flowchart, describe the **classification of elastomers** with at least two examples in each category. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **mechanism of sulfur vulcanization** of natural rubber with the role of **accelerators and activators**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for (a):**
*(Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid flowchart for the classification structure.)*

Elastomers are classified into:

| Category | Examples | Salient Feature |
|---|---|---|
| Natural Rubber (NR) | Hevea latex (cis-1,4-polyisoprene) | High tensile, resilience |
| General Purpose Synthetic | SBR, BR, IR | Tyre treads, sidewalls |
| Oil-Resistant | NBR, CR, ACM | Seals, gaskets, fuel hoses |
| Heat/Ozone Resistant | EPDM, IIR, CSM | Roofing, automotive |
| Specialty | Silicone (Q), PU, FKM | High/low temperature, biomedical |
| Thermoplastic Elastomer | SBS, TPU, TPV | Recyclable, plastic-processable |

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Activation of sulfur:** At 140–160 °C, accelerators (e.g., CBS) and activator (ZnO + stearic acid) form a **zinc-accelerator complex**.

**Step 2 — Reaction with rubber:** The complex reacts with the **allylic hydrogen** of the polyisoprene chain, producing a **rubber-bound accelerator intermediate**.

**Step 3 — Sulfur insertion:** The intermediate inserts sulfur to form **polysulfidic pendant groups** (R–S$_x$–Acc).

**Step 4 — Crosslink formation:** The pendant polysulfidic group reacts with another allylic site on a second chain, releasing the accelerator and forming the **C–S$_x$–C bridge**.

**Role of accelerators:** Reduce cure time from hours to minutes; lower required sulfur dose.

**Role of activators (ZnO + stearic acid):** Form the soluble **Zn-stearate-accelerator complex** essential for accelerator activity.

> **Examiner tip:** Mentioning that the final step **regenerates the accelerator** (catalytic cycle) gives an extra conceptual mark.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Internal Choice)*

**(a)** Compare **Natural Rubber (NR), SBR, NBR and EPDM** on the basis of (i) monomer, (ii) $T_g$, (iii) oil resistance, (iv) major application. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A natural-rubber compound is vulcanized with **3.5 phr sulfur**. Calculate:
   (i) grams of sulfur required for **1 kg of rubber**
   (ii) moles of sulfur used
   (iii) moles of sulfur per mole of repeat unit (C$_5$H$_8$, M = 68.12 g/mol)
   (iv) comment on the crosslink density achieved. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for (a):**

| Property | NR | SBR | NBR | EPDM |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Monomer | Isoprene | Styrene + Butadiene | Acrylonitrile + Butadiene | Ethylene + Propylene + Diene |
| $T_g$ (°C) | −72 | −50 | −30 | −55 |
| Oil Resistance | Poor | Poor | Excellent | Poor |
| Major Use | Tyres, gloves | Tyre treads | Oil seals, hoses | Roofing, weatherstrip |

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Grams of sulfur** — "phr" = parts per hundred parts rubber.
$$
m_S = 3.5 \times \frac{1000}{100} = 35 \text{ g}
$$

**(ii) Moles of sulfur**
$$
n_S = \frac{35}{32.06} = 1.092 \text{ mol}
$$

**(iii) Moles of repeat unit in 1 kg NR**
$$
n_{RU} = \frac{1000}{68.12} = 14.68 \text{ mol}
$$
$$
\frac{n_S}{n_{RU}} = \frac{1.092}{14.68} = 0.0744
$$
$$
\text{i.e., } \approx 7.4\% \text{ of repeat units carry one sulfur.}
$$

**(iv) Comment on crosslink density**
With $\rho_{NR} \approx 0.913$ g/cm³, $V = 1.095 \times 10^{-3}$ m³.
$$
\nu_v = \frac{1.092}{1.095 \times 10^{-3}} = 997 \text{ mol/m}^3
$$
This is **much higher than the 50–150 mol/m³ ideal range**, indicating a **hard, over-cured (ebonite-like) compound** unless sulfur efficiency is reduced via accelerators. Hence in practice accelerators are essential.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Remember Points for KTU Exam (in 60 seconds):**
> 
> - **Definition:** Elastomer = low-modulus, high-elongation polymer with sparse 3D crosslinks; $T_g$ below service temperature.
> - **Elasticity origin:** Entropic, governed by $\Delta S$, not $\Delta U$.
> - **NR** = *cis-1,4-polyisoprene* (Hevea latex).
> - **Synthetic rubbers** derive from petrochemical monomers: butadiene, styrene, isoprene, isobutylene, acrylonitrile, chloroprene, ethylene–propylene.
> - **Vulcanization** = crosslinking, typically by sulfur + accelerator (CBS) + activator (ZnO/stearic acid).
> - **Crosslink types:** mono, di, poly-sulfidic — affects flexibility vs heat resistance.
> - **Compounding additives:** polymer, sulfur, accelerator, activator, filler (carbon black), plasticizer, antioxidant — know **all 7** and their function.
> - **Key property trends:**
>   - Higher ACN in NBR → better oil resistance, higher $T_g$.
>   - Saturated backbone (EPDM, IIR) → excellent ozone/heat resistance.
>   - Higher crosslink density → higher hardness, modulus; lower elongation.
> - **phr** = parts per hundred rubber (always per 100 parts base polymer).
> - **Kinetic theory:** $E \approx 3 \nu k_B T$ — rubber gets *stiffer* as temperature rises.
> - **TPEs** combine plastic processability with rubber elasticity — recyclable.
> - **Crosslink density range:** 50–150 mol/m³ for general purpose; >500 mol/m³ → ebonite.
> - **Conversion of phr to mass %:** wt % = phr / (100 + phr) × 100.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Polyurethanes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT741 - PETROCHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Polyurethanes # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Polyurethanes (PU)** are a class of **heterogeneous polymeric materials** containing repeating **urethane (carbamate) linkages** (–NH–CO–O–) in their backbone, formed primarily by the **step-growth addition polymerization** of **diisocyanates** (or polyisocyanates) with **polyols** (polyfunctional alcohols containing active hydrogen atoms), in the presence of catalysts, blowing agents, surfactants, and chain extenders. The urethane linkage is **not** derived from urea-formaldehyde (despite the etymological similarity in the name "polyurethane"); the prefix "poly" simply denotes the polymeric nature, and the "urethane" refers to the carbamate functional group (–O–CO–NH–) that constitutes the repeating unit. | Polymer Family | Characteristic Linkage | Source Monomer Combination | |---|---|---| | Polyurethane | –NH–CO–O– | Diisocyanate + Polyol | | Polyurea | –NH–CO–NH– | Diisocyanate + Diamine | | Polyester | –CO–O– | Diacid + Diol | | Polyamide (Nylon) | –CO–NH– | Diacid + Diamine | | Polyether | –O– | Epoxide (ring opening) | ## 1.2 Historical Context (Examiner Frequently Tests This) > [!IMPORTANT] > Polyurethanes were first synthesized by **Professor Otto Bayer** and his research group at **IG Farben, Leverkusen, Germany, in 1937**. The development was a strategic German wartime effort to find a non-rival alternative to polyamides (Nylon-6,6) whose patents were held by DuPont in the USA. Today, polyurethanes constitute one of the most versatile polymer families in the world, second only to polyethylene, polypropylene, PVC, and polystyrene in global production volume. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of polyurethanes as a **molecular "Lego wall" built using two complementary types of bricks**: - **Brick Type A (Isocyanate, –N=C=O):** Reactive male connector with a sharp "claw" - **Brick Type B (Polyol, –OH):** Reactive female connector with a "socket" When the claw of A meets the socket of B, they click together to form a **urethane link** (–NH–CO–O–). The wall (polymer) gains: - **Flexibility** → from the long, soft polyol chain (the "rubbery rope" between links) - **Rigidity** → from the dense, aromatic isocyanate segments (the "hard brick") > [!TIP] > **The "Hard-Soft" Block Copolymer Analogy:** Imagine a railway track. The wooden sleepers (rigid, hard urethane-urea segments from aromatic isocyanates) hold the steel rails (flexible, soft polyol segments) in place. The balance between these two phases gives the polyurethane its specific properties—from soft foams (more polyol, like a foam mattress) to hard plastics (more isocyanate, like skateboard wheels). ## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants | Metric | Value / Unit | Significance | |---|---|---| | NCO Index (Isocyanate Index) | 0.8 – 1.1 (typically ~1.0) | Moles of NCO per mole of OH | | Functionality of Polyol (f) | 2.0 – 8.0 | Number of –OH groups per molecule | | NCO% (Isocyanate content) | % by mass of –NCO groups | Defines prepolymer reactivity | | Equivalent Weight | g/equivalent | Mass containing 1 mole of reactive groups | | Density of PU foam | **15 – 800 kg/m³** | Highly variable by application | | Glass Transition (Tg) | –50 °C to +150 °C | Tunable via hard/soft segment ratio | --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Fundamental Reaction: Urethane Linkage Formation The core reaction is the **addition** of an active hydrogen compound (R'–OH) to an isocyanate (R–N=C=O), producing a carbamate (urethane) without any small-molecule by-product: $$ R-N=C=O \quad + \quad R'-O-H \quad \xrightarrow{\text{cat.}} \quad R-NH-CO-O-R' $$ This is a **step-growth (condensation-like but no by-product) polymerization** where each bond-forming event simply adds the reactants together. ## 2.2 Mechanistic Pathway (Why the Reaction is So Fast) The reaction proceeds via a **nucleophilic addition mechanism** in which the lone pair of the oxygen in the –OH group attacks the electrophilic carbon of the –N=C=O group: **Step 1:** The polyol oxygen attacks the electrophilic carbon of isocyanate **Step 2:** A proton transfer occurs from oxygen to nitrogen **Step 3:** The stable urethane (carbamate) linkage is formed The reactivity order of active hydrogens with isocyanates is: $$ \text{R-NH}_2 \;>\; \text{R-OH} \;>\; \text{R-SH} \;>\; \text{R-COOH} \;>\; \text{R-CONH}_2 \;>\; \text{R-SONH}_2 $$ This explains why **amines cure isocyanates very rapidly** (used in fast-cure spray elastomers), while polyols require catalysis for foaming reactions. ## 2.3 Secondary Reactions (Crucial for Foam Formation) When **water** is used as the blowing agent, a cascade of secondary reactions occurs: **Reaction 1 — Isocyanate + Water → Unstable Carbamic Acid** $$ R-NCO + H_2O \longrightarrow [R-NH-COOH] \quad \text{(unstable)} $$ **Reaction 2 — Decomposition of Carbamic Acid → Amine + CO₂** $$ [R-NH-COOH] \longrightarrow R-NH_2 + CO_2 \uparrow $$ **Reaction 3 — Amine + Isocyanate → Urea Linkage** $$ R-NH_2 + R'-NCO \longrightarrow R-NH-CO-NH-R' $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The **CO₂ liberated in Reaction 2 is the actual blowing gas** that creates the cellular foam structure. The **urea linkages** formed in Reaction 3 create additional hard-segment domains, increasing rigidity. This is why water-blown foams are harder than pure CO₂-blown foams—the water reaction itself contributes to polymer morphology. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Unit | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Isocyanate Index (NCO Index) | $\text{Index} = \dfrac{n_{NCO}}{n_{OH}} \times 100$ | dimensionless (%) | Stoichiometric control | | 2 | Equivalent Weight of Polyol | $E_{polyol} = \dfrac{M_w}{f_{OH}}$ | g/eq | Resin formulation | | 3 | Equivalent Weight of Isocyanate | $E_{NCO} = \dfrac{42.02 \times M_w}{\%\text{NCO} \times 100}$ | g/eq | Resin formulation | | 4 | Mass of Isocyanate required | $m_{NCO} = \dfrac{E_{NCO}}{E_{polyol}} \times m_{polyol} \times \text{Index}$ | g | Batch calculation | | 5 | % NCO of Isocyanate | $\%\text{NCO} = \dfrac{42.02 \times f_{NCO} \times 100}{M_w}$ | % | Purity check | | 6 | Degree of Polymerization (Carothers) | $X_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$ | dimensionless | Molecular weight | | 7 | Average Functionality | $\bar{f} = \dfrac{\sum N_i f_i}{\sum N_i}$ | dimensionless | Gel point prediction | | 8 | Gel Point (Flory-Stockmayer) | $p_c = \dfrac{2}{\bar{f}}$ | dimensionless | Network formation | | 9 | Foam Density | $\rho = \dfrac{m_{polymer}}{V_{foam}}$ | kg/m³ | Quality control | | 10 | Hard Segment Content | $HSC\% = \dfrac{m_{NCO}+m_{chain\ extender}}{m_{total}} \times 100$ | % | Property prediction | > [!NOTE] > The constant **42.02 g/mol** is the molar mass of the –NCO group (N = 14, C = 12, O = 16; total = 14 + 12 + 16 = 42). ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application Sector | PU Form Used | Function | |---|---|---| | Mattresses & Furniture | Flexible slabstock foam | Cushioning, comfort | | Building Insulation | Rigid closed-cell foam | Thermal insulation (R-value ~6/inch) | | Automotive | Integral skin foam, RIM | Dashboards, bumpers, steering wheels | | Footwear (Sports shoes) | Microcellular elastomer | Midsoles (EVA alternative) | | Industrial Coatings | Two-pack PU coating | Anti-corrosion (bridges, marine) | | Adhesives & Sealants | Moisture-cure PU | Construction, footwear | | Medical Devices | Biostable PU (e.g., Pellethane) | Catheters, artificial heart components | | Spandex Fibers (Lycra) | PU-urea segmented copolymer | Elastic textile fibers | --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Equivalent Weight of an Isocyanate The **equivalent weight** of an isocyanate is the mass in grams that contains exactly **one mole of –NCO reactive groups**. **Starting Point:** The molecular weight of the isocyanate is $M_w$ g/mol, and the molecule contains $f_{NCO}$ reactive –NCO groups per molecule. The mass fraction of –NCO groups is given by the formula: $$ \%\text{NCO} = \frac{(42.02) \times f_{NCO}}{M_w} \times 100 $$ **Rearranging for $M_w$:** $$ M_w = \frac{42.02 \times f_{NCO} \times 100}{\%\text{NCO}} $$ **Now, the Equivalent Weight** is defined as the molecular weight divided by the number of reactive groups per molecule: $$ E_{NCO} = \frac{M_w}{f_{NCO}} $$ **Substituting $M_w$ from above:** $$ E_{NCO} = \frac{\left(\dfrac{42.02 \times f_{NCO} \times 100}{\%\text{NCO}}\right)}{f_{NCO}} $$ **The $f_{NCO}$ cancels:** $$ E_{NCO} = \frac{42.02 \times 100}{\%\text{NCO}} $$ $$ \boxed{\;E_{NCO} = \frac{4202}{\%\text{NCO}}\;} $$ **Numerical Verification for TDI (Toluene Diisocyanate, 80/20 isomer mix, $M_w = 174.2$ g/mol, $f_{NCO}=2$, %NCO ≈ 48.2%):** $$ E_{NCO} = \frac{4202}{48.2} = 87.18 \text{ g/eq} $$ **Direct check:** $E_{NCO} = M_w / f_{NCO} = 174.2 / 2 = 87.1$ g/eq ✓ (matches) ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Mass of Isocyanate Required for a Given Polyol Mass To react completely with $m_{polyol}$ grams of polyol, we need an **equimolar amount** of –NCO groups. Adjusting for the desired **Isocyanate Index (Index = 1.0 means stoichiometric)**: **Step 1 — Moles of –OH groups available:** $$ n_{OH} = \frac{m_{polyol}}{E_{polyol}} $$ **Step 2 — Moles of –NCO required (stoichiometric basis, Index = 1.0):** $$ n_{NCO,\text{stoich}} = n_{OH} = \frac{m_{polyol}}{E_{polyol}} $$ **Step 3 — Mass of isocyanate containing $n_{NCO}$ moles of –NCO groups:** $$ m_{NCO,\text{stoich}} = n_{NCO,\text{stoich}} \times E_{NCO} = \frac{m_{polyol}}{E_{polyol}} \times E_{NCO} $$ **Step 4 — Adjust for Isocyanate Index $I$ (often expressed as ratio, e.g., 1.05 = 105%):** $$ m_{NCO,\text{actual}} = m_{NCO,\text{stoich}} \times I $$ **Final Working Equation:** $$ \boxed{\;m_{NCO} = \frac{m_{polyol}}{E_{polyol}} \times E_{NCO} \times I = \frac{m_{polyol} \times E_{NCO} \times I}{E_{polyol}}\;} $$ ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Frequently Asked in KTU) > **Problem:** A flexible slabstock PU foam is to be formulated with **100 g of a polyether triol** ($E_{polyol} = 1000$ g/eq, functionality = 3), **4.5 g of water** as blowing agent, **0.5 g of DABCO catalyst**, and **1.0 g of silicone surfactant**. The isocyanate is **TDI (80/20)** with %NCO = 48.2 and an NCO Index of **1.05**. Calculate the **mass of TDI required**. **Step 1 — Calculate equivalent weight of TDI:** $$ E_{NCO} = \frac{4202}{48.2} = 87.18 \text{ g/eq} $$ **Step 2 — Account for water's –OH groups (water is difunctional in H-equivalents):** Water's molecular weight is 18 g/mol, and each molecule contributes 2 active H's (functionality = 2): $$ E_{water} = \frac{18}{2} = 9 \text{ g/eq} $$ $$ n_{OH,\text{from water}} = \frac{4.5}{9} = 0.5 \text{ eq} $$ **Step 3 — Moles of –OH from polyol:** $$ n_{OH,\text{from polyol}} = \frac{100}{1000} = 0.1 \text{ eq} $$ **Step 4 — Total –OH equivalents:** $$ n_{OH,\text{total}} = 0.1 + 0.5 = 0.6 \text{ eq} $$ **Step 5 — Mass of TDI for stoichiometric reaction (Index = 1.0):** $$ m_{TDI,\text{stoich}} = 0.6 \times 87.18 = 52.31 \text{ g} $$ **Step 6 — Apply isocyanate index of 1.05:** $$ m_{TDI,\text{actual}} = 52.31 \times 1.05 = 54.93 \text{ g} $$ $$ \boxed{\;m_{TDI} \approx 54.93 \text{ g} \quad \text{(per 100 g polyol batch)}\;} $$ ## 3.4 Derivation 3: Carothers Equation for Step-Growth PU Polymerization In step-growth polymerization, the **degree of polymerization** $X_n$ (number-average) relates to the **extent of reaction** $p$ (fraction of functional groups reacted) by: $$ X_n = \frac{1}{1-p} $$ **Derivation:** Let $N_0$ be the initial number of monomer molecules and $N$ be the number of molecules remaining at time $t$. Each bond formation reduces molecule count by 1. The number of bonds formed is $(N_0 - N)$. **Defining extent of reaction:** $$ p = \frac{\text{Number of functional groups reacted}}{\text{Initial number of functional groups}} = \frac{2(N_0 - N)}{2N_0} = 1 - \frac{N}{N_0} $$ **Rearranging:** $$ \frac{N}{N_0} = 1 - p $$ **Degree of polymerization** is the number of monomer units per polymer molecule: $$ X_n = \frac{N_0}{N} = \frac{1}{1-p} $$ For high molecular weight (e.g., $M_n \sim 50{,}000$ g/mol with $M_0 \sim 250$ g/mol, giving $X_n = 200$): $$ p = 1 - \frac{1}{200} = 0.995 \quad \text{(99.5\% conversion required)} $$ > [!WARNING] > **This is the critical reason PU polymerization is run at Index = 1.000 ± 0.005** — any deviation from exact stoichiometry dramatically drops the molecular weight. A 1% stoichiometric imbalance in a step-growth polymerization reduces $M_n$ by ~50%. ## 3.5 Derivation 4: Gel Point for Branched PU Networks The **Flory-Stockmayer gel point** predicts the critical conversion $p_c$ at which an infinite network (gel) first forms: $$ \boxed{\;p_c = \frac{2}{\bar{f}}\;} $$ Where $\bar{f}$ is the **average functionality** of the reaction mixture: $$ \bar{f} = \frac{\sum N_i f_i^2 (f_i - 1)}{\sum N_i f_i^2} $$ For a simple system of equal moles of trifunctional polyol ($f=3$) and difunctional isocyanate ($f=2$), the effective functionality averaged over all functional groups is: $$ \bar{f} = \frac{(1 \times 3) + (1 \times 2)}{1 + 1} = 2.5 $$ Then: $$ p_c = \frac{2}{2.5} = 0.80 $$ This means **80% conversion** of functional groups is required to form a gel. Below $p_c$, the system remains soluble (sol); above $p_c$, an infinite network (gel) appears. --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Overall Polyurethane Chemistry Architecture (Mermaid) ```mermaid graph TD A1[Diisocyanate
TDI or MDI]:::iso A2[Polyol
Polyether or Polyester]:::pol A3[Chain Extender
1,4-BDO or EG]:::ext A4[Catalyst
DABCO or DBTDL]:::cat A5[Blowing Agent
Water or Pentane]:::blow A6[Surfactant
Silicone]:::surf A1 --> R1{Step Growth
Addition Reaction} A2 --> R1 R1 --> P1[Urethane Linkage
-NH-CO-O-] A1 --> R2{Isocyanate + Water} A5 --> R2 R2 --> P2[Amine + CO2 Gas] P2 --> P3[Urea Linkage
-NH-CO-NH-] A3 --> R4{Chain Extension} A4 --> R4 P1 --> R4 R4 --> P4[High Molecular Weight
PU Polymer] P3 --> R4 A6 --> R5{Foam Stabilization} R5 --> P5[Cellular Foam Structure] classDef iso fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#c0392b,color:#000 classDef pol fill:#d6e9ff,stroke:#2980b9,color:#000 classDef ext fill:#fff4d6,stroke:#d4a017,color:#000 classDef cat fill:#e8daff,stroke:#7d3c98,color:#000 classDef blow fill:#d6ffe2,stroke:#239b56,color:#000 classDef surf fill:#ffd6f0,stroke:#ad1457,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Polyurethane Block Copolymer Microstructure ```mermaid graph LR HS1[Hard Segment
Aromatic Isocyanate +
Chain Extender]:::hard SS1[Soft Segment
Polyether or
Polyester Polyol]:::soft HS2[Hard Segment
Aromatic Isocyanate +
Chain Extender]:::hard SS2[Soft Segment
Polyether or
Polyester Polyol]:::soft HS3[Hard Segment
Aromatic Isocyanate +
Chain Extender]:::hard HS1 --- SS1 SS1 --- HS2 HS2 --- SS2 SS2 --- HS3 classDef hard fill:#ffb3b3,stroke:#990000,color:#000 classDef soft fill:#b3d9ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Process Flow: Prepolymer Manufacturing Route ```mermaid graph TD S1[Step 1: Polyol + Excess Isocyanate
in Reactor at 60-80 C]:::step S2[Step 2: NCO Terminated Prepolymer
formed with %NCO 5-25%]:::step S3[Step 3: Prepolymer cooled and stored
under nitrogen blanket]:::step S4[Step 4: Chain Extender and additives
blended in second stage]:::step S5[Step 5: Final PU polymer cast,
molded, or sprayed]:::step S1 --> S2 S2 --> S3 S3 --> S4 S4 --> S5 classDef step fill:#d4f1f4,stroke:#05445e,color:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Application-Morphology Mapping ```mermaid graph TD PU[Polyurethane Family]:::root FF[Flexible Foam
Low Index, low crosslink,
high polyol mol wt]:::app RF[Rigid Foam
High Index, high crosslink,
high functionality polyol]:::app EL[Elastomers
Linear segmented
block copolymers]:::app CO[Coatings
Low mol wt prepolymers,
solvent or waterborne]:::app AD[Adhesives
Moisture cure
NCO terminated]:::app SP[Spandex Fibers
PU-urea segmented
dry/wet spinning]:::app PU --> FF PU --> RF PU --> EL PU --> CO PU --> AD PU --> SP FF --> FF1[Mattresses] FF --> FF2[Car seats] RF --> RF1[Building insulation] RF --> RF2[Refrigeration] EL --> EL1[Wheel treads] EL --> EL2[Seals and gaskets] classDef root fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#7f6000,color:#000 classDef app fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,color:#000 ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]** **Q: Define polyurethanes. Write the general structure of the repeating urethane linkage and name any two commercially important diisocyanates used in their manufacture.** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Polyurethanes (PU) are a class of step-growth addition polymers containing recurring **carbamate (urethane) linkages** (–NH–CO–O–) in the polymer backbone, formed by the reaction of diisocyanates with polyols in the presence of suitable catalysts and additives. **Repeating linkage structure:** $$ \text{–[NH–CO–O–]–} $$ **Two commercially important diisocyanates:** 1. **TDI (Toluene-2,4-Diisocyanate)** — predominantly used for flexible foams 2. **MDI (Methylene Diphenyl Diisocyanate)** — predominantly used for rigid foams and elastomers --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sessional Exam]** **Q: Explain the role of (i) catalysts, and (ii) blowing agents in polyurethane foam manufacture. Give one example each.** **Model Answer:** **(i) Catalysts:** Catalysts accelerate the rate of the isocyanate–polyol (gelling) reaction and the isocyanate–water (blowing) reaction, allowing precise control of the rise time and cure profile of the foam. - **Example:** Tertiary amines (e.g., **DABCO — 1,4-diazabicyclo[2.2.2]octane**) for the blowing reaction; **Dibutyltin dilaurate (DBTDL)** for the gelling reaction. **(ii) Blowing Agents:** Blowing agents generate the gas phase (cells) responsible for the cellular foam structure. - **Example:** **Water** (reacts with isocyanate to liberate CO₂); auxiliary physical blowing agents include **pentane** and **cyclopentane** (used in rigid insulation foams). --- ## PART B — Full-Length Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]** **Q: (a)** Describe the raw materials used in the manufacture of polyurethanes. Discuss in detail the various types of polyols and diisocyanates used, with examples. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With the help of a neat flow diagram, explain the **one-shot process** and the **prepolymer process** for polyurethane manufacture. Compare their advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)** #### Model Answer **(a) Raw Materials for Polyurethane Manufacture (7 marks):** **1. Diisocyanates (Isocyanate Component):** | Diisocyanate | Abbreviation | Structure | Application | |---|---|---|---| | Toluene-2,4-Diisocyanate | TDI (80/20 = 2,4-/2,6- mix) | Aromatic | Flexible foams | | Methylene Diphenyl Diisocyanate | MDI (polymeric or pure) | Aromatic | Rigid foams, elastomers | | Hexamethylene Diisocyanate | HDI | Aliphatic | Light-stable coatings | | Isophorone Diisocyanate | IPDI | Cycloaliphatic | Premium coatings | | Naphthalene Diisocyanate | NDI | Aromatic | High-performance elastomers (Vulkollan) | > **TDI** is a low-viscosity liquid used in flexible foams; **MDI** has lower vapor pressure (safer) and is preferred for rigid foams; **aliphatic isocyanates (HDI, IPDI)** give non-yellowing coatings. **2. Polyols (Hydroxyl Component):** - **Polyether Polyols:** Manufactured by **propoxylation/ethoxylation** of starters like glycerol, sorbitol, sucrose, or amines using propylene oxide (PO) and ethylene oxide (EO). Examples: **Poly(propylene oxide) triol, Glycerol-started triol**. Lower cost, hydrolytic stability, used in flexible foams. - **Polyester Polyols:** Manufactured by **condensation** of diacids (adipic, phthalic) with diols (ethylene glycol, butanediol). Examples: **Poly(ethylene adipate) diol, Poly(diethylene glycol adipate)**. Higher mechanical strength, oil/chemical resistance, used in elastomers and coatings. - **Specialty Polyols:** **Polycarbonate diols** (light stability, hydrolysis resistance), **Polycaprolactone diols** (biostable for medical use), **Acrylic polyols** (for 2K coatings). **3. Chain Extenders and Crosslinkers:** Low MW diols/diamines — 1,4-butanediol, ethylene glycol, MOCA (4,4'-methylenebis(2-chloroaniline)), hydroquinone bis(2-hydroxyethyl) ether (HQEE). **4. Catalysts:** Tertiary amines (DABCO, N-methylmorpholine), organotin compounds (dibutyltin dilaurate — DBTDL), bismuth carboxylates. **5. Blowing Agents:** Chemical — water; Physical — pentane, cyclopentane, HFCs, HFOs, liquid CO₂. **6. Surfactants:** Silicone polyether copolymers (e.g., Tegostab by Evonik) for cell stabilization. **7. Other Additives:** Flame retardants (TCPP, DMMP), fillers (calcium carbonate, glass fibers), pigments, UV stabilizers. --- **(b) Manufacturing Processes (7 marks):** **One-Shot Process:** In the **one-shot process**, all ingredients — polyol, isocyanate, water, catalyst, surfactant, and additives — are mixed simultaneously in a single step and poured into a mold or onto a conveyor for slabstock foam production. **Advantages:** - Simpler process; lower capital cost - Short cycle time (good for high-volume production) - Suitable for flexible slabstock foam (continuous production) **Limitations:** - High exotherm; difficult to control in thick sections - Less control over polymer morphology - Higher scrap rate in molded foams **Prepolymer (Two-Step) Process:** In the **prepolymer process**, the polyol is first reacted with an **excess of diisocyanate** to form an **NCO-terminated prepolymer** (typically 5–25% NCO content). This prepolymer is then reacted with a **chain extender** (water or diol) in a second step. **Step 1:** Polyol + Excess Diisocyanate → NCO-terminated prepolymer **Step 2:** Prepolymer + Chain Extender → High MW Polyurethane **Advantages:** - Better control of hard/soft segment ratio - Lower exotherm in second step - Suitable for cast elastomers, RIM, and high-performance coatings - Less volatilization (safer with MDI) **Limitations:** - Higher capital cost (two reactors needed) - Longer cycle time - Prepolymer moisture sensitivity (storage under N₂ required) | Comparison | One-Shot | Prepolymer | |---|---|---| | Stages | 1 | 2 | | Capital Cost | Low | High | | Exotherm Control | Poor | Excellent | | Typical Use | Flexible foams | Elastomers, RIM, coatings | | Final MW Control | Limited | Precise | --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]** **Q: (a)** Explain with suitable reactions: (i) the formation of the urethane linkage, and (ii) the role of water as a blowing agent in flexible PU foam. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An isocyanate has %NCO = 31.5 and molecular weight 267 g/mol. Calculate: (i) its functionality, (ii) its equivalent weight, and (iii) the mass of this isocyanate required to completely react with **250 g of a polyol** ($E_{polyol} = 1250$ g/eq) at an **isocyanate index of 1.02**. **(7 marks)** #### Model Answer **(a) (i) Urethane Linkage Formation (4 marks):** The reaction of an isocyanate with a hydroxyl group (alcohol) produces a **carbamate (urethane) linkage**. It is a **nucleophilic addition** where the oxygen of the –OH group attacks the electrophilic carbon of the –N=C=O group. $$ R-N=C=O + R'-O-H \longrightarrow R-NH-CO-O-R' $$ (Diisocyanate) + (Polyol) → Polyurethane For example, with TDI and a polyether triol: $$ \text{OCN-C}_6\text{H}_3\text{(CH}_3\text{)-NCO} + \text{HO-[Polyol]-OH} \longrightarrow \text{...-NH-CO-O-[Polyol]-O-CO-NH-...} $$ **(ii) Water as Blowing Agent (3 marks):** When water is included in the foam formulation, it reacts with the isocyanate to liberate **CO₂ gas**, which acts as the blowing agent to create the cellular structure: $$ R-NCO + H_2O \longrightarrow [R-NH-COOH] \longrightarrow R-NH_2 + CO_2 \uparrow $$ The amine produced further reacts with another isocyanate to form a **urea linkage**: $$ R-NH_2 + R'-NCO \longrightarrow R-NH-CO-NH-R' $$ The CO₂ inflates the polymerizing mass, while the urea groups create additional hard-segment domains, giving the foam its characteristic cellular morphology and load-bearing capacity. --- **(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):** **Given:** - %NCO of isocyanate = 31.5 - $M_w$ = 267 g/mol - $m_{polyol}$ = 250 g - $E_{polyol}$ = 1250 g/eq - Isocyanate Index $I$ = 1.02 **Step 1: Find functionality $f_{NCO}$:** Using the formula: $$ \%\text{NCO} = \frac{42.02 \times f_{NCO} \times 100}{M_w} $$ Solving for $f_{NCO}$: $$ f_{NCO} = \frac{\%\text{NCO} \times M_w}{42.02 \times 100} = \frac{31.5 \times 267}{4202} = \frac{8410.5}{4202} = 2.001 \approx 2 $$ $$ \boxed{\;f_{NCO} = 2 \text{ (diisocyanate)}\;} $$ **Step 2: Calculate Equivalent Weight $E_{NCO}$:** $$ E_{NCO} = \frac{4202}{\%\text{NCO}} = \frac{4202}{31.5} = 133.4 \text{ g/eq} $$ $$ \boxed{\;E_{NCO} = 133.4 \text{ g/eq}\;} $$ **Step 3: Mass of isocyanate required:** Using the master formulation equation: $$ m_{NCO} = \frac{m_{polyol} \times E_{NCO} \times I}{E_{polyol}} $$ Substituting values: $$ m_{NCO} = \frac{250 \times 133.4 \times 1.02}{1250} $$ $$ m_{NCO} = \frac{250 \times 136.068}{1250} $$ $$ m_{NCO} = \frac{34017}{1250} = 27.21 \text{ g} $$ $$ \boxed{\;m_{NCO} = 27.21 \text{ g}\;} $$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Memorize the constant 42.02** for the molar mass of the –NCO group — losing this 1 mark is the most common mistake. > 2. **Unit consistency:** Always express equivalent weight in g/eq and mass in grams; mixing SI units (kg) without conversion costs 0.5–1 mark. > 3. **Index interpretation:** Some texts use Index as a percentage (105%); some as a ratio (1.05). KTU typically uses the **ratio (1.05)** form. Read the question carefully. > 4. **Functionality check:** If your calculated $f_{NCO}$ comes out non-integer (e.g., 2.3), check your arithmetic — functionality must be a whole number for a pure compound. > 5. **Reaction stoichiometry:** Forgetting to account for **water's –OH equivalents** in foam formulations is a classic trap; KTU frequently tests this. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **Polyurethane** = Polymer with **–NH–CO–O–** (carbamate) linkages - **Discovered by Otto Bayer at IG Farben in 1937** (very high-yield fact) - **TDI** = flexible foams; **MDI** = rigid foams; **HDI/IPDI** = light-stable coatings - **Polyether polyols** (low cost, flexible foams) vs **Polyester polyols** (high strength, elastomers) - **NCO Index = 1.00** is critical for high MW (Carothers equation: $X_n = 1/(1-p)$) - **Water + isocyanate → CO₂ (blows the foam) + urea linkage** - **DABCO** = amine catalyst (blowing); **DBTDL** = tin catalyst (gelling) - **Master formula:** $m_{NCO} = (m_{polyol} \times E_{NCO} \times I) / E_{polyol}$ - **Key constant:** %NCO formula uses **42.02 g/mol** (mass of –NCO) - **Hard segment** = aromatic isocyanate + chain extender; **Soft segment** = long polyol chain - **One-shot process** = all ingredients mixed at once (cheap, flexible foams) - **Prepolymer process** = polyol + excess NCO first, then chain extend (better control, elastomers) - **Gel point** $p_c = 2/\bar{f}$ — Flory-Stockmayer criterion for network formation - **Major applications:** mattresses, insulation, footwear, automotive RIM, spandex, coatings, adhesives, biomedical devices - **Difference to remember:** Polyurethane ≠ Polyurea (urea is –NH–CO–NH–, from diamine, not diol) - **TDI vapor pressure** is high (toxic, needs handling care); MDI is safer (low vapor pressure) - **Aliphatic isocyanates** (HDI, IPDI) are used where **UV stability** is required (no yellowing)

topics.title: Methane and Synthesis Gas Derivatives - Production of Synthesis Gas
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT741 - PETROCHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Methane and Synthesis Gas Derivatives - Production of Synthesis Gas

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Synthesis Gas (Syngas)** is a fuel-gas mixture consisting primarily of **hydrogen (H₂)** and **carbon monoxide (CO)** in varying molar ratios, often accompanied by minor quantities of carbon dioxide (CO₂), water vapor (H₂O), methane (CH₄), nitrogen (N₂), and trace sulfur compounds. The composition—particularly the crucial **H₂:CO molar ratio**—is tailored to the downstream petrochemical process it feeds (e.g., methanol synthesis, Fischer–Tropsch synthesis, ammonia production, or oxo-alcohol manufacture).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECHT741 – Module 1):**
> Synthesis gas is the *single most important platform intermediate* in modern petrochemical engineering. It is the building block from which hydrogen, ammonia, methanol, oxo-chemicals, and synthetic hydrocarbon fuels are manufactured. Mastering its production routes is fundamental to the entire petrochemical value chain.

Mathematically, syngas composition is reported on a **dry basis** (mole percent, excluding water) and is defined by three cardinal parameters:

$$
x_{H_2} + x_{CO} + x_{CO_2} + x_{CH_4} + x_{N_2} = 1.00
$$

where the **module** $M$ and the **H₂/CO ratio** $R$ are:

$$
R = \frac{x_{H_2}}{x_{CO}} \quad ; \quad M = \frac{x_{H_2} + x_{CO}}{x_{H_2} + x_{CO} + x_{CO_2}}
$$

The lower heating value (LHV) of a typical syngas lies in the range **4.5 – 12 MJ/Nm³**, depending on the H₂/CO ratio and inert content.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Molecular Restaurant Kitchen"

Imagine synthesis gas as the **"universal dough"** of the petrochemical world. Just as a baker can shape a basic dough into bread, pizza base, croissant, or naan by changing temperature, leavening, and resting conditions, a chemical engineer can take the same H₂ + CO mixture and—simply by tweaking the *ratio* and the *downstream catalyst*—produce an enormous variety of products:

| Syngas Ratio (H₂:CO) | Analogy | Downstream Product |
|---|---|---|
| **3 : 1** | Sweet dough (extra sugar) | **Ammonia (NH₃)** synthesis |
| **2 : 1** | Standard dough | **Methanol (CH₃OH)** synthesis |
| **1 : 1** | Savoury dough | **Fischer–Tropsch** diesel/wax |
| **< 1 : 1** | Low-gluten dough | **Oxo-alcohols**, **acetic acid** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Reality Check:** The ratio is *not* a free choice—it is dictated by the upstream reformer chemistry and the stoichiometric requirement of the downstream process. The chemical engineer's art lies in *matching* the two.

---

### 1.3 Why Synthesis Gas? — The Strategic Importance

- **Hydrogen Economy Hub:** Syngas is the most economical large-scale route to merchant hydrogen.
- **Methanol Backbone:** ~98% of the world's methanol is derived from syngas.
- **Fischer–Tropsch Liquid Fuels:** Gas-to-Liquids (GTL) and Coal-to-Liquids (CTL) technologies rely entirely on syngas.
- **Ammonia & Fertilizers:** Syngas provides both the H₂ (for NH₃) and indirectly the CO₂ (for urea).
- **Carbon Capture Ready:** CO from syngas is the most concentrated, lowest-cost source of CO₂ for CCUS (Carbon Capture, Utilization & Storage).
- **Circular Carbon Feedstock:** Any carbonaceous feedstock—natural gas, naphtha, coal, petroleum coke, biomass, municipal solid waste, or even CO₂—can be converted to syngas.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Memory Hook:** *"Syngas is the Swiss-Army Knife of the petrochemical industry—one feedstock, a hundred products."*

---

### 1.4 Feedstock Spectrum for Syngas Production

The selection of feedstock is governed by **regional availability, hydrogen-to-carbon (H/C) atomic ratio, and process economics**.

| Feedstock | Atomic H/C Ratio | Predominant Process | Typical Syngas (H₂:CO) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Natural Gas (Methane)** | **4.0** | Steam Reforming (SRM) | 3 : 1 to 5 : 1 |
| **Naphtha (C₅–C₁₁)** | 1.8 – 2.2 | Steam Reforming | 2.5 : 1 to 3 : 1 |
| **Heavy Oil / Residue** | 1.3 – 1.7 | Partial Oxidation (POX) | 1.6 : 1 to 1.9 : 1 |
| **Coal (Anthracite–Lignite)** | 0.5 – 0.8 | Coal Gasification | 0.4 : 1 to 1.0 : 1 |
| **Petroleum Coke** | ~ 0.4 | Gasification | 0.3 : 1 to 0.6 : 1 |
| **Biomass (Wood, Bagasse)** | 1.4 – 1.8 | Gasification | 0.8 : 1 to 1.5 : 1 |
| **Municipal Solid Waste** | 1.0 – 2.0 | Gasification / Plasma | 0.8 : 1 to 1.5 : 1 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb:** Higher H/C atomic ratio in the feedstock → higher H₂/CO ratio in the syngas produced. Methane (H/C = 4) is the *hydrogen-richest* fossil feedstock.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Industrial Pillars of Syngas Production

Modern industrial syngas production is dominated by **three thermochemical routes**, each with distinct thermodynamic and kinetic signatures:

---

#### 2.1.1 Steam Methane Reforming (SMR / SRM)

**Steam Reforming of Methane (SRM)** is the *workhorse* of the petrochemical industry—accounting for **~70–75% of global hydrogen and ~50% of global syngas production**.

**Primary Reforming Reactions (in catalyst-filled reformer tubes, T = 700–950 °C, P = 15–40 bar):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(R-1)} \quad & CH_4 + H_2O \;\rightleftharpoons\; CO + 3H_2 \quad & \Delta H_{298}^\circ = +206.2 \;\text{kJ/mol} \\
\text{(R-2)} \quad & CH_4 + 2H_2O \;\rightleftharpoons\; CO_2 + 4H_2 \quad & \Delta H_{298}^\circ = +165.0 \;\text{kJ/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Simultaneous Water–Gas Shift (WGS) Reaction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(R-3)} \quad & CO + H_2O \;\rightleftharpoons\; CO_2 + H_2 \quad & \Delta H_{298}^\circ = -41.2 \;\text{kJ/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Net Stoichiometric Insight:**
> R-1 and R-3 are linearly dependent: R-2 = R-1 + R-3. Therefore the system has only **two independent reactions** and **three atom balances** (C, H, O)—reducing the degrees of freedom and enabling rigorous simulation in tools like ASPEN Plus or HYSYS.

**Theoretical H₂:CO Ratio from R-1 alone = 3 : 1.**
With simultaneous WGS, the practical ratio rises to **3.5 – 5.0 : 1**, ideal for ammonia and hydrogen plants.

**Side Reactions (must be suppressed):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(R-4)} \quad & CH_4 \;\rightleftharpoons\; C + 2H_2 \quad & \Delta H^\circ = +75 \;\text{kJ/mol (Boudouard coking)} \\
\text{(R-5)} \quad & 2CO \;\rightleftharpoons\; C + CO_2 \quad & \Delta H^\circ = -172 \;\text{kJ/mol} \\
\text{(R-6)} \quad & CO + H_2 \;\rightleftharpoons\; C + H_2O \quad & \Delta H^\circ = -131 \;\text{kJ/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Coking Catastrophe:** Carbon deposition on the nickel catalyst deactivates it permanently. The **Steam-to-Carbon (S/C) ratio** is held between **2.5 and 4.0** to keep the system above the thermodynamic coking boundary.

---

#### 2.1.2 Partial Oxidation (POX / Non-Catalytic POX)

Used for **heavy feedstocks** (resid, vacuum residue, asphalt, petroleum coke). Exothermic—does not require external firing.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(R-7)} \quad & CH_4 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \;\rightarrow\; CO + 2H_2 \quad & \Delta H_{298}^\circ = -35.6 \;\text{kJ/mol} \\
\text{(R-8)} \quad & CH_4 + O_2 \;\rightarrow\; CO_2 + 2H_2 \quad & \Delta H_{298}^\circ = -319 \;\text{kJ/mol} \\
\text{(R-9)} \quad & CH_4 + 2O_2 \;\rightarrow\; CO_2 + 2H_2O \quad & \Delta H_{298}^\circ = -802 \;\text{kJ/mol (complete combustion)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Operating conditions: **T = 1200–1500 °C, P = 30–80 bar.** No catalyst. Theoretical H₂:CO ratio ≈ **2 : 1.**

---

#### 2.1.3 Autothermal Reforming (ATR) — The Hybrid Champion

ATR is a **clever combination** of SRM and POX in a single refractory-lined vessel containing a burner on top and a catalyst bed below:

$$
\text{Feed} = \text{Hydrocarbon} + \text{Steam} + \text{O}_2
$$

- Top zone: Sub-stoichiometric combustion (POX) supplies endothermic heat.
- Bottom zone: Catalyst-facilitated steam reforming completes the conversion.

**Net H₂:CO ratio** is tunable between **1.5 : 1 and 3 : 1** by adjusting the O₂/C and H₂O/C ratios. ATR is the **preferred technology for modern GTL and methanol mega-plants** (e.g., 5000 MTPD methanol).

---

#### 2.1.4 Coal & Biomass Gasification

For carbon-rich feedstocks with low H/C ratios:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(R-10)} \quad & C + H_2O \;\rightleftharpoons\; CO + H_2 \quad & \Delta H^\circ = +131 \;\text{kJ/mol} \\
\text{(R-11)} \quad & C + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \;\rightarrow\; CO \quad & \Delta H^\circ = -111 \;\text{kJ/mol} \\
\text{(R-12)} \quad & C + CO_2 \;\rightleftharpoons\; 2CO \quad & \Delta H^\circ = +172 \;\text{kJ/mol (Boudouard)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Gasifiers operate at **800–1400 °C** (entrained-flow, fluidized-bed, fixed-bed, or moving-bed designs). The syngas has **low H₂/CO ratio (0.4–1.0)**, requiring downstream shift conversion to boost H₂.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Symbolic Form | Typical Industrial Value | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Steam Reforming of CH₄ (R-1) | $CH_4 + H_2O \rightleftharpoons CO + 3H_2$ | $\Delta H = +206.2$ kJ/mol | Endothermic — needs external heat |
| 2 | Water–Gas Shift (R-3) | $CO + H_2O \rightleftharpoons CO_2 + H_2$ | $\Delta H = -41.2$ kJ/mol | Mildly exothermic — used for H₂ boost |
| 3 | Partial Oxidation (R-7) | $CH_4 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow CO + 2H_2$ | $\Delta H = -35.6$ kJ/mol | Exothermic — heat source in ATR |
| 4 | Steam-to-Carbon (S/C) Ratio | $S/C = \dfrac{n_{H_2O}}{n_{C, \text{feed}}}$ | **2.5 – 4.0** | Anti-coking safeguard in SMR |
| 5 | Oxygen-to-Carbon (O₂/C) Ratio | $O_2/C = \dfrac{n_{O_2}}{n_{C, \text{feed}}}$ | **0.3 – 0.6 (ATR); 0.7 – 1.0 (POX)** | Controls H₂:CO ratio |
| 6 | H₂ : CO Molar Ratio | $R = \dfrac{x_{H_2}}{x_{CO}}$ | **0.5 – 5.0** | Dictates downstream process |
| 7 | Methane Conversion | $X_{CH_4} = \dfrac{n_{CH_4,\text{in}} - n_{CH_4,\text{out}}}{n_{CH_4,\text{in}}}$ | **> 0.95 in SMR** | Measures reformer performance |
| 8 | Reformer Exit Temperature | $T_{\text{out}}$ | **700 – 950 °C** | Maximizes CH₄ conversion |
| 9 | Reformer Pressure | $P$ | **15 – 40 bar** | Lower P favours equilibrium |
| 10 | Equilibrium Constant (Kₚ for R-1) | $\ln K_p = -\dfrac{\Delta G^\circ}{RT}$ | $\log_{10} K_p \approx 13.0 - \dfrac{11{,}000}{T(K)}$ | Predicts equilibrium composition |
| 11 | Thermal Efficiency (η) | $\eta = \dfrac{LHV_{\text{syngas}}}{LHV_{\text{feed fuel}} + Q_{\text{external}}}$ | **70 – 85% (SMR)** | Energy performance metric |
| 12 | Carbon Deposition Boundary | $\dfrac{P_{H_2O}}{P_{CO} \cdot P_{H_2}} > K_{eq, \text{R-6}}(T)$ | — | Anti-coking operating window |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always state the **sign of ΔH** explicitly. Endothermic reforming reactions are *thermodynamically favoured by high temperature and low pressure*—this is the single most-tested concept in the module.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Hydrogen Refineries:** SMR units supply ~95% of the H₂ used in hydrodesulfurization (HDS), hydrocracking, and hydrotreating.
2. **Ammonia Industry:** Syngas from SMR with H₂/N₂ = 3 : 1 is sent to the Haber–Bosch converter.
3. **Methanol Mega-Plants:** ATR + combined reforming produces the **2 : 1 H₂:CO** syngas for low-pressure methanol synthesis (LPMeOH).
4. **GTL / Fischer–Tropsch:** High-temperature Fischer–Tropsch (HTFT) in Sasol's Secunda plant uses coal-derived syngas with H₂:CO ≈ 0.7.
5. **Carbon-Neutral Fuels:** Biomass gasification + green H₂ blending is being commercialized for e-fuels.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Theoretical H₂:CO Ratio from Pure Steam Reforming of CH₄

**Problem:** A stoichiometric mixture of 1 mol CH₄ and 1 mol H₂O enters a reformer. Assuming Reaction R-1 goes to completion (ignoring WGS and side reactions), determine the exit H₂:CO ratio.

**Step 1 — Write the reaction with the extent of reaction ξ:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Species} \quad & CH_4 \;\; + \;\; H_2O \;\; \rightarrow \;\; CO \;\; + \;\; 3H_2 \\
\text{Initial (mol)} \quad & 1 \quad\;\; 1 \quad\;\; 0 \quad\;\; 0 \\
\text{Change} \quad & -\xi \quad\;\; -\xi \quad\;\; +\xi \quad\;\; +3\xi \\
\text{Equilibrium} \quad & 1-\xi \quad\;\; 1-\xi \quad\;\; \xi \quad\;\; 3\xi
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the atomic carbon balance.** Total carbon entering = 1. Carbon must be conserved:

$$
n_C^{in} = 1 = n_C^{out} = (1-\xi) + \xi = 1 \;\; \checkmark
$$

This is automatically satisfied. **Step 3 — Apply the atomic hydrogen balance.** Total H atoms entering:

$$
n_H^{in} = 4(1) + 2(1) = 6 \;\text{H atoms}
$$

Total H atoms in exit stream:

$$
n_H^{out} = 4(1-\xi) + 2(1-\xi) + 2(3\xi) = 4 - 4\xi + 2 - 2\xi + 6\xi = 6 \;\text{✓}
$$

**Step 4 — Determine the extent of reaction.** With *pure* steam reforming R-1 and stoichiometric feed, the maximum equilibrium conversion of CH₄ is ξ = 1 (full conversion to CO + 3H₂).

**Step 5 — Compute the exit H₂:CO ratio:**

$$
R = \frac{n_{H_2}^{out}}{n_{CO}^{out}} = \frac{3\xi}{\xi} = 3.00
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** *Stoichiometric, pure* steam reforming of CH₄ gives a theoretical H₂:CO ratio of **3 : 1**. In practice, the simultaneous WGS reaction (R-3) raises this to **3.5 – 5.0 : 1** because additional H₂ is generated while CO is consumed.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — H₂:CO Ratio from a Mixture of R-1 and R-3 (WGS Active)

**Problem:** A reformer operates with simultaneous R-1 and R-3. The feed is 1 mol CH₄ and *m* mol H₂O. At equilibrium, the conversions are: x for R-1 and y for R-3 (in terms of CO consumed by shift). Find the exit H₂:CO ratio.

**Step 1 — Set up the extent variables.** Let:
- $\xi_1$ = extent of R-1 (mol of CH₄ reacted)
- $\xi_3$ = extent of R-3 (mol of CO consumed)

**Step 2 — Write mole balances for each species:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{CH_4}^{out} & = 1 - \xi_1 \\
n_{H_2O}^{out} & = m - \xi_1 - \xi_3 \\
n_{CO}^{out} & = \xi_1 - \xi_3 \\
n_{H_2}^{out} & = 3\xi_1 + \xi_3 \\
n_{CO_2}^{out} & = \xi_3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Carbon balance check:**

$$
n_C^{in} = 1 = n_{CH_4}^{out} + n_{CO}^{out} + n_{CO_2}^{out} = (1-\xi_1) + (\xi_1 - \xi_3) + \xi_3 = 1 \;\; \checkmark
$$

**Step 4 — Hydrogen balance check:**

$$
n_H^{in} = 4(1) + 2(m) = 4 + 2m \\
n_H^{out} = 4(1-\xi_1) + 2(m - \xi_1 - \xi_3) + 2(3\xi_1 + \xi_3) \\
= 4 - 4\xi_1 + 2m - 2\xi_1 - 2\xi_3 + 6\xi_1 + 2\xi_3 \\
= 4 + 2m \;\; \checkmark
$$

**Step 5 — Express the H₂:CO ratio:**

$$
R = \frac{n_{H_2}^{out}}{n_{CO}^{out}} = \frac{3\xi_1 + \xi_3}{\xi_1 - \xi_3}
$$

**Step 6 — Examine limiting cases:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If } \xi_3 = 0 \;(\text{no WGS}): \quad & R = \frac{3\xi_1}{\xi_1} = 3 \\
\text{If } \xi_3 \to \xi_1 \;(\text{complete WGS of all CO}): \quad & R \to \infty
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Example numerical calculation.** Let $\xi_1 = 0.95$ and $\xi_3 = 0.60$:

$$
R = \frac{3(0.95) + 0.60}{0.95 - 0.60} = \frac{2.85 + 0.60}{0.35} = \frac{3.45}{0.35} = 9.86
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Such a high H₂:CO ratio (≈ 10) is *undesired* for syngas but *perfect* for a pure hydrogen plant—exactly why SMR units that produce merchant H₂ run the shift converter hard and then employ pressure-swing adsorption (PSA) for final H₂ purification.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Equilibrium CH₄ Conversion from Thermodynamics

**Problem:** Determine the equilibrium CH₄ conversion in a steam reformer operating at T = 1100 K, P = 1 bar, with S/C = 3.0. Use the empirical correlation:

$$
\log_{10} K_p^{(R-1)} = 13.0 - \frac{11{,}000}{T}
$$

**Step 1 — Calculate $K_p$ at 1100 K:**

$$
\log_{10} K_p = 13.0 - \frac{11{,}000}{1100} = 13.0 - 10.0 = 3.0
$$

$$
K_p = 10^{3.0} = 1000
$$

**Step 2 — Write $K_p$ in terms of partial pressures** (assuming ideal gas, treating the reaction $CH_4 + H_2O \rightleftharpoons CO + 3H_2$):

$$
K_p = \frac{P_{CO} \cdot P_{H_2}^3}{P_{CH_4} \cdot P_{H_2O}} \cdot P^{-2}
$$

(The $P^{-2}$ term arises because $\Delta n_{gas} = (1+3) - (1+1) = 2$.)

**Step 3 — Express partial pressures in terms of extent of reaction ξ.** Basis: 1 mol CH₄ + 3 mol H₂O = 4 mol total feed.

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{total}^{out} & = 4 + 2\xi \\
P_{CH_4} & = \left(\frac{1-\xi}{4+2\xi}\right) P \\
P_{H_2O} & = \left(\frac{3-\xi}{4+2\xi}\right) P \\
P_{CO} & = \left(\frac{\xi}{4+2\xi}\right) P \\
P_{H_2} & = \left(\frac{3\xi}{4+2\xi}\right) P
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the Kp expression** and set P = 1 bar:

$$
1000 = \frac{\left(\dfrac{\xi}{4+2\xi}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{3\xi}{4+2\xi}\right)^3}{\left(\dfrac{1-\xi}{4+2\xi}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{3-\xi}{4+2\xi}\right)} \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{1}\right)^{-2}
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify by combining the $\left(\frac{1}{4+2\xi}\right)$ terms:**

The numerator carries $\left(\frac{1}{4+2\xi}\right)^{1+3} = \left(\frac{1}{4+2\xi}\right)^4$.
The denominator carries $\left(\frac{1}{4+2\xi}\right)^{1+1} = \left(\frac{1}{4+2\xi}\right)^2$.

Net factor: $\left(\frac{1}{4+2\xi}\right)^{4-2} = \left(\frac{1}{4+2\xi}\right)^{2}$.

**Step 6 — Rearrange the equation:**

$$
1000 = \frac{\xi \cdot 27\xi^3}{(1-\xi)(3-\xi)} \cdot \frac{1}{(4+2\xi)^2}
$$

$$
1000 = \frac{27 \xi^4}{(1-\xi)(3-\xi)(4+2\xi)^2}
$$

**Step 7 — Solve iteratively.** Try ξ = 0.98:

$$
\frac{27(0.98)^4}{(0.02)(2.02)(4+1.96)^2} = \frac{27 \times 0.9224}{0.02 \times 2.02 \times 35.52} = \frac{24.90}{1.435} \approx 17.4
$$

Too low. Try ξ = 0.999:

$$
\frac{27(0.999)^4}{(0.001)(2.001)(4+1.998)^2} = \frac{26.97}{0.001 \times 2.001 \times 35.97} = \frac{26.97}{0.07198} \approx 374.7
$$

Still too low. Try ξ = 0.9999:

$$
\frac{27(0.9999)^4}{(0.0001)(2.0001)(4+1.9998)^2} = \frac{26.997}{0.0001 \times 2.0001 \times 35.998} = \frac{26.997}{0.007200} \approx 3749
$$

Closer. Iterating between ξ = 0.9999 and ξ = 0.99995:

**Result:** $X_{CH_4}^{eq} = \xi \approx 0.9999$ — i.e., **> 99.99% equilibrium conversion at 1100 K and atmospheric pressure**.

**Step 8 — Effect of pressure.** At P = 30 bar (typical industrial SMR), the $K_p$ expression is divided by $P^2 = 900$:

$$
K_p^{eff} = \frac{1000}{30^2} = \frac{1000}{900} = 1.11
$$

Re-running the iteration with the much smaller effective constant gives an equilibrium conversion of only **~25–35%**. This explains the industrial compromise: high temperature (1100 K) and **moderate** pressure (15–40 bar) with very high S/C ratio (3–4) to push conversion to acceptable levels.

> [!WARNING]
> **Design Lesson:** *SMR is kinetically controlled at high temperature, but thermodynamically limited at high pressure.* Industrial reformer tubes must be engineered to withstand 950 °C, 30 bar, and external firing—all at once. This is why reformer tubes are made of expensive centrifugally-cast HK-40 or HP-modified alloys.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of the Three Routes

| Parameter | Steam Reforming (SMR) | Partial Oxidation (POX) | Autothermal Reforming (ATR) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Feedstock** | CH₄, naphtha | Heavy oils, residue, pet-coke | CH₄, naphtha, light gases |
| **External heat?** | Yes (tube furnace) | No (autothermal) | No (autothermal) |
| **Catalyst** | Ni on Al₂O₃/CaAl₂O₄ | None (thermal) | Ni on Al₂O₃ |
| **T (°C)** | 700 – 950 | 1200 – 1500 | 950 – 1050 |
| **P (bar)** | 15 – 40 | 30 – 80 | 25 – 40 |
| **S/C ratio** | 2.5 – 4.0 | None (uses O₂) | 1.0 – 2.5 |
| **O₂/C ratio** | — | 0.7 – 1.0 | 0.3 – 0.6 |
| **Theoretical H₂:CO** | 3.0 : 1 | 2.0 : 1 | 1.5 – 2.5 : 1 |
| **CH₄ slip (%)** | 3 – 10 | < 1 | < 2 |
| **Thermal η (%)** | 70 – 85 | 60 – 75 | 80 – 90 |
| **Capital cost** | High (alloy tubes) | Low–Medium (refractory) | Medium |
| **O₂ plant needed?** | No | Yes | Yes |
| **Scale-up limit** | ~ 5000 MTPD (single train) | No real limit | ~ 5000 MTPD |
| **Best for** | H₂, NH₃ | Gasification, IGCC | GTL, MeOH
