## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Industrial SMR Hydrogen/Syngas Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Feed NG 96 percent CH4] --> B[Feed Gas Saturator and Desulfurizer]
    B --> C[ZnO and CoMo Polishing Guard]
    C --> D[Steam Mixer S C equals 3.0 to 3.5]
    D --> E[Primary Reformer Furnace 700 to 950 C Ni Catalyst]
    E --> F[Process Gas Boiler Waste Heat Recovery]
    F --> G[High Temperature Shift Converter Fe3O4 Cr2O3 350 to 450 C]
    G --> H[Low Temperature Shift Converter Cu ZnO Al2O3 200 to 250 C]
    H --> I[CO2 Absorber MEA or Benfield]
    I --> J[PSA Pressure Swing Adsorption H2 Purification]
    J --> K[99.99 percent Pure H2 Product]
    I --> L[CO2 Product for Urea or Sequestration]
    E --> M[Reformed Gas to Syngas Header]
    M --> N[Methanol or Ammonia Synthesis Loop]
    F --> O[High Pressure Steam Superheater]
    O --> P[Steam Turbine Drive]
    P --> D
    P --> E
    F --> Q[Export Steam to Refinery]
    B --> R[Feed Preheater using Flue Gas]
    E --> S[Flue Gas to Stack with Heat Recovery]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Trace the feed path (A→B→C→D→E) and the syngas path (E→F→G→H→I→J→K). Notice the **heat integration loop**—the flue gas heats the feed (B←R←S), and the process gas boiler (F) generates superheated steam (O) that drives the turbine (P) which powers the air/feed compressors. This integrated design is the hallmark of modern SMR plant economics.

    B0 -->|H C less than 1.5| E0[Heavy Oil Coal or Biomass]

```

### 4.3 Thermodynamic Equilibrium Schematic: Effect of T and S/C on CH₄ Conversion

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[Low T 700 C]
        A1[CH4 conv 60 percent] --> A2[High CH4 slip]
    end
    subgraph S2[Mid T 850 C]
        B1[CH4 conv 88 percent] --> B2[Moderate slip]
    end

```

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Visual:** The dual levers of SMR performance are (i) **exit temperature** and (ii) **steam-to-carbon ratio**. Both push the equilibrium toward higher CH₄ conversion, but they do so at different costs—firing duty vs. steam generation. The optimum is the *minimum-cost envelope* that achieves the target conversion.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, PECHT741]**
**Define synthesis gas. Explain why the H₂/CO molar ratio is the most important specification parameter of syngas.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Synthesis gas (syngas) is a mixture of hydrogen (H₂) and carbon monoxide (CO), along with minor amounts of CO₂, H₂O, CH₄, and N₂, produced by the partial oxidation or steam reforming of carbonaceous feedstocks. **(1 mark)**

The H₂:CO molar ratio is the most important specification because it determines which downstream petrochemical process the syngas is suitable for. **(1 mark)** For example, ammonia synthesis requires H₂:CO ≈ 3:1 (with N₂ addition), methanol synthesis requires ≈ 2:1, Fischer–Tropsch synthesis needs ≈ 1:1, and pure hydrogen production shifts the ratio to >5:1. The ratio is controlled by adjusting the reformer feedstock ratio (S/C and O₂/C). **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PECHT741]**
**List the three principal industrial routes for producing synthesis gas. State the typical H₂:CO ratio obtained from each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three principal industrial routes are:
1. **Steam Methane Reforming (SMR):** Natural gas + steam over a Ni catalyst at 700–950 °C and 15–40 bar. H₂:CO = **3.0–5.0 : 1** **(1 mark)**
2. **Partial Oxidation (POX):** Hydrocarbon + O₂ at 1200–1500 °C, no catalyst, autothermal. H₂:CO = **1.6–1.9 : 1** **(1 mark)**
3. **Autothermal Reforming (ATR):** Hybrid of SMR + POX in one refractory vessel with O₂ + steam. H₂:CO = **1.5–2.5 : 1** **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Module-end assessments include *internal choice*. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both questions carry 14 marks each, split into (a) and (b) sub-parts.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1, PECHT741]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat process flow diagram, describe the **Steam Methane Reforming (SMR) process** for the production of synthesis gas. Discuss the operating conditions, catalysts used, and the role of the Water–Gas Shift (WGS) converter. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive an expression for the **equilibrium CH₄ conversion** in a steam reformer operating with a steam-to-carbon ratio of 3.0 at 1100 K and 1 bar, using the equilibrium constant $K_p = 1000$ for the reaction $CH_4 + H_2O \rightleftharpoons CO + 3H_2$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Description of SMR Process (7 marks):**

The SMR process converts methane into a mixture of H₂ and CO using high-temperature steam over a nickel-based catalyst.

**Process Steps (5 marks):**

1. **Feed Pretreatment:** Natural gas is desulfurized in a ZnO/Co–Mo guard to < 0.1 ppm H₂S, because sulfur permanently poisons the Ni catalyst.

2. **Compression & Mixing:** Feed is mixed with process steam at an S/C molar ratio of 3.0–4.0 and preheated to ~ 500 °C in the convective section of the reformer furnace.

3. **Primary Reforming:** The mixture flows through catalyst-filled tubes (Ni on α-Al₂O₃ or CaAl₂O₄ support, promoted with K₂O) at 700–950 °C, 15–40 bar. The endothermic reaction:
$$
CH_4 + H_2O \rightarrow CO + 3H_2 \;\; (\Delta H = +206 \text{ kJ/mol})
$$
occurs, with simultaneous WGS. Furnace burners supply the required heat.

4. **Process Gas Cooling:** The hot reformed gas (≈ 850 °C) is rapidly cooled in a process gas boiler (waste-heat boiler), generating high-pressure superheated steam (≈ 100 bar, 500 °C).

5. **Water–Gas Shift Converter:** The cooled gas (350 °C) passes through a **High-Temperature Shift (HTS)** bed of Fe₃O₄/Cr₂O₃ catalyst, then a **Low-Temperature Shift (LTS)** bed of Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃ catalyst, where CO is converted to additional H₂ and CO₂.

6. **CO₂ Removal and Purification:** CO₂ is absorbed in MEA, hot potassium carbonate (Benfield), or Selexol. Final H₂ purification uses Pressure-Swing Adsorption (PSA).

**Catalyst (1 mark):** Ni (15–25 wt%) on α-Al₂O₃, with K₂O, MgO, and CaO as promoters. Tubes are centrifugally cast in HP-modified alloy (25Cr-35Ni) to withstand 950 °C and 40 bar.

**Role of WGS (1 mark):** The WGS reaction boosts H₂ yield and decreases CO. The two-stage HTS+LTS design exploits the favourable low-temperature equilibrium to achieve > 99% CO conversion.

---

**(b) Equilibrium CH₄ Conversion (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Reaction and Kp expression (1 mark):**

$$
CH_4 + H_2O \rightleftharpoons CO + 3H_2 \qquad K_p = 1000
$$

$$
K_p = \frac{P_{CO} \cdot P_{H_2}^3}{P_{CH_4} \cdot P_{H_2O}} \cdot P^{-2}
$$

**Step 2 — Basis: 1 mol CH₄ + 3 mol H₂O (S/C = 3), let ξ be the extent of reaction (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{CH_4} & = 1 - \xi \\
n_{H_2O} & = 3 - \xi \\
n_{CO} & = \xi \\
n_{H_2} & = 3\xi \\
n_{total} & = 4 + 2\xi
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Partial pressures at P = 1 bar (1 mark):**

$$
P_i = y_i \cdot P = \frac{n_i}{4+2\xi}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into Kp (2 marks):**

$$
1000 = \frac{\left(\dfrac{\xi}{4+2\xi}\right) \left(\dfrac{3\xi}{4+2\xi}\right)^3}{\left(\dfrac{1-\xi}{4+2\xi}\right)\left(\dfrac{3-\xi}{4+2\xi}\right)} \cdot (1)^{-2}
$$

$$
1000 = \frac{27 \xi^4}{(1-\xi)(3-\xi)(4+2\xi)^2}
$$

**Step 5 — Iterate to find ξ (2 marks):**

| ξ (guess) | Numerator $27\xi^4$ | Denominator | Ratio |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0.95 | 22.07 | 0.05 × 2.05 × 35.52 = 3.64 | 6.06 |
| 0.99 | 25.97 | 0.01 × 2.01 × 35.96 = 0.7229 | 35.93 |
| 0.999 | 26.94 | 0.001 × 2.001 × 35.99 = 0.0720 | 374.2 |
| 0.9999 | 26.99 | 0.0001 × 2.0001 × 36.00 = 0.007200 | **3749** |

**Result:** Linear interpolation between ξ = 0.9999 (ratio 3749) and ξ = 0.99993 gives ratio = 1000:

$$
\xi \approx 0.9998 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; X_{CH_4}^{eq} \approx 99.98\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** At 1100 K and 1 bar with S/C = 3, the *thermodynamic* equilibrium CH₄ conversion is essentially complete (~99.98%). The real industrial reformer at 30 bar achieves 88–92% conversion—proving that **kinetics and pressure**, not equilibrium, limit real-world performance.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1, PECHT741]**

**(a)** Compare **Steam Methane Reforming (SMR), Partial Oxidation (POX), and Autothermal Reforming (ATR)** for syngas production in terms of principle, reactions, operating conditions, H₂:CO ratio, advantages, and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A reformer feed contains 1000 Nm³/h of methane (at NTP) and 3000 Nm³/h of steam. The reformer operates at 850 °C and 25 bar with 90% CH₄ conversion. Assuming the only reactions are R-1 and R-3, with 70% of the CO produced by R-1 being consumed by WGS, calculate: **(i)** the molar flow rates of H₂, CO, CO₂, and unreacted CH₄ in the exit gas, and **(ii)** the H₂:CO ratio at the reformer outlet. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Aspect | SMR | POX | ATR |
|---|---|---|---|
| Principle | Steam + hydrocarbon, externally heated | Partial combustion with O₂ | Hybrid SMR + POX in one vessel |
| Primary reaction | $CH_4 + H_2O \rightarrow CO + 3H_2$ (endothermic) | $CH_4 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow CO + 2H_2$ (exothermic) | Both, in series |
| T (°C) | 700–950 | 1200–1500 | 950–1050 |
| P (bar) | 15–40 | 30–80 | 25–40 |
| Catalyst | Ni/Al₂O₃ | None | Ni/Al₂O₃ (bottom section) |
| H₂:CO | 3 – 5 : 1 | 1.6 – 1.9 : 1 | 1.5 – 2.5 : 1 |
| **Advantage** | Highest H₂ yield; well-proven | No catalyst; handles heavy feeds | Compact, thermally efficient, tunable ratio |
| **Limitation** | Expensive alloy tubes; high T metallurgy | Requires O₂ plant; high CO₂ | Requires O₂ plant; complex burner |

**Award 1 mark for the table, 1 mark for the principle, 2 marks for advantages, 2 marks for limitations, 1 mark for the operating-condition comparison.**

---

**(b) Mass Balance Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Convert volumetric feed to molar flow (1 mark):**

At NTP, 1 Nm³ = 1/22.414 kmol = 0.04462 kmol.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{CH_4}^{in} & = 1000 \times 0.04462 = 44.62 \;\text{kmol/h} \\
F_{H_2O}^{in} & = 3000 \times 0.04462 = 133.86 \;\text{kmol/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply 90% CH₄ conversion (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{CH_4}^{reacted} & = 0.90 \times 44.62 = 40.158 \;\text{kmol/h} \\
F_{CH_4}^{out} & = 0.10 \times 44.62 = 4.462 \;\text{kmol/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — From R-1 stoichiometry, 40.158 kmol CH₄ produces 40.158 kmol CO and 3 × 40.158 = 120.474 kmol H₂ (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{CO}^{R-1} & = 40.158 \;\text{kmol/h} \\
F_{H_2}^{R-1} & = 120.474 \;\text{kmol/h} \\
F_{H_2O}^{consumed,\,R-1} & = 40.158 \;\text{kmol/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply 70% WGS conversion of CO (1 mark):**

$$
F_{CO}^{shifted} = 0.70 \times 40.158 = 28.111 \;\text{kmol/h}
$$

**Step 5 — Update exit flows after WGS (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{CO}^{out} & = 40.158 - 28.111 = 12.047 \;\text{kmol/h} \\
F_{CO_2}^{out} & = 28.111 \;\text{kmol/h} \\
F_{H_2}^{additional,\,WGS} & = 28.111 \;\text{kmol/h} \\
F_{H_2}^{out} & = 120.474 + 28.111 = 148.585 \;\text{kmol/h} \\
F_{H_2O}^{out} & = 133.86 - 40.158 - 28.111 = 65.591 \;\text{kmol/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute H₂:CO ratio (1 mark):**

$$
R = \frac{F_{H_2}^{out}}{F_{CO}^{out}} = \frac{148.585}{12.047} = 12.33
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The H₂:CO ratio of 12.33 is *very high*—unsuitable for FT or methanol, but **excellent for a hydrogen plant** after the shift section is fully optimized. For a methanol plant, the WGS must be *limited* to keep the ratio at ≈ 2.

**Step 7 — Final exit gas composition (1 mark):**

| Species | Flow (kmol/h) | Dry mol % |
|---|---|---|
| H₂ | 148.585 | 76.8 |
| CO | 12.047 | 6.2 |
| CO₂ | 28.111 | 14.5 |
| CH₄ | 4.462 | 2.3 |
| H₂O | 65.591 | (excluded from dry basis) |
| **Total dry** | 193.205 | 100.0 |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Syngas Questions (PECHT741):**
> 1. **Forgetting the Δn correction in Kp** (i.e., the $P^{\Delta n}$ term) — *2-mark penalty* in derivations.
> 2. **Confusing the H₂:CO ratio with the H₂/CO₂ ratio** — the WGS reaction alters *both* and students often mix them up.
> 3. **Ignoring WGS coupling with R-1** — the system has *two* independent reactions; treating them as isolated costs marks.
> 4. **Stating ΔH without units** — always write "+206.2 kJ/mol", not "+206.2".
> 5. **Missing the anti-coking rationale for S/C ≥ 2.5** — this is a KTU-favourite conceptual point.
> 6. **Forgetting the basis of 22.414 Nm³/kmol** in volumetric-to-molar conversions.
> 7. **Not specifying dry basis vs. wet basis** when reporting syngas composition.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Syngas** = H₂ + CO mixture; ratio dictates downstream application.
- **Three industrial routes:** SMR (H₂:CO = 3–5), POX (H₂:CO ≈ 2), ATR (H₂:CO = 1.5–2.5).
- **Primary SMR reaction:** $CH_4 + H_2O \rightleftharpoons CO + 3H_2$, $\Delta H = +206.2$ kJ/mol.
- **WGS reaction:** $CO + H_2O \rightleftharpoons CO_2 + H_2$, $\Delta H = -41.2$ kJ/mol.
- **R-1 is endothermic** → favoured by **high T**.
- **R-1 produces 4 mol gas from 2 mol gas** → favoured by **low P**.
- **S/C ratio = 2.5–4.0** in SMR; prevents coking.
- **O₂/C ratio = 0.3–0.6 (ATR)**; 0.7–1.0 (POX).
- **Catalyst:** Ni (15–25 wt%) on α-Al₂O₃, promoted with K₂O.
- **HTS catalyst:** Fe₃O₄/Cr₂O₃ at 350–450 °C.
- **LTS catalyst:** Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃ at 200–250 °C.
- **Reformer tube alloy:** Centrifugally cast 25Cr-35Ni (HK-40 or HP-mod).
- **SMR endothermic heat** is supplied by burning fuel in a top-fired or side-fired furnace.
- **POX is autothermal** — no external heat, no catalyst, but requires cryogenic O₂ plant.
- **ATR is the preferred technology for mega-scale methanol and GTL** because of its tunable ratio and high efficiency.
- **Coal and biomass gasification** produce *low* H₂:CO syngas (0.4–1.0), requiring shift conversion to boost H₂.
- **Theoretical H₂:CO from pure R-1 = 3.0**; with WGS, rises to 3.5–5.0.
- **CH₄ conversion at 1100 K, 1 bar, S/C = 3** is thermodynamically > 99.9% (kinetically limited in practice).
- **Pressure increases reduce equilibrium conversion** dramatically — high-pressure SMR achieves 88–92% CH₄ conversion.
- **Carbon deposition** must be suppressed by maintaining S/C > 2.5 and avoiding low-temperature hot spots.
- **PSA (Pressure-Swing Adsorption)** produces 99.99% pure H₂ from SMR exit gas.
- **Modern sustainability focus:** Green H₂ via electrolysis + CO₂ from syngas → methanol (CO₂ hydrogenation).
- **Global context:** ~ 70% of the world's hydrogen comes from SMR; transition to "blue" H₂ (with CCUS) and "green" H₂ (with renewables) is the central decarbonization challenge of the 2020s–2030s.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Methanol
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT741 - PETROCHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Methanol

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Methanol (CH₃OH)** is the **simplest saturated monohydric alcohol**, consisting of a methyl group (–CH₃) bonded to a hydroxyl group (–OH). It is a **light, volatile, colourless, flammable liquid** with a mild alcoholic odour, and is one of the most important **C₁ platform chemicals** in the petrochemical industry. Industrially, it is almost exclusively produced by the **catalytic hydrogenation of synthesis gas (syngas)** — a mixture of CO, CO₂, and H₂ — over **copper-based catalysts** at high pressure (50–100 bar) and moderate temperature (220–280 °C).

| Property | Value / Description |
|---|---|
| IUPAC Name | Methanol |
| Molecular Formula | **CH₃OH** |
| Molar Mass | **32.04 g/mol** |
| CAS Number | 67-56-1 |
| Boiling Point | **64.7 °C** |
| Melting Point | –97.6 °C |
| Density (at 20 °C) | **0.7918 g/cm³** |
| Flash Point | 11 °C (highly flammable) |
| Auto-Ignition | 470 °C |
| Synonyms | Methyl alcohol, Wood spirit, Carbinol, Methyl hydroxide |

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of Methanol as the "Smaller, Fiery Cousin" of Petrol.** Just as petrol (a mixture of longer-chain hydrocarbons like octane, C₈H₁₈) is the workhorse fuel of vehicles, **methanol is the smallest, simplest "synthetic fuel" molecule** chemists can build from scratch using only three atoms: one carbon, four hydrogens, and one oxygen. Imagine taking a single Lego brick (the methyl group –CH₃) and gluing an oxygen-with-hydrogen "tag" (the –OH group) onto it — that tiny brick, CH₃OH, is the foundation for building plastics, paints, antifreeze, and even clean-burning automobile fuels. Historically, it was called **"wood spirit"** because our ancestors literally distilled it by heating wood in the absence of air (destructive distillation of wood). Today, we make millions of tonnes of it from natural gas, coal, or even captured CO₂ — making methanol a true **bridge molecule** between fossil fuels and a sustainable future.

## 1.3 Historical Context

> [!NOTE]
> - **1661:** Robert Boyle first isolated methanol via the destructive distillation of boxwood (hence *wood alcohol*).
> - **1834:** Jean-Baptiste Dumas and Eugène-Melchior Péligot determined its elementary composition (C, H, O) and named it *méthylène*.
> - **1923:** BASF (Germany) commercialised the first industrial methanol synthesis from syngas at **high pressure (250–350 bar)** using a **ZnO/Cr₂O₃ catalyst** (the "BASF High-Pressure Process").
> - **1966:** Imperial Chemical Industries (ICI, UK) revolutionised the industry with the **Low-Pressure Methanol Process** (50–100 bar) using a **Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃ catalyst**, which remains the **global industry standard** today.

## 1.4 Significance in the Petrochemical Value Chain

Methanol sits at the **top of the C₁ chemistry pyramid** — it is the **single most important derivative of synthesis gas** and a strategic feedstock for over 30 major chemicals. Its key downstream uses include:

- **Formaldehyde (HCHO)** — ~38% of global methanol → resins, plastics, plywood
- **Methyl Tert-Butyl Ether (MTBE)** / Methyl Tert-Amyl Ether (TAME) — gasoline blending
- **Acetic Acid (CH₃COOH)** — via methanol carbonylation (Monsanto / Cativa process)
- **Olefins (MTO/MTP)** — methanol-to-olefins process yielding ethylene & propylene
- **Dimethyl Ether (DME)** — LPG substitute & diesel alternative
- **Methyl Methacrylate, Methylamines, Chloromethanes** — solvents & intermediates
- **Direct fuel applications** — racing fuel, fuel cells, biodiesel transesterification
- **Antifreeze** — historically major use
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Industrial Manufacturing Routes for Methanol

Modern methanol is produced almost exclusively by the **catalytic conversion of synthesis gas (syngas)**. The chemistry involves three key reversible reactions:

### 2.1.1 Core Governing Reactions

**Reaction 1 — CO Hydrogenation (Primary):**

$$\text{CO} + 2\text{H}_2 \rightleftharpoons \text{CH}_3\text{OH} \quad \Delta H_{298\,\text{K}} = -90.8\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

**Reaction 2 — CO₂ Hydrogenation (Secondary, Modern):**

$$\text{CO}_2 + 3\text{H}_2 \rightleftharpoons \text{CH}_3\text{OH} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \Delta H_{298\,\text{K}} = -49.5\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

**Reaction 3 — Reverse Water-Gas Shift (RWGS, Side Reaction):**

$$\text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2 \rightleftharpoons \text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \Delta H_{298\,\text{K}} = +41.2\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All three reactions are **exothermic (Reactions 1 & 2)** and **involve a decrease in the number of moles**. According to **Le Chatelier's Principle**, maximum methanol yield is favoured by:
> 1. **Low temperature** (kinetically limited; industrial compromise 220–280 °C)
> 2. **High pressure** (50–100 bar in modern plants)
> 3. **Stoichiometric or H₂-rich syngas** (typical module M = (H₂ – CO₂)/(CO + CO₂) ≈ 2.0–2.2)

### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Reaction Logic

- **Step 1 — Syngas Generation:** A carbon source (natural gas, coal, biomass, or CO₂) is reformed/partial-oxidised to produce a raw syngas mixture of H₂, CO, and CO₂.
- **Step 2 — Syngas Purification:** Sulphur compounds (H₂S, COS), particulates, and tars are removed to **< 0.1 ppm**, as these are **potent catalyst poisons** for the Cu-based catalyst.
- **Step 3 — Compression:** Purified syngas is compressed to **50–100 bar** in multi-stage centrifugal compressors.
- **Step 4 — Catalytic Conversion:** The pressurised gas passes through a fixed-bed (or gas-cooled) reactor packed with **Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃ catalyst** at **220–280 °C**.
- **Step 5 — Heat Recovery:** The highly exothermic heat is recovered to generate **high-pressure steam** (~40 bar), which drives the large syngas compressor (energy integration).
- **Step 6 — Condensation & Distillation:** The crude methanol (containing water, DME, higher alcohols, dissolved gases) is condensed, depressurised, and purified in a **two-column distillation train** to yield **Grade AA methanol (≥ 99.85% purity)**.

## 2.2 The Three Modern Industrial Processes

| Process | Developer | Pressure (bar) | Temperature (°C) | Catalyst | Reactor Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **ICI Low-Pressure** | Imperial Chemical Industries (UK) | 50–100 | 200–300 | Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃ | Quench / Fixed Bed |
| **Lurgi Low-Pressure** | Lurgi GmbH (Germany) | 50–80 | 250–280 | Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃ | Tube-Cooled (Isothermal) |
| **MegaMethanol** | Various (Lurgi, Toyo, etc.) | 70–100 | 250–270 | Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃ | Once-Through / Large-Scale |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃ Catalyst?**
> - **Cu** provides the active hydrogenation sites.
> - **ZnO** stabilises Cu against sintering and provides structural support.
> - **Al₂O₃** acts as a structural promoter, increasing surface area and preventing thermal degradation.
> - The catalyst is **highly sensitive to sulphur** — even **1 ppm H₂S** can reduce activity by ~50%.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Data Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Definition | Formula / Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $M$ | Syngas Module (stoichiometric number) | $M = \dfrac{\left(\text{H}_2 - \text{CO}_2\right)}{\left(\text{CO} + \text{CO}_2\right)}$ | dimensionless |
| $M_{\text{ideal}}$ | Stoichiometric module for CO reaction | $M = 2.0$ | dimensionless |
| $\text{SV}$ | Gas Hourly Space Velocity | $\text{SV} = \dfrac{\text{Vol. of gas (NTP)}}{\text{Vol. of catalyst} \cdot \text{h}}$ | $\text{h}^{-1}$ |
| $\text{CO}$ Conv. | Single-pass CO conversion | $X_{\text{CO}} = \dfrac{F_{\text{CO,in}} - F_{\text{CO,out}}}{F_{\text{CO,in}}}$ | % |
| $\text{STY}$ | Catalyst Productivity / Space-Time Yield | $\text{STY} = \dfrac{\text{kg CH}_3\text{OH}}{\text{m}^3_{\text{cat}} \cdot \text{h}}$ | $\text{kg/m}^3/\text{h}$ |
| $\Delta H_{298}$ | Enthalpy of formation of MeOH from CO | $-90.8$ | kJ/mol |
| $\Delta H_{298}$ | Enthalpy of formation of MeOH from CO₂ | $-49.5$ | kJ/mol |
| $r$ | Reaction rate (Langmuir-Hinshelwood) | $r = \dfrac{k \cdot p_{\text{CO}} \cdot p_{\text{H}_2}^{2}}{\left(1 + K \cdot p_{\text{CO}}\right)^{2}}$ | mol/g-cat/h |
| $K_{p1}$ | Equilibrium constant, Reaction 1 (at 250 °C) | $\approx 10^{-4}$ (very small) | bar$^{-2}$ |
| Stoichiometry Ratio | $\text{H}_2 : \text{CO}$ for MeOH from CO | $2:1$ | mol : mol |
| Syngas Requirement | Syngas feed per tonne MeOH (ideal) | $\approx 2100$ | $\text{Nm}^3$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The methanol industry is a textbook example of **process intensification & energy integration**:
> - The exothermic heat of reaction (≈ 90 kJ/mol) is **fully recoverable as 40-bar steam** in the Lurgi tube-cooled reactor — making modern plants nearly **thermally neutral** with respect to their compressor drives.
> - Modern **"MegaMethanol" plants** produce **5,000–10,000 tonnes/day** (≈ 2 million tonnes/year) — the largest single-train chemical plants in the world, surpassing ethylene crackers in scale.
> - Methanol is the **only commodity chemical that can be made from literally any carbon source** — natural gas (CH₄), coal (C), biomass, municipal solid waste, or even captured atmospheric CO₂ — making it the cornerstone of the **"Methanol Economy"** concept (proposed by Nobel laureate **George A. Olah**).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stoichiometric Feed Ratio for a Given Syngas Composition

A syngas stream has the following **molar composition on a dry basis**:

- H₂ = 60 mol%
- CO = 30 mol%
- CO₂ = 5 mol%
- N₂ = 5 mol% (inert)

Determine (a) the syngas module $M$, and (b) whether the feed is stoichiometric, hydrogen-rich, or carbon-rich relative to the ideal MeOH synthesis reaction (CO + 2H₂ → CH₃OH).

### Solution (Step-by-Step):

**Step 1 — Recall the definition of syngas module $M$:**

$$M = \frac{\left(\text{H}_2 - \text{CO}_2\right)}{\left(\text{CO} + \text{CO}_2\right)}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the mole fractions (per 100 mol dry syngas):**

$$M = \frac{\left(60 - 5\right)}{\left(30 + 5\right)} = \frac{55}{35} = 1.571$$

**Step 3 — Compare to the ideal stoichiometric value $M_{\text{ideal}} = 2.0$:**

Since $M = 1.571 < 2.0$, the syngas is **hydrogen-deficient (carbon-rich / "lean")**.

**Step 4 — Engineering interpretation:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A carbon-rich syngas (M < 2) means that **all H₂ can be consumed before all the CO is converted**. The unconverted CO will exit the reactor and must be **recycled** (typical recycle ratio 3:1 to 5:1) or used as fuel. A hydrogen-rich syngas (M > 2), in contrast, allows higher single-pass conversion with less recycle.

**Step 5 — Calculate the moles of H₂ required to convert all CO and CO₂ stoichiometrically:**

$$\text{H}_2^{\text{required}} = 2 \cdot \text{CO} + 3 \cdot \text{CO}_2 = 2(30) + 3(5) = 60 + 15 = 75\ \text{mol}$$

Since we have only **60 mol H₂** available, the shortage is:

$$\Delta\text{H}_2 = 75 - 60 = 15\ \text{mol deficiency}$$

This 15 mol deficiency is exactly why the module drops below 2.0.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Material Balance on a Methanol Synthesis Reactor

**Problem:** A methanol synthesis reactor is fed with a syngas (per 100 mol feed) containing:
- 70 mol H₂, 25 mol CO, 3 mol CO₂, 2 mol N₂ (inert).

The **single-pass CO conversion** is 20%, the **CO₂ conversion** is 40%, and the reactions proceed to give only CH₃OH (no side products). Determine the **outlet gas composition (mol%)** and the **moles of methanol produced per 100 mol of feed**.

### Solution (Step-by-Step):

**Step 1 — Write the two governing reactions:**

$$\text{Reaction (a):}\ \text{CO} + 2\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{OH}$$

$$\text{Reaction (b):}\ \text{CO}_2 + 3\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{OH} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Step 2 — Define the extent of reaction (in moles per 100 mol feed):**

- Let $\xi_a$ = extent of Reaction (a) (consumed CO)
- Let $\xi_b$ = extent of Reaction (b) (consumed CO₂)

By definition:
- $\xi_a = 0.20 \times 25 = 5.00\ \text{mol CO consumed}$
- $\xi_b = 0.40 \times 3 = 1.20\ \text{mol CO}_2\ \text{consumed}$

**Step 3 — Calculate outlet mole flows (per 100 mol feed):**

| Species | In (mol) | Consumed (mol) | Produced (mol) | Out (mol) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| H₂ | 70 | $2\xi_a + 3\xi_b = 10 + 3.6 = 13.6$ | 0 | $70 - 13.6 = 56.4$ |
| CO | 25 | $\xi_a = 5.0$ | 0 | $25 - 5.0 = 20.0$ |
| CO₂ | 3 | $\xi_b = 1.2$ | 0 | $3 - 1.2 = 1.8$ |
| N₂ | 2 | 0 | 0 | $2.0$ |
| CH₃OH | 0 | 0 | $\xi_a + \xi_b = 6.2$ | $6.2$ |
| H₂O | 0 | 0 | $\xi_b = 1.2$ | $1.2$ |

**Step 4 — Total outlet moles:**

$$F_{\text{out}} = 56.4 + 20.0 + 1.8 + 2.0 + 6.2 + 1.2 = 87.6\ \text{mol}$$

**Step 5 — Outlet composition (mol%):**

$$y_{\text{CH}_3\text{OH}} = \frac{6.2}{87.6} \times 100 = 7.08\ \text{mol\%}$$

$$y_{\text{H}_2} = \frac{56.4}{87.6} \times 100 = 64.38\ \text{mol\%}$$

$$y_{\text{CO}} = \frac{20.0}{87.6} \times 100 = 22.83\ \text{mol\%}$$

$$y_{\text{CO}_2} = \frac{1.8}{87.6} \times 100 = 2.05\ \text{mol\%}$$

$$y_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} = \frac{1.2}{87.6} \times 100 = 1.37\ \text{mol\%}$$

$$y_{\text{N}_2} = \frac{2.0}{87.6} \times 100 = 2.28\ \text{mol\%}$$

**Step 6 — Verification (overall H, C, O balance):**

- Total C in: $25 + 3 = 28$; Total C out: $20.0 + 1.8 + 6.2 = 28.0$ ✓
- Total O in: $25 + 6 = 31$; Total O out: $20.0 + 3.6 + 6.2 + 1.2 = 31.0$ ✓
- Total H in: $140$; Total H out: $112.8 + 24.8 + 2.4 = 140.0$ ✓

> [!NOTE]
> The mass balance closes perfectly. The methanol yield per 100 mol feed is **6.2 mol**, corresponding to **6.2 × 32.04 g = 198.65 g of methanol** per 100 mol of syngas, or a **per-pass mass yield** depending on feed MW.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Energy Released & Steam Generation in the Lurgi Reactor

A Lurgi-type methanol reactor produces **1,000 kmol/h of crude methanol** (assume pure CH₃OH for energy calculation). Using Reaction 1 (ΔH = –90.8 kJ/mol), calculate the total heat released per hour and the equivalent mass of **40-bar saturated steam** (latent heat ≈ 1,700 kJ/kg) that can be generated if **80% of the reaction heat is recovered**.

### Solution (Step-by-Step):

**Step 1 — Total heat released by the reaction:**

$$Q_{\text{reaction}} = n \cdot \vert \Delta H \vert = 1000\ \text{kmol/h} \times 90.8\ \text{kJ/mol} \times 10^3\ \text{mol/kmol}$$

$$Q_{\text{reaction}} = 9.08 \times 10^7\ \text{kJ/h}$$

**Step 2 — Recoverable heat at 80% efficiency:**

$$Q_{\text{recover}} = 0.80 \times 9.08 \times 10^7 = 7.264 \times 10^7\ \text{kJ/h}$$

**Step 3 — Mass of 40-bar steam generated:**

$$m_{\text{steam}} = \frac{Q_{\text{recover}}}{\lambda_{\text{steam}}} = \frac{7.264 \times 10^7\ \text{kJ/h}}{1700\ \text{kJ/kg}} = 42,729\ \text{kg/h} \approx 42.7\ \text{tonnes/h}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering insight:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This 42.7 tonnes/h of high-pressure steam is more than enough to drive the large centrifugal syngas compressor, making the Lurgi process **a net steam exporter** — a major reason for its dominance in modern mega-plants.

## 3.4 Summary Analytical Matrix

| Process Condition | Effect on MeOH Yield | Effect on Reaction Rate | Industrial Choice |
|---|---|---|---|
| ↑ Temperature (T) | ↓ Yield (exothermic) | ↑ Rate (Arrhenius) | Compromise: 220–280 °C |
| ↑ Pressure (P) | ↑ Yield (Δn < 0) | ↑ Rate (concentration) | Use 50–100 bar |
| ↑ H₂/CO ratio | ↑ Yield (more H₂) | ↑ Rate (bimolecular) | Maintain M ≈ 2.0–2.2 |
| ↑ Space velocity (SV) | ↓ Conversion (per pass) | ↑ Throughput | Optimise at 5,000–15,000 h⁻¹ |
| ↓ Catalyst poison (S) | ↑ Activity & life | ↑ Rate | Purify to < 0.1 ppm H₂S |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Modern Low-Pressure Methanol Plant (Lurgi-type)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Natural Gas Feed CH4] --> B[Desulphurisation ZnO Bed to lt 0.1 ppm H2S]
    B --> C[Steam Reformer SMR 850 deg C Ni Catalyst]
    D[Process Steam] --> C
    C --> E[Raw Syngas H2 plus CO plus CO2 plus H2O]
    E --> F[Heat Recovery Waste Heat Boiler WHB]
    F --> G[Syngas Compression Multi-stage Centrifugal to 80 bar]
    G --> H[Reactor Feed-Effluent Heat Exchanger FEHE]
    H --> I[Lurgi Tube-Cooled Reactor Cu ZnO Al2O3 at 260 deg C]
    I --> J[Steam Drum 40 bar HP Steam to Compressor Driver]
    I --> K[Hot Reactor Effluent Crude MeOH plus Unreacted Gas]
    K --> H
    K --> L[Air-Cooled Condenser]
    L --> M[Flash Separator Liquid Crude MeOH]
    M --> N[Light-End Distillation Column Removes DME plus CO2]
    N --> O[Heavy-End Distillation Column Removes Water plus Higher Alcohols]
    O --> P[Grade AA Methanol Product 99.85 percent Purity]
    M --> Q[Recycle Gas Compressor]
    Q --> H
    P --> R[Storage Tank]
    R --> S[Downstream Formaldehyde MTBE Acetic Acid DME Olefins]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Methanol Value Chain & Downstream Derivatives

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Feedstock Natural Gas Coal Biomass CO2] --> B[Syngas Generation Reforming Gasification]
    B --> C[Syngas Purification Desulphurisation]
    C --> D[Methanol Synthesis Cu-ZnO-Al2O3 Catalyst]
    D --> E[Purified Methanol CH3OH]
    E --> F1[Formaldehyde 38 percent]
    E --> F2[MTBE plus TAME 12 percent]
    E --> F3[Acetic Acid 8 percent]
    E --> F4[DME 5 percent]
    E --> F5[MTO plus MTP Olefins 10 percent]
    E --> F6[Transesterification Biodiesel 5 percent]
    E --> F7[Fuel Cell plus Direct Fuel 7 percent]
    E --> F8[Others Solvents Amines 15 percent]
    F1 --> G1[Plywood Resins Plastics]
    F2 --> G2[Gasoline Blending]
    F3 --> G3[Vinyl Acetate plus PTA]
    F4 --> G4[Aerosol Propellant LPG Substitute]
    F5 --> G5[Polyethylene Polypropylene]
    F6 --> G6[Renewable Diesel]
    F7 --> G7[Transportation Power Generation]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Reactor & Distillation Section (Energy-Integrated)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Syngas In 80 bar] --> B[FEHE Feed Effluent Heat Exchanger]
    B --> C[Reactor Cu-ZnO-Al2O3 Catalyst]
    C --> D[Steam Generation Tubes Boiling Water 40 bar]
    D --> E[Hot Effluent Returns to FEHE]
    E --> F[Trim Cooler Air-Cooled]
    F --> G[Three-Phase Separator Gas-Liquid-Liquid]
    G --> H[Recycle Gas to Compressor]
    G --> I[Crude Liquid Methanol to Distillation]
    I --> J[Pre-Run Column Removes Dissolved Gases DME Acetone]
    J --> K[Refining Column Removes Water Higher Alcohols]
    K --> L[Grade AA Methanol Pumped to Tank]
    K --> M[Wastewater Stripper]
    D --> N[HP Steam Header Drives Compressor Turbine]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (CO1, Remember/Understand Level) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: List any three important industrial applications of methanol with the corresponding downstream product.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Methanol → Formaldehyde (HCHO):** By catalytic oxidation using Fe-Mo or Ag catalyst. Formaldehyde is the largest consumer (~38% of global methanol) and is used to manufacture **urea-formaldehyde, phenol-formaldehyde, and melamine-formaldehyde resins** for plywood, particle board, and moulded plastics. **(1 mark)**

2. **Methanol → MTBE (Methyl Tert-Butyl Ether):** By acid-catalysed reaction of methanol with isobutylene. MTBE is a high-octane **gasoline oxygenate blending agent** that boosts octane number and reduces CO emissions from tailpipes. **(1 mark)**

3. **Methanol → Acetic Acid:** By the **Monsanto process** (Rhodium catalyst) or the **Cativa process** (Iridium catalyst) — collectively called methanol carbonylation. Acetic acid is a feedstock for **vinyl acetate monomer (VAM)**, **purified terephthalic acid (PTA)**, and cellulose acetate. **(1 mark)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often *name* MTBE or formaldehyde but **forget to write the reaction route** (e.g., "methanol + isobutylene → MTBE"). KTU examiners explicitly award marks for the *transformation* route, not just the application. Avoid this 1-mark loss.

---

### Question 2 (CO1, Understand Level) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is synthesis gas (syngas)? Why is it called a "synthesis" gas? Mention the typical H₂/CO ratio required for methanol synthesis.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** Synthesis gas (or "syngas") is a **gaseous mixture of hydrogen (H₂), carbon monoxide (CO), and carbon dioxide (CO₂)** in varying proportions, produced by the **partial oxidation, steam reforming, or gasification** of carbon-containing feedstocks such as **natural gas (CH₄), naphtha, coal, heavy residual oils, biomass, or municipal solid waste**.

- **Why "synthesis"? (1 mark):** It is called *synthesis* gas because it serves as a **versatile, fundamental building block** (a "C₁ platform") from which a large number of industrially important chemicals and liquid fuels can be *synthesised* — including methanol, ammonia, Fischer-Tropsch diesel, dimethyl ether (DME), and synthetic natural gas (SNG). The name reflects its role as the **"universal feedstock" of C₁ chemistry**.

- **H₂/CO ratio for methanol (1 mark):** Stoichiometrically, the ideal ratio for the methanol reaction **CO + 2H₂ → CH₃OH** is **H₂ : CO = 2 : 1 (by moles)**, which corresponds to a syngas module $M = (H_2 - CO_2)/(CO + CO_2) = 2.0$. In practice, plants operate with **M ≈ 2.0–2.2** (slightly H₂-rich) to ensure complete CO conversion and reduce side-product formation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write only **"H₂/CO = 2"** without defining it as a *molar* ratio. Always specify **"molar ratio"** or **"mole ratio"** to avoid losing half a mark.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1 Choice Q1]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks, CO1/CO2 — Understand & Apply

**Q1(a): With the help of neat flow diagrams, describe the ICI low-pressure process for the manufacture of methanol. Clearly state the operating conditions, catalyst used, and the key chemical reactions involved. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Introduction (1 mark):**
The **ICI Low-Pressure Methanol Process** (commercialised by Imperial Chemical Industries, UK, in 1966) is the **most widely used industrial route** for methanol production worldwide. It replaced the older high-pressure BASF process (ZnO/Cr₂O₃ at 300–400 bar) and offers the advantages of **lower capex (smaller, cheaper reactors and compressors), higher per-pass conversion, and superior catalyst life**.

**(ii) Process Flow & Key Steps (4 marks):**

1. **Syngas Generation:** Natural gas (CH₄) is first desulphurised over a **ZnO guard bed** (< 0.1 ppm H₂S) and then fed to a **steam reformer (SMR)** operating at **~850 °C** over a Ni-based catalyst. The reformer reactions are:
   $$\text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO} + 3\text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H = +206\ \text{kJ/mol}$$
   $$\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H = -41\ \text{kJ/mol (WGS)}$$

2. **Compression:** The raw syngas is cooled, compressed to **50–100 bar** (typically ~80 bar) in multi-stage centrifugal compressors.

3. **Methanol Synthesis Reactor (ICI Quench Type):** The pressurised syngas enters the **top of a cold-shot (quench) reactor** packed with **Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃ catalyst** at **200–300 °C** (typically 250 °C). Cold unreacted syngas is injected at several points along the bed to **limit the adiabatic temperature rise** and protect the catalyst from sintering. The two governing reactions are:
   $$\text{CO} + 2\text{H}_2 \rightleftharpoons \text{CH}_3\text{OH} \quad \Delta H = -90.8\ \text{kJ/mol}$$
   $$\text{CO}_2 + 3\text{H}_2 \rightleftharpoons \text{CH}_3\text{OH} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \Delta H = -49.5\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

4. **Heat Recovery & Condensation:** Hot reactor effluent generates **high-pressure steam**, is cooled in a **feed-effluent heat exchanger (FEHE)**, and the crude methanol is condensed in an **air-cooled or water-cooled condenser**.

5. **Purification (Distillation):** The crude methanol is sent to a **two-column distillation train**:
   - **Light-End (Pre-run) Column:** Removes dissolved gases, dimethyl ether (DME), acetone, and methyl formate as overhead.
   - **Heavy-End (Refining) Column:** Removes water and higher alcohols (ethanol, propanol) as bottoms, yielding **Grade AA Methanol (≥ 99.85 wt%)** as the overhead product.

6. **Recycle:** Unreacted gas from the flash separator is **recycled** (recycle ratio 3:1 to 5:1) back to the reactor to maximise overall conversion (95–99%).

**(iii) Operating Conditions & Catalyst (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Pressure | **50–100 bar (typically 80 bar)** |
| Temperature | **200–300 °C (typically 250 °C)** |
| Catalyst | **Cu / ZnO / Al₂O₃** |
| Catalyst Life | **2–5 years** (sensitive to S, Cl poisons) |
| Single-Pass CO Conversion | **15–25%** (overall 95–99% with recycle) |
| Syngas Module M | **2.0–2.2** |
| Methanol Purity | **≥ 99.85 wt% (Grade AA)** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently **forget to state the operating temperature AND pressure** explicitly. KTU examiners deduct 1 full mark if these two numerical values are missing. Also, avoid confusing the **ICI process** (quench reactor) with the **Lurgi process** (tube-cooled reactor) — they are NOT the same.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks, CO2/CO3 — Apply & Analyse

**Q1(b): A methanol synthesis reactor is fed with 100 kmol/h of syngas having the following dry composition: H₂ = 68%, CO = 28%, CO₂ = 3%, N₂ = 1%. If the single-pass conversions are: X(CO) = 18%, X(CO₂) = 35%, calculate:**
**(i) the outlet flow rate of methanol produced (kmol/h),**
**(ii) the unreacted H₂ flow rate (kmol/h), and**
**(iii) the H₂:CO molar ratio of the feed. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Calculate inlet flows per 100 kmol/h feed (1 mark):**

- F(H₂)ᵢₙ = 68 kmol/h
- F(CO)ᵢₙ = 28 kmol/h
- F(CO₂)ᵢₙ = 3 kmol/h
- F(N₂)ᵢₙ = 1 kmol/h

**Step 2 — Apply conversion definitions (2 marks):**

- Moles of CO converted = 0.18 × 28 = **5.04 kmol/h** ($\xi_a$)
- Moles of CO₂ converted = 0.35 × 3 = **1.05 kmol/h** ($\xi_b$)

**Step 3 — Moles of methanol produced (i) (1.5 marks):**

From the two reactions:
$$n_{\text{CH}_3\text{OH}} = \xi_a + \xi_b = 5.04 + 1.05 = 6.09\ \text{kmol/h}$$

**Step 4 — H₂ consumed in each reaction (1.5 marks):**

- H₂ consumed by Reaction (a) = 2 × $\xi_a$ = 2 × 5.04 = 10.08 kmol/h
- H₂ consumed by Reaction (b) = 3 × $\xi_b$ = 3 × 1.05 = 3.15 kmol/h
- **Total H₂ consumed** = 10.08 + 3.15 = **13.23 kmol/h**

**Step 5 — Unreacted H₂ flow (ii) (0.5 mark):**

$$F_{\text{H}_2,\text{out}} = 68 - 13.23 = 54.77\ \text{kmol/h}$$

**Step 6 — H₂:CO molar ratio of feed (iii) (0.5 mark):**

$$\text{H}_2 : \text{CO} = \frac{68}{28} = 2.43 : 1$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since this ratio (2.43) is **greater than the stoichiometric 2.0**, the feed is **slightly hydrogen-rich** — favourable for high per-pass CO conversion with minimal RWGS side reaction.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1 Choice Q2]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks, CO2 — Understand & Apply

**Q2(a): Explain in detail the various feedstocks that can be used for the production of methanol, with their relative advantages and limitations. Discuss the concept of the "Methanol Economy". (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Introduction (0.5 mark):**
One of the unique advantages of methanol is that **almost any carbon-containing feedstock** — fossil, renewable, or waste — can be converted into synthesis gas and then into methanol. This feedstock flexibility is unmatched by any other commodity petrochemical.

**(ii) Major Feedstocks (5 marks):**

| Feedstock | Process | Advantages | Limitations |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Natural Gas (CH₄)** | Steam Methane Reforming (SMR) | Lowest capex, highest H₂/CO ratio (~3:1), cleanest operation, mature technology | Fossil-based, CO₂ footprint from CH₄, price volatility |
| **Coal (C)** | Coal Gasification (e.g., Texaco, Shell gasifiers) | Abundant & cheap in India/China, energy security | High CO₂ emissions, ash/slag handling, lower H₂/CO (~0.5:1) |
| **Naphtha / Refinery Off-gases** | SMR or POX (Partial Oxidation) | Utilises existing refinery streams, integrated | Competes with olefin crackers for naphtha |
| **Biomass (wood, agricultural waste)** | Gasification (air-blown) | Renewable, near-zero net CO₂, carbon-neutral fuel | Bulky feedstock, tar formation, seasonal availability |
| **Municipal Solid Waste (MSW)** | Plasma/thermal gasification | Waste-to-value, landfill diversion | Complex sorting, chlorine contamination |
| **Captured CO₂ (flue gas or DAC)** | CO₂ hydrogenation with green H₂ | Carbon-negative potential, closes the carbon loop | Requires cheap green H₂ from electrolysis; H₂ cost is the bottleneck |

**(iii) The "Methanol Economy" Concept (1.5 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Methanol Economy** is a **techno-economic vision** championed by **George A. Olah** (Nobel Laureate, 1994 Chemistry) and his colleagues at the **Loker Hydrocarbon Research Institute (USC)**. It proposes methanol as the **central liquid fuel and energy carrier** of a future post-fossil economy, because:
> 1. Methanol can be made from **any carbon source** (including CO₂ captured from air or industry).
> 2. It is a **convenient liquid** at ambient conditions — easy to store, transport, and refuel (unlike H₂).
> 3. It can be **directly used** as a clean-burning fuel in modified internal combustion engines, fuel cells (DMFC), and as a feedstock for synthetic hydrocarbons via the **MTO/MTG (Methanol-to-Gasoline)** route.
> 4. It can **decarbonise transport, shipping, and power generation** without requiring a complete change of infrastructure.
> 5. It creates a **closed carbon cycle** when produced from CO₂ + green H₂ (renewable electricity → electrolyser H₂ → CO₂ + 3H₂ → CH₃OH + H₂O → combustion → CO₂ + H₂O → re-captured).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do NOT just list feedstocks — you must **explain why methanol is uniquely feedstock-flexible** and link it to Olah's vision. KTU examiners look for the **systems-level thinking**, not just a tabulation.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks, CO2/CO3 — Apply & Analyse

**Q2(b): With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a Lurgi-type methanol synthesis reactor. Compare its features with the ICI quench reactor. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Lurgi Reactor — Working Principle (3 marks):**

The **Lurgi methanol reactor** (developed by Lurgi GmbH, Germany) is a **tube-cooled, isothermal fixed-bed reactor**. Its key design features are:

- **Catalyst is packed inside several thousand vertical tubes** (typically 1–2 km of total tube length per reactor).
- **Boiling water circulates on the shell side** at **~40 bar pressure** (corresponding to a saturation temperature of ~250 °C).
- The **exothermic methanol reaction heat** passes through the tube walls and **boils the water** on the shell side, generating **high-pressure (HP) steam**.
- Because the temperature of the boiling water is essentially constant (saturated), the **reaction temperature is maintained nearly isothermal** at **~250 °C** — the **optimum** for the Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃ catalyst.
- This gives **higher single-pass CO conversion (30–40%)** and a **longer catalyst life** (4–6 years) compared to the adiabatic ICI reactor.

**(ii) Block Diagram (2 marks):**

*(Insert block diagram showing: Syngas In → Top header → Distribution to thousands of catalyst-filled tubes → Boiling water shell side → Steam drum to 40-bar HP steam → Bottom header → Hot effluent to FEHE → Crude methanol to separator → Recycle compressor)*

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Syngas In 80 bar 220 deg C] --> B[Top Distribution Header]
    B --> C[Tubes Packed with Cu-ZnO-Al2O3 Catalyst]
    C --> D[Shell Side Boiling Water at 40 bar]
    D --> E[Steam Drum Generates 40 bar HP Steam]
    E --> F[Steam Drives Compressor Turbine]
    C --> G[Bottom Header Hot Effluent]
    G --> H[FEHE Heat Recovery]
    H --> I[Condenser]
    I --> J[Separator Crude MeOH and Recycle Gas]
```

**(iii) Comparison Table: Lurgi vs ICI (2 marks):**

| Feature | ICI Quench Reactor | Lurgi Tube-Cooled Reactor |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature profile | **Adiabatic (hot spot)** | **Nearly isothermal** |
| Temperature control | Cold syngas injection at multiple points | Boiling water on shell side |
| Catalyst life | 2–4 years | 4–6 years (longer) |
| Single-pass conversion | 15–25% | 30–40% (higher) |
| HP steam generation | Modest | Significant — drives compressor |
| Reactor size | Larger for same capacity | More compact |
| Capex | Lower | Slightly higher |
| Best suited for | Small-to-medium plants | **Large, mega-plants (5000+ tpd)** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often **omit the block diagram** in this question — losing 1–2 marks. Even a hand-drawn (neat) schematic earns full credit. Also, do NOT confuse the Lurgi **methanol** reactor (tube-cooled, isothermal) with the Lurgi **gasifier** (different piece of equipment).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Methanol" — Module 1:**

- ✅ **Methanol = CH₃OH** — simplest monohydric alcohol; historically "wood spirit"; bp 64.7 °C; highly flammable, toxic (causes blindness/fatality if ingested).
- ✅ **Industrial production = catalytic hydrogenation of syngas** over **Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃** catalyst at **220–280 °C** and **50–100 bar**.
- ✅ **Two governing reactions** (both exothermic, both Δn < 0):
  - CO + 2H₂ → CH₃OH, ΔH = –90.8 kJ/mol
  - CO₂ + 3H₂ → CH₃OH + H₂O, ΔH = –49.5 kJ/mol
- ✅ **Stoichiometric syngas module M = 2.0** (for CO reaction); industrial practice uses M ≈ 2.0–2.2.
- ✅ **Le Chatelier**: low T (kinetically limited) + high P + H₂-rich syngas → maximum MeOH yield.
- ✅ **Three main commercial processes**: **ICI (quench, adiabatic)**, **Lurgi (tube-cooled, isothermal)**, **MegaMethanol (5,000–10,000 tpd)**.
- ✅ **Catalyst poisoning**: Cu catalyst is **extremely sensitive to sulphur** — feed must be desulphurised to **< 0.1 ppm H₂S**.
- ✅ **Major downstream uses**: Formaldehyde (38%), MTBE (12%), Acetic acid (8%), MTO/MTP olefins (10%), DME (5%), direct fuels (7%), others (15%).
- ✅ **Methanol Economy (Olah's concept)**: Methanol as a **universal liquid fuel and energy carrier** synthesised from **any carbon source** (including CO₂ + green H₂), enabling a closed carbon cycle.
- ✅ **Feedstock flexibility**: Natural gas (SMR), coal (gasification), biomass, MSW, captured CO₂.
- ✅ **Distillation**: Crude MeOH purified in a **two-column train** (light-end column removes DME/acetone; heavy-end column removes water) to yield **Grade AA (≥ 99.85%)** product.
- ✅ **Recycle ratio 3:1 to 5:1** essential for high overall conversion (95–99%).
- ✅ **Energy integration**: Lurgi reactor is **net steam exporter** — heat of reaction (90.8 kJ/mol) generates 40-bar HP steam that drives the syngas compressor.
- ✅ **MTO/MTG processes**: Methanol can be catalysed to **olefins (ZSM-5 / SAPO-34)** or **gasoline (ZSM-5, MTG process)**, making it a route to **non-crude-oil-based fuels**.
- ✅ **Syngas can be made from any carbon source** → methanol is the **gateway molecule to sustainable petrochemistry**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Formaldehyde
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT741 - PETROCHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Formaldehyde ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Formaldehyde (HCHO)**, systematically known as **methanal**, is the *simplest member of the homologous series of aliphatic aldehydes* and is industrially classified as the **first C-1 petrochemical building block**. It is manufactured almost exclusively by the **catalytic oxidation–dehydrogenation of methanol** over a fixed-bed silver catalyst or a metal-oxide (Formox) catalyst. Formaldehyde exists at standard conditions (**25 °C, 1 atm**) as a **colorless, highly toxic, and pungent gas** with a characteristic suffocating odor. Because of its high reactivity and tendency to polymerize, it is never shipped in gaseous monomeric form — it is commercialized as: | Commercial Form | Composition | Use | |---|---|---| | **Formalin** | **37–40 % w/w HCHO** in water (with 6–15 % methanol as stabilizer) | Resins, disinfectants | | **Paraformaldehyde** | Solid linear polymer (HO–(CH₂O)ₙ–H), n ≈ 8–100 | Bedding for molding resins | | **1,3,5-Trioxane** | Cyclic trimer (CH₂O)₃ | Engineering plastics (POM) | | **Methylene diisocyanate (MDI)** | Derived from aniline + HCHO | Polyurethanes | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Formaldehyde is consistently ranked as the **largest-volume organic chemical manufactured globally** (excluding fuel products). Annual world production exceeds **30 million metric tons (as 37 % formalin)**. KTU examiners *specifically* test this as a "statistic-marker" question. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview Imagine a "**chameleon molecule**" — formaldehyde has the structure **H₂C=O**, a tiny central carbon double-bonded to oxygen with two hydrogens flanking it. This tiny structure makes it the **most reactive aldehyde** because: - The carbonyl carbon is *desperately electrophilic* (electron-hungry). - It has *no bulky substituents* to shield it, so any nucleophile (–OH, –NH₂, etc.) can attack instantly. > **Real-world analogy:** Think of formaldehyde as the "universal glue" of the chemical industry. Just as a thin layer of strong glue bonds any two surfaces, even *trace* amounts of HCHO can cross-link proteins, resins, and polymers into rigid networks. This is why: > - It **preserves** biological tissue (it cross-links proteins in dead cells → embalming fluid). > - It **hardens** wood panels (UF/PF resins formed from HCHO cure under heat → particle board, plywood). > - It **sterilizes** hospitals (aqueous formalin kills bacteria by the same protein-cross-linking). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Intuition Takeaway:** Formaldehyde's *small size + high polarity of C=O + absence of steric hindrance* → the most reactive aldehyde → the foundation of the world's largest resin industry. ### 1.3 Standard Physical Metrics (Bolded for Quick Recall) | Property | Value | |---|---| | Molecular Formula | **HCHO** (or CH₂O) | | Molecular Weight | **30.026 g mol⁻¹** | | Melting Point | **−92 °C** | | Boiling Point | **−19.1 °C** | | Density (gas at STP) | **1.067 kg m⁻³** | | Autoignition Temperature | **424 °C** | | Explosive Limits in air | **7 – 73 vol %** (extremely wide) | | Heat of Combustion | **−561 kJ mol⁻¹** | | Flash Point (formalin 37 %) | **64 °C** (non-flammable but combustible) | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Industrial Manufacturing Routes — Logic Breakdown Formaldehyde is produced commercially by **three principal processes** (KTU most-frequently-asked section): #### **Route 1: Silver Catalyst Process (BASF / Original Process)** - **Feed:** Methanol vapor + limited air - **Catalyst:** Electrolytic silver gauze (Ag, 99.99 % purity) - **Operating Conditions:** **600–720 °C, 1.2–1.5 atm, contact time ~0.01 s** - **Two simultaneous reactions occur in the catalyst bed:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Reaction A (Oxidation):} \quad & \mathrm{CH_3OH \; + \; \tfrac{1}{2}\,O_2 \;\longrightarrow\; HCHO \; + \; H_2O} \\ & \Delta H_{298} \;=\; -156 \; \mathrm{kJ \, mol^{-1}} \\ \text{Reaction B (Dehydrogenation):} \quad & \mathrm{CH_3OH \;\longrightarrow\; HCHO \; + \; H_2} \\ & \Delta H_{298} \;=\; +84 \; \mathrm{kJ \, mol^{-1}} \end{aligned} $$ - **Why two reactions?** The *exothermic oxidation* supplies the heat needed to drive the *endothermic dehydrogenation*. The combined operation is **thermoneutral** when the ratio is approximately **O₂ : CH₃OH ≈ 0.30** (mol/mol). - **Methanol conversion:** **80–85 % per pass**, overall yield to HCHO = **88–92 %**. - **The unconverted methanol is recovered** by distillation and recycled. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Trick Question:** "Why is pure oxygen not used in the silver process?" > **Answer:** Excess O₂ raises the adiabatic temperature beyond **Ag's melting point (962 °C)** and promotes formic acid + CO₂ side reactions. The Ag catalyst also requires H₂ in the gas mixture (produced in-situ) to keep the surface reduced. #### **Route 2: Formox Process (Metal-Oxide Catalyst Process)** - **Feed:** Methanol vapor + **excess air** - **Catalyst:** **Iron-molybdenum-vanadium oxide (Fe₂O₃-MoO₃ / V₂O₅)** on inert support - **Operating Conditions:** **300–400 °C, atmospheric pressure** - **Only one reaction:** $$ \begin{aligned} & \mathrm{CH_3OH \; + \; \tfrac{1}{2}\,O_2 \;\longrightarrow\; HCHO \; + \; H_2O} \\ & \Delta H_{298} \;=\; -156 \; \mathrm{kJ \, mol^{-1}} \end{aligned} $$ - **Methanol conversion:** **98–99 % per pass** (very high — no recycle required). - **Selectivity to HCHO:** **91–94 %**; side products = CO, CO₂, dimethyl ether (DME), methyl formate. - **Advantages over silver process:** Higher single-pass conversion, lower temperature, longer catalyst life (≥ 1 year), no need for distillation recycle of methanol. #### **Route 3: Combined Silver / Formox Hybrid (Industrial)** - Many modern plants use a *bimetallic* or *dual-catalyst* system to combine the high selectivity of Ag with the high conversion of Fe-Mo-O. ### 2.2 Side Reactions (Important for Yield Calculations) $$ \begin{aligned} &\mathrm{CH_3OH \; \longrightarrow\; CO \; + \; 2\,H_2} \\ &\mathrm{HCHO \; + \; \tfrac{1}{2}\,O_2 \;\longrightarrow\; HCOOH} \\ &\mathrm{HCHO \; + \; O_2 \;\longrightarrow\; CO_2 \; + \; H_2O} \\ &2\,\mathrm{CH_3OH \;\longrightarrow\; CH_3OCH_3 \; + \; H_2O} \quad \text{(DME formation)} \\ &\mathrm{2\,HCHO \; \longrightarrow\; HCOOCH_3} \quad \text{(methyl formate, Tischenko)} \end{aligned} $$ ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Table) > [!IMPORTANT] > **The following table is the *single most important* reference for solving any numerical in the KTU exam on this topic. Memorize the units.** | S.No | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | |:---:|---|---|---| | 1 | **Conversion of methanol (X)** | $X \;=\; \dfrac{n_{\text{CH}_3\text{OH, in}} \;-\; n_{\text{CH}_3\text{OH, out}}}{n_{\text{CH}_3\text{OH, in}}}$ | Dimensionless, fraction (×100 for %) | | 2 | **Selectivity to HCHO (S)** | $S \;=\; \dfrac{n_{\text{HCHO formed}}}{n_{\text{CH}_3\text{OH reacted}}}$ | mol HCHO / mol CH₃OH reacted | | 3 | **Yield (Y)** | $Y \;=\; X \;\times\; S$ | Often expressed as mass yield | | 4 | **Mass Yield** | $Y_m \;=\; \dfrac{m_{\text{HCHO}}}{m_{\text{CH}_3\text{OH fed}}} \times 100$ | % | | 5 | **Stoichiometric O₂ demand** (silver process) | $\mathrm{O_2 / CH_3OH} \;=\; 0.30$ | mol/mol (combined O₂ for both rxns) | | 6 | **Formox: O₂ demand** | $\mathrm{O_2 / CH_3OH} \;=\; 0.5$ | mol/mol (oxidation only) | | 7 | **Air requirement (21 % O₂)** | $n_{\text{air}} \;=\; \dfrac{n_{\text{O}_2}}{0.21}$ | mol | | 8 | **Formalin concentration** | $\% w/w \;=\; \dfrac{m_{\text{HCHO}}}{m_{\text{solution}}} \times 100$ | Standard = 37 % | | 9 | **Net heat of reaction** (combined silver) | $\Delta H_{\text{net}} \;=\; 0.55\,\Delta H_A \;+\; 0.45\,\Delta H_B$ | Depends on split | | 10 | **Catalyst contact time** | $\tau \;=\; V_{\text{cat}} \;/\; \dot{V}_{\text{gas}}$ | seconds (silver: ~0.01 s) | | 11 | **Theoretical air (kg air / kg HCHO)** | $\dfrac{32 \times 0.5}{30} \;\times\; \dfrac{1}{0.233}$ | **≈ 2.29 kg air / kg HCHO** (Formox) | | 12 | **Formaldehyde-water azeotrope** | 37 % w/w HCHO, bp 99 °C | Cannot be concentrated by simple distillation | ### 2.4 Engineering Utility & Real-World Significance Formaldehyde is a **pillar of the global polymer industry**. Its end-use breakdown (industrial statistics, useful for KTU descriptive answers): | End Use | Approx. Global Share | |---|---| | **Urea-Formaldehyde (UF) resins** | **~ 30 %** | | **Phenol-Formaldehyde (PF) resins** | **~ 15 %** | | **Melamine-Formaldehyde (MF) resins** | **~ 7 %** | | **Polyacetal / Polyoxymethylene (POM) engineering plastic** | **~ 12 %** | | **Methylene Diphenyl Diisocyanate (MDI) for polyurethanes** | **~ 14 %** | | **Pentaerythritol (C(CH₂OH)₄)** | **~ 6 %** | | **Disinfectants, embalming, agriculture** | **~ 8 %** | | **Miscellaneous (HMTA, glycols, chelating agents)** | **~ 8 %** | > **Real-world engineering meaning:** When you sit on a *wooden chair* (UF/PF bonded plywood), wear *sneakers* (PU foam from MDI), or open a *car dashboard* (POM gears, PF composite), you are using a formaldehyde-derived product. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Net Enthalpy of Reaction in the Silver Catalyst Process We are asked: *"In the silver-catalyst process, methanol is converted to formaldehyde by simultaneous oxidation and dehydrogenation. If the ratio of methanol consumed by the oxidation pathway to that by the dehydrogenation pathway is **3 : 2**, compute the net enthalpy of reaction per mole of HCHO produced."* **Step 1 — Define the basis.** Let **3 mol** of CH₃OH go via oxidation, and **2 mol** go via dehydrogenation. The total methanol consumed is **5 mol**, producing **5 mol** of HCHO. **Step 2 — Write both reactions with their ΔH values.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{(R1) Oxidation:} \quad & \mathrm{CH_3OH \; + \; \tfrac{1}{2}\,O_2 \;\longrightarrow\; HCHO \; + \; H_2O}, \quad \Delta H_1 \;=\; -156 \; \mathrm{kJ\,mol^{-1}} \\ \text{(R2) Dehydrogenation:} \quad & \mathrm{CH_3OH \;\longrightarrow\; HCHO \; + \; H_2}, \quad \Delta H_2 \;=\; +84 \; \mathrm{kJ\,mol^{-1}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Multiply each reaction by the stoichiometric coefficient (Hess's Law).** $$ \begin{aligned} 3 \times (\text{R1}): \quad & 3\,\mathrm{CH_3OH} \;+\; \tfrac{3}{2}\,\mathrm{O_2} \;\longrightarrow\; 3\,\mathrm{HCHO} \;+\; 3\,\mathrm{H_2O}, \quad \Delta H_{3R1} \;=\; 3 \times (-156) \;=\; -468 \; \mathrm{kJ} \\ 2 \times (\text{R2}): \quad & 2\,\mathrm{CH_3OH} \;\longrightarrow\; 2\,\mathrm{HCHO} \;+\; 2\,\mathrm{H_2}, \quad \Delta H_{2R2} \;=\; 2 \times (+84) \;=\; +168 \; \mathrm{kJ} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Add the scaled reactions to get the net reaction.** $$ \begin{aligned} & 5\,\mathrm{CH_3OH} \;+\; \tfrac{3}{2}\,\mathrm{O_2} \;\longrightarrow\; 5\,\mathrm{HCHO} \;+\; 3\,\mathrm{H_2O} \;+\; 2\,\mathrm{H_2} \\ & \Delta H_{\text{net}} \;=\; -468 \;+\; 168 \;=\; -300 \; \mathrm{kJ} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Normalize per mole of HCHO.** $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta H_{\text{per mol HCHO}} \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta H_{\text{net}}}{n_{\text{HCHO}}} \;=\; \dfrac{-300 \; \mathrm{kJ}}{5 \; \mathrm{mol}} \;=\; -60 \; \mathrm{kJ\,mol^{-1}} \; \mathrm{HCHO} \end{aligned} $$ > **Interpretation:** The net process is *mildly exothermic* (–60 kJ mol⁻¹). The heat must be removed by generating **high-pressure steam** in waste-heat boilers surrounding the reactor — a key *economic credit* in plant design. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Theoretical Air Requirement for the Formox Process **Problem:** A Formox plant produces **10,000 kg day⁻¹ of 37 % formalin**. Compute the theoretical air supply (in Nm³ h⁻¹) required, assuming **complete combustion stoichiometry** and **25 % excess air** for temperature control. **Step 1 — Compute mass of pure HCHO in product.** $$ m_{\text{HCHO}} \;=\; 10{,}000 \;\times\; 0.37 \;=\; 3700 \; \mathrm{kg\,day^{-1}} $$ **Step 2 — Convert to moles of HCHO.** $$ n_{\text{HCHO}} \;=\; \dfrac{3700 \; \mathrm{kg\,day^{-1}}}{30 \; \mathrm{kg\,kmol^{-1}}} \;=\; 123.33 \; \mathrm{kmol\,day^{-1}} $$ **Step 3 — Stoichiometric O₂ from R1 (oxidation only).** Per kmol of HCHO, the stoichiometric O₂ = **0.5 kmol**. $$ n_{\text{O}_2,\text{stoich}} \;=\; 123.33 \;\times\; 0.5 \;=\; 61.67 \; \mathrm{kmol\,day^{-1}} $$ **Step 4 — Apply 25 % excess air.** $$ n_{\text{O}_2,\text{actual}} \;=\; 61.67 \;\times\; 1.25 \;=\; 77.08 \; \mathrm{kmol\,day^{-1}} $$ **Step 5 — Convert O₂ to air using 21 mol-% O₂ in dry air.** $$ n_{\text{air}} \;=\; \dfrac{77.08}{0.21} \;=\; 367.05 \; \mathrm{kmol\,day^{-1}} $$ **Step 6 — Convert kmol day⁻¹ to Nm³ h⁻¹.** At STP, **1 kmol of ideal gas = 22.414 Nm³**. There are 24 h in a day. $$ \dot{V}_{\text{air}} \;=\; \dfrac{367.05 \;\times\; 22.414}{24} \;=\; 342.7 \; \mathrm{Nm^3\,h^{-1}} $$ > **Final Answer:** ≈ **343 Nm³ h⁻¹** of air supply is required. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Silver vs. Formox Process (Comparison Table) | Parameter | Silver Catalyst Process | Formox (Fe-Mo-O) Process | |---|---|---| | **Catalyst** | Electrolytic Ag gauze (0.5–2 mm wire) | **Fe₂O₃ – MoO₃ – V₂O₅** on SiO₂/Al₂O₃ | | **Temperature (°C)** | 600 – 720 | 300 – 400 | | **Pressure** | 1.2 – 1.5 atm (slightly elevated) | ~ 1 atm | | **O₂ : CH₃OH (mol/mol)** | 0.30 (combined) | 0.50 (oxidation only) | | **Per-pass conversion (%)** | 80 – 85 | 98 – 99 | | **Selectivity to HCHO (%)** | 92 – 95 | 91 – 94 | | **Recycle needed?** | Yes (distillation column) | No (high single-pass) | | **Catalyst life** | 2 – 6 months (sintering) | 1 – 2 years | | **Energy balance** | Nearly thermoneutral | Highly exothermic — needs heat removal | | **Capital cost** | Higher (recycle column, Ag cost) | Lower (simpler flow) | | **Methanol quality required** | High (anhydrous) | Moderate (anhydrous) | | **Product HCHO concentration** | 37 – 50 % formalin (after distillation) | 37 – 55 % formalin | | **Main side products** | HCOOH (corrosion issue) | DME, CO, CO₂ | | **Reactor type** | Fixed-bed adiabatic, multi-tube | Fixed-bed, with molten-salt cooling | | **KTU examiner frequency** | **Very high** (always asked) | **High** (often asked with silver) | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Formox Process (Mermaid) The following block-level functional architecture flow maps the **interactions** between each unit operation in a **Formox (metal-oxide) formaldehyde plant**. It avoids attempting an impossible-to-draw physical schematic and instead maps the *material and energy flows* explicitly. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[methanolStorage
CH3OH Liquid] --> B[vaporizerHeater
85 to 95 C] B --> C[blowerMixer
Air + Methanol Vapor] D[airFilter
Clean Dry Air] --> C C --> E[preheater
200 to 280 C] E --> F[fixedBedReactor
Fe Mo O Catalyst
320 to 400 C
1 atm] F -- exothermic heat --> G[moltenSaltCooler
Steam Generation
2.5 to 4 MPa] F -- hot reactor gas --> H[gasCooler
Heat Recovery] H --> I[absorberTower1
HCHO absorption in water
37 percent formalin] F -- unreacted methanol traces --> I I --> J[absorberTower2
Polishing Section] J --> K[ionExchange
HCOOH removal] K --> L[storageTank
37 to 55 percent Formalin] L --> M[dispatchToMarket
Resin Plant Disinfectants] H -- waste gas --> N[thermalOxidizer
VOC Abatement] G -- high pressure steam --> O[steamHeader
Plant Utility] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Follow the **methanol** from `A` → vaporization → mixing with **air** from `D` → preheating → reaction in the catalyst bed `F` → absorption into **water** in the two-stage absorber `I` and `J` → ion-exchange polishing `K` → finished formalin `L`. The exothermic heat of reaction is captured in `G` (molten-salt cooler) as high-pressure steam sent to the plant header `O`. Off-gases are cleaned in `N`. ### 4.2 Functional Architecture Flow — Silver Catalyst Process ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[methanolFeed
CH3OH] --> S2[vaporizer] S2 --> S3[airMixer
O2 to CH3OH equals 0.30] S3 --> S4[preheater
200 C] S4 --> S5[silverCatalystReactor
Ag gauze
650 to 720 C] S5 -- heat --> S6[wasteHeatBoiler
Steam 4 MPa] S5 -- gas --> S7[absorber
H2O absorption] S7 --> S8[distillationColumn
Methanol Recovery] S8 --> S9[recycleCH3OH] S9 --> S2 S8 --> S10[ionExchange
HCOOH removal] S10 --> S11[37 percent Formalin Product] S6 --> S12[steamHeader] ``` ### 4.3 Chemical Reaction Network — Mermaid ```mermaid flowchart TD M[Methanol CH3OH] -- oxidation with 0.5 O2 --> F[Formaldehyde HCHO] M -- dehydrogenation --> F F -- further oxidation --> FA[Formic Acid HCOOH] F -- combustion --> CO2[CO2 plus H2O] M -- condensation --> DME[Dimethyl Ether DME] F -- Tischenko condensation --> MF[Methyl Formate HCOOCH3] F -- oligomerization --> P[Paraformaldehyde solid] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]** **Q1. (3 marks)** *"What is formaldehyde? Why is it never sold in pure monomeric gaseous form?"* **Model Answer:** Formaldehyde (HCHO, methanal) is the **simplest aliphatic aldehyde** and the **first C-1 petrochemical**, produced industrially by the catalytic oxidation-dehydrogenation of methanol. It is never shipped as a pure gas because (i) it is a **highly toxic, pungent gas** with bp = –19 °C, requiring cryogenic containment; (ii) it **spontaneously polymerizes** to paraformaldehyde at room temperature; and (iii) it is industrially consumed in *aqueous form*. Hence it is marketed as **37–40 % formalin** (aqueous solution stabilized with 6–15 % methanol), as **paraformaldehyde** (solid polymer), or as **trioxane** (cyclic trimer). --- > **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **Q2. (3 marks)** *"Compare the silver and Formox (Fe-Mo-O) processes for formaldehyde manufacture on the basis of catalyst, temperature, and per-pass conversion."* **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Silver Process | Formox Process | |---|---|---| | Catalyst | Electrolytic Ag gauze | Fe₂O₃-MoO₃-V₂O₅ on inert support | | Temperature (°C) | **600 – 720** | **300 – 400** | | Per-pass conversion (%) | **80 – 85** | **98 – 99** | | Recycle needed? | Yes (CH₃OH distillation) | No | --- ### 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) — Internal Choice > **[KTU University Exam – Module 1, 14-mark compulsory choice question]** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a) (7 marks)** With the help of a neat flow-sheet description, explain the manufacture of formaldehyde by the **silver catalyst process**. Discuss the **two simultaneous reactions** that occur on the catalyst surface and explain why both reactions are necessary. **(b) (7 marks)** A Formox plant produces **15,000 kg day⁻¹ of 44 % formalin** with a methanol conversion of **98 %** and a selectivity of **93 %**. Calculate: 1. The daily mass of pure HCHO produced. 2. The daily mass of methanol fed to the reactor. 3. The mass of methanol recycled, assuming unconverted methanol is recovered completely and re-used. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a) (7 marks)** Describe the **Formox (Fe-Mo-O) process** for the manufacture of formaldehyde in detail, including the **catalyst composition, operating conditions, reactions, and yield data**. Compare its advantages and disadvantages with the silver catalyst process. **(b) (7 marks)** Explain the major **industrial applications** of formaldehyde with reference to (i) **urea-formaldehyde and phenol-formaldehyde resins**, (ii) **polyacetal (POM) engineering plastic**, and (iii) **pentaerythritol**. Give one chemical reaction for each. --- ### 5.3 Model Solutions for Question A (Step-by-Step) #### **Solution to A(a) — Silver Catalyst Process (7 marks)** **Step 1 — Definition [1 mark]** The silver catalyst process is the **original and most widely used** route to formaldehyde, employing a fixed bed of **electrolytic silver gauze** as catalyst at **600–720 °C** and slightly above atmospheric pressure. **Step 2 — Reactions [2 marks]** $$ \mathrm{CH_3OH \; + \; \tfrac{1}{2}\,O_2 \;\longrightarrow\; HCHO \; + \; H_2O} \quad \Delta H = -156 \; \mathrm{kJ\,mol^{-1}} $$ $$ \mathrm{CH_3OH \;\longrightarrow\; HCHO \; + \; H_2} \quad \Delta H = +84 \; \mathrm{kJ\,mol^{-1}} $$ **Step 3 — Why both reactions [2 marks]** The exothermic **oxidation** supplies the thermal energy required to drive the endothermic **dehydrogenation**. Together, the two reactions yield a **thermoneutral** operation when ~ 60 % of methanol reacts by oxidation and ~ 40 % by dehydrogenation, giving the O₂ : CH₃OH feed ratio of **0.30**. **Step 4 — Flow-sheet description [2 marks]** Liquid methanol from storage is **vaporized**, mixed with **filtered air** (O₂ : CH₃OH = 0.30), superheated to ~200 °C, and passed through the **silver gauze catalyst bed** in a vertical tubular reactor. The hot reactor effluent goes to a **waste-heat boiler** (raising 4 MPa steam), then to a **two-stage absorber** where HCHO dissolves in demineralized water to give **37–50 % formalin**. The absorber overhead (containing H₂, N₂, unreacted methanol vapour) passes through a **distillation column** that recovers methanol for recycle, with the bottoms sent to an **ion-exchange unit** for formic acid removal before final product storage. --- #### **Solution to A(b) — Numerical (7 marks)** **Given:** - Formalin product rate = 15,000 kg day⁻¹ at 44 % w/w HCHO - Methanol conversion, X = 98 % - Selectivity, S = 93 % **Step 1 — Mass of pure HCHO produced [1 mark]** $$ m_{\text{HCHO}} \;=\; 15{,}000 \;\times\; 0.44 \;=\; 6600 \; \mathrm{kg\,day^{-1}} $$ **Step 2 — Moles of HCHO [1 mark]** $$ n_{\text{HCHO}} \;=\; \dfrac{6600}{30} \;=\; 220 \; \mathrm{kmol\,day^{-1}} $$ **Step 3 — Moles of CH₃OH reacted [1 mark]** From the definition of **selectivity**, S = moles HCHO formed / moles CH₃OH reacted. Hence: $$ n_{\text{CH}_3\text{OH, reacted}} \;=\; \dfrac{n_{\text{HCHO}}}{S} \;=\; \dfrac{220}{0.93} \;=\; 236.56 \; \mathrm{kmol\,day^{-1}} $$ **Step 4 — Moles of CH₃OH fed [1 mark]** From **conversion**, X = reacted / fed. Hence: $$ n_{\text{CH}_3\text{OH, fed}} \;=\; \dfrac{n_{\text{CH}_3\text{OH, reacted}}}{X} \;=\; \dfrac{236.56}{0.98} \;=\; 241.39 \; \mathrm{kmol\,day^{-1}} $$ **Step 5 — Mass of CH₃OH fed [1 mark]** $$ m_{\text{CH}_3\text{OH, fed}} \;=\; 241.39 \;\times\; 32 \;=\; 7724.4 \; \mathrm{kg\,day^{-1}} $$ **Step 6 — Mass of CH₃OH recycled [1 mark]** Unreacted CH₃OH = 241.39 – 236.56 = 4.83 kmol day⁻¹ $$ m_{\text{CH}_3\text{OH, recycled}} \;=\; 4.83 \;\times\; 32 \;=\; 154.6 \; \mathrm{kg\,day^{-1}} $$ **Step 7 — Final statement [1 mark]** The plant therefore requires a **fresh methanol feed of 7724.4 kg day⁻¹**, of which **154.6 kg day⁻¹** is recycled after distillation, and produces **6600 kg day⁻¹ of pure formaldehyde** (= 15,000 kg day⁻¹ of 44 % formalin). --- ### 5.4 Model Solutions for Question B (Step-by-Step Summary) #### **Solution to B(a) — Formox Process (7 marks)** | Aspect | Description | |---|---| | **Catalyst [2 marks]** | Iron-molybdenum oxide (Fe₂O₃-MoO₃) with V₂O₅ promoter, supported on silica or alumina. | | **Reactions [2 marks]** | Only the **oxidation** reaction occurs: CH₃OH + ½O₂ → HCHO + H₂O, ΔH = –156 kJ mol⁻¹. The feed uses **excess air** (O₂ : CH₃OH = 0.5). | | **Operating conditions [1 mark]** | **300 – 400 °C**, ~ 1 atm, contact time 0.1 – 0.5 s. | | **Conversion & selectivity [1 mark]** | **98 – 99 %** conversion per pass, **91 – 94 %** selectivity. **No methanol recycle** required. | | **Comparison [1 mark]** | Advantages: higher conversion, lower temperature, longer catalyst life, no recycle. Disadvantages: lower selectivity, larger air blower, more waste gas to treat. | #### **Solution to B(b) — Applications (7 marks)** | Application | Reaction (one example) | |---|---| | **(i) UF / PF resins [3 marks]** | $\mathrm{CH_2O \; + \; H_2N\!-\!CO\!-\!NH_2 \;\longrightarrow\; HO\!-\!CH_2\!-\!NH\!-\!CO\!-\!NH_2}$ (methylolation, repeated to form cross-linked UF resin). PF analogous with phenol. | | **(ii) Polyacetal / POM [2 marks]** | $n\,\mathrm{HCHO} \;\longrightarrow\; (\!-\!\mathrm{CH_2\!-\!O}\!)_n$ (cationic polymerization, then end-capping with acetic anhydride to give **acetal homopolymer**). | | **(iii) Pentaerythritol [2 marks]** | $4\,\mathrm{HCHO} \;+\; \mathrm{CH_3CHO} \;+\; \mathrm{NaOH} \;\longrightarrow\; \mathrm{C(CH_2OH)_4} \;+\; \mathrm{HCOONa}$ (crossed Cannizzaro). | --- ### 5.5 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mark-losing mistakes on Formaldehyde problems:** > 1. **Confusing the two feed ratios:** Students frequently write O₂ : CH₃OH = 0.5 for both the silver and Formox processes. **Remember: 0.30 (silver) vs 0.50 (Formox).** This alone can lose 1–2 marks. > 2. **Forgetting methanol recycle in the silver process:** If a numerical asks for "feed rate" of methanol in a silver process with 80 % conversion, you must compute the *unreacted* amount and add the **recycle stream** to the *fresh* feed — examiners specifically test this. > 3. **Confusing "formalin" with "formaldehyde":** A *37 % formalin* solution is **NOT** 37 % HCHO + 63 % water exactly; it also contains **6–15 % methanol** as a polymerization inhibitor. Writing "37 % HCHO in water" is a partial answer. > 4. **Mixing up Pentaerythritol and Pentaerythrose:** Pentaerythritol is **C(CH₂OH)₄** — a tetrahydric alcohol — made by the crossed Cannizzaro of 4 HCHO + 1 acetaldehyde + NaOH. > 5. **Forgetting units in numericals:** The KTU board *deducts 0.5 mark per missing unit*. Always write **kg day⁻¹, kmol h⁻¹, Nm³ h⁻¹, kJ mol⁻¹** explicitly. > 6. **Selecting only the oxidation reaction in the silver process:** A common trap — examiners want **both** oxidation and dehydrogenation mentioned. Drawing only one reaction = 1 mark penalty. --- ### 5.6 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print and Pin on Wall!)** - [ ] **Formaldehyde = HCHO = methanal = simplest aldehyde = first C-1 petrochemical.** - [ ] **World's largest-volume organic chemical** (~ 30 million t/yr as 37 % formalin). - [ ] **Commercial forms:** formalin (37 % aq), paraformaldehyde (solid polymer), trioxane (cyclic trimer), MDI. - [ ] **Silver process:** Ag catalyst, 600–720 °C, 1.2–1.5 atm, O₂ : CH₃OH = 0.30, two reactions (oxidation + dehydrogenation), 80–85 % conversion, **recycle required**, ~thermoneutral. - [ ] **Formox process:** Fe-Mo-O/V₂O₅ catalyst, 300–400 °C, ~1 atm, O₂ : CH₃OH = 0.50, **one reaction** (oxidation only), 98–99 % conversion, **no recycle**, exothermic. - [ ] **Key ΔH values:** oxidation = –156 kJ mol⁻¹; dehydrogenation = +84 kJ mol⁻¹. - [ ] **Formulas to memorize:** - Conversion X = (n_in – n_out)/n_in - Selectivity S = n_HCHO / n_CH₃OH reacted - Yield Y = X × S - Air = O₂/0.21, 1 kmol = 22.414 Nm³ - [ ] **Top applications:** UF resins (30 %), PF resins (15 %), MDI (14 %), POM (12 %), pentaerythritol (6 %). - [ ] **Azeotrope:** 37 % HCHO in water (cannot be concentrated by simple distillation; uses vacuum + ion exchange). - [ ] **Toxicity:** Highly toxic, pungent, TLV = 0.5 ppm (8 h). - [ ] **Polyacetal (POM):** engineering plastic with high crystallinity, made by polymerization of pure HCHO. - [ ] **Pentaerythritol:** C(CH₂OH)₄, made from 4 HCHO + 1 CH₃CHO via crossed Cannizzaro. - [ ] **Always mention *both* reactions** when describing the silver process; *only oxidation* for Formox. - [ ] **PFD essentials to draw:** storage → vaporizer → mixer → preheater → reactor → waste-heat boiler → absorber → ion exchange → storage. - [ ] **Examiner's pet topics:** Comparison of silver vs Formox (Table 3.3), uses of HCHO, enthalpy calculations, methanol recycle numericals.

topics.title: Acetic Acid
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT741 - PETROCHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Acetic Acid # 🧪 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Acetic Acid ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned) > [!NOTE] > **Definition:** Acetic acid (ethanoic acid), chemical formula **CH₃COOH** (systematic IUPAC name: **ethanoic acid**), is the second simplest carboxylic acid after formic acid. It is a colourless, corrosive, hygroscopic liquid with a pungent, vinegar-like odour. Industrially, it is the most important aliphatic carboxylic acid and a fundamental **C2 petrochemical building block** used to produce vinyl acetate monomer (VAM), acetate esters, purified terephthalic acid (PTA), acetic anhydride, and as a solvent in industrial processes.
