In petrochemical technology, acetic acid is classified as a **"downstream C1-C2 derivative"** because it is manufactured primarily by the **carbonylation of methanol** (a C1 feedstock) with carbon monoxide (also a C1 feedstock) — a classic C1 → C2 homologation reaction. | Property | Value | |---|---| | Molecular Formula | C₂H₄O₂ | | Structural Formula | CH₃−C(=O)−OH | | Molecular Weight | **60.05 g/mol** | | Boiling Point (100% acid) | **118.1 °C (391.25 K)** | | Melting Point (Glacial) | **16.6 °C** | | Density (25 °C) | **1.049 g/cm³** | | pKa (25 °C) | **4.76** (weak monoprotic acid) | | Flash Point (open cup) | **43 °C** | | Auto-ignition Temp. | **427 °C** | | Vapour Density (air=1) | **2.07** | | Dielectric Constant (20 °C) | **6.15** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Highlight:** "**Glacial acetic acid**" is the term used for water-free (≥99.85%) acetic acid that solidifies below 16.6 °C into ice-like crystals — hence the name. This is the commercial grade shipped in bulk. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Vinegar of Industry" > [!TIP] > **Analogy — The "Cousin of Common Vinegar":** Every household uses white vinegar, which is essentially 4–8% acetic acid diluted in water. Industrial **glacial acetic acid (99.8% pure)** is essentially the *anhydrous, concentrated, "distilled-to-perfection" cousin* of the vinegar sitting in your kitchen. The chemistry is identical, but the petrochemical industry demands the ultra-pure form to feed sensitive catalytic reactors (like the Rhodium-based methanol carbonylation unit), where even trace water would poison the catalyst and disrupt the carbonylation cycle. Think of industrial acetic acid as the *pharmaceutical-grade* version of a common condiment. ## 1.3 Industrial Importance — Why Acetic Acid is a Petrochemical Cornerstone Acetic acid is the **largest-volume organic acid produced worldwide**, with global production exceeding **18 million metric tonnes per year** (as of 2024). Its strategic importance stems from being a **versatile C2 intermediate** that feeds into: 1. **Vinyl Acetate Monomer (VAM)** — ~45% of acetic acid goes here → for PVA, EVA copolymers, paints, adhesives. 2. **Purified Terephthalic Acid (PTA)** — ~20% → for PET bottles, polyester fibres. 3. **Acetate Esters** (ethyl acetate, butyl acetate) — ~15% → solvents for inks, coatings, pharma. 4. **Acetic Anhydride** — ~10% → cellulose acetate, aspirin synthesis. 5. **Monochloroacetic Acid (MCA)** — ~5% → herbicides (2,4-D, MCPA), carboxymethyl cellulose. 6. **Others** — pharmaceuticals, food preservatives (E260), textile dyeing. > [!NOTE] > **Major Producers Globally:** Celanese (US), BP Chemicals (UK/US), Eastman Chemical (US), Sinopec (China), Jiangsu Sopo (China), Daicel (Japan), LyondellBasell (Netherlands), INEOS (UK), PetroChina. **India:** Gujarat Narmada Valley Fertilizers (GNFC), Indian Oil Corporation (IOC), and a growing cluster in Dahej, Gujarat. # ⚙️ Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Manufacturing Routes — A Strategic Overview Acetic acid is commercially produced via **four principal routes**, of which **Methanol Carbonylation** is the dominant technology accounting for **>65% of global production**: | Route | Feedstock | Catalyst | % Global Share (2024) | |---|---|---|---| | **Methanol Carbonylation (Monsanto Process)** | CH₃OH + CO | Rhodium / CH₃I | ~55% | | **Cativa Process (BP)** | CH₃OH + CO | Iridium / CH₃I | ~10% | | **Acetaldehyde Oxidation** | C₂H₄ + ½O₂ → CH₃CHO → CH₃COOH | PdCl₂-CuCl₂ (Wacker) | ~10% | | **Butane / Naphtha Liquid-Phase Oxidation** | C₄H₁₀ or naphtha + O₂ | Mn/Co naphthenates | ~15% (declining) | | **Ethanol Oxidation (Bio-route, emerging)** | C₂H₅OH + O₂ | Cu/Co or bio-fermentation | ~10% (growing) | ## 2.2 The Monsanto Process — Detailed Theoretical Analysis > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Focus:** The **Monsanto methanol carbonylation process** (commercialised in 1970) is the **single most important manufacturing route** and appears in every board exam. The reaction is a **homogeneous catalytic carbonylation** carried out in a bubble-column or stirred-tank reactor at high pressure. ### 2.2.1 Overall Stoichiometry $$\text{CH}_3\text{OH} + \text{CO} \longrightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{COOH} \quad \Delta H_{298} = -137.9 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ This is a **highly exothermic, atom-economical** reaction (100% atom efficiency). Operating conditions: **150–200 °C**, **30–60 bar CO pressure**, Rh catalyst (~300–1000 ppm), CH₃I promoter (~7–14 wt%). ### 2.2.2 The Catalytic Cycle (Koch-Haaf Cycle) The reaction proceeds via **five key elementary steps** catalysed by the square-planar complex **[Rh(CO)₂I₂]⁻**: **Step 1 — Oxidative Addition of CH₃I:** $$[\text{Rh(CO)}_2\text{I}_2]^- + \text{CH}_3\text{I} \longrightarrow [\text{CH}_3\text{Rh(CO)}_2\text{I}_3]^-$$ **Step 2 — CO Insertion (Migratory Insertion):** $$[\text{CH}_3\text{Rh(CO)}_2\text{I}_3]^- \longrightarrow [\text{CH}_3\text{CO-Rh(CO)I}_3]^-$$ **Step 3 — Reductive Elimination of Acetyl Iodide:** $$[\text{CH}_3\text{CO-Rh(CO)I}_3]^- \longrightarrow [\text{Rh(CO)I}_2]^- + \text{CH}_3\text{COI}$$ **Step 4 — Hydrolysis of Acetyl Iodide:** $$\text{CH}_3\text{COI} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{COOH} + \text{HI}$$ **Step 5 — HI Reduction with Methanol (regenerates promoter):** $$\text{CH}_3\text{OH} + \text{HI} \longrightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{I} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$ > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic for the cycle:** **"O-I-H-R"** — **O**xidative addition, **I**nsertion (CO), **H**ydrolysis, **R**eduction of HI. The cycle is closed: Rh is regenerated, CH₃I is regenerated. ## 2.3 The Cativa Process (BP — Successor to Monsanto) The **Cativa process** (commercialised by BP in 1995) replaces rhodium with **iridium** as the active metal. The catalytic cycle is similar, but with key thermodynamic differences: | Parameter | Monsanto (Rh) | Cativa (Ir) | |---|---|---| | Operating Temperature | 150–200 °C | 150–250 °C (higher) | | Operating Pressure | 30–60 bar | 15–40 bar (lower) | | Water Requirement | High (14–15 wt%) | Low (<8 wt%) | | Catalyst Cost | Very High (Rh > Ir) | Lower | | By-products (propionic acid) | ~500–1000 ppm | ~50–200 ppm | | CO Efficiency | Lower | Higher (stable complex) | > [!NOTE] > **Why the shift to Ir?** Iridium forms a more stable **[Ir(CO)₂I₂]⁻ → [Ir(CO)₃I]** system and is less prone to deactivation by precipitation of RhI₃. The Cativa process also uses a **ruthenium iodide (RuI₃ or [Ru(CO)₃I₃]⁻)** co-catalyst as a **promoter** to enhance the rate of oxidative addition of CH₃I to Ir(I). ## 2.4 The Wacker–Acetaldehyde Oxidation Route (Historical) $$\text{C}_2\text{H}_4 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{PdCl}_2/\text{CuCl}_2} \text{CH}_3\text{CHO}$$ $$2\text{CH}_3\text{CHO} + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Mn(OAc)}_2,\, 50-80\,°C} 2\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$$ This two-step route was dominant before 1970 and is now mainly used in regions where **ethylene is cheaper than methanol + CO**. ## 2.5 The Butane / Naphtha Oxidation Route (Single-Step) $$\text{C}_4\text{H}_{10} + \tfrac{5}{2}\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 2\text{CH}_3\text{COOH} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$ Operating at **150–225 °C** and **55 bar** with **Mn(II)/Co(II) naphthenate** catalysts. Co-products include formic acid, propionic acid, and methyl ethyl ketone. The selectivity to acetic acid is only **~60–70%**, with CO₂ as a major by-product (~25%). ## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula & Engineering Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Relation | Application | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Selectivity (S) = (Moles of desired product) ÷ (Moles of reactant consumed) | Catalyst performance | % | | 2 | Conversion (X) = (Moles reacted) ÷ (Moles fed) | Reactor efficiency | % | | 3 | Yield (Y) = S × X | Overall process efficiency | % | | 4 | Space-Time Yield (STY) = (Mass of product) ÷ (Reactor volume × time) | Reactor sizing | kg·L⁻¹·h⁻¹ | | 5 | Atom Economy (AE) = (MW of desired product) ÷ (Σ MW of all reactants) × 100 | Green chemistry metric | % | | 6 | ΔHrxn = Σ ΔHf(products) − Σ ΔHf(reactants) | Heat of carbonylation | kJ/mol | | 7 | Acid Strength: pKa = −log₁₀(Ka) | Acetic acid: pKa = 4.76 → Ka = 1.74 × 10⁻⁵ | — | | 8 | Carbonylation rate: r = k · p(CO) · [CH₃OH] · [cat] | Monsanto rate law (1st order in CO, MeOH) | mol·L⁻¹·s⁻¹ | | 9 | Annual Production = STY × V_reactor × t_operation × 8000 h/yr | Plant capacity | tonnes/yr | | 10 | CO partial pressure: pCO = yCO × P_total | Reactor design | bar | > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL LaTeX/Table Rule:** Whenever you need to write absolute value or modulus in any KTU answer sheet, use the `$\vert$` or `$\mid$` command (e.g., $|\Delta H|$ → `$\vert \Delta H \vert$`) — never the bare vertical pipe `|`, as it will break the markdown table in the digital answer sheet. ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Plant Capacity Benchmark:** A single world-scale methanol carbonylation train produces **~500,000 – 1,000,000 tonnes/year** of acetic acid. - **Energy Integration:** The exothermicity (−137.9 kJ/mol) is recovered as low-pressure steam (4–10 bar) to drive the distillation columns — an excellent example of **process integration / pinch analysis** taught in Chemical Engineering Thermodynamics. - **Corrosion Engineering:** Acetic acid + trace water + HI → forms **hydroxyacetic acid + HI** which is **highly corrosive**. Therefore, reactors use **Hastelloy C-276** or **zirconium-clad** vessels, and distillation columns use **type 316L stainless steel** with a **molybdenum content ≥2.5%** (also called **"Alloy 20"** or **"Carpenter 20"**). # 📐 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Thermodynamics of Methanol Carbonylation We will derive the **standard enthalpy change (ΔH°ᵣₓₙ)** for the Monsanto reaction from standard heats of formation. ### Given Data (Standard Heats of Formation at 298 K) | Species | ΔH°f (kJ/mol) | Phase | |---|---|---| | CH₃OH (methanol) | −201.5 | g | | CO (carbon monoxide) | −110.5 | g | | CH₃COOH (acetic acid) | −432.8 | l | | H₂O (water) | −241.8 | l | ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1:** Write the balanced reaction with phases. $$\text{CH}_3\text{OH(g)} + \text{CO(g)} \longrightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{COOH(l)} \quad \Delta H_{rxn}^\circ = ?$$ **Step 2:** Apply Hess's Law: $$\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ = \sum \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{products}) - \sum \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{reactants})$$ **Step 3:** Substitute values — products side: $$\sum \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{products}) = \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{CH}_3\text{COOH, l}) = -432.8 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ **Step 4:** Substitute values — reactants side: $$\sum \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{reactants}) = \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{CH}_3\text{OH, g}) + \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{CO, g})$$ $$= (-201.5) + (-110.5) = -312.0 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ **Step 5:** Compute final ΔH°ᵣₓₙ: $$\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ = (-432.8) - (-312.0) = -432.8 + 312.0 = -120.8 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ > [!NOTE] > Note: The textbook value of **−137.9 kJ/mol** uses different reference-state data (often liquid methanol, not gaseous). For KTU exam purposes, both **−120.8 to −137.9 kJ/mol** are acceptable, provided the student clearly states the reference phases. ## 3.2 Numerical Problem (Worked Example) — A KTU-Style Calculation > **Question:** A Monsanto acetic acid plant produces **750 tonnes/day** of glacial acetic acid. The reactor operates at 180 °C, 50 bar, with a methanol single-pass conversion of 95% and selectivity of 99% to acetic acid. Calculate: > (a) The daily feed of methanol (in tonnes) required. > (b) The **space-time yield (STY)** if the reactor volume is 95 m³. > (c) The CO consumption at STP (Nm³/day), assuming 5% CO is unreacted. **Given:** - Production = 750 t/day of CH₃COOH - MW (CH₃OH) = 32.04 g/mol - MW (CH₃COOH) = 60.05 g/mol - MW (CO) = 28.01 g/mol - 1 mole of gas at STP = 22.414 L = 0.022414 Nm³ ### (a) Daily Methanol Feed **Step 1:** Calculate moles of acetic acid produced per day. $$n_{\text{AA}} = \frac{750 \times 10^6 \text{ g/day}}{60.05 \text{ g/mol}} = 1.249 \times 10^7 \text{ mol/day}$$ **Step 2:** By stoichiometry, 1 mol CH₃OH → 1 mol CH₃COOH. So: $$n_{\text{MeOH, reacted}} = \frac{n_{\text{AA}}}{\text{Selectivity}} = \frac{1.249 \times 10^7}{0.99} = 1.2616 \times 10^7 \text{ mol/day}$$ **Step 3:** Account for 95% conversion (5% unreacted methanol leaves the reactor and is recycled — but for *fresh feed*, we use): $$n_{\text{MeOH, feed}} = \frac{n_{\text{MeOH, reacted}}}{\text{Conversion}} = \frac{1.2616 \times 10^7}{0.95} = 1.3280 \times 10^7 \text{ mol/day}$$ **Step 4:** Convert to mass: $$m_{\text{MeOH}} = 1.3280 \times 10^7 \text{ mol} \times 32.04 \text{ g/mol} = 4.255 \times 10^8 \text{ g} = 425.5 \text{ tonnes/day}$$ ### (b) Space-Time Yield (STY) $$\text{STY} = \frac{\text{Mass of product per day}}{\text{Reactor volume} \times 24 \text{ h}} = \frac{750{,}000 \text{ kg}}{95 \text{ m}^3 \times 24 \text{ h}}$$ $$\text{STY} = \frac{750{,}000}{2280} = 328.95 \text{ kg/(m}^3 \cdot \text{h)} \approx 329 \text{ kg}\cdot\text{m}^{-3}\cdot\text{h}^{-1}$$ ### (c) CO Consumption at STP **Step 1:** Moles of CO reacted per day (1:1 stoichiometry with MeOH reacted): $$n_{\text{CO, reacted}} = 1.2616 \times 10^7 \text{ mol/day}$$ **Step 2:** Fresh CO feed (5% unreacted in tail gas, so 95% reacts): $$n_{\text{CO, feed}} = \frac{1.2616 \times 10^7}{0.95} = 1.3280 \times 10^7 \text{ mol/day}$$ **Step 3:** Convert to volume at STP: $$V_{\text{CO}} = 1.3280 \times 10^7 \text{ mol} \times 0.022414 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{mol} = 2.977 \times 10^5 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{day}$$ $$\boxed{V_{\text{CO}} \approx 2.98 \times 10^5 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{day} \; (\text{or } 297{,}700 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{day})}$$ > [!TIP] > **Engineering Sanity Check:** This is a **large but realistic** number — a world-class acetic acid plant uses **~300,000 Nm³/day** of CO, which is typically generated on-site from partial oxidation of natural gas or imported from a syngas complex. ## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Process | Decision Criterion | Best Choice | Reasoning | |---|---|---| | Lowest catalyst cost | Cativa (Ir) | Ir price ~⅓ of Rh (highly volatile) | | Lowest pressure equipment CAPEX | Cativa (15–40 bar) | Lower pressure = thinner vessel walls | | Lowest water usage | Cativa (<8%) | Saves steam in dehydration column | | Highest catalyst activity at low T | Monsanto (Rh) | Faster kinetics at 150 °C | | Cheapest feedstock region (N. America, ME) | Butane oxidation | Abundant C₄ LPG | | Renewable / bio-based product market | Ethanol oxidation (fermentation) | "Bio-attributed" acetic acid, premium pricing | | Maximum product purity (>99.95%) | Cativa + reactive distillation | Fewer heavy by-products | # 🏗️ Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Monsanto Process Plant ```mermaid flowchart TB A[CH3OH Feed Tank] --> B[CO Feed Compressor] B --> C[Reactor Feed Preheater] A --> C C --> D[Stirred-Tank Carbonylation Reactor
180 C, 50 bar, Rh-CH3I Catalyst] D --> E[Flash Drum
Pressure Let-down to 1.5 bar] E --> F[Light Ends Distillation Column
Removes CH3I and HI] F --> G[Dehydration Distillation Column
Removes Water and Formic Acid] G --> H[Heavy Ends Column
Removes Propionic Acid and Residue] H --> I[Glacial Acetic Acid Storage
Purity 99.85 percent] I --> J[Shipping - ISO Tankers or Drums] E --> K[CO Recycle Scrubber
Vent Absorber with Methanol] K --> A F --> L[CH3I Recovery Distillation] L --> D G --> M[Wastewater Treatment Bioreactor] H --> N[Heavy Residue Incinerator
Energy Recovery] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation:** The reactor (node D) is the heart of the plant. Three recycle streams (CO, CH₃I, water) are returned from downstream columns to maximise atom economy and minimise fresh feed — this is a classic example of **mass-integration / recycle-network synthesis** (El-Halwagi, 1997). ## 4.2 Catalytic Cycle Schematic — Koch-Haaf Cycle (Rhodium) ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Rh I 2 CO 2 minus] --> A2[CH3Rh I 3 CO 2 minus] A2 --> A3[CH3CO-Rh I 3 CO minus] A3 --> A4[Rh I 2 CO 2 minus] A4 --> A1 A3 --> B1[CH3COI Acetyl Iodide] B1 --> B2[CH3COOH + HI] B2 --> B3[CH3I + H2O via Methanol] B3 --> A1 C1[CH3OH] -.feed.-> B3 C2[CO] -.feed.-> A2 C3[H2O] -.feed.-> B1 style A1 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000 style A2 fill:#ffcc80,stroke:#e65100,color:#000 style A3 fill:#ffb74d,stroke:#e65100,color:#000 style A4 fill:#ffa726,stroke:#e65100,color:#000 style B1 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 style B2 fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 style B3 fill:#81c784,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 ``` > [!TIP] > **How to read the cycle:** The orange loop (top) is the **Rhodium organometallic cycle** (catalytic). The green loop (right) is the **promoter cycle** (CH₃I/IO⁻). Both loops are coupled — this is why the rate of CH₃I oxidative addition is the **rate-determining step (RDS)** of the entire process. ## 4.3 Downstream Derivatives — "Acetic Acid Value Chain" ```mermaid flowchart TD AA[Acetic Acid CH3COOH] --> VAM[Vinyl Acetate Monomer
via Oxidative Coupling with C2H4 + O2] AA --> PTA[Purified Terephthalic Acid
via Air Oxidation of p-Xylene] AA --> AAE[Acetic Anhydride
via Ketene Route or Carbonylation] AA --> EST[Ethyl Acetate
via Fischer Esterification with Ethanol] AA --> MCA[Monochloroacetic Acid
via Chlorination with Cl2] AA --> DMAc[Dimethylacetamide
via Reaction with DMA] VAM --> V1[PVA, EVA, Paints, Adhesives] PTA --> P1[PET Bottles, Polyester Fibre] AAE --> A1[Cellulose Acetate, Aspirin] EST --> E1[Printing Inks, Pharma Solvent] MCA --> M1[Herbicides 2,4-D and MCPA, CMC] DMAc --> D1[Pharma Solvent, Spandex] ``` # 📝 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Paper] **List any three major industrial applications of acetic acid.** **Model Answer:** 1. Manufacture of **Vinyl Acetate Monomer (VAM)** — for PVA emulsions used in paints, adhesives, and textile sizing. 2. Production of **Purified Terephthalic Acid (PTA)** — via the Amoco/Mid-Century air-oxidation of p-xylene; PTA feeds PET polymerisation for bottles and polyester fibre. 3. Synthesis of **acetate esters** (ethyl acetate, n-butyl acetate) used as solvents in printing inks, pharmaceutical extraction, and coatings. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Supplementary] **What is meant by "glacial acetic acid"? Why is it so called?** **Model Answer:** Glacial acetic acid is the **water-free (anhydrous) commercial grade of acetic acid** with a minimum purity of **99.85 wt%**. It is called "glacial" because, at temperatures below **16.6 °C**, the pure liquid solidifies into **colourless, ice-like crystalline flakes** — resembling a glacier. The term originates from the Latin *glacies* (ice) and was adopted in 19th-century German chemistry. The freezing point purity test (16.6 °C) is a quick QC method to verify water content — even **0.1 wt% water** depresses the freezing point by ~0.2 °C. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer (14 Marks) with Internal Choice ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Jun 2024] **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Monsanto process** for the manufacture of acetic acid with a neat flow-sheet and the **catalytic cycle**. State the operating conditions and the role of methyl iodide. **(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the **Monsanto** and **Cativa** processes in terms of catalyst, operating conditions, water requirement, by-products, and cost. Under what circumstances would you recommend a switch from Rh to Ir catalyst? --- #### Model Solution (a): **Introduction:** The Monsanto process, commercialised in 1970, is a **homogeneous liquid-phase carbonylation of methanol** with carbon monoxide using a **rhodium-based catalyst** and **methyl iodide (CH₃I) as a promoter**. It accounts for over 50% of global acetic acid production. **Main Reaction:** $$\text{CH}_3\text{OH} + \text{CO} \xrightarrow{\text{Rh/I}^-,\,180\,°C,\,50\text{ bar}} \text{CH}_3\text{COOH} \quad \Delta H = -137.9 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ **Operating Conditions:** | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | Temperature | 150–200 °C (optimum 180 °C) | | Pressure | 30–60 bar (optimum 50 bar) | | Catalyst | Rhodium (200–1000 ppm) as [Rh(CO)₂I₂]⁻ | | Promoter | CH₃I (7–14 wt% in reactor) | | Water | 14–15 wt% in reactor | | Reactor | Stainless steel 316L / Hastelloy C-276, stirred tank | **Role of CH₃I (Methyl Iodide):** 1. Acts as a **co-catalyst / promoter** that undergoes **oxidative addition** to the active Rh(I) species to form the Rh(III) methyl complex. 2. Is regenerated in situ by the reaction of **HI + CH₃OH → CH₃I + H₂O** (Step 5 of the cycle). 3. Speeds up the carbonylation rate by **2–3 orders of magnitude** vs. catalysis without it. **Catalytic Cycle (5 steps):** (Explain as in Section 2.2.2 of these notes.) **Process Description:** Liquid methanol + CO are fed continuously to the stirred-tank reactor. The exothermic heat is removed via an external jacket generating 4-bar steam. The reactor effluent is depressurised in a **flash drum**; the vapour (mostly CO, CH₃I, light gases) is scrubbed and recycled. The liquid goes to a **light-ends column** (recovers CH₃I overhead), then a **dehydration column** (removes water azeotropically), then a **heavy-ends column** (removes propionic acid). The bottoms product is **glacial acetic acid (99.85% purity)**. **Conclusion:** The Monsanto process offers >99% selectivity, ~95% MeOH conversion per pass, and STYs of 300–400 kg·m⁻³·h⁻¹ — making it the benchmark technology for acetic acid manufacture. --- #### Model Solution (b): | Parameter | Monsanto (Rh) | Cativa (Ir) | |---|---|---| | Active metal | Rhodium | Iridium (with Ru promoter) | | Optimum temperature | 180 °C | 200–220 °C | | Optimum pressure | 50 bar | 30–35 bar | | Water in reactor | 14–15 wt% | <8 wt% (often 5%) | | CO efficiency | Moderate | Higher (lower CO/AA ratio) | | Propionic acid by-product | 500–1000 ppm | 50–200 ppm | | Catalyst cost | Very high (Rh > USD 15,000/oz) | ~40% lower (Ir) | | Catalyst stability | Prone to RhI₃ precipitation at low pCO | Very stable | | Equipment CAPEX | Higher (thicker walls) | Lower (lower pressure) | | Energy for water removal | High | Low (less water in feed) | **When to switch from Rh to Ir:** 1. When **CO supply pressure** is limited (Cativa runs at lower CO partial pressure). 2. When **water** is expensive to remove (lower water = smaller dehydration column). 3. When **catalyst market** makes Rh prohibitively expensive (Ir is more stable in price). 4. When the operator wants **higher acetic acid purity** with less propionic acid (important for PTA-grade AA). 5. For **new grass-root plants** in regions with low-cost Ir supply. **Conclusion:** For new plants post-2000, Cativa is generally preferred unless the site has a ready supply of cheap Rh recovery infrastructure. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Alternative Choice] **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Wacker–Acetaldehyde oxidation route** for acetic acid manufacture. Write the reactions, conditions, and advantages/disadvantages. **(b) [7 Marks]** A plant produces **1000 kg/h** of acetic acid by butane oxidation. The butane feed is **900 kg/h** at 95% purity (rest is inert propane). The selectivity to acetic acid is **65%** on a butane basis. Calculate (i) the **conversion of butane**, (ii) the **overall yield**, and (iii) the **atom economy**. --- #### Model Solution (a): **Introduction:** The Wacker process oxidises ethylene to acetaldehyde, which is then further oxidised to acetic acid. This was the dominant route before 1970 and is still used in India and China where ethylene is abundant. **Reactions:** **Step 1 — Wacker Oxidation of Ethylene:** $$\text{C}_2\text{H}_4 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{PdCl}_2/\text{CuCl}_2,\, 120\,°C,\, 3\text{ bar}} \text{CH}_3\text{CHO}$$ (Mechanism: Pd(II) is the actual oxidant; Cu(II) re-oxidises Pd(0) back to Pd(II); O₂ re-oxidises Cu(0) → Cu(II).) **Step 2 — Acetaldehyde Oxidation:** $$2\text{CH}_3\text{CHO} + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Mn(OAc)}_2,\, 50\text{–}80\,°C} 2\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$$ **Operating Conditions:** Reactor 1: 120 °C, 3 bar, aqueous PdCl₂-CuCl₂; Reactor 2: 50–80 °C, 1–5 bar, manganese(II) acetate catalyst, O₂ or air. **Advantages:** 1. Uses cheap, abundant ethylene. 2. Mild temperature and pressure → low CAPEX. 3. Acetaldehyde is also a valuable co-product (can be sold separately). **Disadvantages:** 1. **Multi-step** → higher OPEX and complexity. 2. PdCl₂ catalyst is expensive and prone to **Pd black** precipitation (deactivation). 3. Acetaldehyde is **highly toxic and carcinogenic** → handling and safety are critical. 4. Corrosive chlorides require **titanium or glass-lined** reactors. 5. **Atom economy** for the two-step route = 100%, but the net carbon efficiency is lower than Monsanto due to side products (CO₂, chlorinated by-products). **Conclusion:** The Wacker–acetaldehyde route is **technically obsolete for new plants** but still operates in legacy units in regions with cheap ethylene. --- #### Model Solution (b): **Given:** - AA production rate = 1000 kg/h - Butane feed (95% pure) = 900 kg/h → butane mass flow = 0.95 × 900 = **855 kg/h** - Propane (inert) = 45 kg/h - Selectivity to AA on butane basis = 65% = 0.65 - MW of n-butane (C₄H₁₀) = 58.12 g/mol - MW of AA (CH₃COOH) = 60.05 g/mol **Main reaction:** $$\text{C}_4\text{H}_{10} + \tfrac{5}{2}\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 2\text{CH}_3\text{COOH} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$ **Stoichiometry:** 1 mol C₄H₁₀ → 2 mol CH₃COOH **Molar masses:** n-butane = 58.12 g/mol; AA = 60.05 g/mol - Mass of AA from 1 mol butane = 2 × 60.05 = 120.10 g - Mass of butane consumed per mol AA produced = 58.12 / 2 = 29.06 g --- **(i) Conversion of Butane:** **Step 1:** Mass of AA produced per hour = 1000 kg/h. **Step 2:** Theoretical mass of butane required for 100% selectivity: $$m_{\text{butane, theo}} = \frac{1000 \text{ kg}}{120.10/58.12} = \frac{1000 \times 58.12}{120.10} = 483.93 \text{ kg/h}$$ **Step 3:** Actual mass of butane reacted (using 65% selectivity): $$m_{\text{butane, reacted}} = \frac{m_{\text{butane, theo}}}{0.65} = \frac{483.93}{0.65} = 744.5 \text{ kg/h}$$ **Step 4:** Conversion: $$X_{\text{butane}} = \frac{m_{\text{butane, reacted}}}{m_{\text{butane, fed}}} = \frac{744.5}{855} = 0.8707 = 87.07\%$$ $$\boxed{X_{\text{butane}} \approx 87.1\%}$$ --- **(ii) Overall Yield:** $$\text{Yield} = \text{Selectivity} \times \text{Conversion} = 0.65 \times 0.8707 = 0.5660 = 56.6\%$$ $$\boxed{Y_{\text{overall}} \approx 56.6\%}$$ --- **(iii) Atom Economy:** **Step 1:** Total mass of reactants (per the balanced reaction): $$M_{\text{reactants}} = 58.12 \text{ (C}_4\text{H}_{10}) + \tfrac{5}{2} \times 32.00 \text{ (O}_2) = 58.12 + 80.00 = 138.12 \text{ g}$$ **Step 2:** Mass of desired product (acetic acid) per stoichiometric cycle = 2 × 60.05 = 120.10 g **Step 3:** $$\text{AE} = \frac{120.10}{138.12} \times 100 = 86.96\%$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistake Alert:** > Many students confuse **selectivity**, **conversion**, and **yield**. If the question says "selectivity is 65% on a butane basis," it means **65% of the butane that REACTED** went to AA, not 65% of the butane fed. So your formula must be: **Yield = (Selectivity) × (Conversion)** — apply this only after correctly identifying what 65% refers to. **Losing 2–3 marks** for this confusion is extremely common. Also remember to use **molar masses** (g/mol) and convert kg to consistent units. Always show a **unit-cancellation table** in your KTU answer sheet for full marks. $$\boxed{\text{AE} \approx 87.0\%}$$ --- ## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **High-Yield Revision Bullets — Pin this for the night before your KTU exam!** - ✅ Acetic acid = **CH₃COOH** (ethanoic acid), MW **60.05 g/mol**, BP **118.1 °C**, pKa **4.76**. - ✅ Glacial AA = anhydrous grade (≥99.85%), freezes at **16.6 °C**. - ✅ Largest single use = **Vinyl Acetate Monomer (VAM)**. - ✅ **Monsanto process** = **CH₃OH + CO → CH₃COOH** (Rh catalyst, CH₃I promoter, 180 °C, 50 bar) — most important. - ✅ **Cativa process** (BP) uses **Ir + Ru promoter**, lower pressure, lower water, lower propionic acid by-product. - ✅ Catalytic cycle (Monsanto) = **5 steps**: oxidative addition → CO insertion → reductive elimination → hydrolysis → HI reduction. Mnemonic: **"O-I-H-R"**. - ✅ Wacker route: **C₂H₄ → CH₃CHO → CH₃COOH**, uses **PdCl₂/CuCl₂**, is being phased out. - ✅ Butane oxidation: **C₄H₁₀ + 5/2 O₂ → 2 CH₃COOH + H₂O**, selectivity only ~60–70%. - ✅ **Yield = Selectivity × Conversion** — do not mix up definitions! - ✅ Atom Economy of Monsanto = **100%** (all atoms in products). - ✅ Exothermicity ΔH ≈ **−138 kJ/mol** → recovered as 4–10 bar LP steam (process integration). - ✅ Reactor material: **Hastelloy C-276** or **zirconium-clad** vessels (resists HI corrosion). - ✅ Global production > **18 million tonnes/year** (2024). - ✅ Major Indian producer: **GNFC, Bharuch, Gujarat** (Monsanto technology, 200,000 TPA). - ✅ For KTU derivations, always show **Hess's Law step** with reference phases (g/l). - ✅ **KTU Board Favourite Topics:** Monsanto cycle, Cativa vs Monsanto comparison, butane oxidation numerical, atom economy calculation.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT741 - PETROCHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Acetylene

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Acetylene** (IUPAC name: **Ethyne**, molecular formula **C₂H₂**) is the simplest and most industrially significant member of the **alkyne** family of unsaturated aliphatic hydrocarbons. Its structure is characterized by a **carbon–carbon triple bond** (one sigma bond and two pi bonds), which makes it the most thermodynamically unstable (endothermic) of the common hydrocarbons.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Acetylene is classified as a *petrochemical building block* because it sits at the apex of the **C₂-chemistry** value chain. Almost all major acetylene derivatives (vinyl chloride monomer, acrylonitrile, vinyl acetate, acetic acid, 1,4-butanediol, neoprene) are produced via addition reactions across the C≡C triple bond.

**Key Physical Constants (Standard State, 25 °C, 1 atm):**

| Property | Value | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Molecular Weight | **26.04 g/mol** | Lowest among stable hydrocarbons |
| Boiling Point | **−84 °C** (sublimes at 1 atm) | Must be stored dissolved or refrigerated |
| Density (gas) | **1.097 kg/m³** at STP | Lighter than air |
| Heat of Formation (ΔH_f°) | **+226.7 kJ/mol** | Strongly endothermic → explosive decomposition tendency |
| Flame Temperature (in O₂) | **~3148 °C** | Highest flame temperature of any common fuel gas |
| Auto-Ignition Temperature | **305 °C** | Extremely flammable |
| Explosive Limits in Air | **2.5 % – 81 % (v/v)** | The widest flammable range of any gas |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Spring-Loaded Pencil":**
> Think of acetylene's C≡C triple bond as a **highly compressed spring**. The molecule is packed with chemical energy (positive heat of formation). The moment you provide a small "nudge" (heat, spark, or shock), the spring violently releases this stored energy and snaps back to its most stable form — **CO₂ + H₂O + a tremendous amount of heat**. This is why a tiny cylinder of acetylene can cut through thick steel: the released energy is enormous and concentrated.
>
> The **double bonds** in ethylene (C=C) are like a partially compressed spring, while the **single bonds** in ethane (C–C) are like a relaxed spring. Acetylene = fully compressed, ethane = fully relaxed.

## 1.3 Bond Structure & Hybridization

The two carbon atoms in acetylene are **sp-hybridized**. Each carbon forms:
- **1 sp–sp sigma (σ) bond** (head-on overlap along the internuclear axis)
- **2 sp–(2p) pi (π) bonds** (lateral overlap of unhybridized 2p orbitals)
- **1 C–H sigma bond** using the remaining sp orbital and the 1s of hydrogen

The bond length of C≡C is **1.20 Å** (shortest carbon–carbon bond), and the bond dissociation energy is **~965 kJ/mol**.

```
H–C≡C–H   (linear geometry, bond angle = 180°)
```

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Acetylene is a Petrochemical Cornerstone

Acetylene's industrial importance stems from three unique properties:

1. **Endothermic Nature** — Because ΔH_f° is positive (+226.7 kJ/mol), decomposition to its elements releases enormous energy:
$$2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_2 \rightarrow 2\,\text{C}_{(s)} + \text{H}_{2(g)} \quad \Delta H = -453.4 \text{ kJ/mol}$$
This makes it a **high-energy fuel gas** used in oxy-acetylene welding and cutting.

2. **High Reactivity of the Triple Bond** — The π-electrons are loosely held and readily undergo **electrophilic addition, nucleophilic addition, polymerization, and cyclization** (e.g., Reppe chemistry, Diels–Alder reactions).

3. **Versatile Platform Molecule** — A single C₂ unit can be built into virtually the entire spectrum of C₂, C₄, C₆, C₈... petrochemicals.

## 2.2 Industrial Production Routes — Master Classification

| Category | Process | Feedstock | Key Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Carbide Route** | Calcium Carbide Process | CaO + C (coke) | Electric arc, ~2000 °C |
| **Thermal Cracking** | Wulff Process | C₂H₆ / C₃H₈ | Cyclic, 1200 °C, regenerative |
| **Partial Oxidation** | BASF / Sachsse Process | CH₄ (+ O₂) | 1500 °C, sub-atmospheric, ~0.05 s |
| **Partial Combustion** | Hoechst (HTP / Flame Crack) | CH₄ / C₃H₈ / naphtha | 1500 °C, premixed flame, <10 ms |
| **Plasma / Electric** | Plasma Arc / Huels Arc | CH₄ / hydrocarbons | 2000–3000 °C plasma jet |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Modern acetylene manufacture has shifted decisively from the **calcium-carbide route** to **partial-oxidation of natural gas (methane)**, because the carbide route consumes ~10 MWh of electricity per ton of acetylene and produces enormous Ca(OH)₂ waste.

The table below collects every equation you need for acetylene problems in ESE/university exams.

| 2 | $\text{CaO} + 3\,\text{C} \rightarrow \text{CaC}_2 + \text{CO}$ | Endothermic, ΔH ≈ +1110 kJ/mol |
| 3 | $2\,\text{CH}_4 \rightarrow \text{C}_2\text{H}_2 + 3\,\text{H}_2$ | ΔH ≈ +376 kJ/mol (highly endothermic) |
| 4 | $2\,\text{CH}_4 + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{C}_2\text{H}_2 + 3\,\text{H}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$ | Partial oxidation (BASF) |

| 15 | $3\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{C}_6\text{H}_6$ | Trimerization (Berthelot) |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Welding & Metal Cutting:** Oxy-acetylene torches reach the **highest practical flame temperature** of any fuel combination (≈ 3148 °C with pure O₂), enabling fusion of steel, cast iron, and non-ferrous metals without electric arc.
- **C₂-Chemical Platform:** ~70 % of the world's acetylene is consumed in the manufacture of **vinyl chloride (PVC)**, **acrylonitrile (ABS/SAN fibres)**, and **vinyl acetate (PVA adhesives & paints)**.
- **Specialty / Reppe Chemistry:** Catalytic reactions of acetylene with aldehydes, alcohols, CO, and amines produce **1,4-butanediol, tetrahydrofuran, propargyl alcohol, and acrylic esters** — the basis of BASF, GAF, and DuPont specialty lines.
- **Atomic-Absorption Spectroscopy:** Acetylene is the standard fuel gas in flame-AAS for trace metal analysis (Fe, Mn, Cr, Cu, Zn).
- **Rocket & Missile Propellants:** Acetylene derivatives (e.g., vinyl acetylene, polyacetylene) are precursors to advanced energetic materials.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Theoretical Minimum Air for Combustion

We need to find the **stoichiometric volume of air** required to completely combust 1 m³ of pure acetylene at NTP (0 °C, 1 atm).

**Step 1 — Write the balanced combustion equation.**

$$2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_2 + 5\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 4\,\text{CO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the molar ratio of C₂H₂ to O₂.**

$$\frac{n_{\text{O}_2}}{n_{\text{C}_2\text{H}_2}} = \frac{5}{2} = 2.5$$

So, 1 mol (or 1 vol) of C₂H₂ requires 2.5 mol (or 2.5 vol) of O₂.

**Step 3 — Convert O₂ requirement to air requirement.**

Air is approximately 21 % O₂ by volume. Therefore, the volume of air required is:

$$\dot{V}_{\text{air}} = \frac{\dot{V}_{\text{O}_2}}{0.21} = \frac{2.5\,\dot{V}_{\text{C}_2\text{H}_2}}{0.21}$$

For 1 m³ of C₂H₂:

$$\dot{V}_{\text{air}} = \frac{2.5 \times 1}{0.21} = 11.905 \text{ m}^3 \text{ of air per m}^3 \text{ of C}_2\text{H}_2$$

**Step 4 — Express as an air-to-fuel ratio (by volume).**

$$AF_{\text{vol}} = 11.905 : 1 \quad (\text{or approximately } 12:1)$$

**Step 5 — Mass basis (if required, for energy calculations).**

$$AF_{\text{mass}} = \frac{11.905 \times 1.293}{1.097} = 14.03 \text{ kg air / kg C}_2\text{H}_2$$

where 1.293 kg/m³ is the density of air at STP and 1.097 kg/m³ is the density of acetylene at STP.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Thermodynamic Check on Partial Oxidation of Methane

We want to verify that partial oxidation of methane to acetylene is *thermoneutral* (or only mildly endothermic), explaining why it is the process of choice industrially.

**Step 1 — Write the partial-oxidation equation.**

$$2\,\text{CH}_4 + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{C}_2\text{H}_2 + 3\,\text{H}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

Actually, the *operational* partial-oxidation equation (net of added O₂ providing the endothermic heat) is usually written as:

$$2\,\text{CH}_4 \rightarrow \text{C}_2\text{H}_2 + 3\,\text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H_1 = +376 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Step 2 — Write the combustion (heat-supply) reaction.**

$$\text{CH}_4 + 2\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \Delta H_2 = -891 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Step 3 — Combine via Hess's law.**

Let `x` be the moles of CH₄ burned to supply the heat. The energy balance requires:

$$x \cdot (-\Delta H_2) = \Delta H_1$$

$$x = \frac{376}{891} = 0.422 \text{ mol CH}_4 \text{ burned per 2 mol CH}_4 \text{ cracked}$$

Equivalent O₂ required:

$$n_{\text{O}_2} = 0.422 \times 2 = 0.844 \text{ mol O}_2 \text{ per 2 mol CH}_4$$

$$\frac{n_{\text{O}_2}}{n_{\text{CH}_4}} = 0.422$$

**Step 4 — Net (practical) equation.**

$$(2 + 0.422)\,\text{CH}_4 + 0.844\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 0.422\,\text{CO}_2 + 0.844\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{C}_2\text{H}_2 + 3\,\text{H}_2$$

Per mole of acetylene produced, **~2.42 mol CH₄ + 0.84 mol O₂** are consumed — confirming the operation is *autothermal* (self-sustaining) at the optimum.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Yield of Acetylene in Calcium Carbide Process

**Step 1 — Carbide formation from lime and coke.**

$$\text{CaO} + 3\,\text{C} \rightarrow \text{CaC}_2 + \text{CO} \uparrow$$

**Step 2 — Acetylene generation in water.**

$$\text{CaC}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{C}_2\text{H}_2 \uparrow + \text{Ca(OH)}_2$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical acetylene yield per ton of CaC₂.**

$$M_{\text{CaC}_2} = 40 + 12 + 2(16) = 64 \text{ g/mol}$$

$$M_{\text{C}_2\text{H}_2} = 2(12) + 2(1) = 26 \text{ g/mol}$$

$$\text{Mass yield} = \frac{26}{64} = 0.40625 \text{ kg C}_2\text{H}_2 \text{ per kg CaC}_2$$

**Step 4 — Per ton of pure CaC₂.**

$$m_{\text{C}_2\text{H}_2} = 0.40625 \times 1000 = 406.25 \text{ kg} \approx 0.406 \text{ ton}$$

**Step 5 — Volumetric yield at NTP (molar volume = 22.414 m³/kmol).**

$$V_{\text{C}_2\text{H}_2} = \frac{1000 \text{ kg}}{64 \text{ kg/kmol}} \times 22.414 \text{ m}^3/\text{kmol} = 350.2 \text{ m}^3 \text{ per ton CaC}_2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial reality:** Commercial calcium carbide is only ~80–85 % pure, so practical yields drop to **~280–300 m³ C₂H₂ per ton of technical-grade CaC₂**.

## 4.4 Reaction Network — Why Quenching is Critical

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    C2H6[C2H6] --> RX
    RX --> C2H4[Ethylene C2H4]

    RX --> C6H6[Benzene and Higher]
    C2H4 --> C2H2
    C2H2 --> CC[Carbon and H2 Decomposition Undesired]

    C2H2 --> Q[Quench to 200 C within 10 ms]
    C2H4 --> Q
    C6H6 --> Q
    CC -.blocked.-> Q
    POL -.blocked.-> Q
    Q --> SEP[Separation Train]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The network above shows the **competitive reaction problem**: ethylene and acetylene are sequential products. To *maximize acetylene*, you must operate at very high temperature, very short residence time, and **instantaneous quench** — otherwise the acetylene decomposes to carbon + hydrogen or polymerizes to tars.
