# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define acetylene. List any four of its important physical properties that make it industrially significant.**

**Model Answer:**
Acetylene (IUPAC: **ethyne**, C₂H₂) is the simplest alkyne containing a carbon–carbon triple bond. It is a colourless, highly flammable gas with a faintly sweet odour (pure grade).

Four industrially significant physical properties:

1. **Highest flame temperature** of any common fuel gas — ~3148 °C in pure oxygen (oxy-acetylene welding).
2. **Strongly endothermic** (ΔH_f° = +226.7 kJ/mol) — releases large energy on decomposition; used as a high-energy fuel.
3. **Wide flammability range in air** (2.5 % – 81 % v/v) — but also a high explosion hazard; requires special handling.
4. **Low molecular weight (26.04)** and **low boiling point (−84 °C)** — must be stored in acetone-filled cylinders under pressure, never as a free gas at >2 bar.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] Why is acetylene considered an important building block in the petrochemical industry? Give two major derivatives.**

**Model Answer:**
Acetylene is an important petrochemical building block because its **reactive C≡C triple bond** undergoes addition, polymerization, and cyclization reactions to produce a wide range of C₂, C₄, C₆, and C₈ chemicals from a single C₂ unit.

Two major derivatives:

1. **Vinyl chloride monomer (VCM), CH₂=CHCl** — produced by addition of HCl; the precursor to **PVC**, the world's third-largest commodity plastic.
2. **Acrylonitrile, CH₂=CHCN** — produced by addition of HCN; the monomer for **acrylic fibres, ABS, SAN, and nitrile rubber**.

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> ### Question A — *(Internal Choice Option 1)* [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
> **(a)** With a neat flow diagram, explain the **partial oxidation process (BASF/Sachsse)** for the manufacture of acetylene from natural gas. Discuss the operating conditions and the role of quenching. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Describe the **calcium carbide process** for acetylene production. Give the reactions, the energy requirement, and discuss why this route is gradually being replaced. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

**(a) BASF Partial Oxidation Process** *(7 marks)*

**Principle:** Methane is cracked to acetylene using the heat released by the simultaneous partial combustion of part of the methane feed with oxygen. The reaction is **autothermal** (self-sustaining).

**Reactions:**

$$2\,\text{CH}_4 \rightarrow \text{C}_2\text{H}_2 + 3\,\text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H = +376 \text{ kJ/mol (endothermic)}$$

$$\text{CH}_4 + 2\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \Delta H = -891 \text{ kJ/mol (heat supply)}$$

**Operating Conditions:**

- **Temperature:** 1500 – 1600 °C
- **Pressure:** 0.05 – 0.1 atm (sub-atmospheric) to favour acetylene over ethylene
- **Residence time:** 1 – 10 milliseconds
- **Oxygen purity:** 95 %+ (to avoid diluting with N₂)
- **Methane:O₂ ratio:** ~2 : 1 (stoichiometric for autothermal balance)

**Role of Quenching:**
The cracker exit gas contains acetylene mixed with ethylene, methane, H₂, CO, CO₂, and unconverted methane. If this mixture is allowed to cool slowly, acetylene **decomposes to carbon + hydrogen** (ΔH = −227 kJ/mol) and **polymerizes to tars**. Therefore, the gas must be **quenched to < 200 °C within 5 – 10 ms** using:
- **Oil quench** (BASF original), or
- **Water spray quench** (modern variant)

This freezes the equilibrium and preserves the acetylene yield.

**Process Steps (Block Flow):**
1. **Pre-mixing:** CH₄ and O₂ are intimately mixed in a specially designed burner.
2. **Combustion + Cracking:** The mixture burns in a refractory-lined burner block at 1500 °C.
3. **Quench:** The hot gas is rapidly cooled with oil/water spray.
4. **Soot removal:** Water scrubber removes entrained carbon black.
5. **Compression:** Gas compressed to 9 bar.
6. **Absorption:** Acetylene is selectively absorbed in **N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP)** or DMF solvent.
7. **Desorption:** Acetylene is stripped from the solvent under vacuum.
8. **Product:** 99.5 %+ pure acetylene sent to storage.

**Yield:** ~25 – 35 % per pass, but unconverted CH₄ is recycled → overall conversion > 90 %.

---

**(b) Calcium Carbide Process** *(7 marks)*

**Step 1 — Carbide Manufacture (Electric Arc Furnace):**
Quicklime (CaO) and metallurgical coke are charged into an **electric arc furnace** at **~2000 °C**.

$$\text{CaO} + 3\,\text{C} \rightarrow \text{CaC}_2 + \text{CO} \uparrow \quad \Delta H = +1110 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

The molten CaC₂ is tapped, cooled, crushed, and sized. The CO off-gas is recycled to the furnace.

**Step 2 — Acetylene Generation (Wet Process):**
Pure CaC₂ is fed into a water-filled generator:

$$\text{CaC}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{C}_2\text{H}_2 \uparrow + \text{Ca(OH)}_2$$

The acetylene gas is collected, scrubbed, and dried.

**Energy Requirement:**
The electric-arc step consumes **~10 MWh per ton of acetylene**, making it the most energy-intensive route. The overall energy demand is **~190 GJ per ton of C₂H₂**.

**Why this route is declining:**
1. **Huge electrical energy demand** — uneconomical in regions with high power tariffs.
2. **Large solid waste** — for every ton of C₂H₂, ~3 tons of Ca(OH)₂ sludge is generated, creating disposal/land-fill problems.
3. **Environmental burden** — high CO₂ footprint from coal-based power and coke.
4. **Better alternatives exist** — partial oxidation from cheap natural gas is far cheaper where CH₄ is abundant.
5. **Last strongholds:** China and a few countries with cheap coal-based electricity.

**Yield:** Theoretical ~406 kg C₂H₂ per ton pure CaC₂ (i.e., ~350 m³ at NTP per ton); commercial yields ~280 – 300 m³ per ton of 80 % technical CaC₂.

---

> ### Question B — *(Internal Choice Option 2)* [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
> **(a)** Compare the **Wulff process** and the **Hoechst (HTP) flame-crack process** for acetylene manufacture with respect to feedstock, temperature, residence time, selectivity, and economics. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the major **downstream derivatives of acetylene** with balanced reactions. Explain why acetylene is called the *"cornerstone of C₂-chemistry."* **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

**(a) Comparison: Wulff vs Hoechst (HTP) Process** *(7 marks)*

| Parameter | Wulff Process | Hoechst (HTP / Flame Crack) |
|---|---|---|
| **Feedstock** | C₂H₆ / C₃H₈ (ethane/propane) | CH₄, C₃H₈, naphtha, light hydrocarbons |
| **Heat Source** | Externally heated regenerative checker bricks | Partial combustion of the feed itself |
| **Furnace Type** | Cyclic regenerative furnace (2 stacks) | Single-pass tubular burner |
| **Temperature** | 1100 – 1300 °C | 1400 – 1500 °C |
| **Residence Time** | 0.1 – 1 s | 0.005 – 0.01 s (5–10 ms) |
| **Pressure** | ~0.5 atm (cyclic) | 0.2 – 0.5 atm |
| **Selectivity to C₂H₂** | 50 – 60 % (with H₂ as co-product) | 30 – 40 % |
| **Co-products** | H₂, ethylene, propylene | Ethylene, CO, CO₂, H₂, soot |
| **Quench** | Internal water spray in furnace | External oil/water quench in milliseconds |
| **Economics** | High CAPEX, energy-intensive cyclic operation | Lower CAPEX, autothermal, more flexible feed |
| **Modern status** | Largely obsolete; limited installations | Widely used in Europe; BASF, Hoechst plants |

**Key Differences Explained:**
- The Wulff is **non-combustion** — heat is stored in refractory bricks during the heating half-cycle and released to the hydrocarbon during the cracking half-cycle. The feedstock does not see any flame.
- The HTP process **burns a portion of the feed** with oxygen to supply the heat for cracking the remaining feed — autothermal design.
- The Wulff gives **higher selectivity** but is mechanically complex (large valves, periodic reversals) and the throughput is lower.
- HTP is more **flexible on feed** (can run on naphtha) and has lower CAPEX, but produces soot and CO that must be managed.

---

**(b) Downstream Derivatives of Acetylene** *(7 marks)*

Acetylene is the *"cornerstone of C₂-chemistry"* because from a single 2-carbon unit, the entire family of C₂, C₄, C₆, C₈ chemicals can be built.

**Reactions:**

1. **Vinyl Chloride (PVC route):**
$$\text{C}_2\text{H}_2 + \text{HCl} \xrightarrow{\text{HgCl}_2/\text{C}} \text{CH}_2=\text{CHCl}$$

2. **Acrylonitrile (acrylics route):**
$$\text{C}_2\text{H}_2 + \text{HCN} \xrightarrow{\text{CuCl}} \text{CH}_2=\text{CHCN}$$

3. **Vinyl Acetate (PVA route):**
$$\text{C}_2\text{H}_2 + \text{CH}_3\text{COOH} \xrightarrow{\text{Zn(OAc)}_2/\text{C}} \text{CH}_3\text{COOCH}=\text{CH}_2$$

4. **Acetaldehyde (Kucherov, Reppe):**
$$\text{C}_2\text{H}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{HgSO}_4/\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4} \text{CH}_3\text{CHO}$$
Acetaldehyde → Acetic acid, Acetic anhydride, Pentaerythritol, Crotonaldehyde.

5. **Acrylic Acid (Reppe carbonylation):**
$$2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_2 + 2\,\text{CO} + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{NiBr}_2} 2\,\text{CH}_2=\text{CHCOOH}$$

6. **1,4-Butanediol (Reppe formaldehyde):**
$$\text{C}_2\text{H}_2 + 2\,\text{HCHO} \xrightarrow{\text{Cu}} \text{HOCH}_2\text{C}\equiv\text{CCH}_2\text{OH} \xrightarrow{+\text{H}_2} \text{HOCH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$$
1,4-BDO → **THF, γ-butyrolactone, polyurethanes, spandex fibres (Lycra)**.

7. **Vinyl Acetylene (Nieuwland):**
$$2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{CuCl}/\text{NH}_4\text{Cl}} \text{CH}_2=\text{CH}-\text{C}\equiv\text{CH} \xrightarrow{+\text{HCl}} \text{Chloroprene} \xrightarrow{\text{polymerize}} \text{Neoprene rubber}$$

8. **Cyclization Reactions:**
$$3\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Ni or Fe}} \text{C}_6\text{H}_6 \quad (\text{Benzene, Berthelot})$$
$$4\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Ni(CN)}_2} \text{C}_8\text{H}_8 \quad (\text{Cyclooctatetraene, Reppe})$$

9. **Acetylene Black:** Decomposition of C₂H₂ at high T → high-purity carbon black used in batteries and conductive rubber.

**Why "Cornerstone of C₂-Chemistry":**
- Acetylene provides the **shortest, most reactive C₂ unit** in petrochemistry.
- Both carbon atoms are in the *sp* state — the most reactive hybridization state — enabling addition, polymerization, and cyclization.
- Almost every C₂-derivative (vinyl chloride, vinyl acetate, acrylonitrile, acetaldehyde) can be made by a single-step addition across the triple bond.
- Through oligomerization, C₂ acetylene can be built up to **C₄, C₆, C₈, C₁₂** products that would otherwise require multi-step ethylene chemistry.
- Hence acetylene is the **historical and reactive foundation** of the entire C₂-petrochemical value chain.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the auto-ignition hazard** — examiners specifically deduct marks if you do not mention that acetylene is *unstable above 2 bar* and must be dissolved in acetone.
> 2. **Wrong sign of ΔH_f°** — always write **+226.7 kJ/mol** (positive = endothermic); a negative sign is an instant zero for that sub-part.
> 3. **No mention of quenching** — in any partial-oxidation or cracking answer, **quenching in < 10 ms is mandatory** to prevent acetylene decomposition to carbon.
> 4. **Confusing ethylene with acetylene routes** — do not write ethylene reactions when the question asks about acetylene derivatives.
> 5. **Skipping the by-product credit** — in calcium carbide, always mention the **Ca(OH)₂ sludge problem** as the reason for the route's decline.
> 6. **No unit on yield** — always state acetylene yields in **both kg/ton CaC₂ and m³/ton at NTP**.
> 7. **Forgetting catalyst names** — HgSO₄ for Kucherov, CuCl for vinyl acetylene, NiBr₂ for Reppe carbonylation. Examiners award partial marks for these.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Bulletins — Acetylene**

- ✅ Acetylene = **ethyne** = C₂H₂, IUPAC name is **ethyne**.
- ✅ Triple bond; **sp-hybridized**; **linear geometry (180°)**; bond length **1.20 Å**.
- ✅ ΔH_f° = **+226.7 kJ/mol** (strongly endothermic — most unstable common hydrocarbon).
- ✅ Flame temperature in O₂ = **~3148 °C** (highest of all common fuel gases).
- ✅ Flammability range in air = **2.5 – 81 % v/v** (widest range of any common gas).
- ✅ Unstable above **2 bar** — must be stored **dissolved in acetone** in porous-filled cylinders.
- ✅ **Calcium carbide route:** CaO + 3C → CaC₂ + CO (endothermic); then CaC₂ + 2H₂O → C₂H₂ + Ca(OH)₂.
- ✅ Energy for carbide route = **~10 MWh per ton C₂H₂**; ~3 ton Ca(OH)₂ waste per ton C₂H₂.
- ✅ **Partial oxidation (BASF/Sachsse):** CH₄ + O₂ at 1500 °C, 0.05 atm, 1–10 ms residence time, autothermal.
- ✅ **Wulff process:** cyclic regenerative furnace, C₂H₆ / C₃H� feed, 1200 °C, 50–60 % selectivity, no flame.
- ✅ **HTP / Flame crack (Hoechst):** premixed flame, 1500 °C, 5 ms residence, naphtha-capable.
- ✅ **Mandatory quenching** to < 200 °C within **≤ 10 ms** — else acetylene decomposes to C + H₂.
- ✅ **Major derivatives** to memorize: VCM, acrylonitrile, vinyl acetate, acetaldehyde, acetic acid, acrylates, 1,4-BDO, vinyl acetylene (→ neoprene), cyclooctatetraene.
- ✅ **Kucherov reaction:** C₂H₂ + H₂O → CH₃CHO (catalyst = HgSO₄ / H₂SO₄).
- ✅ **Nieuwland reaction:** 2 C₂H₂ → vinyl acetylene (catalyst = CuCl / NH₄Cl).
- ✅ **Reppe chemistry:** C₂H₂ + CO + H₂O / HCHO / ROH → acrylates, 1,4-BDO, propargyl alcohol.
- ✅ **Berthelot trimerization:** 3 C₂H₂ → C₆H₆ (benzene).
- ✅ Modern acetylene is dominantly produced by **partial oxidation of natural gas (methane)**; the **carbide route survives only in China** due to cheap coal-based electricity.
- ✅ Flame-AAS uses acetylene as the **standard fuel gas** for trace metal analysis.
- ✅ Acetylene's wide flammability and endothermic nature make it the **most hazardous common industrial gas** — proper handling is part of the KTU safety syllabus.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steam Cracking for Production of Olefins
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT741 - PETROCHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Steam Cracking for Production of Olefins

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Steam cracking** is a high-temperature, short-residence-time petrochemical process in which saturated hydrocarbons (paraffins, naphthenes, and alkyl-aromatics) are thermally decomposed in the presence of diluted steam to produce a primary slate of **light olefins** — predominantly **ethylene (C₂H₄)**, **propylene (C₃H₆)**, and **1,3-butadiene (1,3-C₄H₆)** — along with a secondary slate of **aromatics** (BTX — benzene, toluene, xylenes), **methane**, and **pyrolysis gasoline (pygas)**.

The process is conducted in externally-fired tubular reactors (cracking coils) at:
- **Coil outlet temperature (COT):** 750 °C – 900 °C (typical 820 °C for naphtha)
- **Residence time:** 0.05 s – 0.5 s (millisecond furnaces operate near **0.1 s**)
- **Steam-to-Hydrocarbon Ratio (SHR):** 0.25 – 1.0 (mass basis)
- **Pressure at outlet:** 1 atm – 1.7 atm (slightly above atmospheric)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per the KTU 2024 syllabus for Module 2, the focus of this topic is the **production of lower olefins (ethylene and propylene)**, the **kinetics and thermodynamics** of the cracking reaction, **feedstock selection**, **furnace design**, **severity parameters**, and **primary/secondary quench systems**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pressure-Cooker" of Petrochemistry

Imagine you are trying to break a long, tightly-bound pearl necklace (a heavy hydrocarbon molecule like C₁₂H₂₆ — dodecane) into smaller, more valuable necklaces (C₂H₄, C₃H₆) inside a kitchen.

- The **fire** under the pot represents the radiant heat of the cracking furnace (≈ 900 °C).
- The **water** you add to the pot represents **dilution steam** — it absorbs excess heat, lowers the partial pressure of hydrocarbons (favoring primary olefins over recombination to heavy tars), and suppresses coke formation on the pot walls.
- The **very short cooking time** (only a few seconds before you snatch the pot off the fire) represents the **short residence time**, which prevents the freshly formed olefins from "over-cooking" and breaking further into methane and coke.
- The **cold water bath** you plunge the pot into immediately after cooking is the **Transfer Line Exchanger (TLE) / primary quench**, which freezes the reaction and protects the valuable olefins.

If you cook for too long, you get charcoal (coke). If you cook too briefly, you get unreacted feedstock. The art of steam cracking is to operate in the **narrow "sweet spot"** that maximises olefin yield.

### 1.3 Why Steam Cracking is the Backbone of Petrochemicals

> [!NOTE]
> **Global production context (industry-standard benchmarks):**
> - Global ethylene capacity exceeds **230 million metric tonnes per annum (MMTA)** — nearly all of it is produced via steam cracking.
> - Approximately **50%** of global ethylene comes from **naphtha cracking** (Asia), **30%** from **ethane cracking** (USA, Middle East), and the balance from LPG and gas oil.
> - **Ethylene** is the building block of polyethylene (PE), ethylene oxide/ethylene glycol, vinyl chloride monomer (PVC precursor), ethylbenzene/styrene, and alpha-olefins.

Steam cracking is therefore the **upstream gatekeeper** of the entire polyolefin, synthetic rubber, and detergent alcohol value chain.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Thermodynamic Foundation

The cracking reaction is strongly **endothermic** and **entropy-favoured** at high temperatures.

For a generic paraffin CₙH₂ₙ₊₂ → olefins + paraffins + H₂:

$$\Delta H_{rxn} \approx +90 \text{ to } +130 \text{ kJ/mol of C-C bond broken}$$

The high temperature shifts the equilibrium (per **Le Chatelier's principle**) toward the side with more gaseous moles — the cracked products. Dilution steam lowers the hydrocarbon partial pressure, which by **DALTON'S LAW** further favours cracking (mole fraction increase of products).

### 2.2 Free-Radical Chain Mechanism (Rice–Herzfeld Mechanism)

Steam cracking follows a **free-radical chain mechanism** with three stages:

**Stage 1 — Initiation (Homolytic bond cleavage):**
$$\text{C-C bond} \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{R}^{\prime \bullet}$$

**Stage 2 — Propagation (Hydrogen abstraction and β-scission):**
$$\text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2} \rightarrow \text{RH} + \text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+1}^{\bullet}$$
$$\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+1}^{\bullet} \xrightarrow{\beta\text{-scission}} \text{olefin} + \text{R}^{\prime\prime\bullet}$$

**Stage 3 — Termination (Radical recombination/disproportionation):**
$$\text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{R}^{\prime\bullet} \rightarrow \text{R-R} \quad \text{(or disproportionation)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why olefins, not paraffins, dominate products:**
> The **β-scission rule** strongly prefers forming a **small primary radical + an α-olefin**. This is why ethylene is the single most abundant product — it is the smallest, most thermodynamically and kinetically favoured α-olefin at severe conditions.

### 2.3 Role of Steam — The Three Critical Functions

| Function | Mechanism | Engineering Effect |
|----------|-----------|-------------------|
| **Dilution** | Lowers hydrocarbon partial pressure (Dalton's law) | Suppresses secondary bimolecular reactions, reduces recombination to tar |
| **Heat carrier** | High heat capacity, low residence time co-current | Uniform coil temperature, reduces hot spots |
| **Coke suppressant** | Steam gasifies carbon: C + H₂O → CO + H₂ | Extends run-length between decoking cycles from days → months |

### 2.4 Feedstock Spectrum and Product Distribution

| Feedstock | Typical C-Number | Ethylene Yield (wt%) | P/E Ratio (kg/kg) | Notes |
|-----------|------------------|----------------------|-------------------|-------|
| **Ethane** | C₂ | 77 – 84 | 0.02 – 0.04 | Highest ethylene, lowest propylene |
| **Propane** | C₃ | 40 – 45 | 0.30 – 0.40 | Good ethylene + propylene |
| **n-Butane** | C₄ | 35 – 42 | 0.40 – 0.55 | Moderate ethylene, more C₄ olefins |
| **Naphtha** | C₅ – C₁₁ | 28 – 35 | 0.45 – 0.60 | Balanced olefin + aromatics |
| **Gas oil (AGO)** | C₁₂ – C₂₅ | 20 – 25 | 0.55 – 0.75 | Lower olefins, more pygas + fuel oil |

> [!NOTE]
> As feedstock gets **heavier**, the **P/E ratio increases** and the production of aromatics (BTX) and pyrolysis gasoline (C₅–C₉) increases — at the cost of ethylene yield.

### 2.5 Cracking Severity — The Single Most Important Operational Variable

**Severity** is the integrated measure of how hard the feedstock has been "cracked". Three equivalent definitions are used industrially:

**A. Kinetic Severity Function (KSF)** — defined by **Zdonik and Green (1968, Industrial & Engineering Chemistry)**:

$$KSF = \int_0^{\tau} k_{C_2H_6}(T)\, d\tau$$

where $k_{C_2H_6}(T)$ is the **first-order rate constant for ethane cracking** evaluated at the local coil temperature, integrated along the reactor residence time $\tau$.

**B. Equivalent Residence Time (ERT)** — defined at a **reference temperature of 830 °C**:

$$ERT = \int_0^{\tau} \exp\left[\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_{ref}} - \frac{1}{T}\right)\right] d\tau$$

**C. Methane Index (MI)** — an empirical on-line measure from effluent composition:

$$MI = \frac{[CH_4]_{effluent}}{[C_1 + C_2 + C_3 + \cdots]_{effluent}} \times 100\%$$

> [!TIP]
> High severity → high MI → high ethylene yield but also high coke. There is a **practical maximum severity** beyond which the run-length drops below economic viability.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula / Definition | Units |
|--------|----------|----------------------|-------|
| $X$ | Conversion of feed | $X = \dfrac{n_{feed,\,in} - n_{feed,\,out}}{n_{feed,\,in}}$ | dimensionless, fraction |
| $S_i$ | Selectivity to product $i$ | $S_i = \dfrac{n_{i,\,produced}}{n_{feed,\,converted}}$ | mol/mol |
| $Y_i$ | Yield of product $i$ | $Y_i = S_i \cdot X$ | mol product / mol feed |
| $SHR$ | Steam-to-Hydrocarbon Ratio | $SHR = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{steam}}{\dot{m}_{HC}}$ | mass / mass |
| $KSF$ | Kinetic Severity Function | $KSF = \displaystyle\int_0^{\tau} k_{C_2H_6}(T)\, d\tau$ | dimensionless (scaled) |
| $ERT$ | Equivalent Residence Time (at $T_{ref} = 1103\text{ K}$) | $ERT = \displaystyle\int_0^{\tau} \exp\!\left[\dfrac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_{ref}} - \dfrac{1}{T}\right)\right] d\tau$ | seconds |
| $MI$ | Methane Index | $MI = \dfrac{[CH_4]}{\sum [C_x H_y]}\times 100\%$ | % |
| $P/E$ | Propylene-to-Ethylene ratio | $P/E = \dfrac{\dot{n}_{C_3H_6}}{\dot{n}_{C_2H_4}}$ | kg/kg or mol/mol |
| $\Delta P_{coil}$ | Coil pressure drop | $\Delta P_{coil} = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho u^2}{2}$ | Pa |
| $T_{COT}$ | Coil Outlet Temperature | Engineering setpoint | °C or K |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU valuation tip:** Always carry **units in every intermediate step**. Examiners deduct 0.5 – 1 mark for unitless answers in calculation problems.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Plant capacity planning:** The ethylene yield determines the **front-end coil count** and **downstream compressor/column sizing**.
- **Feedstock flexibility:** Modern furnaces (e.g., Lummus SRT®, UOP/Emission Less, KTI GK) accept multiple feedstocks — the severity is tuned to maintain a target MI regardless of feed.
- **Energy integration:** The TLE generates **high-pressure super-high-pressure (SP/HP) steam** — typically **9 – 12 MPa** — which drives the cracked gas compressor (CGC) turbine. This integration gives modern plants a **net positive electricity export**.
- **Decoking cycle:** On-stream time (OST) of 60 – 90 days is standard; severity directly controls coking rate.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Relationship Between Conversion, Selectivity, and Yield

**Statement:** The fractional yield of any product $i$ is the product of the overall conversion of the limiting feedstock and the selectivity toward product $i$.

**Given:**
- $F_{in}$ = molar flow rate of feed into the reactor (mol/s)
- $F_{out}$ = molar flow rate of **unreacted** feed leaving the reactor (mol/s)
- $F_i$ = molar flow rate of product $i$ in the reactor effluent (mol/s)

**Step 1 — Define the fractional conversion of feed:**

$$X = \frac{F_{in} - F_{out}}{F_{in}}$$

**Step 2 — Define the selectivity to product $i$ (moles of $i$ produced per mole of feed converted):**

$$S_i = \frac{F_i}{F_{in} - F_{out}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the denominator of $S_i$ with $F_{in} \cdot X$:**

$$S_i = \frac{F_i}{F_{in} \cdot X}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $F_i$:**

$$F_i = S_i \cdot X \cdot F_{in}$$

**Step 5 — Define the yield $Y_i$ as the ratio of $F_i$ to $F_{in}$:**

$$Y_i = \frac{F_i}{F_{in}}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $F_i$ from Step 4 into Step 5:**

$$\boxed{Y_i = S_i \cdot X}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** A high yield can be achieved either by high conversion (deep cracking) or high selectivity (favourable product distribution). In practice, both are traded off against each other and against coking rate.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Kinetic Severity Function (KSF) from First-Order Kinetics

**Statement:** The KSF is the integrated first-order rate constant of ethane pyrolysis along the reactor residence time, scaled to a reference temperature.

**Given:** The thermal cracking of ethane follows **first-order kinetics** in the ethane partial pressure:

$$-r_{C_2H_6} = -\frac{dP_{C_2H_6}}{d\tau} = k_{C_2H_6}(T) \cdot P_{C_2H_6}$$

The Arrhenius temperature dependence:

$$k_{C_2H_6}(T) = A \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 1 — Rearrange the rate equation to isolate the rate constant integral:**

$$k_{C_2H_6}(T) \cdot d\tau = -\frac{dP_{C_2H_6}}{P_{C_2H_6}}$$

**Step 2 — Integrate from inlet (0) to outlet ($\tau$) of the coil:**

$$\int_0^{\tau} k_{C_2H_6}(T)\, d\tau = -\int_{P_{in}}^{P_{out}} \frac{dP}{P} = \ln\!\left(\frac{P_{in}}{P_{out}}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Recognise the left-hand side as the KSF definition:**

$$KSF = \int_0^{\tau} k_{C_2H_6}(T)\, d\tau = \ln\!\left(\frac{P_{in}}{P_{out}}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Expand $k_{C_2H_6}(T)$ using Arrhenius law:**

$$KSF = \int_0^{\tau} A \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT(\tau)}\right) d\tau$$

**Step 5 — Note that the integrand is path-dependent on the local temperature profile $T(\tau)$:**

$$KSF = A \cdot \int_0^{\tau} \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{R\,T(\tau)}\right) d\tau$$

**Step 6 — Convert to a reference temperature $T_{ref}$ (typically 1103 K or 830 °C) by factoring out $\exp(-E_a/RT_{ref})$:**

$$KSF = A \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{R\,T_{ref}}\right) \cdot \int_0^{\tau} \exp\!\left[\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_{ref}} - \frac{1}{T(\tau)}\right)\right] d\tau$$

**Step 7 — Identify the integral as the Equivalent Residence Time (ERT) at the reference temperature:**

$$\boxed{ERT = \int_0^{\tau} \exp\!\left[\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_{ref}} - \frac{1}{T(\tau)}\right)\right] d\tau}$$

**Step 8 — The final relationship between KSF and ERT:**

$$KSF = A \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{R\,T_{ref}}\right) \cdot ERT = k_{C_2H_6}(T_{ref}) \cdot ERT$$

**Engineering interpretation:** $ERT$ is a **time-equivalent** — it represents how many seconds at 830 °C the cracking coil "feels" the feed has experienced. A typical naphtha cracker runs at $ERT \approx 0.3 - 0.6$ s.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Mass Balance Across the Radiant Coil

**Statement:** Under steady-state, the total carbon mass entering equals total carbon mass leaving (carbon balance), and the total hydrogen mass is conserved (hydrogen balance). Steam is treated as inert diluent in this stage.

**Given:** Feedstock of molecular formula CₐHᵦ, mass flow $\dot{m}_f$, dilution steam flow $\dot{m}_{st}$.

**Carbon balance (per unit time):**

$$\frac{a}{M_f} \cdot \dot{m}_f = \sum_{i} \frac{a_i}{M_i} \cdot \dot{m}_i$$

**Hydrogen balance (per unit time):**

$$\frac{b}{M_f} \cdot \dot{m}_f + \frac{2}{18} \cdot \dot{m}_{st} = \sum_{i} \frac{b_i}{M_i} \cdot \dot{m}_i + \frac{2}{18} \cdot \dot{m}_{st,\,out}$$

**Simplification (steam is conserved through the furnace):**

$$\frac{2}{18} \cdot \dot{m}_{st} = \frac{2}{18} \cdot \dot{m}_{st,\,out} \Rightarrow \dot{m}_{st,\,out} = \dot{m}_{st}$$

Therefore the **net hydrogen balance** reduces to:

$$\boxed{\frac{b}{M_f} \cdot \dot{m}_f = \sum_{i} \frac{b_i}{M_i} \cdot \dot{m}_i}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** These two balance equations, combined with component yield data, are the basis of **stoichiometric simulation** of the cracking furnace using a molecular reactor model (e.g., SPYRO® by Technip, COILSIM1D).

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style Problem)

**Problem:** A naphtha cracking furnace processes **20 000 kg/h** of feedstock (assumed C₇H₁₆) with **SHR = 0.50**. The coil achieves **65% conversion** of the naphtha and the **selectivity to ethylene is 32 mol%** of the converted feed. Calculate:
(a) Mass of steam added.
(b) Molar flow of ethylene produced per hour.
(c) Mass yield of ethylene (wt% on feed).

**Given Data:**
- $\dot{m}_{f} = 20\,000$ kg/h
- Molecular weight of C₇H₁₆, $M_f = 7 \times 12 + 16 = 100$ kg/kmol
- Molecular weight of C₂H₄, $M_{C_2H_4} = 28$ kg/kmol

**Step (a) — Mass of steam added:**

$$\dot{m}_{st} = SHR \times \dot{m}_f = 0.50 \times 20\,000 = 10\,000 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step (b) — Molar flow of ethylene produced:**

First, convert feed to kmol/h:
$$n_f = \frac{20\,000}{100} = 200 \text{ kmol/h}$$

Moles of feed converted:
$$n_{conv} = X \times n_f = 0.65 \times 200 = 130 \text{ kmol/h}$$

Moles of ethylene produced:
$$n_{C_2H_4} = S_{C_2H_4} \times n_{conv} = 0.32 \times 130 = 41.6 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step (c) — Mass yield of ethylene (wt% on feed):**

$$\dot{m}_{C_2H_4} = n_{C_2H_4} \times M_{C_2H_4} = 41.6 \times 28 = 1164.8 \text{ kg/h}$$

$$Y_{C_2H_4,\,wt\%} = \frac{1164.8}{20\,000} \times 100 = 5.824\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> The mass-based yield is lower than the molar selectivity because ethylene (28 g/mol) is lighter than naphtha (100 g/mol). **KTU valuation tip:** Students frequently confuse molar and mass yields — specify the basis.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Steam Cracking Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Feedstock Storage: Ethane / Naphtha / LPG]
    B[Feedstock Preheater]
    C[Steam Superheater]
    D[Primary Mixer]
    E[Convection Section of Furnace]
    F[Radiant Section: Cracking Coils]
    G[Transfer Line Exchanger TLE / Primary Quench]
    H[Secondary Quench Tower]
    I[Cracked Gas Compressor CGC 4-Stage]
    J[Acid Gas Removal: Amine + Caustic]
    K[Demethanizer Cold Box]
    L[Deethanizer]
    M[Ethylene Fractionator C2 Splitter]
    N[Depropanizer]
    O[Propylene Fractionator C3 Splitter]
    P[Pyrolysis Gasoline pygas Hydrogenation]
    Q[Decoking System Steam Air]

    A --> B
    B --> D
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H
    H --> I
    I --> J
    J --> K
    J --> L
    L --> M
    J --> N
    N --> O
    I --> P
    F -.coke buildup.-> Q
    Q -.offline decoke.-> F
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture of the Cracking Furnace (SRT-type)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CONV[Convection Section]
        FEEDIN[Hydrocarbon Feed Inlet]
        SH1[Steam Generation Bank]
        SH2[High Pressure Superheater]
        ECO[Economiser]
        MIX[Mixed Feed to Radiant]
        STACK1[Flue Gas to Stack]
    end

    subgraph RAD[Radiant Section]
        COIL1[Inlet Coil pass 1]
        COIL2[Mid Coil pass 2]
        COIL3[Outlet Coil pass 3]
        BURN[Floor + Wall Burners]
    end

    subgraph QUENCH[Quench System]
        TLE[Transfer Line Exchanger]
        QT[Quench Tower]
    end

    FEEDIN --> SH1 --> SH2 --> ECO --> MIX
    MIX --> COIL1
    COIL1 --> COIL2
    COIL2 --> COIL3
    COIL3 --> TLE
    TLE --> QT
    BURN -.heat.-> COIL1
    BURN -.heat.-> COIL2
    BURN -.heat.-> COIL3
    ECO -.uses flue gas.-> STACK1
```

### 4.3 Free-Radical Mechanism Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INIT[Initiation: C-C bond homolysis R-R to R dot + R dot]
    AB[Abstraction: R dot + C-nH-2n+2 to RH + C-nH-2n+1 dot]
    BS[Beta-Scission: C-nH-2n+1 dot to alpha-olefin + smaller R dot]
    TERM[Termination: R dot + R dot to R-R or disproportionation]
    COKE[Coke Formation: side reaction at metal surface]
    STMC[Steam gasification: C + H2O to CO + H2]

    INIT --> AB
    AB --> BS
    BS --> TERM
    BS --> AB
    TERM --> COKE
    STMC -.suppresses.-> COKE
```

### 4.4 Severity-Product Yield Correlation Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LOW[Low Severity]
        L1[Low methane]
        L2[Moderate ethylene]
        L3[High unconverted feed]
    end
    subgraph MED[Medium Severity]
        M1[Balanced ethylene + propylene]
        M2[Growing BTX]
    end
    subgraph HIGH[High Severity]
        H1[Maximum ethylene]
        H2[High methane]
        H3[High coke]
        H4[Low propylene]
    end

    LOW --> MED --> HIGH
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** As severity increases from left → right, the curve traces the industrial optimum at "medium-high" severity where ethylene peaks and the run-length remains economically viable.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: Define steam cracking. List its major primary products.
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 (Similar)]**

**Model Answer:**

Steam cracking is a high-temperature (750 – 900 °C) thermal decomposition process in which saturated hydrocarbons (ethane, propane, naphtha, gas oil) are broken down into smaller, predominantly unsaturated hydrocarbons in the presence of dilution steam. The process is carried out in externally-fired tubular reactors at short residence times (0.05 – 0.5 s) to maximise olefin yield.

**Primary products:** ethylene, propylene, 1,3-butadiene.
**Secondary products:** BTX aromatics (benzene, toluene, xylenes), methane, hydrogen, pyrolysis gasoline, fuel oil.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student error:** Writing "steam cracking produces gasoline" — gasoline is a *byproduct* (pygas), not the main product. Examiners deduct ½ mark for confusing primary vs secondary products.

---

#### Question 2: What is the role of steam in steam cracking? Explain briefly.
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 (Similar)]**

**Model Answer:**

Dilution steam serves **three critical functions** in steam cracking:

1. **Partial pressure reduction:** Lowers the hydrocarbon partial pressure, which by Le Chatelier's principle favours the formation of lower-molecular-weight olefins and suppresses secondary bimolecular reactions (e.g., recombination to tars).
2. **Heat carrier:** Provides a uniform temperature distribution along the coil, reducing hot spots and improving selectivity.
3. **Coke suppression:** Reacts with deposited carbon via the gasification reaction $\text{C} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO} + \text{H}_2$, extending furnace run-length between decoking cycles.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often say "steam acts as a catalyst" — **steam is NOT a catalyst**. It is a diluent and reactant. Avoid the word "catalyst" in your answer.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 (a): Discuss the chemistry and free-radical mechanism of steam cracking with a neat sketch. (7 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Adapted)]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Overall Reaction Chemistry (2 marks):**

For a generic paraffin CₙH₂ₙ₊₂, the net cracking reaction is:

$$\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2} \xrightarrow{\Delta,\ \text{steam}} \text{C}_x\text{H}_{2x} + \text{C}_y\text{H}_{2y+2} + \text{H}_2$$

The reaction is **endothermic** ($\Delta H \approx +90$ to $+130$ kJ/mol of C–C bond) and proceeds via a **free-radical chain mechanism**.

**(ii) Free-Radical Chain Mechanism (4 marks):**

| Stage | Reaction | Description |
|-------|----------|-------------|
| **Initiation** | $\text{R–R} \rightarrow \text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{R}^{\prime\bullet}$ | Homolytic cleavage of C–C bond at high T |
| **Propagation 1** (H-abstraction) | $\text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2} \rightarrow \text{RH} + \text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+1}^{\bullet}$ | Radical abstracts H from a stable paraffin |
| **Propagation 2** (β-scission) | $\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+1}^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{C}_x\text{H}_{2x} + \text{R}^{\prime\prime\bullet}$ | C–C bond β to the radical breaks; **an α-olefin is formed** and a smaller radical is regenerated |
| **Termination** | $\text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{R}^{\prime\bullet} \rightarrow \text{R–R}$ or disproportionation | Radicals combine or disproportionate, ending the chain |

The **β-scission rule** (the bond β to the radical is preferentially broken) explains why **ethylene is the most abundant product** — it is the smallest α-olefin and is kinetically and thermodynamically favoured.

**(iii) Effect of Operating Conditions (1 mark):**

- **Higher temperature** → more initiation events, deeper cracking → more ethylene + methane.
- **Lower residence time** → fewer secondary reactions, higher olefin selectivity, less coke.
- **Higher SHR** → lower hydrocarbon partial pressure, suppresses bimolecular secondary reactions.

---

#### Question 1 (b): Explain the different feedstocks used in steam cracking and their effect on product distribution. (7 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Adapted)]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Feedstock Spectrum (3 marks):**

| Feedstock | Carbon Range | Source | Ethylene Yield (wt%) |
|-----------|--------------|--------|----------------------|
| Ethane | C₂ | Natural gas | 77 – 84 |
| Propane | C₃ | LPG / NGL | 40 – 45 |
| n-Butane | C₄ | NGL | 35 – 42 |
| Naphtha | C₅ – C₁₁ | Refinery | 28 – 35 |
| Atmospheric Gas Oil (AGO) | C₁₂ – C₂₅ | Refinery | 20 – 25 |
| Vacuum Gas Oil (VGO) | C₂₅+ | Refinery | 15 – 20 |

**(ii) Effect on Product Distribution (3 marks):**

- **Lighter feedstocks (ethane, propane):** Higher ethylene yield, lower propylene/butadiene yield, **low P/E ratio** (≈ 0.02 – 0.40). Less aromatic byproducts.
- **Heavier feedstocks (naphtha, gas oil):** Lower ethylene yield, higher propylene, butadiene, and BTX aromatics. **Higher P/E ratio** (≈ 0.45 – 0.75). More pyrolysis gasoline (pygas) and fuel oil as byproducts.
- **Naphtha** is the most flexible — produces a balanced slate suitable for an integrated petrochemical complex.
- **Ethane crackers** dominate the US Gulf Coast and Middle East due to cheap shale-gas ethane; **naphtha crackers** dominate Europe and Asia.

**(iii) Key Selection Criteria (1 mark):**

1. **Ethylene yield on feed** — sets the throughput for downstream units.
2. **P/E ratio** — matches the cracker to the polymer product mix.
3. **Feedstock price volatility** — ethane prices have decoupled from crude oil since the US shale boom.
4. **Run-length and coking rate** — heavier feeds coke faster.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** When asked about "feedstock effect on product distribution," students often only state ethylene yield. Examiners expect **explicit comparison** of P/E ratio and aromatic yield as well. Covering all three earns full 3 marks.

---

#### Question 2 (a): With a neat block diagram, describe the major sections of a steam cracking plant. (7 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Similar)]**

**Model Solution:**

A modern steam cracking plant consists of the following **six functional sections** (refer to Section 4.1 diagram):

| Section | Function | Key Equipment |
|---------|----------|---------------|
| **1. Feed Preparation** | Mixes hydrocarbon feed with dilution steam in the desired SHR | Feed preheater, primary mixer |
| **2. Cracking Furnace** | Provides heat for endothermic cracking | Convection section (preheat + steam superheat) + Radiant section (coils + burners) |
| **3. Primary Quench** | Rapidly cools effluent to ~400 °C to freeze the reaction and recover heat as HP steam | Transfer Line Exchanger (TLE) — shell-and-tube type |
| **4. Secondary Quench** | Further cools to ~40 °C and removes heavy pygas | Quench tower with bottom (pygas + quench water) and top (cracked gas) streams |
| **5. Compression** | Compresses cracked gas from ~0.5 bar to ~35 bar for downstream separation | 4- or 5-stage centrifugal cracked gas compressor (CGC) with inter-stage cooling |
| **6. Separation Train** | Sequential distillation separates the products | Demethanizer → Deethanizer → C₂ splitter → Depropanizer → C₃ splitter → Debutanizer |

**Block Flow (text-rendered for the answer sheet):**

Feed + Steam → Furnace (Conv + Rad) → TLE → Quench Tower → CGC → Acid Gas Removal → Demethanizer → Deethanizer → C₂ Splitter (Ethylene + Ethane) || Depropanizer → C₃ Splitter (Propylene + Propane)

---

#### Question 2 (b): Define cracking severity. Explain the different parameters used to quantify severity. (7 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Similar)]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Definition (2 marks):**

Cracking severity is an **integrated measure of the thermal stress** experienced by the feedstock as it traverses the cracking coil. It captures the combined effect of **temperature, residence time, and partial pressure** and correlates directly with the product distribution.

**(ii) Quantification Parameters (5 marks):**

**A. Kinetic Severity Function (KSF):**
$$KSF = \int_0^{\tau} k_{C_2H_6}(T)\, d\tau$$
where $k_{C_2H_6}(T) = A \exp(-E_a/RT)$ is the first-order rate constant of ethane cracking. Higher KSF means deeper cracking.

**B. Equivalent Residence Time (ERT):** Time-equivalent at a reference temperature $T_{ref} = 1103$ K:
$$ERT = \int_0^{\tau} \exp\!\left[\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_{ref}} - \frac{1}{T(\tau)}\right)\right] d\tau$$
Typical ERT for naphtha: 0.3 – 0.6 s.

**C. Methane Index (MI):** Empirical on-line measure from effluent composition:
$$MI = \frac{[CH_4]}{[C_1 + C_2 + C_3 + \cdots]}\times 100\%$$
MI increases monotonically with severity because methane is a "terminal" product of deep cracking.

**D. Coil Outlet Temperature (COT):** A practical, easily measured proxy — not a true severity metric because it ignores residence time, but widely used in plant control.

**E. Propylene-to-Ethylene (P/E) Ratio:** Inverse indicator — P/E falls as severity rises because propylene undergoes secondary cracking to ethylene and ethylene does not crack further under typical conditions.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often treat COT and KSF as interchangeable. COT is a **local measurement** (single point at coil outlet), while KSF is a **path-integrated** quantity. Examiners award 1 mark extra for explicitly noting this distinction.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — Steam Cracking:**

- [ ] **Steam cracking** = thermal decomposition of hydrocarbons in presence of steam at **750 – 900 °C**, **0.05 – 0.5 s residence time**, **SHR 0.25 – 1.0**.
- [ ] **Primary products:** ethylene, propylene, 1,3-butadiene. **Secondary products:** BTX, methane, H₂, pygas, fuel oil.
- [ ] **Mechanism:** Rice–Herzfeld free-radical chain (initiation → H-abstraction → β-scission → termination).
- [ ] **Role of steam:** dilution (partial pressure), heat carrier, coke suppression via $\text{C} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO} + \text{H}_2$.
- [ ] **Feedstock ranking for ethylene yield:** Ethane > Propane > n-Butane > Naphtha > AGO > VGO.
- [ ] **P/E ratio increases** as feedstock gets heavier; **P/E ratio decreases** as severity increases.
- [ ] **Severity parameters:** KSF (integrated rate constant), ERT (time-equivalent at 830 °C), MI (methane index), COT (proxy), P/E ratio (inverse).
- [ ] **Plant sections:** Feed prep → Furnace (conv + rad) → TLE → Quench tower → CGC → Acid gas removal → Cold box (demethanizer) → Deethanizer → C₂ splitter → Depropanizer → C₃ splitter.
- [ ] **TLE** generates **HP steam (9 – 12 MPa)** that drives the cracked gas compressor turbine — a key energy-integration feature.
- [ ] **Master formulas:** $Y_i = S_i \cdot X$, $KSF = k_{C_2H_6}(T_{ref}) \cdot ERT$, $MI = [CH_4]/\sum [C_xH_y] \times 100\%$.
- [ ] **Always state units** in numerical problems and **distinguish molar vs mass basis** for yield and selectivity.
- [ ] **Steam is NOT a catalyst** — examiners deduct marks for this common error.
- [ ] **β-scission rule** explains why ethylene dominates products.
- [ ] **Run-length** of a modern cracking furnace is **60 – 90 days** between decoking cycles.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ethylene and Ethylene Derivatives - Production and Application of Ethylene Oxide
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT741 - PETROCHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ethylene and Ethylene Derivatives - Production and Application of Ethylene Oxide

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Ethylene Oxide — The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ethylene Oxide (EO), C₂H₄O** is the simplest **epoxide** (oxirane) — a three-membered cyclic ether in which an oxygen atom is bonded to two adjacent carbon atoms. It is a colourless, flammable, sweet-smelling gas at room temperature (boiling point **10.7 °C**) and is the single most important downstream derivative of ethylene, accounting for roughly **15–20 %** of the global ethylene consumption.

The molecule is a strained heterocycle:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Structure:} \quad & \text{H}_2\text{C} \!-\! \text{CH}_2 \\
                           & \;\;\;\; \backslash \;\;\; / \\
                           & \;\;\;\;\;\;\; \text{O}
\end{aligned}$$

### Molecular Parameters Worth Memorising
- **Molecular weight:** **44.05 g mol⁻¹**
- **Boiling point:** **10.7 °C** (≈ 283.8 K)
- **Melting point:** **−111.3 °C**
- **Density (liquid at 4 °C):** **0.882 g cm⁻³**
- **Flash point:** **−20 °C** (extremely flammable)
- **Auto-ignition temperature:** **429 °C**
- **Explosive limits in air:** **3 % – 100 % v/v** (one of the widest flammable ranges known)

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Spring-Loaded Triangle"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Ethylene oxide is like a compressed spring triangle.**
> Imagine a three-membered ring (C–C–O) bent tightly out of its preferred tetrahedral angle. The bond angles are forced from the natural 109.5° down to about **60°**, storing enormous *ring strain* (~ **27 kcal mol⁻¹**). This makes the molecule highly reactive — it "wants" to pop open and relieve that strain, which is why EO readily adds water, alcohols, ammonia, and amines to form a vast family of derivatives.

Because of this strain, EO behaves as a powerful **electrophile**: any nucleophile (water, alcohol, amine) can attack the carbon and "open" the ring, producing industrially vital products such as ethylene glycol, ethanolamines, and glycol ethers.

## 1.3 Why Ethylene Oxide Matters in Petrochemical Industry

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** EO is the *bridge* between olefin feedstocks (ethylene) and oxygenated industrial chemicals. It is the gateway to:
> - **Mono-, Di-, Tri-ethylene glycols (MEG, DEG, TEG)**
> - **Ethanolamines (MEA, DEA, TEA)**
> - **Ethylene glycol ethers (cellosolves, carbitols)**
> - **Polyethylene glycols (PEGs)**
> - **Non-ionic surfactants**
> - **Medical sterilisation gas**

Ethylene oxide sits at the top of the **C₂-oxygenates value chain**, as shown schematically below.

| Derivative Family | Largest Single End-Use | Global Share |
|---|---|---|
| Mono-ethylene Glycol (MEG) | PET polyester fibres & bottles | ~ **60 %** of EO |
| Ethylene Glycol Ethers | Solvents, paints, brake fluids | ~ **10 %** |
| Ethanolamines | Detergents, gas treatment (MEA) | ~ **8 %** |
| Ethoxylates / Surfactants | Detergents, agrochemicals | ~ **12 %** |
| Sterilisation / Pharma | Hospital sterilisation of medical devices | ~ **5 %** |
| Others (PEG, etc.) | Cosmetics, pharma, lubricants | ~ **5 %** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Industrial Routes to Ethylene Oxide

Ethylene oxide is industrially produced by **two fundamentally different chemistries**:

### 2.1.1 (A) The Classical Chlorohydrin Process (Historical)

- Ethylene reacts with hypochlorous acid (HOCl) to form **ethylene chlorohydrin**.
- The chlorohydrin is then dehydrochlorinated with Ca(OH)₂ to give EO.

**Step 1 — Chlorohydrin formation:**

$$\text{C}_2\text{H}_4 + \text{HOCl} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{ClCH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$$

**Step 2 — Cyclisation with lime:**

$$2\,\text{ClCH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{OH} + \text{Ca(OH)}_2 \;\longrightarrow\; 2\,\underset{\text{EO}}{\text{C}_2\text{H}_4\text{O}} + \text{CaCl}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

> [!WARNING]
> The chlorohydrin route has been **almost completely abandoned** worldwide because it produces large quantities of CaCl₂ brine, consumes stoichiometric chlorine, and gives low selectivity (~ **80 %**). It is **only** still relevant in older KTU exam questions and in regions where ethylene is more expensive than chlorine.

### 2.1.2 (B) The Direct Catalytic Oxidation (Modern Dominant Route)

Ethylene is oxidised with **molecular oxygen (O₂)** or **air** over a **silver (Ag) catalyst** at high temperature.

**Overall reaction:**

$$2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_4 + \text{O}_2 \;\xrightarrow{\text{Ag, 250–300 °C}}\; 2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_4\text{O} \qquad \Delta H = -105\;\text{kJ mol}^{-1}$$

**Competing (unwanted) side reaction — total combustion:**

$$\text{C}_2\text{H}_4 + 3\,\text{O}_2 \;\longrightarrow\; 2\,\text{CO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \qquad \Delta H = -1323\;\text{kJ mol}^{-1}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **selectivity** of this reaction is the central engineering challenge. Modern Ag catalysts supported on α-Al₂O₃ and promoted with **Cs, K, Re, Mo, or Cl** achieve **selectivities of 80–92 %** to EO at **ethylene conversions of 10–15 %** per pass (the process is run with **recycle**).

## 2.2 Process Operating Variables (KTU High-Yield)

| Variable | Typical Range | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| **Temperature** | **220 – 300 °C** | Above 300 °C the combustion pathway dominates and selectivity collapses. |
| **Pressure** | **1 – 3 MPa** | Higher P increases EO yield per pass but raises flammability risk. |
| **O₂/C₂H₄ ratio** | O₂ : C₂H₄ ≈ 1 : (2–4) | Must stay *outside* the flammable envelope. |
| **Space velocity (GHSV)** | 3 000 – 6 000 h⁻¹ | Higher GHSV → lower conversion but higher selectivity. |
| **Catalyst** | Ag/α-Al₂O₃, ~ **10–20 % Ag** | Only Ag gives useful EO selectivity; Pd/Pt give mostly CO₂. |
| **Promoter** | Cs, K, Re, Cl, Mo | Cs lowers the work function of Ag, suppresses deep oxidation. |
| **Inhibitor (in O₂-mode)** | 1,2-dichloroethane (~ 0.1–5 ppm) | Suppresses total combustion by poisoning Ag "hot sites". |

## 2.3 The Two Process Variants — Air vs. Oxygen

| Feature | **Air-Based Process** (Union Carbide / older) | **Oxygen-Based Process** (Shell OMEGA, Scientific Design) |
|---|---|---|
| Oxidant | Atmospheric air | Pure O₂ (~ 99.5 %) |
| Inert diluent | N₂ (from air) | CH₄ or CO₂ recycle |
| Selectivity | 75 – 80 % | **85 – 92 %** |
| Conversion / pass | 30 – 50 % | 10 – 15 % |
| Recycle gas treatment | Needs frequent purge | Mostly internal recycle |
| Capital cost | Lower | Higher (needs air-separation unit, ASU) |
| Operating cost | Higher (more ethylene lost) | Lower (better EO yield) |
| New-plant dominance today | < 5 % of capacity | > **95 %** of new plants |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Students commonly confuse *conversion* and *selectivity*. Remember:
> $$\text{Selectivity} = \frac{\text{moles EO formed}}{\text{moles ethylene reacted}} \times 100\%$$
> $$\text{Conversion} = \frac{\text{moles ethylene reacted}}{\text{moles ethylene fed}} \times 100\%$$
> $$\text{Overall Yield} = \text{Selectivity} \times \text{Conversion}$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning / Units |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Selectivity} = \dfrac{n_{EO}}{n_{C_2H_4,reacted}} \times 100$ | Dimensionless (%) |
| 2 | $\text{Conversion} = \dfrac{n_{C_2H_4,reacted}}{n_{C_2H_4,fed}} \times 100$ | Dimensionless (%) |
| 3 | $\text{Yield} = S \times X \;\big/\; 100$ | Dimensionless (%) |
| 4 | $\Delta H_{EO} = -105\;\text{kJ mol}^{-1}$ | Exothermic main reaction |
| 5 | $\Delta H_{comb} = -1323\;\text{kJ mol}^{-1}$ | Highly exothermic side reaction |
| 6 | $\text{GHSV} = \dfrac{\dot{V}_{gas}}{V_{catalyst}}\;\;[\text{h}^{-1}]$ | Gas hourly space velocity |
| 7 | $\text{TON} = \dfrac{\text{mol EO}}{\text{mol Ag} \cdot \text{s}}$ | Turnover number |
| 8 | $\dot{Q}_{removal} = \dot{n}_{C_2H_4,in}\cdot X \cdot (-\Delta H)$ | Heat duty of reactor |
| 9 | $\dot{n}_{C_2H_4,air\,limit} = \dfrac{\dot{n}_{O_2}}{3}$ | Stoichiometric air ratio for complete combustion |
| 10 | $\text{Stiochiometric Air Ratio (SAR)} = \dfrac{F_{air}}{F_{air,\,stoich}}$ | > 1 → lean; < 1 → rich (avoid explosive range) |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Reactor design** uses multi-tubular fixed-bed reactors with boiling-water cooling to remove ~ **3 200 kcal per kg EO** of heat.
- **Selectivity engineering** is the heart of EO plant economy — every 1 % rise in selectivity saves roughly **$3–5 million/year** in a 500 ktpa EO plant.
- **Catalyst life** is typically **2 – 5 years**; activity loss is mainly due to Ag sintering and Cl leaching.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Maximum Yield vs. Per-Pass Selectivity

In an EO reactor the ethylene feed is split into three destinations:

1. **Converted to EO** (desired, moles $n_{EO}$)
2. **Burned to CO₂ + H₂O** (unwanted, moles $n_{CO_2}$)
3. **Unreacted** (recycled, moles $n_{C_2H_4,\,out}$)

By carbon balance on ethylene:

$$\begin{aligned}
n_{C_2H_4,\,fed} &= n_{EO} + n_{CO_2} + n_{C_2H_4,\,out} \\
\text{Define per-pass conversion } X &\equiv \frac{n_{EO} + n_{CO_2}}{n_{C_2H_4,\,fed}} \times 100 \\
\text{Define per-pass selectivity } S &\equiv \frac{n_{EO}}{n_{EO} + n_{CO_2}} \times 100
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 1 — Express product moles in terms of $X$ and $S$:**

$$n_{EO} + n_{CO_2} = \frac{X}{100}\,n_{C_2H_4,\,fed}$$

$$n_{EO} = \frac{S}{100}\,(n_{EO} + n_{CO_2}) = \frac{S \cdot X}{10000}\,n_{C_2H_4,\,fed}$$

**Step 2 — Hence overall single-pass yield:**

$$\boxed{\,Y_{pass} = \frac{S \cdot X}{100}\, \%\, }$$

**Step 3 — Once-recycle overall yield** (assuming complete conversion of recycle with the same $S$ and $X$ per pass):

$$Y_{overall} = \frac{S \cdot X}{100} \cdot \left[\,1 + (1-X) + (1-X)^2 + \cdots\,\right] = \frac{S \cdot X}{100 - [100-X]} \cdot 100$$

$$Y_{overall} = S \;\text{ (in the limit of total recycle)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight:** With *infinite recycle* and *no purge losses*, the **overall yield equals the per-pass selectivity**. This is exactly why **improving selectivity is the central economic driver** of EO plant design.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Problem)

**Problem (KTU-style):** An EO reactor is fed with 5 000 kmol h⁻¹ of ethylene and 1 250 kmol h⁻¹ of oxygen (5 % excess over the 1 : 1 stoichiometric ratio). The per-pass ethylene conversion is 12 % and the selectivity to EO is 86 %. The unconverted ethylene is recycled with 100 % efficiency. Calculate:

(a) the moles of EO produced per hour,
(b) the moles of CO₂ produced per hour,
(c) the overall EO yield, and
(d) the heat released per hour in the reactor (kW).

### (a) EO produced

$$\begin{aligned}
n_{C_2H_4,\,reacted} &= X \cdot n_{C_2H_4,\,fed} = 0.12 \times 5\,000 = 600 \;\text{kmol h}^{-1} \\
n_{EO} &= S \times n_{C_2H_4,\,reacted} = 0.86 \times 600 = \boxed{516 \;\text{kmol h}^{-1}}
\end{aligned}$$

### (b) CO₂ produced

$$\begin{aligned}
n_{CO_2} &= (1 - S) \times n_{C_2H_4,\,reacted} \\
&= (1 - 0.86) \times 600 = \boxed{84 \;\text{kmol h}^{-1}}
\end{aligned}$$

### (c) Overall EO yield

In a recycle loop with $X = 0.12$ and $S = 0.86$:

$$\begin{aligned}
Y_{overall} &= S \left[\frac{X}{1-(1-X)}\right] \\
&= 0.86 \times \frac{0.12}{0.12} = 0.86 \\
&= \boxed{86\%}
\end{aligned}$$

### (d) Heat released

Two contributions:

1. EO formation: $516 \times 105 = 54\,180$ kW
2. Combustion: $84 \times 1323 = 111\,132$ kW

$$\dot{Q}_{total} = 54\,180 + 111\,132 = \boxed{165\,312 \;\text{kW} \;\;(\approx 165\;\text{MW})}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often forget the CO₂ contribution to heat release. The *combustion* side-reaction is **~ 12× more exothermic per mole** than EO formation. Even at 86 % selectivity, ~ **67 %** of the total heat comes from the *unwanted* combustion.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Chlorohydrin vs. Direct Oxidation

| Parameter | Chlorohydrin Process | Direct Oxidation (O₂) | Direct Oxidation (Air) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Catalyst | None (homogeneous) | Ag/α-Al₂O₃ | Ag/α-Al₂O₃ |
| Oxidant | Cl₂ + Ca(OH)₂ | O₂ | Air |
| Selectivity | 80 – 82 % | 88 – 92 % | 75 – 80 % |
| By-products | CaCl₂ brine (10–15 t/t EO) | CO₂, H₂O | CO₂, H₂O, N₂ purge |
| Steam export | Low | High (3–4 t steam/t EO) | Moderate |
| Capex | Low | High (ASU) | Moderate |
| Opex | High (chlorine cost) | Lowest | Moderate |
| Environmental load | **Severe** | Mild | Mild |
| Current global share | < **1 %** | > **85 %** | < 14 % |
| KTU relevance | Historical / theory | **Primary** | Secondary |

## 3.4 Mass & Energy Balance Skeleton (Reactor Block)

| Stream | Component | Flow (kmol h⁻¹) | T (°C) | P (MPa) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Feed** | C₂H₄ | 5 000 | 240 | 2.0 |
| | O₂ | 1 250 | 240 | 2.0 |
| | CH₄ (diluent) | 10 000 | 240 | 2.0 |
| **Outlet** | C₂H₄ (unreacted) | 4 400 | 280 | 1.9 |
| | EO | 516 | 280 | 1.9 |
| | CO₂ | 84 | 280 | 1.9 |
| | H₂O | 84 | 280 | 1.9 |
| | O₂ (unreacted) | 1 166 | 280 | 1.9 |
| | CH₄ | 10 000 | 280 | 1.9 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The reactor outlet must be **rapidly quenched** to prevent EO decomposition, and the EO is then absorbed in water in a packed-column absorber. The aqueous EO is stripped and distilled to **> 99.9 %** polymer-grade purity.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Overall EO Plant Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Ethylene Feed Tank] --> nodeB[Feed Gas Mixer]
    nodeC[Air Separation Unit O2 Supply] --> nodeB
    nodeD[Methane Recycle Compressor] --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeE[Pre-heater 240 C]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Multi-tubular Reactor Ag Catalyst 250-280 C 2 MPa]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Reactor Effluent Quench Boiler Steam Generation]
    nodeG --> nodeH[EO Absorber Water Scrubbing]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Lean Aqueous EO Solution]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[EO Stripping Column]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Refine Column 99.9 percent EO Product]
    nodeJ --> nodeL[Water Recycle to Absorber]
    nodeF --> nodeM[CO2 Absorber Carbonate Wash]
    nodeM --> nodeN[CO2 Vent]
    nodeF --> nodeO[Recycle Gas Compressor]
    nodeO --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeB
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Process Logic — Conversion vs Selectivity Trade-Off

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Increase Temperature] --> nodeB[Conversion UP]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Selectivity DOWN]
    nodeD[Increase GHSV] --> nodeE[Conversion DOWN]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Selectivity UP]
    nodeG[Add Cs or Cl Promoter] --> nodeH[Selectivity UP]
    nodeG --> nodeI[Conversion slight DOWN]
    nodeJ[Increase Pressure] --> nodeK[Conversion UP]
    nodeJ --> nodeL[Selectivity mild DOWN]
    nodeM[Operate in Oxygen Mode] --> nodeN[Selectivity UP 5-10 percent]
    nodeM --> nodeO[Higher Capex ASU]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Application Tree — EO Downstream Chemistry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Ethylene Oxide C2H4O]
    nodeA --> nodeB[Hydrolysis with H2O]
    nodeB --> nodeB1[Mono-ethylene Glycol MEG]
    nodeB --> nodeB2[Di-ethylene Glycol DEG]
    nodeB --> nodeB3[Tri-ethylene Glycol TEG]
    nodeB --> nodeB4[Polyethylene Glycols PEG]

    nodeA --> nodeC[Reaction with NH3]
    nodeC --> nodeC1[Mono-ethanolamine MEA]
    nodeC --> nodeC2[Di-ethanolamine DEA]
    nodeC --> nodeC3[Tri-ethanolamine TEA]

    nodeA --> nodeD[Reaction with Alcohols ROH]
    nodeD --> nodeD1[Ethylene Glycol Ethers]
    nodeD1 --> nodeD1a[Cellosolve 2-ethoxyethanol]
    nodeD1 --> nodeD1b[Carbitol 2-2-butoxyethoxy ethanol]

    nodeA --> nodeE[Ethoxylation of Fatty Alcohols]
    nodeE --> nodeE1[Non-ionic Surfactants]
    nodeE --> nodeE2[Detergents and Shampoos]

    nodeA --> nodeF[Direct Use as Sterilisation Gas]
    nodeF --> nodeF1[Medical Device Sterilisation]
    nodeF --> nodeF2[Spice and Herb Fumigation]

    nodeA --> nodeG[Polymerisation]
    nodeG --> nodeG1[Polyether Polyols for Polyurethanes]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Sequence — Direct Oxidation Molecular Mechanism on Ag

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[C2H4 gas] --> nodeB[Adsorbed on Ag Surface]
    nodeO2[O2 gas] --> nodeC[Dissociatively Adsorbed 2O atom]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Surface Oxametallacycle Intermediate]
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE[C2H4O EO Desorbs]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Total Oxidation Pathway]
    nodeC --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG[CO2 and H2O desorb]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The oxametallacycle intermediate is the **key branching point**. Cs promoter modifies the electronic environment of Ag so that ring closure (→ EO) is favoured over C–C scission (→ CO₂).
