# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Question]
**"What is ethylene oxide? Give its molecular formula and two major industrial applications."**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ethylene oxide (EO)** is the simplest epoxide (oxirane) with molecular formula **C₂H₄O** and molecular weight **44.05 g mol⁻¹**. It is a colourless, flammable gas (boiling point **10.7 °C**) produced by direct catalytic oxidation of ethylene over a silver catalyst.
>
> **Two major applications:**
> 1. **Manufacture of mono-ethylene glycol (MEG)** — used in PET polyester fibre and bottle resin; consumes ~ **60 %** of global EO.
> 2. **Production of ethylene glycol ethers and ethanolamines** — used as industrial solvents, gas-treatment chemicals, and detergent intermediates.

*(Marks: 1 for definition + formula, 1 for applications — 1 for relevance in petrochemical industry.)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]
**"Distinguish between the chlorohydrin route and the direct oxidation route for ethylene oxide manufacture."**

**Model Answer (tabular):**

| Feature | Chlorohydrin | Direct Oxidation |
|---|---|---|
| Catalyst | None (lime + HOCl) | Ag/α-Al₂O₃ |
| Selectivity | ~ 80 % | **86 – 92 %** |
| Main by-product | CaCl₂ brine | CO₂, H₂O |
| Environmental load | Severe | Mild |
| Current global share | < 1 % | > 99 % |

*(3 marks: 1 for chlorohydrin, 1 for direct oxidation, 1 for the distinguishing comment.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A** (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]

**(a)** Describe with a neat flow diagram the **direct oxidation of ethylene to ethylene oxide** over a silver catalyst. Discuss the role of temperature, pressure, and space velocity on selectivity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An EO plant processes **8 000 kg h⁻¹ of ethylene** with **per-pass conversion = 10 %** and **selectivity to EO = 88 %**. Calculate:
  - (i) the mass of EO produced per hour,
  - (ii) the mass of CO₂ produced per hour,
  - (iii) the overall yield (with 100 % recycle). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a):**
- **Reaction:** $\,2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_4 + \text{O}_2 \to 2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_4\text{O}\,$, with side combustion.
- **Catalyst:** Ag (10–20 wt %) on α-Al₂O₃, promoted with Cs, K, Re, Cl.
- **Reactor:** Multi-tubular fixed-bed, with boiling-water cooling to remove the highly exothermic heat (~ 3 200 kcal kg⁻¹ EO).
- **Operating conditions:** T = 220–280 °C, P = 1–3 MPa, GHSV = 3 000–6 000 h⁻¹, SAR > 1 (outside flammable envelope).
- **Effect of variables:**
  - **T ↑** → conversion ↑ but selectivity ↓ (combustion dominates above 300 °C).
  - **P ↑** → conversion ↑; mild negative effect on selectivity; raises explosion risk.
  - **GHSV ↑** → selectivity ↑ (less residence time) but conversion ↓; an optimum exists at the *product S × X maximum*.

**Part (b):**

Given: $\dot{m}_{C_2H_4} = 8\,000$ kg h⁻¹, $M_{C_2H_4} = 28$ kg kmol⁻¹.

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{n}_{C_2H_4,\,fed} &= \frac{8\,000}{28} = 285.71 \;\text{kmol h}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

**(i) EO produced:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{n}_{C_2H_4,\,reacted} &= 0.10 \times 285.71 = 28.571 \;\text{kmol h}^{-1} \\
\dot{n}_{EO} &= 0.88 \times 28.571 = 25.143 \;\text{kmol h}^{-1} \\
\dot{m}_{EO} &= 25.143 \times 44.05 = \boxed{1\,107.5 \;\text{kg h}^{-1}}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) CO₂ produced:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{n}_{CO_2} &= (1 - 0.88) \times 28.571 = 3.4286 \;\text{kmol h}^{-1} \\
\dot{m}_{CO_2} &= 3.4286 \times 44 = \boxed{150.86 \;\text{kg h}^{-1}}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Overall yield (100 % recycle):**

$$Y_{overall} = S = \boxed{88 \%}$$

---

### **Question B** (14 Marks) — Alternative Module Choice

**(a)** Discuss the **major industrial applications of ethylene oxide** with reaction equations for at least three downstream products. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **chlorohydrin process** produces 4 000 kg h⁻¹ of EO. If the selectivity to EO is 82 % and the stoichiometric Ca(OH)₂ requirement is met exactly, calculate:
  - (i) the kg of CaCl₂ produced per hour,
  - (ii) the kg of ethylene chlorohydrin intermediate required per hour, and
  - (iii) the kg of Cl₂ consumed per hour. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a):** Equations and applications:

1. **Hydrolysis → MEG:** $\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_4\text{O} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \to \text{HOCH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$ — antifreeze, PET resin.

2. **Ammonolysis → MEA:** $\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_4\text{O} + \text{NH}_3 \to \text{HOCH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{NH}_2$ — gas sweetening, detergents.

3. **Alcoholysis → Cellosolve:** $\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_4\text{O} + \text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} \to \text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OCH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$ — solvent for paints, lacquers.

4. **Ethoxylation → non-ionic surfactants:** $\,\text{ROH} + n\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_4\text{O} \to \text{R(OCH}_2\text{CH}_2)_n\text{OH}$ — detergents.

5. **Sterilisation gas:** pure EO used in autoclaves to sterilise heat-sensitive medical devices.

**Part (b):**

Given: $\dot{m}_{EO} = 4\,000$ kg h⁻¹, $M_{EO} = 44.05$ kg kmol⁻¹, $S = 0.82$.

$$\dot{n}_{EO} = \frac{4\,000}{44.05} = 90.81 \;\text{kmol h}^{-1}$$

**Total ethylene reacted:**

$$\dot{n}_{C_2H_4,\,reacted} = \frac{\dot{n}_{EO}}{S} = \frac{90.81}{0.82} = 110.74 \;\text{kmol h}^{-1}$$

From overall reaction:

$$2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_4 \to 2\,\text{EO} \to 1\,\text{CaCl}_2$$

Stoichiometry: 2 kmol EO ⇔ 1 kmol CaCl₂.

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{n}_{CaCl_2} &= \frac{\dot{n}_{EO}}{2} = \frac{90.81}{2} = 45.41 \;\text{kmol h}^{-1} \\
\dot{m}_{CaCl_2} &= 45.41 \times 110.98 = \boxed{5\,039 \;\text{kg h}^{-1}}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Ethylene chlorohydrin intermediate (ClCH₂CH₂OH, M = 80.51 kg kmol⁻¹):**

From step-1 stoichiometry 1 kmol C₂H₄ → 1 kmol chlorohydrin → 1 kmol EO; so the chlorohydrin required equals $\dot{n}_{EO} = 90.81$ kmol h⁻¹.

$$\dot{m}_{chlorohydrin} = 90.81 \times 80.51 = \boxed{7\,310 \;\text{kg h}^{-1}}$$

**(iii) Cl₂ consumed (M = 70.9 kg kmol⁻¹):**

From step-1: 1 kmol Cl₂ → 1 kmol chlorohydrin → 1 kmol EO.

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{n}_{Cl_2} &= 90.81 \;\text{kmol h}^{-1} \\
\dot{m}_{Cl_2} &= 90.81 \times 70.9 = \boxed{6\,438 \;\text{kg h}^{-1}}
\end{aligned}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in EO questions:**
> 1. **Confusing selectivity, conversion, and yield.** Always write the *definition formula* before plugging in numbers — examiners reward that. Marks are lost when $S$, $X$, and $Y$ are used interchangeably.
> 2. **Forgetting the CO₂ side-reaction heat.** Even at 90 % selectivity, ~ 50 % of reactor heat comes from the *unwanted* combustion. Always add both $\Delta H$ contributions.
> 3. **Ignoring the unit “kmol h⁻¹” vs. “kg h⁻¹”.** Many KTU solutions lose 1–2 marks because students do not convert molecular weights (e.g., 28 for C₂H₄, 44.05 for EO, 44 for CO₂, 110.98 for CaCl₂).
> 4. **Drawing flow diagrams with no labels on streams.** KTU evaluators deduct marks when feeds/products are not clearly identified.
> 5. **Mixing up air- and oxygen-mode processes.** Always state explicitly which variant you are discussing.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Ethylene oxide = C₂H₄O**, MW = 44.05, bp = **10.7 °C**; an epoxide with high ring strain (~ **27 kcal mol⁻¹**) — the source of its reactivity.
- **Two industrial routes:** *chlorohydrin* (obsolete, gives CaCl₂ waste) and **direct catalytic oxidation** (modern, Ag-catalysed, dominant).
- **Catalyst:** Ag on α-Al₂O₃, promoted with Cs, K, Re, Cl; only Ag gives useful EO selectivity.
- **Operating window:** T = 220–300 °C, P = 1–3 MPa, GHSV = 3 000–6 000 h⁻¹, SAR outside the explosive envelope.
- **Selectivity (86–92 %)** is the *economic* driver; **conversion per pass (10–15 %)** is intentionally low to preserve selectivity.
- **Heat removal** is critical: ~ 3 200 kcal per kg EO; multi-tubular reactor with boiling-water cooling is the standard.
- **Oxygen-mode** has replaced **air-mode** in > 95 % of new plants due to higher selectivity and lower ethylene loss.
- **EO must be rapidly quenched** at the reactor outlet to prevent decomposition back to CO₂ + H₂O.
- **Major applications (in order of volume):**
  1. **MEG → PET** (antifreeze, polyester fibre, bottles) — ~ **60 %**
  2. **Ethoxylates / surfactants** — ~ **12 %**
  3. **Glycol ethers** (solvents) — ~ **10 %**
  4. **Ethanolamines** (MEA, DEA, TEA) — ~ **8 %**
  5. **Medical sterilisation gas** — ~ **5 %**
- **Key formulas to memorise:**
  - $Y_{pass} = S \cdot X / 100$
  - $Y_{overall} = S$ (with total recycle)
  - $\dot{Q} = \dot{n}_{EO}|\Delta H_{EO}| + \dot{n}_{CO_2}|\Delta H_{comb}|$
- **Industrial safety fact:** EO has one of the **widest flammable ranges in air (3–100 %)** and is a confirmed *human carcinogen* — handle in closed systems with continuous monitoring.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vinyl Chloride
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT741 - PETROCHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Vinyl Chloride

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Vinyl Chloride?

**Vinyl Chloride (VC)**, also known as **chloroethene** or **vinyl chloride monomer (VCM)**, is a colorless, flammable gas at room temperature (boiling point **−13.4 °C**) with a faintly sweet, ether-like odor. Its molecular formula is **C₂H₃Cl** and its structure is **CH₂=CHCl**. It is the **monomeric precursor to Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC)**, the world's third-most-produced synthetic plastic polymer after polyethylene and polypropylene.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Vinyl chloride monomer (VCM) is a petrochemical intermediate manufactured predominantly via the chlorination/oxychlorination of ethylene followed by dehydrochlorination of 1,2-dichloroethane (EDC). It is the sole monomer used in the industrial production of PVC.*

**IUPAC Name:** Chloroethene
**CAS Number:** 75-01-4
**Molecular Weight:** 62.50 g/mol
**Density (liquid at 0 °C):** 0.97 g/cm³

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of vinyl chloride as a **"Lego brick"** with a special magnetic end (the C=C double bond). Each brick is too small to be useful alone, but when you string millions of these bricks end-to-end in a **polymerization reactor** (the builder's table), you create a long, repeating chain called **PVC**, which is the rigid, weather-resistant plastic used in plumbing pipes, electrical cable insulation, window frames, and blood-storage bags.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is Vinyl Chloride important to KTU students?**
> - It is a **core building-block petrochemical** (C1/C2 chemistry)
> - It links **ethylene (from crackers)** to **downstream polymer industries**
> - It involves **key reaction engineering concepts** — chlorination, oxychlorination, and thermal cracking (pyrolysis)
> - It is a **classical example of a balanced/integrated chemical process** where HCl is recycled

## 1.3 Industrial Significance of PVC

- **Global production capacity:** > 60 million metric tons/year
- **Single largest end-use:** Construction (pipes, profiles, flooring — ~60% of PVC demand)
- **Feedstock flexibility:** Ethylene-based (90% global) vs. Acetylene-based (mainly in China due to coal-derived calcium carbide)
- **Hazard profile:** VCM is a **Group 1 human carcinogen (IARC)**; therefore, manufacturing plants follow **Closed Loop, Zero-Emission** reactor designs to protect workers and the environment.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Vinyl Chloride Manufacturing Routes

Vinyl chloride is produced commercially by **four major process families**, classified by the C₂ feedstock used:

| Route | Feedstock | Pre-dominance | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Acetylene Route** | CaC₂-derived C₂H₂ | Small-scale, China | Declining |
| **2. Ethylene — Direct Chlorination + EDC Cracking** | C₂H₄ + Cl₂ | Most common (integrated) | Industry standard |
| **3. Ethylene — Oxychlorination + EDC Cracking** | C₂H₄ + HCl + O₂ | HCl recycle | Industry standard |
| **4. Ethane Direct Route (e.g., EVC, INNOVERA process)** | C₂H₆ + Cl₂ (one-step) | Pilot/Commercial | Emerging |

## 2.2 Reaction Chemistry — Step by Step

### A. The Acetylene Route (CH≡CH + HCl)
This is the **historically oldest** and **thermodynamically simplest** route.

$$CH \equiv CH + HCl \longrightarrow CH_2 = CHCl \quad \Delta H = -98.5 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

- Catalysts: **HgCl₂ on activated carbon** (historically) or **Au-based catalysts** (modern, less toxic).
- Reaction type: **Electrophilic addition** of HCl across the triple bond.
- Temperature: **140–200 °C**, Pressure: **1–1.5 atm**.
- Selectivity to VCM: ~98%, by-product is 1,1-dichloroethane.

### B. The Ethylene Route (Two-Step Process)
This is the **modern, world-standard process** and constitutes ~90% of global VCM capacity. It comprises **two intermediate reactions** at EDC level, **plus one cracking step**.

#### Step B.1 — Direct Chlorination of Ethylene
$$CH_2 = CH_2 + Cl_2 \longrightarrow CH_2 Cl - CH_2 Cl \quad \Delta H = -180 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

- **1,2-Dichloroethane (EDC)** is produced.
- Carried out in **liquid EDC** (acts as solvent) using **FeCl₃ or SbCl₅** as Lewis-acid catalyst.
- Temperature: **40–70 °C**; exothermic; needs cooling.

#### Step B.2 — Thermal Cracking (Pyrolysis) of EDC
$$CH_2 Cl - CH_2 Cl \longrightarrow CH_2 = CHCl + HCl \quad \Delta H = +71 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

- **Endothermic**; carried out in **fired tubular furnaces** (similar to ethylene cracking).
- Temperature: **480–550 °C**, Pressure: **10–35 atm**.
- Conversion per pass: ~50–60%; selectivity to VCM: ~99%.
- **HCl** produced here is **recycled** to the oxychlorination reactor.

#### Step B.3 — Oxychlorination of Ethylene
$$CH_2 = CH_2 + 2HCl + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \longrightarrow CH_2 Cl - CH_2 Cl + H_2O \quad \Delta H = -238 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

- **Hughes / Stauffer / Kelchlor processes** use **CuCl₂ on alumina** catalyst (fluidized bed or fixed bed).
- Temperature: **220–250 °C**, Pressure: **1–10 atm**.
- **Cu²⁺ is reduced to Cu⁺** during chlorination; **O₂ re-oxidizes Cu⁺ to Cu²⁺** in situ (Deacon-type redox cycle).

## 2.3 The "Balanced Process" (Net Reaction)

Adding Steps B.1 + B.2 + 2×(B.3) and cancelling intermediate EDC and HCl, the **net reaction** is:

$$2 \, CH_2=CH_2 + Cl_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \longrightarrow 2 \, CH_2=CHCl + H_2O$$

**Interpretation:** The balanced process consumes **only ethylene, chlorine, and oxygen** and produces **only VCM and water** — no net HCl by-product, no net EDC inventory. This is a **textbook example of a perfectly atom-economical industrial process** and is therefore heavily emphasized in KTU exams.

> [!NOTE]
> **Multiplication factor for Step B.3:**
> Since Step B.2 produces 1 mol HCl per mol EDC cracked, and Step B.1 consumes 1 mol Cl₂ (and no HCl), the *recycled* HCl must be exactly the amount produced in Step B.2. Therefore Step B.3 is run at a rate equal to Step B.2. Hence, the overall molar ratio is 1:1:1:1 (ethylene : chlorine : oxygen : VCM) for the balanced route.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Standard Conditions | Industrial Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_2H_2 + HCl \rightarrow CH_2=CHCl$ | $\Delta H = -98.5$ kJ/mol, 140–200 °C, HgCl₂/Au cat. | Obsolete route, China |
| 2 | $C_2H_4 + Cl_2 \rightarrow C_2H_4Cl_2$ (EDC) | $\Delta H = -180$ kJ/mol, 40–70 °C, FeCl₃ cat. | Direct chlorination |
| 3 | $C_2H_4Cl_2 \rightarrow CH_2=CHCl + HCl$ | $\Delta H = +71$ kJ/mol, 480–550 °C | EDC cracking |
| 4 | $C_2H_4 + 2HCl + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow C_2H_4Cl_2 + H_2O$ | $\Delta H = -238$ kJ/mol, 220–250 °C, CuCl₂ cat. | Oxychlorination |
| 5 | $2\,C_2H_4 + Cl_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow 2\,CH_2=CHCl + H_2O$ | Net / Balanced | Atom-economy ideal |
| 6 | VCM Boiling Point = $-13.4\ ^\circ C$ | Latent heat of vap = 358 kJ/kg | Refrigeration/Storage |
| 7 | Activation Energy (EDC cracking) $\approx$ 220 kJ/mol | Arrhenius, first-order | Reactor design |
| 8 | HCl recycle ratio = 1:1 (mol/mol) with C₂H₄ in oxychlorination | Stoichiometric | Mass balance |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Reaction engineering:** The integrated ethylene route teaches students the **concept of recycle streams, redox catalyst cycles (Cu²⁺/Cu⁺), and process integration**.
- **Polymer linkage:** VCM is the gateway to **PVC**, which is the most chlorine-consuming polymer globally — hence the chlorine industry is heavily tied to ethylene availability.
- **Safety engineering:** VCM's carcinogenicity makes it the cornerstone case study in **closed-loop process design, vent scrubbing, and fugitive-emission control** in the chemical industry.
- **Sustainability:** Newer **ethane direct routes** (e.g., Single-step EDC-to-VCM via oxychlorination of ethane) eliminate EDC cracking and reduce CO₂ footprint by ~30%.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the "Balanced Process" Net Equation

This is the **most frequently asked KTU 14-marker** derivation. We will start from the three elementary reactions and combine them algebraically.

### Step 1 — Write down the three elementary reactions
The integrated process consists of three reactors in series/parallel:
- **Reactor A** (Direct Chlorination):
$$R_1:\ CH_2=CH_2 + Cl_2 \longrightarrow CH_2Cl-CH_2Cl \quad (\text{EDC})$$
- **Reactor B** (EDC Cracking):
$$R_2:\ CH_2Cl-CH_2Cl \longrightarrow CH_2=CHCl + HCl$$
- **Reactor C** (Oxychlorination):
$$R_3:\ CH_2=CH_2 + 2HCl + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \longrightarrow CH_2Cl-CH_2Cl + H_2O$$

### Step 2 — Combine Reactor A and Reactor B
The EDC produced in $R_1$ feeds directly into $R_2$. Adding $R_1 + R_2$:

$$\begin{aligned}
(R_1):\quad & CH_2=CH_2 + Cl_2 \longrightarrow CH_2Cl-CH_2Cl \\
(R_2):\quad & CH_2Cl-CH_2Cl \longrightarrow CH_2=CHCl + HCl \\
\hline
(R_1 + R_2):\quad & CH_2=CH_2 + Cl_2 \longrightarrow CH_2=CHCl + HCl
\end{aligned}$$

**Net result:** 1 mol C₂H₄ + 1 mol Cl₂ → 1 mol VCM + 1 mol HCl.

### Step 3 — Incorporate Reactor C to consume the HCl
Reactor $R_3$ consumes 2 mol HCl per mol of C₂H₄, but the net reaction $(R_1 + R_2)$ produces only 1 mol HCl. Therefore, $R_3$ must be multiplied by a factor that consumes exactly 1 mol HCl.

**Required multiplier for $R_3$:**

$$\text{Multiplier} = \frac{\text{HCl produced in } (R_1+R_2)}{\text{HCl consumed per }R_3} = \frac{1}{2}$$

Therefore, we use $\tfrac{1}{2} \times R_3$:

$$\tfrac{1}{2}R_3:\ \tfrac{1}{2}CH_2=CH_2 + HCl + \tfrac{1}{4}O_2 \longrightarrow \tfrac{1}{2}CH_2Cl-CH_2Cl + \tfrac{1}{2}H_2O$$

### Step 4 — Add $(R_1 + R_2)$ and $\tfrac{1}{2}R_3$, then cancel EDC
The EDC produced in $\tfrac{1}{2}R_3$ equals $\tfrac{1}{2}$ mol, but $(R_1 + R_2)$ consumes 1 mol of EDC internally. Therefore, the *external* EDC that needs to be produced is **only $\tfrac{1}{2}$ mol**, which is exactly what $\tfrac{1}{2}R_3$ provides.

Adding all three:

$$\begin{aligned}
(R_1 + R_2):\quad & CH_2=CH_2 + Cl_2 \longrightarrow CH_2=CHCl + HCl \\
\tfrac{1}{2}(R_3):\quad & \tfrac{1}{2}CH_2=CH_2 + HCl + \tfrac{1}{4}O_2 \longrightarrow \tfrac{1}{2}CH_2Cl-CH_2Cl + \tfrac{1}{2}H_2O \\
\hline
\text{Sum}:\quad & \tfrac{3}{2}CH_2=CH_2 + Cl_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}CH_2=CH_2 + HCl + \tfrac{1}{4}O_2 \longrightarrow \\
& CH_2=CHCl + HCl + \tfrac{1}{2}CH_2Cl-CH_2Cl + \tfrac{1}{2}H_2O
\end{aligned}$$

Wait — this is the *un-cancelled* sum. The internal EDC from $R_1$ (1 mol) is consumed by $R_2$ *internally*, while the **fresh EDC** from $\tfrac{1}{2}R_3$ is $\tfrac{1}{2}$ mol, which must enter $R_2$ along with the EDC from $R_1$.

### Step 5 — Correct algebraic cancellation
Let us treat the **integrated plant** by using a **stoichiometric table** for 2 mol of VCM output (to avoid fractions).

For 2 mol VCM produced, we need **2 mol EDC cracked** in $R_2$. This 2 mol EDC comes from:
- 1 mol from $R_1$ (using 1 mol C₂H₄ + 1 mol Cl₂)
- 1 mol from $R_3$ (using 1 mol C₂H₄ + 2 mol HCl + ½ mol O₂)

Now $R_2$ on 2 mol EDC produces 2 mol VCM + **2 mol HCl**, which is exactly the 2 mol HCl consumed by $R_3$. So HCl is **internally recycled**. EDC is **internally recycled**. No external EDC or HCl is needed.

**Net (for 2 mol VCM output):**

$$\begin{aligned}
(R_1):\quad & CH_2=CH_2 + Cl_2 \longrightarrow C_2H_4Cl_2 \\
(R_3):\quad & CH_2=CH_2 + 2HCl + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \longrightarrow C_2H_4Cl_2 + H_2O \\
(R_2):\quad & C_2H_4Cl_2 + C_2H_4Cl_2 \longrightarrow CH_2=CHCl + HCl + CH_2=CHCl + HCl \\
\hline
\text{Net}:\quad & 2\,CH_2=CH_2 + Cl_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \longrightarrow 2\,CH_2=CHCl + H_2O
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6 — Final Balanced Equation

$$\boxed{2\,CH_2=CH_2 + Cl_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \longrightarrow 2\,CH_2=CHCl + H_2O}$$

**Dividing throughout by 2 (per mol of VCM):**

$$CH_2=CH_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}Cl_2 + \tfrac{1}{4}O_2 \longrightarrow CH_2=CHCl + \tfrac{1}{2}H_2O$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Routes

| Parameter | Acetylene Route | Ethylene Direct-Chlorination | Ethylene Oxychlorination | Ethane Direct |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Net reaction** | C₂H₂ + HCl → VCM | C₂H₄ + Cl₂ → VCM + HCl | C₂H₄ + 2HCl + ½O₂ → VCM + 2H₂O | C₂H₆ + Cl₂ → VCM + 2HCl |
| **Feedstock cost** | High (CaC₂-based) | Moderate | Moderate (uses waste HCl) | Low (ethane abundant) |
| **By-products** | None major | HCl (1 mol/mol) | H₂O | HCl, EDC |
| **Catalyst** | HgCl₂ / Au | FeCl₃, SbCl₅ | CuCl₂/Al₂O₃ | Cu/K/La oxide |
| **Temp (°C)** | 140–200 | 40–70 (chlorination), 480–550 (cracking) | 220–250 | 400–500 |
| **Selectivity** | ~98% | ~99% | ~95% | ~85–90% |
| **KTU relevance** | Historical + 3-marker | Core 14-marker | Core 14-marker | Advanced 14-marker |
| **Industrial share** | ~10% (China) | Embedded in balanced route | Embedded in balanced route | < 2% (emerging) |

## 3.3 Numerical Example (KTU-style Problem)

> **Problem:** A VCM plant produces 100,000 tonnes/year of vinyl chloride. The plant uses the **balanced ethylene route**. Assuming 100% conversion and 95% selectivity, calculate:
> (a) Moles of ethylene consumed per year.
> (b) Mass of chlorine and oxygen required per year.
> (c) Mass of water produced as by-product.

**Given:**
- Production rate: 100,000 tonnes VCM/year = $1 \times 10^{8}$ kg/year
- Molecular weight VCM = 62.5 g/mol
- Operating days: 330 days/year (assumed)

### Solution:

**(a) Moles of VCM produced per year:**

$$n_{VCM} = \frac{1 \times 10^{11} \text{ g}}{62.5 \text{ g/mol}} = 1.6 \times 10^{9} \text{ mol/year}$$

From the balanced equation, **2 mol VCM requires 2 mol C₂H₄** (1:1 ratio), so:

$$n_{C_2H_4} = 1.6 \times 10^{9} \text{ mol/year}$$

Mass of ethylene (MW = 28 g/mol):

$$m_{C_2H_4} = 1.6 \times 10^{9} \times 28 \times 10^{-3} = 44{,}800 \text{ tonnes/year}$$

**(b) Chlorine required (1 mol per 2 mol VCM):**

$$n_{Cl_2} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.6 \times 10^{9} = 8.0 \times 10^{8} \text{ mol/year}$$

$$m_{Cl_2} = 8.0 \times 10^{8} \times 71 \times 10^{-3} = 56{,}800 \text{ tonnes/year}$$

**Oxygen required (½ mol per 2 mol VCM):**

$$n_{O_2} = \tfrac{1}{4} \times 1.6 \times 10^{9} = 4.0 \times 10^{8} \text{ mol/year}$$

$$m_{O_2} = 4.0 \times 10^{8} \times 32 \times 10^{-3} = 12{,}800 \text{ tonnes/year}$$

**(c) Water produced (1 mol per 2 mol VCM):**

$$n_{H_2O} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.6 \times 10^{9} = 8.0 \times 10^{8} \text{ mol/year}$$

$$m_{H_2O} = 8.0 \times 10^{8} \times 18 \times 10^{-3} = 14{,}400 \text{ tonnes/year}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Always state the **operating days/year** assumption clearly and **watch for selectivity** — multiply feed by $1/\text{selectivity}$ if the question states <100% selectivity.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**"Define vinyl chloride monomer (VCM). Mention its IUPAC name and the primary polymer derived from it."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Vinyl chloride monomer (VCM) is a chlorinated C₂ hydrocarbon with the molecular formula **CH₂=CHCl**, manufactured industrially from ethylene or acetylene and used exclusively as the monomer for polyvinyl chloride (PVC) production.
- **IUPAC name (1 mark):** **Chloroethene**
- **Polymer derived (1 mark):** **Polyvinyl chloride (PVC)** — a thermoplastic polymer produced by free-radical polymerization of VCM.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**"Why is vinyl chloride stored as a liquid under pressure rather than as a gas at ambient conditions?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Boiling point of VCM is −13.4 °C** (well below ambient) — so at room temperature, VCM exists as a **highly flammable gas** (1 mark).
2. Liquid storage under moderate pressure (~3–7 bar) reduces the risk of large flammable vapor cloud formation in case of a leak (1 mark).
3. Liquid storage also **reduces explosion limits exposure** and simplifies metering to downstream PVC polymerization reactors (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — Direct Chlorination + Cracking Route

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of balanced chemical equations, explain the **direct chlorination of ethylene followed by EDC cracking** as a route to vinyl chloride. Mention the catalysts, operating conditions, and the role of HCl. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A VCM plant operates the **balanced integrated process** (direct chlorination + oxychlorination + EDC cracking). Derive the **net balanced equation** and calculate the **feed requirements (in kg/day)** for a plant producing **150 tonnes/day of VCM**. Assume 100% conversion and 100% selectivity. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Direct chlorination + EDC cracking route (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Direct Chlorination (2 marks):**

$$CH_2=CH_2 + Cl_2 \xrightarrow{FeCl_3, 40-70\,^\circ C} CH_2Cl-CH_2Cl$$

- Ethylene and chlorine react in **liquid EDC solvent** with **FeCl₃ or SbCl₅** as Lewis acid catalyst.
- Reaction is **strongly exothermic** ($\Delta H = -180$ kJ/mol) — needs jacket cooling.

**Step 2 — EDC Cracking (Pyrolysis) (3 marks):**

$$CH_2Cl-CH_2Cl \xrightarrow{480-550\,^\circ C, 10-35\text{ atm}} CH_2=CHCl + HCl$$

- Carried out in a **fired tubular furnace** (radiant section) similar to a steam cracker.
- **Endothermic** ($\Delta H = +71$ kJ/mol).
- Conversion per pass: ~55%, selectivity: ~99%.
- **HCl produced is recycled to the oxychlorination reactor** to prevent loss of chlorine value.

**Step 3 — Role of HCl (2 marks):**
- HCl is a by-product of the cracking step.
- In an integrated plant, HCl is **recycled** to the oxychlorination reactor (Step B.3), where it reacts with ethylene and O₂ to regenerate EDC.
- This makes the **overall process atom-economical**: only fresh Cl₂ and O₂ are added externally.

---

**(b) Net balanced equation + feed calculation (7 marks):**

**Derivation of net equation (3 marks):**

Combining the three reactions as derived in Section 3.1:

$$2\,CH_2=CH_2 + Cl_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \longrightarrow 2\,CH_2=CHCl + H_2O$$

- HCl and EDC are **internally recycled**; no net by-product.
- 2 mol C₂H₄ → 2 mol VCM (1:1 stoichiometry).

**Feed calculations (4 marks):**

Given: VCM production = 150 tonnes/day = 150 × 10⁶ g/day
Moles of VCM = $\frac{150 \times 10^6}{62.5} = 2.4 \times 10^6$ mol/day

**Ethylene feed** (1:1 mol ratio with VCM):
$$m_{C_2H_4} = 2.4 \times 10^6 \times 28 \times 10^{-3} = 67{,}200 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Chlorine feed** (½ mol per mol VCM):
$$m_{Cl_2} = 1.2 \times 10^6 \times 71 \times 10^{-3} = 85{,}200 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Oxygen feed** (¼ mol per mol VCM):
$$m_{O_2} = 6.0 \times 10^5 \times 32 \times 10^{-3} = 19{,}200 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Water produced** (½ mol per mol VCM):
$$m_{H_2O} = 1.2 \times 10^6 \times 18 \times 10^{-3} = 21{,}600 \text{ kg/day}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply R3 by ½** — students often write the net equation as $C_2H_4 + Cl_2 + 2HCl + ½ O_2 \to 2 VCM + H_2O$ and never cancel HCl (lose 2 marks).
> 2. **Unit errors** — confusing tonnes with kg/day. Always write units explicitly: **1 tonne = 10³ kg**.
> 3. **Stoichiometry of VCM-to-C₂H₄ = 1:1** is true ONLY for the balanced route. For the pure direct-chlorination route, the ratio is **1 mol VCM per 1 mol Cl₂** (not ethylene).
> 4. **Selectivity adjustment**: if the question states selectivity <100%, multiply feed by $\frac{1}{\text{selectivity}}$. Most students forget this and lose 1–2 marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Acetylene Route + Comparison

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Describe the **acetylene-based route** for the manufacture of vinyl chloride. Include the reaction equation, catalyst, conditions, advantages, and limitations. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Compare the **acetylene route** with the **ethylene-based balanced route** in terms of feedstock cost, environmental impact, by-products, and process safety. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Acetylene route (7 marks):**

**Reaction (2 marks):**
$$CH \equiv CH + HCl \xrightarrow{HgCl_2 / C,\ 140-200\,^\circ C} CH_2=CHCl \quad \Delta H = -98.5 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Catalyst (1 mark):**
- **Mercuric chloride (HgCl₂) on activated carbon** — classical catalyst.
- Modern variants use **gold-based catalysts** to avoid mercury toxicity.

**Process (2 marks):**
- Acetylene is generated from **calcium carbide** (CaC₂ + 2H₂O → C₂H₂ + Ca(OH)₂) or from natural gas partial oxidation.
- Gaseous HCl is mixed with C₂H₂ in a fixed-bed reactor at **1–1.5 atm**.
- Heat must be **removed** (exothermic); temperature control critical to avoid coking.

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- **Single-step, high selectivity (~98%)**.
- No equilibrium limitations.

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- Acetylene is **expensive** (energy-intensive CaC₂ route) and **endangered substance** due to high explosivity.
- Mercury catalyst is **highly toxic**; environmental concerns.
- Obsolete except in **coal-rich China**.

---

**(b) Comparison (7 marks):**

| Criterion (1.75 marks each) | Acetylene Route | Ethylene Balanced Route |
|---|---|---|
| **Feedstock cost** | High (CaC₂ / coal) | Moderate (cheap ethylene from crackers) |
| **Environmental impact** | Hg toxicity, CO₂ from CaC₂ | Catalysts are Cu/Fe-based; CO₂ from cracking furnace |
| **By-products** | 1,1-DCE (minor) | **Only water** (clean) |
| **Process safety** | C₂H₂ explosive (>2.5% in air); toxic Hg catalyst | VCM carcinogen; mitigated by closed-loop design |
| **Atom economy** | 100% (additive) | ~100% (balanced) |
| **Industrial status** | Declining (~10% share, China) | World standard (~90% share) |
| **Net reaction** | C₂H₂ + HCl → VCM | 2C₂H₄ + Cl₂ + ½O₂ → 2VCM + H₂O |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes in comparison questions (KTU):**
> 1. **Writing one-sided answers** — examiners want *both* routes discussed, not just one.
> 2. **No quantitative data** — always include at least one number (e.g., 90% global share for ethylene route).
> 3. **Ignoring the carcinogenic nature of VCM** — the question often has a CO link to "process safety" or "industrial hygiene."
> 4. **No mention of Hg catalyst toxicity** in the acetylene route.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for Vinyl Chloride**

- **VCM = chloroethene = CH₂=CHCl**, MW = 62.5 g/mol, BP = **−13.4 °C**, IARC **Group 1 carcinogen**.
- **PVC** is the third-largest commodity plastic, made by free-radical polymerization of VCM.
- **Four industrial routes**: Acetylene-HCl, Ethylene-Direct-Chlorination, Ethylene-Oxychlorination, Ethane-Direct (emerging).
- **Direct chlorination** uses **FeCl₃/SbCl₅** catalyst at **40–70 °C** in liquid EDC solvent; **strongly exothermic**.
- **EDC cracking** at **480–550 °C, 10–35 atm** in fired tubular furnace; **endothermic**, generates **HCl**.
- **Oxychlorination** at **220–250 °C** with **CuCl₂ on Al₂O₃** catalyst; consumes HCl + O₂.
- **Net balanced reaction:** $\mathbf{2\,C_2H_4 + Cl_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \to 2\,VCM + H_2O}$ — **only water is the net by-product**.
- **HCl recycle** is the heart of process integration — never shown in textbooks as a "waste" stream in the integrated route.
- **Catalyst redox cycle** in oxychlorination: Cu²⁺ chlorinates ethylene → Cu⁺; O₂ re-oxidizes Cu⁺ → Cu²⁺ (Deacon-type).
- **Acetylene route** uses **HgCl₂ on carbon** catalyst — toxic; being phased out.
- **Stoichiometric ratio** for balanced process: 1 mol VCM = 1 mol C₂H₄ = ½ mol Cl₂ = ¼ mol O₂.
- **KTU 14-marker classic:** Derive the net equation; always multiply R3 by ½ and cancel internal EDC/HCl.
- **Safety hallmark:** All modern VCM plants are **closed-loop, zero-emission** due to VCM's carcinogenicity.
- **Numerical problem format:** Given production in tonnes/day, find feed in kg/day using MWs — VCM = 62.5, C₂H₄ = 28, Cl₂ = 71, O₂ = 32, H₂O = 18.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Module:** Module 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Vinyl Acetate (VAc)** is an unsaturated organic ester with the chemical formula **CH₂=CH–O–CO–CH₃** (molecular formula **C₄H₆O₂**, molecular weight **86.09 g/mol**). It is the vinyl ester of acetic acid, belonging to the family of **vinyl monomers** that polymerize via free-radical addition through the C=C double bond. Vinyl acetate is the world's most-produced vinyl monomer by volume after vinyl chloride and is the key precursor to a wide family of emulsion and solution polymers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Vinyl Acetate is studied as a classic example of an **oxidative acetoxylation reaction** (also called the vinylation of acetic acid), and is industrially produced by the **vapor-phase acetoxylation of ethylene** in the presence of oxygen and a noble-metal catalyst.

### Key Physical & Thermodynamic Properties

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Boiling Point | **72.7 °C** |
| Melting Point | **−93.2 °C** |
| Density (at 20 °C) | **0.934 g/cm³** |
| Flash Point (closed cup) | **−8 °C** (highly flammable) |
| Auto-ignition Temperature | **402 °C** |
| Refractive Index (nD²⁰) | **1.3959** |
| Vapor Pressure (at 20 °C) | **88 mm Hg** |
| Solubility in Water | **23 g/L (at 20 °C)** |
| Heat of Vaporization | **32.7 kJ/mol** |
| Heat of Polymerization | **~89 kJ/mol** |
| Explosive Limits in Air | **2.6 – 13.4 vol %** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these numbers matter in KTU exams:** Vapor pressure, flash point, and explosive limits are direct questions every year. The **low boiling point (72.7 °C)** and **wide flammable range** make VAc a Class IB flammable liquid (NFPA) and drive the entire downstream separation design (distillation must be done under vacuum or low pressure with inhibitors like hydroquinone).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you have a "key" (the **C=C double bond** of ethylene) and a "lock" (the **–OH group of acetic acid**). Normally, ethylene and acetic acid don't want to react with each other directly because both are stable. However, if you pass a **mixture of ethylene + acetic acid + oxygen** over a **special metallic surface (a palladium-based catalyst)** at moderate temperature, the palladium "matchmaker" grabs onto the ethylene, loosens its double bond, and simultaneously activates the acetic acid — making them want to unite. The oxygen acts like a broom that sweeps away the hydrogen "leftover" (forming water). The result is **Vinyl Acetate** — a hybrid molecule with an "acetate handle" on one side and a reactive "vinyl tail" on the other.

This **vinyl tail** is the magic: thousands of these tails can hook onto each other in a chain reaction, producing **polyvinyl acetate (PVA)** — the white glue used in school carpentry classes, the binder in interior latex paints, and the water-soluble film used in laundry detergent pods.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Industrial Manufacturing Route — Vapor-Phase Acetoxylation

The **only commercially dominant route** to vinyl acetate today is the **vapor-phase oxidative acetoxylation of ethylene**, originally developed by **Bayer (Hoechst) in the 1960s** and now operated worldwide by **Celanese, LyondellBasell, Dow, INEOS, Sinopec, Sipchem** and others. It superseded the older liquid-phase (mercury-catalyzed) acetylene route.

### 2.1.1 The Master Reaction (Net Stoichiometry)

The **main reaction** is the oxidative coupling of ethylene with acetic acid in the presence of oxygen:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CH}_2\!=\!\text{CH}_2 \; + \; \text{CH}_3\text{COOH} \; + \; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{O}_2 &\longrightarrow \text{CH}_2\!=\!\text{CH–O–CO–CH}_3 \; + \; \text{H}_2\text{O} \\
\Delta H_{298} &\approx -175 \;\text{kJ/mol (exothermic)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.1.2 Inevitable Side Reactions (Why yield is never 100%)

The presence of **free oxygen** at 150–200 °C guarantees competing **total oxidation** pathways:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CH}_2\!=\!\text{CH}_2 + 3\,\text{O}_2 &\longrightarrow 2\,\text{CO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \quad (\Delta H \approx -1323 \;\text{kJ/mol})\\
\text{CH}_3\text{COOH} + 2\,\text{O}_2 &\longrightarrow 2\,\text{CO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \quad (\text{acid burn})\\
\text{CH}_2\!=\!\texttext{CH}_2 + \text{CH}_3\text{COOH} &\longrightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{COOC}_2\text{H}_5 \quad (\text{ethyl acetate byproduct})\\
\text{CH}_2\!=\!\text{CH}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{O}_2 &\longrightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{CHO} \quad (\text{acetaldehyde})\\
2\,\text{CH}_2\!=\!\text{CH–OAc} &\longrightarrow \text{(acetals, dimers, polymers)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heat Management Reality Check:** A single mole of **CO₂ side reaction releases 7.5× more heat** than the desired VAc reaction. Even **1% selectivity loss to CO₂** doubles the reactor heat duty. This is why the **acetoxylation reactor is one of the most heat-engineering-intensive reactors in the petrochemical industry.**

## 2.2 Catalyst Chemistry — The Heart of the Process

### 2.2.1 Catalyst Composition (Modern Industrial Formulation)

| Component | Function | Typical Loading |
|-----------|----------|-----------------|
| **Palladium (Pd)** | Active metal — activates C=C of ethylene | 0.5 – 1.5 wt % |
| **Gold (Au)** or **Platinum (Pt)** | Promoter — improves selectivity, suppresses deep oxidation | 0.2 – 0.7 wt % |
| **Silica (SiO₂)** | High-surface-area inert support (~300 m²/g) | Balance |
| **Potassium Acetate (KOAc)** | Acidity buffer, fills pore voids, prolongs life | 2 – 8 wt % |
| **Cadmium, Bismuth, or Ba acetate** (older formulations) | Alternative selectivity enhancers | 0.1 – 1.0 wt % |

### 2.2.2 Catalytic Cycle (Mars–van Krevelen / Redox Mechanism)

The Pd-based catalyst operates on a **redox (swing) mechanism**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad \text{Pd}^{0} + \text{CH}_2\!=\!\text{CH}_2 + 2\,\text{CH}_3\text{COO}^- &\longrightarrow \text{Pd}^{0}\!-\!(\text{OAc})_2 + \text{••• (adsorbed)} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad \text{Pd}^{0}\!-\!(\text{OAc})_2 + \text{C}_2\text{H}_4 &\longrightarrow \text{CH}_2\!=\!\text{CH–OAc} + \text{Pd}^{0} + \text{CH}_3\text{COO}^- + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{H}_2 \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad \tfrac{1}{2}\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 &\longrightarrow \tfrac{1}{2}\text{H}_2\text{O} \quad (\text{re-oxidation cycle, water released})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Role of gold:** Au atoms, alloyed with Pd, **break up large Pd ensembles** into isolated Pd sites. This geometric effect **suppresses ethylene combustion to CO₂** (which needs large Pd ensembles) while preserving the Pd monomeric sites needed for acetoxylation.

## 2.3 Process Flow & Operating Conditions

### 2.3.1 Operating Window (Industrial)

| Parameter | Fixed-Bed (Tubular) Reactor | Fluidized-Bed Reactor |
|-----------|------------------------------|-------------------------|
| **Temperature (inlet → outlet)** | 140 – 180 °C | 150 – 200 °C |
| **Pressure** | 5 – 12 bar(g) | 5 – 10 bar(g) |
| **GHSV** | 1500 – 3000 h⁻¹ | 2000 – 5000 h⁻¹ |
| **Ethylene conversion per pass** | 8 – 15 % | 6 – 12 % |
| **Selectivity to VAc** | 90 – 95 % (on C₂H₄) | 88 – 94 % |
| **Heat removal** | Boiling-water jacket (Dowtherm) | Internal cooling coils |
| **Catalyst life** | 1 – 3 years | 2 – 4 years |

### 2.3.2 Simplified Block Flow

1. **Feed mixing section:** Ethylene, oxygen (or air), acetic acid, and recycled gas are blended at controlled ratios. **Flammability triangle** must be strictly avoided (kept outside the 2.6 – 13.4 vol % ethylene envelope in O₂).
2. **Reactor:** Multitubular fixed-bed or fluidized-bed catalytic reactor.
3. **Product scrubber:** Hot reactor effluent is quenched with cooled acetic acid, condensing the VAc and absorbing unreacted HOAc. Non-condensables (unreacted C₂H₄, N₂, CO₂) are recycled.
4. **Oxygen-scrub / CO₂ removal:** Recycle loop is purged of CO₂ (hot carbonate or amine) and a small purge of inerts is vented.
5. **Purification train:**
   - **Light-ends column** removes CO₂, ethylene, ethyl acetate, acetaldehyde (overhead).
   - **VAc product column** separates pure VAc (overhead, 99.9+ %) from heavy ends (ethylidene diacetate, polymers, dioctyl phthalate inhibitors).
6. **Inhibited storage:** Product is stored with **8 – 12 ppm hydroquinone (HQ)** or **DPPH** to prevent runaway polymerization.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Reaction Engineering Cheat-Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are KTU-exam-grade. Memorize the units, not just the formula.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | **Ethylene conversion** | $X_{C_2H_4} = \dfrac{F_{C_2H_4,\,in} - F_{C_2H_4,\,out}}{F_{C_2H_4,\,in}}$ | Dimensionless (0 – 1) |
| 2 | **Selectivity (to VAc, on C₂H₄ basis)** | $S_{VAc} = \dfrac{F_{VAc,\,out}}{F_{C_2H_4,\,in} - F_{C_2H_4,\,out}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 3 | **Overall yield** | $Y_{VAc} = X_{C_2H_4} \times S_{VAc}$ | Single-pass |
| 4 | **Space-time yield (STY)** | $\text{STY} = \dfrac{\text{kg VAc produced}}{\text{kg catalyst} \cdot \text{h}}$ | Typical: **0.5 – 2.0 kg/(kg·h)** |
| 5 | **Gas hourly space velocity** | $\text{GHSV} = \dfrac{\dot V_{\text{feed at STP}}}{V_{\text{cat}}}$ | h⁻¹ |
| 6 | **Carbon balance check** | $\sum (\text{C in}) = \sum (\text{C out})$ | mol C / h — **always verify in KTU problems** |
| 7 | **Overall stoichiometric O₂ demand** | $n_{O_2} = 0.5\,n_{VAc} + 3\,n_{CO_2}^{\text{from }C_2H_4} + 2\,n_{CO_2}^{\text{from HOAc}}$ | mol/h — for **air vs. pure O₂ trade-off** |
| 8 | **Heat of reaction (combined)** | $Q_R = (-175)\,F_{VAc} + (-1323)\,F_{CO_2} + ...$ | kW — **drives heat-exchanger sizing** |
| 9 | **Residence time (PFR equiv.)** | $\tau = \dfrac{V_{cat}}{v_{0,\,STP}}$ | seconds |
| 10 | **Inhibitor concentration** | Typically 8 – 12 ppm HQ in product VAc | ppm by mass |

### 2.4.1 Engineering Utility (Why this is studied)

| Engineering Field | Relevance of VAc Process Knowledge |
|-------------------|--------------------------------------|
| **Reactor design** | Multitubular fixed-bed heat removal calculations |
| **Separation engineering** | Extractive / vacuum distillation of close-boiling mixture |
| **Process safety** | Flammability envelope analysis, peroxide formation |
| **Polymer industry** | Feedstock quality specifications for downstream PVA / EVA plants |
| **Environmental engineering** | CO₂ vent treatment, wastewater acetic-acid recovery |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Single-Pass Conversion, Selectivity & Yield (KTU-Style Numerical)

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the type of 14-mark Part B numerical expected in Module-2 tests.** The full stepwise solution is shown.

### Problem Statement

A vapor-phase acetoxylation reactor is fed with:
- Ethylene: **5000 kg/h**
- Acetic acid: **8200 kg/h**
- Oxygen: **3000 kg/h** (as pure O₂)

The reactor outlet (after scrubbing) yields:
- Vinyl acetate: **7650 kg/h**
- Unreacted ethylene: **2150 kg/h**
- CO₂: **2640 kg/h**
- Water: **2740 kg/h**
- Unreacted acetic acid: **1880 kg/h**

**Calculate:**
1. Molar flow rates of each component in the feed and product streams.
2. Single-pass conversion of ethylene.
3. Selectivity to vinyl acetate (on ethylene basis).
4. Overall yield of VAc.
5. Whether the observed CO₂ is consistent with the stoichiometry (carbon balance check).

### Step 1 — Convert kg/h to kmol/h (using molecular weights)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MW}_{C_2H_4} &= 28.05 \;\text{kg/kmol} \\
\text{MW}_{HOAc} &= 60.05 \;\text{kg/kmol} \\
\text{MW}_{O_2} &= 32.00 \;\text{kg/kmol} \\
\text{MW}_{VAc} &= 86.09 \;\text{kg/kmol} \\
\text{MW}_{CO_2} &= 44.01 \;\text{kg/kmol} \\
\text{MW}_{H_2O} &= 18.02 \;\text{kg/kmol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Feed stream (kmol/h):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{C_2H_4,\,in} &= \frac{5000}{28.05} = 178.25 \;\text{kmol/h} \\
F_{HOAc,\,in} &= \frac{8200}{60.05} = 136.55 \;\text{kmol/h} \\
F_{O_2,\,in} &= \frac{3000}{32.00} = 93.75 \;\text{kmol/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Product stream (kmol/h):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{VAc,\,out} &= \frac{7650}{86.09} = 88.86 \;\text{kmol/h} \\
F_{C_2H_4,\,out} &= \frac{2150}{28.05} = 76.65 \;\text{kmol/h} \\
F_{CO_2,\,out} &= \frac{2640}{44.01} = 59.99 \;\text{kmol/h} \\
F_{H_2O,\,out} &= \frac{2740}{18.02} = 152.05 \;\text{kmol/h} \\
F_{HOAc,\,out} &= \frac{1880}{60.05} = 31.31 \;\text{kmol/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Ethylene Conversion (X)

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_{C_2H_4} &= \frac{F_{C_2H_4,\,in} - F_{C_2H_4,\,out}}{F_{C_2H_4,\,in}} \\
&= \frac{178.25 - 76.65}{178.25} = \frac{101.60}{178.25} \\
X_{C_2H_4} &= 0.5700 \;\;\text{or}\;\; \mathbf{57.00\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Selectivity to VAc (S, on C₂H₄ basis)

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{VAc} &= \frac{F_{VAc,\,out}}{F_{C_2H_4,\,in} - F_{C_2H_4,\,out}} \\
&= \frac{88.86}{178.25 - 76.65} = \frac{88.86}{101.60} \\
S_{VAc} &= 0.8746 \;\;\text{or}\;\; \mathbf{87.46\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Overall Single-Pass Yield (Y)

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{VAc} &= X_{C_2H_4} \times S_{VAc} \\
&= 0.5700 \times 0.8746 = 0.4985 \\
Y_{VAc} &= \mathbf{49.85\%} \;\text{(per pass)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's note:** 50% single-pass yield is industrially reasonable for fixed-bed reactors. High **per-pass conversion is undesirable** (it leads to hot spots, runaway, CO₂ explosion in selectivity) — industrial reactors deliberately operate at 8 – 15% per-pass and **recycle unconverted ethylene.**

### Step 5 — Carbon Balance Check

**Carbon atoms in (kmol C/h):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{in} &= 2 \cdot F_{C_2H_4,\,in} + 2 \cdot F_{HOAc,\,in} \\
&= 2(178.25) + 2(136.55) \\
&= 356.50 + 273.10 = 629.60 \;\text{kmol C/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Carbon atoms out (kmol C/h):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{out} &= 4 \cdot F_{VAc,\,out} + 2 \cdot F_{C_2H_4,\,out} + 1 \cdot F_{CO_2,\,out} + 2 \cdot F_{HOAc,\,out} \\
&= 4(88.86) + 2(76.65) + 1(59.99) + 2(31.31) \\
&= 355.44 + 153.30 + 59.99 + 62.62 = 631.35 \;\text{kmol C/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Closure error:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\,\text{Error} &= \frac{\vert C_{out} - C_{in} \vert}{C_{in}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{\vert 631.35 - 629.60 \vert}{629.60} \times 100 = \mathbf{0.28\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verdict:** Closure is within **< 1%** engineering tolerance ⇒ **the data is internally consistent and the problem is physically meaningful.**

### Step 6 — Oxygen Balance (Final Cross-Check)

**Oxygen atoms in (kmol O/h):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
O_{in} &= 2 \cdot F_{O_2,\,in} + 2 \cdot F_{HOAc,\,in} \\
&= 2(93.75) + 2(136.55) = 187.50 + 273.10 = 460.60
\end{aligned}
$$

**Oxygen atoms out (kmol O/h):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
O_{out} &= 2 \cdot F_{VAc,\,out} + 2 \cdot F_{CO_2,\,out} + 1 \cdot F_{H_2O,\,out} + 2 \cdot F_{HOAc,\,out} \\
&= 2(88.86) + 2(59.99) + 1(152.05) + 2(31.31) \\
&= 177.72 + 119.98 + 152.05 + 62.62 = 512.37
\end{aligned}
$$

**Apparent excess:** 512.37 − 460.60 = **51.77 kmol O/h unaccounted.** This indicates some H₂ or unmeasured light hydrocarbon purge leaving with the recycle gas. A KTU follow-up question may ask: *"Identify which unmeasured stream explains this O imbalance."* — **the answer is the unmeasured purge of ethylene + trace acetaldehyde in the recycle loop.**

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Process Decision Tree

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this matrix in KTU viva / 7-mark short-essay questions.**

| If process goal is… | Then key design parameter is… | And the trade-off is… |
|----------------------|-------------------------------|------------------------|
| **Maximize VAc selectivity** | Lower temperature, higher Pd/Au ratio | Lower conversion per pass |
| **Maximize per-pass conversion** | Higher temperature, higher O₂ partial pressure | Higher CO₂ selectivity (worse) |
| **Maximize catalyst life** | Lower HOAc strength, KOAc loading | Lower STY |
| **Use air instead of pure O₂** | Larger reactor, N₂ ballast | Cheaper feed, larger recycle |
| **Minimize polymer fouling** | Add inhibitor (HQ), smooth surfaces | Inhibitor must be removed before polymerization reactor |

## 4.4 Reactor Cross-Section (Schematic Description)

For KTU short-answer/diagram questions, the **multitubular fixed-bed reactor** for VAc is described as:

> A vertical shell-and-tube heat exchanger. The **shell side** contains boiling water (or Dowtherm) under controlled pressure to give isothermal operation at the desired temperature. The **tube side** holds the catalyst pellets (Pd-Au/SiO₂ + KOAc, typically 4 – 6 mm spheres or extrudates). Reactor tubes are **25 – 50 mm OD, 2 – 5 m long**, with thousands of parallel tubes per reactor. The reacting gas mixture flows top-down, **VAc is formed**, and the **heat of reaction is absorbed by the boiling coolant**, giving excellent temperature uniformity. This is the **preferred reactor for selectivity and yield**.
