# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]
**Define the term "acetoxylation" as applied to petrochemical reactions. Write the balanced chemical equation for the vapor-phase acetoxylation of ethylene to vinyl acetate.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Acetoxylation is an **oxidative coupling reaction** in which an **acetoxy group (CH₃COO–)** from an organic acid (typically acetic acid) is introduced across a C=C double bond of an olefin, in the presence of **oxygen and a noble-metal catalyst** (Pd-based). The general reaction involves the alkene, the carboxylic acid, and oxygen, producing a vinyl ester and water as a byproduct.

**Balanced equation:**
$$
\text{CH}_2\!=\!\text{CH}_2 + \text{CH}_3\text{COOH} + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Pd-Au/SiO}_2} \text{CH}_2\!=\!\text{CHOCOCH}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

The reaction is **strongly exothermic** (ΔH ≈ −175 kJ/mol) and is industrially carried out in the **vapor phase at 140 – 200 °C and 5 – 12 bar** over a palladium-gold or palladium-platinum catalyst supported on silica. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]
**List the major applications of vinyl acetate and the polymers derived from it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Vinyl acetate is a reactive monomer that polymerizes and copolymerizes to give a wide spectrum of commercially important polymers:

1. **Polyvinyl Acetate (PVA / PVAc):** the base of white glue, paper coatings, and interior latex paints.
2. **Polyvinyl Alcohol (PVOH/PVA):** made by saponification of PVAc; used in water-soluble films (detergent pods, hospital laundry bags), textile sizing, and as a paper adhesive.
3. **Ethylene-Vinyl Acetate (EVA) copolymer:** flexible films, hot-melt adhesives, foam shoe soles, and photovoltaic module encapsulant film.
4. **Vinyl Acetate-Ethylene (VAE) emulsions:** architectural coatings, carpet backing, and non-woven binders.
5. **Polyvinyl Butyral (PVB):** made via the acetal of PVOH; used as the interlayer in **automotive laminated safety glass** (windshields).

All five applications arise from the **reactive vinyl group** of VAc, which permits free-radical polymerization and copolymerization. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on VAc Numericals**
> 1. **Forgetting the ½ coefficient on O₂** when balancing the master equation. Even one missing half gives a 10–15% error in O₂ balance. Always write: *ethylene + HOAc + ½O₂ → VAc + H₂O*.
> 2. **Conflating conversion and yield.** Conversion = ethylene reacted; yield = conversion × selectivity. Examiners will NOT give marks if these are mixed up.
> 3. **Ignoring the recycle loop in single-pass calculations.** Industrial VAc data typically gives only single-pass numbers. Do NOT assume 100% conversion. Read the problem carefully.
> 4. **Mixing molecular weights.** VAc is 86.09 g/mol, NOT 86. If the problem gives kg/h, the answer MUST be in kmol/h with MW in kg/kmol.
> 5. **Omitting the CO₂ / heat duty calculation.** A complete KTU answer should also comment on **heat release per kmol of CO₂** to demonstrate reactor-safety understanding.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a **neat flow diagram**, describe the **vapor-phase acetoxylation process** for the manufacture of vinyl acetate from ethylene. Explain the role of each catalyst component. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A VAc plant produces **120,000 tonnes/year** of vinyl acetate. The **single-pass ethylene conversion is 12%** and the **selectivity to VAc is 92% (on C₂H₄ basis)**. Calculate:
   1. The **molar flow rate of ethylene** required at the reactor inlet (per hour).
   2. The **molar flow rate of acetic acid** required (assume 5% molar excess over stoichiometric).
   3. The **molar flow rate of oxygen** required (pure O₂, with 10% molar excess over stoichiometric).
   4. The **single-pass VAc yield (in %)** and comment on the result.
   5. The **kg of CO₂ produced per hour** if 6% of the ethylene burned to CO₂. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A**

**(a) Process Description (7 marks):**

The **vapor-phase acetoxylation of ethylene** is the dominant industrial route to vinyl acetate, replacing the older liquid-phase mercury-catalyzed acetylene route.

**Process Steps (with diagram, see Section 4.1):**

1. **Feed preparation:** Ethylene, oxygen (or air), acetic acid, and recycle gas are mixed in carefully controlled ratios to stay **outside the flammability envelope** (must keep O₂/C₂H₄ ratio below the lower explosive limit of 2.6 vol% in O₂).
2. **Preheating:** The mixed feed is preheated to ~140 °C in a feed-effluent heat exchanger.
3. **Reactor:** The feed passes through a **multitubular fixed-bed catalytic reactor** (or fluidized-bed in some designs) packed with **Pd-Au/KOAc on SiO₂ catalyst** at 140 – 180 °C and 5 – 12 bar. The reaction is highly exothermic, and heat is removed by **boiling water in the shell side**.
4. **Product scrubbing:** Reactor effluent is quenched with cold acetic acid in a scrubber; VAc and unreacted HOAc are absorbed.
5. **CO₂ removal & recycle:** The non-condensable gas (unreacted C₂H₄, N₂, CO₂) is sent to a **hot potassium carbonate or amine absorption unit** to remove CO₂, and the cleaned gas is recycled.
6. **Purification:** The crude liquid is distilled in two columns — a **light-ends column** (removes CO₂, ethyl acetate, acetaldehyde) and a **VAc product column** (produces 99.9% pure VAc overhead).
7. **Storage:** Pure VAc is stored with **8 – 12 ppm hydroquinone (HQ)** as a polymerization inhibitor.

**Role of catalyst components (1 mark each, max 3):**
- **Palladium (Pd):** Active site — activates the C=C bond of ethylene and inserts the acetoxy group.
- **Gold (Au) / Platinum (Pt):** Promoter — alloyed with Pd, breaks up large Pd ensembles to suppress deep oxidation (CO₂) and boost selectivity.
- **Silica (SiO₂):** Support — provides high surface area (~300 m²/g) and thermal stability.
- **Potassium acetate (KOAc):** Acidity buffer and pore filler, prolongs catalyst life.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** Production = 120,000 t/year, X = 12%, S = 92% → Y = 0.12 × 0.92 = 0.1104 (per pass).

**Annual operating hours (KTU standard):** 8000 h/year.
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{VAc output} &= \frac{120{,}000 \times 10^3 \;\text{kg}}{8000 \;\text{h}} = 15{,}000 \;\text{kg/h} \\
F_{VAc} &= \frac{15{,}000}{86.09} = 174.23 \;\text{kmol/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(1) Ethylene feed:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{VAc} &= \frac{F_{VAc}}{F_{C_2H_4,\,reacted}} \;\Rightarrow\; F_{C_2H_4,\,reacted} = \frac{174.23}{0.92} = 189.38 \;\text{kmol/h} \\
X_{C_2H_4} &= 0.12 \;\Rightarrow\; F_{C_2H_4,\,in} = \frac{189.38}{0.12} = \mathbf{1578.2 \;\text{kmol/h}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(2) Acetic acid (5% molar excess):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Stoichiometric HOAc} &= 189.38 \;\text{kmol/h} \\
F_{HOAc,\,in} &= 1.05 \times 189.38 = \mathbf{198.85 \;\text{kmol/h}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(3) Oxygen (10% molar excess over stoichiometric 0.5 per VAc):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{O_2,\,stoich} &= 0.5 \times 189.38 = 94.69 \;\text{kmol/h} \\
F_{O_2,\,in} &= 1.10 \times 94.69 = \mathbf{104.16 \;\text{kmol/h}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(4) Single-pass yield:**
$$
Y = X \times S = 0.12 \times 0.92 = \mathbf{11.04\%}
$$
*Comment:* The low single-pass yield (~11%) is **intentional** — it allows the use of lower temperatures and prevents hot spots. The unreacted 88% of ethylene is recovered and recycled, so the **overall plant yield is ~94%**.

**(5) CO₂ from 6% of ethylene burned:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{C_2H_4 \to CO_2} &= 0.06 \times F_{C_2H_4,\,reacted} = 0.06 \times 189.38 = 11.36 \;\text{kmol/h} \\
\text{From } \text{C}_2\text{H}_4 + 3\text{O}_2 \to 2\text{CO}_2: \quad F_{CO_2} &= 2 \times 11.36 = 22.73 \;\text{kmol/h} \\
\text{Mass CO}_2 &= 22.73 \times 44.01 = \mathbf{1000.3 \;\text{kg/h}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total heat release check (bonus mark):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{VAc} &= 174.23 \times 175 = 30{,}490 \;\text{kW} \\
Q_{CO_2} &= 11.36 \times 1323 = 15{,}029 \;\text{kW} \\
\text{Total } Q &\approx 45{,}519 \;\text{kW} \quad \text{(governs the multitubular heat-exchanger design)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Discuss the **role of gold (Au) in Pd-Au catalysts** for vinyl acetate synthesis. Why is Pd alone insufficient? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A VAc reactor feed (basis: 100 kmol/h ethylene) has the following **molar composition:** 100 C₂H₄, 95 HOAc, 30 O₂, 150 N₂, 5 H₂O, balance inerts. The reactor operates at **180 °C, 8 bar**, with **selectivity 91% on ethylene** and **conversion 14% per pass**. Calculate:
   1. The **molar flow of VAc produced per hour**.
   2. The **molar flow of CO₂ produced** if 7% of the reacted ethylene forms CO₂.
   3. The **molar flow of water** in the outlet stream.
   4. The **O₂ conversion (%)** assuming 5% of the O₂ is consumed by HOAc combustion and the rest goes to VAc + CO₂ formation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B**

**(a) Role of Gold in Pd-Au Catalysts (7 marks):**

**Why Pd alone is insufficient:**
Pure Pd is highly active for ethylene activation but also catalyzes **total combustion to CO₂ and H₂O** because it readily dissociates O₂ and breaks C–C bonds. On a continuous Pd surface, ethylene adsorbs and burns non-selectively, giving VAc selectivities often below 50%.

**How Au improves selectivity:**

1. **Geometric ensemble effect:** Au atoms, being larger and immiscible with Pd, intermix to form a **Pd-Au alloy**. The Au atoms **isolate Pd into single-atom or small-cluster sites**, geometrically preventing the **multi-site adsorption** required for C–C bond scission (which produces CO₂). The acetoxylation pathway only needs **one isolated Pd site**.

2. **Electronic effect:** Alloying with Au **reduces the electron density** of Pd, making it **less electron-rich** and less able to fully reduce oxygen. This suppresses deep oxidation.

3. **Suppression of PdO formation:** Au stabilizes metallic Pd⁰ under reaction conditions, which is the **active phase** for selective acetoxylation.

4. **Improved lifetime:** Au reduces carbonaceous deposits and Pd sintering, extending catalyst life from ~1 year (Pd only) to 2 – 3 years (Pd-Au).

5. **Selectivity boost:** Industrial Pd-Au catalysts achieve **90 – 95% selectivity** to VAc at 8 – 15% per-pass conversion, vs. only 50 – 70% for pure Pd.

6. **Comparison with Pt:** Pt (group 10) is sometimes used instead of Au, but Pt–Pd gives slightly lower activity. Modern plants prefer **Pd-Au** (e.g., Celanese, LyondellBasell) for optimum selectivity-life balance.

7. **Commercial example:** The **Celanese "VAntage"** process and **BP/INEOS "LEAP"** fluidized-bed process both use Pd-Au/SiO₂ + KOAc catalyst.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given (per 100 kmol/h C₂H₄ feed):** X = 14%, S = 91% (on C₂H₄), CO₂ side-reaction consumes 7% of reacted C₂H₄.

**(1) VAc produced:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{C_2H_4,\,reacted} &= 0.14 \times 100 = 14.0 \;\text{kmol/h} \\
F_{VAc} &= S \times F_{C_2H_4,\,reacted} = 0.91 \times 14.0 = \mathbf{12.74 \;\text{kmol/h}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(2) CO₂ produced:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{C_2H_4 \to CO_2} &= 0.07 \times 14.0 = 0.98 \;\text{kmol/h} \\
\text{From } \text{C}_2\text{H}_4 + 3\text{O}_2 \to 2\text{CO}_2: \quad F_{CO_2} &= 2 \times 0.98 = \mathbf{1.96 \;\text{kmol/h}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(3) Water in outlet:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{From VAc reaction: } &\text{H}_2\text{O formed} = 12.74 \;\text{kmol/h} \quad (\text{1:1 with VAc})\\
\text{From CO}_2 \text{ reaction: } &\text{H}_2\text{O formed} = 2 \times 0.98 = 1.96 \;\text{kmol/h} \\
\text{Total } \text{H}_2\text{O produced} &= 12.74 + 1.96 = 14.70 \;\text{kmol/h} \\
F_{H_2O,\,out} &= 5 \;(\text{feed}) + 14.70 = \mathbf{19.70 \;\text{kmol/h}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(4) O₂ conversion:**

First, compute total O₂ consumed:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{O}_2 \text{ for VAc} &= 0.5 \times F_{VAc} = 0.5 \times 12.74 = 6.37 \;\text{kmol/h} \\
\text{O}_2 \text{ for CO}_2 \text{ (from } \text{C}_2\text{H}_4\text{)} &= 3 \times 0.98 = 2.94 \;\text{kmol/h} \\
\text{O}_2 \text{ for HOAc combustion (5% of feed)} &= 0.05 \times 30 = 1.50 \;\text{kmol/h} \\
\text{Total O}_2 \text{ reacted} &= 6.37 + 2.94 + 1.50 = 10.81 \;\text{kmol/h} \\
\%\,\text{O}_2 \text{ conversion} &= \frac{10.81}{30} \times 100 = \mathbf{36.03\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Sanity check:* O₂ is fed in **large stoichiometric excess** (typical ratio 1.5 – 2× over stoichiometric) to keep it on the **safe side of the flammability envelope**. 36% conversion in a single pass is consistent with industrial practice.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this checklist in the last 5 minutes before the KTU exam.**

- [ ] **Master reaction:** C₂H₄ + HOAc + ½O₂ → VAc + H₂O ; ΔH ≈ −175 kJ/mol
- [ ] **Catalyst:** Pd-Au (or Pd-Pt) on SiO₂ + KOAc buffer ; Pd active site, Au = selectivity promoter
- [ ] **Operating window:** T = 140 – 200 °C ; P = 5 – 12 bar ; per-pass X = 8 – 15% ; S = 90 – 95%
- [ ] **Reactor type:** Multitubular fixed-bed (best selectivity) or fluidized-bed (best heat removal, longer life)
- [ ] **Key side reactions:** Combustion of ethylene to CO₂ (ΔH ≈ −1323 kJ/mol — **7.5× hotter than main reaction**), ethyl acetate, acetaldehyde
- [ ] **Inhibitor for storage:** 8 – 12 ppm hydroquinone (HQ) or DPPH — prevents radical polymerization
- [ ] **Boiling point of VAc:** 72.7 °C — separation must use vacuum or low-pressure distillation
- [ ] **Flammability limits in air:** 2.6 – 13.4 vol% — process must stay outside the envelope
- [ ] **Conversion ≠ Yield:** Y = X × S (always!)
- [ ] **STY benchmark:** 0.5 – 2.0 kg VAc per kg catalyst per hour
- [ ] **Major applications (rank by global volume):** PVA → EVA → PVOH → VAE → PVB
- [ ] **Recycle rule:** Unreacted ethylene is recovered (88% of feed) and recycled → overall plant yield ~ 94%
- [ ] **Carbon balance closure tolerance:** ± 1% for a numerically consistent problem
- [ ] **O₂ source choice:** Pure O₂ preferred (smaller reactor, no N₂ ballast) ; air is cheaper but larger
- [ ] **Environmental point:** CO₂ vent from recycle loop — must be scrubbed before atmospheric release
- [ ] **Heat removal is THE design driver** — never ignore the Q balance in a 14-mark numerical

> **END OF TOPIC: VINYL ACETATE — MODULE 2, PECHT741**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Acetaldehyde
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT741 - PETROCHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Acetaldehyde

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
**Acetaldehyde** (IUPAC name: *ethanal*), with the molecular formula **C₂H₄O** or **CH₃CHO**, is the second-simplest member of the aldehyde homologous series. It is a colorless, volatile, flammable liquid with a pungent, fruity, suffocating odor. It is one of the most important **C₂-oxygenated petrochemicals** produced industrially, serving as a versatile building block (a "chemical pivot") for the synthesis of acetic acid, ethyl acetate, pentaerythritol, crotonaldehyde, peracetic acid, and 1,3-butylene glycol.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In the 2024 Scheme, Acetaldehyde is studied under *C₂ Oxygenated Petrochemicals* as a key intermediate. Students must focus on its manufacturing routes (Wacker, ethanol oxidation, acetylene hydration) and downstream derivatives.

### 1.2 Physical & Thermodynamic Properties (Standard Metrics)

| Property | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Molecular Weight | 44.053 | g/mol |
| Boiling Point | 20.2 | °C (293.35 K) |
| Melting Point | −123.37 | °C |
| Density (at 20 °C) | 0.7836 | g/cm³ |
| Flash Point (closed cup) | −39 | °C |
| Autoignition Temperature | 175 | °C |
| Vapor Density (vs air) | 1.52 | — |
| Heat of Vaporization | 25.8 | kJ/mol |
| Solubility in Water | Miscible in all proportions | — |
| Explosive Limits in Air | **4.0 – 60.0** | vol % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boiling Point vs. Room Temperature:** Notice the boiling point is **20.2 °C** — almost exactly room temperature! This means acetaldehyde must be stored in **pressurized, refrigerated tanks** to prevent it from boiling away. This single fact controls its entire storage and transport logistics.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a **chemical "pivot joint"** in a giant factory. Just as a pivot joint in mechanics allows rotation in many directions, acetaldehyde sits at a chemical crossroads where multiple raw material streams (ethylene, ethanol, acetylene) can all be converted **into** it, and from it, multiple high-value products (acetic acid, ethanol derivatives, polyols) can be manufactured **out** of it. If you control this pivot, you control the economics of an entire C₂-chemistry segment.

### 1.4 Industrial Significance
Acetaldehyde is a **strategic petrochemical intermediate** because:
- It bridges the **petrochemical (ethylene)** and **fermentation (ethanol)** value chains.
- It is the parent compound for **acetic acid** (the world's largest-volume organic acid), historically via the Wacker route.
- Its derivatives are essential for **paints, plastics, pharmaceuticals, and explosives** (pentaerythritol → PENT → TNT intermediates).

> [!WARNING]
> **Health Note:** Acetaldehyde is a probable human carcinogen (Group 2B, IARC). Plant design must incorporate closed-loop reactors and rigorous vent scrubbing — a frequent viva question on plant safety.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Industrial Manufacturing Routes
There are **three principal industrial routes** to acetaldehyde. Each has distinct feedstock, catalyst, and economic profiles that are heavily tested in KTU exams.

#### 2.1.1 Wacker Oxidation Process (Ethylene Route) — *Dominant Method*
The **Wacker–Hoechst process** (also called the Wacker process) directly oxidizes ethylene to acetaldehyde using a homogeneous PdCl₂/CuCl₂ catalyst system in aqueous solution.

**Overall Reaction:**
$$CH_2=CH_2 + \tfrac{1}{2} O_2 \xrightarrow{\text{PdCl}_2/\text{CuCl}_2} CH_3CHO$$

**Mechanism (Two Coupled Cycles):**
- **Cycle 1 (Oxidation of ethylene):**
$$CH_2=CH_2 + PdCl_2 + H_2O \rightarrow CH_3CHO + Pd^{0} + 2HCl$$
- **Cycle 2 (Regeneration of catalyst):**
$$Pd^{0} + 2CuCl_2 \rightarrow PdCl_2 + 2CuCl$$
$$2CuCl + 2HCl + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow 2CuCl_2 + H_2O$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the two cycles?** The catalyst Pd²⁺ is reduced to Pd⁰ during the reaction and would become inactive (a black precipitate). Cu²⁺ re-oxidizes Pd⁰ back to Pd²⁺, while O₂ regenerates Cu²⁺. This is a classic **redox catalytic cycle** — always draw both cycles in your exam for full marks.

**Operating Conditions:**
- Temperature: **120 – 130 °C**
- Pressure: **3 – 4 atm**
- Catalyst: PdCl₂ (1–2 mmol/L) + CuCl₂ (100–200 mmol/L) in aqueous HCl

**Selectivity / Yield:** **>95% acetaldehyde selectivity** based on ethylene.

#### 2.1.2 Ethanol Dehydrogenation / Oxidation Route
Two sub-routes exist:
- **Oxidative dehydrogenation** (using air or O₂):
$$C_2H_5OH + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Cu or Ag, 300–450 °C}} CH_3CHO + H_2O$$
- **Pure dehydrogenation** (endothermic, requires heat):
$$C_2H_5OH \xrightarrow{\text{Cu, 260–290 °C}} CH_3CHO + H_2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why pure dehydrogenation also matters:** It produces **byproduct H₂**, which has economic value. The oxidative route is exothermic and self-sustaining, but the H₂ is lost as water.

**Operating Conditions (Oxidative):**
- Temperature: **300 – 450 °C**
- Catalyst: Copper or silver gauze
- Conversion: **45 – 60%**, Selectivity: **~95%**

#### 2.1.3 Acetylene Hydration (Reppe Process)
$$CH \equiv CH + H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{HgSO}_4 / \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4, \, 70–90\,°C} CH_3CHO$$

This route was historically significant but is **phased out** in most countries due to the toxicity of mercury catalysts and the dominance of the Wacker route.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Molecular formula | C₂H₄O = CH₃CHO | MW = 44.053 g/mol |
| Wacker overall | C₂H₄ + ½O₂ → CH₃CHO | ΔH ≈ −243 kJ/mol (exothermic) |
| Wacker rate (simplified) | $r = k \cdot [C_2H_4] \cdot [Pd^{2+}] / ([Cl^-]^2 \cdot [H^+])$ | Cl⁻ inhibits; high [H⁺] inhibits |
| Ethanol dehydrogenation | C₂H₅OH → CH₃CHO + H₂ | ΔH = +84 kJ/mol (endothermic) |
| Ethanol oxidation | C₂H₅OH + ½O₂ → CH₃CHO + H₂O | ΔH = −180 kJ/mol (exothermic) |
| Acetylene hydration | C₂H₂ + H₂O → CH₃CHO | ΔH = −139 kJ/mol |
| Selectivity | $S = \frac{\text{moles of CH}_3\text{CHO formed}}{\text{moles of C}_2\text{H}_4 \text{ reacted}}$ | Expressed in % |
| Conversion | $X = \frac{\text{moles reacted}}{\text{moles fed}}$ | Expressed in % |
| Yield | $Y = S \times X$ | Engineering check |
| Explosive limit (LEL/UEL) | 4.0 – 60.0 vol% in air | Wide range! |

> [!NOTE]
> **In all formula tables, vertical bar symbols have been replaced with `\vert` to avoid breaking the markdown table parser.** Always present units in your final numerical answer.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Petrochemical Industry:** Determines whether a country builds an **ethylene-based** (Wacker) or **bio-ethanol-based** (fermentation) acetaldehyde plant — a matter of feedstock economics.
- **Environmental Engineering:** Wastewater from Wacker plants contains Cu²⁺ and Cl⁻; treatment design requires ion exchange and precipitation.
- **Process Safety:** LEL of 4% means **any minor leak forms an explosive mixture** — dictates explosion-proof (Ex-d) electrical zoning in the plant.
- **Pharma:** Acetaldehyde is a precursor for **pyridine derivatives** and certain vitamins.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Overall Stoichiometry of the Wacker Process
The Wacker process is presented in two half-steps that must be summed to yield the net reaction. This summation is a **classic KTU 14-mark question**.

**Given:** The two elementary steps in aqueous PdCl₂/CuCl₂ catalyst.

**Step 1 (Oxidative addition of ethylene to Pd²⁺):**
$$CH_2=CH_2 + PdCl_2 + H_2O \rightarrow CH_3CHO + Pd^{0} + 2HCl \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 2 (Re-oxidation of Pd⁰ by Cu²⁺):**
$$Pd^{0} + 2CuCl_2 \rightarrow PdCl_2 + 2CuCl \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 3 (Re-oxidation of Cu⁺ by O₂):**
$$2CuCl + 2HCl + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow 2CuCl_2 + H_2O \quad \text{...(iii)}$$

**Step 4 — Adding (i) + (ii) + (iii):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LHS:} \quad & CH_2=CH_2 + PdCl_2 + H_2O + Pd^{0} + 2CuCl_2 + 2CuCl + 2HCl + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \\
\text{RHS:} \quad & CH_3CHO + Pd^{0} + 2HCl + PdCl_2 + 2CuCl + 2CuCl_2 + H_2O
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Cancelling identical terms on both sides:**
- PdCl₂ cancels (1 LHS, 1 RHS)
- H₂O cancels (1 LHS, 1 RHS)
- Pd⁰ cancels (1 LHS, 1 RHS)
- 2HCl cancels (1 LHS, 1 RHS)
- 2CuCl cancels (1 LHS, 1 RHS)
- 2CuCl₂ cancels (1 LHS, 1 RHS)

**Step 6 — Net reaction (only remaining species):**
$$CH_2=CH_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow CH_3CHO$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Net result:** Both Pd and Cu species appear on neither side — they are **true catalysts**, not consumed. This is the conceptual definition of a *catalytic cycle*. The reaction consumes only ethylene and oxygen, and produces only acetaldehyde. **Always write this cancellation explicitly in your KTU exam.**

### 3.2 Derivation: Mass Balance on a Wacker Reactor
**Problem:** A Wacker reactor is fed **1000 kg/h of ethylene** and **1500 kg/h of oxygen** (excess). If the conversion of ethylene is **40%** and selectivity to acetaldehyde is **95%**, calculate:
(a) The mass of acetaldehyde produced per hour.
(b) The mass of unconverted ethylene leaving the reactor.
(c) The mass of byproducts (assume only CO₂ + H₂O from over-oxidation).

**Given Data:**
- M(C₂H₄) = 28.05 g/mol
- M(CH₃CHO) = 44.05 g/mol
- M(CO₂) = 44.01 g/mol

**Solution:**

**(a) Moles of ethylene fed:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{n}_{C_2H_4}^{in} &= \frac{1000 \text{ kg/h}}{28.05 \text{ kg/kmol}} = 35.65 \text{ kmol/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Moles of ethylene reacted (40% conversion):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{n}_{C_2H_4}^{reacted} &= 0.40 \times 35.65 = 14.26 \text{ kmol/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Moles of acetaldehyde formed (95% selectivity):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{n}_{CH_3CHO} &= 0.95 \times 14.26 = 13.55 \text{ kmol/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Mass of acetaldehyde produced:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{CH_3CHO} &= 13.55 \text{ kmol/h} \times 44.05 \text{ kg/kmol} \\
&= 596.6 \text{ kg/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Mass of unconverted ethylene:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{C_2H_4}^{out} &= (1 - 0.40) \times 1000 = 600 \text{ kg/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(c) Byproduct formation:**
The 5% of reacted ethylene that does not form acetaldehyde is over-oxidized. Per mole, the complete combustion is:
$$C_2H_4 + 3O_2 \rightarrow 2CO_2 + 2H_2O$$
- Moles of C₂H₄ over-oxidized = 0.05 × 14.26 = 0.713 kmol/h
- Moles of CO₂ produced = 2 × 0.713 = 1.426 kmol/h
- Mass of CO₂ = 1.426 × 44.01 = 62.8 kg/h
- Moles of H₂O = 2 × 0.713 = 1.426 kmol/h
- Mass of H₂O = 1.426 × 18.02 = 25.7 kg/h

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Trick:** The 1500 kg/h of O₂ supplied is in *vast excess* (the stoichiometric requirement for 40% conversion of 1000 kg/h ethylene is only ~407 kg/h). You are not asked to balance O₂ — but in a real plant this excess is needed to maintain the catalytic cycle. State this explicitly to gain the *process understanding* mark.

### 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix of the Three Routes

| Parameter | Wacker (Ethylene) | Ethanol Oxidation | Acetylene Hydration |
|---|---|---|---|
| Feedstock | Ethylene (petro) | Ethanol (bio or petro) | Acetylene (calcium carbide) |
| Catalyst | PdCl₂/CuCl₂ | Cu or Ag | HgSO₄/H₂SO₄ |
| T (°C) | 120 – 130 | 300 – 450 | 70 – 90 |
| P (atm) | 3 – 4 | 1 | 1 |
| Selectivity to CH₃CHO | **>95%** | ~95% | ~90% |
| Major byproduct | Chlorinated organics | H₂O (+ H₂ if pure dehydro.) | Crotonaldehyde, polymers |
| Catalyst toxicity | Cu effluent | Low | **High (Hg)** |
| Current status | Dominant | Niche / bio-route | Obsolete in West |
| Capital cost | High | Medium | Low |
| Exam weight (KTU) | **High** | Medium | Low |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block: Wacker Process Flow Diagram
This diagram shows the reactor section, catalyst regeneration loop, and product recovery train of a Wacker acetaldehyde plant.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ethylene Feed C2H4] --> M1[Mixer 1: Combine C2H4 + Recycle Gas]
    M1 --> R1[Wacker Reactor PdCl2-CuCl2 in H2O, 125 C, 3.5 atm]
    O1[O2 Feed] --> R1
    R1 --> S1[Flash Separator: Catalyst Recovery]
    S1 --> CR[Catalyst Regeneration Loop: Cu+ to Cu2+ by O2]
    CR --> S1
    S1 --> D1[Distillation Column 1: Light Ends Stripper]
    D1 --> D2[Distillation Column 2: Pure Acetaldehyde 99.5 percent]
    D2 --> P1[Product Storage: Pressurized Refrigerated Tank]
    P1 --> P2[Downstream: Acetic Acid Plant OR Ethyl Acetate Plant]
    D2 --> W1[Wastewater Treatment: Cu Ion Exchange]
    S1 --> W1
    W1 --> E1[Effluent to ETP]
    D1 --> G1[Vent Gas Scrubber: Absorb Unreacted C2H4]
    G1 --> M1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Ethylene and oxygen enter the **Wacker Reactor (R1)** together with the aqueous catalyst. The reactor effluent goes to a **Flash Separator (S1)**, where the catalyst-rich aqueous phase is sent to the **Regeneration Loop (CR)** where Cu⁺ is re-oxidized to Cu²⁺ by oxygen and recycled. The vapor phase is sent to a **two-column distillation train (D1, D2)** to recover pure acetaldehyde. Unreacted ethylene is scrubbed and recycled via **G1 → M1**.

### 4.2 Mermaid Block: Catalytic Redox Cycle of Wacker Process
This diagram illustrates the *interlocking* of the two half-cycles of the Wacker reaction at the molecular level.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Ethylene C2H4] --> B[Pd2+ in Aqueous Solution]
    B --> C[Palladium Ethylene Complex]
    C --> D[Acetaldehyde CH3CHO + Pd0]
    D --> E[Pd0 Metal Precipitate if not reoxidized]
    E --> F[Cu2+ Ions Reoxidize Pd0]
    F --> G[Pd2+ Regenerated + Cu+ Formed]
    G --> B
    H[O2 from Air] --> I[Cu+ Reoxidized to Cu2+]
    I --> G
    J[H2O and HCl Consumed and Regenerated] --> B
    J --> I
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why two cycles?** Pd²⁺ is the *true oxidant*; Cu²⁺ is the *mediator*; O₂ is the *ultimate oxidant*. The student must understand that without Cu²⁺, Pd²⁺ would be permanently reduced and the process would stop. This is the **rate-controlling step** of the Wacker process and a guaranteed KTU viva question.

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Acetaldehyde as a "Chemical Hub"
This block diagram maps the **upstream feedstocks** and **downstream derivatives** of acetaldehyde — the *pivot concept* introduced in Section 1.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F1[Ethylene from Naphtha Cracker] --> P1
    F2[Bioethanol from Fermentation] --> P2
    F3[Acetylene from Calcium Carbide] --> P3
    P1[Wacker Process] --> C[Acetaldehyde CH3CHO - The Hub]
    P2[Ethanol Oxidation] --> C
    P3[Acetylene Hydration] --> C
    C --> D1[Acetic Acid: O2, Mn or Co Catalyst]
    C --> D2[Ethyl Acetate: Tishchenko Reaction]
    C --> D3[Pentaerythritol: Aldol + Crossed Cannizzaro with HCHO]
    C --> D4[1,3 Butylene Glycol: Aldol Condensation]
    C --> D5[Crotonaldehyde: Aldol Self Condensation]
    C --> D6[Peracetic Acid: Liquid Phase Oxidation]
    C --> D7[Pyridine Bases: Condensation with NH3]
    D1 --> APP1[Vinyl Acetate, Cellulose Acetate, Purified Terephthalic Acid]
    D3 --> APP2[Alkyd Resins, Explosives PENT, Lubricants]
    D2 --> APP3[Solvent, Inks, Coatings]
    D4 --> APP4[Plasticizers, Polyesters]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visual Learning Strategy:** In your KTU exam, always draw this hub-and-spoke diagram for any "derivatives of acetaldehyde" question. It instantly conveys the breadth of the chemistry and earns you the *process understanding* mark.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`
**Define acetaldehyde. Give its molecular formula, IUPAC name, and one physical property that dictates its storage condition.**

**Model Answer:**
- Acetaldehyde is the second member of the aldehyde homologous series, an important C₂ oxygenated petrochemical.
- **Molecular formula:** C₂H₄O
- **IUPAC name:** Ethanal
- **Storage-critical property:** Its **boiling point is 20.2 °C**, very close to room temperature, so it must be stored in **refrigerated, pressurized** tanks to prevent vaporization and explosive vapor cloud formation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip (3 marks):** 1 mark for definition, 1 for formula + IUPAC, 1 for the property with reasoning. A bare property without justification fetches only half a mark.

---

#### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]`
**List any three industrial methods for the manufacture of acetaldehyde.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Wacker oxidation process** — direct oxidation of ethylene with O₂ using PdCl₂/CuCl₂ catalyst in aqueous solution.
2. **Ethanol oxidation / dehydrogenation** — catalytic conversion of ethanol over Cu or Ag catalysts at 300–450 °C.
3. **Acetylene hydration (Reppe process)** — hydration of acetylene using HgSO₄/H₂SO₄ catalyst.

> [!TIP]
> **Pro tip:** Always name the catalyst and one operating condition in addition to the raw material. This adds the *process depth* mark examiners look for.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

#### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Wacker process** for the manufacture of acetaldehyde from ethylene with a balanced reaction sequence, catalyst system, operating conditions, and a neat flow diagram.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **role of CuCl₂** in the Wacker process. Why is it preferred over other oxidants?

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Wacker Process Description:**

The Wacker–Hoechst process is the dominant industrial route for the manufacture of acetaldehyde from ethylene. The overall net reaction is:
$$CH_2=CH_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \xrightarrow{\text{PdCl}_2 / \text{CuCl}_2} CH_3CHO$$

**Catalyst system:** Homogeneous, consisting of **PdCl₂ (1–2 mmol/L)** and **CuCl₂ (100–200 mmol/L)** dissolved in aqueous HCl.

**Two coupled redox cycles:**

*Cycle 1 — Oxidation of ethylene:*
$$CH_2=CH_2 + PdCl_2 + H_2O \rightarrow CH_3CHO + Pd^{0} + 2HCl$$

*Cycle 2 — Catalyst regeneration:*
$$Pd^{0} + 2CuCl_2 \rightarrow PdCl_2 + 2CuCl$$
$$2CuCl + 2HCl + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow 2CuCl_2 + H_2O$$

**Operating conditions:**
- Temperature: **120 – 130 °C**
- Pressure: **3 – 4 atm**
- Reactor type: **Bubble-column / stirred-tank reactor** (gas–liquid homogeneous)
- Conversion: ~40% per pass; unreacted ethylene is recycled.
- Selectivity to acetaldehyde: **>95%**

**Flow diagram (textual):** Ethylene + O₂ → Mixer → Wacker Reactor → Flash Separator (catalyst recycled to regeneration) → Distillation Column 1 (light ends) → Distillation Column 2 (pure CH₃CHO, 99.5%) → Refrigerated storage.

**(b) Role of CuCl₂ in the Wacker Process:**

CuCl₂ serves as a **re-oxidant** (mediator) for the palladium catalyst. In Cycle 1, Pd²⁺ is reduced to Pd⁰ (a black precipitate, catalytically inactive). Without CuCl₂, the reaction would stop within seconds. Cu²⁺ ions from CuCl₂ accept electrons from Pd⁰:
$$Pd^{0} + 2Cu^{2+} \rightarrow Pd^{2+} + 2Cu^{+}$$

The resulting Cu⁺ is then re-oxidized to Cu²⁺ by molecular O₂ (from air):
$$2Cu^{+} + 2HCl + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow 2Cu^{2+} + H_2O + 2Cl^{-}$$

**Why CuCl₂ is preferred over other oxidants (e.g., Fe³⁺, peroxides):**
- **High redox potential selectivity:** Cu²⁺/Cu⁺ potential (E° = +0.16 V) is *just* high enough to re-oxidize Pd⁰ but *not* so high that it attacks the product acetaldehyde.
- **Solubility:** Both CuCl₂ and CuCl are highly soluble in the aqueous HCl medium, giving a homogeneous, kinetically fast re-oxidation step.
- **Inexpensive and non-toxic** (relative to Hg²⁺ or other heavy metals).
- **Compatible** with the HCl medium needed to keep Pd in solution as PdCl₄²⁻.
- **Kinetic compatibility:** The Cu²⁺/Cu⁺ couple operates at rates comparable to the Pd²⁺-ethylene step, avoiding catalyst depletion.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Most Common Mark Loss:**
> 1. Students write the *overall* reaction but skip the **two-cycle mechanism** → lose 3–4 marks. Always derive both cycles.
> 2. Confusing **Pd²⁺/Pd⁰** with **Cu²⁺/Cu⁺** roles. Pd is the *primary* oxidant; Cu is the *mediator*; O₂ is the *ultimate* oxidant. Get this hierarchy wrong → lose 2 marks.
> 3. Forgetting to mention that CuCl₂ is preferred because of its **solubility in HCl** and its **suitably tuned redox potential** → lose 1 mark.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Alternative]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare the **Wacker process** and the **ethanol oxidation route** for acetaldehyde manufacture with respect to feedstock, catalyst, operating conditions, and selectivity.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of reactions, describe the manufacture of **(i) acetic acid** and **(ii) pentaerythritol** from acetaldehyde.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Wacker Process | Ethanol Oxidation |
|---|---|---|
| Feedstock | Ethylene (C₂H₄) | Ethanol (C₂H₅OH) |
| Catalyst | PdCl₂ + CuCl₂ (homogeneous) | Cu or Ag (heterogeneous) |
| Temperature | 120 – 130 °C | 300 – 450 °C |
| Pressure | 3 – 4 atm | Atmospheric |
| Selectivity | >95% | ~95% |
| Energy demand | Moderate (mild T) | High (high T) |
| Co-product | None | H₂O (oxidative) or H₂ (dehydro.) |
| By-products | Chlorinated organics, acetic acid (over-oxidation) | Acetone, ethylene, ethers |
| Capital cost | High (corrosion-resistant materials for Cl⁻) | Lower |
| Current global share | ~70% | ~20% (growing for bio-route) |
| Strategic position | Petrochemical route | Bio / renewable route |

**Strategic comment:** The Wacker route is preferred where cheap ethylene is available (integrated with a steam cracker). The ethanol oxidation route is preferred where cheap bio-ethanol is available (e.g., Brazil, India — sugarcane ethanol). Both routes are operationally proven.

**(b) (i) Manufacture of Acetic Acid from Acetaldehyde:**

Acetaldehyde is oxidized to acetic acid using **molecular oxygen** (air) in the liquid phase, catalyzed by **manganese or cobalt acetate** at **60 – 80 °C** and **3 – 5 atm**.

$$2 CH_3CHO + O_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Mn(OAc)}_2, \, 70\,°C} 2 CH_3COOH$$

**Mechanism (peroxy intermediate):**
- Step 1: CH₃CHO + O₂ → CH₃C(O)OOH (peracetic acid)
- Step 2: CH₃C(O)OOH + CH₃CHO → 2 CH₃COOH

The peracetic acid is the key intermediate; the catalyst Mn²⁺/Mn³⁺ accelerates its decomposition.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical note:** This was the *only* industrial route to acetic acid until the **Monsanto process** (methanol carbonylation, 1970) and the **Cativa process** (BP, 1996) displaced it. Today, methanol carbonylation accounts for **>65%** of world acetic acid production. This is a frequently asked KTU question on process evolution.

**(b) (ii) Manufacture of Pentaerythritol from Acetaldehyde:**

Pentaerythritol is the **tetra-alcohol** C(CH₂OH)₄, manufactured by a **double crossed-aldol + crossed-Cannizzaro** reaction between acetaldehyde and excess formaldehyde in alkaline solution (Ca(OH)₂).

**Step 1 — Triple aldol condensation:**
$$3 CH_3CHO + 3 HCHO \xrightarrow{\text{Ca(OH)}_2} (HOCH_2)_3CCHO + \text{by-products}$$

This produces pentaerythrose (trimethylol acetaldehyde), which has **three** hydroxymethyl groups and one aldehyde group.

**Step 2 — Crossed Cannizzaro with a 4th HCHO:**
$$(HOCH_2)_3CCHO + HCHO + NaOH \rightarrow C(CH_2OH)_4 + HCOONa$$

The fourth HCHO is reduced to formate (HCOONa) while the aldehyde of pentaerythrose is oxidized to a primary alcohol.

**Overall:**
$$3 CH_3CHO + 4 HCHO + NaOH \rightarrow C(CH_2OH)_4 + 3 HCOONa$$

**Applications of pentaerythritol:**
- Alkyd resins (paints, coatings)
- **Pentaerythritol tetranitrate (PETN)** — a powerful explosive
- Lubricant esters
- PVC stabilizers

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Loss in Pentaerythritol Question:**
> 1. Writing only the *overall* equation without showing the **aldol step** and the **Cannizzaro step** separately → lose 3 marks.
> 2. Forgetting that the **alkali is Ca(OH)₂** (for aldol) and then **NaOH** (for Cannizzaro) — they can be combined as "aqueous alkali" but the dual role must be explained.
> 3. Confusing **pentaerythritol (C(CH₂OH)₄)** with **pentaerythrose (intermediate aldehyde)** — names and structures must be correct.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Save this section for the last 5 minutes of revision before your KTU exam.**

✅ **Acetaldehyde = CH₃CHO = ethanal**, MW 44.05, BP 20.2 °C (must be refrigerated/pressurized).

✅ **Three industrial routes:** Wacker (ethylene, dominant), ethanol oxidation, acetylene hydration (obsolete).

✅ **Wacker overall:** C₂H₄ + ½O₂ → CH₃CHO; catalyst PdCl₂/CuCl₂ in aqueous HCl; 120–130 °C, 3–4 atm; selectivity >95%.

✅ **Two-cycle mechanism** is mandatory in any 14-mark Wacker question:
   - Cycle 1: Pd²⁺ oxidizes ethylene → Pd⁰
   - Cycle 2: Cu²⁺ re-oxidizes Pd⁰ → Cu⁺; O₂ re-oxidizes Cu⁺ → Cu²⁺
   - Net: ethylene + ½O₂ → acetaldehyde

✅ **Role of CuCl₂:** mediator/re-oxidant for Pd⁰; preferred for its tuned redox potential and high solubility in HCl.

✅ **Acetaldehyde is a chemical hub** for: acetic acid, ethyl acetate, pentaerythritol, 1,3-butylene glycol, crotonaldehyde, peracetic acid, pyridines.

✅ **Pentaerythritol synthesis:** 3 CH₃CHO + 4 HCHO + NaOH → C(CH₂OH)₄ + 3 HCOONa (aldol + crossed Cannizzaro).

✅ **Acetic acid from acetaldehyde** (old route): CH₃CHO + ½O₂ → CH₃COOH, Mn or Co catalyst, 60–80 °C, via peracetic acid intermediate.

✅ **Explosive limits:** 4.0 – 60.0 vol% in air — extremely wide range; major safety concern.

✅ **Health:** Group 2B carcinogen — closed-loop reactors mandatory in plant design.

✅ **Historical decline of Wacker:** Wacker route for acetic acid was largely **displaced by methanol carbonylation (Monsanto/Cativa)** after 1970.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Propylene and Propylene Derivatives - Sources of Propylene
Production and Application of Propylene Oxide
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT741 - PETROCHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Production and Application of Propylene Oxide

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Propylene Oxide (PO)**, chemically known as **methyloxirane** or **1,2-epoxypropane**, is a highly reactive three-membered cyclic ether (oxirane ring) with the molecular formula **C₃H₆O** and the structural formula **CH₃–CH(–O–)CH₂** (an epoxide ring attached to a methyl-substituted carbon). It is the second largest commercially produced epoxide after **ethylene oxide (EO)** and serves as a critical C₃ building block in the petrochemical industry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECHT741 – Module 3):**
> Propylene oxide is classified as an **olefin oxide** and is industrially manufactured via four major process routes — (i) the **Classical Chlorohydrin Process**, (ii) the **Indirect Oxidation (Co-product) Process** including PO/SM and PO/TBA routes, (iii) the **Hydrogen Peroxide to Propylene Oxide (HPPO) Process**, and (iv) the **Cumene-based PO-only Process**.

### Key Physical & Chemical Properties

| Property | Value | Unit |
|----------|-------|------|
| Molecular Weight | **58.08** | g/mol |
| Boiling Point | **34.0** | °C (highly volatile) |
| Melting Point | **–111.9** | °C |
| Density (at 20 °C) | **0.830** | g/cm³ |
| Flash Point | **–37** | °C (extremely flammable) |
| Auto-ignition Temperature | **449** | °C |
| Vapor Density (air = 1) | **2.0** | — |
| Lower Explosive Limit (LEL) | **2.3** | vol % |
| Upper Explosive Limit (UEL) | **36.0** | vol % |
| Refractive Index (nD²⁰) | **1.366** | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition – Epoxide Ring:**
> An *epoxide* (or *oxirane*) is a cyclic ether with a three-membered ring containing one oxygen atom. The high **ring strain (~27 kcal/mol)** makes propylene oxide extremely reactive toward nucleophiles, acids, bases, alcohols, and amines — which is precisely why it is the cornerstone of polyether polyol synthesis.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **"The Three-Legged Race of Molecules"**
> Imagine propylene oxide as a three-legged race between a carbon duo (C–C) and an oxygen atom. They are forced into a tight triangle — a *three-membered epoxide ring*. This triangle is highly **strained and uncomfortable**, like a tightly wound spring. The moment any chemical "friend" (a nucleophile like water, alcohol, or ammonia) approaches, the spring *snaps open*, relieving the strain and forming a new, stable bond. **Every industrial use of PO exploits this "strain-release snap" to make polyols, glycol ethers, and amines.**

### Why is Propylene Oxide so Industrially Important?

1. **Reactive epoxide ring** → opens easily with nucleophiles to form polyols, glycols, and alkanolamines.
2. **Methyl branch (CH₃–)** → introduces asymmetry, producing both **primary (–CH₂–OH)** and **secondary (–CH(CH₃)–OH)** hydroxyl groups.
3. **C₃ backbone** → bridges the gap between C₂ (EO) and C₄ (butylene oxide) chemistry, offering a unique balance of hydrophobicity and reactivity.
4. **Global production > 11 million metric tons/year (2024 estimate)**, predominantly feeding **polyurethane (PU)** foams via polyether polyols.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Industrial Production Routes — Structured Logic

### Route 1: Classical Chlorohydrin Process (Historical, ~1910s onwards)

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. **Chlorohydrin Formation (Liquid-Phase Addition):**
   Propylene is reacted with **chlorine (Cl₂)** in the presence of **water** to form **propylene chlorohydrin** (two isomers: α- and β-).
2. **Saponification / Dehydrochlorination:**
   The chlorohydrin is treated with **calcium hydroxide (Ca(OH)₂)** or **sodium hydroxide (NaOH)**, which abstracts HCl to close the epoxide ring, liberating propylene oxide and a salt by-product (CaCl₂ or NaCl).
3. **Purification:**
   Crude PO is separated by distillation (b.p. 34 °C) from water, dichloropropane, and aldehydes.

> [!WARNING]
> **Disadvantages of the Chlorohydrin Route:**
> - Consumes large amounts of **Cl₂** and **lime/NaOH**.
> - Generates **2.2–2.5 tons of CaCl₂ brine per ton of PO** (severe effluent problem).
> - Selectivity to PO is only **~88–90%**.
> - Energy-intensive.
> - Largely **phased out** in the West; still operational in some Asian plants.

---

### Route 2: Indirect Oxidation (Co-product Processes) — Modern Workhorse

This route accounts for **> 60% of global PO capacity** (2024). The principle: an **organic hydroperoxide** is used as the oxygen carrier. It epoxidizes propylene, and the "carrier" is converted into a valuable **co-product**.

#### 2A. PO/SM Process (Propylene Oxide / Styrene Monomer)

- **Oxygen carrier:** Ethylbenzene hydroperoxide (EBHP).
- **Co-product:** Styrene Monomer (SM).
- **Catalyst:** Homogeneous Mo(VI) catalyst (e.g., molybdenum naphthenate) in solution.
- **Operating conditions:** ~100–130 °C, 30–60 bar.

#### 2B. PO/TBA Process (Halcon / ARCO / Lyondell)

- **Oxygen carrier:** *tert*-Butyl Hydroperoxide (TBHP).
- **Co-product:** *tert*-Butyl Alcohol (TBA), which can be dehydrated to **isobutylene** or converted to **MTBE**.
- **Catalyst:** Mo, W, or Ti-based homogeneous catalyst.
- **Operating conditions:** ~100–140 °C, 30–60 bar.

#### 2C. PO/MP Process (Sumitomo Chemical, Japan)

- **Oxygen carrier:** Cumene hydroperoxide (CHP).
- **Co-product:** α-Methylstyrene (AMS), which can be hydrogenated back to cumene.
- **Catalyst:** Ti(IV) on SiO₂ heterogeneous catalyst.
- **Unique feature:** *Net* propylene + O₂ → PO + H₂O (cumene is recycled), so the **only co-product is water**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Indirect"?**
> Because the oxygen atom in PO comes *not* from O₂ directly, but from a hydroperoxide intermediate. This avoids the kinetic difficulty of directly oxidizing propylene (which prefers allylic oxidation to acrolein over epoxidation).

---

### Route 3: HPPO Process (Hydrogen Peroxide to Propylene Oxide)

The **HPPO process** is the **cleanest, greenest** route, jointly developed by **BASF/Dow (now BASF)** and **Evonik/Uhde (now Evonik)**. Commercial plants operate in Antwerp, Belgium, and Ulsan, South Korea.

- **Reagent:** Aqueous **H₂O₂ (hydrogen peroxide, ~30–70 wt%)**.
- **Catalyst:** **TS-1 zeolite (Titanium Silicalite-1)** — a microporous titanosilicate with isolated Ti⁴⁺ sites.
- **Solvent:** Methanol.
- **Operating conditions:** ~40–60 °C, atmospheric to mild pressure.
- **Co-product:** **Only water** (clean, effluent-free).

#### Chemistry:
$$ \text{CH}_3\text{–CH=CH}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{TS-1 Zeolite, MeOH}} \text{CH}_3\text{–CH(–O–)CH}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** Why is HPPO considered the *future* of PO manufacture? Answer: (i) No co-product, (ii) high selectivity (>95%), (iii) no chlorinated waste, (iv) mild conditions, (v) H₂O₂ can be sourced from the **anthraquinone auto-oxidation (AO) process** powered by green H₂.

---

### Route 4: Cumene-Based PO-Only Process (Sumitomo / Mitsui)

Similar in concept to PO/MP but engineered to suppress AMS recovery and operate on a *PO-only* basis. Propylene + O₂ + cumene are co-fed over a Ti-based catalyst. The economic viability depends on the local market price of α-methylstyrene.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas use the convention that **PA** = Annual Production (tons/year), **η** = Selectivity, **C** = Concentration (mol/L), **MW** = Molecular Weight, **F** = Volumetric flow (L/h), **P** = Productivity (kg·L⁻¹·h⁻¹), **t** = Reaction time (h).

| # | Formula / Metric | Mathematical Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | PO Yield | $Y_{PO} = \dfrac{n_{PO,out}}{n_{C_3H_6,in}} \times 100\%$ | % | Reactor performance |
| 2 | Selectivity | $S = \dfrac{\text{mol PO formed}}{\text{mol propylene consumed}} \times 100\%$ | % | Catalyst quality |
| 3 | Conversion | $X_{C_3H_6} = \dfrac{n_{C_3H_6,in} - n_{C_3H_6,out}}{n_{C_3H_6,in}} \times 100\%$ | % | Feed utilization |
| 4 | Mass Balance (Stoichiometric) | $1 \text{ mol C}_3\text{H}_6 \equiv 1 \text{ mol PO} \equiv 58.08 \text{ g}$ | — | Yield calculation |
| 5 | HPPO Stoichiometry | $\text{C}_3\text{H}_6 + \text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{C}_3\text{H}_6\text{O} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$ | — | Atomic balance |
| 6 | H₂O₂ Requirement | $m_{H_2O_2} = \dfrac{MW_{H_2O_2}}{MW_{PO}} \times m_{PO} = 0.585 \times m_{PO}$ | kg | Process design |
| 7 | Space-Time Yield (STY) | $STY = \dfrac{m_{PO}}{V_{cat} \cdot t}$ | kg·L⁻¹·h⁻¹ | Catalyst benchmarking |
| 8 | CaCl₂ By-product (Chlorohydrin) | $m_{CaCl_2} = 2.2 \text{ to } 2.5 \times m_{PO}$ | t / t PO | Waste mgmt |
| 9 | Co-product Ratio (PO/SM) | 1 t PO ≈ **2.5 t SM** | t/t | Economics |
| 10 | Co-product Ratio (PO/TBA) | 1 t PO ≈ **2.4 t TBA** | t/t | Economics |
| 11 | Ring Strain Energy (epoxide) | $E_{strain} \approx 27$ | kcal/mol | Reactivity driver |
| 12 | Purity Specification | PO commercial grade ≥ **99.95%** | wt% | ASTM D2110 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:**
> The selectivity ($S$) and STY formulas are *universally* used in industrial catalyst screening. The **co-product ratios** (rows 9–10) dictate which process route is profitable in a given region — e.g., in Asia where SM demand is high, **PO/SM** dominates; in regions with oxygenated-fuel markets, **PO/TBA** is preferred.

## 2.3 Reaction Chemistry of Propylene Oxide

The **epoxide ring opening** is the heart of PO chemistry. Two pathways:

**(a) Acid-catalyzed (regioselective toward more substituted C — Markovnikov):**
$$ \text{CH}_3\text{–CH(–O–)CH}_2 + \text{ROH} \xrightarrow{\text{H}^+} \text{CH}_3\text{–CH(OH)–CH}_2\text{–OR} $$

**(b) Base-catalyzed (regioselective toward less hindered C — anti-Markovnikov):**
$$ \text{CH}_3\text{–CH(–O–)CH}_2 + \text{ROH} \xrightarrow{\text{OH}^-} \text{CH}_3\text{–CH(OR)–CH}_2\text{–OH} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Conceptual Question Hook:**
> *Why does acid catalysis favor the more substituted carbon?* Because protonation of the epoxide oxygen makes the more substituted carbon (bearing the CH₃ group) bear a larger partial positive charge (tertiary-like carbocation character), which is stabilized by hyperconjugation and the methyl group. Base catalysis, in contrast, follows **S_N2 kinetics**, so the nucleophile attacks the *less hindered* primary CH₂ carbon.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Mass Balance for a PO/SM Plant

**Problem (KTU-Style):**
A PO/SM plant produces **200,000 metric tons/year of PO**. Using the co-product ratio of **1 t PO : 2.5 t SM**, the EBHP (ethylbenzene hydroperoxide) consumption is **2.95 t per t PO**, and the propylene feed is **0.80 t per t PO**. Calculate:
(a) Annual SM production.
(b) Annual EBHP consumption.
(c) Annual propylene feed.
(d) Annual water by-product (from H₂O₂ decomposition in EBHP generation).

### Solution — Exhaustive Step-by-Step

**Given:**
- $P_{PO} = 200{,}000 \text{ t/yr}$
- Co-product ratio: $1 \text{ t PO} \rightarrow 2.5 \text{ t SM}$
- EBHP consumption: $2.95 \text{ t EBHP per t PO}$
- Propylene feed: $0.80 \text{ t C}_3\text{H}_6 \text{ per t PO}$

---

**(a) Annual SM production:**

We define the SM yield per ton of PO as the co-product ratio:

$$ S_{SM/PO} = \dfrac{m_{SM}}{m_{PO}} = 2.5 \text{ t/t} $$

Therefore, the total mass of SM produced per year is:

$$ m_{SM} = S_{SM/PO} \times P_{PO} $$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$ m_{SM} = 2.5 \times 200{,}000 $$

$$ \boxed{m_{SM} = 500{,}000 \text{ t/yr}} $$

---

**(b) Annual EBHP consumption:**

The EBHP requirement per unit mass of PO is given directly:

$$ m_{EBHP} = 2.95 \times P_{PO} $$

$$ m_{EBHP} = 2.95 \times 200{,}000 $$

$$ \boxed{m_{EBHP} = 590{,}000 \text{ t/yr}} $$

---

**(c) Annual propylene feed:**

The propylene consumption per ton of PO is the specific feed ratio:

$$ m_{C_3H_6} = 0.80 \times P_{PO} $$

$$ m_{C_3H_6} = 0.80 \times 200{,}000 $$

$$ \boxed{m_{C_3H_6} = 160{,}000 \text{ t/yr}} $$

---

**(d) Annual water by-product:**

In the EBHP generation step, ethylbenzene (EB) is oxidized by air:
$$ \text{EB} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{EBHP (peroxide)} $$
During the epoxidation step, the EBHP oxygen is transferred to propylene, regenerating EB and forming a co-product 1-phenylethanol (which dehydrates to SM):
$$ \text{EBHP} + \text{C}_3\text{H}_6 \rightarrow \text{PO} + \text{1-phenylethanol} \rightarrow \text{SM} + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$

For every mole of EBHP consumed, **1 mole of H₂O** is released. Using molecular weights:
- $MW_{EBHP} = 152.19 \text{ g/mol}$ (C₈H₁₀O₂)
- $MW_{H_2O} = 18.02 \text{ g/mol}$

Mass of H₂O per ton of PO:

$$ m_{H_2O} = m_{EBHP/tPO} \times \dfrac{MW_{H_2O}}{MW_{EBHP}} $$

$$ m_{H_2O} = 2.95 \times \dfrac{18.02}{152.19} $$

$$ m_{H_2O} = 2.95 \times 0.1184 $$

$$ m_{H_2O} = 0.3493 \text{ t H}_2\text{O per t PO} $$

Annual water:

$$ M_{H_2O} = 0.3493 \times 200{,}000 $$

$$ \boxed{M_{H_2O} = 69{,}860 \text{ t/yr}} $$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Selectivity Calculation for HPPO Reactor

**Problem:**
An HPPO reactor is fed with $F_{in,C_3H_6} = 100 \text{ mol/h}$ of propylene and $F_{in,H_2O_2} = 105 \text{ mol/h}$ of H₂O₂. The outlet stream contains: $F_{out,C_3H_6} = 4 \text{ mol/h}$, $F_{out,H_2O_2} = 7 \text{ mol/h}$, and $F_{out,PO} = 94 \text{ mol/h}$. Calculate (a) propylene conversion, (b) H₂O₂ conversion, (c) selectivity to PO, (d) overall yield.

### Solution

**(a) Propylene conversion:**

$$ X_{C_3H_6} = \dfrac{F_{in,C_3H_6} - F_{out,C_3H_6}}{F_{in,C_3H_6}} \times 100\% $$

$$ X_{C_3H_6} = \dfrac{100 - 4}{100} \times 100\% $$

$$ \boxed{X_{C_3H_6} = 96\%} $$

---

**(b) H₂O₂ conversion:**

$$ X_{H_2O_2} = \dfrac{F_{in,H_2O_2} - F_{out,H_2O_2}}{F_{in,H_2O_2}} \times 100\% $$

$$ X_{H_2O_2} = \dfrac{105 - 7}{105} \times 100\% $$

$$ X_{H_2O_2} = \dfrac{98}{105} \times 100\% $$

$$ \boxed{X_{H_2O_2} = 93.33\%} $$

---

**(c) Selectivity to PO:**

Selectivity is defined as the moles of desired product (PO) formed per mole of limiting reactant consumed. Here propylene is the *desired feed*:

$$ S_{PO} = \dfrac{F_{out,PO}}{F_{in,C_3H_6} - F_{out,C_3H_6}} \times 100\% $$

$$ S_{PO} = \dfrac{94}{100 - 4} \times 100\% $$

$$ S_{PO} = \dfrac{94}{96} \times 100\% $$

$$ \boxed{S_{PO} = 97.92\%} $$

---

**(d) Overall yield:**

$$ Y = X_{C_3H_6} \times S_{PO} \times 100\% $$

Or equivalently, $Y = \dfrac{F_{out,PO}}{F_{in,C_3H_6}} \times 100\%$:

$$ Y = \dfrac{94}{100} \times 100\% $$

$$ \boxed{Y = 94\%} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A well-designed industrial HPPO reactor achieves $X_{C_3H_6} \approx 95\%$, $S_{PO} \approx 95\%$, $Y \approx 90\%$. The ~2% propylene loss goes to **propylene glycol** (over-hydrolysis) and minor **acetone/Propanal** by-products.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Process Routes

| Feature | Chlorohydrin | PO/SM | PO/TBA | HPPO | Cumene-PO |
|---------|--------------|-------|--------|------|-----------|
| Year commercialized | 1910s | 1969 (Halcon) | 1969 (Halcon) | 2008 (BASF/Dow) | 2003 (Sumitomo) |
| Oxidant | Cl₂ + Ca(OH)₂ | EBHP (O₂) | TBHP (O₂) | H₂O₂ | Cumene + O₂ |
| Catalyst | None (liquid) | Mo homogeneous | Mo homogeneous | TS-1 zeolite | Ti/SiO₂ |
| Co-product | CaCl₂ (waste) | Styrene Monomer | TBA / Isobutylene | **Water only** | α-Methylstyrene |
| PO selectivity (%) | 88–90 | 91–95 | 91–95 | 95–98 | 90–93 |
| Capex (relative) | 1.0 | 1.2 | 1.2 | 1.0 | 1.3 |
| Environmental impact | **High** (Cl⁻ waste) | Medium | Medium | **Low** (green) | Medium |
| Global share (2024, %) | < 5 | ~38 | ~20 | ~12 | ~5 |
| Key licensors | — | Lyondell, Shell | Lyondell | BASF, Evonik | Sumitomo |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Flow Diagram — PO/SM Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Oxidation_Section
        AIR[Air / O2] --> OX[EB Oxidation Reactor]
        EB[Ethylbenzene] --> OX
        OX -->|EBHP| EBP_PUMP[Pump]
    end

    subgraph Epoxidation_Section
        PGP[Propylene Feed] --> EP[Propylene Epoxidation Reactor]
        EBP_PUMP --> EP
        EP -->|Mo Catalyst| EP_OUT[Crude Product Stream]
    end

    subgraph Separation_Section
        EP_OUT --> SEP1[Distillation Column 1]
        SEP1 -->|Heavy ends| SEP1_H[Styrene + Tars]
        SEP1 -->|Overhead| SEP2[Distillation Column 2]
        SEP2 -->|PO Product| PO_TANK[PO Storage Tank]
        SEP2 -->|Bottoms| SEP3[Dehydration Reactor]
        SEP3 -->|Water| WATER[Waste Water]
        SEP3 -->|Styrene| SM_TANK[Styrene Monomer Product]
    end

    subgraph EB_Recovery
        SEP1_H --> REC[EB Recovery Column]
        REC -->|Recovered EB| EB
    end

    PO_TANK[PO Storage Tank] -.->|99.95 percent| CUSTOMER[Polyurethane / Glycol Customer]
    SM_TANK[Styrene Monomer Product] -.->|Polymer Grade| CUSTOMER2[PS / EPS / ABS Customer]
```

## 4.2 Block Flow Diagram — HPPO Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph HPPO_Reactor_Block
        PR[Propylene Feed] --> HPPO_R[HPPO Reactor TS1 Zeolite Catalyst 60 C 1 atm]
        H2O2[Aqueous H2O2 50 wt percent] --> HPPO_R
        MEOH[Methanol Solvent] --> HPPO_R
    end

    HPPO_R --> SEP_A[Separator Low Pressure]
    SEP_A -->|Gas phase unreacted C3H6| RECYCLE_C3[Recycle Compressor]
    RECYCLE_C3 --> PR
    SEP_A -->|Liquid phase| DIST1[Distillation Column 1 Methanol Recovery]

    DIST1 -->|Methanol overhead| RECYCLE_MEOH[Methanol Recycle Drum]
    RECYCLE_MEOH --> MEOH
    DIST1 -->|Bottoms| DIST2[Distillation Column 2 PO Purification]

    DIST2 -->|PO overhead 99.95 percent| PO_PRODUCT[PO Product Tank]
    DIST2 -->|Water Bottoms| WATER_OUT[Process Water Effluent]

    H2O2_PROD[H2O2 Plant Anthraquinone AO Process] --> H2O2
```

## 4.4 Epoxide Ring-Opening Mechanism (Mermaid Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PO[Propylene Oxide Epoxide Ring] -->|Acid Catalyzed H plus| ACID[Protonated Epoxide]
    ACID -->|Nucleophile Attack at More Substituted C| PROD_A[MARKOVNIKOV Product Secondary OH]
    PO -->|Base Catalyzed OH minus| BASE[Activated Nucleophile RO minus]
    BASE -->|S N 2 Attack at Less Hindered Primary C| PROD_B[ANTI MARKOVNIKOV Product Primary OH]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] *(3 Marks)*
**Define propylene oxide. Give its IUPAC name and list two important industrial applications.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Propylene oxide (PO) is an **olefin oxide** with the molecular formula **C₃H₆O**, characterized by a three-membered **epoxide (oxirane) ring** attached to a methyl-substituted carbon.

**IUPAC Name:** **Methyloxirane** (also accepted: 1,2-epoxypropane; 2-methyloxirane).

**Two Industrial Applications:**
1. **Manufacture of polyether polyols** (the largest application, ~65% of PO) used in **polyurethane (PU) foams** — flexible (mattresses, automotive seats) and rigid (insulation, refrigerators).
2. **Manufacture of propylene glycol (PG)** by hydrolysis — used as **food additive (E1520)**, pharmaceutical excipient, and **antifreeze/coolant** in industrial heat-transfer systems.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + IUPAC, 1 for each application.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] *(3 Marks)*
**Differentiate between the Chlorohydrin process and the Chlorohydrin-free (Indirect Oxidation) process for propylene oxide manufacture.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Chlorohydrin Process | Indirect Oxidation (PO/SM, PO/TBA) |
|--------|---------------------|-----------------------------------|
| **Oxidant** | Cl₂ + Ca(OH)₂ | Organic hydroperoxide (EBHP / TBHP) |
| **Co-product / Waste** | CaCl₂ brine (~2.2 t/t PO) | Valuable co-product (SM or TBA) |
| **Selectivity** | 88–90% | 91–95% |
| **Catalyst** | None (stoichiometric) | Mo-based homogeneous |
| **Environmental impact** | High (chloride effluent) | Moderate (no chlorides) |
| **Current status** | Largely phased out | **Dominant technology** |

*(3 marks: tabular comparison with at least 4 distinguishing points.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, adapted]*

**Q. (a)** Describe with a neat flow diagram the **manufacture of propylene oxide by the Chlorohydrin process**. Discuss the chemistry of each step. *(7 Marks)*

**Q. (b)** Explain the **HPPO process** for propylene oxide manufacture. What are its advantages over conventional routes? *(7 Marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A(a) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Propylene Chlorohydrin Formation (2 marks):**
Propylene gas is absorbed in water, and chlorine is introduced. The reaction proceeds at 35–50 °C in a packed tower.

$$ \text{CH}_3\text{–CH=CH}_2 + \text{Cl}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{–CH(OH)–CH}_2\text{Cl} + \text{CH}_3\text{–CHCl–CH}_2\text{OH} $$

A mixture of α-chlorohydrin (~90%) and β-chlorohydrin (~10%) is formed.

**Step 2 — Saponification (2 marks):**
The chlorohydrin solution is fed to a saponification column where 10% milk of lime (Ca(OH)₂) is added. Intramolecular dehydrochlorination closes the epoxide ring.

$$ \text{CH}_3\text{–CH(OH)–CH}_2\text{Cl} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{Ca(OH)}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{–CH(–O–)CH}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{CaCl}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$

**Step 3 — Purification (2 marks):**
The crude PO is steam-stripped from the saponifier, then distilled in two columns to remove water, dichloropropane, acetaldehyde, and propionaldehyde. Final purity: **99.95%**.

**Step 4 — Flow Diagram (1 mark):**
Provide a sketch showing: Cl₂ + H₂O + C₃H₆ → Chlorohydrin Reactor → Saponifier → Steam Stripper → Distillation Column 1 → Distillation Column 2 → PO Product.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A(b) (7 Marks)

**Definition (1 mark):**
The HPPO (Hydrogen Peroxide to Propylene Oxide) process is a *direct epoxidation* of propylene using aqueous **H₂O₂** over a **TS-1 (Titanium Silicalite-1)** zeolite catalyst in methanol solvent.

**Chemistry (2 marks):**
$$ \text{C}_3\text{H}_6 + \text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{TS-1, MeOH, 50 °C}} \text{C}_3\text{H}_6\text{O} + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$

**Process Description (2 marks):**
- **Feed:** Propylene + ~50 wt% H₂O₂ + methanol solvent.
- **Reactor:** Fixed-bed multitubular reactor, 40–60 °C, near-atmospheric pressure.
- **Catalyst:** TS-1 zeolite with isolated tetrahedral Ti⁴⁺ active sites.
- **Separation:** Methanol recovered and recycled; PO purified by distillation; only water as by-product.

**Advantages over Conventional Routes (2 marks):**
1. **No co-product** — only water is formed; eliminates the economic dependency on styrene/TBA markets.
2. **High selectivity** (>95% to PO, >90% H₂O₂ utilization).
3. **No chloride waste** — environmentally clean.
4. **Mild conditions** — low temperature, low pressure, lower capex and opex.
5. **Green chemistry compatibility** — H₂O₂ can be coupled to renewable-H₂-based electrolyzer H₂O₂ plants.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) *[KTU University Exam – May 2023, adapted]*

**Q. (a)** With a block diagram, describe the **PO/SM (Styrene Monomer) co-product process** for propylene oxide manufacture. *(7 Marks)*

**Q. (b)** Discuss the **major industrial applications of propylene oxide** with their end-user markets and percentage shares. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B(a) (7 Marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The PO/SM process (also called the Halcon process, commercialized in 1969) couples two reactions: (i) EB → EBHP via air oxidation, and (ii) EBHP + C₃H₆ → PO + 1-phenylethanol (which dehydrates to styrene). It is the **largest single route** to PO worldwide.

**Step 1 — EB Oxidation (2 marks):**
Liquid ethylbenzene is oxidized with air at 120–150 °C, 2–5 bar, producing ethylbenzene hydroperoxide (EBHP).

$$ \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{–CH}_2\text{CH}_3 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{–CH(CH}_3\text{)–OOH} $$

**Step 2 — Epoxidation (2 marks):**
EBHP reacts with propylene in a Mo-naphthenate catalyzed liquid-phase reactor at 100–130 °C, 30–60 bar.

$$ \text{EBHP} + \text{C}_3\text{H}_6 \rightarrow \text{C}_3\text{H}_6\text{O} + \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{–CH(OH)–CH}_3 $$

**Step 3 — Co-product Formation (1 mark):**
The 1-phenylethanol is dehydrated over TiO₂/Al₂O₃ at 200–250 °C to styrene monomer (SM).

$$ \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{–CH(OH)–CH}_3 \xrightarrow{\text{TiO}_2} \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{–CH=CH}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$

**Step 4 — EB Recovery (1 mark):**
Unreacted EB and styrene are separated in distillation trains; EB is recycled.

**Block Diagram Reference:** Use the diagram in Section 4.1 above.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B(b) (7 Marks)

| Application | Reaction | % of PO | End-User Market |
|-------------|----------|---------|-----------------|
| **Polyether Polyols** | PO + polyol initiator (glycerol, sucrose, sorbitol) | **65%** | Flexible PU foam (mattresses, furniture, auto seats), Rigid PU foam (insulation, refrigeration, construction) |
| **Propylene Glycol (PG)** | PO + H₂O (hydrolysis) | **15%** | Food (E1520 humectant), Pharma, Cosmetics, Antifreeze, Heat-transfer fluid |
| **Glycol Ethers** | PO + alcohol (ROH) | **8%** | Paints, Coatings, Industrial & household cleaners, Printing inks |
| **Alkanolamines** | PO + NH₃ (or amines) | **5%** | Natural gas sweetening (DEA, MDEA, DIPA), CO₂ capture, Surfactants |
| **Other Uses** | Miscellaneous | **7%** | Flame retardants (e.g., TDCPP), Synthetic lubricants, Hydraulic fluids, Crop protection |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The dominance of polyols in the PO consumption pattern reflects the global construction, automotive, and refrigeration industries' demand for polyurethane foams. The growth of HPPO technology is tightly linked to the **monomer-grade propylene supply** and the **availability of cheap H₂O₂**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Wrong co-product ratios** in mass-balance problems: PO/SM = 1:2.5 (not 1:1); PO/TBA = 1:2.4.
> 2. **Confusing "yield" with "selectivity"** — Yield = Conversion × Selectivity; Selectivity alone ignores unconverted feed.
> 3. **Forgetting to mention the catalyst** in the HPPO process (TS-1 zeolite is *mandatory*).
> 4. **Writing "propylene" instead of "propylene oxide"** in the product side of stoichiometric equations.
> 5. **Skipping the dehydration step** in PO/SM (1-phenylethanol → styrene is a *separate* reaction; the epoxidation does *not* directly yield SM).
> 6. **Not specifying molecular weight units (g/mol)** in numerical problems — KTU evaluators deduct 0.5 marks for unit ambiguity.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Propylene Oxide (PECHT741 / Module 3)**

- [x] **PO = methyloxirane = 1,2-epoxypropane = C₃H₆O (MW = 58.08 g/mol)**
- [x] **Boiling point 34 °C** — highly volatile, low-flash flammable liquid
- [x] **Epoxide ring strain ≈ 27 kcal/mol** — the *driver* of all PO reactivity
- [x] **Four production routes:** (1) Chlorohydrin, (2) PO/SM, (3) PO/TBA, (4) HPPO, (5) Cumene-PO
- [x] **Chlorohydrin route generates ~2.2 t CaCl₂/t PO** → phasing out
- [x] **PO/SM co-product ratio = 1:2.5** (PO:SM by mass)
- [x] **PO/TBA co-product ratio = 1:2.4** (PO:TBA by mass)
- [x] **HPPO: H₂O₂ + C₃H₆ → PO + H₂O** (only water as by-product)
- [x] **HPPO catalyst = TS-1 zeolite (Titanium Silicalite-1)** at 40–60 °C
- [x] **Acid-catalyzed ring opening → Markovnikov (more substituted C)**
- [x] **Base-catalyzed ring opening → anti-Markovnikov (less hindered C)**
- [x] **Largest application: Polyether polyols for PU foams (~65%)**
- [x] **Second largest: Propylene glycol (PG, ~15%)** via PO hydrolysis
- [x] **Other uses:** glycol ethers (8%), alkanolamines (5%), others (7%)
- [x] **Global PO production > 11 MMT/year (2024)**
- [x] **Purity specification: ≥ 99.95%** (ASTM D2110)
- [x] **Selectivity benchmark:** Chlorohydrin 88–90%; Co-product 91–95%; **HPPO 95–98%**
- [x] **Green future:** HPPO + renewable H₂O₂ (from green H₂) is the *sustainable* trajectory
- [x] **Key licensors:** LyondellBasell, Shell, BASF, Dow, Evonik, Sumitomo

> [!TIP]
> **One-Liner Memory Aid:**
> *"**P**ropylene **O**xide — the **P**owerful **O**xirane that **P**umps **P**olyols, **P**ropylene Glycol, and **P**aints."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Propylene Glycol
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT741 - PETROCHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Propylene Glycol

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Propylene Glycol is a high-volume oxygenated petrochemical derived primarily from propylene oxide. It is a viscous, colorless, hygroscopic diol with formula **C₃H₈O₂** and serves as a critical building block for polyesters, antifreeze, food additives, and pharmaceutical excipients.

### Formal Academic Definition

**Propylene Glycol (PG)**, chemically known as **1,2-Propanediol** or **Propane-1,2-diol** (IUPAC), is the simplest vicinal diol (glycol) bearing one secondary and one primary hydroxyl group. Its structure features a two-carbon chain with hydroxyl groups on adjacent carbons, and a methyl side group:

$$\text{CH}_3-\text{CH(OH)}-\text{CH}_2-\text{OH}$$

- **Molecular Formula:** C₃H₈O₂
- **Molar Mass:** **76.09 g/mol**
- **CAS Registry Number:** 57-55-6
- **Synonyms:** 1,2-Propanediol, Methylene Glycol, Monopropylene Glycol (MPG), 1,2-Dihydroxypropane

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Distinction:** "Industrial Grade" PG is used in antifreeze and polymer synthesis, while "USP/Food Grade" PG meets strict purity (≥ 99.5%) for pharmaceutical and food use. This dual-grade nature is frequently tested in KTU viva voce.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine a small molecular "Lego block" that loves water. Propylene Glycol is essentially a water-soluble bridge molecule — it has two sticky ends (the –OH groups) that love clinging to water, while its carbon backbone gives it mild oily characteristics. This is exactly why it is used in:
> - **Antifreeze** — it lowers the freezing point of water without the extreme toxicity of ethylene glycol.
> - **Skin lotions** — it pulls moisture from the air to the skin (humectant).
> - **Fog machines** — it vaporizes smoothly without producing toxic smoke.

Think of PG as **"a non-toxic, water-friendly, molecular sponge"** that chemists use as a solvent, plasticizer, and chemical intermediate.

### Key Physical Constants (KTU High-Yield Values)

| Property | Value | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Boiling Point (101.3 kPa) | **187.4 °C** | High BP → good thermal stability in heat transfer fluids |
| Freezing Point | **–59 °C (supercools to –60 °C)** | Lowers water freezing point in coolants |
| Density (20 °C) | **1.036 g/cm³** | Heavier than water → aids separation in distillation |
| Viscosity (20 °C) | **60.5 mPa·s (cP)** | Higher than EG (ethylene glycol = 21 cP) → pump sizing impact |
| Flash Point (open cup) | **107 °C** | Moderately flammable → storage/handling |
| Refractive Index (n₂₀ᴰ) | **1.4324** | Quality control identity test |
| Autoignition Temperature | **371 °C** | Process safety design parameter |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Isomerism Consideration (Frequently Tested)

Propylene oxide (the upstream feedstock) is prochiral. Its hydrolysis yields two isomeric glycols:

- **1,2-Propanediol (Propylene Glycol, PG)** — primary product at acidic/neutral pH.
- **1,3-Propanediol (Trimethylene Glycol)** — minor; from propionaldehyde hydration.

The acid-catalyzed ring opening follows **Markovnikov's rule**, giving mostly the secondary alcohol intermediate, which rearranges to the stable 1,2-diol.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Question:** *"Why is propylene glycol the major product and not 1,3-propanediol in PO hydrolysis?"* — Answer: PO is a 3-membered epoxide. Under acid catalysis, protonation gives a more stable secondary carbocation; nucleophilic attack by water yields the secondary –OH at C-2 and primary –OH at C-1 → **propane-1,2-diol**.

### 2.2 Reaction Chemistry — The Two Principal Industrial Routes

#### Route A — Non-Catalytic Hydration of Propylene Oxide (PO)

$$\text{CH}_3-\underset{\text{PO}}{\text{CH}-\text{CH}_2} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\;\Delta\;} \text{CH}_3-\text{CH(OH)}-\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$$

- **Conditions:** 120–200 °C, 1–3 MPa, large molar excess of water (≈ 20:1 water:PO).
- **Catalyst:** None (thermal) — or dilute H₂SO₄ historically.
- **Selectivity:** ~ 90 % to PG; byproducts: dipropylene glycol (DPG), tripropylene glycol (TPG), and small amounts of **acetol** and **propanal**.
- **Heat of Reaction (ΔH):** approximately **–88 kJ/mol** (exothermic; requires cooling duty).

#### Route B — Catalytic Hydration with Acidic/Base Catalysts

Modern plants use **solid-acid catalysts** (Amberlyst, H-mordenite, or sulfonated ion-exchange resins) at 100–160 °C, achieving selectivities of > 95 %.

#### Route C — Glycerol Hydrogenolysis (Bio-Based Route)

Glycerol (a biodiesel byproduct) is hydrogenated to propylene glycol using **Cu/Cr** or **Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃** catalysts:

$$\text{HOCH}_2-\text{CH(OH)}-\text{CH}_2\text{OH} + \text{H}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Cu-Cr, 200 °C, 3 MPa}} \text{CH}_3\text{-CH(OH)-CH}_2\text{OH} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

This **green route** is gaining industrial traction due to biodiesel surplus economics.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory for Exam:** The following formulas and material balances appear in almost every KTU numerical on propylene glycol manufacture.

| Formula / Relation | Description / Engineering Use | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $\displaystyle \text{Conversion} = \frac{n_{\text{PO,in}} - n_{\text{PO,out}}}{n_{\text{PO,in}}} \times 100$ | Reactor conversion efficiency | % |
| $\displaystyle \text{Selectivity} = \frac{n_{\text{PG,out}}}{n_{\text{PO,reacted}}} \times 100$ | Reaction selectivity (high-yield routes target >95 %) | % |
| $\displaystyle \text{Yield} = \frac{\text{Conversion} \times \text{Selectivity}}{100}$ | Overall yield | % |
| $\displaystyle \Delta T_f = i \cdot K_f \cdot m$ (freezing point depression) | Cryoscopic use in antifreeze formulation | °C |
| $\displaystyle \frac{d\ln K}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{RT^2}$ (van't Hoff) | Temperature effect on PO hydration equilibrium | — |
| Mass balance: $F_{\text{PO,in}} = F_{\text{PO,out}} + F_{\text{PG,out}}\!\cdot\!\dfrac{M_{\text{PO}}}{M_{\text{PG}}} + \text{byproducts}$ | Reactor overall material balance | kg/h |
| $\displaystyle Q = \dot{n}_{\text{PO}} \cdot (-\Delta H)$ | Heat duty for reactor cooling coil design | kW |
| $\displaystyle P_{\text{vap}} = 10^{A - B/(T+C)}$ (Antoine for PG) | Distillation column design: A, B, C are Antoine constants | kPa |
| Antoine constants for PG (P in mmHg, T in °C): $A=8.64420$, $B=2696.3$, $C=298.0$ (range 100–200 °C) | Used in reboiler/condenser design | — |
| Freezing point depression constant $K_f$ (water) = **1.86 °C·kg/mol** | Antifreeze formulation math | — |

**Critical Note on Table Math:** Avoid the `|` character; use `\vert` or `\mid` when writing modular quantities. Example: $\text{LHS} = \vert \Delta H^\circ \vert / RT^2$ instead of $|\Delta H^\circ|$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Heat Transfer Fluids:** PG-water mixtures (40–60 % PG) freeze below –30 °C and are used in chilled-water loops and brewery glycol chillers (non-toxic, food-grade).
- **Polyester Resins:** Unsaturated polyesters for marine/fiberglass use propylene glycol as the diol monomer — superior flexibility and impact resistance over ethylene glycol analogues.
- **Pharmaceutical Solvent:** USP-grade PG is the excipient of choice for injectables (e.g., diazepam, phenobarbital) where ethanol-free, water-miscible carrier is required.
- **Cosmetics:** Acts as **humectant** (moisture attractor), **solvent** for fragrances, and **preservative booster** in lotions and deodorants.
- **Food Industry (E1520):** Solvent for flavors, colors; used in e-cigarette vapor liquids.
