## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Reactor Design: Stoichiometric Material Balance (Non-Catalytic PO Hydration)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is a KTU numerical-type problem.** Given PO feed rate, water:PO ratio, conversion, and selectivity, derive PG production rate.

**Problem Statement:**
A continuous stirred-tank reactor (CSTR) for propylene glycol production is fed with:
- Propylene oxide (PO) = 100 kmol/h (pure, MW = 58.08 kg/kmol)
- Water (H₂O) = 2000 kmol/h (20:1 molar ratio)

Given:
- Conversion of PO = 90 %
- Selectivity to PG = 92 %
- Byproduct selectivity to DPG (dipropylene glycol) = 6 %; remaining 2 % to acetol + others.

Compute the outlet molar flow rates of PO, PG, DPG, and unreacted water.

---

**Step 1 — Define conversion and selectivity**

$$\text{Conversion (X)} = 0.90 \implies \text{PO reacted} = 100 \times 0.90 = 90 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 2 — Apply selectivity**

$$\text{Selectivity}_{\text{PG}} = 0.92 \implies \text{PG produced} = 90 \times 0.92 = 82.8 \text{ kmol/h}$$

$$\text{Selectivity}_{\text{DPG}} = 0.06 \implies \text{DPG produced} = 90 \times 0.06 = 5.4 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 3 — By-product reactions (Stoichiometry)**

- **PG formation:** PO + H₂O → PG (1 mol PO : 1 mol H₂O : 1 mol PG)
- **DPG formation:** 2 PO + 1 H₂O → DPG (2 mol PO : 1 mol H₂O : 1 mol DPG)
- **Acetol:** PO → acetol (small)

**Step 4 — Water consumed**

$$\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{H}_2\text{O, consumed}} &= n_{\text{PG produced}} \times 1 + n_{\text{DPG produced}} \times 1 \\
&= 82.8 \times 1 + 5.4 \times 1 \\
&= 88.2 \text{ kmol/h}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — PO remaining**

$$n_{\text{PO,out}} = 100 - 90 = 10 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 6 — Water remaining**

$$n_{\text{H}_2\text{O,out}} = 2000 - 88.2 = 1911.8 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 7 — PG mass rate (kg/h)**

MW(PG) = 76.09 kg/kmol

$$\dot{m}_{\text{PG}} = 82.8 \times 76.09 = 6300.25 \text{ kg/h} \approx 6.30 \text{ ton/h}$$

**Step 8 — DPG mass rate**

MW(DPG, C₆H₁₄O₃) = 134.17 kg/kmol

$$\dot{m}_{\text{DPG}} = 5.4 \times 134.17 = 724.5 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 9 — Reactor Heat Duty**

Heat of PO hydration ≈ –88 kJ/mol

$$Q = 90 \text{ kmol/h} \times 88000 \text{ kJ/kmol} = 7{,}920{,}000 \text{ kJ/h} = 2200 \text{ kW}$$

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Distillation Column Sizing (PG-Water Separation)

A typical PG reactor effluent is fed to an **evaporator + vacuum distillation column** to break the water-PG azeotrope (none, but PG boiling point is high).

| Section | Operating Condition | Engineering Basis |
|---|---|---|
| Reactor effluent | 200 °C, 1.8 MPa | Hot feed → flash to atmospheric pressure |
| Evaporator | 105 °C, 50 kPa (vacuum) | Reduces PG degradation (PG darkens > 220 °C) |
| Distillation column | 18–22 trays, reflux ratio 0.8–1.2 | PG tops at 105 °C (50 kPa), bottoms crude PG |
| Vacuum system | Steam ejector + barometric condenser | Avoid air contact → PG color stability |
| Final purifier | Activated carbon + ion exchange | Removes aldehydes, acids → USP grade |

**Vacuum Justification (Antoine equation):**

Using $A=8.64420$, $B=2696.3$, $C=298.0$ for PG (P in mmHg, T in °C):

$$\text{At } T = 187.4\ ^\circ\text{C},\ \log_{10} P_{\text{vap}} = 8.6442 - \frac{2696.3}{187.4 + 298.0}$$

$$\log_{10} P_{\text{vap}} = 8.6442 - \frac{2696.3}{485.4} = 8.6442 - 5.554 = 3.090$$

$$P_{\text{vap}} \approx 1230 \text{ mmHg} \approx 1.62 \text{ atm} \approx 164 \text{ kPa}$$

This confirms PG boils at ~ 187 °C at 1 atm, justifying vacuum operation to lower reboiler temperature below 150 °C for thermal protection.

---

### 3.3 Energy Comparison — PG vs EG (Ethylene Glycol) Manufacture

A typical KTU examiner question is to compute and compare energy consumption:

| Parameter | Ethylene Glycol (EG) | Propylene Glycol (PG) |
|---|---|---|
| Synthesis gas to olefin (steam cracker) | Ethylene (larger volume) | Propylene (smaller) |
| Olefin → oxide | Direct oxidation (more exothermic, O₂) | PO via chlorohydrin or PO/tert-butanol |
| Oxide → glycol | Hydration (exothermic ~ 90 kJ/mol) | Hydration (exothermic ~ 88 kJ/mol) |
| Total energy (GJ/tonne PG or EG) | **~ 35–40** | **~ 42–48** |
| Toxicity | High (lethal dose LD₅₀ = 4700 mg/kg) | Low (LD₅₀ = 20000 mg/kg) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** PG production is more energy-intensive per mole than EG due to lower PO selectivity and heavier vacuum distillation, but its **lower toxicity and food-grade safety** justify the higher cost in pharma/food applications.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Process flow diagrams (PFDs) for propylene glycol manufacture are a high-frequency Part B (14-mark) question. Master both block-level and detailed PFDs.

### 4.1 Overall Block Diagram — Propylene Glycol Manufacture (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP["PROPYLENE FEED"] --> nodeR1["PROPYLENE OXIDE PLANT"]
    nodeW["WATER FEED 20:1 MOLAR"] --> nodeR2["PO HYDRATION REACTOR"]
    nodeR1 --> nodeR2
    nodeR2 --> nodeE["EVAPORATOR VACUUM"]
    nodeE --> nodeD["DISTILLATION COLUMN VACUUM"]
    nodeD --> nodeT["TOP LIGHT ENDS WATER"]
    nodeD --> nodeB["BOTTOMS CRUDE PG"]
    nodeB --> nodeP1["PURIFICATION AC + IE"]
    nodeP1 --> nodeP2["FINAL PRODUCT USP GRADE PG"]
    nodeP2 --> nodeS["STORAGE TANKS FOOD AND PHARMA GRADE"]

    classDef proc fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef feed fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef prod fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class nodeR1,nodeR2,nodeE,nodeD,nodeP1 proc
    class nodeP,nodeW feed
    class nodeP2,nodeS prod
```

**Explanation:**
- **Propylene Feed** enters the **PO Plant** (chlorohydrin or peroxidation route).
- **Water Feed** enters the hydration reactor at 20:1 molar ratio.
- The reactor product stream goes to a **vacuum evaporator** to flash off excess water.
- The concentrated liquor enters the **vacuum distillation column** to separate PG from heavier glycols (DPG/TPG).
- The bottoms undergo **purification** (activated carbon + ion exchange) to produce USP-grade PG, stored for distribution.

---

### 4.2 Detailed PFD — Catalytic PO Hydration Route

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph FEED["FEED SECTION"]
        nodeA["PROPYLENE FEED C3H6"]
        nodeB["CHLORINE OR PEROXIDE"]
        nodeC["PROCESS WATER 20:1 MOLAR"]
    end

    subgraph POUNIT["PROPYLENE OXIDE UNIT"]
        nodeD["CHLOROHYDRIN REACTOR"]
        nodeE["SAB UNIT EPOXIDATION"]
    end

    subgraph HYDRATION["HYDRATION SECTION"]
        nodeF["PO STORAGE TANK"]
        nodeG["CSTR HYDRATION REACTOR SOLID ACID CATALYST"]
        nodeH["HEAT EXCHANGER COOLING DUTY 2200 KW"]
        nodeI["FLASH EVAPORATOR VACUUM 50 KPA"]
    end

    subgraph SEPARATION["SEPARATION SECTION"]
        nodeJ["DISTILLATION COLUMN 22 TRAYS"]
        nodeK["OVERHEAD WATER RECYCLE"]
        nodeL["BOTTOMS CRUDE PG"]
        nodeM["SIDE DRAW DPG BYPRODUCT"]
    end

    subgraph PURIFY["PURIFICATION SECTION"]
        nodeN["ACTIVATED CARBON COLUMN"]
        nodeO["ION EXCHANGE RESIN"]
        nodeP["FINAL FILTER 0.2 MICRON"]
    end

    subgraph STORAGE["STORAGE AND DESPATCH"]
        nodeQ["USP GRADE PG STORAGE SS316"]
        nodeR["INDUSTRIAL GRADE PG STORAGE"]
    end

    nodeA --> nodeD --> nodeE --> nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeC --> nodeG
    nodeG <--> nodeH
    nodeG --> nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeK -. recycle .-> nodeC
    nodeJ --> nodeL
    nodeJ --> nodeM
    nodeL --> nodeN --> nodeO --> nodeP
    nodeP --> nodeQ
    nodeP --> nodeR
```

**Reading Guide:**
- Feed section: propylene + chlorine → chlorohydrin → PO; alternatively, peroxidation (PO/TBA co-product process).
- Hydration: PO + water over solid-acid catalyst at 130–160 °C; cooling removes the –88 kJ/mol exotherm.
- Separation: vacuum distillation breaks PG from heavy glycols; water is recycled back.
- Purification: carbon removes aldehydes/colour bodies; ion exchange removes acidic impurities; final filter ensures USP clarity.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PECHT741 Module 3]**

> Define propylene glycol. Write its molecular formula, molar mass, and mention two important industrial applications.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition:** Propylene glycol (PG) is a viscous, colorless, hygroscopic diol with the structure CH₃–CH(OH)–CH₂–OH. It is also called **1,2-propanediol**.

- **Molecular formula:** C₃H₈O₂
- **Molar mass:** 76.09 g/mol
- **Industrial applications (any two):**
  1. **Antifreeze / heat transfer fluid** in food-grade chillers (non-toxic).
  2. **Polyester resin manufacture** (unsaturated polyesters for marine/fiberglass).
  3. **Pharmaceutical excipient** (solvent in injectables).
  4. **Cosmetic humectant** (moisturizer in skin lotions).
  5. **Food additive E1520** (carrier for flavors and colors).

*(1 mark for correct definition + formula; 1 mark for molar mass; 1 mark for two distinct applications.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PECHT741 Module 3]**

> Why is propylene glycol preferred over ethylene glycol in food and pharmaceutical applications, even though it is costlier to manufacture?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Propylene glycol is preferred because:
1. **Lower toxicity** — LD₅₀ (oral, rat) for PG ≈ 20,000 mg/kg versus EG ≈ 4,700 mg/kg. PG is **GRAS (Generally Recognized As Safe)** by the US-FDA for food use, while EG is toxic and causes kidney damage.
2. **Biodegradable and environmentally safer** — PG breaks down rapidly in soil and water; EG produces harmful oxalic acid metabolites.
3. **Higher viscosity** gives better film-forming in cosmetics and lower volatility in fog machines.

Hence, despite higher manufacturing cost (energy-intensive vacuum distillation), PG's safety profile justifies use in **USP food/pharma grades**.

*(1 mark per point: toxicity, biodegradability, plus concluding statement = 3 marks.)*

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert (Module 3):** Many students lose marks because they (a) confuse ethylene glycol and propylene glycol properties; (b) forget to specify temperature/pressure conditions; (c) fail to show by-product formation (DPG, acetol, propanal) in mass balances; and (d) omit purification steps. Always state operating conditions and grade (USP vs industrial) when writing manufacturing flowsheets. Also, do not omit the heat duty calculation in reactor design problems.

---

#### Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]

**Q. (a)** With the help of a neat process flow diagram, describe the **catalytic hydration of propylene oxide** for the manufacture of propylene glycol. Discuss the role of catalyst, operating conditions, and by-product formation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PO hydration reactor is fed with 150 kmol/h PO and 3000 kmol/h water. The PO conversion is 88 %, and the selectivities to PG and DPG are 90 % and 7 % respectively (remainder to acetol/propanal). Compute:
  (i) Molar flow rates of all products and unreacted streams.
  (ii) Mass of PG produced per hour.
  (iii) Reactor cooling duty in kW (ΔH = –88 kJ/mol). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Catalytic Hydration of PO (7 marks):**

**Reaction:**
$$\text{CH}_3-\underset{\text{PO}}{\text{CH}-\text{CH}_2} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{H}^+ \text{catalyst}} \text{CH}_3-\text{CH(OH)}-\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$$

**Process Flow Diagram:**
*(Draw the Mermaid Block Diagram from Section 4.2 — Hydration Section + Separation Section + Purification.)*

**Catalyst role:** Solid-acid ion-exchange resins (e.g., Amberlyst-15, sulfonated styrene-divinylbenzene) provide protonation of the epoxide oxygen, polarising the C–O bond and enabling nucleophilic attack by water at the more substituted (Markovnikov) carbon, leading to **propane-1,2-diol**.

**Operating conditions:**
- Temperature: 120–160 °C
- Pressure: 1.5–3 MPa (liquid phase)
- Molar ratio water:PO = 15–25:1 (excess water suppresses DPG formation)
- Catalyst lifetime: 6–12 months before regeneration

**By-products:**
- Dipropylene glycol (DPG): 5–8 %
- Tripropylene glycol (TPG): 1–2 %
- Acetol (hydroxyacetone): 0.5–1 %
- Propanal: 0.2–0.5 %

**Purification:** Vacuum distillation (50 kPa, 105 °C top, 145 °C bottoms) → activated carbon → ion exchange → USP grade PG (≥ 99.5 % purity).

*(1 mark reaction; 2 marks PFD; 2 marks catalyst+conditions; 2 marks by-products and purification.)*

---

**(b) Material Balance (7 marks):**

**Given:** F_PO,in = 150 kmol/h; F_W,in = 3000 kmol/h; X_PO = 0.88; S_PG = 0.90; S_DPG = 0.07.

**Step 1 — PO reacted and PO out:**

$$F_{\text{PO,reacted}} = 150 \times 0.88 = 132 \text{ kmol/h}$$

$$F_{\text{PO,out}} = 150 - 132 = 18 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 2 — PG formed:**

$$F_{\text{PG}} = 132 \times 0.90 = 118.8 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 3 — DPG formed:**

$$F_{\text{DPG}} = 132 \times 0.07 = 9.24 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 4 — Acetol/propanal formed (combined):**

$$F_{\text{acetol+others}} = 132 \times 0.03 = 3.96 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 5 — Water consumed (PG: 1 mol H₂O; DPG: 1 mol H₂O per 2 mol PO):**

$$\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{H}_2\text{O consumed}} &= n_{\text{PG}} \times 1 + n_{\text{DPG}} \times 1 \\
&= 118.8 + 9.24 = 128.04 \text{ kmol/h}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Water out:**

$$F_{\text{H}_2\text{O,out}} = 3000 - 128.04 = 2871.96 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 7 — Mass of PG produced per hour:**

$$\dot{m}_{\text{PG}} = 118.8 \times 76.09 = 9039.5 \text{ kg/h} \approx 9.04 \text{ ton/h}$$

**Step 8 — Cooling duty:**

$$Q = 132 \times 88 = 11{,}616 \text{ kJ/s} = 11{,}616 \text{ kW} \approx 11.6 \text{ MW}$$

*(Step 1+2: 2 marks; Step 3–5: 2 marks; Step 6–7: 2 marks; Step 8 with proper units: 1 mark.)*

---

#### Question B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]

**Q. (a)** Compare the **PO hydration route** and the **glycerol hydrogenolysis route** for propylene glycol manufacture. Discuss the merits and limitations of each, citing catalysts and conditions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the major industrial applications of propylene glycol, with emphasis on its role in:
  (i) Polyester resin manufacture,
  (ii) Pharmaceutical formulations, and
  (iii) Heat transfer fluids. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison (7 marks):**

| Parameter | PO Hydration Route | Glycerol Hydrogenolysis Route |
|---|---|---|
| **Feedstock** | Propylene oxide (petrochemical) | Crude glycerol (biodiesel byproduct) |
| **Reaction** | PO + H₂O → PG | Glycerol + H₂ → PG + H₂O |
| **Catalyst** | Solid acid (Amberlyst, H-mordenite) | Cu/Cr or Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃ |
| **Conditions** | 120–160 °C, 1.5–3 MPa | 180–220 °C, 3–6 MPa H₂ |
| **Selectivity** | 92–95 % to PG | 85–92 % to PG; byproducts: EG, 1,2-PD |
| **Merits** | Established, large capacity, high purity | Renewable feedstock, valorizes biodiesel waste |
| **Limitations** | Petro-based, PO toxic, capital intensive | Lower selectivity, requires high H₂ pressure, catalyst deactivation |
| **Capacity** | > 800,000 ton/yr global | ~ 50,000 ton/yr emerging |
| **Carbon footprint** | Higher (fossil-derived) | Lower (renewable, CO₂ neutral) |

**Conclusion:** PO hydration is the dominant industrial route today, but bio-based glycerol hydrogenolysis is rapidly gaining share in regions with biodiesel surplus (EU, Brazil, India).

*(2 marks on each route reaction+conditions; 2 marks comparative table; 1 mark concluding remark.)*

---

**(b) Industrial Applications (7 marks):**

**(i) Polyester Resin Manufacture:**
Unsaturated polyesters are produced by reacting **propylene glycol + maleic anhydride + phthalic anhydride** at 200–220 °C with an esterification catalyst (e.g., Fascat 4100). PG imparts:
- Lower viscosity than EG resins (better fibre wetting).
- Greater flexibility and impact resistance.
- Lower crystallization tendency → better transparency.

These resins are used in marine boats, automotive panels, bathroom fixtures, and pipelines.

*(2 marks for chemistry + advantages.)*

**(ii) Pharmaceutical Formulations:**
USP-grade PG is used as:
- **Injectable solvent** for water-insoluble drugs (diazepam, phenobarbital, nitroglycerin).
- **Oral vehicle** for syrups and pediatric formulations.
- **Topical carrier** in ointments, transdermal patches (improves skin penetration due to low molecular weight and amphiphilicity).
- **E-liquid base** in e-cigarettes (vaporization temperature ~ 188 °C is comfortable for inhalation).

It is preferred over ethanol (less irritating) and over vegetable glycerine (lower viscosity, cleaner vapor).

*(2 marks for major uses.)*

**(iii) Heat Transfer Fluids:**
PG–water mixtures (30–60 % PG by mass) are used in:
- **Brewery and dairy chilling loops** (food-grade).
- **Cold-storage warehouses** and ice rinks (operating down to –30 °C).
- **HVAC secondary loops** where toxicity is a concern (hospitals, schools).
- **Solar thermal collectors** (PG has a high boiling point → wider operating range).

**Advantage over EG:** Non-toxic, food-safe, lower corrosion (with inhibitors), and biodegradable.

*(2 marks for fluids; 1 mark for concluding comparison.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Top 3 Reasons Students Lose Marks in Module 3 PG Questions:**
> 1. **Missing operating conditions** (T, P, molar ratio) in process descriptions — examiners explicitly look for these in the 7-mark "manufacturing process" questions.
> 2. **Incorrect stoichiometry** in mass balances — DPG requires **2 PO + 1 H₂O**, not 1+1 like PG. This single mistake can cost 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Confusing ethylene glycol and propylene glycol** in toxicity, freezing point, or vapour pressure questions. Memorise the two columns side-by-side: EG = toxic, BP 197 °C, viscosity 21 cP; PG = non-toxic, BP 187 °C, viscosity 60 cP.

---

> **Save this list — these are the must-remember KTU exam facts for Propylene Glycol.**

- ✅ **PG = 1,2-Propanediol**, C₃H₈O₂, MW = **76.09 g/mol**, CAS 57-55-6.
- ✅ **Major manufacturing route:** Propylene oxide hydration (catalytic, 120–160 °C, 1.5–3 MPa, water:PO 20:1).
- ✅ **Reaction:** PO + H₂O → CH₃CH(OH)CH₂OH; ΔH = **–88 kJ/mol** (exothermic).
- ✅ **By-products:** DPG, TPG, acetol, propanal.
- ✅ **Bio-route:** Glycerol + H₂ → PG + H₂O (Cu/Cr or Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃, 200 °C, 3 MPa).
- ✅ **Key physical properties:** BP 187.4 °C; FP –59 °C; density 1.036 g/cm³; viscosity 60.5 cP.
- ✅ **Vacuum distillation** required because PG darkens above ~ 220 °C.
- ✅ **Antoine constants** (P in mmHg, T in °C): A = 8.6442, B = 2696.3, C = 298.0.
- ✅ **USP grade purity:** ≥ 99.5 %; food-grade (E1520) regulated by FDA as GRAS.
- ✅ **LD₅₀ (oral, rat):** PG ≈ 20,000 mg/kg (low toxicity); EG ≈ 4,700 mg/kg (toxic).
- ✅ **Top applications:** (1) Polyester resins; (2) Pharma excipient; (3) Cosmetics humectant; (4) Food-grade antifreeze; (5) Heat transfer fluid; (6) E-cigarette base.
- ✅ **Material balance formula (numerical):** $F_{\text{PO,out}} = F_{\text{PO,in}}(1-X)$; $F_{\text{PG}} = F_{\text{PO,in}} \cdot X \cdot S_{\text{PG}}$; $F_{\text{H}_2\text{O, consumed}} = F_{\text{PG}} + F_{\text{DPG}}$.
- ✅ **Heat duty:** $Q = \dot{n}_{\text{PO, reacted}} \times \lvert \Delta H \rvert$ in kW.
- ✅ **Industrial vs USP grade:** Industrial (polyester, antifreeze) tolerates DPG and colour; USP (pharma, food) needs polishing by activated carbon + ion exchange.
- ✅ **Freezing-point depression** in antifreeze: $\Delta T_f = i \cdot K_f \cdot m$ (for PG, $i = 1$ in dilute aqueous solution).

> **End of Notes — Propylene Glycol (PECHT741, Module 3, KTU 2024 Scheme).**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Isopropyl Alcohol
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT741 - PETROCHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Isopropyl Alcohol

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Isopropyl Alcohol (IPA)**, also known as **2-Propanol** (IUPAC: **Propan-2-ol**), is the simplest **secondary alcohol** and the first homologue of the monohydric aliphatic alcohol series containing a branched-chain isomeric structure.

Its molecular formula is **C₃H₈O** and its structural formula is **CH₃–CH(OH)–CH₃**, in which the hydroxyl (–OH) functional group is attached to the **central (secondary) carbon atom** of the propane skeleton.

**Key Identifiers (KTU Board Standard):**

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Molecular Formula | C₃H₈O |
| Molar Mass | 60.10 g/mol |
| CAS Number | 67-63-0 |
| Synonyms | Isopropanol, 2-Propanol, sec-Propyl alcohol, Dimethyl carbinol |
| Functional Group | Secondary –OH (hydroxyl) |
| State at STP | Colorless, volatile liquid |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Middle Child" Alcohol

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Think of the three-carbon alcohol family as three siblings: **n-Propanol** (straight-chain "elder"), **Isopropanol** (the "middle child" with the –OH tucked on the central carbon), and the small molecule **ethanol** as the "younger cousin." Because the –OH is shielded by two methyl (–CH₃) groups on either side, IPA behaves differently from its straight-chain sibling — it cannot form the same strong hydrogen-bonded network as primary alcohols, making it **slightly more volatile** and **less acidic**.

A useful real-world analogy: Isopropyl alcohol is the **"rubbing alcohol"** sitting in your medicine cabinet. It is the active ingredient in hand sanitizers, disinfectants, and the cleaning solvent that evaporates almost instantly from your skin, leaving a cooling sensation because of its high **heat of vaporization (≈ 44 kJ/mol)**.

## 1.3 Position in the Petrochemical Value Chain

> [!TIP]
> IPA is a **C₃-oxygenate** petrochemical derived directly from the **C₃ fraction (propylene)** obtained during the steam cracking of naphtha or natural gas liquids. It sits at the intersection of the **olefins-to-oxygenates** conversion segment of the petrochemical industry.

```
Crude Oil / NGL → Steam Cracker → C3 Fraction (Propylene) → IPA → Acetone / Solvents
```

## 1.4 Physical & Thermodynamic Properties (Board-Standard Values)

> [!NOTE]
> The following constants are **verbatim board values** that examiners expect to see written correctly in answer scripts.

- **Boiling Point:** 82.6 °C (lower than n-propanol at 97.4 °C, due to reduced hydrogen bonding)
- **Melting Point:** −89.5 °C
- **Density at 20 °C:** 0.786 g/cm³
- **Refractive Index (nD²⁰):** 1.3776
- **Flash Point (closed cup):** 11.7 °C
- **Autoignition Temperature:** 399 °C
- **Vapor Pressure at 20 °C:** 4.4 kPa
- **Heat of Vaporization:** 44.0 kJ/mol
- **Viscosity at 20 °C:** 2.4 mPa·s
- **Miscibility:** Fully miscible with water, ethanol, and most organic solvents (ether, chloroform)

## 1.5 Why IPA Matters Industrially (Syllabus Highlight)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Learning Outcome (CO3):** The student must be able to **describe the manufacture, properties, and end-use applications of C₃-oxygenates** — IPA is the **anchor molecule** of this outcome because it serves as the **gateway to acetone** (via dehydrogenation) and to **isopropyl acetate, isopropyl ether, and hydrogen peroxide** (via the isopropyl peroxide process — historically the dominant route to H₂O₂).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Industrial Routes — Comparative Master Framework

There are **three principal manufacturing routes** for IPA. KTU examiners routinely ask comparative questions between them.

### Route A — Indirect Hydration of Propylene (Sulfuric Acid Process)
This is the **classical, dominant KTU-syllabus route**. Propylene reacts with concentrated sulfuric acid to form **isopropyl hydrogen sulfate** (an ester), which is then hydrolyzed with water to yield IPA and regenerate sulfuric acid.

### Route B — Direct Hydration of Propylene
Propylene and water react in a single step over an acidic catalyst. Sub-divided into:
- **Vapor-phase direct hydration** (phosphoric acid / silica gel catalyst)
- **Liquid-phase direct hydration** (cation-exchange resin / strong acid catalyst, more modern)

### Route C — Catalytic Hydrogenation of Acetone
A secondary, niche route that has gained importance in regions where **acetone is in surplus** (e.g., as a by-product of phenol manufacture via the cumene process).

## 2.2 Reaction Chemistry (Step-by-Step Logic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Indirect Hydration (Two-Stage Esterification–Hydrolysis):**

**Stage 1 — Absorption / Esterification:**

$$\text{CH}_3\text{–CH=CH}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \longrightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{–CH(OSO}_3\text{H)–CH}_3$$

The acid is **protonated propylene** that gets attacked by the bisulfate ion (HSO₄⁻). This obeys **Markownikoff's rule** — the H adds to the carbon already bearing the most hydrogens, and the –OSO₃H adds to the more substituted (secondary) carbon, which is why we get the **secondary sulfate ester** rather than the n-propyl ester.

**Stage 2 — Hydrolysis:**

$$\text{CH}_3\text{–CH(OSO}_3\text{H)–CH}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{–CH(OH)–CH}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$$

The acid is **regenerated**, making this an **acid-catalyzed overall reaction** with H₂SO₄ acting catalytically in the recycle loop.

**Direct Hydration (Single-Stage, Acid-Catalyzed):**

$$\text{CH}_3\text{–CH=CH}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{H}^+ \text{ catalyst}} \text{CH}_3\text{–CH(OH)–CH}_3 \quad \Delta H \approx -50 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

This reaction is **exothermic, reversible, and equilibrium-limited**. Low temperatures favor higher equilibrium conversion, but kinetic rates are slow — hence the use of elevated pressure and a strong acid catalyst.

**Hydrogenation of Acetone:**

$$\text{CH}_3\text{–CO–CH}_3 + \text{H}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Ni or Cu catalyst, 150 °C}} \text{CH}_3\text{–CH(OH)–CH}_3$$

This is the **reverse** of IPA dehydrogenation to acetone and is a standard gas-phase fixed-bed reaction.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Constant Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize this table verbatim** — KTU valuation keys award full marks only when students quote the correct numerical values with units.

| Parameter | Indirect Hydration | Direct Hydration (Vapor) | Direct Hydration (Liquid) | Acetone Hydrogenation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Reaction Stages | 2 (Esterification + Hydrolysis) | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| Catalyst / Reagent | 70–85 % H₂SO₄ | H₃PO₄ on SiO₂ / SiC | Sulfonated polystyrene resin | Raney Ni or Cu |
| Temperature (°C) | 20–60 (absorber) / 70–90 (hydrolyzer) | 200–260 | 130–160 | 130–180 |
| Pressure (bar) | 10–35 | 25–65 | 80–100 | 1–3 |
| Conversion per Pass | ~90 % | 5–7 % (low — recycle) | 60–75 % | ~95 % |
| Selectivity to IPA | ~95 % | ~96 % | ~92 % | ~99 % |
| Overall Yield (C₃H₆ basis) | 90–95 % | 70–85 % | 88–93 % | >98 % |
| Acid Corrosion | Severe (requires lead-lined / Monel) | Mild | None (resin) | Mild |
| Product Purity | 91–95 % (requires distillation) | 99.5 %+ | 99.5 %+ | 99.9 % |
| Typical Plant Size | Large | Medium | Medium | Small–Medium |
| Capital Cost | High | Medium | Medium | Low |
| Operating Cost | High (acid make-up) | Medium | Low | Low |

## 2.4 KTU Calculation Formulas (Pure Math Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are the **only five** that KTU examiners will quote in a 14-mark Part B problem. Memorize them.

**1. Stoichiometric Mass Balance (per 100 kg propylene feed):**

$$\text{Theoretical IPA yield} = m_{\text{C}_3\text{H}_6} \times \frac{M_{\text{IPA}}}{M_{\text{C}_3\text{H}_6}}$$

where the molecular weights are **M_IPA = 60.10 g/mol** and **M_C₃H₆ = 42.08 g/mol**, giving a stoichiometric mass ratio of **60.10 / 42.08 = 1.428 kg IPA per kg propylene**.

**2. Actual IPA Yield (with conversion and selectivity):**

$$Y_{\text{actual}} = m_{\text{C}_3\text{H}_6} \times 1.428 \times X_{\text{propylene}} \times S_{\text{IPA}}$$

where $X$ is fractional conversion and $S$ is fractional selectivity.

**3. Equilibrium Constant for Direct Hydration:**

$$K_p = \frac{p_{\text{IPA}}}{p_{\text{C}_3\text{H}_6} \cdot p_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}}$$

Typical value at 250 °C: **K_p ≈ 1.6 × 10⁻² atm⁻¹**.

**4. Dilution of Sulfuric Acid (Material Balance on Acid Loop):**

$$\text{Acid strength (recycle)} = \frac{m_{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4}}{m_{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4} + m_{\text{H}_2\text{O, added}}} \times 100\%$$

**5. Steam Economy in Hydrolyzer (kg steam per kg IPA):**

$$\text{Steam economy} = \frac{m_{\text{steam}}}{m_{\text{IPA, produced}}}$$

Typical design value: **0.7 – 1.2 kg steam / kg IPA** (multi-effect evaporators reduce this to as low as 0.45).

## 2.5 Why These Equations Matter in the Field

> [!TIP]
> A process engineer designing a **10,000 tonnes/year IPA plant** must use the yield equation (Formula 2) to size the **propylene storage sphere** and the **distillation column reflux drum**. The acid dilution equation (Formula 4) is critical for **corrosion management** — if recycle H₂SO₄ drops below **70 wt%**, the absorber efficiency collapses; if it rises above **90 wt%**, Monel-lined piping must be specified. Direct hydration plants, by contrast, eliminate acid handling entirely and are favored in **modern grassroots installations** for environmental reasons.

## 2.6 Functional Comparison — Why Direct Hydration is Replacing Indirect

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trend (KTU 2024 Module 3 Highlight):** Pre-1980, ~80 % of global IPA was made by the indirect route. Post-2010, **direct hydration accounts for over 90 % of new capacity** because of (i) zero acid disposal, (ii) lower operating cost, and (iii) higher on-stream time. The remaining indirect plants still operate because they tolerate **lower-purity propylene streams** (e.g., 50–70 % cracker-grade), whereas direct hydration requires **polymer-grade propylene (≥ 99 %)**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Problems

## 3.1 Problem 1 — Stoichiometric Yield on Propylene Feed (Foundation Problem)

> **KTU 2024 Typical Part (a) 7-mark Question:** *In an indirect hydration plant, 1,500 kg/h of 92 % pure propylene is fed to the absorber. If the conversion of propylene is 90 % and selectivity to IPA is 95 %, calculate: (i) the theoretical yield of IPA, (ii) the actual yield of IPA, and (iii) the kg of 100 % H₂SO₄ consumed per hour (assuming 5 % acid loss).*

### Given Data
- Feed rate of propylene-rich stream: $m_{\text{feed}} = 1500$ kg/h
- Purity of feed: $\eta = 92\%$
- Conversion: $X = 0.90$
- Selectivity: $S = 0.95$
- Acid loss fraction: $f_{\text{acid loss}} = 0.05$
- $M_{\text{C}_3\text{H}_6} = 42.08$ g/mol, $M_{\text{IPA}} = 60.10$ g/mol, $M_{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4} = 98.08$ g/mol

### Step (i) — Theoretical Yield of IPA

**Step 1 — Mass of pure propylene in feed:**

$$m_{\text{C}_3\text{H}_6} = m_{\text{feed}} \times \eta = 1500 \times 0.92 = 1380 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 2 — Moles of propylene:**

$$n_{\text{C}_3\text{H}_6} = \frac{1380 \text{ kg/h} \times 1000 \text{ g/kg}}{42.08 \text{ g/mol}} = 32{,}794.7 \text{ mol/h}$$

**Step 3 — Stoichiometry:** 1 mole C₃H₆ → 1 mole IPA, so:

$$n_{\text{IPA, theoretical}} = 32{,}794.7 \text{ mol/h}$$

**Step 4 — Theoretical mass of IPA:**

$$m_{\text{IPA, theoretical}} = 32{,}794.7 \times 60.10 = 1{,}970{,}963.5 \text{ g/h} \approx 1970.96 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Alternative shortcut (mass-ratio form):**

$$m_{\text{IPA, theoretical}} = 1380 \times \frac{60.10}{42.08} = 1380 \times 1.4281 = 1970.78 \text{ kg/h}$$

> Both methods agree to within rounding error. The mass-ratio method is **preferred in board answers** because it is faster.

### Step (ii) — Actual Yield of IPA

$$m_{\text{IPA, actual}} = m_{\text{IPA, theoretical}} \times X \times S = 1970.78 \times 0.90 \times 0.95$$

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{IPA, actual}} &= 1970.78 \times 0.855 \\
&= 1685.02 \text{ kg/h}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step (iii) — Sulfuric Acid Consumption per Hour

From the reaction stoichiometry, **1 mole H₂SO₄ is consumed per mole IPA produced** (acid is regenerated, but 5 % is lost in the recycle loop and must be replenished).

**Step 1 — Moles of IPA actually produced:**

$$n_{\text{IPA}} = \frac{1685.02 \times 1000}{60.10} = 28{,}036.6 \text{ mol/h}$$

**Step 2 — Mass of 100 % H₂SO₄ to replace the 5 % loss:**

$$m_{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4} = 28{,}036.6 \times 98.08 \times 0.05$$

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4} &= 28{,}036.6 \times 4.904 \\
&= 137{,}491.5 \text{ g/h} \\
&\approx 137.5 \text{ kg/h of 100% H}_2\text{SO}_4
\end{aligned}$$

### Final Answer Summary

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Theoretical IPA yield | **1970.78 kg/h** |
| Actual IPA yield | **1685.02 kg/h** |
| 100 % H₂SO₄ consumed | **137.5 kg/h** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark allocation insight:** Part (i) carries 2 marks (stoichiometry), Part (ii) carries 3 marks (yield with conversion + selectivity), Part (iii) carries 2 marks (acid balance). The units (kg/h) must appear on every numerical answer or 0.5 marks are deducted per missing unit.

---

## 3.2 Problem 2 — Multi-Component Mass Balance with Recycle

> **KTU 2024 Typical Part (b) 7-mark Question:** *A direct hydration plant receives a feed stream containing 95 % propylene and 5 % propane (by mass) at 3,000 kg/h. The reactor operates at 220 °C, 50 bar, with a per-pass conversion of 6 % and selectivity to IPA of 96 %. The unreacted propylene is separated and recycled. Calculate: (i) the kg/h of fresh propylene feed, (ii) the kg/h of recycle stream, and (iii) the kg/h of IPA in the crude product.*

### Step (i) — Fresh Propylene Feed

$$m_{\text{C}_3\text{H}_6, \text{ fresh}} = 3000 \times 0.95 = 2850 \text{ kg/h}$$

### Step (ii) — Recycle Stream

In a recycle system, **fresh feed + recycle = reactor feed**. Let $R$ be the recycle rate and $F$ the fresh feed. At steady state, the **unreacted propylene from the reactor** is the recycle stream. The reacted fraction of the fresh feed (after accounting for 6 % per-pass conversion and the buildup of inert propane) determines the purge.

**Step 1 — Define single-pass conversion of propylene:**

$$X_{sp} = 0.06$$

**Step 2 — Overall conversion required:** Since the inert propane must eventually leave the system, the overall propylene conversion must be **100 %** of the fresh feed (otherwise propane accumulates indefinitely). Let $P$ be the purge rate.

**Step 3 — Component balance on the recycle loop:**

The propane entering with fresh feed (5 % × 3000 = 150 kg/h) must leave entirely in the purge. So the purge carries 150 kg/h of propane. The purge also carries unreacted propylene; let that be $y$ kg/h.

**Step 4 — Overall propylene balance:**

$$2850 = 2850 \times X_{\text{overall}} + y$$

Since overall conversion = 1 (no propylene in purge would violate inert balance), we have $y = 0$ **only if** we account for inert buildup. In a properly designed system, the purge stream has the same composition as the reactor effluent. Solving by standard recycle algebra:

$$\text{Purge ratio} = \frac{\text{Inert in feed}}{\text{Inert in purge}} = \frac{0.05}{0.05} = 1.0$$

Thus purge = 3000 kg/h × (5/95) = 157.9 kg/h.

**Reactor feed** = fresh feed + recycle = 3000 + (reactor feed − reacted) = **constant loop calculation yields:**

For a single-pass conversion of 6 %, the recycle-to-fresh-feed ratio is:

$$R/F = \frac{1 - X_{sp}}{X_{sp}} \times \frac{\text{reacted C}_3\text{H}_6}{...}$$

A simpler, KTU-board-acceptable shortcut:

$$\text{Recycle} = F \times \frac{(1 - X_{sp})}{X_{sp}} \times \frac{\text{reactable fraction}}{1} = 3000 \times \frac{0.94}{0.06}$$

$$\text{Recycle} = 47{,}000 \text{ kg/h}$$

> This large recycle is the **defining disadvantage of vapor-phase direct hydration** — the per-pass conversion is so low that recycle pumping cost is significant.

### Step (iii) — IPA in Crude Product

$$m_{\text{IPA}} = 2850 \times 1.4281 \times 0.06 \times 0.96 = 234.16 \text{ kg/h}$$

(The 0.06 is the single-pass conversion that actually forms product; the rest is recycled.)

### Final Answer Summary

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Fresh propylene feed | **2850 kg/h** |
| Recycle stream | **~47,000 kg/h** |
| IPA in crude product | **234.16 kg/h** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse "per-pass conversion" with "overall conversion" in recycle problems. In direct hydration, **per-pass conversion (≈ 6 %) is much smaller than overall conversion (≈ 100 %)** because the unreacted material is recycled. Always read the question carefully and identify which conversion is given.

---

## 3.3 Problem 3 — Equilibrium Conversion Calculation for Direct Hydration

> **KTU 2024 Advanced Practice:** *For the vapor-phase direct hydration of propylene, the equilibrium constant at 200 °C is K_p = 2.8 × 10⁻² atm⁻¹ and at 260 °C is K_p = 1.05 × 10⁻² atm⁻¹. If equimolar propylene and steam are fed at 40 bar total pressure, calculate the equilibrium conversion of propylene at both temperatures.*

For an equimolar feed, let the initial partial pressures of C₃H₆ and H₂O both equal $p_0 = P/2 = 20$ atm (ideal-gas assumption for the high-T vapor phase).

At equilibrium, if the fractional conversion is $\alpha$:

$$p_{\text{C}_3\text{H}_6} = p_0(1-\alpha), \quad p_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} = p_0(1-\alpha), \quad p_{\text{IPA}} = p_0 \alpha$$

Substituting into the equilibrium expression:

$$K_p = \frac{p_0 \alpha}{p_0^2 (1-\alpha)^2} = \frac{\alpha}{p_0 (1-\alpha)^2}$$

Solving for $\alpha$ at 200 °C with $K_p = 2.8 \times 10^{-2}$ atm⁻¹ and $p_0 = 20$ atm:

$$2.8 \times 10^{-2} = \frac{\alpha}{20(1-\alpha)^2}$$

$$\alpha = 0.56 (1-\alpha)^2$$

Iterating with a Newton-Raphson step: $\alpha \approx 0.252$ or **25.2 % conversion at 200 °C**.

At 260 °C with $K_p = 1.05 \times 10^{-2}$:

$$1.05 \times 10^{-2} = \frac{\alpha}{20(1-\alpha)^2}$$

$$\alpha \approx 0.218 \text{ or } 21.8\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Lowering temperature from 260 °C to 200 °C raises equilibrium conversion from 21.8 % to 25.2 %, but slows the rate dramatically — this is the **classic thermodynamic-vs-kinetic trade-off** that justifies operating at an intermediate temperature with a recycle loop.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Flow Diagram — Indirect Hydration of Propylene (Sulfuric Acid Process)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["FRESH C3H6 FEED 92 PCT PURITY"] --> nodeB["ABSORBER 70 PCT H2SO4 20 TO 60 C 10 TO 35 BAR"]
    nodeC["RECYCLE H2SO4 80 PCT FROM CONCENTRATOR"] --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeD["ISOPROPYL HYDROGEN SULFATE ESTER MIXTURE"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["HYDROLYZER STEAM STRIPPER 70 TO 90 C 1 BAR"]
    nodeF["WATER MAKE UP"] --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeG["CRUDE IPA AQ PHASE 30 TO 50 PCT IPA"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["DILUTE H2SO4 45 PCT TO RECYCLE"]
    nodeH --> nodeC
    nodeG --> nodeI["CONCENTRATOR VAC EVAPORATION"]
    nodeI --> nodeC
    nodeG --> nodeJ["DISTILLATION COLUMN REFINING"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["PURE IPA 99.9 PCT OVERHEAD"]
    nodeJ --> nodeL["WATER BOTTOM RECYCLE TO ABSORBER"]
    nodeM["MAKE UP FRESH H2SO4 100 PCT"] --> nodeC
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The fresh propylene and the recycled H₂SO₄ enter the **Absorber (top-left)**. The ester product goes to the **Hydrolyzer** where steam liberates crude IPA. The crude IPA splits into a **distillation train** yielding pure IPA overhead and a recycle-water stream, while the dilute H₂SO₄ bottoms are sent to a **Concentrator** and back to the absorber. A small amount of fresh H₂SO₄ replaces the 5 % lost in the loop.

## 4.2 Block Flow Diagram — Direct Hydration (Vapor Phase, Modern Route)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["POLYMER GRADE C3H6 99.5 PCT FEED"] --> nodeD["MIXER WITH RECYCLED C3H6"]
    nodeB["DEIONIZED WATER FEED"] --> nodeD
    nodeE["RECYCLE C3H6 UNREACTED"] --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeF["REACTOR FIXED BED H3PO4 ON SiO2 220 TO 260 C 40 TO 60 BAR"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["REACTOR EFFLUENT COOLER HEAT RECOVERY"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["SEPARATOR LIQUID VAPOR 3 PHASE"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["CRUDE IPA 12 TO 20 PCT AQ"]
    nodeH --> nodeJ["VAPOR PHASE UNREACTED C3H6 AND WATER"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["COMPRESSOR RECYCLE COMPRESSOR"]
    nodeK --> nodeD
    nodeJ --> nodeL["PURGE STREAM INERT REMOVAL"]
    nodeI --> nodeM["DISTILLATION TRAIN EXTRACTIVE"]
    nodeM --> nodeN["PURE IPA 99.9 PCT PRODUCT"]
    nodeM --> nodeO["WATER RECYCLE TO REACTOR"]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Fresh propylene and water are mixed with a **large recycle stream** and fed to a fixed-bed catalytic reactor. The reactor effluent is cooled and phase-separated; the liquid phase is purified in a distillation column, and the vapor phase is compressed and recycled. A small purge removes inerts.

## 4.3 Process Comparison Matrix (Block-Level View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["START C3H6 SOURCE"] --> nodeQ{"ROUTE SELECTION"}

    nodeQ --> nodeB1["INDIRECT H2SO4 PATH"]
    nodeQ --> nodeB2["DIRECT CATALYTIC PATH"]
    nodeQ --> nodeB3["ACETONE HYDROGENATION"]

    nodeB1 --> nodeC1["ACID PROOF EQUIPMENT HIGHER CAPEX"]
    nodeB1 --> nodeC2["TOLERATES DILUTE C3 FEED 50 TO 70 PCT"]
    nodeB1 --> nodeC3["HIGHER MAINTENANCE ACID CORROSION"]

    nodeB2 --> nodeC4["POLYMER GRADE C3 REQUIRED 99 PLUS"]
    nodeB2 --> nodeC5["NO ACID WASTE CLEANER"]
    nodeB2 --> nodeC6["LOWER OPEX HIGHER UPTIME"]

    nodeB3 --> nodeC7["DEPENDENT ON PHENOL PLANT"]
    nodeB3 --> nodeC8["HIGHEST PURITY 99.9 PLUS"]
    nodeB3 --> nodeC9["SMALL SCALE NICHE"]
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture Flow — IPA End-Use Value Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["IPA PRODUCTION PLANT"] --> nodeB1["SOLVENT USE 40 PCT GLOBAL DEMAND"]
    nodeA --> nodeB2["ACETONE VIA DEHYDROGENATION 25 PCT"]
    nodeA --> nodeB3["ISOPROPYL ACETATE VIA ESTERIFICATION 10 PCT"]
    nodeA --> nodeB4["PHARMACEUTICAL AND COSMETIC 15 PCT"]
    nodeA --> nodeB5["OTHER ISOPROPYL ETHERS AND AMINES 10 PCT"]

    nodeB1 --> nodeC1["CLEANING AGENTS THINNERS"]
    nodeB1 --> nodeC2["PHARMA EXTRACTION SOLVENT"]

    nodeB2 --> nodeD1["ACETONE TO METHYL METHACRYLATE"]
    nodeB2 --> nodeD2["ACETONE TO BISPHENOL A"]

    nodeB3 --> nodeE1["COATINGS AND INKS VEHICLE"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation Guide for Exams:** When asked to draw a flow diagram, KTU examiners award **1 mark for correctness of unit sequence**, **1 mark for labeling streams (feed, product, recycle)**, and **1 mark for indicating operating conditions (T, P) on the reactor block**. Include the recycle loop explicitly — it is the most-often-missed element.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Isopropyl Alcohol. Write its molecular formula, IUPAC name, and two important physical properties. [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

Isopropyl Alcohol (IPA) is the **simplest secondary monohydric aliphatic alcohol**, in which the hydroxyl (–OH) group is attached to the **central carbon atom** of a three-carbon chain.

- **IUPAC Name:** Propan-2-ol
- **Molecular Formula:** C₃H₈O
- **Molar Mass:** 60.10 g/mol
- **Two physical properties:**
  - **Boiling point:** 82.6 °C (lower than n-propanol due to branching and reduced H-bonding)
  - **Density at 20 °C:** 0.786 g/cm³
  - It is **completely miscible** with water, ethanol, and most organic solvents.
  - It has a characteristic **alcoholic odor** and a **flash point of 11.7 °C** (highly flammable).

> **Mark split:** 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula + IUPAC, 1 mark for physical properties.

---

### Q2. List any three industrial applications of Isopropyl Alcohol. [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

1. **Solvent:** IPA is used as a cleaning and thinning solvent in the **paints, coatings, and printing-ink** industry because of its ability to dissolve both polar and non-polar substances and its fast evaporation rate.
2. **Chemical Intermediate:** IPA is dehydrogenated to **acetone**, which in turn is the feedstock for **methyl methacrylate (MMA) and bisphenol-A (BPA)**.
3. **Pharmaceutical and Cosmetic:** IPA is the active ingredient in **rubbing alcohol, hand sanitizers (70 % v/v aqueous solution), and pharmaceutical extraction** because of its broad-spectrum antimicrobial activity and rapid skin evaporation.
4. **De-icing and Antifreeze:** Used in **aircraft wing de-icers** and as an antifreeze additive in gasoline (used in place of methanol/ethanol in some markets).

> **Mark split:** 1 mark per correct application with brief justification.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Format)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks split into (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

### ⭐ QUESTION A — 14 Marks

#### Part (a) [7 Marks] — Indirect Hydration Process

> **Q.A(a):** Describe in detail the **manufacture of Isopropyl Alcohol by the indirect hydration of propylene (sulfuric acid process)** with a neat flow diagram. Discuss the reaction conditions, material of construction, and the function of each unit. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

The indirect hydration route, also called the **sulfuric acid process**, is the **classical two-stage process** for IPA manufacture. It tolerates dilute propylene streams (50–92 % purity) and gives high overall yields (90–95 %).

**Stage 1 — Absorption / Esterification (Reactor: Absorber):**

Fresh propylene (92 % pure) is fed into the **bottom of an absorption tower** where it bubbles up against a counter-current stream of **70–85 % sulfuric acid** at **20–60 °C and 10–35 bar**. Propylene dissolves in the acid and reacts to form **isopropyl hydrogen sulfate (the secondary ester)** following Markownikoff addition.

**Reaction:**
$$\text{CH}_3\text{–CH=CH}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{–CH(OSO}_3\text{H)–CH}_3$$

**Stage 2 — Hydrolysis (Reactor: Steam Hydrolyzer):**

The ester-acid mixture is mixed with water in a **hydrolyzer-steam stripper** at **70–90 °C and near-atmospheric pressure**. Live steam strips the IPA vapour overhead, which is then condensed to give **30–50 % aqueous IPA** (crude).

**Reaction:**
$$\text{CH}_3\text{–CH(OSO}_3\text{H)–CH}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{–CH(OH)–CH}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$$

**Stage 3 — Acid Recovery:**

The bottoms from the hydrolyzer contain **dilute H₂SO₄ (~45 %)**. This is sent to a **vacuum evaporator/concentrator** to bring it back to ~80 % strength for recycle to the absorber. About **5 % of the acid is lost** per cycle and must be replenished with fresh 100 % H₂SO₄.

**Stage 4 — Purification:**

The crude aqueous IPA is fed to a **two- or three-column distillation train** (rectifying column + finishing column). The overhead is **99.9 % pure IPA**; the bottoms are water recycled to the hydrolyzer.

**Materials of Construction:** Absorber and acid piping are **lead-lined steel or Monel (Ni–Cu alloy)** to resist hot sulfuric acid corrosion. Hydrolyzer and distillation columns are **stainless steel (SS-304 / SS-316)**.

> **Mark split:** 2 marks for reactions + conditions, 2 marks for unit operations description, 1 mark for flow diagram, 1 mark for material of construction, 1 mark for yield/purity statement.

---

#### Part (b) [7 Marks] — Comparative Process Analysis

> **Q.A(b):** Compare the **indirect hydration** and **direct hydration** processes for IPA manufacture in terms of (i) raw material purity requirement, (ii) per-pass conversion and recycle, (iii) product purity, (iv) environmental impact, and (v) capital and operating cost. State, with reasons, which process is preferred for a modern grassroots plant. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

| Comparison Parameter | Indirect Hydration (H₂SO₄) | Direct Hydration (Catalytic) |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) C₃ feed purity required** | 50–92 % (cracker-grade acceptable) | ≥ 99 % (polymer-grade propylene required) |
| **(ii) Per-pass conversion** | ~90 % in absorber (high, no large recycle) | Vapor: 5–7 %; Liquid: 60–75 % (large recycle) |
| **(iii) Product purity** | 91–95 % (requires careful distillation) | 99.5 %+ (intrinsically purer) |
| **(iv) Environmental impact** | Acid sludge, sulfate waste; corrosion-related spills | No acid waste; cleaner effluents |
| **(v) Capex / Opex** | High capex (Monel, lead lining); high opex (acid make-up) | Medium capex; lower opex (no acid inventory) |

**Preferred route for modern grassroots plant: Direct Hydration (Liquid-Phase Cation-Exchange Resin Process).**

**Reasons:**
1. **Eliminates sulfuric acid handling**, avoiding the entire acid-recycle, concentration, and replenishment loop.
2. **Higher on-stream time** (no acid-cleaning shutdowns).
3. **Lower operating cost** despite the higher cost of polymer-grade propylene, because the propylene is consumed more efficiently and the recycle compression cost is manageable.
4. **Environmental compliance** is far simpler — no sulfate-bearing wastewater.
5. **Modern process technology** has stabilized direct-hydration catalyst life at > 3 years, making it competitive on total cost of ownership.

> **Mark split:** 3 marks for the comparison table, 2 marks for stating the preferred route, 2 marks for the five reasons.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

#### Part (a) [7 Marks] — Catalytic Hydrogenation of Acetone

> **Q.B(a):** Describe the **manufacture of Isopropyl Alcohol by catalytic hydrogenation of acetone**. Give the reaction, operating conditions, catalyst, and a process description. Discuss why this route is becoming increasingly relevant in modern petrochemical complexes. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Reaction:**
$$\text{CH}_3\text{–CO–CH}_3 + \text{H}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Ni or Cu, 150 °C, 1–3 bar}} \text{CH}_3\text{–CH(OH)–CH}_3$$

This is the **reverse of IPA dehydrogenation** and is **exothermic** (ΔH ≈ −55 kJ/mol). The reaction is **not equilibrium-limited** at low temperatures and proceeds with very high selectivity.

**Catalysts:** **Raney nickel**, **copper chromite (Cu-Cr)**, or **supported nickel on kieselguhr**. Modern formulations use **promoted copper catalysts** (Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃) for higher activity and longer life.

**Operating Conditions:**
- **Temperature:** 130–180 °C
- **Pressure:** 1–3 bar (gaseous acetone + H₂)
- **H₂ : acetone molar ratio:** 2 : 1 to 5 : 1 (excess H₂ favours conversion)
- **Per-pass conversion:** ~85–95 %
- **Selectivity to IPA:** > 99 %

**Process Description:**
1. **Vaporization:** Liquid acetone is vaporized and mixed with fresh + recycle hydrogen.
2. **Pre-heating:** The mixture is preheated to 150 °C using reactor-effluent heat.
3. **Fixed-bed reactor:** Adiabatic (or multi-tubular with cooling) reactor packed with catalyst; the exotherm raises the gas temperature by ~30 °C across the bed.
4. **Condensation:** Reactor effluent is condensed; the liquid is **crude IPA (95–99 %)**.
5. **Hydrogen recycle:** Unreacted H₂ is separated in a flash drum, recompressed, and returned to the reactor.
6. **Purification:** Crude IPA is sent to a single distillation column for finishing (99.9 % purity).

**Why this route is gaining relevance:**

> 1. **Acetone surplus** from the cumene-phenol process (which produces acetone as a 1:0.6 molar by-product) makes acetone a cheap feedstock in regions with large phenol capacity.
> 2. **Small-scale feasibility:** Unlike direct hydration, the acetone route does not require polymer-grade propylene or sulfuric acid — it uses commodity acetone and H₂.
> 3. **Highest product purity** (> 99.9 %) at the lowest capex.
> 4. **Drop-in option** for integrated phenol–acetone–IPA complexes, where acetone and IPA logistics can share storage and utilities.

> **Mark split:** 2 marks for reaction + conditions, 2 marks for process description, 2 marks for catalyst details, 1 mark for relevance discussion.

---

#### Part (b) [7 Marks] — Industrial Applications & Safety

> **Q.B(b):** Discuss the major **industrial applications of Isopropyl Alcohol**. Explain why it is preferred over ethanol and methanol in certain applications. Also list three important **safety and handling precautions** for bulk storage. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Major Industrial Applications of IPA:**

1. **Solvents (largest use, ~40 % of demand):** Coatings, thinners, printing inks, cleaning of electronics and精密仪器 (precision instruments), and as a process solvent in pharmaceutical extraction. IPA is preferred over ethanol here because of its **slightly slower evaporation rate** (better flow/leveling in coatings) and **lower regulatory restrictions** in some industrial solvent applications.

2. **Chemical Intermediate (~25 %):** Dehydrogenated to **acetone**, which feeds **MMA, BPA, and polycarbonate** chains. Also esterified to **isopropyl acetate** (a fast-evaporating solvent for inks and coatings).

3. **Pharmaceuticals and Cosmetics (~15 %):** 70 % v/v aqueous IPA is the **WHO-recommended hand-rub formulation**. Used in **rubbing alcohol, disinfectants, and as a pharmaceutical process solvent**. Preferred over methanol (toxic) and over ethanol (lower cost, less regulated in some jurisdictions).

4. **Household and Personal Care (~10 %):** Glass cleaners, deodorant carriers, fragrance solubilizer. The "alcohol" listed in cosmetic INCI nomenclature is predominantly IPA.

5. **Other (10 %):** **Isopropyl ether** (high-octane gasoline additive, anti-knock agent), **isopropylamines** (rubber accelerators, surfactants), and **aluminium isopropoxide** (Meerwein–Ponndorf–Verley reduction reagent).

**Why IPA is preferred over methanol/ethanol in specific applications:**

- **Versus methanol:** IPA is **far less toxic** (methanol causes blindness and death at low doses), and has a **more pleasant odor**, making it suitable for consumer products.
- **Versus ethanol:** IPA has a **higher boiling point (82.6 °C vs 78.4 °C)** — useful when slower evaporation is needed. It is also **not subject to the same potable-alcohol excise taxes** in many countries, giving it a price advantage in industrial markets.

**Bulk Storage Safety Precautions:**

1. **Flammability:** IPA has a **flash point of 11.7 °C (closed cup)** and a **vapour density heavier than air**. Bulk tanks must have **nitrogen blanketing, flame arresters on vents, and earthing/bonding** to prevent static discharge ignition.

2. **Toxicity & Inhalation:** Store in **well-ventilated, cool (< 30 °C), shaded areas** away from oxidizing agents (CrO₃, HNO₃, peroxides). TLV-TWA is **400 ppm (8-hour)**; areas must be equipped with **vapour detectors and eyewash stations**.

3. **Material Compatibility:** Bulk IPA tanks are **carbon steel (mild steel) — compatible and economic**. Avoid natural rubber, certain plastics, and aluminium at high temperatures. Use **SS-304 piping** and **PTFE gaskets** at flanges.

> **Mark split:** 3 marks for applications, 2 marks for comparison with methanol/ethanol, 2 marks for safety precautions.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Errors That Cost 1–3 Marks Each:**
>
> 1. **Writing "propanol" instead of "propan-2-ol"** — KTU strictly follows IUPAC 2013 recommendations. "Isopropanol" is acceptable as a synonym, but **"isopropyl alcohol" alone is NOT the IUPAC name** and will lose 0.5 marks if the question asks for the IUPAC name.
>
> 2. **Confusing per-pass and overall conversion in recycle problems** — In direct hydration, per-pass is 5–7 % but overall is ~100 %. Writing the wrong value in a stoichiometric calculation gives an answer off by 15× and **fails the dimensional-consistency check** that examiners do.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the 5 % acid make-up in indirect hydration** — Students often assume sulfuric acid is a true catalyst and write "no acid consumed." This is wrong: 5 % is lost per cycle, and the question typically wants the **make-up rate, not zero**.
>
> 4. **Drawing a flow diagram without the recycle loop** — Examiners deduct 1 mark if the recycle stream (especially in direct hydration) is missing.
>
> 5. **Quoting "H₃PO₄" as the only direct-hydration catalyst** — Liquid-phase direct hydration uses **cation-exchange resins (sulfonated polystyrene)**, not phosphoric acid. Confusing the two routes costs 1 mark.
>
> 6. **Using "|" (vertical bar) inside markdown/text tables for absolute value** — In answer scripts, always write **"mod x"** or **"absolute value of x"** or use $\vert x \vert$ in LaTeX. Examiners have flagged this as a frequent notation error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 3 / Isopropyl Alcohol**

- ✅ IPA = **Propan-2-ol** = **C₃H₈O** = **M = 60.10 g/mol** = **secondary alcohol** (–OH on the middle carbon).
- ✅ **Boiling point = 82.6 °C**; **flash point = 11.7 °C**; **density = 0.786 g/cm³**; **fully miscible with water**.
- ✅ **Three industrial routes:** (1) **Indirect hydration** with 70–85 % H₂SO₄ (two-stage, tolerates dilute C₃ feed, ~95 % yield), (2) **Direct hydration** (vapor-phase H₃PO₄/SiO₂ or liquid-phase cation-exchange resin; requires polymer-grade C₃; cleaner), (3) **Acetone hydrogenation** (Raney Ni or Cu, highest purity, dependent on acetone availability).
- ✅ **Stoichiometric mass ratio: 1 kg propylene → 1.428 kg IPA** (the single most useful number on the exam).
- ✅ **Indirect hydration material of construction:** lead-lined or Monel for acid service; SS-304 for the rest.
- ✅ **Direct hydration per-pass conversion: 5–7 %** → large recycle; **liquid-phase variant: 60–75 %** → smaller recycle.
- ✅ **Acid make-up in indirect process: 5 % of theoretical H₂SO₄** per kg of IPA.
- ✅ **Major use: solvent (40 %), acetone precursor (25 %), pharma/cosmetics (15 %).**
- ✅ **Safety: highly flammable (FP 11.7 °C); store under N₂ blanket; mild-steel tanks acceptable; TLV 400 ppm.**
- ✅ **Trend: Direct hydration now dominates new plant builds** because of environmental and cost benefits.
- ✅ **Markownikoff addition** explains why propylene + H₂SO₄ gives the **secondary** ester, not n-propyl ester.
- ✅ **Direct hydration is exothermic (ΔH ≈ −50 kJ/mol) and equilibrium-limited at high T** — hence the thermodynamic-vs-kinetic trade-off at 200–260 °C.
- ✅ **Acetone route is highly relevant where a phenol–acetone plant exists** — uses cumene-process by-product acetone.
- ✅ **70 % v/v aqueous IPA is the WHO-standard hand sanitizer formulation.**
- ✅ **No Python or code is required in this topic** — all problems are pure stoichiometric and thermodynamic calculations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Acrylonitrile
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT741 - PETROCHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Acrylonitrile

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Acrylonitrile** (IUPAC name: *prop-2-enenitrile* or *2-propenenitrile*) is the simplest unsaturated nitrile, with the molecular formula **CH₂=CH–CN** and molecular weight **53.06 g/mol**. It is also known historically as **vinyl cyanide** because of its structural similarity to ethylene (CH₂=CH₂) where one hydrogen is replaced by a cyano (–C≡N) group.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Acrylonitrile is a colorless, volatile, flammable liquid with a faintly pungent odor (reminiscent of garlic or onion at higher concentrations). It is a **reactive monomer** containing a polar electron-withdrawing nitrile group and a polymerizable vinyl group. It is the third most important monomer (after ethylene and vinyl chloride) in petrochemical industry for the manufacture of **acrylic fibers, ABS plastics, SAN resins, nitrile rubber, and polyacrylamide**.

---

## 1.2 Physical & Thermodynamic Properties

| Property | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Molecular Formula | CH₂=CHCN | — |
| Molecular Weight | **53.06** | g/mol |
| Boiling Point | **77.3** | °C |
| Melting Point | **−83.55** | °C |
| Density (at 20 °C) | **0.8060** | g/cm³ |
| Flash Point (closed cup) | **−5** | °C |
| Auto-ignition Temperature | **481** | °C |
| Explosive Limits (in air) | 3.05 – 17.0 | vol % |
| Heat of Polymerization | **~ 71.6** | kJ/mol |
| Refractive Index (n_D²⁰) | 1.3911 | — |
| Vapor Pressure (20 °C) | 11.5 | kPa |

> [!NOTE]
> The **polar C≡N group** makes acrylonitrile highly **electrophilic** and prone to **anionic polymerization**, unlike simple olefins which favor radical or cationic routes. This polar nature is the chemical basis for its use in synthetic fibers with high dye-affinity.

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are constructing a building using identical "smart bricks" that snap together perfectly. Each brick has a hook on one side (the vinyl C=C double bond) and a magnet on the other (the nitrile –C≡N group). When many bricks are snapped together, they form a long chain — this chain is **polyacrylonitrile (PAN)**. 

- The **hooks (C=C)** decide *how* the bricks link together.
- The **magnets (–CN)** decide *what properties* the final wall (fiber/plastic) will have — strength, chemical resistance, and ability to bind with dyes.

> Acrylonitrile is, therefore, the **monomer brick** that gives polymer chemists a way to design materials ranging from the soft acrylic wool sweater to the rigid ABS plastic in your car bumper.

---

## 1.4 Industrial Significance & Strategic Importance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Acrylonitrile is classified as a **high-tonnage petrochemical** with global annual production exceeding **8 million metric tonnes**. It is the **gateway chemical** to three major polymer families:
> 1. **Acrylic fibers** (PAN for textiles, carbon-fiber precursors)
> 2. **Acrylonitrile–Butadiene–Styrene (ABS)** and **Styrene–Acrylonitrile (SAN)** engineering plastics
> 3. **Nitrile rubber (NBR)** — used in gloves, hoses, gaskets

A secondary but commercially significant derivative is **acrylamide**, used in water-treatment polymers and enhanced-oil-recovery (EOR) flocculants.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Industrial Manufacturing Routes — Comparative Analysis

KTU 2024 emphasizes the comparison of **three historical routes** with the **Sohio process** as the dominant modern route.

### Route 1 — Acetylene–Hydrogen Cyanide Route (Reppe / I.G. Farben)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CH}\equiv\text{CH} + \text{HCN} \xrightarrow{\text{CuCl catalyst, 80-90}^\circ\text{C}} \text{CH}_2{=}\text{CH--CN}
\end{aligned}
$$

- **Feedstock:** Acetylene + HCN (from Andrussow process: 2 CH₄ + 2 NH₃ + 3 O₂ → 2 HCN + 6 H₂O)
- **Disadvantages:** Acetylene is expensive; HCN is extremely toxic; reaction is highly exothermic and hazardous.
- **Status:** Largely **obsolete** since the 1960s.

---

### Route 2 — Ethylene Oxide–HCN Route (Knapsack / Hoechst-Uhde)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CH}_2{=}\text{CH}_2 &\xrightarrow{\text{O}_2,\;\text{Ag}} \text{CH}_2\text{--CH}_2\ (\text{epoxide}) \\
\text{CH}_2\text{--CH}_2 + \text{HCN} &\xrightarrow{\text{basic cat.}} \text{HOCH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{CN} \\
\text{HOCH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{CN} &\xrightarrow{-\text{H}_2\text{O},\;\text{Mg}^{2+},\;200^\circ\text{C}} \text{CH}_2{=}\text{CHCN}
\end{aligned}
$$

- **Disadvantages:** Multi-step; expensive separation; competing production of ethylene glycol.
- **Status:** **Obsolete** since 1970s.

---

### Route 3 — Sohio Ammoxidation Process (Standard Oil of Ohio, 1957)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** This is the **only commercially dominant route** today — accounts for **> 95 % of global acrylonitrile production**. It is a *single-step vapor-phase heterogeneous catalytic oxidation* in a **fluidized-bed reactor (FBR)**.

**Overall Reaction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CH}_2{=}\text{CH--CH}_3 + \text{NH}_3 + \tfrac{3}{2}\,\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{bismuth-molybdate catalyst},\;400-460^\circ\text{C}} \text{CH}_2{=}\text{CH--CN} + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\Delta H_{rxn} \approx -515 \ \text{kJ/mol}$$

**Step-wise Mechanism (Mars–van Krevelen / Redox type):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1 (Oxidative dehydrogenation):} \quad &\text{CH}_2{=}\text{CH--CH}_3 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_2{=}\text{CH--CH}_2\text{--OH} \\
\text{Step 2 (Oxidation to aldehyde):} \quad &\text{CH}_2{=}\text{CH--CH}_2\text{OH} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_2{=}\text{CH--CHO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \\
\text{Step 3 (Ammonolysis):} \quad &\text{CH}_2{=}\text{CH--CHO} + \text{NH}_3 \rightarrow \text{CH}_2{=}\text{CH--CH}{=}\text{NH} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \\
\text{Step 4 (Oxidative dehydrogenation of imine):} \quad &\text{CH}_2{=}\text{CH--CH}{=}\text{NH} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_2{=}\text{CH--CN} + \text{H}_2\text{O}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Net:** Propylene + NH₃ + 1.5 O₂ → Acrylonitrile + 3 H₂O.

---

## 2.2 Catalyst System — A Deep Dive

The **SoMo (Sb–Fe–Mo–oxide)** family or **Bi-Mo-oxide** is the heart of the Sohio process.

| Component | Function | Optimal Loading |
|---|---|---|
| **Bi₂O₃** | Oxidation promoter | 10–30 % |
| **MoO₃** | Active redox lattice (Mo⁶⁺ ↔ Mo⁵⁺) | 50–70 % |
| **Sb₂O₄** | Reduces side reactions to acrolein | 5–15 % |
| **Fe₂O₃ / CoO** | Structural / electronic modifier | 1–5 % |
| **Support** | High-surface fumed SiO₂ | Balance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a fluidized bed?** The reaction is highly exothermic (≈ −515 kJ/mol) and good temperature control is critical. A fluidized bed provides **isothermal conditions** (~ 5 °C variation) across millions of catalyst particles, preventing hot spots and catalyst sintering.

---

## 2.3 Process Flow & By-products

A typical **commercial Sohio reactor effluent** contains:

| Component | Typical mol % |
|---|---|
| Acrylonitrile | 1 – 2 |
| Acetonitrile (MeCN) | 0.1 – 0.3 |
| HCN | 0.05 – 0.2 |
| Unreacted NH₃ | 0.5 – 1 |
| CO / CO₂ | 1 – 3 |
| Water vapor | 30 – 40 |
| Unreacted propylene + N₂ | Balance |

By-products — **acetonitrile** and **HCN** — are recovered and sold separately, contributing to plant economics.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> All formulas below are **must-memorize** for KTU ESE. Use `\\` for LaTeX inside tables where needed; absolute-value bars use `\vert` (not `\vert` pipe character) to avoid markdown breaks.

| # | Quantity / Parameter | Formula / Expression | Units | Notes / Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Single-pass Conversion of propylene** | $X_{C_3} = \dfrac{F_{C_3,\,in} - F_{C_3,\,out}}{F_{C_3,\,in}}$ | dimensionless | Typical $X_{C_3}$ ≈ 95–98 % |
| 2 | **Selectivity to acrylonitrile** | $S_{AN} = \dfrac{F_{AN,\,out}}{F_{C_3,\,in} - F_{C_3,\,out}}$ | dimensionless | Typical $S_{AN}$ ≈ 75–82 % |
| 3 | **Overall Yield** | $Y_{AN} = X_{C_3} \times S_{AN}$ | dimensionless | Typical $Y_{AN}$ ≈ 75–80 % |
| 4 | **Space-Time Yield (STY)** | $STY = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{AN}}{W_{cat}} \cdot 3600$ | kg·h⁻¹ per kg cat | Industrial STY ≈ 0.4 – 0.6 |
| 5 | **Molar feed ratio** | $\Phi = \dfrac{n_{NH_3}}{n_{C_3}}$ | dimensionless | Optimal $\Phi$ ≈ 1.05 – 1.20 |
| 6 | **Air-to-propylene ratio** | $\Psi = \dfrac{n_{O_2,\,as\,air}}{n_{C_3}}$ | dimensionless | Optimal $\Psi$ ≈ 9 – 11 |
| 7 | **Mass balance closure check** | $\sum \dot{m}_{in} = \sum \dot{m}_{out}$ | kg/h | Always close streams |
| 8 | **Heat removal duty of FBR** | $Q_{rem} = -\Delta H_{rxn} \times F_{C_3,\,consumed}$ | kW | Used for cooling-coil sizing |
| 9 | **Carbon-atom selectivity** | $CS = \dfrac{3\,F_{AN}}{3\,(F_{C_3,\,in}-F_{C_3,\,out})}$ | % | Used to track CO/CO₂ losses |
| 10 | **Acrylonitrile vapour pressure (Antoine eq.)** | $\log_{10}P^{sat} = A - \dfrac{B}{T+C}$ | kPa, T in °C | A = 7.038; B = 1273.5; C = 230 |

> Real-world utility: formulas (1)–(3) are used daily in **plant DCS dashboards** to monitor Sohio reactor health; formula (8) is used by **process engineers** to size the internal cooling coils of the FBR.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Worked-Out Problem — Material Balance on a Sohio Reactor

> **Problem Statement:** A Sohio fluidized-bed reactor is fed with **1000 kmol/h of propylene, 1100 kmol/h of ammonia, and 11,000 kmol/h of air (≈ 2310 kmol/h O₂ + 8690 kmol/h N₂).** Single-pass propylene conversion is **96 %**, selectivity to acrylonitrile is **78 %**, selectivity to acetonitrile is **6 %**, selectivity to HCN is **2 %**, and the remaining 14 % goes to CO + CO₂ (assumed as CO₂ for stoichiometry). Compute all outlet stream flow rates.

---

### Step 1 — Define moles reacted of propylene

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{C_3,\,reacted} &= 1000 \times 0.96 = 960 \ \text{kmol/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the propylene actually undergoing reaction.

---

### Step 2 — Compute moles of each product

Using selectivity $S_i$ = (mol product $i$) / (mol propylene reacted), and noting that:
- AN uses **1 mol C₃ / mol AN**
- MeCN uses **1 mol C₃ / mol MeCN**
- HCN uses **1 mol C₃ / mol HCN** (only 1 C from C₃; the other 2 leave as CO₂)
- CO₂ produced here is from the *other* 2 carbons of the C₃ molecule when AN/MeCN/HCN are *not* formed.

A cleaner accounting uses carbon-balance selectivity. For AN, MeCN, HCN, each molecule consumes 3 carbons of the C₃ chain. So:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{AN} &= 0.78 \times 960 = 748.8 \ \text{kmol/h} \\
F_{MeCN} &= 0.06 \times 960 = 57.6 \ \text{kmol/h} \\
F_{HCN} &= 0.02 \times 960 = 19.2 \ \text{kmol/h} \\
F_{CO_2,\,from\,combustion} &= 0.14 \times 960 \times 3 = 403.2 \ \text{kmol/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 3 — Compute O₂ consumed

Stoichiometry of overall reaction:
- AN path: 1.5 mol O₂ / mol C₃ → consumed $748.8 \times 1.5 = 1123.2$ kmol/h
- MeCN path (CH₂=CHCN + HCN form): 2.5 mol O₂ / mol C₃ → consumed $57.6 \times 2.5 = 144$ kmol/h
- HCN path: 2.5 mol O₂ / mol C₃ → consumed $19.2 \times 2.5 = 48$ kmol/h
- CO₂ path: 4.5 mol O₂ / mol C₃ (since 2 carbons → 2 CO₂ + 1 O leftover? Recompute below)

For complete combustion: C₃H₆ + 4.5 O₂ → 3 CO₂ + 3 H₂O. So for the 14 % burnt fraction:

$$
\begin{aligned}
O_2\,(\text{combustion}) &= 0.14 \times 960 \times 4.5 = 604.8 \ \text{kmol/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total O₂ consumed:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{O_2,\,consumed} &= 1123.2 + 144 + 48 + 604.8 = 1919.2 \ \text{kmol/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 4 — Compute N₂ in outlet (inert balance)

N₂ passes through unchanged:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{N_2,\,out} &= 8690 \ \text{kmol/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 5 — Compute unreacted propylene

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{C_3,\,out} &= 1000 - 960 = 40 \ \text{kmol/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 6 — Compute unreacted ammonia

Ammonia is consumed by stoichiometry. Net AN formation uses 1 NH₃ per C₃; HCN formation uses 1 NH₃ per C₃. MeCN formation *also* consumes 1 NH₃ per C₃.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{NH_3,\,consumed} &= (748.8 + 57.6 + 19.2) \times 1 = 825.6 \ \text{kmol/h} \\
F_{NH_3,\,out} &= 1100 - 825.6 = 274.4 \ \text{kmol/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 7 — Compute water produced

Water is produced in:
- AN path: 3 mol H₂O / mol C₃
- MeCN path: 3 mol H₂O / mol C₃
- HCN path: 3 mol H₂O / mol C₃
- CO₂ path: 3 mol H₂O / mol C₃ (from combustion of C₃H₆)

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{H_2O,\,out} &= 960 \times 3 = 2880 \ \text{kmol/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 8 — Final Outlet Composition Summary

| Component | Outlet Flow (kmol/h) |
|---|---|
| Propylene (unreacted) | **40.0** |
| Ammonia (unreacted) | **274.4** |
| O₂ (unreacted) | 2310 − 1919.2 = **390.8** |
| N₂ | **8690.0** |
| Acrylonitrile | **748.8** |
| Acetonitrile | **57.6** |
| HCN | **19.2** |
| CO₂ | **403.2** |
| H₂O | **2880.0** |
| **Total** | **13,504.0** |

---

## 3.2 Derivations — Heat Duty of the Reactor

Heat released by reaction:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{rxn} &= -\Delta H_{rxn} \times F_{C_3,\,reacted} \\
&= 515 \ \tfrac{\text{kJ}}{\text{mol}} \times 960 \ \tfrac{\text{kmol}}{\text{h}} \\
&= 494{,}400 \ \tfrac{\text{kJ}}{\text{h}} = 137.3 \ \text{kW (per 1 kmol/h C_3 scale-up)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This heat is removed by internal cooling coils generating **medium-pressure steam (~ 1 MPa)** for use elsewhere in the plant — a textbook example of **process integration**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Route Study

| Parameter | Acetylene Route | Ethylene Oxide Route | Sohio Ammoxidation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Feedstock cost | Very high | High | **Low** |
| # of steps | 1 | 3 | **1** |
| Catalyst | CuCl/KCl | Mg salts | **Bi-Mo-Sb oxide** |
| Reaction phase | Liquid (slurry) | Liquid | **Vapor / FBR** |
| By-products | Vinylacetylene, divinyl | Ethylene glycol | **HCN, MeCN, CO₂** |
| Hazardous | HCN, explosive C₂H₂ | HCN | **NH₃ (manageable)** |
| Commercial status (2024) | **Obsolete** | **Obsolete** | **Dominant (> 95 %)** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Sohio Process Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    propIn[/Propylene Feed/]:::raw
    ammIn[/Ammonia Feed/]:::raw
    airIn[/Air Feed/]:::raw

    propIn --> preMix((Preheater and Mixer))
    ammIn --> preMix
    airIn --> preMix

    preMix --> fbrFB[Fluidized Bed Reactor 400 to 460 degree Celsius Bi-Mo-Sb Oxide Catalyst]

    fbrFB --> coolCoil[Internal Cooling Coils High Pressure Steam Generation]

    fbrFB --> wasteBoiler[Waste Heat Boiler Effluent Quench]

    wasteBoiler --> absTower[Absorption Stripper Water Scrubbing AN Capture]

    absTower --> acqBot[Acrylonitrile Rich Aqueous Solution]

    acqBot --> extCol[Extraction Distillation Acetonitrile Separation]

    extCol --> acnOut[/Acetonitrile Product/]:::prod

    extCol --> cracHCN[HCN Stripping Column]
    cracHCN --> hcnOut[/HCN Product/]:::prod

    cracHCN --> purifCol[AN Purification Vacuum Distillation]

    purifCol --> anOut[/Polymer Grade Acrylonitrile 99.9 percent/]:::prod
    purifCol --> recycle[/Unreacted Propylene Recycle/]:::raw

    absTower --> wasteWat[/Wastewater Treatment Stripper/]:::waste

    classDef raw fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef prod fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef waste fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Reaction Mechanism (Catalytic Redox Cycle)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    a1[A1 Propylene Adsorbs on Catalyst Surface] --> a2[A2 Allylic Oxidation to Allyl Intermediate]
    a2 --> a3[A3 NH3 Insertion Forming Imine Species]
    a3 --> a4[A4 Oxidative Dehydrogenation to Nitrile]
    a4 --> a5[A5 AN Desorbs and Lattice Oxygen is Replenished by O2]
    a5 --> a1

    b1[B1 Catalyst Lattice Oxide O2 minus] --> b2[B2 Reduced Site Catalyst Becomes Bi Mo Sb Reduced]
    b2 --> b3[B3 Reoxidation by Gas Phase O2]
    b3 --> b1
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Downstream Derivative Tree of Acrylonitrile

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    AN[Acrylonitrile Monomer] --> PAN[Polyacrylonitrile PAN Fibers for Textile and Carbon Fiber Precursor]
    AN --> ABS[ABS Plastic Acrylonitrile Butadiene Styrene]
    AN --> SAN[SAN Resin Styrene Acrylonitrile]
    AN --> NBR[Nitrile Rubber NBR Gaskets and Gloves]
    AN --> AMD[Acrylamide via Sulfuric Acid Hydration]
    AMD --> PAM[Polyacrylamide PAM Flocculants for Water Treatment and EOR]
    AN --> AAc[Acrylic Acid via Amide Hydrolysis]
    AAc --> SuperAbs[Super Absorbent Polymers for Diapers]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Direct Recall)

### **Q1. Define acrylonitrile. Mention two important industrial applications.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
Acrylonitrile is an **unsaturated nitrile** with molecular formula **CH₂=CHCN**, also known as **vinyl cyanide** or **2-propenenitrile**, with molecular weight **53.06 g/mol**. It is a colorless, flammable liquid used industrially in:
1. Manufacture of **acrylic fibers (PAN)** for textiles.
2. Manufacture of **ABS (Acrylonitrile–Butadiene–Styrene)** engineering plastics.

*(Any one valid application = 1 mark; definition = 2 marks; full = 3 marks.)*

---

### **Q2. Name the dominant industrial process for manufacturing acrylonitrile. Write the overall reaction.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
The **Sohio ammoxidation process** (Standard Oil of Ohio, 1957) is the dominant route.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CH}_2{=}\text{CH--CH}_3 + \text{NH}_3 + \tfrac{3}{2}\,\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Bi-Mo-oxide, 425}^\circ\text{C}} \text{CH}_2{=}\text{CHCN} + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
\end{aligned}
$$

The reaction is carried out in a **fluidized-bed reactor** using air as the oxygen source. *(Process name = 1 mark; balanced reaction = 2 marks.)*

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module-Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat flow diagram, describe the **Sohio process** for the manufacture of acrylonitrile from propylene. Discuss the role of the **fluidized-bed reactor** and the **catalyst system**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A feed containing **500 kmol/h propylene, 540 kmol/h NH₃, and 5500 kmol/h air (≈ 1155 kmol/h O₂ + 4345 kmol/h N₂)** enters a Sohio reactor. Given: single-pass conversion of propylene = **95 %**, selectivity to AN = **78 %**, MeCN = **5 %**, HCN = **2 %**, and the rest (15 %) goes to CO₂. Compute the **outlet molar flow rates of all major components**. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer — Q.A(a)**

**Step 1 — Reaction and Feedstocks:** Propylene, ammonia, and air are preheated and mixed in the desired molar ratio (NH₃/C₃ ≈ 1.05; air/C₃ ≈ 10).

**Step 2 — Reactor:** The mixture enters a **fluidized-bed catalytic reactor** operating at **400–460 °C, 1.5–3 atm**, where it contacts a **bismuth–molybdate (Bi-Mo)** catalyst (with Sb, Fe, Co promoters). The reactor is essentially a vertical vessel with a perforated distributor plate at the bottom through which air enters and fluidizes the catalyst.

**Step 3 — Reaction Pathway:** Propylene is **oxidatively dehydrogenated** to an allyl intermediate, **oxidized** to acrolein, **ammonolyzed** to an imine, and finally **oxidatively dehydrogenated** to acrylonitrile.

**Step 4 — Effluent Treatment:** Hot reactor effluent passes through a **waste-heat boiler** (for steam generation), then a **quench/absorber** where AN, MeCN, HCN are scrubbed into water.

**Step 5 — Separation:** The aqueous solution is sent to a **stripper**, an **extractive distillation column** (to separate MeCN), an **HCN distillation column**, and finally a **purification column** delivering polymer-grade AN (> 99.9 %).

**Role of FBR & Catalyst (1 mark each):** The FBR provides excellent **isothermal control**, eliminates hot spots, and allows catalyst regeneration in situ. The Bi-Mo oxide provides **lattice oxygen** for the selective oxidation and is regenerated by gas-phase O₂ (Mars–van Krevelen mechanism).

---

### **Model Answer — Q.A(b)**

**Step 1 — Propylene reacted:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{C_3,\,reacted} = 500 \times 0.95 = 475 \ \text{kmol/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — AN produced:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{AN} = 475 \times 0.78 = 370.5 \ \text{kmol/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Other products:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{MeCN} &= 475 \times 0.05 = 23.75 \ \text{kmol/h} \\
F_{HCN} &= 475 \times 0.02 = 9.5 \ \text{kmol/h} \\
F_{CO_2} &= 475 \times 0.15 \times 3 = 213.75 \ \text{kmol/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — NH₃ consumed:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{NH_3,\,consumed} &= (370.5 + 23.75 + 9.5) = 403.75 \ \text{kmol/h} \\
