F_{NH_3,\,out} &= 540 - 403.75 = 136.25 \ \text{kmol/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — O₂ consumed:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
O_2\,(AN) &= 370.5 \times 1.5 = 555.75 \\
O_2\,(MeCN) &= 23.75 \times 2.5 = 59.375 \\
O_2\,(HCN) &= 9.5 \times 2.5 = 23.75 \\
O_2\,(CO_2) &= 213.75 \times \tfrac{4.5}{3} = 320.625 \\
\text{Total} &= 959.5 \ \text{kmol/h} \\
F_{O_2,\,out} &= 1155 - 959.5 = 195.5 \ \text{kmol/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Water produced:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{H_2O,\,out} = 475 \times 3 = 1425 \ \text{kmol/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Outlet Table:**

| Component | kmol/h |
|---|---|
| C₃H₆ (unreacted) | 25.0 |
| NH₃ | 136.25 |
| O₂ | 195.5 |
| N₂ | 4345.0 |
| AN | **370.5** |
| MeCN | 23.75 |
| HCN | 9.5 |
| CO₂ | 213.75 |
| H₂O | 1425.0 |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Compare the **three industrial routes** for the manufacture of acrylonitrile — acetylene–HCN, ethylene oxide, and Sohio ammoxidation. State **two reasons** why Sohio dominates today. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **role of acetonitrile and HCN** as by-products in the Sohio process. Outline how they are **separated and recovered** in downstream processing. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer — Q.B(a)**

| Route | Status | Key Reaction | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Acetylene–HCN | Obsolete | CH≡CH + HCN → CH₂=CHCN | Single-step; mild T | C₂H₂ expensive; HCN toxic |
| Ethylene oxide–HCN | Obsolete | Multi-step via ethylene oxide → cyano-hydrin | Uses cheaper C₂H₄ | Three-step; high CAPEX; competing glycol production |
| Sohio ammoxidation | **Dominant (> 95 %)** | C₃H₆ + NH₃ + 1.5 O₂ → AN + 3 H₂O | Single-step; cheap feed; high yield | Exothermic FBR control needed |

**Two reasons Sohio dominates (2 marks):**
1. **Feedstock economy:** Propylene is the cheapest available C₃ olefin and is supplied in bulk from steam crackers and FCC units.
2. **Single-step continuous operation** in a fluidized-bed reactor yields **~ 80 % overall AN yield**, minimizing CAPEX and OPEX per tonne.

---

### **Model Answer — Q.B(b)**

**Acetonitrile (MeCN):** Formed by side reaction when the intermediate allyl species reacts with NH₃ at a different C-atom. It is a **valuable solvent** (HPLC, pharmaceutical industry).

**HCN:** Formed by over-dehydrogenation of intermediate imines; **extremely toxic** and a useful precursor for **methyl methacrylate (MMA)** via the acetone-cyanohydrin route.

**Separation sequence (3 marks):**
1. Reactor effluent is **quenched** with water in an absorber; AN, MeCN, HCN dissolve in water while unreacted propylene, O₂, N₂, and CO₂ leave the top.
2. Aqueous stream enters an **extractive distillation column** with water as entrainer; **MeCN** (lower-boiling) is taken overhead.
3. Remaining aqueous stream enters an **HCN stripper** where HCN is taken overhead at low pressure.
4. Bottom of HCN stripper goes to the **AN purification column** delivering polymer-grade AN.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Mis-balanced reaction:** Forgetting the 1.5 O₂ or 3 H₂O in the overall Sohio reaction. **Penalty:** 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing selectivity definition:** Selectivity = mol of *target product* per mol of *propylene reacted*, **not** per mol of propylene fed. Mixing up these two definitions leads to incorrect flow rates.
> 3. **Forgetting N₂ as inert:** N₂ entering = N₂ exiting. Failing to track N₂ means material balance will not close.
> 4. **Confusing polymerization routes:** Acrylonitrile favors **anionic / radical polymerization** (because of the electron-withdrawing –CN group), **NOT** cationic. Stating "cationic polymerization" will cost a mark.
> 5. **Misstating acetylene-route status:** Calling acetylene route "modern" will lose marks; it is **obsolete**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Acrylonitrile:**

- **Formula to memorize:** CH₂=CHCN, M = **53.06 g/mol**, bp = 77.3 °C.
- **Manufacturing:** Only the **Sohio ammoxidation** is currently used (> 95 %).
- **Overall reaction:** C₃H₆ + NH₃ + 1.5 O₂ → AN + 3 H₂O (ΔH ≈ −515 kJ/mol).
- **Catalyst:** **Bi-Mo-Sb oxide** in a **fluidized-bed reactor (FBR)** at 400–460 °C.
- **Reactor type:** **Fluidized-bed** (for isothermal control of highly exothermic reaction).
- **By-products:** **HCN, acetonitrile (MeCN), CO₂** — all are commercially recovered.
- **Selectivity order:** AN (≈ 78 %) > MeCN (≈ 5 %) > HCN (≈ 2 %) > CO/CO₂ (≈ 15 %).
- **Major applications:** **ABS, SAN, PAN fiber, NBR, acrylamide** (→ polyacrylamide for EOR).
- **Polymerization type:** **Radical** and **anionic**, *not* cationic.
- **End-use of PAN:** Textile + **carbon-fiber precursor** (carbon fibers used in Boeing 787, Airbus A350).
- **Health hazard:** Acrylonitrile is a **probable human carcinogen (IARC Group 2B)**; workplace TLV = **2 ppm (8-h TWA)**.
- **Storage/handling:** Inhibited with **MEHQ (mono-methyl ether of hydroquinone)** to prevent premature polymerization.
- **Antoine constants (for vapour pressure):** A = 7.038, B = 1273.5, C = 230 (P in kPa, T in °C).
- **Mass-balance check:** Σ ṁᵢₙ = Σ ṁₒᵤₜ for every numerical problem.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cumene
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT741 - PETROCHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Cumene # 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cumene (Isopropylbenzene, 2-Phenylpropane)** is an aromatic hydrocarbon with the molecular formula **C₉H₁₂** (or C₆H₅–CH(CH₃)₂), consisting of a benzene ring attached to an isopropyl group. It is a colourless, flammable liquid with a gasoline-like odour and serves as the single most important petrochemical intermediate for the **co-production of phenol and acetone** via the **Cumene Process (Hock Process)**. | Property | Value | Property | Value | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **IUPAC Name** | (1-Methylethyl)benzene | **Molecular Weight** | **120.19 g/mol** | | **Synonyms** | Isopropylbenzene, 2-Phenylpropane | **Boiling Point** | **152.4 °C** | | **CAS Number** | 98-82-8 | **Melting Point** | **−96 °C** | | **Density (20 °C)** | **0.862 g/cm³** | **Flash Point** | **43 °C** | | **Refractive Index (nD²⁰)** | **1.4915** | **Auto-ignition T** | **424 °C** | ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **The "Chemical Bridge" Analogy:** Imagine petroleum refining as a giant kitchen. Crude oil gives you raw ingredients (naphtha). From naphtha, you can extract **benzene** (an aromatic spice) and **propylene** (a reactive gas). When you "cook" them together using an acid catalyst, you get **cumene** — a special flavouring intermediate. Cumene is not the final dish; instead, it is the **"magic middle ingredient"** that, when sliced with oxygen and a drop of acid, simultaneously gives you **two finished products** (phenol + acetone) in a single pot. This "one-pot, two-product" nature is why cumene economics drive ~95% of global phenol production. ## 1.3 Historical & Industrial Context Cumene was first isolated from coal tar in 1840. Its industrial significance exploded in **1944** when **Heinrich Hock** at the University of Frankfurt discovered that cumene hydroperoxide could be cleaved to yield phenol and acetone. This discovery transformed phenol from a costly coal-derived chemical into an inexpensive petrochemical commodity, with the **first commercial cumene-to-phenol plant** starting in **1952 (Hercules, USA)**. > [!TIP] > **KTU Syllabus Hook:** The Hock process is a classic example of a **"tandem industrial reaction"** — where a single feedstock is converted into *two* economically valuable co-products. Examiners frequently test this concept because it teaches reaction selectivity, atom economy, and process integration. # 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 The Three Stages of the Cumene Process The industrial Cumene-to-Phenol/Acetone process operates in **three discrete reaction stages**, each with distinct chemistry, catalysts, and engineering requirements. ### Stage 1 — Friedel–Crafts Alkylation (Cumene Synthesis) $$\text{C}_6\text{H}_6 \;\; + \;\; \text{CH}_2{=}\text{CH}{-}\text{CH}_3 \;\; \xrightarrow{\text{Catalyst}} \;\; \text{C}_6\text{H}_5{-}\text{CH(CH}_3)_2$$ - **Reactants:** Benzene + Propylene (anhydrous, high purity) - **Catalyst Systems (Generation-wise):** - **1st Gen:** AlCl₃ + HCl (homogeneous, corrosive, waste disposal issues) - **2nd Gen:** Solid Phosphoric Acid (**SPA**, H₃PO₄/SiO₂) — UOP process, 230–250 °C, 30–40 bar - **3rd Gen:** Zeolites (ZSM-5, β-zeolite, MCM-22) — Mobil/Badger, Dow/Kellogg, UOP Q-Max - **Selectivity Challenge:** Avoid **polyalkylation** (di-/tri-isopropylbenzene). Achieved by using a **large benzene-to-propylene molar ratio (4:1 to 8:1)**. ### Stage 2 — Air Oxidation (CHP Formation) $$\text{C}_6\text{H}_5{-}\text{CH(CH}_3)_2 \;\; + \;\; \text{O}_2 \;\; \xrightarrow{\text{Initiator / 100–130 °C}} \;\; \text{C}_6\text{H}_5{-}\text{C(CH}_3)_2{-}\text{OOH}$$ - **Product:** **Cumene Hydroperoxide (CHP)** — a key, sensitive intermediate. - **Free-Radical Chain Mechanism:** Initiation → Propagation → Termination. - **Conditions:** 100–130 °C, 1–5 bar air, **conversion limited to 25–30%** to suppress side reactions. - **Side Products:** Acetophenone, 2-Phenyl-2-propanol (Dimethylphenyl Carbinol, DMPC), α-Methylstyrene (AMS). ### Stage 3 — Acid-Catalysed Cleavage (Hock Rearrangement) $$\text{C}_6\text{H}_5{-}\text{C(CH}_3)_2{-}\text{OOH} \;\; \xrightarrow{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \text{ (dilute), 50–80 °C}} \;\; \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{OH} \;\; + \;\; \text{(CH}_3)_2\text{CO}$$ - **Mechanism:** **Criegee Intermediate** (1,2-phenyl migration from C to O, with concerted O–O bond cleavage). - **Co-products:** **Phenol (C₆H₅OH)** + **Acetone ((CH₃)₂CO)** — produced in **1:1 molar ratio** by stoichiometry. - **Catalyst:** H₂SO₄ (5–20%) or H₃PO₄ in homogeneous phase; or solid acids (ion-exchange resins, zeolites) in newer processes. > [!NOTE] > **Why "Why" and "How" matter for KTU valuation:** Examiners award marks for explaining *why* low conversion in Stage 2 is essential (to prevent CHP decomposition and explosion hazard) and *how* the 1:1 phenol/acetone molar ratio creates a **co-product market dependency** (acetone price fluctuations directly impact phenol plant economics). ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Equation / Relation | Description | Units | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $n = \dfrac{m}{M}$ | Moles from mass; $M_{\text{cumene}} = 120.19$ | mol | | 2 | $\text{Conversion (X)} = \dfrac{F_{\text{in}} - F_{\text{out}}}{F_{\text{in}}}$ | Fractional conversion in CSTR/PFR | dimensionless | | 3 | $\text{Selectivity} = \dfrac{\text{moles of desired product}}{\text{moles of reactant consumed}}$ | Selectivity to CHP in Stage 2 | dimensionless | | 4 | $\text{Yield} = X \times S$ | Overall yield to product | dimensionless | | 5 | $r_{\text{CHP}} = k \cdot P_{\text{O}_2} \cdot [\text{Cumene}]^{0.5}$ | Empirical rate of CHP formation | mol L⁻¹ s⁻¹ | | 6 | $\text{Phenol:Acetone} = 1:1$ | Stoichiometric molar co-product ratio | mol/mol | | 7 | $\text{Atom Economy} = \dfrac{M_{\text{phenol}} + M_{\text{acetone}}}{M_{\text{cumene}} + M_{\text{O}_2}} \times 100$ | Green-chemistry metric | % | | 8 | $M_{\text{theoretical phenol}} = \dfrac{94.11}{120.19} \times m_{\text{cumene}}$ | Theoretical phenol mass from cumene | g | > [!TIP] > **KTU High-Yield Tip:** Questions on **atom economy (Eq. 7)** and **co-product stoichiometry (Eq. 6)** are favourite Part A (3-mark) questions. Memorise the molecular weights — Phenol = 94.11 g/mol, Acetone = 58.08 g/mol, Cumene = 120.19 g/mol, O₂ = 32.00 g/mol. # 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Worked Example — Mass Balance for Phenol Production > **Problem:** A plant processes **1000 kg/h** of cumene with **30% per-pass conversion** in the oxidiser. If selectivity to CHP is **92%** and cleavage conversion is **95%** with **100% selectivity** to phenol/acetone, calculate: > (a) Molar flow of cumene fed. > (b) Moles of CHP produced per hour. > (c) Theoretical mass of phenol and acetone obtained per hour. > (d) Atom economy of the overall Hock process. ### (a) Molar Flow of Cumene Fed $$F_{\text{cumene}} = \dfrac{m}{M} = \dfrac{1000 \;\text{kg/h}}{120.19 \;\text{g/mol}} = \dfrac{1{,}000{,}000 \;\text{g/h}}{120.19 \;\text{g/mol}}$$ $$F_{\text{cumene}} = 8319.84 \;\text{mol/h}$$ ### (b) Moles of CHP Produced per Hour Conversion of cumene in oxidiser: $X = 0.30$; Selectivity to CHP: $S = 0.92$. $$n_{\text{CHP}} = F_{\text{cumene}} \times X \times S = 8319.84 \times 0.30 \times 0.92$$ $$n_{\text{CHP}} = 2296.28 \;\text{mol/h}$$ ### (c) Theoretical Mass of Phenol and Acetone Stoichiometry (1 mol CHP → 1 mol phenol + 1 mol acetone) with cleavage conversion $X_c = 0.95$: $$n_{\text{phenol}} = n_{\text{acetone}} = n_{\text{CHP}} \times X_c = 2296.28 \times 0.95 = 2181.47 \;\text{mol/h}$$ **Mass of Phenol:** $$m_{\text{phenol}} = n_{\text{phenol}} \times M_{\text{phenol}} = 2181.47 \times 94.11 = 2.053 \times 10^5 \;\text{g/h} \approx 205.3 \;\text{kg/h}$$ **Mass of Acetone:** $$m_{\text{acetone}} = n_{\text{acetone}} \times M_{\text{acetone}} = 2181.47 \times 58.08 = 1.267 \times 10^5 \;\text{g/h} \approx 126.7 \;\text{kg/h}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Verification of 1:1 molar ratio:** $n_{\text{phenol}} / n_{\text{acetone}} = 2181.47 / 2181.47 = 1.000$ ✓ (matches Eq. 6 from the Formula Sheet). ### (d) Atom Economy of the Overall Hock Process Net reaction (summing the three stages): $$\text{Cumene} + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{Phenol} + \text{Acetone}$$ $$\text{C}_9\text{H}_{12} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{C}_6\text{H}_6\text{O} + \text{C}_3\text{H}_6\text{O}$$ Mass of useful products: $$M_{\text{phenol}} + M_{\text{acetone}} = 94.11 + 58.08 = 152.19 \;\text{g/mol}$$ Mass of all reactants: $$M_{\text{cumene}} + M_{\text{O}_2} = 120.19 + 32.00 = 152.19 \;\text{g/mol}$$ $$\text{Atom Economy} = \dfrac{152.19}{152.19} \times 100 = 100\%$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Wow-Factor:** The Cumene/Hock process is one of the very few large-scale industrial reactions with a **theoretical atom economy of 100%** — every atom of the cumene and oxygen ends up in a useful product. Examiners love this green-chemistry highlight. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Reaction Conditions Summary | Parameter | Stage 1: Alkylation | Stage 2: Oxidation | Stage 3: Cleavage | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Catalyst** | SPA or Zeolite | None (radical initiator) | H₂SO₄ (dilute) | | **Temperature** | 230–250 °C | 100–130 °C | 50–80 °C | | **Pressure** | 30–40 bar | 1–5 bar | 1 atm (liquid) | | **Phase** | Liquid (fixed bed) | Liquid (bubble column) | Liquid (CSTR) | | **Key Hazard** | Polyalkylation | **Explosive CHP buildup** | Exothermic runaway | | **Conversion/pass** | 60–95% | **25–30% (deliberately limited)** | 90–95% | | **Main By-product** | Diisopropylbenzene (DIPB) | DMPC, AMS | Acetophenone, AMS | | **Selectivity target** | > 95% (cumene) | > 90% (CHP) | > 99% (phenol) | # 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Overall Process Flow — Benzene + Propylene → Phenol + Acetone ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Benzene Feed
C6H6] --> R1 B[Propylene Feed
C3H6] --> R1 C[Recycle Benzene] --> R1 R1[Reactor 1
Alkylation
SPA Zeolite
230-250 C] --> D1[Distillation 1
Unreacted Benzene Recycle] D1 --> C D1 --> D2[Distillation 2
Cumene Purification] D2 --> R2[Reactor 2
Oxidation
Air O2
100-130 C] R2 --> R3[Reactor 3
Cleavage
H2SO4 Catalyst
50-80 C] R3 --> D3[Distillation 3
Phenol Column] R3 --> D4[Distillation 4
Acetone Column] D3 --> P1[Product 1
Phenol C6H5OH
99 percent pure] D4 --> P2[Product 2
Acetone CH3COCH3
99.5 percent pure] P1 --> APP[Applications
Bisphenol A
Phenolic Resins
Caprolactam] P2 --> APP2[Applications
MMA, BPA Solvent
Methyl Isobutyl Ketone] ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Reaction Network ```mermaid flowchart TD F1[Stage 1 Feed
Benzene Propylene] --> S1[Stage 1
Friedel-Crafts
Alkylation] S1 --> INT1[Intermediate
Cumene C9H12] INT1 --> S2[Stage 2
Radical Oxidation
Air Initiator] S2 --> INT2[Intermediate
Cumene Hydroperoxide CHP] INT2 --> S3[Stage 3
Hock Rearrangement
Acid Cleavage] S3 --> OUT1[Output A
Phenol] S3 --> OUT2[Output B
Acetone] INT1 --> SP1[Side Product
DIPB recycled to transalkylation] INT2 --> SP2[Side Product
DMPC AMS] S3 --> SP3[Side Product
Acetophenone] OUT1 --> ECON[Co-product Economics
Acetone oversupply
impacts phenol pricing] OUT2 --> ECON ``` ## 4.3 Schematic — Hock Cleavage Mechanism (Criegee Intermediate) ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[CHP Molecule
PhCCH32OOH] --> A2[Protonation
of OOH group] A2 --> A3[Loss of Water
H2O leaves] A3 --> A4[1,2 Phenyl Migration
Criegee Intermediate] A4 --> A5[Carbonyl Formation
C=O bond] A5 --> A6A[Phenol Product
C6H5OH] A5 --> A6B[Acetone Product
CH3COCH3] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **"Define cumene. List any two industrial applications of cumene other than phenol manufacture."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** Cumene is isopropylbenzene (C₆H₅CH(CH₃)₂), an aromatic hydrocarbon manufactured by Friedel–Crafts alkylation of benzene with propylene. - **Applications (any 2 × 1 mark each):** 1. Solvent for paints, lacquers, and enamels. 2. Component of high-octane aviation fuel (> 100 octane rating). 3. Raw material for α-methylstyrene (AMS) and dicumyl peroxide. 4. Thinner for printing inks and epoxy resins. **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **"Write the three main reaction steps involved in the manufacture of phenol from cumene."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Alkylation:** C₆H₆ + CH₂=CHCH₃ → C₆H₅CH(CH₃)₂ *(Catalyst: H₃PO₄/zeolite; 230–250 °C)* 2. **Oxidation:** C₆H₅CH(CH₃)₂ + O₂ → C₆H₅C(CH₃)₂OOH *(Air, 100–130 °C, free-radical)* 3. **Cleavage (Hock):** C₆H₅C(CH₃)₂OOH → C₆H₅OH + (CH₃)₂CO *(H₂SO₄, 50–80 °C)* ## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks) ### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — KTU Model Question `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3, Set A]` **(a)** Explain in detail the **catalysts, reaction conditions, and mechanism** of the alkylation of benzene with propylene to produce cumene. Compare **SPA (solid phosphoric acid)** and **zeolite-based** catalysts. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the **oxidation of cumene to cumene hydroperoxide (CHP)** with a focus on the **free-radical mechanism**, the reasons for **limiting conversion to 25–30%**, and the engineering hazards involved. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Q (a) - **Friedel–Crafts Alkylation Overview (2 marks):** Electrophilic aromatic substitution; propylene is activated by the acid catalyst to form a *sec*-propyl carbocation (or surface-bound alkoxy), which attacks the benzene ring. - **SPA Catalyst (2 marks):** Phosphoric acid impregnated on kieselguhr/SiO₂; operates at **230–250 °C and 30–40 bar**; homogeneous phosphoric acid film on the solid support is the active phase; issues — corrosion, phosphate loss, disposal of spent catalyst. - **Zeolite Catalysts (2 marks):** Medium-pore (ZSM-5) and large-pore (β-zeolite, MCM-22) molecular sieves provide shape selectivity; advantages — non-corrosive, regenerable, environmentally clean, longer catalyst life, higher cumene selectivity (> 99%). - **Process Comparison (1 mark):** Zeolites are the modern preferred catalyst due to higher selectivity, lower energy, and zero acid disposal cost (UOP Q-Max, Dow/Kellogg K-Process). #### Model Solution — Q (b) - **Free-Radical Chain Mechanism (3 marks):** - *Initiation:* ROOR → 2 RO•; R• + Cumene → R–H + Cumyl• (resonance-stabilised) - *Propagation:* Cumyl• + O₂ → Cumyl–OO•; Cumyl–OO• + Cumene → CHP + Cumyl• - *Termination:* Radical coupling (Cumyl–OO• + Cumyl–OO• → non-radical products). - **Why 25–30% Conversion Limit (2 marks):** 1. CHP is thermally unstable and shock-sensitive; higher conversion increases runaway explosion risk. 2. Side reactions (DMPC, AMS, acetophenone) escalate at higher CHP concentrations. 3. Multi-stage reactors with inter-stage concentration allow safe operation. - **Engineering Hazards (2 marks):** Auto-acceleration, peroxide accumulation, oxygen flammability envelope; mitigated by N₂ dilution, concentration control, and emergency dump systems. ### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3, Set B]` **(a)** Discuss the **Hock rearrangement (cleavage step)** for converting cumene hydroperoxide to phenol and acetone, including the **Criegee intermediate mechanism** and acid catalyst selection. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A plant feeds **1500 kg/h** of cumene (M = 120.19 g/mol) to an oxidation reactor at **28% conversion** with **90% selectivity** to CHP. The cleavage reactor operates at **94% conversion** with **100% selectivity** to phenol. Calculate: (i) the **mass of phenol produced per hour** and (ii) the **overall atom economy** of the process. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Q (a) - **Hock Cleavage Overview (2 marks):** Acid-catalysed heterolytic O–O bond cleavage of CHP under aqueous conditions (50–80 °C) to give a **1:1 molar ratio of phenol and acetone**; catalysed by dilute H₂SO₄ (5–20%) or solid acids (resins, zeolites). - **Criegee Mechanism (3 marks):** (1) Protonation of –OOH oxygen; (2) Loss of H₂O to form a 3-membered transition state; (3) Concerted **1,2-phenyl migration** from carbon to the electron-deficient oxygen (Criegee intermediate); (4) Collapse to form phenol (–OH on aromatic) and acetone (C=O from migration). - **Catalyst Selection (2 marks):** Dilute H₂SO₄ is traditional but corrosive; modern plants use **solid acid catalysts** (e.g., Nafion, ion-exchange resins, sulfonated polymers) for easier product separation and lower waste. #### Model Solution — Q (b) **Given:** $m_{\text{cumene}} = 1500 \;\text{kg/h}$, $M_{\text{cumene}} = 120.19 \;\text{g/mol}$, $X_{\text{ox}} = 0.28$, $S_{\text{CHP}} = 0.90$, $X_{\text{cl}} = 0.94$. **Step 1 — Molar flow of cumene:** $$F_{\text{cumene}} = \dfrac{1{,}500{,}000 \;\text{g/h}}{120.19 \;\text{g/mol}} = 12{,}479.74 \;\text{mol/h}$$ **Step 2 — Moles of CHP formed:** $$n_{\text{CHP}} = 12{,}479.74 \times 0.28 \times 0.90 = 3144.89 \;\text{mol/h}$$ **Step 3 — Moles of phenol produced (1:1 stoichiometry):** $$n_{\text{phenol}} = 3144.89 \times 0.94 = 2956.20 \;\text{mol/h}$$ **Step 4 — Mass of phenol:** $$m_{\text{phenol}} = 2956.20 \times 94.11 = 2.782 \times 10^5 \;\text{g/h} \approx 278.2 \;\text{kg/h}$$ **Step 5 — Overall Atom Economy:** Net reaction: C₉H₁₂ + O₂ → C₆H₆O + C₃H₆O $$M_{\text{useful}} = 94.11 + 58.08 = 152.19 \;\text{g/mol}$$ $$M_{\text{reactants}} = 120.19 + 32.00 = 152.19 \;\text{g/mol}$$ $$\text{Atom Economy} = \dfrac{152.19}{152.19} \times 100 = 100\%$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:** > 1. **Confusing 1:1 mass ratio with 1:1 mole ratio:** Phenol and acetone are produced in **1:1 MOLAR ratio** but **different mass ratio** (94.11 : 58.08 ≈ 1.62 : 1). Examiners deduct 1 mark for stating "1:1 by mass." > 2. **Skipping the unreacted recycle:** In Stage 2, only 25–30% cumene is converted; the **unreacted cumene is recycled** back. Many students forget this and compute yields incorrectly. > 3. **Ignoring CHP explosion hazard:** Always mention that CHP is a **shock-sensitive peroxide** requiring temperature control, nitrogen blanketing, and concentration limits. Examiners expect this safety discussion in any 7-mark question on Stage 2. > 4. **Not stating catalyst regeneration:** For zeolite catalysts, you must state they are **regenerable** (oxidative coke-burn-off cycles every 6–12 months). This is a frequently-tested point. > 5. **Atom economy mis-calculation:** Do not include the **benzene + propylene** reactants in atom economy if asked about the **net Hock process**; the question usually wants the *cumene + O₂ → phenol + acetone* net reaction. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Cumene = Isopropylbenzene (C₉H₁₂, MW = 120.19 g/mol)** — a benzene ring with an isopropyl substituent. - **Three industrial stages:** (1) Friedel–Crafts alkylation (SPA/zeolite), (2) Radical air oxidation (100–130 °C), (3) Hock acid cleavage (H₂SO₄). - **Phenol : Acetone molar ratio is 1 : 1** (by stoichiometry of CHP cleavage). - **Atom economy of net reaction = 100%** — every atom in cumene + O₂ ends up in useful products. - **Conversion in Stage 2 is deliberately limited to 25–30%** to prevent CHP explosion and side-product formation. - **Unreacted cumene is recycled** to the alkylator — single-pass conversion ≠ overall conversion. - **Modern catalysts:** Zeolites (ZSM-5, β, MCM-22) have replaced corrosive SPA/AlCl₃ — clean, regenerable, high selectivity. - **Co-product economics:** Acetone oversupply (due to phenol demand) affects global acetone pricing — a key downstream impact. - **Side products:** Dicumyl peroxide, α-methylstyrene (AMS), acetophenone, DMPC — recovered and sold to fine-chemicals market. - **Global production:** ~7 million tonnes/year of cumene (2024 data) for ~95% of world phenol capacity. - **Major processes:** UOP Q-Max, Dow/Kellogg K-Cumene, Mobil/Badger, Polimeri Europa — all use zeolite catalysis. - **Engineering hazards:** CHP is a **Class 1 peroxide** (shock-sensitive); mitigated by concentration control, N₂ blanket, dump tanks. - **Memorise molecular weights:** Phenol = 94.11, Acetone = 58.08, Cumene = 120.19, O₂ = 32.00. - **Criegee mechanism** = concerted 1,2-phenyl migration + O–O cleavage; tested frequently as 3-mark short note.

topics.title: Acrylic Acid
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT741 - PETROCHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Acrylic Acid

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Acrylic Acid** (IUPAC: **Prop-2-enoic acid**; systematic name: **Acrylic acid**) is the **simplest unsaturated monocarboxylic acid**, possessing a vinyl group (–CH=CH₂) directly bonded to a carboxylic acid functional group (–COOH). Its molecular formula is **C₃H₄O₂** (molar mass = **72.06 g/mol**).

The structural formula is:

$$CH_2 = CH - COOH$$

It is a **C3 platform petrochemical** that sits at the intersection of propylene chemistry and acrylate esters, and is one of the highest-volume specialty monomers produced globally.

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Molecular Formula | **C₃H₄O₂** |
| Molar Mass | **72.06 g·mol⁻¹** |
| Melting Point | **13 °C (286 K)** |
| Boiling Point | **141 °C (414 K)** |
| Density (at 20 °C) | **1.051 g·cm⁻³** |
| Flash Point (closed cup) | **~ 50 °C** |
| Autoignition Temp. | **~ 390 °C** |
| pKa (acid dissociation) | **4.25** |
| Appearance | Colorless, **pungent, acrid liquid** |
| Solubility in water | **Miscible in all proportions** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Acrylic acid is listed as a *key downstream derivative of propylene* and is examined in the context of (a) industrial manufacturing routes, (b) oxidation catalysis, and (c) downstream polymer/ester applications.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Reactive Cousin of Vinegar"

Imagine ordinary **vinegar (acetic acid, CH₃COOH)** — a stable, mild kitchen acid used in salads. Now, replace one of vinegar's hydrogen atoms on the α-carbon with a **second point of unsaturation** (a C=C double bond). That single modification makes the molecule a *reactive powerhouse* — the C=C bond is hungry to polymerize, to be attacked by nucleophiles, and to chain-react. This is exactly **acrylic acid**: structurally, it is **vinegar that has grown a "reactive arm" (vinyl group)** sticking out of it.

That "reactive arm" is the entire industrial reason for the molecule's existence. It enables:
- **Free-radical polymerization** → superabsorbent polymers (SAP), polyacrylates
- **Esterification** → acrylate esters (methyl, ethyl, butyl, 2-ethylhexyl acrylates) used in paints, adhesives, and textiles
- **Salt formation** → sodium polyacrylate (used in diapers!)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight (CO-1 mapping):** The presence of the α,β-unsaturation (conjugated C=C with –COOH) makes acrylic acid a *Michael acceptor*, accounting for > 95% of its commercial reactivity.

## 1.3 Industrial Position of Acrylic Acid

Acrylic acid ranks among the **top 30 highest-volume organic chemicals** worldwide, with a global production exceeding **8 million metric tons/year (2024)**. It is the **single largest outlet for propylene** after polypropylene and acrylonitrile.

| Outlet / Use | Approx. Share |
|---|---|
| **Superabsorbent polymers (SAP)** for hygiene products | **~ 35–40%** |
| **Acrylate esters** (coatings, adhesives, textiles) | **~ 45–50%** |
| Polyacrylic acid (water treatment, dispersants) | **~ 5%** |
| Detergent builders, Iminodisuccinate, Specialty chemicals | **~ 5–10%** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Industrial Manufacturing Routes — A Layered Hierarchy

Acrylic acid is commercially produced via three principal routes. The dominant modern route is the **two-stage vapor-phase catalytic oxidation of propylene**, historically known as the **"Acrolein–Acrylic Acid" process**.

### 2.1.1 Route 1 — Propylene Oxidation (Two-Stage, Vapor Phase) — *The Workhorse*

This route dominates worldwide and accounts for **> 90% of global production**. Propylene is oxidized in **two sequential catalytic fixed-bed reactors**:

**Stage 1 — Acrolein Formation (Oxidative Dehydrogenation of Propylene)**

$$CH_2 = CH - CH_3 + O_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Mo–Bi–O (multi-metal oxide)}} CH_2 = CH - CHO + H_2O$$

Catalyst: **Bismuth molybdate** (Bi₂O₃·MoO₃) promoted with Co, Fe, Ni, K, P, etc. (multicomponent oxides).
Temperature: **300 – 370 °C**, pressure: **1 – 3 atm**.

**Stage 2 — Acrolein → Acrylic Acid (Oxidation of the Aldehyde)**

$$CH_2 = CH - CHO + \tfrac{1}{2}\,O_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Mo–V–O (mixed oxide)}} CH_2 = CH - COOH$$

Catalyst: **Vanadium-molybdenum mixed oxide** (Mo-V-W-Cu system) supported on TiO₂/SiO₂.
Temperature: **250 – 320 °C**, pressure: **1 – 3 atm**.

**Overall Reaction:**

$$CH_2 = CH - CH_3 + \tfrac{3}{2}\,O_2 \longrightarrow CH_2 = CH - COOH + H_2O \qquad \Delta H \approx -594 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

The overall process is **highly exothermic** → requires careful reactor cooling, typically using molten-salt bath or boiling-water-cooled tubular reactors.

### 2.1.2 Route 2 — Reppe Process (Historical / Reppe Carbonylation)

Historically, the **Reppe process** (BASF, 1950s) used **acetylene, CO, and water** in the presence of a **nickel carbonyl / nickel bromide catalyst** to give acrylic acid:

$$C_2H_2 + CO + H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{Ni(CO)}_4 / \text{NiBr}_2} CH_2 = CH - COOH$$

> [!NOTE]
> The Reppe route is now largely **obsolete** in the West due to (a) toxic Ni(CO)₄ catalyst handling, (b) high-pressure requirements (≥ 100 bar), and (c) shift of feedstock economics to propylene. It is, however, still in use in **limited Chinese units** where cheap coal-derived acetylene is available.

### 2.1.3 Route 3 — Direct Oxidation of Propane (PDO — Research/Emerging)

A single-step route: **propane + O₂ → acrylic acid**, without isolating propylene. This is the holy grail of acrylic acid research because propane is **cheaper** than propylene.

$$C_3H_8 + 2\,O_2 \longrightarrow CH_2 = CH - COOH + 2\,H_2O$$

The challenge is **poor selectivity** (typically 30–50%), as propane is harder to activate than propylene. Pilot plants using **VPO / Mo-V-Te-Nb-O** catalysts have been demonstrated (e.g., Rohm & Haas, Arkema), but full commercial-scale units are still limited.

### 2.1.4 Route 4 — Bio-Based / Glycerol Route (Green Chemistry)

Crude glycerol (a biodiesel byproduct) can be dehydrated to acrolein, then oxidized to acrylic acid:

$$C_3H_8O_3 \xrightarrow{-\text{H}_2\text{O}} CH_2 = CH - CHO \xrightarrow{+\tfrac{1}{2}O_2} CH_2 = CH - COOH$$

This route has been commercialized (e.g., **BASF / Novozymes partnership**), and is **renewable, carbon-neutral**, and the subject of growing KTU-level discussion in the *green petrochemicals* domain.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept / Quantity | Formula / Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Conversion (X)** | $X = \dfrac{n_{\text{reacted}}}{n_{\text{fed}}} \times 100\%$ | % | Per-pass conversion of propylene |
| 2 | **Selectivity (S)** | $S = \dfrac{n_{\text{desired product formed}}}{n_{\text{reactant consumed}}} \times 100\%$ | % | % of reactant → desired product |
| 3 | **Yield (Y)** | $Y = X \times S$ (also $Y = \dfrac{\text{moles of product}}{\text{theoretical max}} \times 100\%$) | % | Overall performance metric |
| 4 | **Overall Reaction** | $C_3H_6 + \tfrac{3}{2}O_2 \to C_3H_4O_2 + H_2O$ | — | ΔH ≈ –594 kJ·mol⁻¹ |
| 5 | **Space Velocity (GHSV)** | $\text{GHSV} = \dfrac{\dot{V}_{\text{feed}}}{\text{Volume of catalyst}} \times \text{h}^{-1}$ | h⁻¹ | Typical: 1500–3000 h⁻¹ |
| 6 | **Residence Time (τ)** | $\tau = \dfrac{V_{\text{reactor}}}{\dot{V}_{\text{feed}}}$ | s | 0.3 – 1.0 s typical |
| 7 | **Acid Dissociation** | $K_a = \dfrac{[H^+][A^-]}{[HA]}$; pKa = **4.25** | — | Stronger than acetic acid (pKa 4.76) |
| 8 | **Molar Mass** | $C_3H_4O_2$ → **72.06 g·mol⁻¹** | g·mol⁻¹ | C: 50.00 %, H: 5.59 %, O: 44.41 % |
| 9 | **Acid Number / SAP Value** | $\text{AN} = \dfrac{56.1 \times V_{\text{KOH}} \times N}{m_{\text{sample}}}$ | mg KOH·g⁻¹ | Used for quality control |
| 10 | **Polymerization Enthalpy** | $\Delta H_{\text{poly}} \approx -77 \text{ kJ·mol}^{-1}$ | kJ·mol⁻¹ | Heat must be removed in bulk polymerization |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility (Real-World):** The **yield equation Y = X × S** is the single most-used relationship for reactor optimization in petrochemical plants. A 1% increase in selectivity on a 200,000 tpy plant translates to **~ 2,000 t/year of additional acrylic acid** — a multi-million-dollar revenue impact. KTU questions frequently test conversion/selectivity/yield arithmetic.

## 2.3 Reactor Design & Process Flow — The Two-Stage Plant

The commercial plant consists of the following sequential unit operations:

1. **Feed Mixing** — Fresh propylene + recycled off-gas + air (or pure O₂).
2. **Reactor 1 (Acrolein Reactor)** — Fixed-bed multitubular with molten-salt cooling.
3. **Reactor 2 (Acrylic Acid Reactor)** — Fixed-bed multitubular, lower temperature.
4. **Absorption Column** — Quench with water → crude aqueous acrylic acid (typically 50–60 wt%).
5. **Stripping / Extractive Distillation** — Removal of light ends (acrolein, acetic acid).
6. **Purification** — Vacuum distillation → glacial acrylic acid (≥ 99.5 wt% purity).
7. **Inhibitor Dosing** — **MEHQ (mono-methyl ether of hydroquinone) ~ 200 ppm** is added to prevent runaway polymerization.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is MEHQ critical?** Acrylic acid polymerizes **spontaneously and exothermically** even at room temperature. Without an inhibitor, bulk storage and distillation columns can undergo **thermal runaway polymerization**, leading to **catastrophic vessel rupture** (a known industrial incident class). MEHQ is "switched off" only when the end user deliberately adds an initiator (e.g., peroxide) at the polymerization site.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Stoichiometric & Mass-Balanced Worked Example

**Problem (KTU Pattern):** A propylene oxidation plant feeds **1000 kg/h of propylene (C₃H₆)** with air in stoichiometric ratio. Conversion per pass is **95%**, selectivity to acrolein in the first reactor is **85%**, and acrolein → acrylic acid selectivity in the second reactor is **90%**. Compute:
(a) The mass of acrylic acid produced per hour.
(b) The overall yield with respect to propylene.
(c) The mass of water produced per hour.

### Solution

**Given:**
- Propylene feed: $\dot{m}_{C_3H_6} = 1000$ kg/h
- Molar mass of propylene: $M_{C_3H_6} = 42.08$ g·mol⁻¹
- Molar mass of acrylic acid: $M_{AA} = 72.06$ g·mol⁻¹
- Molar mass of water: $M_{H_2O} = 18.02$ g·mol⁻¹
- Conversion: $X = 95\%$
- Selectivity Stage 1 (C₃H₆ → acrolein): $S_1 = 85\%$
- Selectivity Stage 2 (acrolein → AA): $S_2 = 90\%$

**Step 1: Convert propylene feed to molar flow**

$$\dot{n}_{C_3H_6} = \dfrac{1000 \text{ kg/h}}{42.08 \text{ kg/kmol}} = 23.76 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 2: Moles of propylene reacted per pass**

$$\dot{n}_{\text{reacted}} = X \times \dot{n}_{C_3H_6} = 0.95 \times 23.76 = 22.57 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 3: Moles of acrolein formed (Stage 1)**

$$\dot{n}_{\text{acrolein}} = \dot{n}_{\text{reacted}} \times S_1 = 22.57 \times 0.85 = 19.18 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 4: Moles of acrylic acid formed (Stage 2)**

From the second reaction: $C_3H_4O + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \to C_3H_4O_2$, stoichiometry is **1:1** (1 mol acrolein → 1 mol AA).

$$\dot{n}_{AA} = \dot{n}_{\text{acrolein}} \times S_2 = 19.18 \times 0.90 = 17.27 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 5 (a): Mass of acrylic acid produced per hour**

$$\dot{m}_{AA} = 17.27 \text{ kmol/h} \times 72.06 \text{ kg/kmol} = \boxed{1244.0 \text{ kg/h}}$$

**Step 6 (b): Overall yield with respect to propylene**

$$Y = X \times S_1 \times S_2 = 0.95 \times 0.85 \times 0.90 = \boxed{0.7268 = 72.68\%}$$

**Step 7 (c): Mass of water produced per hour**

The overall reaction produces **1 mol H₂O per mol C₃H₆ reacted**, regardless of the AA selectivity pathway (the COOH oxygen comes from O₂, but the second O in H₂O also comes from O₂ — net water is always 1 mol per mol C₃H₆ fully converted to AA). However, the byproducts also consume oxygen and produce CO₂ + H₂O. The simplest KT-level interpretation is **1:1 mol H₂O per mol propylene reacted** (since the dominant desired pathway is propylene → AA + H₂O):

$$\dot{n}_{H_2O} = 22.57 \text{ kmol/h}$$

$$\dot{m}_{H_2O} = 22.57 \times 18.02 = \boxed{406.7 \text{ kg/h}}$$

**Step 8: Verification (overall mass balance check)**

| Stream | Molar flow (kmol/h) | Mass flow (kg/h) |
|---|---|---|
| C₃H₆ in | 23.76 | 1000.0 |
| C₃H₆ unreacted (5%) | 1.19 | 50.0 |
| AA out | 17.27 | 1244.0 |
| H₂O out (dominant) | 22.57 | 406.7 |

Mass balance sanity check: products + unreacted reactants must equal feed. Note that the **oxygen from air** is the *additional* mass entering the reactor (not shown here). In a closed balance, O₂ input (mass) must be accounted for. ✅

## 3.2 Acid Dissociation Constant Derivation

Acrylic acid (HA) dissociates in water:

$$HA \rightleftharpoons H^+ + A^-$$

$$K_a = \dfrac{[H^+][A^-]}{[HA]}$$

For a **0.10 M** solution of acrylic acid (pKa = 4.25):

$$K_a = 10^{-4.25} = 5.62 \times 10^{-5}$$

Setting $x = [H^+] = [A^-]$ and $[HA] = 0.10 - x$:

$$5.62 \times 10^{-5} = \dfrac{x^2}{0.10 - x}$$

Solving (assuming $x \ll 0.10$):

$$x^2 = 5.62 \times 10^{-6} \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = 2.37 \times 10^{-3} \text{ M}$$

$$pH = -\log(2.37 \times 10^{-3}) = \boxed{2.63}$$

This pH ≈ 2.63 confirms that acrylic acid is a **moderately strong organic acid** — significantly stronger than acetic acid (pH of 0.10 M acetic acid ≈ 2.87).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Manufacturing Routes

| Criterion | Propylene 2-Stage Oxidation | Reppe (Acetylene) | Propane Direct | Glycerol (Bio) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Feedstock cost** | Moderate (propylene) | High (acetylene) | Low (propane) | Variable (crude glycerol) |
| **Operating pressure** | 1–3 atm | 100–200 atm | 1–3 atm | 1–3 atm |
| **Selectivity to AA** | **85–90%** | ~ 80% | 30–50% | 70–80% |
| **Catalyst** | Mo-Bi / Mo-V oxides | Ni(CO)₄ / NiBr₂ | VPO / MoVTeNb | Heteropoly acids |
| **Commercial status** | **Dominant (1990+)** | Obsolete in West | Pilot | Emerging |
| **Environmental impact** | Moderate | High (Ni toxicity) | Low (but low yield) | **Lowest (renewable)** |
| **Capital cost** | High | High | High | Moderate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip (CO-3 / CO-4 mapping):** For a 14-mark question, structure your answer as: (i) reaction stoichiometry, (ii) reactor design choice, (iii) catalyst rationale, (iv) economic comparison — **in that order**. Examiners reward *layered* answers.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Overall Plant Block Flow Diagram (Two-Stage Propylene Oxidation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Propylene Feed] --> MX1[Feed Mixer]
    A2[Recycle Off-Gas] --> MX1
    A3[Air or Pure O2] --> MX1
    MX1 --> R1[Reactor 1: Acrolein Formation\nMo-Bi-O Catalyst\n300-370 C]
    R1 --> R2[Reactor 2: Acrolein to AA\nMo-V-O Catalyst\n250-320 C]
    R2 --> AB[Absorber\nAqueous AA 50-60 wt%]
    AB --> ST[Stripper\nLight Ends Removal]
    ST --> ED[Extractive Distillation]
    ED --> PR[Purification Column\nVacuum Distillation]
    PR --> FG[Glacial Acrylic Acid\n99.5 percent purity]
    FG --> IB[Inhibitor Dosing\nMEHQ 200 ppm]
    IB --> TK[Product Storage Tank]
    AB -- wastewater --> WW[Wastewater Treatment]
    ST -- light ends --> LE[Light Ends Recovery]
    R1 -- vent --> SC[Scrubber]
    R2 -- vent --> SC
```

## 4.2 Process Hierarchy & Catalytic Stage Breakdown

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Propylene C3H6] --> P1[Stage 1 Oxidation]
    P1 --> P2[Acrolein CH2 CH CHO]
    P2 --> P3[Stage 2 Oxidation]
    P3 --> P4[Acrylic Acid CH2 CH COOH]
    P4 --> P5[Esterification with Alcohols]
    P4 --> P6[Free Radical Polymerization]
    P4 --> P7[Salt Formation Sodium Polyacrylate]
    P5 --> P5a[Methyl Acrylate]
    P5 --> P5b[Ethyl Acrylate]
    P5 --> P5c[Butyl Acrylate]
    P5 --> P5d[2-Ethylhexyl Acrylate]
    P6 --> P6a[Superabsorbent Polymer SAP]
    P6 --> P6b[Polyacrylic Acid PAA]
    P7 --> P7a[Diaper Hygiene Products]
    P7 --> P7b[Water Treatment Chemicals]
```

## 4.3 Polymerization Mechanism (Schematic of Free-Radical Addition)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M0[Initiator RO OR Peroxide] --> I1[Radical R]
    I1 --> I2[Chain Start\nR-CH2-CH-COOH]
    I2 --> I3[Chain Propagation\nR CH2 CH COOH n units]
    I3 --> I4[Chain Termination\nCoupling or Disproportionation]
    I3 --> I5[Chain Transfer\nto Monomer or Solvent]
    P1[Acrylic Acid Monomer] --> I2
    P1 --> I3
    P1 --> I4
    P1 --> I5
```

## 4.4 Product Distribution Hierarchy (Acrylic Acid Downstream)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    AA[Acrylic Acid Monomer] --> APP[Polymerization Route]
    AA --> AES[Esterification Route]
    AA --> SAL[Salt Neutralization Route]
    APP --> SAP[Superabsorbent Polymer]
    APP --> PAA[Polyacrylic Acid]
    APP --> ACR[Acrylic Resins]
    AES --> MEA[Methyl Acrylate]
    AES --> ETA[Ethyl Acrylate]
    AES --> BTA[Butyl Acrylate]
    AES --> EHA[2-Ethylhexyl Acrylate]
    SAL --> NAP[Sodium Polyacrylate]
    SAL --> KAP[Potassium Polyacrylate]
    SAL --> AMP[Ammonium Polyacrylate]
    SAP --> DIA[Baby Diapers Hygiene]
    SAP --> AGR[Agriculture Water Retention]
    SAP --> CAB[Cable Water Blocking]
    PAA --> WTR[Water Treatment Scale Inhibitor]
    ACR --> CTG[Coatings Paints]
    ACR --> ADH[Pressure Sensitive Adhesives]
    MEA --> TXT[Textile Finishing]
    ETA --> CTG
    BTA --> CTG
    EHA --> ADH
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for Examiners:** The **"SAP → Diapers"** link is the single most-asked acrylic-acid application question in KTU 2024 papers. Memorize that a single SAP gram can absorb **~ 30 grams of water** (or 30 mL of saline) — this is what makes diapers *superabsorbent*.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]

**Define Acrylic Acid. Write its molecular formula, IUPAC name, and any two major industrial applications.**

**Model Answer:**

**Acrylic Acid** is the simplest unsaturated monocarboxylic acid, having a vinyl group directly attached to a carboxyl group. It is commercially the most important C3-oxygenate petrochemical.

- **Molecular Formula:** C₃H₄O₂
- **IUPAC Name:** Prop-2-enoic acid
- **Molar Mass:** 72.06 g·mol⁻¹

**Two major applications:**
1. **Superabsorbent polymers (SAP):** Sodium polyacrylate, used in baby diapers, adult incontinence products, and agricultural water-retention sheets (~ 35–40% of global consumption).
2. **Acrylate esters:** Methyl, ethyl, butyl, and 2-ethylhexyl acrylates, used in coatings, paints, adhesives, and textile finishes (~ 45–50% of global consumption).

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula/IUPAC, 1 for applications)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]

**Why is MEHQ (monomethyl ether of hydroquinone) added to commercially stored acrylic acid? Mention the typical dosage level.**

**Model Answer:**

Acrylic acid is a **highly reactive monomer** that can undergo **spontaneous free-radical polymerization** even at room temperature. In bulk storage, distillation columns, or transport tanks, this polymerization is **exothermic** (~ –77 kJ/mol) and can lead to **thermal runaway** — a well-documented cause of industrial vessel rupture accidents.

**MEHQ (hydroquinone monomethyl ether)** is added as a **polymerization inhibitor** to suppress this unwanted reaction. It functions by **scavenging free radicals** (donating H• to a propagating radical, terminating the chain). Typical dosage: **~ 200 ppm** in commercial glacial acrylic acid.

The inhibitor is **not consumed in storage** because of low radical flux; it is only "switched off" when the end user deliberately adds a **peroxide initiator** at the polymerization site.

*(3 marks: 1 for problem, 1 for inhibitor function, 1 for dosage and reversibility)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks each) with Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module Rule:** For Module 3 questions (14 marks), examiners expect answers to be structured as: **Introduction → Reaction → Reactor → Catalyst → Process Flow → Economics → Conclusion**. Avoid bullet-only or text-only answers — use **labelled diagrams wherever possible**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Acrylic Acid Manufacturing

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model — Module 3, Set A]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe with a neat flow diagram the **two-stage vapor-phase catalytic oxidation of propylene** for the manufacture of acrylic acid. Discuss the catalysts used and the operating conditions in each reactor.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A propylene oxidation plant processes 1500 kg/h of propylene at 90% per-pass conversion. Stage 1 selectivity to acrolein is 88%, and Stage 2 selectivity to acrylic acid is 92%. Calculate (i) the mass of acrylic acid produced per hour, and (ii) the overall yield with respect to propylene. Also comment on the importance of MEHQ dosing.

**Model Answer:**

**(a)** The two-stage vapor-phase oxidation of propylene is the **dominant commercial route** for acrylic acid manufacture, accounting for > 90% of global production.

**Reactions:**

$$C_3H_6 + O_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Stage 1}} CH_2 = CH - CHO + H_2O$$

$$CH_2 = CH - CHO + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Stage 2}} CH_2 = CH - COOH$$

**Overall:** $C_3H_6 + \tfrac{3}{2}O_2 \to C_3H_4O_2 + H_2O$, ΔH ≈ –594 kJ·mol⁻¹

**Reactor Configuration:** Two **fixed-bed multitubular reactors** in series, with molten-salt bath cooling (salt is a eutectic mixture of KNO₃, NaNO₃, NaNO₂ — mp ~ 142 °C) to remove the large exotherm.

| Stage | Catalyst | Temp (°C) | Pressure | Conversion | Selectivity |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (Acrolein) | **Mo-Bi-O** (bismuth molybdate, with Co/Fe/K promoters) | 300–370 | 1–3 atm | 95–98% | 80–90% |
| 2 (Acrolein → AA) | **Mo-V-O** (mixed oxide with W, Cu on TiO₂) | 250–320 | 1–3 atm | ~ 99% | 85–95% |

**Process Flow (after reactors):**
- Reactor 1 outlet → Reactor 2 inlet (no intermediate separation)
- Reactor 2 outlet → **Absorber** (water quench → 50–60 wt% crude AA)
- → **Stripper** (removes light ends: unreacted propylene, acrolein, acetic acid)
- → **Extractive Distillation** (separates AA from acetic acid using a high-boiling solvent)
- → **Vacuum Distillation** (purification to ≥ 99.5% glacial AA)
- → **MEHQ dosing** (200 ppm) → **Storage**

*(7 marks: 2 for reactions + flow diagram, 2 for catalyst justification, 2 for operating conditions, 1 for downstream processing)*

---

**(b)** 

**Given:**
- $\dot{m}_{C_3H_6} = 1500$ kg/h
- $X = 0.90$, $S_1 = 0.88$, $S_2 = 0.92$
- $M_{C_3H_6} = 42.08$ kg/kmol, $M_{AA} = 72.06$ kg/kmol

**Step 1: Molar feed of propylene**

$$\dot{n}_{C_3H_6} = \dfrac{1500}{42.08} = 35.65 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 2: Moles reacted per pass**

$$\dot{n}_{\text{reacted}} = 0.90 \times 35.65 = 32.08 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 3: Acrolein formed**

$$\dot{n}_{\text{acrolein}} = 32.08 \times 0.88 = 28.23 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 4: Acrylic acid formed (1:1 stoichiometry)**

$$\dot{n}_{AA} = 28.23 \times 0.92 = 25.97 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 5 (i): Mass of AA per hour**

$$\dot{m}_{AA} = 25.97 \times 72.06 = \boxed{1871.4 \text{ kg/h}}$$

**Step 6 (ii): Overall yield**

$$Y = X \times S_1 \times S_2 = 0.90 \times 0.88 \times 0.92 = 0.7286 = \boxed{72.86\%}$$

**Importance of MEHQ dosing:** Acrylic acid polymerizes spontaneously and exothermically (~ –77 kJ/mol) even at ambient temperatures. Without an inhibitor (typically 200 ppm **MEHQ**), bulk storage, distillation columns, and transport vessels can undergo **thermal runaway polymerization**, leading to **catastrophic vessel rupture** with fire/explosion consequences. MEHQ functions as a free-radical scavenger and is *not* consumed under normal storage, ensuring safe transport. At the polymerization site, the user adds a peroxide initiator to override the inhibitor.

*(7 marks: 5 for numerical derivation, 2 for MEHQ explanation)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model — Module 3, Set B]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare the **Reppe (acetylene-based) process** and the **two-stage propylene oxidation process** for acrylic acid manufacture. Discuss reasons for the dominance of the latter.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the **major downstream applications** of acrylic acid, with emphasis on **superabsorbent polymers (SAP)** and **acrylate esters**. Include the chemistry involved in each.

**Model Answer:**

**(a)** The two routes differ in feedstock, pressure, catalyst, and product economics.

| Parameter | Reppe Process | Propylene 2-Stage Oxidation |
|---|---|---|
| **Feedstock** | Acetylene + CO + H₂O | Propylene + O₂ (air) |
| **Catalyst** | Ni(CO)₄ / NiBr₂ (homogeneous) | Mo-Bi-O + Mo-V-O (heterogeneous) |
| **Pressure** | 100 – 200 atm | 1 – 3 atm |
| **Temperature** | 150 – 200 °C | 300 – 370 °C / 250 – 320 °C |
| **Selectivity** | ~ 80% | **85 – 95%** |
| **Toxicity / Hazard** | **High** (Ni(CO)₄ is a deadly volatile nickel carbonyl) | Low–moderate |
| **CAPEX** | Very high (thick-wall pressure vessels) | Moderate (atmospheric multitubular) |
| **OPEX (feedstock)** | High (acetylene from CaC₂) | **Moderate** (propylene from steam crackers) |
| **Global share (2024)** | < 5% (mostly China, coal-based) | **> 90%** |

**Reasons for dominance of propylene route:**

1. **Feedstock economics:** Steam crackers produce propylene in enormous volumes (a byproduct of ethylene manufacture). The marginal cost of propylene is much lower than acetylene (which requires energy-intensive CaC₂ production or partial oxidation of natural gas).
2. **Process safety:** Avoidance of **Ni(CO)₄** (a Class A poison, OSHA exposure limit 1 ppb) eliminates a major hazard.
3. **Mild conditions:** 1–3 atm vs 100–200 atm dramatically reduces capital cost.
4. **Higher selectivity and yield** overall.
5. **Better catalyst longevity** in fixed-bed operation; homogeneous Ni catalysts require separation.

*(7 marks: 2 for reaction comparison, 2 for parameter table, 2 for dominance reasons, 1 for safety/economics summary)*

---

**(b)** Acrylic acid has two principal downstream platforms:

**(i) Superabsorbent Polymers (SAP):**
- **Chemistry:** Sodium polyacrylate, formed by **free-radical aqueous polymerization** of acrylic acid partially neutralized with NaOH.
- **Mechanism:** Initiation (e.g., persulfate), propagation (–CH₂–CH(COONa)– repeat unit), crosslinking (with a difunctional comonomer like methylene bis-acrylamide, ~ 0.1–1 mol%).
- **Property:** The crosslinked network carries many –COO⁻Na⁺ groups. When contacted with water, **osmotic pressure** drives water into the polymer, while **crosslinks** prevent dissolution. Result: a hydrogel absorbing **~ 30 g water per g polymer** (or 30 mL saline solution).
- **Applications:** Baby diapers (~ 70% of SAP market), adult incontinence, feminine hygiene, agricultural water retention, cable water-blocking.

**(ii) Acrylate Esters:**
- **Chemistry:** Fischer esterification of acrylic acid with C1–C8 alcohols in the presence of an acid catalyst (e.g., H₂SO₄, p-toluenesulfonic acid), with removal of water to drive equilibrium.
$$CH_2 = CH - COOH + ROH \rightleftharpoons CH_2 = CH - COOR + H_2O$$
- **Major esters:** Methyl acrylate (MA), ethyl acrylate (EA), butyl acrylate (BA), 2-ethylhexyl acrylate (2-EHA).
- **Applications:** Paints and coatings (BA, 2-EHA — weatherability and flexibility), pressure-sensitive adhesives (2-EHA), textile finishes (MA), leather coatings, plastic modifiers.
- **Combined consumption:** ~ 45–50% of all acrylic acid produced.

**Other Applications (briefly):**
- Polyacrylic acid homopolymer (water treatment, dispersants, scale inhibitors)
- Ammonium polyacrylate (detergents, dishwasher formulations)
- Specialty: hydrogels, ion-exchange resins, artificial cartilage.

*(7 marks: 3 for SAP chemistry, 3 for acrylate ester chemistry, 1 for minor applications)*

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Avoid These in 2024 Exams):**
> 1. **Stage 1 vs Stage 2 confusion:** Many students mix up the catalyst assignments. Memorize: **Mo-Bi = Acrolein** (Stage 1), **Mo-V = Acrylic Acid** (Stage 2). A common wrong answer: "Stage 1 uses Mo-V".
> 2. **Stoichiometry off-by-half:** The Stage 2 reaction is $C_3H_4O + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \to C_3H_4O_2$ — students often incorrectly write $O_2$ instead of $\tfrac{1}{2}O_2$. Watch the **mole ratio of oxygen**.
> 3. **Forgetting to add $\times 100$:** "Selectivity = 0.85" is a number; "Selectivity = 85%" is the correct reporting unit. Examiners deduct 0.5 marks for unit errors.
> 4. **Confusing pKa with pH:** pKa is a property of the molecule; pH is a property of the solution. Do not interchange.
> 5. **Omitting MEHQ in flow diagrams:** A flow diagram that does NOT show an **inhibitor dosing step** at the final product storage is considered incomplete in KTU 2024 evaluation.
> 6. **Wrong IUPAC name:** "Acrylic acid" is acceptable as a retained trivial name, but the **preferred IUPAC is "prop-2-enoic acid"** — use the latter to score full marks.
> 7. **Yield = Conversion × Selectivity, NOT Conversion + Selectivity.** A frequent arithmetic error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> **Quick-Fire Bullets — KTU Module 3: Acrylic Acid**

- **Molecule:** C₃H₄O₂, molar mass 72.06 g/mol, IUPAC = prop-2-enoic acid, pKa = 4.25
- **Structure:** Vinyl group + carboxyl group (α,β-unsaturated acid, Michael acceptor)
- **Dominant manufacturing route:** **Two-stage vapor-phase propylene oxidation** (> 90% global share)
- **Stage 1:** C₃H₆ + O₂ → acrolein + H₂O on **Mo-Bi-O** catalyst at 300–370 °C
- **Stage 2:** acrolein + ½O₂ → AA on **Mo-V-O** catalyst at 250–320 °C
- **Overall:** C₃H₆ + 3/2 O₂ → AA + H₂O, **ΔH ≈ –594 kJ/mol (highly exothermic)**
- **Reactor type:** Fixed-bed multitubular with **molten-salt bath** cooling
- **Purification:** Absorber → Stripper → Extractive Distillation → Vacuum Distillation
- **Inhibitor:** **MEHQ (200 ppm)** to prevent thermal-runaway polymerization
- **Reppe (acetylene) route:** Obsolete in the West, still used in China; requires **Ni(CO)₄** (highly toxic)
- **Emerging routes:** Direct propane oxidation (low selectivity, ~ 30–50%); bio-based glycerol route (renewable, BASF/Novozymes)
- **Top application #1 — SAP (Superabsorbent Polymer):** Sodium polyacrylate, ~ 35–40% market, **~ 30 g water / g polymer** absorbent capacity
- **Top application #2 — Acrylate esters:** MA, EA, BA, 2-EHA — used in paints/coatings, adhesives, textiles (~ 45–50% market)
- **Other uses:** Polyacrylic acid (water treatment), ammonium polyacrylate (detergents), specialty hydrogels
- **Key relationship:** **Yield = Conversion × Selectivity** (multiplicative, NOT additive)
- **Safety note:** Acrylic acid is **corrosive, flammable (flash point ~ 50 °C), polymerizes readily** — handle under inhibited conditions
- **Global production (2024):** > 8 million tpy; one of the top 30 organic chemicals
- **Storage & Transport:** Nitrogen-blanketed, inhibited, temperature-controlled; avoid contact with initiators, heat, or amines

> **Last-Mile Tip:** When asked "Why is acrylic acid industrially important?" — answer in three short lines: **(1) High reactivity of C=C enables polymerization & esterification; (2) Drives the SAP hygiene industry (multi-billion USD); (3) Largest single outlet for propylene after polypropylene and acrylonitrile.** This three-line crisp answer scores full marks on any 3-mark "discuss the importance" question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: C4 and C5 Compounds - Production and Application of Butadiene
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT741 - PETROCHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** C4 and C5 Compounds - Production and Application of Butadiene

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition of C4 and C5 Petrochemical Compounds

**C4 Compounds** are unsaturated and saturated four-carbon hydrocarbons that constitute the **C4 fraction** obtained as a co-product during the **steam cracking of naphtha, ethane, or gas oils**, and from **Fluid Catalytic Cracking (FCC)** of heavy feedstocks. The major components are:

- **1,3-Butadiene (CH₂=CH–CH=CH₂)** — the most important conjugated diene
- **Isobutylene (isobutene, (CH₃)₂C=CH₂)**
- **1-Butene and 2-Butene (n-butenes)**
- **n-Butane and Isobutane**

**C5 Compounds** are five-carbon dienes and olefins, primarily recovered from the **C5 fraction** (also called the **Diels hydrocarbon pool**). Key members include:

- **Isoprene (2-methyl-1,3-butadiene, CH₂=C(CH₃)–CH=CH₂)**
- **Cyclopentadiene (CPD, C₅H₆)** — typically recovered as its dimer **dicyclopentadiene (DCPD)**
- **Piperylene (1,3-pentadiene, CH₂=CH–CH=CH–CH₃)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 PECHT741 Module 3):** Among C4/C5 compounds, **1,3-Butadiene** is the central petrochemical building block. It accounts for nearly **50% of the global C4 chemicals market** and is the monomer for the largest-volume synthetic rubbers.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **refinery as a giant kitchen** where crude oil is "cooked" (cracked) into smaller useful molecules. The C4 and C5 fractions are like the **aromatic garnishes and spice oils** that fall out during cooking — small in volume but extremely valuable as specialty ingredients. Butadiene, specifically, is the **"active yeast" of the rubber world** — without it, we cannot bake the synthetic rubber used in tyres, gaskets, and shoe soles.

> [!NOTE]
> The **C4 cut** from a steam cracker typically contains **~40–50% butadiene** (by weight of the total C4 stream), depending on cracker severity. Higher severity = higher butadiene yield.

### 1.3 Physical Constants of 1,3-Butadiene

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Molecular Formula | C₄H₆ |
| Molecular Weight | **54.09 g/mol** |
| Boiling Point | **−4.4 °C** |
| Melting Point | **−108.9 °C** |
| Density (liquid at 0 °C) | **0.65 g/cm³** |
| Flash Point | **−85 °C** (highly flammable) |
| Autoignition Temperature | **420 °C** |

> [!WARNING]
> 1,3-Butadiene is a **highly flammable, carcinogenic (Category 1A)** gas at room temperature. It is stored and transported as a liquefied gas under pressure or as an inhibited liquid to prevent dangerous **peroxide formation and Diels–Alder dimerization** to **4-vinylcyclohexene**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Sources of C4 and C5 Fractions

The C4 and C5 streams originate from two principal refining/petrochemical processes:

1. **Steam Cracking of Naphtha / Gasoils (Ethylene Plants)** — primary source
2. **Fluid Catalytic Cracking (FCC) of VGO** — secondary source

In a steam cracker operating at **800–900 °C** with short residence times, the C4 fraction (called **"C4 raffinate"** before separation) contains:

- 1,3-Butadiene
- Isobutylene
- 1-Butene
- cis-2-Butene and trans-2-Butene
- n-Butane and Isobutane (inerts)

The C5 fraction contains isoprene, cyclopentadiene, piperylene, pentenes, and pentanes.

### 2.2 Production Routes of 1,3-Butadiene

#### Route 1: Extractive Distillation from Steam Cracker C4 Fraction (Dominant Commercial Method)

Because the boiling points of the C4 hydrocarbons are very close (within a few °C of each other), ordinary distillation cannot separate them. **Extractive distillation** uses a polar solvent (such as **N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP)**, **dimethylformamide (DMF)**, or **acetonitrile (ACN)**) that selectively dissolves butadiene due to its conjugated π-system and high polarizability.

**Process Steps:**

1. **Pre-distillation** to remove C3 and C5+ components
2. **Butadiene extraction** using a polar solvent in an extractive distillation column
3. **Solvent recovery** and solvent regeneration
4. **Butadiene purification** to polymer-grade (≥ 99.5%)

The relative volatility of butadiene to isobutylene in the presence of NMP is enhanced from near **1.0 (no separation)** to about **1.7–2.0**, enabling efficient separation.

#### Route 2: Catalytic Dehydrogenation of n-Butane / n-Butenes (Houdry Process / Catadiene Process)

A historical but still relevant process. The reactions are:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{n-Butane} &\xrightarrow{\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_3/\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3,\ \Delta} \text{n-Butenes} + \text{H}_2 \\
\text{n-Butene} &\xrightarrow{\text{cat.,}\ \Delta} \text{1,3-Butadiene} + \text{H}_2
\end{aligned}$$

The net reaction is:

$$n\text{-C}_4\text{H}_{10} \rightarrow \text{CH}_2=\text{CH–CH}=\text{CH}_2 + 2\text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H = +220\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

This reaction is **highly endothermic** and equilibrium-limited. Industrial operation uses:

- **Temperature**: 600–700 °C
- **Pressure**: 0.05–0.2 atm (vacuum, to shift equilibrium toward butadiene)
- **Catalyst**: Ferric oxide / chromium oxide on alumina, often with potassium carbonate promoter

#### Route 3: Oxidative Dehydrogenation of n-Butenes (Oxo-D Process)

To overcome the thermodynamic limitation, oxygen is co-fed. The reaction becomes **exothermic**:

$$2\,\text{C}_4\text{H}_8 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\,\text{C}_4\text{H}_6 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \Delta H = -110\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

Catalysts: **Bismuth molybdate (Bi₂Mo₃O₁₂)**, ferrite-based catalysts. Used by **Texas Petrochemicals** and Chinese producers.

#### Route 4: Lebedev Process (From Ethanol — Historic)

$$2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} \rightarrow \text{C}_4\text{H}_6 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{H}_2$$

Catalyst: MgO–SiO₂ doped with Cr₂O₃ at **400–450 °C**. Now obsolete in Western nations but still used in India and Brazil (e.g., **Indian Petrochemicals Corporation, Vadodara**) using bio-ethanol.

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Chemistry of Catalytic Dehydrogenation

1. n-Butane adsorbs on an acidic site of the catalyst surface
2. First C–H bond cleavage produces a **butyl radical / surface alkyl**
3. β-hydride elimination yields **n-butene** (mixed 1-butene and 2-butene)
4. Second dehydrogenation on the allylic intermediate forms the conjugated **1,3-butadiene**
5. Hydrogen atoms combine and desorb as **H₂**

> [!NOTE]
> The **selectivity** of the Houdry process to butadiene is ~70–80% per pass, with by-products being **cracked gases (C1–C3)** and **coke** deposited on the catalyst. Catalyst regeneration by burning off coke is required every 15–30 minutes in a fixed-bed swing reactor system.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Equation / Value | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Net dehydrogenation enthalpy | $\Delta H = +220\ \text{kJ/mol}$ | n-Butane → Butadiene |
| Oxidative dehydrogenation enthalpy | $\Delta H = -110\ \text{kJ/mol}$ | Exothermic, thermodynamically favorable |
| Butadiene global production | $\approx 15\ \text{million tonnes/year}$ | 2023 data, KTU highlight |
| Cracker C4 butadiene content | 40–50 wt% | Severity dependent |
| Polymer-grade purity | $\geq 99.5\%$ | Required for SBR/BR polymerization |
| Extractive distillation solvent | NMP / DMF / ACN | Selectivity $\alpha \approx 1.7$–$2.0$ |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Tyre Industry** (largest consumer): SBR + BR rubbers
- **Automotive plastics**: ABS and HIPS impact modifiers
- **Nylon-6,6 production**: Butadiene → Adiponitrile → HMDA
- **Synthetic rubber latex**: NBR gloves, surgical gloves, conveyor belts
- **Chloroprene production**: Butadiene → Chloroprene → Neoprene rubber
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Equilibrium Conversion for n-Butane Dehydrogenation

For the reaction:

$$n\text{-C}_4\text{H}_{10} \rightleftharpoons \text{C}_4\text{H}_6 + 2\,\text{H}_2$$

The **equilibrium constant** in terms of partial pressures at temperature $T$ is:

$$K_p = \frac{P_{\text{butadiene}} \cdot P_{\text{H}_2}^{\,2}}{P_{\text{n-butane}}}$$

Let the initial moles of n-butane = $1$. Let the fractional conversion = $x$. Assume 1 mole of inert diluent (steam) is added to suppress coke.

| Species | Initial | Change | Equilibrium |
|---|---|---|---|
| n-Butane | 1 | $-x$ | $1 - x$ |
| Butadiene | 0 | $+x$ | $x$ |
| H₂ | 0 | $+2x$ | $2x$ |
| Steam (inert) | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| **Total moles** | 2 | — | $3 + 2x$ |

Let total pressure = $P$. The partial pressure of species $i$ is $P_i = y_i \cdot P$, where $y_i$ is the mole fraction.

$$P_{\text{butadiene}} = \frac{x}{3 + 2x}\,P$$

$$P_{\text{H}_2} = \frac{2x}{3 + 2x}\,P$$

$$P_{\text{n-butane}} = \frac{1 - x}{3 + 2x}\,P$$

Substituting into $K_p$:

$$K_p = \frac{\left(\dfrac{x}{3+2x}P\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{2x}{3+2x}P\right)^2}{\left(\dfrac{1-x}{3+2x}P\right)}$$

Simplifying:

$$K_p = \frac{4x^3\,P^2}{(1 - x)(3 + 2x)^2}$$

> This is the master equation for designing the dehydrogenation reactor. Rearranging gives the **equilibrium conversion** as a function of $K_p$ and $P$.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Given:** $K_p = 0.05\ \text{atm}^2$ at 600 °C, total pressure $P = 0.1\ \text{atm}$, with steam dilution (1:1 steam:hydrocarbon).

**Find:** Equilibrium conversion $x$.

Substituting:

$$0.05 = \frac{4x^3\,(0.1)^2}{(1 - x)(3 + 2x)^2}$$

$$0.05 = \frac{0.04\,x^3}{(1 - x)(3 + 2x)^2}$$

This is a **cubic equation** that must be solved iteratively. Using the iteration sequence:

| Iteration | Trial $x$ | LHS | RHS | Adjustment |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.50 | 0.05 | 0.0042 | $x \uparrow$ |
| 2 | 0.70 | 0.05 | 0.0224 | $x \uparrow$ |
| 3 | 0.85 | 0.05 | 0.0602 | $x \downarrow$ |
| 4 | 0.80 | 0.05 | 0.0461 | $x \uparrow$ |
| 5 | 0.82 | 0.05 | 0.0502 | $x \downarrow$ |
| **Converged** | **0.818** | — | — | — |

**Result:** Equilibrium conversion $x \approx 0.82$ or **82%**.

> This confirms that even with the unfavorable thermodynamics, **low pressure and high temperature** shift the equilibrium toward butadiene.

### 3.3 Selectivity and Yield Analysis for Houdry Process

For an industrial reactor operating at $T = 650\ ^\circ\text{C}$ and $P = 0.1\ \text{atm}$:

- **Single-pass conversion** of n-butane: ~30–40%
- **Selectivity to butadiene**: ~70–80%
- **Overall yield**: Conversion × Selectivity ≈ 25–30%
- **Recycle**: Unreacted n-butane is recycled to extinction

The **per-pass yield** $Y_{pp}$ is calculated as:

$$Y_{pp} = X \cdot S$$

where $X$ is fractional conversion and $S$ is fractional selectivity. For a recycle-to-extinction system, **total butadiene yield** approaches ~85% of the feed butane.

### 3.4 Mass Balance Across Extractive Distillation Column

For a feed of **10,000 kg/hr** of C4 raffinate containing 45 wt% butadiene:

- **Butadiene in feed** = 4,500 kg/hr
- **Solvent (NMP) flow** = ~25,000 kg/hr (typical solvent-to-feed ratio = 2.5)
- **Butadiene recovery** in extract = ~99% → 4,455 kg/hr
- **Polymer-grade product** (99.7% purity) = ~4,400 kg/hr
- **Solvent losses** to overhead = < 5 ppm (must be minimized due to cost)
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

