## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**List any three important C4 compounds obtained from the steam cracking of naphtha and give one industrial application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **1,3-Butadiene (CH₂=CH–CH=CH₂)** — Used as a monomer for **SBR (Styrene-Butadiene Rubber)** in tyre manufacturing.
2. **Isobutylene (isobutene, (CH₃)₂C=CH₂)** — Used in the production of **MTBE (Methyl tert-butyl ether)** as a gasoline oxygenate, and for **butyl rubber (IIR)**.
3. **1-Butene (CH₂=CH–CH₂–CH₃)** — Used as a **comonomer with ethylene for LLDPE (Linear Low-Density Polyethylene)** to control density and improve tear strength.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Examiners expect **chemical names + formulae + a specific end-use product**. Avoid vague answers like "used in polymer industry."

#### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Why is extractive distillation used for the separation of 1,3-butadiene from the C4 fraction instead of ordinary distillation?**

**Model Answer:**

The boiling points of all C4 hydrocarbons lie in a very narrow range (from **−6.3 °C for 1-butene to −0.5 °C for n-butane**), a span of less than **6 °C**. Ordinary distillation requires a relative volatility $\alpha > 1.2$ for feasible separation, but the C4 components have $\alpha \approx 1.0$–$1.05$, making separation impossible.

**Extractive distillation** uses a high-boiling polar solvent such as **N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP)**, **DMF**, or **acetonitrile**, which selectively interacts with the conjugated diene (butadiene) through dipole-induced dipole and π-complex interactions. This **increases the relative volatility** of butadiene to isobutylene from ~1.0 to **~1.7–2.0**, enabling efficient separation.

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a)** With a neat flow diagram, describe the **extractive distillation process** for the production of 1,3-butadiene from the C4 fraction of a steam cracker using **NMP as solvent**. Discuss the role of the solvent and state the operating conditions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Houdry process** for the manufacture of 1,3-butadiene from n-butane. Write the reactions, catalyst, and operating conditions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Extractive Distillation of C4 Fraction Using NMP:**

**Flow description:**

The C4 fraction from the steam cracker is first passed through a **pre-distillation column** to remove C3 (lighter) and C5+ (heavier) impurities. The overhead C4 cut is then fed to the **extractive distillation column**, where it meets a counter-current flow of hot NMP solvent introduced near the top.

Inside the column, NMP preferentially dissolves butadiene (the most polarizable component). The **raffinate** (top product) contains isobutylene, 1-butene, 2-butenes, and butanes — these have lower affinity for NMP and pass overhead. The **extract** (bottom product) is rich in butadiene dissolved in NMP.

The extract is sent to a **solvent recovery (stripping) column**, where butadiene is vaporized and recovered overhead, while lean NMP is recycled from the bottom back to the extractive column. The crude butadiene then goes to a **final purification column** to achieve **polymer grade (≥ 99.5% purity)**.

**Role of NMP solvent:** NMP is a polar aprotic solvent with high boiling point (**202 °C**) and strong affinity for conjugated dienes. It breaks the azeotrope-like behavior of the C4 mixture, raising the relative volatility of butadiene/isobutylene to ~1.8.

**Operating conditions:**

- Column pressure: **4–6 atm**
- Solvent-to-feed ratio: **2.5–3.0 (mass basis)**
- Solvent temperature at top: **~50–60 °C**
- NMP make-up rate: < 0.05% per pass (very low losses)

**(b) Houdry Process:**

**Reactions:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{n-C}_4\text{H}_{10} &\rightleftharpoons \text{n-C}_4\text{H}_8 + \text{H}_2 \quad \text{(endothermic)} \\
\text{n-C}_4\text{H}_8 &\rightleftharpoons \text{C}_4\text{H}_6 + \text{H}_2 \quad \text{(endothermic)} \\
\text{Net:}\quad \text{n-C}_4\text{H}_{10} &\rightleftharpoons \text{C}_4\text{H}_6 + 2\text{H}_2
\end{aligned}$$

**Catalyst:** Chromium oxide (18%) on alumina, promoted with potassium carbonate and calcium oxide to enhance selectivity.

**Operating conditions:**

- Temperature: **600–700 °C**
- Pressure: **0.05–0.2 atm (vacuum operation)**
- Steam dilution: 1:1 to 1:4 (steam:hydrocarbon) to suppress coke
- Space velocity: ~1–2 v/v/hr
- **Regeneration cycle:** Every 15–30 minutes, catalyst is regenerated by burning coke with air. A **swing reactor system** (3–4 reactors) is used so that one reactor is always in regeneration mode while the others are in dehydrogenation.

**Per-pass conversion:** 30–40%; **selectivity to butadiene:** 70–80%; **overall yield to extinction:** ~85%.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes (Examiner Pitfall):**
> 1. Writing "ordinary distillation" for C4 separation — **lose 2 marks**; extractive distillation is mandatory.
> 2. Forgetting the **vacuum operation** and steam dilution in the Houdry process — these are the key thermodynamic levers to shift equilibrium.
> 3. Confusing **isobutylene** with **n-butenes** when discussing applications — isobutylene goes to MTBE/butyl rubber, NOT to butadiene production.

#### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**(a)** Discuss the **oxidative dehydrogenation of n-butenes** for butadiene production. Compare it with the Houdry process in terms of thermodynamics and selectivity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** List and explain any **four major applications of 1,3-butadiene** in the petrochemical industry, with the corresponding downstream products. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Oxidative Dehydrogenation (Oxo-D Process):**

The reaction:

$$2\,\text{C}_4\text{H}_8 + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Bi}_2\text{Mo}_3\text{O}_{12}} 2\,\text{C}_4\text{H}_6 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \Delta H = -110\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

The H₂ produced is immediately oxidized to H₂O, which is **irreversible**, thereby overcoming the equilibrium limitation of the Houdry process. The reaction is **exothermic**, eliminating the need for external heat input and enabling high single-pass conversion.

**Comparison with Houdry:**

| Parameter | Houdry (Catalytic) | Oxo-D (Oxidative) |
|---|---|---|
| $\Delta H$ | +220 kJ/mol (endothermic) | −110 kJ/mol (exothermic) |
| Single-pass conversion | 30–40% | 70–90% |
| Selectivity to butadiene | 70–80% | 80–90% |
| Operating pressure | 0.05–0.2 atm (vacuum) | 1–3 atm (atmospheric) |
| Catalyst | Cr₂O₃/Al₂O₃ | Bismuth molybdate / ferrite |
| Reactor type | Fixed-bed swing | Fixed-bed or fluidized bed |

The **drawback** of Oxo-D is that it requires careful **O₂/C₄H₈ ratio control** (typically 0.5–0.6) to avoid butadiene combustion to CO₂ and water, and there is a risk of **explosive mixtures** in the feed.

**(b) Four Major Applications of 1,3-Butadiene:**

1. **Styrene-Butadiene Rubber (SBR)**: Copolymer of butadiene (~75%) and styrene (~25%). Used in **automobile tyres, shoe soles, gaskets**. SBR is the largest-volume synthetic rubber.

2. **Polybutadiene Rubber (BR)**: Homopolymer of butadiene using Ziegler-Natta or neodymium catalysts. Used in **tyre treads** for high abrasion resistance and in **golf ball cores**.

3. **Acrylonitrile-Butadiene-Styrene (ABS) Plastic**: Butadiene provides impact strength; used in **automotive parts, electronic housings, LEGO bricks, and helmets**.

4. **Adiponitrile (ADN) — Intermediate for Nylon-6,6**: Butadiene is hydrochlorinated to 1,4-dichlorobutene, then converted to adiponitrile via **DuPont's process**, which is hydrogenated to **HMDA (hexamethylenediamine)**, a monomer for **Nylon-6,6**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Do NOT confuse **chloroprene (CH₂=CCl–CH=CH₂)** with chlorination of butadiene. Chloroprene is made from butadiene through **chlorination → isomerization → dehydrochlorination**, NOT direct chlorination.
> - For the application question, mention the **specific end-product** and the **function** of butadiene in that product — partial answers lose marks.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- C4 fraction components: **Butadiene, isobutylene, 1-butene, 2-butenes, butanes**
- C5 fraction components: **Isoprene, cyclopentadiene (CPD), piperylene**
- Butadiene is the **most important C4 petrochemical** — precursor to SBR, BR, ABS, NBR, Neoprene, and Nylon-6,6
- **Extractive distillation with NMP/DMF/ACN** is the dominant separation method due to close boiling points of C4s
- **Houdry process:** Cr₂O₃/Al₂O₃ catalyst, 600–700 °C, vacuum, endothermic
- **Oxo-D process:** Bi₂Mo₃O₁₂ catalyst, exothermic, overcomes equilibrium limit
- **Lebedev process:** Historical route from ethanol; still used in India (IPCL Vadodara)
- **Butadiene storage:** Must contain **TBC (tert-butyl catechol) inhibitor** at 100–200 ppm to prevent peroxide formation and Diels–Alder dimerization to 4-vinylcyclohexene
- **Polymer grade purity:** ≥ 99.5%, with very low levels of acetylenes (< 50 ppm)
- Global production: ~15 million tonnes/year (2023 estimate)
- 1,3-Butadiene is a **confirmed human carcinogen (IARC Group 1)** — strict industrial hygiene required
- **Application linkages to remember:** Butadiene → SBR (tyres), BR (tyre treads), ABS (impact plastic), NBR (oil seals/gaskets), ADN (Nylon-6,6), Chloroprene (Neoprene)

> **End of KTU Module 3 Notes — C4 and C5 Compounds: Production and Application of Butadiene**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Isoprene
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT741 - PETROCHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Isoprene

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Isoprene** (IUPAC: **2-methyl-1,3-butadiene**) is a volatile, conjugated aliphatic **diene hydrocarbon** with the molecular formula **C₅H₈** and molecular weight **68.12 g·mol⁻¹**. It is the simplest and most industrially significant C₅ conjugated diene, serving as the fundamental monomeric building block for **polyisoprene rubber**, which is the closest synthetic analogue to natural rubber (cis-1,4-polyisoprene).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor — Module 3 Focus:**
> Isoprene is studied as a representative C₅ petrochemical building block. The module emphasizes (a) its isolation from C₅ cracker streams, (b) synthetic routes (dehydrogenation of isopentane, Prins reaction, isobutylene–formaldehyde condensation), and (c) its polymerization behavior and downstream polymer applications.

**Molecular Structure:**

$$ \text{CH}_2\!=\!\text{C}(\text{CH}_3)\!-\!\text{CH}\!=\!\text{CH}_2 $$

**Key Physical Metrics (Bold-Required Constants):**

| Property | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Molecular Weight | **68.12 g·mol⁻¹** |
| Boiling Point @ 101.325 kPa | **34.07 °C** |
| Melting Point | **−145.95 °C** |
| Density (liquid, 20 °C) | **0.681 g·cm⁻³** |
| Refractive Index (nD, 20 °C) | **1.4219** |
| Flash Point | **−54 °C** |
| Auto-Ignition Temperature | **220 °C** |

> [!NOTE]
> Isoprene is the structural unit that nature itself uses to assemble natural rubber, terpene resins, and many biological isoprenoids (carotenoids, steroids). Industrially, it is the bridge between **C₅ cracker byproducts** and the **elastomer industry**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **"lego brick"** that has **two snap-connectors** on its ends (the two C=C double bonds) and a small **bump** (the methyl group) on the middle carbon. This double-snap design allows the brick to **link with four neighbors simultaneously** in a long, springy chain — and that springy chain is what gives rubber its bounce and elasticity.

A second, equally vivid analogy: Isoprene is the **"C₅ twin" of butadiene**. Just as butadiene (C₄H₆) is the monomer of SBR (styrene–butadiene rubber), isoprene is the monomer of **polyisoprene (IR)**, the synthetic version of natural rubber. The extra methyl group on isoprene is what allows the polymer chain to coil in the **cis-1,4 configuration** with such natural elasticity.

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize the "5-Carbon Rule":** In petrochemicals, every carbon number has its flagship diene — C₄ → butadiene, **C₅ → isoprene**, C₆ → hexadiene (uncommon). Recognizing this helps you anchor isoprene in the broader family of conjugated dienes.

---

## 1.3 Industrial Significance Snapshot

- **Polyisoprene Rubber (IR):** Tire treads, automotive parts, footwear, medical gloves.
- **Styrene–Isoprene–Styrene (SIS) Block Copolymers:** Hot-melt adhesives, pressure-sensitive adhesives.
- **Butyl Rubber (IIR):** Isobutylene + isoprene copolymer for inner tubes and tire liners.
- **Terpene Resins:** Cationic polymerization with C₅ streams → tackifier resins.
- **Intermediate:** Epoxidation → 1,2-epoxyisoprene; Diels–Alder → specialty chemicals.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural & Electronic Theory

Isoprene belongs to the **conjugated diene family** (alternating single–double–single–double bond system). The two double bonds are separated by **one single (C–C) σ-bond**, allowing p-orbital overlap and π-electron delocalization across C1–C2–C3–C4.

### 2.1.1 Why the Conjugation Matters

- **Resonance stabilization energy ≈ 9 kJ·mol⁻¹** (lower than butadiene's ~12 kJ·mol⁻¹ because the methyl group distorts perfect symmetry).
- The HOMO is raised in energy, making isoprene an **excellent Diels–Alder diene** and an **active dienophile partner**.
- Two primary reactive sites for addition:
  1. **1,2-Addition:** Across the C1=C2 double bond.
  2. **1,4-Addition (preferred for conjugated dienes):** Across the C1 and C4 termini, leaving an internal C2=C3 double bond in the product.

### 2.1.2 Geometric Isomerism in Polymerization

Polymerization of isoprene can produce **four stereoisomeric microstructures**:

| Microstructure | Configuration | Abbreviation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| *cis*-1,4 | Both substituents on same side | **c-IR** (natural rubber analogue) |
| *trans*-1,4 | Substituents on opposite sides | **t-IR** (gutta-percha analogue) |
| 1,2-Addition | Pendant vinyl group at C3 | **1,2-IR** |
| 3,4-Addition | Pendant isopropenyl group at C1 | **3,4-IR** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "cis" matters:** Natural rubber is **>99 % cis-1,4-polyisoprene**. Synthetic *cis*-1,4-IR is made using **Ziegler–Natta catalysts** (e.g., TiCl₄/Al(i-C₄H₉)₃) and rivals natural rubber in tensile strength, resilience, and elastic recovery. *trans*-1,4-IR is hard, crystalline, and resembles gutta-percha.

---

## 2.2 Industrial Production Routes — Logic Decomposition

Five commercial/semi-commercial routes exist. The dominant route depends on regional feedstock economics.

### Route A — Extractive Distillation from C₅ Steam-Cracker Streams (PRIMARY)

When naphtha/ethane is steam-cracked to produce ethylene, the **C₅ fraction** ("C₅ raffinate") contains isoprene, cyclopentadiene (CPD), piperylenes, and C₅ olefins/paraffins.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Steam cracking of naphtha yields **~2–5 wt%** of a C₄–C₅ byproduct stream. From this, **~1–2 kg of isoprene is recovered per 1000 kg of ethylene produced** — a powerful economic argument for co-production in integrated petrochemical complexes.

**Process Steps (Each step's "WHY"):**

1. **Fractional Distillation:** Crude C₅ cut is distilled → enriches the isoprene concentrate to ~10–15 wt%.
2. **Dimerization of CPD:** Cyclopentadiene is selectively dimerized to **dicyclopentadiene (DCPD)** at 100–130 °C → easily separated because DCPD has a much higher boiling point (170 °C) than isoprene (34 °C). *Why?* Removes the most reactive impurity that would otherwise polymerize in the next column.
3. **Extractive Distillation with NMP or DMF:** Polar solvent (N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone, **NMP**, or **DMF**) selectively dissolves isoprene and piperylenes. *Why NMP?* High selectivity, low toxicity, easy regeneration, low corrosion versus older ACN (acetonitrile) solvents.
4. **Solvent Stripping & Final Distillation:** Isoprene is recovered at >99.5 wt% purity (polymer-grade).

### Route B — Catalytic Dehydrogenation of Isopentane (SECONDARY)

$$ \text{CH}_3\text{-CH(CH}_3\text{)-CH}_2\text{-CH}_3 \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{CH}_2\!=\!\text{C(CH}_3\text{)-CH}\!=\!\text{CH}_2 \;+\; 2\,\text{H}_2 $$

- **Catalysts:** Chromium oxide on alumina (Cr₂O₃/Al₂O₃) at **550–620 °C**, with promoted variants (K₂O, CeO₂) for selectivity.
- **Equilibrium-driven, endothermic (ΔH ≈ +210 kJ·mol⁻¹)** → favors high temperature, low pressure.
- Conversions limited to ~30–40 % per pass to avoid cracking; H₂ is recovered for refinery use.

### Route C — Isobutylene–Formaldehyde Condensation (Prins Reaction) (SYNTHETIC)

A two-step route, the **"Princeton Process"** developed by Exxon:

**Step 1 — Prins Reaction (acid-catalyzed):**

$$ \text{CH}_2\!=\!\text{C(CH}_3)_2 \;+\; \text{HCHO} \;\xrightarrow{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4}\; \text{(CH}_3)_2\text{C=CH-CH}_2\text{OH} \;(\text{3-methyl-3-buten-1-ol}) $$

**Step 2 — Dehydration over acidic catalyst:**

$$ (\text{CH}_3)_2\text{C=CH-CH}_2\text{OH} \;\xrightarrow{\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4/\text{SiO}_2,\; 250\,°C}\; \text{isoprene} \;+\; \text{H}_2\text{O} $$

> [!NOTE]
> This route is **independent of cracker economics** and is favored in regions with cheap isobutylene (e.g., from MTBE-decommissioned plants) and cheap formaldehyde (methanol oxidation).

### Route D — Propylene Dimerization → 2-Methylpentane → Oxidative Dehydrogenation

Two-step: propylene → 2-methyl-1-pentene → 2-methylpentane → isoprene (via oxidative dehydrogenation). Historical; rarely used now.

### Route E — Acetylene–Acetone Route (Reppe Chemistry)

Historical route, now **obsolete** in most regions:

$$ 2\,\text{CH}\!\equiv\!\text{CH} \;+\; \text{CH}_3\text{COCH}_3 \;\rightarrow\; \text{2-methyl-3-butyn-2-ol} \;\rightarrow\; \text{isoprene} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **Do NOT mention the Reppe route as "current" in KTU answers.** It is academically important but industrially discontinued due to acetylene cost, toxicity, and handling hazards.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-day reference** for all isoprene-related numerical and stoichiometric problems. Memorize symbols, units, and boundary conditions.

| # | Equation / Identity | Variables & Units | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $n = \dfrac{m}{M}$ | n = moles, m = mass (g), M = molar mass (g·mol⁻¹) | Mass–mole conversion |
| 2 | $Y_{ip} = \dfrac{m_{ip}}{m_{feed}} \times 100\,\%$ | $m_{ip}$ = isoprene mass, $m_{feed}$ = feed mass | Yield calculation |
| 3 | $S_{ip} = \dfrac{n_{ip}}{n_{C_5\text{-}diene}} \times 100\,\%$ | $n_{ip}$ = isoprene moles, $n_{C_5\text{-}diene}$ = all C₅ diene moles | Selectivity |
| 4 | $X_{feed} = \dfrac{n_{feed,in} - n_{feed,out}}{n_{feed,in}}$ | Inlet/outlet feed moles | Conversion |
| 5 | $K_p = \dfrac{p_{isoprene}\,p_{H_2}^{\,2}}{p_{isopentane}}$ | Partial pressures (atm) | Dehydrogenation equilibrium |
| 6 | $\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT\ln Q$ | J·mol⁻¹ | Reaction spontaneity |
| 7 | $K_p = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\Delta G^\circ}{RT}\right)$ | — | Equilibrium constant from ΔG° |
| 8 | $\Delta H_{rxn} = \sum \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{products}) - \sum \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{reactants})$ | kJ·mol⁻¹ | Enthalpy of dehydrogenation |
| 9 | $\text{RAV} = \dfrac{V_{reactor}}{Q_{feed}}$ | s or h | Reactor residence time |
| 10 | $\rho_{\text{mix}} = \sum y_i \rho_i$ | yᵢ = mass fraction, ρ = density | Liquid mixture density |
| 11 | $\text{CISPR} = \dfrac{m_{cis\text{-}1,4}}{m_{polymer}} \times 100\,\%$ | — | Cis-1,4 content in polyisoprene |
| 12 | $DP_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$ | p = monomer conversion | Number-average degree of polymerization |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize:** ΔH°f values for isopentane (–32.0 kJ·mol⁻¹) and isoprene (75.7 kJ·mol⁻¹). Plug them in Equation 8 to get the **+210 kJ·mol⁻¹** dehydrogenation enthalpy — a frequently tested numeric.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Sector | Role of Isoprene |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Tire Industry** | cis-1,4-polyisoprene → passenger & aircraft tire components |
| **Medical Devices** | Surgical gloves, catheters (latex-free polyisoprene) |
| **Adhesives** | SIS block copolymers → hot-melt, pressure-sensitive adhesives |
| **Pharma Intermediates** | Epoxidation → isoprene oxide → chiral synthons |
| **Diels–Alder Chemistry** | Isoprene + maleic anhydride → terpene-like cyclic anhydride |
| **Specialty Elastomers** | Butyl rubber (IIR) — isobutylene + ~2–3 % isoprene |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 3 Examination Expectation:** KTU 2024 scheme questions on this topic are **70 % descriptive + 30 % numerical**. The three most common numericals are (i) yield/selectivity from a cracker C₅ stream, (ii) ΔG and Kp for dehydrogenation of isopentane, and (iii) material balance on a Prins reactor. All three are worked out below in full.

---

## 3.1 Worked Numerical 1 — Isoprene Yield from a C₅ Cracker Stream

### Problem Statement (Modelled on KTU Dec 2023 pattern)

A naphtha steam-cracker produces **1.0 × 10⁴ kg/h** of a C₅ distillate. The mass composition is: isoprene **12 wt%**, piperylenes (1,3-pentadiene) **8 wt%**, cyclopentadiene **15 wt%**, isopentane **20 wt%**, n-pentane **25 wt%**, and C₅-olefins (2-methyl-1-butene, 2-methyl-2-butene) **20 wt%**. The downstream extractive distillation unit recovers **92 %** of the isoprene fed. Calculate (a) the **isoprene mass flow** in the feed and (b) the **recovered isoprene mass flow** per hour.

### Step 1 — Identify the mass fraction of isoprene

The problem states isoprene is **12 wt%** of the C₅ distillate:

$$ w_{ip} = 0.12 \quad (\text{dimensionless, mass fraction}) $$

### Step 2 — Apply the yield formula $m_{ip} = w_{ip} \cdot m_{feed}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{ip} &= w_{ip} \times m_{feed} \\
       &= 0.12 \times (1.0 \times 10^{4}\,\text{kg/h}) \\
       &= 1.2 \times 10^{3}\,\text{kg/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Therefore, the isoprene mass flow in the feed is 1.2 × 10³ kg/h (1200 kg/h).**

### Step 3 — Apply the recovery factor

Recovery efficiency $\eta_{rec} = 0.92$ :

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{ip,\,recovered} &= \eta_{rec} \times m_{ip,\,feed} \\
                    &= 0.92 \times 1200\,\text{kg/h} \\
                    &= 1104\,\text{kg/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $m_{ip,\,recovered} = 1.104 \times 10^{3}\,\text{kg/h}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse **mass fraction (wt%)** with **mole fraction (mol%)**. Always check the units: wt% uses molecular weights, mol% uses moles. For isoprene (M = 68.12) vs isopentane (M = 72.15), the difference is small but exam-evaluators may deduct a mark for sloppy wording.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical 2 — Equilibrium Constant for Isopentane Dehydrogenation

### Problem Statement

For the dehydrogenation of isopentane to isoprene:

$$ \text{C}_5\text{H}_{12(l)} \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{C}_5\text{H}_{8(g)} \;+\; 2\,\text{H}_{2(g)} $$

Given:
- $\Delta H^\circ_{rxn} = +210\,\text{kJ·mol}^{-1}$ (assumed temperature-independent over the range)
- $\Delta S^\circ_{rxn} = +314\,\text{J·mol}^{-1}\text{·K}^{-1}$
- Reactor temperature: $T = 873\,\text{K}$ (≈ 600 °C)

Calculate (a) the standard Gibbs free energy $\Delta G^\circ$ and (b) the equilibrium constant $K_p$.

### Step 1 — Apply the Gibbs–Helmholtz relation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta G^\circ &= \Delta H^\circ - T\Delta S^\circ \\
               &= (210 \times 10^{3}\,\text{J·mol}^{-1}) - (873\,\text{K})(314\,\text{J·mol}^{-1}\text{·K}^{-1}) \\
               &= 210{,}000 - 274{,}122 \\
               &= -64{,}122\,\text{J·mol}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Therefore, $\Delta G^\circ \approx -64.1\,\text{kJ·mol}^{-1}$.**

> [!NOTE]
> At 873 K, the **TΔS term dominates**, making the reaction **spontaneous** despite being **endothermic**. This is the classic "high-temperature, entropically-driven" dehydrogenation scenario — the increase in moles (1 → 3 gas-phase species) drives the entropy gain.

### Step 2 — Compute $K_p$ from $\Delta G^\circ$

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_p &= \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta G^\circ}{RT}\right) \\
    &= \exp\!\left(-\frac{-64{,}122}{(8.314)(873)}\right) \\
    &= \exp\!\left(\frac{64{,}122}{7258.1}\right) \\
    &= \exp(8.834) \\
    &\approx 6900
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $K_p \approx 6.9 \times 10^{3}$ at 873 K.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap Question:** If the examiner specifies a **lower temperature** (say 700 K), the calculation yields $\Delta G^\circ > 0$, $K_p < 1$, and the reaction is **non-spontaneous**. The student is expected to conclude that the dehydrogenation is **thermodynamically unfavorable below ~720 K** at atmospheric pressure — which is why the process runs at 600+ °C despite the high energy cost.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical 3 — Material Balance on a Prins Reactor

### Problem Statement

A Prins reactor is fed with **1000 kg/h** of isobutylene (M = 56.11 g·mol⁻¹) and **750 kg/h** of aqueous formaldehyde (37 wt% HCHO, balance water). The conversion of isobutylene is **88 %** and selectivity to **3-methyl-3-buten-1-ol (3M3B1OL)** (M = 86.13 g·mol⁻¹) is **94 %**. Calculate the **mass flow of 3M3B1OL** produced per hour.

### Step 1 — Convert isobutylene feed to molar flow

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{n}_{iC_4^=} &= \frac{1000\,\text{kg/h}}{56.11 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{kg/mol}} \\
                &= 17{,}822.5\,\text{mol/h} \\
                &= 17.82\,\text{kmol/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply conversion and selectivity

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{n}_{3M3B1OL,\,produced} &= \dot{n}_{iC_4^=} \times X \times S \\
                            &= 17.82 \times 0.88 \times 0.94 \\
                            &= 14.74\,\text{kmol/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Convert back to mass flow

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{3M3B1OL} &= \dot{n} \times M \\
                  &= 14.74 \times 10^{3}\,\text{mol/h} \times 86.13 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{kg/mol} \\
                  &= 1269.4\,\text{kg/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** Approximately **1.27 × 10³ kg/h of 3-methyl-3-buten-1-ol** is produced.

> [!TIP]
> **Conversion vs Selectivity vs Yield — Examiner's Mantra:**
> - **Conversion (X):** How much of the limiting reactant was consumed.
> - **Selectivity (S):** Of the consumed reactant, how much went to the desired product.
> - **Yield (Y):** $Y = X \times S$ — the fraction of feed that became the desired product.
> Using the wrong one in an answer is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.

---

## 3.4 Conceptual Derivation — Why cis-1,4-polyisoprene is Elastically Superior

The methyl group on C2 of the isoprene unit, combined with **Ziegler–Natta (or stereospecific metallocene) catalysis**, restricts chain propagation to one face of the growing chain. The resulting **all-cis-1,4 microstructure** allows:

- **Steric regularity** → chains pack into ordered domains but with weak secondary bonds.
- **Stress-induced crystallization** at high strain (natural rubber's self-reinforcing property).
- **Low glass transition temperature** ($T_g \approx -73\,°C$) → chain mobility at room temperature.

This is the molecular foundation of the "rubber elasticity" property that makes **synthetic polyisoprene (IR-307, Natsyn, Cariflex IR)** a near-drop-in replacement for natural rubber.

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram for the exam:**
> - Each rectangular block = a **process unit**.
> - The loop between ExtDist and SolventRegen is the **solvent recycle loop** — a key energy-economics feature in extractive distillation.
> - DCPD removal precedes extraction because CPD would otherwise polymerize inside the extractive column and foul trays.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define isoprene and list its two principal industrial applications.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Isoprene** is 2-methyl-1,3-butadiene, a C₅ conjugated diene with molecular formula **C₅H₈** and molecular weight **68.12 g·mol⁻¹**. It is a colorless, highly flammable liquid boiling at **34.07 °C**.
>
> **Two principal industrial applications:**
> 1. **Polyisoprene Rubber (IR):** Ziegler–Natta-catalyzed stereospecific polymerization yields cis-1,4-polyisoprene, used as a synthetic substitute for natural rubber in tire treads, conveyor belts, footwear, and medical gloves.
> 2. **Copolymer with Isobutylene → Butyl Rubber (IIR):** 2–3 mol% isoprene incorporated into polyisobutylene provides the unsaturation required for sulfur vulcanization, used in inner tubes and tire inner liners.

---

### **Q2. State the role of NMP in the extractive distillation of C₅ streams for isoprene recovery.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`

**Model Answer:**

> **N-Methyl-2-Pyrrolidone (NMP)** is a **polar, aprotic, high-boiling solvent** used as the extractive agent in the recovery of isoprene from C₅ cracker streams. Its function is to **selectively increase the relative volatility** of the C₅ dienes (isoprene, piperylenes) over the C₅ paraffins and olefins.
>
> - NMP preferentially solvates the **π-electrons of the conjugated dienes** through dipole–π interactions, leaving paraffins and mono-olefins in the raffinate overhead.
> - NMP is non-corrosive, thermally stable, easily regenerable by vacuum distillation, and has largely replaced the older, toxic **acetonitrile (ACN)** route in modern plants.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]`

> **(a)** With the help of a neat flow diagram, describe the **extractive distillation process for the recovery of isoprene from the C₅ fraction of steam cracking**. Explain the role of NMP and the dimerization step in your answer. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the **standard Gibbs free energy change** and the **equilibrium constant $K_p$** at **T = 873 K** for the catalytic dehydrogenation of isopentane to isoprene, given $\Delta H^\circ = +210\,\text{kJ·mol}^{-1}$ and $\Delta S^\circ = +314\,\text{J·mol}^{-1}\text{·K}^{-1}$. Comment on the spontaneity of the reaction. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Part A(a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Source of C₅ Feed:**
The C₅ fraction (~2–5 wt% of cracker effluent) is obtained from the **crude C₅ cut** of a steam cracker. Initial composition: isoprene ~10–15 wt%, CPD ~15 wt%, piperylenes ~8 wt%, C₅-olefins ~20 wt%, C₅-paraffins ~30 wt%.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Dimerization of CPD:**
CPD is the most reactive component (dienophilic self-reactivity). It is selectively dimerized to **dicyclopentadiene (DCPD, BP 170 °C)** at 110–130 °C in a dimerizer. DCPD is easily separated downstream because of its much higher boiling point.

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Extractive Distillation with NMP:**
The CPD-depleted C₅ cut enters an extractive distillation column using NMP as the polar solvent. NMP preferentially dissolves the dienes (isoprene, piperylenes) by **dipole–π and donor–acceptor interactions**, allowing the C₅ paraffins and mono-olefins to exit overhead as raffinate. The solvent is regenerated in a separate stripping column and recycled.

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Final Distillation:**
The diene-rich extract is fractionated to give **polymer-grade isoprene (≥99.5 wt%)** and separate piperylene streams.

---

#### Part A(b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks) — State the reaction and data:**

$$ \text{C}_5\text{H}_{12(g)} \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{C}_5\text{H}_{8(g)} \;+\; 2\,\text{H}_{2(g)} $$

Given: $\Delta H^\circ = +210\,\text{kJ·mol}^{-1}$, $\Delta S^\circ = +314\,\text{J·mol}^{-1}\text{·K}^{-1}$, $T = 873\,\text{K}$, $R = 8.314\,\text{J·mol}^{-1}\text{·K}^{-1}$.

**Step 2 (3 marks) — Apply Gibbs equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta G^\circ &= \Delta H^\circ - T\Delta S^\circ \\
               &= (+210{,}000) - (873)(314) \\
               &= 210{,}000 - 274{,}122 \\
               &= -64{,}122\,\text{J·mol}^{-1} \approx -64.1\,\text{kJ·mol}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Compute $K_p$ and interpret:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_p &= \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta G^\circ}{RT}\right) = \exp\!\left(\frac{64{,}122}{8.314 \times 873}\right) = \exp(8.834) \approx 6.9 \times 10^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** At 873 K, $\Delta G^\circ < 0$ and $K_p \gg 1$, so the reaction is **spontaneous** despite being endothermic. The positive entropy (1 mol gas → 3 mol gas) drives the reaction. Below ~720 K, $K_p$ drops below unity, justifying the high operating temperature of industrial dehydrogenation reactors.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]`

> **(a)** Explain the **Prins reaction route** for the synthesis of isoprene from isobutylene and formaldehyde. Include balanced chemical equations, catalyst systems, and operating conditions for each step. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare the **cis-1,4-, trans-1,4-, 1,2-, and 3,4-microstructures** of polyisoprene in terms of (i) catalyst used, (ii) stereochemistry, and (iii) major end-use application. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Part B(a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks) — The Prins Reaction (Step 1):**

The Prins condensation of isobutylene with aqueous formaldehyde in the presence of a strong acid catalyst (dilute **H₂SO₄**, ~1–5 wt%, at **60–90 °C**) produces **3-methyl-3-buten-1-ol (3M3B1OL)**:

$$ \text{CH}_2\!=\!\text{C(CH}_3)_2 \;+\; \text{HCHO} \;\xrightarrow{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4,\; 70\,°C}\; \text{(CH}_3)_2\text{C=CH-CH}_2\text{OH} $$

Mechanism: protonation of formaldehyde → electrophilic attack on isobutylene's C=C → carbocation intermediate → deprotonation to alcohol.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Dehydration (Step 2):**
The intermediate 3M3B1OL is dehydrated over a **H₃PO₄/SiO₂ (or alumina phosphate)** catalyst at **220–280 °C** to yield crude isoprene:

$$ \text{(CH}_3)_2\text{C=CH-CH}_2\text{OH} \;\xrightarrow{\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4/\text{SiO}_2,\; 250\,°C}\; \text{CH}_2\!=\!\text{C(CH}_3)\text{-CH}\!=\!\text{CH}_2 \;+\; \text{H}_2\text{O} $$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Purification:**
The crude stream is cooled, water is decanted, and the isoprene is rectified to **>99.5 wt% polymer grade** via two distillation columns. Tertiary butanol (a by-product from formaldehyde addition across two isobutylenes) and unconverted 3M3B1OL are recycled.

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Engineering Significance:**
This route decouples isoprene production from steam-cracker economics, making it attractive in regions with **cheap isobutylene and methanol-derived formaldehyde**.

---

#### Part B(b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

| Microstructure | Catalyst System | Stereochemistry | Major Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **cis-1,4** | Ziegler–Natta (TiCl₄ / AlR₃) or Nd-based | Both substituents same side → regular helical coil | Tires, mechanical goods, synthetic natural rubber |
| **trans-1,4** | Alfin catalyst (allyl Na/NaCl/RCl) | Substituents opposite side → rigid, crystalline | Gutta-percha substitutes, golf-ball covers, cable insulation |
| **1,2-Addition** | Anionic (BuLi) with polar modifiers | Vinyl pendant group on C3 | Specialty elastomers, reactive intermediates |
| **3,4-Addition** | Anionic (BuLi) at low T | Isopropenyl pendant on C1 | Reactive polyisoprene, functionalizable elastomers |
| **Mixed (1,4 + 3,4)** | Anionic (BuLi) in hydrocarbon | Variable | Liquid polyisoprene for adhesives, sealants |

> [!NOTE]
> **Cis-content is the key quality metric.** Commercial IR grades (Natsyn 2200, Cariflex IR-307) report **>96 % cis-1,4 content**. Low cis-content polyisoprene is a non-tacky, low-elasticity product — unsuitable for tire applications.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students LOSE MARKS on Isoprene questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the methyl group on C2.** Many students draw the isoprene structure as straight butadiene (no –CH₃). This is an instant 1-mark deduction.
> 2. **Confusing isoprene with isopentane.** Isopentane (C₅H₁₂) is a *paraffin* and a *feedstock*; isoprene (C₅H₈) is the *product*. Use the dehydrogenation equation to clarify.
> 3. **Missing the "why" in the dimerization step.** Just stating "CPD is removed" is incomplete — explain *that DCPD is formed (BP 170 °C) for easy separation*. 1 mark is reserved for the "why."
> 4. **Sign errors in $\Delta G^\circ$.** $T\Delta S$ is **subtracted** from $\Delta H^\circ$ (not added). A positive $\Delta H$ and positive $\Delta S$ do NOT cancel automatically — check the *sign* of $T\Delta S$ vs $\Delta H$.
> 5. **Naming the catalyst for the Prins route as "concentrated H₂SO₄."** The correct industrial answer is *dilute* H₂SO₄ at 1–5 wt%. Concentrated acid causes sulfonation and charring.
> 6. **Omitting units in numerical answers.** $\Delta G^\circ = -64.1$ (without kJ·mol⁻¹) loses 0.5 mark. Always include units.
> 7. **Equating "yield" with "conversion" or "selectivity."** These are three distinct metrics — see the boxed tip in §3.3.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Isoprene Module 3 (KTU 2024):**
>
> - **Identity:** 2-methyl-1,3-butadiene, C₅H₈, **MW = 68.12 g·mol⁻¹**, **BP = 34.07 °C**, **ρ = 0.681 g·cm⁻³**.
> - **Conjugated diene** → 1,2- and 1,4-addition chemistry.
> - **Primary industrial source:** Recovery from C₅ steam-cracker streams via **extractive distillation (NMP solvent)**.
> - **Synthetic routes:** (1) Isopentane dehydrogenation (Cr₂O₃/Al₂O₃, 600 °C), (2) Prins reaction (isobutylene + HCHO), (3) Acetone–acetylene (obsolete).
> - **Polymerization microstructures:** cis-1,4 (natural rubber analogue, ZN catalyst), trans-1,4 (Alfin), 1,2/3,4 (anionic).
> - **Key equilibrium numeric:** Dehydrogenation $\Delta H^\circ \approx +210\,\text{kJ·mol}^{-1}$, $\Delta S^\circ \approx +314\,\text{J·mol}^{-1}\text{·K}^{-1}$ → at **873 K**, $K_p \approx 6.9 \times 10^{3}$.
> - **End-uses:** Polyisoprene rubber (tires, gloves), butyl rubber (inner tubes), SIS block copolymers (adhesives), terpene resins (tackifiers).
> - **Examiner traps to avoid:** Wrong structure (no –CH₃), yield/selectivity/conversion confusion, sign errors in $\Delta G$, omitting units, citing obsolete Reppe route as "current."
> - **"Three C's" for the exam:** **Conversion, Selectivity, Cis-content** — know each one, its definition, and its numerical anchor.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pyrolysis Gasoline as Aromatic Feedstock
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT741 - PETROCHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pyrolysis Gasoline as Aromatic Feedstock

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Pyrolysis Gasoline (Pygas)** is the C₅–C₉ (and heavier) liquid hydrocarbon fraction obtained as a high-value byproduct from the **steam cracking (pyrolysis)** of hydrocarbon feedstocks such as ethane, propane, butane, naphtha, gas oil, or vacuum gas oil in olefin plants. It is a complex mixture of paraffins, olefins, diolefins (dienes), naphthenes, and aromatics, and is industrially regarded as the **most concentrated and economically attractive liquid source of benzene, toluene, and xylenes (BTX)** in the petrochemical industry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Pygas is the **primary source of benzene** in modern steam-cracking-based petrochemical complexes. Approximately **1 kg of pygas is produced per 2.5–3.0 kg of ethylene** generated in a naphtha cracker.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "By-Product Treasure Chest"

Imagine a **fruit juice factory** that primarily sells packaged apple juice. While pressing the apples, the company also collects the **apple peels, seeds, and pulp residue** — parts originally considered "waste." Clever chemists later discovered that these residues contain high concentrations of pectin, vitamin E, and natural flavors that are *more valuable* than the juice itself. The factory now sells these "waste" extracts at premium prices.

Pygas is exactly this: it is a **byproduct stream from ethylene plants** that, once treated, becomes a richer, more aromatic-dense feedstock than crude oil reformate. Steam crackers aim to maximize ethylene, but the leftover liquid stream (pygas) is *deliberately* recovered and upgraded because it is the **single largest liquid source of benzene, toluene, and xylenes (BTX)** in a modern petrochemical complex.

## 1.3 Origin and Position in the Steam Cracking Process

Pygas is recovered downstream of the **cracked-gas compressor** in an olefin plant. The cracked gas leaving the furnace is rapidly quenched, and after acid-gas removal and drying, it passes through a series of **demethanizer, deethanizer, depropanizer, and debutanizer** distillation columns. The overhead of the debutanizer (or top of the C₃/C₄ splitter) gives **fuel gas**, while the **bottom product of the debutanizer** — the C₅+ liquid — is **raw pyrolysis gasoline**.

## 1.4 Standard Quality Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical Properties of Raw Pygas (Before Treatment):**
> - **Density at 15 °C:** ~0.80–0.85 g/cm³
> - **Boiling Range:** 30–220 °C (ASTM D86)
> - **Bromine Number:** 50–80 g Br₂/100 g (indicates high unsaturation)
> - **Diene Number (Maleic Anhydride Value):** 25–50
> - **Sulfur Content:** 100–1000 ppmw
> - **Aromatic Content:** 60–80 wt%
> - **Color:** Yellow to dark brown (rapidly oxidizes/polymerizes)

The very high bromine number and color instability of raw pygas are the precise reasons it **cannot be used as motor gasoline** and must be hydrogenated before any downstream application.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Pygas is the Best Aromatic Feedstock

Aromatics (BTX) can be obtained from three principal liquid sources:

| Source | Typical BTX Yield (wt% in feed) | Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| Catalytic Reformate | 60–70 | Toluene & xylenes dominant; benzene limited by reformer thermodynamics |
| **Pyrolysis Gasoline** | **60–80** | **Benzene dominant — best liquid source of benzene** |
| Coal Tar | 50–60 | Naphthalene-heavy; declining importance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Reformers operate at high temperature and pressure that *thermodynamically suppress* benzene formation. Steam crackers, however, operate at **800–900 °C** with very short residence times — conditions that *favor the formation* of benzene, toluene, and xylenes via Diels–Alder cyclization of C₄/C₅ dienes with olefins. This is why **a naphtha cracker producing 1 million tonnes/year of ethylene co-produces ~500,000 tonnes/year of pygas containing ~350,000 tonnes/year of BTX**.

## 2.2 Composition Profile of Naphtha-Cracker Pygas

A typical naphtha-based pygas has the following composition (after hydrogenation):

- **Benzene (C₆H₆):** 20–30 wt%
- **Toluene (C₇H₈):** 12–18 wt%
- **Xylenes + Ethylbenzene (C₈H₁₀):** 8–12 wt%
- **C₉ Aromatics:** 6–10 wt%
- **Styrene (raw, before hydro):** 4–6 wt%
- **Cyclopentadiene (CPD, raw):** 5–15 wt%
- **Isoprene, Piperylene, Pentenes:** 5–10 wt%
- **Naphthenes & Non-aromatic C₅–C₉:** 5–10 wt%
- **Sulfur Compounds (thiophene, mercaptans):** 100–1000 ppmw

## 2.3 The Three Critical Problems of Raw Pygas

For raw pygas to be processed downstream, three issues must be resolved:

1. **Diolefins & Reactive Olefins (e.g., cyclopentadiene, isoprene, styrene):** Polymerize and form gums → must be selectively hydrogenated.
2. **Sulfur Compounds:** Poison noble-metal catalysts in downstream BTX extraction units → must be removed via **hydrodesulfurization (HDS)**.
3. **Color & Gum Instability:** Even trace dienes cause dark color and peroxides → hydrogenation stabilizes the stream.

## 2.4 Two-Stage Hydrogenation of Pygas

Pygas is treated industrially in **two sequential catalytic stages**:

### Stage 1 — First-Stage Hydrogenation (Diene Saturation)
- **Reactor Type:** Fixed-bed, adiabatic, liquid-phase
- **Catalyst:** Palladium (Pd) on alumina support (0.3–0.5 wt% Pd)
- **Temperature:** 40–100 °C
- **Pressure:** 20–40 bar
- **Purpose:** Selective saturation of **diolefins → mono-olefins** and acetylenes
- **Reactor Configuration:** Typically **two reactors in series with inter-stage cooling** to control the highly exothermic reaction (ΔH ≈ −200 kJ/mol for CPD)

> [!NOTE]
> The first stage is intentionally operated at **mild temperature** so that olefins (mono-olefins) and aromatics are *not* hydrogenated — only the dienes. Pd catalysts are excellent for this chemoselectivity.

### Stage 2 — Second-Stage Hydrogenation (HDS + Olefin Saturation)
- **Reactor Type:** Fixed-bed, trickle-bed
- **Catalyst:** Co–Mo or Ni–Mo on alumina (sulfided form)
- **Temperature:** 280–380 °C
- **Pressure:** 30–50 bar
- **Purpose:** 
  - Hydrodesulfurization: thiophene → H₂S + hydrocarbons
  - Saturation of remaining olefins → paraffins
  - Saturation of residual styrene → ethylbenzene
  - Conversion of organic sulfur to H₂S (then stripped)

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for Pygas Hydrogenation and BTX Recovery**

| # | Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stoichiometric H₂ for diene saturation | $n_{H_2} = \sum_i \nu_i \cdot n_i$ | mol | Calculating H₂ consumption in Stage-1 |
| 2 | Cyclopentadiene (CPD) hydrogenation | $\mathrm{C_5H_6 + 2\,H_2 \rightarrow C_5H_{10}}$ | — | ΔH = −210 kJ/mol (Stage 1) |
| 3 | Styrene hydrogenation | $\mathrm{C_6H_5{-}CH{=}CH_2 + H_2 \rightarrow C_6H_5{-}C_2H_5}$ | — | Stage 2; converts styrene → ethylbenzene |
| 4 | Thiophene HDS | $\mathrm{C_4H_4S + 4\,H_2 \rightarrow C_4H_{10} + H_2S}$ | — | Stage 2; sulfur removal |
| 5 | Heat of reaction (general hydrogenation) | $\Delta H_{hyd} \approx -120\, \text{kJ/mol per C=C}$ | kJ/mol | Reactor temperature-rise estimation |
| 6 | Bromine Number → Olefins content | $\text{Olefins (wt\%)} \approx \frac{BN \times M_{olefin}}{160}$ | wt% | Quality control of pygas |
| 7 | Aromatic recovery by extraction | $Y_{arom} = \frac{m_{BTX,\,out}}{m_{feed}} \times 100$ | % | Plant yield KPI |
| 8 | Solvent-to-feed ratio | $S/F = \frac{m_{solvent}}{m_{feed}}$ | mass basis | Extractive distillation design |
| 9 | Adiabatic temperature rise | $\Delta T_{ad} = \frac{-\Delta H_{rxn}}{\sum n_i C_{p,i}}$ | K | Stage-1 reactor safety |
| 10 | Hydrogen consumption (overall) | $H_{2,\,cons} = \frac{V_{H_2,\,STP}}{m_{pygas}}$ | Nm³/tonne | Plant H₂ balance |

> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Equations 2, 3, 4, and 9 appear most frequently in 14-mark Part B derivations. Memorize the **stoichiometry of CPD hydrogenation and thiophene HDS** — these are exam favorites.

## 2.6 Why Steam-Cracker Pygas is Aromatic-Rich: Mechanistic Insight

The Diels–Alder chemistry inside the cracking furnace is what builds the aromatic ring:

$$\mathrm{CH_2{=}CH{-}CH{=}CH_2 \;(butadiene) + CH_2{=}CH_2 \;(ethylene) \xrightarrow{\,\Delta\,} C_6H_6 \;(benzene)}$$

$$\mathrm{2\,C_4H_6 \xrightarrow{\,\Delta\,} C_8H_{10} \;(xylene)}$$

$$\mathrm{3\,C_2H_2 \xrightarrow{\,\Delta\,} C_6H_6 \;(benzene)}$$

These thermal cyclization and dehydrogenation reactions are kinetically favored at **800–900 °C** with residence times of **0.1–0.5 seconds** — explaining the high BTX yield in pygas.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Hydrogen Consumption in Stage-1 Pygas Hydrogenation

A naphtha-cracker pygas feed of **10,000 kg/h** has the following unsaturated components entering the first-stage hydrogenator:

| Component | Mass Flow (kg/h) | Molecular Mass (g/mol) | Moles (kmol/h) | Diene? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cyclopentadiene (CPD) | 800 | 66 | 12.12 | Yes |
| Isoprene | 400 | 68 | 5.88 | Yes |
| Piperylene | 300 | 68 | 4.41 | Yes |
| Styrene | 500 | 104 | 4.81 | No (mono-olefin) |
| Pentene-1 | 350 | 70 | 5.00 | No |
| Other olefins | 1,200 | varies | 18.00 | No |

> **Required:** Calculate the **Stage-1 H₂ consumption (kmol/h and Nm³/h)** and the **adiabatic temperature rise** if all diolefins are selectively hydrogenated to mono-olefins and the reactor Cp,avg ≈ 2.0 kJ/(kg·K) and H₂ Cp ≈ 14.3 kJ/(kmol·K).

### Step 1 — Write Balanced Reactions

The selective hydrogenation of each diene to its corresponding mono-olefin consumes **1 mole of H₂ per C=C reduced**:

$$\mathrm{C_5H_6 + H_2 \rightarrow C_5H_8} \quad (\text{CPD} \rightarrow \text{cyclopentene}) \qquad \Delta H = -105 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

$$\mathrm{C_5H_8 + H_2 \rightarrow C_5H_{10}} \quad (\text{isoprene} \rightarrow \text{methylbutene}) \qquad \Delta H = -110 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

$$\mathrm{C_5H_8 + H_2 \rightarrow C_5H_{10}} \quad (\text{piperylene} \rightarrow \text{pentene}) \qquad \Delta H = -110 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

> **Note:** Stage-1 stops at the mono-olefin level (Pd catalyst chemoselectivity), so only **1 mol H₂ per mol of diene** is consumed at this stage. The remaining olefin (and styrene) is dealt with in Stage 2.

### Step 2 — Moles of H₂ Consumed

$$\mathrm{n_{H_2} = n_{CPD} + n_{isoprene} + n_{piperylene}}$$

$$\mathrm{n_{H_2} = 12.12 + 5.88 + 4.41 = 22.41 \; \text{kmol/h}}$$

### Step 3 — Convert to Standard Volume (Nm³/h)

At STP (0 °C, 1 atm), 1 kmol of ideal gas occupies **22.414 Nm³**:

$$\mathrm{V_{H_2} = 22.41 \times 22.414 = 502.3 \; Nm^3/h}$$

> **Conclusion for H₂ Consumption:**
> $$\mathrm{H_2\;consumption = 22.41 \; \text{kmol/h} = 502.3 \; \text{Nm}^3/\text{h} \approx 45 \; \text{kg/h}}$$

### Step 4 — Heat Released (ΔH_total)

Using average ΔH = −108 kJ/mol for diene → mono-olefin:

$$\mathrm{Q_{released} = 22{,}410 \;\text{mol/h} \times 108 \;\text{kJ/mol} = 2{,}420{,}280 \;\text{kJ/h} = 672.3 \; \text{kW}}$$

### Step 5 — Adiabatic Temperature Rise

Total mass in reactor (liquid pygas + dissolved H₂) ≈ **10,000 kg/h** of feed plus **45 kg/h** H₂:

$$\mathrm{m_{total} \approx 10{,}045 \; kg/h}$$

$$\mathrm{C_{p,avg} \approx 2.0 \; kJ/(kg \cdot K)}$$

$$\mathrm{\Delta T_{ad} = \frac{Q_{released}}{m_{total} \cdot C_{p,avg}} = \frac{2{,}420{,}280}{10{,}045 \times 2.0} = 120.5 \; K}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Engineering Implication:** A 120 K adiabatic rise is **dangerously high** for a Pd catalyst that operates at 40–100 °C. This is why **Stage-1 reactors are designed in 2–3 beds in series with inter-bed H₂ quench and liquid coolers**, keeping ΔT per bed below ~30 K.

### Step 3.1 — Final Stage-1 Outlet Composition

| Component | In (kmol/h) | Consumed | Out (kmol/h) | Form |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cyclopentadiene | 12.12 | 12.12 | 0 | → Cyclopentene |
| Isoprene | 5.88 | 5.88 | 0 | → Methylbutene |
| Piperylene | 4.41 | 4.41 | 0 | → Pentene |
| Styrene | 4.81 | 0 | 4.81 | Unchanged (Stage 2 will convert) |
| Pentene-1 | 5.00 | 0 | 5.00 | Unchanged |
| H₂ | 22.41 + small excess | 22.41 | small excess | — |

## 3.2 Derivation: BTX Recovery Yield from Hydrogenated Pygas

**Given:** Hydrogenated pygas analysis (after Stage 2) on a 10,000 kg/h feed basis:

- Benzene: 2,500 kg/h
- Toluene: 1,600 kg/h
- Xylenes + EB: 1,000 kg/h
- C₉+ aromatics: 800 kg/h
- Non-aromatic C₅–C₉: 4,000 kg/h
- Losses (column, etc.): 100 kg/h

**Total BTX recovered** (excluding C₉+ unless the unit extracts them too):

$$\mathrm{Y_{BTX} = \frac{2500 + 1600 + 1000}{10{,}000} \times 100 = 51 \; wt\%}$$

If C₉+ is also recovered (some units integrate C₉ transalkylation with toluene to make xylenes):

$$\mathrm{Y_{BTX+C9} = \frac{2500 + 1600 + 1000 + 800}{10{,}000} \times 100 = 59 \; wt\%}$$

> **Engineering Benchmark:** World-class pygas-based BTX plants report overall **aromatic recovery of 98–99.5%** of the in-feed aromatic content, using extractive distillation with **N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP), sulfolane, or N-formylmorpholine (NFM)** as the selective polar solvent.

## 3.3 Derivation: Adiabatic Conversion of Styrene to Ethylbenzene (Stage 2)

For the **styrene hydrogenation reaction** in Stage 2:

$$\mathrm{C_6H_5{-}CH{=}CH_2 + H_2 \rightarrow C_6H_5{-}C_2H_5} \qquad \Delta H = -118 \; \text{kJ/mol}$$

With 4.81 kmol/h styrene entering Stage 2:

$$\mathrm{Q_{Stage2,\,styrene} = 4810 \; \text{mol/h} \times 118 \; \text{kJ/mol} = 567{,}580 \; \text{kJ/h} = 157.7 \; \text{kW}}$$

Adding thiophene HDS contribution (~ 4.81 kmol/h × 4 H₂ × 220 kJ/mol per H₂ addition):
(Approximating HDS enthalpy as ~ −100 kJ/mol thiophene converted, the contribution is roughly **50 kW**.)

> **Total Stage-2 heat duty ≈ 200 kW**, manageable in a single trickle-bed reactor with inter-stage H₂ injection.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Pygas Recovery & Treatment Block Flow Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **complete process architecture** of a naphtha-cracker pygas recovery and hydrogenation train, including BTX extraction:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Cracked Gas from Steam Cracker Furnace 800-900 C] --> B[Transfer Line Exchanger TLE Quench]
    B --> C[Cracked Gas Compressor 3-4 Stages]
    C --> D[Acid Gas Removal Caustic / Amine]
    D --> E[Drying Molecular Sieve]
    E --> F[Demethanizer]
    F --> G[Deethanizer]
    G --> H[Depropanizer]
    H --> I[Debutanizer]
    I --> J[Fuel Gas C1-C4 Overhead]
    I --> K[Raw Pyrolysis Gasoline C5+ Bottom]

    K --> L[Stage 1 Hydrogenation Pd-Al2O3 40-100 C 25 bar]
    L --> M[Stage 2 Hydrogenation CoMo or NiMo 280-380 C 40 bar HDS]
    M --> N[Stabilizer Column H2S and Light Ends Removal]
    N --> O[Extractive Distillation with NMP or Sulfolane]
    O --> P[Benzene Column]
    O --> Q[Toluene Column]
    O --> R[Xylene Column]
    O --> S[Non-Aromatic Raffinate Stream]

    P --> T[Benzene Product 99.9 percent]
    Q --> U[Toluene Product]
    R --> V[Ethylbenzene and Xylenes Product]
    S --> W[Returned to Cracker or Gasoline Pool]

    style A fill:#fbb,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style K fill:#ff9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style L fill:#9cf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style M fill:#9cf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style O fill:#9f9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style T fill:#cfc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style U fill:#cfc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style V fill:#cfc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Two-Stage Hydrogenation Reactor Architecture (Detailed)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Pygas Feed] --> B[Feed-Effluent Exchanger]
    B --> C[Heater Trim]
    C --> D[Stage 1 Reactor Bed 1 Pd Catalyst]
    D --> E[Inter-Bed H2 Quench]
    E --> F[Stage 1 Reactor Bed 2 Pd Catalyst]
    F --> G[Inter-Bed Cooler]
    G --> H[Heater Reheat 280 C]
    H --> I[Stage 2 Reactor Bed 1 NiMo Catalyst]
    I --> J[Inter-Bed Quench]
    J --> K[Stage 2 Reactor Bed 2 NiMo Catalyst]
    K --> L[High Pressure Separator]
    L --> M[Recycle H2 Compressor]
    L --> N[Hot Separator Liquid to Stabilizer]
    M --> O[Make-up H2 Inlet]

    style D fill:#cce,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style F fill:#cce,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style I fill:#eec,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style K fill:#eec,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.3 BTX Recovery Train — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hydrogenated Pygas] --> B[Extractive Distillation Column with NMP Solvent]
    B --> C[Raffinate Non-Aromatic C5-C9 Top]
    B --> D[Aromatic Rich Solvent Bottom]
    D --> E[Solvent Recovery Stripper]
    E --> F[Crude BTX Overhead]
    E --> G[Lean Solvent Recycle to Column B]
    F --> H[Benzene Fractionator]
    F --> I[Toluene Fractionator]
    F --> J[Xylene Fractionator]
    H --> K[Pure Benzene Product]
    I --> L[Pure Toluene Product]
    J --> M[Pure Xylenes and Ethylbenzene]
    C --> N[Returned to Steam Cracker Recycle]
    J --> O[C9 Aromatics to Transalkylation Unit]

    style B fill:#9f9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style K fill:#cfc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style L fill:#cfc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style M fill:#cfc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Define pyrolysis gasoline (pygas). Why is it considered a premium feedstock for BTX production compared to catalytic reformate?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Pyrolysis gasoline is the **C₅–C₉+ liquid hydrocarbon byproduct** obtained from the steam cracking of hydrocarbons (naphtha, ethane, gas oil) in ethylene plants, recovered as the bottoms of the debutanizer column.

It is a **premium BTX feedstock** for two reasons:
1. **High aromatic concentration** (60–80 wt%), with benzene as the dominant component (20–30 wt%), whereas reformate is thermodynamically limited in benzene.
2. **Origin from high-temperature (800–900 °C) Diels–Alder and cyclization reactions**, which favor aromatic ring formation, in contrast to catalytic reformers that operate at lower severity.

> **Mark Distribution:** Definition (1 mark), Reason 1 (1 mark), Reason 2 (1 mark).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is the role of the two-stage hydrogenation in pygas treatment? Name the catalysts used in each stage.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **two-stage hydrogenation** stabilizes raw pygas by sequentially removing reactive impurities:
- **Stage 1 — Selective Diene Hydrogenation:** Converts diolefins (cyclopentadiene, isoprene, piperylene) to mono-olefins, preventing gum formation. Catalyst: **Palladium (Pd) on alumina (Al₂O₃)**, operated at 40–100 °C, 20–40 bar.
- **Stage 2 — Hydrodesulfurization + Olefin Saturation:** Removes sulfur compounds (thiophenes → H₂S) and saturates remaining olefins and styrene. Catalyst: **Sulfided Co–Mo or Ni–Mo on alumina**, operated at 280–380 °C, 30–50 bar.

> **Mark Distribution:** Stage 1 role (1 mark), Stage 2 role (1 mark), Catalysts (1 mark).

---

## **Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)**

### **QUESTION A — [14 Marks]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat process flow diagram, describe the **recovery and treatment of pyrolysis gasoline from a naphtha steam cracking unit.** Discuss the **role of each distillation column** in the cracked-gas separation train. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **two-stage catalytic hydrogenation of raw pygas** in detail. Include balanced chemical equations, operating conditions, catalyst specifications, and the **engineering reason for using two separate stages.** **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer for Question A**

#### **Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Cracked Gas Recovery Train:**

1. **Transfer Line Exchanger (TLE):** Rapidly quenches the cracked gas from 800 °C to ~400 °C, generating high-pressure steam and arresting secondary reactions. *(1 mark)*

2. **Cracked Gas Compressor (CGC):** Compresses the gas to ~15–20 bar to enable downstream distillation. Operates in 4–5 stages with inter-stage cooling and acid-gas removal. *(1 mark)*

3. **Acid-Gas Removal (Caustic/Amine Treating):** Removes CO₂ and H₂S to prevent downstream corrosion and catalyst poisoning. *(0.5 mark)*

4. **Drying (Molecular Sieves):** Removes moisture to prevent hydrate formation in the cold section. *(0.5 mark)*

5. **Distillation Train (in order):**
   - **Demethanizer:** Removes CH₄ and H₂ as overhead.
   - **Deethanizer:** Separates C₂ (ethylene/ethane) as overhead.
   - **Depropanizer:** Separates C₃ (propylene/propane) as overhead.
   - **Debutanizer:** Separates C₄ (butadiene/butenes) as overhead. **The bottom product of the debutanizer is raw pyrolysis gasoline (C₅+).** *(3 marks)*

6. The **raw pygas** is sent to the hydrogenation unit for stabilization before BTX extraction. *(1 mark)*

#### **Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Two-Stage Hydrogenation:**

**Stage 1 — Selective Diene Hydrogenation:**
- **Catalyst:** 0.3–0.5 wt% Pd on γ-Al₂O₃
- **Reactor Type:** Adiabatic fixed-bed, liquid phase
- **T = 40–100 °C, P = 20–40 bar**
- **Reactions:**
$$\mathrm{C_5H_6 + H_2 \rightarrow C_5H_8} \quad \text{(CPD → cyclopentene)} \qquad \Delta H \approx -105 \text{ kJ/mol}$$
$$\mathrm{C_5H_8 + H_2 \rightarrow C_5H_{10}} \quad \text{(isoprene → methylbutene)}$$
$$\mathrm{C_5H_8 + H_2 \rightarrow C_5H_{10}} \quad \text{(piperylene → pentene)}$$

**Stage 2 — Hydrodesulfurization + Olefin Saturation:**
- **Catalyst:** Sulfided Co–Mo or Ni–Mo on Al₂O₃
- **Reactor Type:** Trickle-bed fixed-bed
- **T = 280–380 °C, P = 30–50 bar**
- **Reactions:**
$$\mathrm{C_6H_5{-}CH{=}CH_2 + H_2 \rightarrow C_6H_5{-}C_2H_5} \quad \text{(styrene → ethylbenzene)} \qquad \Delta H = -118 \text{ kJ/mol}$$
$$\mathrm{C_4H_4S + 4\,H_2 \rightarrow C_4H_{10} + H_2S} \quad \text{(thiophene → n-butane + H}_2\text{S)}$$

**Why Two Stages?** *(2 marks)*
- **Chemoselectivity:** Pd catalyst at low T is highly selective for diene → mono-olefin; it does *not* attack aromatics or mono-olefins. Performing this at high T (Stage 2 conditions) would *unselectively* saturate valuable aromatics.
- **Thermal Management:** Stage 1 diene hydrogenation is highly exothermic (ΔT_ad ≈ 120 K); operating at low T with inter-bed quench prevents runaway.
- **Catalyst Stability:** Sulfur would *poison* the Pd catalyst in Stage 1. Stage 2 must come *after* HDS, but a sulfided catalyst is needed for the HDS reaction — this is fundamentally incompatible with Pd in the same reactor.

---

### **QUESTION B (Alternative to Question A) — [14 Marks]**

**(a)** Discuss the **composition of naphtha-cracker pyrolysis gasoline**, highlighting the major aromatic and non-aromatic components. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pygas feed of **12,000 kg/h** contains **6 wt% cyclopentadiene**. Calculate: **(i)** the kmol/h of CPD in the feed, **(ii)** the H₂ required for complete hydrogenation of CPD to cyclopentane in kmol/h and Nm³/h, and **(iii)** the heat released (kW) using ΔH = −210 kJ/mol for full saturation. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer for Question B**

#### **Part (a) — 7 marks**

A typical **hydrogenated naphtha-cracker pygas** has the following composition (mark distribution in brackets):

1. **Benzene (C₆H₆): 20–30 wt%** — *most valuable product.* (1 mark)
2. **Toluene (C₇H₈): 12–18 wt%** — used for toluene di-isocyanate (TDI) and as transalkylation feed. (0.5 mark)
3. **Xylenes + Ethylbenzene (C₈H₁₀): 8–12 wt%** — feed for para-xylene, phthalic anhydride. (0.5 mark)
4. **C₉ Aromatics (e.g., trimethylbenzenes, indane): 6–10 wt%** — transalkylated with toluene to produce additional xylenes. (0.5 mark)
5. **Styrene (raw): 4–6 wt%** — *reactive; hydrogenated to ethylbenzene in Stage 2.* (1 mark)
6. **Cyclopentadiene (CPD): 5–15 wt% (raw)** — *most reactive diene; recovered as DCPD or hydrogenated in Stage 1.* (1 mark)
7. **Isoprene & Piperylene (C₅ dienes): 5–8 wt%** — selectively hydrogenated in Stage 1. (0.5 mark)
8. **Non-aromatic C₅–C₉ paraffins/naphthenes: 5–10 wt%** — form the raffinate stream. (1 mark)
9. **Sulfur compounds (thiophenes, mercaptans): 100–1000 ppmw** — removed in Stage 2 HDS. (0.5 mark)
10. **Trace gums, peroxides, and nitrogen compounds (acetonitrile, acrylonitrile traces).** (0.5 mark)

#### **Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** Feed = 12,000 kg/h, CPD = 6 wt%, M(CPD, C₅H₆) = 66 g/mol, ΔH = −210 kJ/mol.

**(i) Moles of CPD in feed:**

$$\mathrm{m_{CPD} = 12{,}000 \times 0.06 = 720 \; kg/h}$$

$$\mathrm{n_{CPD} = \frac{720{,}000 \; g/h}{66 \; g/mol} = 10{,}909 \; mol/h = 10.91 \; kmol/h} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**(ii) H₂ Required for Full Hydrogenation to Cyclopentane:**

The full-saturation reaction is:
$$\mathrm{C_5H_6 + 2\,H_2 \rightarrow C_5H_{10}} \quad (\text{CPD} \rightarrow \text{cyclopentane})$$

$$\mathrm{n_{H_2} = 2 \times 10.91 = 21.82 \; kmol/h} \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}$$

Converting to Nm³/h:
$$\mathrm{V_{H_2} = 21.82 \times 22.414 = 489.0 \; Nm^3/h} \quad \textbf{(0.5 mark)}$$

**(iii) Heat Released:**

$$\mathrm{Q = n \times \vert \Delta H \vert = 10{,}909 \; \text{mol/h} \times 210 \; \text{kJ/mol} = 2{,}290{,}890 \; \text{kJ/h}}$$

$$\mathrm{Q = \frac{2{,}290{,}890}{3600} = 636.4 \; \text{kW}} \quad \textbf{(3 marks)}$$

> **Conclusion:** Complete hydrogenation of CPD to cyclopentane in this feed consumes **21.82 kmol/h (489 Nm³/h) of H₂** and releases **636 kW of heat** — confirming the **necessity of inter-bed quench and careful temperature control** in industrial Stage-1 reactors.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Wrong stoichiometry for CPD hydrogenation:** Students frequently use *1 mol H₂* instead of *2 mol H₂* when converting CPD all the way to cyclopentane. Stage-1 uses 1 mol H₂ (selective, → cyclopentene); *full* saturation to cyclopentane requires 2 mol H₂. **Read the question wording carefully.**
> 2. **Unit conversion errors:** 1 kmol of ideal gas at STP = 22.414 Nm³ (NOT 22.4 L). Confusing Nm³ with m³ is a common 1-mark loss.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert kJ/h to kW:** kW = kJ/h ÷ 3600. Marks are often split between the two units.
> 4. **Missing the "Why two stages" explanation in Part A(b):** Examiners award 2 marks specifically for the chemoselectivity + thermal management + catalyst poisoning arguments. A one-line answer loses both marks.

---

## 📋 **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)**

- ✅ **Pygas = C₅+ liquid from steam cracker** debutanizer bottoms; co-produced with ethylene at ~0.30–0.40 kg pygas per kg ethylene.
- ✅ **Composition (raw):** ~60–80 wt% aromatics; rich in **benzene, toluene, xylenes (BTX)**, plus dienes (CPD, isoprene, piperylene), styrene, and sulfur.
- ✅ **Benzene concentration in pygas (20–30 wt%) is the highest among all liquid feedstocks** — making pygas the *premium* benzene source.
- ✅ **Two-stage hydrogenation is mandatory:** Stage 1 (Pd/Al₂O₃, 40–100 °C) selectively saturates dienes; Stage 2 (Co–Mo or Ni–Mo, 280–380 °C) removes sulfur and saturates olefins.
- ✅ **ΔH of diene hydrogenation ≈ −110 kJ/mol** (mono-olefin) and **−210 kJ/mol** (full saturation to paraffin) — Stage-1 reactors need **inter-bed H₂ quench** for ΔT control.
- ✅ **Styrene → Ethylbenzene** is the key Stage-2 upgrading reaction, adding value by feeding the ethylbenzene-dehydrogenation-to-styrene chain.
- ✅ **Thiophene + 4 H₂ → n-butane + H₂S** is the **HDS reaction**; H₂S is stripped in the **stabilizer column**.
- ✅ **BTX recovery uses extractive distillation** with **NMP, sulfolane, or NFM** as the polar solvent; recovery efficiency is **98–99.5%**.
- ✅ **C₅–C₉ non-aromatic raffinate** is typically recycled to the steam cracker as feed.
- ✅ **C₉+ aromatics** are sent to a **transalkylation unit** with toluene to produce additional xylenes (Tatoray / TransPlus / XyMax processes).
- ✅ **Sulfur specification for downstream units:** typically **<1 ppmw** after Stage-2 HDS to protect noble-metal catalysts.
- ✅ **Adiabatic temperature rise calculation** in Stage 1 is a KTU favorite — practice the formula: $\Delta T_{ad} = \frac{-\Delta H_{rxn}}{\sum n_i C_{p,i}}$.
- ✅ **1 kmol of ideal gas at STP = 22.414 Nm³** — memorize this conversion.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
