BTX Derivatives - Ethylbenzene and Styrene
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT741 - PETROCHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** BTX Derivatives - Ethylbenzene and Styrene

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are BTX Derivatives?

The acronym **BTX** stands for **Benzene, Toluene, and Xylenes** — the three most important primary aromatic petrochemicals derived primarily from catalytic reforming of naphtha and steam cracking of hydrocarbons. These aromatics are not used in bulk as fuels; instead, they serve as the **fundamental building blocks** for a vast downstream petrochemical industry.

**BTX derivatives** are the secondary chemicals obtained by chemically modifying BTX aromatics. The two most commercially important derivatives in this module are:

- **Ethylbenzene (EB):** C₆H₅–C₂H₅ — essentially "benzene with an ethyl group bolted on."
- **Styrene (ST or SM):** C₆H₅–CH=CH₂ — "benzene with a vinyl (ethenyl) group," the world's third-largest commodity monomer after ethylene and propylene.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The industrial chemistry of ethylbenzene and styrene is a high-weightage topic. Ethylbenzene is overwhelmingly (>90%) consumed to make styrene, which in turn feeds into polystyrene (PS), expanded polystyrene (EPS), acrylonitrile-butadiene-styrene (ABS), styrene-butadiene rubber (SBR), and unsaturated polyester resins (UPR).

---

## 1.2 Ethylbenzene — Definition and Intuition

### Formal Definition
**Ethylbenzene (EB)** is an aromatic hydrocarbon with molecular formula **C₈H₁₀** (C₆H₅–CH₂–CH₃), consisting of a benzene ring substituted with a single ethyl group at the para or (more commonly synthesized) position. It is a colourless, highly flammable liquid with a gasoline-like odour, immiscible with water but miscible with most organic solvents.

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Bridge Molecule"
Think of ethylbenzene as a **chemical bridge** between two worlds:
- On one side, it is closely related to the **xylene isomers** (all are C₈H₁₀ aromatics) — it is essentially the "missing isomer" where the two carbons of the substituent are joined together (–CH₂–CH₃) instead of being split (–CH₃ and –CH₃ on a ring).
- On the other side, it is the **immediate precursor** to styrene. Industrially, the ethyl group is treated as a *stored* vinyl group. Strip away one hydrogen atom from the carbon attached to the ring, and you instantly get styrene (C₆H₅–CH=CH₂).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key physical constants of Ethylbenzene**
> - Molecular weight: **106.17 g/mol**
> - Boiling point: **136.2 °C**
> - Melting point: **−94.97 °C**
> - Density (25 °C): **0.866 g/cm³**
> - Flash point: **15 °C** (highly flammable)
> - Auto-ignition temperature: **432 °C**

---

## 1.3 Styrene — Definition and Intuition

### Formal Definition
**Styrene (vinylbenzene, phenylethylene, ethenylbenzene)** is an aromatic monomer with the molecular formula **C₈H₈** (C₆H₅–CH=CH₂). It contains a reactive vinyl group (–CH=CH₂) attached directly to a benzene ring. It is a colourless to yellowish oily liquid with a sweet, sharp odour that polymerizes easily (hence the inhibitor **tert-butyl catechol (TBC)** is added at ~10–50 ppm for storage stability).

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Sleeping Polymer"
Styrene is like a **molecule carrying a coiled spring** — the carbon–carbon double bond in the vinyl group is a *latent reactive site*. When a radical initiator (heat, peroxide, or light) is applied, the double bond "unlocks," and thousands of styrene molecules link head-to-tail to form long chains of **polystyrene (PS)** — the rigid plastic used in disposable cups, packaging, and insulation. The same reactive vinyl group is what makes styrene a building block for SBR (car tyres), ABS (Lego bricks), and ion-exchange resins.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key physical constants of Styrene**
> - Molecular weight: **104.15 g/mol**
> - Boiling point: **145.0 °C**
> - Melting point: **−30.6 °C**
> - Density (25 °C): **0.9019 g/cm³**
> - Flash point: **31 °C**
> - Polymerization enthalpy: **−69.8 kJ/mol** (exothermic — runaway risk)
> - Inhibitor: **TBC (4-tert-butylcatechol) at 10–50 ppm**

---

## 1.4 The Industrial Chain — How EB and ST Link Together

$$
\text{Benzene} \xrightarrow{\text{+ Ethylene, AlCl}_3 \text{ or Zeolite}} \text{Ethylbenzene} \xrightarrow{\text{−H}_2, \text{ Fe}_2\text{O}_3 \text{ catalyst, 600–650 °C}} \text{Styrene} \xrightarrow{\text{Polymerization}} \text{Polystyrene/ABS/SBR}
$$

This **two-step chain (alkylation → dehydrogenation)** is the dominant global route. Approximately **30+ million tonnes/year** of styrene are produced worldwide, with global capacity concentrated in Asia, North America, and Western Europe.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Boards Favourite:** The student must clearly distinguish the **alkylation step** (formation of C–C bond, exothermic, low temperature) from the **dehydrogenation step** (breaking of C–H bonds to form C=C, endothermic, high temperature). This contrast is a common 3-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step 1 — Manufacture of Ethylbenzene by Alkylation of Benzene

### 2.1.1 The Core Reaction

The principal industrial reaction is the **Friedel–Crafts alkylation** of benzene with ethylene:

$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_6 \; + \; \text{C}_2\text{H}_4 \; \xrightarrow{\text{catalyst}} \; \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{–C}_2\text{H}_5 \quad \Delta H_{298} = -113 \text{ kJ/mol}
$$

- **Type:** Electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS)
- **Heat of reaction:** **Exothermic (ΔH = −113 kJ/mol)** — must remove heat to control selectivity
- **Δn (gas):** −1 (one mole of gas consumed per mole of EB formed) — favours high pressure
- **Equilibrium:** Essentially **irreversible** at industrial conditions; conversion limited by kinetics and side reactions, not equilibrium.

### 2.1.2 Why Side Reactions Matter

If the *para* position of ethylbenzene is alkylated again, you get **diethylbenzenes (DEB)** and **triethylbenzene** — wasteful by-products. To suppress this:

- **Benzene is kept in large excess** (typical molar ratio **B:E = 3:1 to 8:1**) so the electrophile (C₂H₅⁺) preferentially encounters benzene.
- **Temperature is kept low** (liquid phase ~85–130 °C; vapor phase ~300–450 °C with zeolites).
- The unreacted benzene is recovered by distillation and recycled.

### 2.1.3 Two Commercial Routes

| Route | Catalyst | Phase | T (°C) | P (bar) | Selectivity to EB |
|------|---------|-------|--------|---------|-------------------|
| **Classical Friedel–Crafts (Monsanto/Lummus)** | AlCl₃ + HCl (homogeneous) | Liquid | 85–130 | 1–5 | 95–97 % |
| **Vapor-phase Zeolite (Mobil/Badger)** | ZSM-5 zeolite | Vapor | 370–450 | 20–30 | 98–99 % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why ZSM-5 zeolite won the market:** Its medium pore size (≈ 0.55 nm) admits *mono*-alkylated benzene (EB) but **excludes** di- and tri-ethylbenzenes by shape selectivity. The result is higher EB selectivity, no acidic waste, no corrosion, and the catalyst is regenerable by simple coke burn-off.

### 2.1.4 The Transalkylation Loop

To boost overall yield, **polyethylbenzenes (PEBs)** separated from the product stream are sent to a **transalkylation reactor**, where they react with fresh benzene:

$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_4(\text{C}_2\text{H}_5)_2 \; + \; \text{C}_6\text{H}_6 \; \xrightarrow{\text{zeolite}} \; 2\,\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{–C}_2\text{H}_5
$$

The combined **alkylation + transalkylation** system pushes overall benzene-based yield to > 99 %.

---

## 2.2 Step 2 — Manufacture of Styrene by Dehydrogenation of Ethylbenzene

### 2.2.1 The Core Reaction

$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{–C}_2\text{H}_5 \; \rightleftharpoons \; \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{–CH=CH}_2 \; + \; \text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H_{298} = +117.6 \text{ kJ/mol}
$$

- **Type:** Endothermic catalytic dehydrogenation
- **ΔH = +117.6 kJ/mol** (heat must be supplied)
- **Δn (gas) = +1** mole per mole of styrene — favoured by **low pressure**
- **Equilibrium constant Kp** increases strongly with temperature (Le Chatelier).
- **Catalyst:** Iron oxide promoted with **K₂O (or K₂CO₃)**, with **Cr₂O₃** and **CeO₂/V₂O₅** as activity/stability promoters (e.g., Shell 105, Sud-Chemie G-64).

### 2.2.2 Why Dilute with Superheated Steam?

Industrial feeds use a **steam-to-ethylbenzene mass ratio of 1.0 : 1 to 3.0 : 1** (typically ~1.5 : 1 to 2.2 : 1). The steam does **three** jobs:

1. **Heat carrier:** Supplies the large endothermic heat of reaction (≈ 117.6 kJ/mol EB).
2. **Partial-pressure reducer:** Lowers the partial pressure of EB and styrene, shifting equilibrium toward styrene.
3. **Coke suppressor:** Steam reacts with deposited carbon via the **water–gas reaction** (C + H₂O → CO + H₂), continuously regenerating the catalyst and prolonging on-stream time.

### 2.2.3 Side Reactions (must be controlled)

$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{–C}_2\text{H}_5 \;\rightarrow\; \text{C}_6\text{H}_6 \; + \; \text{C}_2\text{H}_4 \quad \text{(dealkylation)}
$$

$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{–C}_2\text{H}_5 \;\rightarrow\; \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{–CH}_3 \; + \; \text{CH}_4 \quad \text{(cracking/disproportionation)}
$$

Plus **toluene + benzene + methane + CO + CO₂ + coke** as minor products. Typical **selectivity to styrene ≈ 95–97 %** at 60–65 % conversion per pass.

### 2.2.4 Industrial Reactor Types

| Process | Reactor Type | T (°C) | P (bar) | Conversion | Selectivity |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Lummus–Monsanto Classic** | Adiabatic fixed-bed (multi-stage) | 580–650 | 0.5–1.5 | 60–65 % | 95 % |
| **Fina–Badger SMART** | Isothermal moving-bed radial flow | 600–640 | 0.3–0.7 | 70–80 % | 95–97 % |
| **Lummus/UOP Stylener** | Adiabatic with interstage reheat | 600–650 | ~0.5 | 65 % | 96 % |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|----------------------|----------------|-------|
| 1 | $X = \dfrac{n_{\text{in}} - n_{\text{out}}}{n_{\text{in}}} \times 100$ | **Conversion** of EB or benzene | % |
| 2 | $S = \dfrac{n_{\text{desired}}}{n_{\text{reacted}}} \times 100$ | **Selectivity** to desired product | % |
| 3 | $Y = \dfrac{n_{\text{desired}}}{n_{\text{feed,limiting}}} \times 100$ | **Yield** | % |
| 4 | $Y = X \times S \; / \; 100$ | Yield–Selectivity–Conversion identity | % |
| 5 | $\ln K_p = -\dfrac{\Delta G^\circ}{RT}$ | Van 't Hoff relation for equilibrium | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\dfrac{d\ln K_p}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{RT^2}$ | Temperature dependence of Kp | K⁻¹ |
| 7 | $K_p \;(\text{EB dehydrogenation}) \approx 1.27 \;\text{at}\; 700\,^\circ\text{C}$ | Equilibrium constant (literature) | bar |
| 8 | $\Delta H_{\text{rxn}}^\circ \approx +117.6 \;\text{kJ/mol}$ | Heat of styrene dehydrogenation | kJ/mol |
| 9 | $\Delta H_{\text{rxn}}^\circ \approx -113 \;\text{kJ/mol}$ | Heat of benzene alkylation | kJ/mol |
| 10 | $\text{S/E mass ratio} \in [1.0,\, 3.0]$ | Steam to ethylbenzene feed ratio | kg/kg |
| 11 | $\text{STY} = \dfrac{m_{\text{styrene}}}{m_{\text{cat}} \cdot t}$ | Space–time yield (lab metric) | kg/(kg·h) |
| 12 | $\eta_{\text{thermal}} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{\text{comb,styrene}}}{Q_{\text{fired}}}$ | Furnace thermal efficiency | % |
| 13 | $r_{\text{dehydrogenation}} = k \, P_{\text{EB}} \;-\; \dfrac{k}{K_p}\, P_{\text{ST}}\,P_{\text{H}_2}$ | Reversible Langmuir rate form | mol/(g·s) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The Van 't Hoff equation (formula 6) and the yield–selectivity–conversion identity (formula 4) are the two most-tested formulas for this module. Memorize the numerical sign of ΔH for both alkylation (negative) and dehydrogenation (positive).

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refinery–Petrochemical integration:** Steam crackers produce both ethylene (for EB) and benzene (from pyrolysis gasoline), so an integrated petrochemical complex co-locates an EB/SM plant with a steam cracker to share utilities and feed.
- **Polymer industry feeder:** Every tonne of polystyrene or ABS ultimately traces back to this EB → ST chain.
- **Energy intensity:** Styrene plants are major steam consumers — modern designs (SMART) include a low-pressure-drop radial reactor to reduce both capital and operating cost.
- **Environmental concern:** Ethylbenzene and styrene are **VOC** and are classified as **hazardous air pollutants (HAPs)**; plants install vent recovery and flare systems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Equilibrium Constant and Conversion for EB Dehydrogenation

### 3.1.1 Statement of the Problem

For the reversible dehydrogenation:
$$
\text{EB(g)} \rightleftharpoons \text{ST(g)} + \text{H}_2\text{(g)}
$$
At **T = 700 °C (973 K)** and total pressure **P = 1.0 bar**, derive the equilibrium conversion $X_e$ of ethylbenzene as a function of dilution by steam. Use the literature value $K_p(973\,\text{K}) = 1.27$ (and assume steam is inert and does not appear in $K_p$).

### 3.1.2 Setup with Steam Dilution

Let the feed contain **1 mol EB** and **N mol H₂O (steam)**. Total moles of inert = N.
Define:
- $X_e$ = equilibrium fractional conversion of EB
- $n_{\text{EB}} = 1 - X_e$
- $n_{\text{ST}} = X_e$
- $n_{\text{H}_2} = X_e$
- $n_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} = N$ (inert)
- $n_{\text{total}} = (1 - X_e) + X_e + X_e + N = 1 + X_e + N$

### 3.1.3 Partial Pressures

The partial pressure of species *i* is $P_i = y_i \cdot P$, where $y_i$ is the mole fraction:

$$
P_{\text{EB}} = \frac{1 - X_e}{1 + X_e + N}\,P
$$

$$
P_{\text{ST}} = \frac{X_e}{1 + X_e + N}\,P
$$

$$
P_{\text{H}_2} = \frac{X_e}{1 + X_e + N}\,P
$$

### 3.1.4 The Equilibrium Expression

$$
K_p = \frac{P_{\text{ST}}\cdot P_{\text{H}_2}}{P_{\text{EB}}} = \frac{\left(\dfrac{X_e}{1 + X_e + N}\,P\right)\left(\dfrac{X_e}{1 + X_e + N}\,P\right)}{\dfrac{1 - X_e}{1 + X_e + N}\,P}
$$

Simplifying (one power of $(1 + X_e + N)$ cancels):

$$
K_p = \frac{X_e^{\,2}\,P}{(1 - X_e)(1 + X_e + N)}
$$

### 3.1.5 Solving for $X_e$

Multiply both sides:

$$
K_p\,(1 - X_e)(1 + X_e + N) = X_e^{\,2}\,P
$$

Expand $(1 - X_e)(1 + X_e + N) = (1 + X_e + N) - X_e(1 + X_e + N) = 1 + N - X_e^{\,2} - X_e N - X_e$

Wait — a cleaner way is to expand the product as a polynomial in $X_e$:

$$
(1 - X_e)(1 + X_e + N) = 1 + X_e + N - X_e - X_e^{\,2} - N X_e = 1 + N - X_e^{\,2} - N X_e
$$

So:

$$
K_p\,(1 + N - X_e^{\,2} - N X_e) = X_e^{\,2}\,P
$$

Rearranging:

$$
K_p + K_p N - K_p N X_e - K_p X_e^{\,2} = P \cdot X_e^{\,2}
$$

$$
(K_p + P)\,X_e^{\,2} + K_p N\,X_e - K_p(1 + N) = 0
$$

This is a **quadratic in $X_e$**. Solving:

$$
X_e = \frac{-K_p N + \sqrt{(K_p N)^2 + 4\,(K_p + P)\,K_p(1 + N)}}{2\,(K_p + P)}
$$

(Taking the positive root since $X_e \geq 0$.)

### 3.1.6 Numerical Plug-In (KTU-Style Numerical)

**Case A — No steam dilution, N = 0, P = 1 bar, $K_p$ = 1.27:**

$$
X_e = \frac{\sqrt{4 \cdot (1.27 + 1) \cdot 1.27 \cdot 1}}{2 \cdot (1.27 + 1)} = \frac{\sqrt{11.557}}{4.54} = \frac{3.4}{4.54} \approx 0.749 = 74.9 \%
$$

**Case B — With steam, N = 10 (≈ 5 : 1 molar S/EB), P = 1 bar, $K_p$ = 1.27:**

$$
X_e = \frac{-1.27 \cdot 10 + \sqrt{(1.27 \cdot 10)^2 + 4 \cdot (2.27) \cdot 1.27 \cdot 11}}{2 \cdot 2.27}
$$

$$
= \frac{-12.7 + \sqrt{161.29 + 126.85}}{4.54} = \frac{-12.7 + \sqrt{288.14}}{4.54} = \frac{-12.7 + 16.97}{4.54} = \frac{4.27}{4.54} \approx 0.941 = 94.1\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Diluting EB with ~10 mol of steam per mol EB raises equilibrium conversion from ~75 % to ~94 % at the *same* temperature and pressure. This is the single biggest reason industrial styrene plants use a large steam-to-EB ratio. The trade-off is increased furnace duty and steam cost.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Material Balance Around a Dehydrogenation Reactor

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

A dehydrogenation reactor is fed with **1000 kg/h ethylbenzene** and **1500 kg/h steam** (mass ratio S/EB = 1.5). The conversion of EB per pass is **60 %** and the selectivity to styrene is **96 %**. Determine:
1. The hourly production rate of styrene (kg/h).
2. The hourly rate of unconverted EB leaving the reactor (kg/h).
3. The mass of benzene by-product (kg/h).

### 3.2.2 Molar Feed Rates

Molecular weights: EB = **106.17**, ST = **104.15**, H₂O = **18.015**, B = **78.11**.

$$
F_{\text{EB}} = \frac{1000}{106.17} = 9.418\;\text{kmol/h}
$$

$$
F_{\text{steam}} = \frac{1500}{18.015} = 83.27\;\text{kmol/h}
$$

### 3.2.3 EB Reacted

$$
F_{\text{EB,reacted}} = X \cdot F_{\text{EB,feed}} = 0.60 \cdot 9.418 = 5.651\;\text{kmol/h}
$$

### 3.2.4 Styrene Produced

$$
F_{\text{ST}} = S \cdot F_{\text{EB,reacted}} = 0.96 \cdot 5.651 = 5.425\;\text{kmol/h}
$$

Convert to mass:

$$
m_{\text{ST}} = 5.425 \cdot 104.15 = 565.0\;\text{kg/h}
$$

### 3.2.5 Unreacted Ethylbenzene

$$
F_{\text{EB,out}} = (1 - X)\,F_{\text{EB,feed}} = 0.40 \cdot 9.418 = 3.767\;\text{kmol/h}
$$

$$
m_{\text{EB,out}} = 3.767 \cdot 106.17 = 399.9 \;\text{kg/h}
$$

### 3.2.6 Benzene By-Product (dealkylation side reaction)

The selectivity lost (1 − S = 0.04) is assumed to form benzene + ethylene (1 : 1 molar). Benzene produced:

$$
F_{\text{B}} = 0.04 \cdot 5.651 = 0.226\;\text{kmol/h}
$$

$$
m_{\text{B}} = 0.226 \cdot 78.11 = 17.65\;\text{kg/h}
$$

### 3.2.7 Summary Table

| Stream | kmol/h | kg/h |
|--------|--------|------|
| EB feed | 9.418 | 1000.0 |
| Steam feed | 83.27 | 1500.0 |
| **Styrene produced** | **5.425** | **565.0** |
| EB unreacted | 3.767 | 399.9 |
| Benzene by-product | 0.226 | 17.65 |
| Hydrogen (co-product) | 5.651 | 11.36 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Strategy:** Always carry *3–4 significant figures* in your intermediate steps. KTU valuators specifically check unit consistency and the final reported value. Lose a half-mark for a missing unit, full mark for a wrong conversion.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Adiabatic Flame Temperature & Heat Duty of the EB Furnace

### 3.3.1 Problem
Feed enters a dehydrogenation reactor at **T_in = 650 °C** and leaves at **T_out = 565 °C**. EB conversion = 60 %. The steam/EB mass ratio is 1.5. Estimate the **net heat removed (or supplied) per kmol of EB converted**, given average heat capacities:
- $C_{p,\text{EB}} \approx 200$ J/(mol·K)
- $C_{p,\text{ST}} \approx 180$ J/(mol·K)
- $C_{p,\text{H}_2\text{O (steam)}} \approx 38$ J/(mol·K)
- $C_{p,\text{H}_2} \approx 29$ J/(mol·K)
- $\Delta H_{\text{rxn}} = +117.6$ kJ/mol EB

### 3.3.2 Energy Balance

Per 1 kmol EB feed (with 0.60 converted):

$$
Q = F_{\text{EB,reacted}} \cdot \Delta H_{\text{rxn}} - \sum_i F_i \,C_{p,i}\,(T_{\text{out}} - T_{\text{in}})
$$

But for an **adiabatic** reactor ($Q = 0$ from external duty), the temperature drop is *caused* by the endothermic reaction. Solving for the temperature drop $\Delta T$:

$$
F_{\text{EB,reacted}} \cdot \Delta H_{\text{rxn}} = \left[F_{\text{EB}}C_{p,\text{EB}} + F_{\text{steam}}C_{p,\text{steam}} + F_{\text{ST}}C_{p,\text{ST}} + F_{\text{H}_2}C_{p,\text{H}_2}\right] \cdot |\Delta T|
$$

For 1 kmol EB feed with S/EB molar ratio $r$ (here $r = 1.5 \times 18.015 / 106.17 = 0.255$ kmol steam per kmol EB):

- $F_{\text{EB,reacted}} = 0.60$ kmol
- $F_{\text{ST}} = 0.60$ kmol
- $F_{\text{H}_2} = 0.60$ kmol
- $F_{\text{EB,unreacted}} = 0.40$ kmol
- $F_{\text{steam}} = 0.255$ kmol

Substituting:

$$
0.60 \cdot 117600 = \left[0.40 \cdot 200 + 0.60 \cdot 180 + 0.60 \cdot 29 + 0.255 \cdot 38\right] \cdot |\Delta T|
$$

$$
70560 = \left[80 + 108 + 17.4 + 9.69\right] \cdot |\Delta T| = 215.09 \cdot |\Delta T|
$$

$$
|\Delta T| = \frac{70560}{215.09} \approx 328\;^\circ\text{C}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** A 60 % conversion of ethylbenzene at stoichiometric conditions cools the reacting mixture by roughly **328 °C** in an adiabatic reactor — which is why industrial dehydrogenators use **multiple catalyst beds with interstage reheat** (Lummus Classic) or an **isothermal moving bed** (Fina–Badger SMART). The huge ΔT cannot be tolerated in a single adiabatic bed.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often forget that *steam* has a meaningful heat capacity that buffers the temperature drop. The steam is not just a "diluent" — it is a **heat ballast** that reduces the magnitude of ΔT. A common KTU mistake is to ignore the steam $C_p$ term and get a falsely high ΔT.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]
**Differentiate between the alkylation of benzene and the dehydrogenation of ethylbenzene with respect to (i) reaction type, (ii) enthalpy change, and (iii) preferred temperature and pressure.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Alkylation (Benzene + Ethylene → EB) | Dehydrogenation (EB → Styrene) |
|---|---|---|
| Reaction type | Electrophilic aromatic substitution, exothermic | Reversible catalytic dehydrogenation, endothermic |
| Enthalpy change | ΔH = **−113 kJ/mol** | ΔH = **+117.6 kJ/mol** |
| Preferred T | Low (85–130 °C liquid; 370–450 °C vapor) | High (580–650 °C) |
| Preferred P | Moderate to high (1–30 bar) | Low (0.3–1.5 bar) with steam dilution |
| Catalyst | AlCl₃ (homogeneous) or ZSM-5 zeolite | Fe₂O₃–K₂O–Cr₂O₃ |
| Equilibrium | Essentially irreversible | Strongly temperature-dependent |

(½ mark per correct row × 6 = 3 marks)

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]
**Why is superheated steam mixed with ethylbenzene in the feed to a styrene dehydrogenation reactor? List any two reasons.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Heat supply:** The reaction is highly endothermic (ΔH ≈ +117.6 kJ/mol). Superheated steam (700 °C) supplies the heat of reaction. (1½ marks)
2. **Partial pressure reduction:** Steam acts as an inert diluent, lowering the partial pressures of EB and styrene. Since Δn > 0, this shifts the equilibrium toward styrene formation, increasing conversion. (1 mark)
3. **Coke suppression:** Steam reacts with deposited carbon on the catalyst (C + H₂O → CO + H₂), continuously regenerating the catalyst surface and prolonging on-stream life. (½ mark — for the third point)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Compulsory Choice

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]**
**(a)** Describe the **vapor-phase alkylation of benzene with ethylene** over a ZSM-5 zeolite catalyst for the production of ethylbenzene. Discuss the role of shape selectivity and the importance of the transalkylation step. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A plant produces **800 kg/h of styrene** by dehydrogenation of ethylbenzene at 60 % conversion per pass and 95 % selectivity. The feed uses a steam-to-EB mass ratio of 1.8. Calculate:
(i) The mass of ethylbenzene fed per hour. (ii) The mass of unconverted EB in the product. (iii) The mass of benzene by-product per hour (assuming all loss in selectivity forms benzene + ethylene). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Vapor-Phase Alkylation (7 marks)**

- **Catalyst & Reactor:** Fresh benzene + ethylene (molar ratio 5:1 to 8:1) is vaporized, superheated, and passed over a fixed bed of **ZSM-5 zeolite** at **370–450 °C** and **20–30 bar**. The reaction is exothermic, so the bed is divided into multiple stages with interstage cooling (or a tubular reactor design) to limit temperature rise. (2 marks)
- **Shape Selectivity of ZSM-5:** ZSM-5 is a medium-pore (10-membered ring, ≈ 0.55 nm) zeolite whose pore aperture admits *n*-alkylbenzenes up to mono-substituted benzene (ethylbenzene, toluene) but **sterically excludes** the bulkier *ortho*-diethylbenzene and triethylbenzenes. Therefore, mono-alkylation is highly favored, and over-alkylation is suppressed. This gives EB selectivity of **98–99 %** and avoids the corrosive AlCl₃ waste of the older Friedel–Crafts route. (2 marks)
- **Transalkylation Step:** A small fraction (1–3 %) of the product is unavoidably poly-ethylbenzenes (mainly *para*-diethylbenzene). These are sent to a **transalkylation reactor** (also zeolite-based, lower temperature ~250 °C) where they react with fresh benzene to form additional EB:  C₆H₄(C₂H₅)₂ + C₆H₆ → 2 C₆H₅C₂H₅. This step pushes overall benzene-based yield above 99 % and is a major reason for the high economic efficiency of the modern process. (2 marks)
- **Advantages over AlCl₃ route:** (i) non-corrosive, (ii) regenerable, (iii) no HCl/alkyl chloride waste, (iv) higher EB selectivity, (v) lower environmental footprint. (1 mark)

**(b) Numerical (7 marks)**

Given:
- $m_{\text{ST}} = 800$ kg/h, $M_{\text{ST}} = 104.15$ g/mol, $M_{\text{EB}} = 106.17$ g/mol, $M_{\text{B}} = 78.11$ g/mol
- $X = 0.60$, $S = 0.95$, S/EB mass ratio = 1.8

**Step 1 — Molar flow of styrene produced:**

$$
F_{\text{ST}} = \frac{800}{104.15} = 7.681\;\text{kmol/h}
$$

**Step 2 — Moles of EB reacted (using $F_{\text{ST}} = S \cdot F_{\text{EB,reacted}}$):**

$$
F_{\text{EB,reacted}} = \frac{F_{\text{ST}}}{S} = \frac{7.681}{0.95} = 8.085\;\text{kmol/h}
$$

**Step 3 — Total EB feed:**

$$
F_{\text{EB,feed}} = \frac{F_{\text{EB,reacted}}}{X} = \frac{8.085}{0.60} = 13.475\;\text{kmol/h}
$$

**Step 4 — Mass of EB feed:**

$$
m_{\text{EB,feed}} = 13.475 \cdot 106.17 = 1430.6\;\text{kg/h} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}
$$

**Step 5 — Unreacted EB:**

$$
F_{\text{EB,out}} = (1 - X) \cdot F_{\text{EB,feed}} = 0.40 \cdot 13.475 = 5.390\;\text{kmol/h}
$$

$$
m_{\text{EB,out}} = 5.390 \cdot 106.17 = 572.2\;\text{kg/h} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}
$$

**Step 6 — Benzene by-product:**

Selectivity loss = 1 − 0.95 = 0.05. Moles of benzene produced:

$$
F_{\text{B}} = 0.05 \cdot F_{\text{EB,reacted}} = 0.05 \cdot 8.085 = 0.404\;\text{kmol/h}
$$

$$
m_{\text{B}} = 0.404 \cdot 78.11 = 31.6\;\text{kg/h} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}
$$

**Bonus cross-check — Steam fed:** $m_{\text{steam}} = 1.8 \cdot 1430.6 = 2575$ kg/h. *(1 mark for using the S/EB ratio somewhere meaningful in the solution; KTU gives a bonus if you mention it.)*

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model]**
**(a)** With the help of a neat process flow diagram, describe the **Lummus–Monsanto process for the manufacture of styrene** by catalytic dehydrogenation of ethylbenzene. Discuss (i) the function of superheated steam, (ii) the role of the iron-oxide catalyst, and (iii) why multiple catalyst beds with interstage reheat are used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the dehydrogenation of ethylbenzene:
$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{–C}_2\text{H}_5 \rightleftharpoons \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{–CH=CH}_2 + \text{H}_2
$$
At **700 °C**, the equilibrium constant $K_p = 1.27$ bar. If the feed contains **1 mol EB and 6 mol steam** (assumed inert), calculate the **equilibrium conversion of EB** at a total pressure of 1 bar. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Lummus–Monsanto Process (7 marks)**

- **Feed Preparation:** Pure ethylbenzene is mixed with superheated steam (S/EB mass ratio ≈ 1.5–2.2) in a mixer. The mixture is then superheated in a gas-fired furnace to about **680–700 °C**. (1 mark)
- **Reactor Section:** The superheated mixture passes through **two or three adiabatic fixed catalyst beds** in series, each containing an iron-oxide catalyst (Shell 105: Fe₂O₃–K₂O–Cr₂O₃–CeO₂). The temperature drops about 30–60 °C across each bed due to the endothermic reaction. Interstage reheaters restore the temperature to ~620 °C before the next bed. (2 marks)
- **Functions of Superheated Steam:** (i) supplies the large endothermic heat of reaction (~118 kJ/mol); (ii) reduces partial pressure of reactants, shifting equilibrium toward styrene; (iii) reacts with coke on catalyst to keep it active (steam–coke gasification). (1 mark)
- **Role of Iron-Oxide Catalyst:** The active phase is non-stoichiometric iron oxide (Fe₃O₄–Fe₂O₃). K₂O (potassium oxide) acts as a **promoter** that accelerates carbon gasification, suppresses coke, and prevents catalyst sintering. Cr₂O₃ and CeO₂ improve mechanical strength and oxidation–reduction stability. The dehydrogenation reaction is structure-insensitive, so the catalyst has a long life (typically 1–3 years on stream). (1 mark)
- **Reason for Multiple Beds with Interstage Reheat:** Each bed is limited to ~20–25 % conversion to keep the temperature drop moderate (otherwise selectivity crashes). With 2–3 beds and interstage reheat, the cumulative conversion reaches 60–65 % per pass at 95–97 % selectivity, after which unconverted EB is separated and recycled. (1 mark)
- **Downstream:** The effluent is cooled in a waste-heat boiler (generating high-pressure steam), partially condensed, then sent to a **vacuum distillation column** (60–80 torr) to separate styrene from unreacted EB (recycled), toluene, benzene, and tars. Pure styrene is stored with **TBC inhibitor** (10–50 ppm). (1 mark)

**(b) Numerical (7 marks)**

- **Setup:** With 1 mol EB feed, 6 mol steam, fractional conversion $X_e$:
  - $n_{\text{EB}} = 1 - X_e$
  - $n_{\text{ST}} = X_e$, $\;n_{\text{H}_2} = X_e$, $\;n_{\text{steam}} = 6$ (inert)
  - $n_{\text{total}} = 1 + X_e + 6 = 7 + X_e$

- **Partial pressures (P = 1 bar):**

$$
P_{\text{EB}} = \frac{1 - X_e}{7 + X_e}, \quad P_{\text{ST}} = \frac{X_e}{7 + X_e}, \quad P_{\text{H}_2} = \frac{X_e}{7 + X_e}
$$

- **Equilibrium expression:**

$$
K_p = \frac{P_{\text{ST}} P_{\text{H}_2}}{P_{\text{EB}}} = \frac{X_e^{\,2}}{(1 - X_e)(7 + X_e)} \cdot P
$$

Substituting $K_p = 1.27$ and $P = 1$:

$$
1.27 = \frac{X_e^{\,2}}{(1 - X_e)(7 + X_e)}
$$

- **Cross-multiply:**

$$
1.27\,(1 - X_e)(7 + X_e) = X_e^{\,2}
$$

Expand the LHS:

$$
(1 - X_e)(7 + X_e) = 7 + X_e - 7 X_e - X_e^{\,2} = 7 - 6 X_e - X_e^{\,2}
$$

So:

$$
1.27\,(7 - 6 X_e - X_e^{\,2}) = X_e^{\,2}
$$

$$
8.89 - 7.62 X_e - 1.27 X_e^{\,2} = X_e^{\,2}
$$

$$
2.27 X_e^{\,2} + 7.62 X_e - 8.89 = 0
$$

- **Solve the quadratic (using the quadratic formula):**

$$
X_e = \frac{-7.62 \pm \sqrt{(7.62)^2 + 4 \cdot 2.27 \cdot 8.89}}{2 \cdot 2.27}
$$

$$
= \frac{-7.62 \pm \sqrt{58.06 + 80.72}}{4.54} = \frac{-7.62 \pm \sqrt{138.78}}{4.54} = \frac{-7.62 \pm 11.78}{4.54}
$$

Taking the positive root:

$$
X_e = \frac{-7.62 + 11.78}{4.54} = \frac{4.16}{4.54} \approx 0.916 = 91.6\%
$$

**Final answer:** $X_e \approx$ **91.6 %** at 700 °C, 1 bar, with 6 mol steam per mol EB. (Full 7 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A large number of students **drop the negative sign** on the quadratic formula's first term and write $X_e = (7.62 + 11.78)/4.54 = 4.27$, which is > 1 and **physically impossible** (you cannot have more than 100 % conversion). The correct answer always uses the **negative** of the $b$-coefficient: $X_e = (-b + \sqrt{b^2 + 4ac})/2a$. You will lose **2 marks** for forgetting the negative sign and another **½ mark** if your numerical value exceeds 1.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 4 — Rapid Revision Checklist for BTX Derivatives (EB & Styrene)**

- ✅ **Ethylbenzene** is made by **alkylation of benzene with ethylene**, either by classical AlCl₃ (Friedel–Crafts) or modern **ZSM-5 zeolite vapor-phase** route.
- ✅ The alkylation reaction is **exothermic (ΔH = −113 kJ/mol)** and essentially **irreversible**.
- ✅ **Transalkylation** of polyethylbenzenes with fresh benzene boosts overall EB yield to **> 99 %**.
- ✅ **Styrene** is made by **catalytic dehydrogenation of ethylbenzene** over **Fe₂O₃–K₂O–Cr₂O₃** at 580–650 °C.
- ✅ The dehydrogenation reaction is **endothermic (ΔH = +117.6 kJ/mol)** and **reversible** — favored by **high T**, **low P**, and **steam dilution**.
- ✅ **Steam** plays three roles: (1) heat carrier, (2) partial-pressure diluent, (3) coke suppressant.
- ✅ Typical **steam/EB mass ratio = 1.5–2.2**; typical per-pass **conversion = 60–70 %**, **selectivity = 95–97 %**.
- ✅ Two commercial reactor types: **adiabatic fixed bed with interstage reheat** (Lummus–Monsanto, Lummus/UOP Stylener) and **isothermal moving-bed radial** (Fina–Badger SMART).
- ✅ Pure styrene must be stored with **TBC inhibitor (10–50 ppm)** to prevent runaway polymerization.
- ✅ Major styrene end-uses: **polystyrene (PS), expanded polystyrene (EPS), ABS, SBR rubber, UPR resins, ion-exchange resins**.
- ✅ **Key formulas to memorize for KTU 2024 ESE:**
  - $Y = X \times S / 100$
  - $K_p = X_e^{\,2} P / [(1 - X_e)(1 + X_e + N)]$  (with steam dilution N)
  - $\Delta H_{\text{alkylation}} = -113$ kJ/mol; $\Delta H_{\text{dehydrogenation}} = +117.6$ kJ/mol
  - Adiabatic $\Delta T \approx 328\,^\circ$C at 60 % conversion and S/EB = 1.5 (use energy balance with steam $C_p$).
- ✅ **Exam traps to avoid:** (i) Forgetting the negative sign in the quadratic formula; (ii) ignoring steam $C_p$ in energy balances; (iii) writing $K_p$ with units inside the logarithm of Van 't Hoff; (iv) confusing xylene (two methyls on ring) with ethylbenzene (one ethyl group on ring) — they are isomers, C₈H₁₀, but chemically very different.
- ✅ **Global production scale:** > 30 million tonnes/year styrene; > 50 million tonnes/year ethylbenzene. Styrene is the **third-largest commodity monomer** after ethylene and propylene.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linear Alkyl Benzene
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT741 - PETROCHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Linear Alkyl Benzene

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!NOTE]
> **Linear Alkyl Benzene (LAB)** is an aromatic hydrocarbon consisting of a **benzene ring** (C6H5–) substituted with a **straight-chain (linear) alkyl group** of typically 10 to 14 carbon atoms. Its general chemical formula is **C6H5–CnH2n+1**, where *n* ranges from **10 to 14**.

It belongs to the family of **alkylbenzenes**, but is specifically distinguished by the *linear* (unbranched) nature of the alkyl side-chain. The most commercially significant grade is **C10–C13 LAB** (also called the *detergent-grade LAB*).

| Property | Value / Description |
|---|---|
| Chemical Class | Aromatic hydrocarbon (Alkylbenzene) |
| General Formula | C6H5–CnH2n+1 |
| Carbon Chain Length (n) | 10 – 14 (Detergent grade: 10 – 13) |
| Molecular Weight Range | ~ 218 – 318 g/mol |
| Appearance | Colorless, oily liquid |
| Density | ~ **0.86 g/cm³** at 25 °C |
| Boiling Point Range | ~ 280 – 320 °C (depends on chain length) |
| Solubility in Water | Negligible (hydrophobic) |
| Primary Derivative | **LABSA / LAS** (Linear Alkylbenzene Sulfonate) |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Detergent Backbone"**
>
> Think of Linear Alkyl Benzene as a **"molecular lollipop"**:
> - The **benzene ring** is the *candy head* — it is *water-loving* (hydrophilic) once sulfonated, because of the sulfonic acid (–SO3H) group attached to it.
> - The **linear alkyl tail** is the *stick* — it is *oil-loving* (hydrophobic, lipophilic), so it dissolves grease and oily dirt.
>
> When a sulfonate group (–SO3H) is added to the benzene ring, the molecule becomes a **surfactant** — a substance that lowers surface tension and lets water mix with oil. This is the heart of every **laundry detergent, dish-wash liquid, and industrial cleaner** you have ever used.
>
> The *linearity* of the tail is critical. Branched alkylbenzenes (like the older **AB – Alkylbenzene** made via propylene polymerization) produced detergents that **did not biodegrade** and caused massive river foaming in the 1960s. **Linear** chains, on the other hand, are **readily biodegraded by microorganisms**, which is why LAB replaced AB globally.

---

## 1.3 Why LAB Matters in Petrochemical Industry

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Significance**
> - Global LAB production capacity exceeds **4 million metric tons/year**.
> - More than **85 %** of all synthetic detergents worldwide use **Linear Alkylbenzene Sulfonate (LAS)** as the active surfactant.
> - India is one of the largest consumers and producers of LAB due to massive detergent demand.
> - The key raw material for LAB is **kerosene-range straight-run naphtha (C10–C14 n-paraffins)** from crude oil distillation.

The benzene ring in LAB comes from the catalytic reforming / pyrolysis gasoline fraction, and the linear alkyl chain comes from the **kerosene fraction** (boiling range **150 – 250 °C**) of crude oil after **n-paraffin separation** by molecular sieve adsorption.

---

## 1.4 Industry-Standard Specifications of LAB

| Parameter | Typical Specification | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **n-Alkyl chain length** | C10 – C13 (avg C12) | Determines detergency |
| **2-Phenyl isomer content** | ≥ **25 – 30 %** (preferably high) | Higher = better biodegradability & solubility |
| **Total sulfur** | < 5 ppm | Prevents catalyst poisoning in sulfonation |
| **Bromine number** | < 0.05 | Indicates low residual olefin content |
| **Saybolt color** | + 30 (water-white) | Indicates purity |
| **Density (15.6 °C)** | 0.855 – 0.870 g/cm³ | QC parameter |
| **Flash point** | > 130 °C | Safety / transport regulation |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Principal Commercial Routes to LAB

There are **two major industrial process families** used worldwide for LAB manufacture:

### Route A : UOP HF Process (Historical & Most Widely Used)
Uses **Hydrofluoric Acid (HF)** as the alkylation catalyst.
- **Steps**: Kerosene → n-Paraffin separation (Molex) → Dehydrogenation (Pacol) → Olefins separation (Olex) → HF alkylation with benzene → Product purification.
- About **70 %** of global LAB capacity still uses HF catalysis.

### Route B : UOP DETAL Process (Modern, Solid Catalyst)
Uses a **solid heterogeneous catalyst** (zeolite-based) — no liquid HF.
- **Steps**: Kerosene → Molex → Pacol → **Direct alkylation of benzene with mixed n-olefins/n-paraffins** → Distillation.
- The DETAL process is **environmentally safer** and is preferred for *new* plants.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Alkar Process** (older, UOP/BP) used **AlCl3** catalyst and ethylene for short-chain LAB. Now largely obsolete.

---

## 2.2 Process Flow Logic (Step-by-Step Logic)

### Step 1 — Feedstock Preparation
- Kerosene (C10–C14 straight-run fraction, boiling ~ 150 – 250 °C) is fed to the **UOP Molex process**.
- Molex uses **5A molecular sieves** in a continuous adsorptive separation to extract **n-paraffins** (linear alkanes) from branched and cyclic hydrocarbons.
- Purity of extracted n-paraffins: **> 98 % linear**.

### Step 2 — Dehydrogenation
- n-Paraffins are converted to **n-mono-olefins** in the **UOP Pacol (Paraffin to Olefin by Catalytic dehydrogenation)** reactor.
- **Catalyst**: Pt/Al2O3 promoted with Li, Sn, or In.
- **Reaction**:
  $$C_n H_{2n+2} \rightleftharpoons C_n H_{2n} + H_2$$
- **Conversion per pass**: 10 – 12 % (limited by thermodynamic equilibrium).
- **Selectivity to mono-olefins**: ~ 90 %.
- Operating conditions: **460 – 510 °C, 1 – 3 atm, H2 : hydrocarbon molar ratio ~ 4–6**.
- The reaction is **endothermic** (ΔH ≈ +125 kJ/mol), so heat must be supplied (typically by inter-stage re-heaters or by fired furnace).

### Step 3 — Olefins Separation
- Pacol effluent is sent to the **UOP Olex** process (similar adsorptive separation as Molex) to separate **n-olefins** from unconverted n-paraffins.
- Unconverted n-paraffins are **recycled** to the Pacol reactor (overall paraffin conversion ~ 90–95 %).

### Step 4 — Alkylation of Benzene
- n-Olefins are reacted with **excess benzene** in the presence of catalyst.
- **Reaction** (Friedel–Crafts Alkylation):
  $$C_6H_6 + C_n H_{2n} \longrightarrow C_6H_5 - C_n H_{2n+1}$$
  $$C_6H_6 + \alpha\text{-olefin} \longrightarrow \text{LAB}$$
- The alkylation is **exothermic** (ΔH ≈ –90 kJ/mol) and proceeds via a **carbocation intermediate** (Markovnikov addition when using HF; more linear with solid zeolite).
- The **benzene : olefin molar ratio** is maintained at **5 – 10 : 1** to **minimize dialkylation** and **suppress olefin polymerization**.

### Step 5 — Product Purification
- The crude LAB is fractionated in distillation columns to:
  1. Recover **unreacted benzene** (recycled back).
  2. Remove **heavy alkylate** (dialkylbenzene — by-product).
  3. Obtain the **detergent-grade LAB** (C10–C13 LAB) as the heart-cut.
- Vacuum distillation is used because of the high boiling points.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **mathematically derived and verifiable** for the KTU 2024 syllabus problem-set.

| # | Formula / Equation | Description / Variables |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_n H_{2n+2} \rightleftharpoons C_n H_{2n} + H_2$ | Paraffin dehydrogenation (Pacol) |
| 2 | $C_6H_6 + C_n H_{2n} \rightarrow C_6H_5 - C_n H_{2n+1}$ | LAB formation (alkylation) |
| 3 | $X = \dfrac{F_{in} - F_{out}}{F_{in}}$ | **Conversion (X)** — fraction of reactant converted |
| 4 | $S = \dfrac{Y_{desired}}{Y_{total\ products}}$ | **Selectivity (S)** to LAB |
| 5 | $Y = X \times S$ | **Overall yield (Y)** of LAB |
| 6 | $\text{2-PIC} = \dfrac{\text{moles of 2-phenyl LAB}}{\text{total moles LAB}} \times 100$ | 2-Phenyl Isomer Content (biodegradability index) |
| 7 | $\dot{n}_{LAB} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{olefin}}{M_{olefin}} \times S_{alkylation}$ | Molar flow rate of LAB produced |
| 8 | $M_{LAB} = 78 + (14 \times n)$ | Approx. molecular weight (78 = C6H6) |
| 9 | $\text{TON} = \dfrac{\text{moles product}}{\text{moles catalyst}} = \dfrac{\dot{n}_{LAB}\cdot t}{\dot{n}_{cat}}$ | Catalyst Turnover Number |
| 10 | $\Delta H_{rxn}^{alkyl} \approx -90\ \text{kJ/mol}$ | Heat released in alkylation (exothermic) |
| 11 | $\Delta H_{rxn}^{dehyd} \approx +125\ \text{kJ/mol}$ | Heat required in dehydrogenation (endothermic) |
| 12 | $\eta_{thermal} = \dfrac{125}{Q_{fired}}$ | Thermal efficiency of Pacol furnace |
| 13 | $R_{B/O} = \dfrac{\dot{n}_{benzene}}{\dot{n}_{olefin}}$ | Benzene-to-Olefin molar ratio (5 – 10 typical) |
| 14 | $\dot{Q}_{rxn} = \dot{n}_{olefin} \cdot \vert \Delta H_{rxn} \vert$ | Heat duty of alkylation reactor (W or kW) |

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE**: All absolute-value bars in this table are rendered as **\vert** to preserve the KTU markdown table integrity.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Process Section | Real-World Application |
|---|---|
| **Molex** | Produces **n-paraffins** which are also feedstocks for **n-alcohols, plasticizers, n-olefins for LLDPE co-monomer (1-butene, 1-hexene)**. |
| **Pacol + Olex** | Provides α-olefins used in **HDPE, LLDPE, synthetic lubricants, plasticizers**. |
| **LAB** | Converted to **LABSA** (Linear Alkylbenzene Sulfonic Acid) by **SO3 sulfonation**. |
| **LABSA / Na-LAS** | Active ingredient in **detergents, shampoos, dish-wash liquids, industrial cleaners**. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Downstream Integration**: The LAB plant is often co-located with a **sulfonation plant (SO3 film reactor)** to produce **LABSA (66-BA, 96-SA, 90-grade)** directly — a standard petrochemical complex integration strategy.

---

## 2.5 Reaction Mechanism (HF Alkylation — Conceptual)

> [!NOTE]
> The HF-catalyzed alkylation proceeds via a **Markovnikov-type carbocation intermediate**:
>
> 1. **HF protonates the n-olefin** at the double bond → forms a **secondary carbocation** (C+).
> 2. The carbocation is **stabilized by the HF counterion** (a tight ion pair) in the acid phase.
> 3. The **π-electrons of benzene** attack the C+ → forms a **sigma complex (Wheland intermediate)**.
> 4. **Deprotonation** of the Wheland intermediate by F⁻ regenerates HF and yields **LAB**.
> 5. The **2-phenyl isomer** is the kinetically favoured product and is critical for biodegradability.
> 6. **Side reactions**: dialkylation (2 moles olefin on 1 benzene), olefin oligomerization, skeletal isomerization of the alkyl chain (yielding branched LAB — undesirable).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Single-Pass Conversion in Pacol Reactor

> **Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**
> A Pacol dehydrogenation reactor processes **12 000 kg/h** of n-dodecane (C12H26). If the **single-pass conversion is 11 %**, calculate:
> (a) The mass flow rate of **n-dodecene (C12H24)** produced.
> (b) The mass flow rate of **hydrogen (H2)** released.
> (c) The mass flow rate of **unreacted n-dodecane** exiting the reactor.

**Given:**
- Feed: $\dot{m}_{C12H26} = 12\,000\ \text{kg/h}$
- Conversion: $X = 0.11$
- Reaction: $C_{12}H_{26} \rightarrow C_{12}H_{24} + H_2$

**Step 1 — Calculate molecular weights (standard atomic masses: C = 12, H = 1):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{C_{12}H_{26}} &= (12 \times 12) + (26 \times 1) \\
&= 144 + 26 = 170\ \text{g/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{C_{12}H_{24}} &= (12 \times 12) + (24 \times 1) \\
&= 144 + 24 = 168\ \text{g/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{H_2} &= 2\ \text{g/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert mass feed to molar feed:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{n}_{C_{12}H_{26},in} &= \dfrac{12\,000\ \text{kg/h}}{170\ \text{g/mol}} \\
&= \dfrac{12\,000\,000\ \text{g/h}}{170\ \text{g/mol}} \\
&\approx 70\,588.24\ \text{mol/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Moles reacted (= moles of C12H24 formed = moles of H2 formed, by 1:1:1 stoichiometry):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{n}_{reacted} &= X \times \dot{n}_{C_{12}H_{26},in} \\
&= 0.11 \times 70\,588.24 \\
&\approx 7\,764.71\ \text{mol/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 (a) — Mass flow rate of n-dodecene produced:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{C_{12}H_{24}} &= \dot{n}_{reacted} \times M_{C_{12}H_{24}} \\
&= 7\,764.71 \times 168\ \text{g/h} \\
&\approx 1\,304\,471\ \text{g/h} \\
&= 1\,304.47\ \text{kg/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

> ✅ **Answer (a):** $\dot{m}_{C_{12}H_{24}} \approx 1\,304.5\ \text{kg/h}$

**Step 4 (b) — Mass flow rate of hydrogen released:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{H_2} &= \dot{n}_{reacted} \times M_{H_2} \\
&= 7\,764.71 \times 2\ \text{g/h} \\
&\approx 15\,529\ \text{g/h} \\
&= 15.53\ \text{kg/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

> ✅ **Answer (b):** $\dot{m}_{H_2} \approx 15.53\ \text{kg/h}$

**Step 4 (c) — Mass flow rate of unreacted n-dodecane:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{C_{12}H_{26},out} &= (1 - X) \times \dot{m}_{C_{12}H_{26},in} \\
&= (1 - 0.11) \times 12\,000 \\
&= 0.89 \times 12\,000 \\
&= 10\,680\ \text{kg/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

> ✅ **Answer (c):** $\dot{m}_{C_{12}H_{26},out} = 10\,680\ \text{kg/h}$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — LAB Alkylation: Material Balance with Recycle

> **Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**
> In an alkylation reactor, **3 000 kg/h of n-olefin (C12, M = 168 g/mol)** is reacted with **excess benzene**. The **single-pass conversion of olefin is 95 %** and the **selectivity to LAB is 92 %**.
> (a) Calculate the **mass of LAB produced per hour**.
> (b) If the **benzene-to-olefin molar ratio (R_B/O) is 7 : 1**, calculate the **fresh benzene feed** required (in kg/h) if benzene is fed in slight excess and all unreacted benzene is recovered and recycled.

**Given:**
- $\dot{m}_{olefin} = 3\,000\ \text{kg/h}$
- $X_{olefin} = 0.95$
- $S_{LAB} = 0.92$
- $R_{B/O} = 7$ (moles)
- $M_{olefin} = 168\ \text{g/mol}$
- $M_{benzene} = 78\ \text{g/mol}$

**Step 1 — Moles of olefin fed per hour:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{n}_{olefin} &= \dfrac{3\,000\,000\ \text{g/h}}{168\ \text{g/mol}} \\
&\approx 17\,857.14\ \text{mol/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Moles of LAB produced (selectivity applied):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{n}_{LAB} &= \dot{n}_{olefin} \times X_{olefin} \times S_{LAB} \\
&= 17\,857.14 \times 0.95 \times 0.92 \\
&\approx 15\,605.95\ \text{mol/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 (a) — Molecular weight of LAB (C6H5–C12H25):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{LAB} &= 78 + (12 \times 14 + 1) \\
&= 78 + (168 + 1) \\
&\text{(using the formula: M = 78 + 14n for n = 12)} \\
M_{LAB} &= 78 + 14(12) = 78 + 168 = 246\ \text{g/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 (a) — Mass flow rate of LAB produced:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{LAB} &= \dot{n}_{LAB} \times M_{LAB} \\
&= 15\,605.95 \times 246\ \text{g/h} \\
&\approx 3\,839\,064\ \text{g/h} \\
&\approx 3\,839.06\ \text{kg/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

> ✅ **Answer (a):** $\dot{m}_{LAB} \approx 3\,839\ \text{kg/h}$

**Step 5 (b) — Moles of benzene entering reactor:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{n}_{benzene,feed} &= R_{B/O} \times \dot{n}_{olefin} \\
&= 7 \times 17\,857.14 \\
&\approx 125\,000\ \text{mol/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 (b) — Moles of benzene consumed (1:1 with olefin converted):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{n}_{benzene,consumed} &= \dot{n}_{olefin} \times X_{olefin} \\
&= 17\,857.14 \times 0.95 \\
&\approx 16\,964.29\ \text{mol/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 (b) — Moles of benzene to be made up (the fresh feed = net consumption if 100 % unreacted is recycled):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{n}_{benzene,fresh} &= \dot{n}_{benzene,consumed} \\
&\approx 16\,964.29\ \text{mol/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 (b) — Mass of fresh benzene:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{benzene,fresh} &= 16\,964.29 \times 78\ \text{g/h} \\
&\approx 1\,323\,214\ \text{g/h} \\
&\approx 1\,323.21\ \text{kg/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

> ✅ **Answer (b):** $\dot{m}_{benzene,fresh} \approx 1\,323.2\ \text{kg/h}$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Heat Duty of the Alkylation Reactor

> **Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**
> For the same alkylation reactor as Worked Example 2, calculate the **heat released (kW)** by the alkylation reaction. Use $|\Delta H_{rxn}| = 90\ \text{kJ/mol}$.

**Given:**
- $\dot{n}_{olefin,converted} = 16\,964.29\ \text{mol/h}$
- $|\Delta H_{rxn}| = 90\ \text{kJ/mol}$

**Step 1 — Heat released per hour:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q} &= \dot{n}_{converted} \times \vert \Delta H_{rxn} \vert \\
&= 16\,964.29 \times 90\ \text{kJ/h} \\
&\approx 1\,526\,786\ \text{kJ/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to kW (1 kW = 1 kJ/s = 3 600 kJ/h):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{kW} &= \dfrac{1\,526\,786}{3\,600} \\
&\approx 424.1\ \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

> ✅ **Answer:** $\dot{Q} \approx 424.1\ \text{kW}$ of heat that must be removed by the reactor cooling system (typically via boiling refrigerant or cooling water in tube-side coils).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — HF vs DETAL Process

| Parameter | HF Process | DETAL Process |
|---|---|---|
| **Catalyst state** | Homogeneous (liquid HF) | Heterogeneous (solid zeolite) |
| **Operating T** | 35 – 50 °C | 120 – 180 °C |
| **Operating P** | 4 – 8 bar | 10 – 30 bar |
| **Conversion/selectivity** | High (X ≈ 95–98 %, S ≈ 92–94 %) | Slightly lower (X ≈ 90 %, S ≈ 90 %) |
| **2-Phenyl isomer** | ~ 25 – 30 % | **~ 30 – 35 %** (better biodegradability) |
| **Environmental risk** | HIGH (HF is toxic, corrosive) | LOW (no liquid acid) |
| **CAPEX** | Moderate | Higher (more reactor volume needed) |
| **OPEX** | High (HF handling, regeneration) | Lower |
| **World share (2024)** | ~ 65 – 70 % | ~ 25 – 30 % (growing) |
| **Safety systems** | HF acid spill mitigation, water spray curtains | Standard |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Overall LAB Process Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["KEROSENE FEED\nC10-C14 STRAIGHT RUN"] --> B1["MOLEX UNIT\n5A MOLECULAR SIEVE"]
    B1 --> B2["n-PARAFFIN PRODUCT\nLINEAR C10-C14"]
    B1 --> B3["BRANCHED + CYCLIC\nREJECT STREAM"]
    B2 --> C1["PACOL REACTOR\nPt-Al2O3 CATALYST\n460-510 C"]
    C1 --> C2["DEHYDROGENATED\nMIXTURE"]
    C2 --> C3["OLEX UNIT\nOLEFIN SEPARATION"]
    C3 --> C4["n-OLEFIN\nPRODUCT STREAM"]
    C3 --> C5["UNCONVERTED\nn-PARAFFIN RECYCLE"]
    C5 --> C1
    BENZ["BENZENE FEED\nEXCESS 5-10 MOLAR"] --> D1["ALKYLATION\nREACTOR"]
    C4 --> D1
    D1 --> D2["CRUDE LAB\nSEPARATION COLUMN"]
    D2 --> D3["UNREACTED BENZENE\nRECYCLE"]
    D3 --> D1
    D2 --> D4["HEAVY ALKYLATE\nBY PRODUCT"]
    D2 --> P1["DETERGENT GRADE\nLINEAR ALKYL BENZENE\nC10-C13 LAB"]
    P1 --> E1["SO3 SULFONATION\nFILM REACTOR"]
    E1 --> P2["LABSA\nLINEAR ALKYL BENZENE\nSULFONIC ACID"]
    P2 --> E2["NEUTRALIZATION\nNaOH"]
    E2 --> P3["Na-LAS\nSODIUM SALT\nFINAL DETERGENT"]
```

---

## 4.2 Process Flow Diagram — Pacol-Olex-Alkylation Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FEED["FRESH n-PARAFFIN\n+ RECYCLE n-PARAFFIN"] --> MIX["MIXING TEE\n+ H2 DILUENT"]
    MIX --> HEAT["INTER STAGE\nREHEATER FURNACE"]
    HEAT --> REACTOR["PACOL FIXED BED\nDEHYDROGENATION\nPt / Al2O3"]
    REACTOR --> COOL["EFFLUENT\nQUENCH COOLER"]
    COOL --> COMPRESS["COMPRESSOR\n+ FLASH SEPARATOR"]
    COMPRESS --> OLEX["OLEX ADSORPTIVE\nSEPARATION"]
    OLEX --> C2H4OLEFIN["n-OLEFIN\nC10-C14 STREAM"]
    OLEX --> RECY["UNCONVERTED\nn-PARAFFIN RECYCLE"]
    RECY --> FEED
    C2H4OLEFIN --> ALK["HF / DETAL\nALKYLATION REACTOR"]
    BZ["FRESH + RECYCLED\nBENZENE"] --> ALK
    ALK --> SEP["DEALKYLATION\nSEPARATION COLUMN"]
    SEP --> BZRECY["BENZENE RECYCLE\nOVERHEAD"]
    BZRECY --> BZ
    SEP --> HEAVY["DIALKYL BENZENE\nBOTTOM PRODUCT"]
    SEP --> LAB["LAB HEART CUT\nFINAL PRODUCT\nC10-C13 LINEAR\nALKYL BENZENE"]
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — LAB → LABSA → LAS Value Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FEEDSTOCK ["FEEDSTOCK ZONE"]
        K1["KEROSENE\n150-250 C"] --> M1["MOLEX"]
        M1 --> P1["n-PARAFFIN C10-C14"]
        P1 --> P2["PACOL DEHYD"]
        P2 --> P3["n-OLEFIN STREAM"]
        BX1["BENZENE\nREFORMATE"] --> ALK1
    end
    subgraph REACTION ["REACTION ZONE"]
        P3 --> ALK1["HF / DETAL\nALKYLATION"]
        ALK1 --> DST1["DISTILLATION"]
        DST1 --> LAB1["C10-C13 LAB\nLINEAR ALKYL BENZENE"]
    end
    subgraph DOWNSTREAM ["DOWNSTREAM ZONE"]
        LAB1 --> SO3["SO3 SULFONATION\nFALLING FILM REACTOR"]
        SO3 --> LABSA["LABSA 96 PERCENT\nLINEAR ALKYL BENZENE\nSULFONIC ACID"]
        LABSA --> NEUT["NaOH NEUTRALIZATION"]
        NEUT --> LAS["SODIUM LAS\nFINAL SURFACTANT"]
    end
```

---

## 4.4 Diagram — HF Alkylation Reaction Mechanism (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["STEP 1\nHF PROTONATES\nn-OLEFIN DOUBLE BOND"] --> A2["STEP 2\nSECONDARY CARBOCATION\n+ HF2 COUNTERION FORMS"]
    A2 --> A3["STEP 3\nBENZENE PI ELECTRONS\nATTACK C PLUS"]
    A3 --> A4["STEP 4\nWHELAND SIGMA COMPLEX\nARENIUM ION FORMS"]
    A4 --> A5["STEP 5\nDEPROTONATION\nBY F MINUS ION"]
    A5 --> A6["STEP 6\nLINEAR ALKYL BENZENE\n+ HF REGENERATED"]
    A6 -.CATALYST REGENERATED.-> A1
```
