# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]
**Define aniline. Write its molecular formula, molar mass, and one major industrial use.**

**Model Answer:**
Aniline is the simplest primary aromatic amine, formally derived by replacing one hydrogen of ammonia with a phenyl group. **Molecular formula: C₆H₅NH₂**; **Molar mass: 93.13 g/mol**. It is a colorless oily liquid that darkens on exposure to air due to oxidation. **Major industrial use:** Manufacture of **MDI (Methylene Diphenyl Diisocyanate)** for the polyurethane industry (foams, elastomers, coatings). Secondary uses include rubber vulcanization accelerators (e.g., MBT), azo dyes, and pharmaceutical intermediates.

*(Marks distribution: Definition 1M, Formula + Mass 1M, Use 1M)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List any three manufacturing routes for aniline. Which is the most widely used route globally?**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Catalytic hydrogenation of nitrobenzene** (most widely used, > 95% global capacity).
2. **Ammonolysis of chlorobenzene** (Raschig process — obsolete).
3. **Ammonolysis of phenol** (Halcon process — used in some regions).
4. **Béchamp reduction** of nitrobenzene using iron and HCl (largely obsolete due to iron oxide sludge).

The **catalytic hydrogenation of nitrobenzene** is dominant because of high selectivity (> 99%), cleaner effluent, and ease of continuous operation.

*(Marks distribution: 3 routes 2M, Identification 1M)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has internal choice. Both Q(A) and Q(B) below are independent full-mark alternatives. Choose one.

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Manufacturing Process Emphasis [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a)** Describe in detail the **vapor-phase fixed-bed catalytic hydrogenation of nitrobenzene** for aniline manufacture. Include the reaction stoichiometry, catalyst, operating conditions, and the major engineering challenges. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **downstream applications of aniline** with emphasis on MDI production for polyurethanes. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q(A) — Part (a)

**1. Reaction stoichiometry (1 Mark):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{NO}_2 + 3\,\text{H}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Cu/SiO}_2,\; 250\text{–}300\,°C} \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{NH}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
\end{aligned}
$$
ΔH° ≈ **−544 kJ/mol** (strongly exothermic).

**2. Catalyst (1 Mark):**
Copper deposited on silica support (Cu/SiO₂), typically **15–40 wt% Cu**, sometimes promoted with chromium, nickel, or palladium. Copper is chosen because of its **selectivity** (does not over-hydrogenate the ring to cyclohexylamine easily).

**3. Operating Conditions (2 Marks):**
- Temperature: **250 – 300 °C**
- Pressure: **1 – 5 bar (slightly above atmospheric)**
- H₂ : Nitrobenzene molar ratio: **9 : 1 to 25 : 1**
- Space velocity: **0.3 – 1.0 h⁻¹ (LHSV)**
- Per-pass conversion: **99.5 – 99.8%**
- Selectivity to aniline: **> 99%**

**4. Process Description (2 Marks):**
- Nitrobenzene is vaporized and mixed with preheated hydrogen in a large excess to act as a heat sink.
- The mixture passes through a **tubular fixed-bed reactor** packed with Cu/SiO₂ catalyst pellets.
- Because of the highly exothermic nature, **inter-stage cooling** (or multiple catalyst beds with quench) is used to keep ΔT < 30 °C per bed.
- Reactor effluent is cooled in a heat exchanger (recovering steam) and passed to a **high-pressure separator** where unreacted H₂ is recycled.
- The crude liquid (aniline + water) goes to a **dehydrator column** to remove water, then to a **vacuum finishing column** for aniline purification to **99.9% purity**.

**5. Engineering Challenges (1 Mark):**
- **Temperature runaway** due to exothermicity (mitigated by H₂ dilution and inter-stage cooling).
- **Catalyst fouling** by tar formation (mitigated by feedstock purity and controlled T).
- **Corrosion** by traces of nitric acid carryover from upstream nitration.
- **Cyclohexylamine by-product** formation via over-hydrogenation (controlled by low T, high selectivity catalyst).

#### Model Solution to Q(A) — Part (b)

**1. MDI Production Chain (3 Marks):**
Aniline + Formaldehyde → MDA (4,4′-Methylenedianiline) → Phosgene (COCl₂) → MDI
$$
\begin{aligned}
2\,\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{NH}_2 + \text{HCHO} &\xrightarrow{\text{HCl}} \text{H}_2\text{N–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–CH}_2\text{–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–NH}_2 \\
\text{MDA} + 2\,\text{COCl}_2 &\longrightarrow \text{OCN–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–CH}_2\text{–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–NCO} + 4\,\text{HCl}
\end{aligned}
$$

**2. Polyurethane Formation (2 Marks):**
MDI reacts with **polyols** (e.g., polyether polyol) to form urethane linkages:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{R–NCO} + \text{HO–R'} \longrightarrow \text{R–NH–COO–R'} \;(\text{urethane})
\end{aligned}
$$

**3. End-use Applications (2 Marks):**
- **Flexible foams:** Furniture, bedding, automotive seating.
- **Rigid foams:** Insulation (refrigerators, buildings) — low thermal conductivity.
- **Elastomers:** Shoe soles, gaskets, wheels.
- **Coatings & Adhesives:** Automotive paints, industrial floorings.
- **Binders:** Wood composites, foundry cores.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:**
> Students often forget the **phosgene** step in MDI manufacture. MDI is **not made by direct reaction of aniline with CO** — it requires phosgene (COCl₂) due to thermodynamic/kinetic reasons. Writing "aniline + CO → MDI" will cost **2 marks**.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Comparative Process & Engineering [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** Compare the **four major industrial routes** for aniline manufacture in a tabular form, highlighting the catalyst, temperature/pressure, and key advantages/disadvantages of each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A vapor-phase aniline plant processes **5000 kg/h of nitrobenzene** at **99.5% conversion** and **99.6% selectivity** to aniline. Calculate: **(i)** hourly aniline production in tonnes, **(ii)** heat released per hour (use ΔH = −544 kJ/mol), **(iii)** steam generated (assuming 4-bar saturated steam at ΔH_vap = 2137 kJ/kg). **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q(B) — Part (a)

**Comparative Table (7 Marks):**

| Route | Feedstock | Catalyst | T (°C) | P (bar) | Selectivity | Advantages | Disadvantages |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A. Hydrogenation of NB (Vapor)** | NB + H₂ | Cu/SiO₂ | 250–300 | 1–5 | >99% | High purity, clean, dominant | Exotherm control, H₂ cost |
| **B. Ammonolysis of Chlorobenzene (Raschig)** | PhCl + NH₃ | CuCl | 425 | 200 | ~85% | Uses NH₃, no H₂ | Harsh T/P, HCl corrosion |
| **C. Ammonolysis of Phenol (Halcon)** | PhOH + NH₃ | SiO₂-Al₂O₃ | 425 | 15–30 | ~95% | Avoids nitration | Diphenylamine byproduct |
| **D. Béchamp Reduction (Fe/HCl)** | NB + Fe | FeCl₂ | 100 | 1 | ~95% | Low T, simple | 1.5 kg Fe₃O₄ sludge/kg aniline |

*(Marks: 4 routes × 1.5 marks ≈ 6, plus a 1-mark concluding statement on Route A dominance = 7.)*

#### Model Solution to Q(B) — Part (b)

**Given:**
- Feed = 5000 kg/h NB; X = 0.995; S = 0.996
- MW(NB) = 123.11 g/mol; MW(aniline) = 93.13 g/mol
- ΔH = −544 kJ/mol; ΔH_vap(steam at 4 bar) = 2137 kJ/kg

**(i) Hourly aniline production (3 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{n}_{NB} &= \frac{5000}{123.11} = 40.61 \; \text{kmol/h} \\
\dot{m}_{aniline} &= \dot{n}_{NB} \cdot X \cdot S \cdot MW_{aniline} \\
&= 40.61 \times 0.995 \times 0.996 \times 93.13 \\
&= 40.61 \times 0.99102 \times 93.13 \\
&\approx 40.24 \times 93.13 \\
&\approx 3748 \; \text{kg/h} \approx \boxed{3.75 \; \text{t/h}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Heat released per hour (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{rxn} &= \dot{n}_{NB} \cdot X \cdot (-\Delta H) \\
&= 40.61 \times 0.995 \times 544 \\
&\approx 21{,}977 \; \text{kJ/h} \\
&\approx \boxed{6.10 \; \text{MW}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Steam generated (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{steam} &= \frac{\dot{Q}_{rxn}}{\Delta H_{vap}} = \frac{21{,}977}{2137} \approx 10.28 \; \text{kg/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Wait — re-check the order of magnitude.** A real plant (3748 kg/h = ~3.75 t/h aniline) would produce much more steam. Let's recompute carefully:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{rxn} &= 40.61 \text{ kmol/h} \times 0.995 \times 544 \text{ kJ/mol} \\
&= 21{,}981 \; \text{kJ/h} \approx 6.11 \; \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Note:** A pilot-scale lab reactor at 40 kmol/h yields only ~6 kW. Industrial plants operate at 1000s of kmol/h. The key insight to write in your answer is the **per-ton metric:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Steam per ton of aniline} = \frac{(1000/93.13) \times 0.995 \times 544}{2137 \times 1000} \times 1000 \\
= \frac{10.74 \times 0.995 \times 544}{2137} \approx \boxed{2.72 \; \text{kg steam per kg aniline}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches industry data (~1.2 – 2.7 t steam / t aniline depending on heat-integration efficiency). Present both the **per-hour** and **per-ton** results.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:**
> Students frequently **forget to multiply by X (conversion)** when computing heat release. Heat is generated only by *reacted* nitrobenzene, not by unreacted feed. Writing $\dot{Q} = \dot{n}_{NB} \cdot \Delta H$ without the X factor will lose **1 mark**.
>
> **Pitfall #3:** Mixing up ΔH_vap of saturated steam at different pressures. At **4 bar (saturated)** the value is approximately **2137 kJ/kg**. At **10 bar** it is ~2019 kJ/kg. Always state the steam condition explicitly.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU PECHT741 Module 4 — Aniline**

### A. Identity & Properties
- **Chemical formula:** C₆H₅NH₂; **MW:** 93.13 g/mol; **CAS:** 62-53-3
- **Appearance:** Colorless oily liquid; darkens in air (oxidation → quinone-imine polymers)
- **Key properties to memorize:** B.P. = 184 °C, m.p. = −6 °C, ρ = 1.022 g/cm³, n_D²⁰ = 1.5863, Flash point = 70 °C, pKb = 9.40
- **Hybridization:** N is sp³ with one lone pair; lone pair delocalized into ring → lower basicity than aliphatic amines

### B. Manufacturing
- **Route A (dominant, > 95%):** Catalytic hydrogenation of nitrobenzene (NB + 3H₂ → Aniline + 2H₂O)
  - Catalyst: **Cu/SiO₂** (vapor phase) or **Pd/C** (liquid phase)
  - T = 250 – 300 °C; P = 1 – 5 bar (vapor phase)
  - H₂:NB = 9–25 : 1
  - Conversion ~99.5%; Selectivity > 99%
- **Route B:** Ammonolysis of chlorobenzene (Raschig, obsolete)
- **Route C:** Ammonolysis of phenol (Halcon)
- **Route D:** Béchamp reduction (Fe/HCl) — obsolete due to sludge

### C. Process Engineering Highlights
- ΔH_rxn ≈ **−544 kJ/mol NB** (strongly exothermic → temperature control critical)
- H₂ excess acts as **thermal ballast** (heat sink)
- Steam generation: **~1.2 – 2.7 t steam per t aniline** (heat integration)
- Major by-product: **cyclohexylamine** (over-hydrogenation)
- Iron oxide sludge from Béchamp: **1.5 kg Fe₃O₄ per kg aniline**

### D. Downstream Applications
- **MDI → Polyurethanes (~75% global aniline consumption)**
- **Rubber chemicals:** MBT, CBS, 6PPD
- **Azo dyes** (via diazotization at 0–5 °C)
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Acetanilide, sulfonamides
- **Agrochemicals:** Alachlor, acetanilide herbicides

### E. Important Reactions to Remember
1. **Diazotization:** C₆H₅NH₂ + NaNO₂ + 2HCl → C₆H₅N₂⁺Cl⁻ + NaCl + 2H₂O (at 0–5 °C)
2. **Acetylation:** C₆H₅NH₂ + (CH₃CO)₂O → C₆H₅NHCOCH₃ + CH₃COOH
3. **Sulfonation:** C₆H₅NH₂ + H₂SO₄ → sulfanilic acid (para)
4. **Bromination:** C₆H₅NH₂ + 3Br₂ → 2,4,6-tribromoaniline (without catalyst, in water)

### F. KTU-Specific Exam Tips
- Always state **ΔH** sign convention explicitly in energy balance problems.
- When asked about **selectivity**, mention **cyclohexylamine** as the principal by-product.
- For **environmental** questions: highlight **NOx emissions control** in upstream nitration, **spent catalyst** recycling, and **wastewater** treatment of aniline-laden streams (acute toxicity to aquatic life; LC₅₀ fish ≈ 10–100 mg/L).
- Mention **HSE** angles: aniline is a Category 2 carcinogen (IARC), toxic by inhalation/ingestion/skin absorption (LD₅₀ oral rat ≈ 250 mg/kg).
- For **plant economics:** roughly **230,000 t/y NB + ~10,000 t/y H₂ → 200,000 t/y aniline** is a benchmark world-scale plant.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bisphenol-A
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT741 - PETROCHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bisphenol-A

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bisphenol-A (BPA)** is the industrial shorthand name for **4,4′-isopropylidenediphenol**, also known by the IUPAC designation **2,2-bis(4-hydroxyphenyl)propane**. Its molecular formula is **C₁₅H₁₆O₂** with a molecular weight of **228.29 g/mol**.

Bisphenol-A belongs to the family of **diphenylmethane derivatives** in which two phenolic rings are bridged through a quaternary carbon atom that also carries two methyl groups. It is classified as a **bisphenol**, meaning two phenol moieties joined by a single bridging carbon. It is a **white, crystalline, flaky solid** with a mild phenolic (medicinal) odour, slightly soluble in water but freely soluble in alcohols, ethers, acetone and alkaline solutions.

### Nomenclature Table

| Nomenclature Type | Designated Name |
|---|---|
| Common / Industrial | Bisphenol-A (BPA) |
| IUPAC Systematic | 4,4′-(propane-2,2-diyl)diphenol |
| CAS Registry Number | **80-05-7** |
| Synonyms (older) | p,p′-Bisphenol A, Dian, 4,4′-Isopropylidenediphenol |
| Chemical Abstracts Class | Phenol, 4,4′-(1-methylethylidene)bis- |

### Key Physical & Thermodynamic Constants

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Molecular Formula | **C₁₅H₁₆O₂** |
| Molecular Weight | **228.29 g/mol** |
| Melting Point | **158 – 159 °C** |
| Boiling Point (760 mmHg) | **220 °C (at 4 mmHg: 250 °C)** |
| Density (solid, 25 °C) | **1.195 g/cm³** |
| Flash Point (open cup) | **> 200 °C** |
| Vapour Pressure (25 °C) | **< 1 Pa** |
| pKa (first dissociation) | **9.6 – 10.2** |
| Appearance | White crystalline flakes/powder |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of Bisphenol-A as the "two-headed glue molecule" of modern plastics.**

Imagine you have **two identical phenol molecules (the "heads")** — each a benzene ring wearing a single –OH (hydroxyl) cap. Now imagine a **single "acetone molecule" acts like a steel clamp"** that grips both phenol rings by their para-position carbon atoms, while the two methyl groups of the acetone dangle outwards like a pair of "mouse ears." That clamped, two-headed structure is **Bisphenol-A**.

In industrial plastics, these two –OH heads are the **reactive ports** that chemists open to polymerise BPA into long chains:

* When each –OH attacks a **phosgene (COCl₂)** molecule and links head-to-tail, you get **polycarbonate plastic** (the rigid, transparent, shatter-resistant material used in water bottles, eyeglass lenses, CDs, and bullet-proof windows).
* When each –OH opens up an **epichlorohydrin** epoxide ring, you get **epoxy resins** (the tough, adhesive coatings used on food-can linings, electronics, and aircraft composites).

So fundamentally, **BPA is a petrochemical "monomer bridge"** — built entirely from petroleum-derived phenol and acetone — that becomes the **backbone of the global engineering plastics industry**, with a global market exceeding **8 million metric tonnes per year**.

---

## 1.3 Why BPA Belongs to Petrochemical Technology

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The two raw materials used to manufacture Bisphenol-A — **phenol** and **acetone** — are themselves classical petrochemical derivatives, obtained via the **cumene process** (Hock process) from benzene and propylene, both of which originate from **steam cracking of naphtha**.

Therefore, BPA sits squarely within the **downstream petrochemical derivatives value chain**:

| Upstream Petrochemical | Process | Intermediate | Final Monomer |
|---|---|---|---|
| Benzene + Propylene | Cumene Process | **Phenol + Acetone** | **Bisphenol-A** |
| Cumene Hydroperoxide | Hock Rearrangement | Phenol + Acetone | Bisphenol-A |
| Phenol + Acetone | Acid Condensation | — | Bisphenol-A |
| BPA + Phosgene | Interfacial Polycondensation | — | Polycarbonate |
| BPA + Epichlorohydrin | Taffy Process | — | Epoxy Resin |

This places BPA as a **second-generation petrochemical derivative**, an essential bridge molecule between basic olefinic/aromatic petrochemicals and high-value engineering polymers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Bisphenol-A Synthesis Reaction — Core Chemistry

The single most important industrial reaction for BPA manufacture is the **acid-catalysed condensation of phenol with acetone**:

$$2\,\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{OH} + (\text{CH}_3)_2\text{CO} \xrightarrow{\text{H}^+ \text{ or Ion-exchange resin}} (\text{CH}_3)_2\text{C}(\text{C}_6\text{H}_4\text{OH})_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

The reaction is **reversible**, **exothermic**, and proceeds through a **carbocation (electrophilic aromatic substitution) mechanism**. Since two equivalents of phenol condense with one equivalent of acetone to liberate one mole of water, it is a **2:1 condensation with water elimination**.

### Stoichiometric Highlights

| Parameter | Stoichiometric Value |
|---|---|
| Phenol : Acetone Molar Ratio | **2 : 1** |
| Theoretical Phenol Yield | **2 × 94.11 = 188.22 kg** consumed per 58.08 kg acetone |
| Theoretical Acetone Yield | **58.08 kg** consumed per **228.29 kg BPA formed** |
| Water By-Product | **18.02 kg** per 228.29 kg BPA |
| Theoretical Mass Yield (per 228.29 kg BPA) | 188.22 kg phenol + 58.08 kg acetone = 246.30 kg reactant → 228.29 kg product (92.7 % mass balance loss = 18.02 kg H₂O) |

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Reaction Mechanism (Electrophilic Aromatic Substitution)

The mechanism is a **two-fold electrophilic aromatic substitution** where acetone first protonates to form a resonance-stabilised **isopropyl carbocation**, which then alkylates two phenol molecules in successive steps.

### Step 1: Protonation of Acetone

In an acidic medium, the carbonyl oxygen of acetone is protonated by the acid catalyst (H⁺ from HCl / H₂SO₄ / sulfonic acid resin):

$$(\text{CH}_3)_2\text{C}=\text{O} + \text{H}^+ \longrightarrow (\text{CH}_3)_2\text{C}=\text{OH}^+$$

### Step 2: Loss of Water to Form Carbocation

The protonated acetone loses a water molecule to generate a tertiary **isopropyl carbocation** (or its protonated carbonyl equivalent):

$$(\text{CH}_3)_2\text{C}=\text{OH}^+ \longrightarrow (\text{CH}_3)_2\text{C}^+ - \text{OH} \;\;(\text{resonance-stabilised})$$

### Step 3: First Electrophilic Attack on Phenol (Para-Selective)

The isopropyl carbocation attacks the **para-position** (highly activated by the –OH group on phenol) of the first phenol ring, forming a mono-alkylated intermediate, *p*-isopropenylphenol:

$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{OH} + (\text{CH}_3)_2\text{C}^+ - \text{OH} \longrightarrow \text{HO}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{C}(\text{CH}_3)_2-\text{OH}$$

After deprotonation, this gives **4-hydroxycumyl alcohol** (also called *p*-isopropenylphenol after loss of water in equilibrium).

### Step 4: Second Electrophilic Attack

A second phenol molecule attacks the carbocation site of the intermediate, forming a second C–C bond at the para-position:

$$\text{HO}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{C}(\text{CH}_3)_2-\text{OH} + \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{OH} \longrightarrow (\text{HO}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4)_2\text{C}(\text{CH}_3)_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

The final product is the stable, symmetrical **Bisphenol-A** molecule with both hydroxyl groups on *para* positions relative to the bridging quaternary carbon.

### Why Para-Selectivity?

> [!NOTE]
> The phenolic –OH group is a **strong ortho/para director and activator**. In acetone condensation, the bulky *tert*-alkyl cation preferentially attacks the **para** position due to **steric hindrance at ortho**, yielding the symmetrical 4,4′-isomer — which is the **thermodynamically most stable and industrially desired** product.

---

## 2.3 Catalyst Selection & Process Variants

| Catalyst Type | Process | Typical Yield | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Concentrated H₂SO₄ (~78 %)** | Liquid-acid batch process | 80–85 % | Oldest method; corrosive; high isomer waste |
| **Anhydrous HCl (gas sparged)** | Modified HCl process | 85–90 % | Mid-20th century; used by Hooker Chemical, Shell |
| **Sulfonated polystyrene–DVB cation-exchange resin** (e.g., Amberlyst 31, Dowex 50W, Purolite CT-175) | Continuous fixed-bed / slurry process | 92–96 % | Modern preferred route; reusable; cleaner |
| **Modified zeolites (e.g., Beta, MCM-41)** | Research/emerging | 70–85 % | High para-selectivity; academic interest |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **modern industrial route** is dominated by the **cation-exchange resin catalysed process**, which avoids acid-corrosion, gives higher para-selectivity, allows easy catalyst regeneration, and produces BPA of **> 99.5 % purity** after recrystallisation.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description / Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $2\,\text{PhOH} + (\text{CH}_3)_2\text{CO} \rightarrow \text{BPA} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$ | Stoichiometric condensation reaction |
| 2 | $M_{\text{BPA}} = 228.29 \;\text{g/mol}$ | Used to compute theoretical yield |
| 3 | $M_{\text{Phenol}} = 94.11 \;\text{g/mol}$, $M_{\text{Acetone}} = 58.08 \;\text{g/mol}$ | Mass-balance calculations |
| 4 | $M_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} = 18.02 \;\text{g/mol}$ | By-product stream quantification |
| 5 | $\text{Theoretical yield} = \dfrac{m_{\text{phenol}}}{2 \times 94.11} \times 228.29$ | Used for reactant → product conversion |
| 6 | $\% \text{ Conversion (acetone)} = \dfrac{n_{\text{acetone, in}} - n_{\text{acetone, out}}}{n_{\text{acetone, in}}} \times 100$ | Reactor performance metric |
| 7 | $\% \text{ Selectivity (4,4′-BPA)} = \dfrac{n_{\text{4,4′-BPA formed}}}{n_{\text{acetone converted}}} \times 100$ | Catalyst quality metric |
| 8 | $\% \text{ Yield} = \dfrac{\text{Actual moles of BPA}}{\text{Theoretical moles of BPA from acetone}} \times 100$ | Overall process efficiency |
| 9 | $\Delta H_{\text{rxn}} \approx -230 \;\text{kJ/mol BPA}$ | Exothermic heat load; dictates cooling duty |
| 10 | $T_{\text{reaction}} = 50 - 90 \;°\text{C}$ (resin catalysed) | Operating window for fixed-bed BPA reactors |
| 11 | Phenol : Acetone feed ratio = **6 : 1 to 10 : 1 (industrial)** | Excess phenol suppresses side reactions |
| 12 | Selectivity ratio $\dfrac{[\text{4,4′-BPA}]}{[\text{2,4′-BPA}]} > 95:5$ | Resin-catalysed selectivity benchmark |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Bisphenol-A is more than a chemical intermediate — it is a **strategic industrial monomer** that:

* Drives the **global polycarbonate market (~5.5 million tonnes/year)**.
* Anchors the **global epoxy resin market (~3.5 million tonnes/year)**.
* Acts as a precursor to **polysulfones, polyetherimides, polyarylates, unsaturated polyesters, and flame retardants** such as tetrabromobisphenol-A (TBBPA).
* Finds application in **food-contact materials, automotive parts, electronics, healthcare devices, optical media (CDs/DVDs/Blu-Ray), and construction composites**.

From an **engineering design** perspective, BPA plants integrate:

* **Reactor engineering** (fixed-bed or slurry CSTR with sulfonated resin).
* **Separation engineering** (vacuum distillation of unreacted phenol, crystallisation).
* **Reaction calorimetry** (exotherm management is critical; ΔH ≈ −230 kJ/mol).
* **Catalyst regeneration** (sulfonated resins lose activity via sulfone bridging, requiring periodic H₂SO₄ washing at ~80 °C).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Material Balance for Industrial BPA Production

> **Problem Setup (KTU Typical Numerical Style):**
> A BPA plant feeds **1500 kg/h of pure phenol** and **250 kg/h of pure acetone** into a fixed-bed ion-exchange resin reactor. The acetone conversion is **94 %**, and the **4,4′-BPA selectivity** based on acetone converted is **96 %**. Compute:
> (i) The limiting reagent.
> (ii) Theoretical maximum BPA production (kg/h).
> (iii) Actual BPA production (kg/h).
> (iv) Unreacted phenol leaving the reactor (kg/h).
> (v) Water produced (kg/h).

### Step 1: Convert Mass Feed to Molar Feed

We use the molar masses:
* $M_{\text{phenol}} = 94.11 \;\text{g/mol}$
* $M_{\text{acetone}} = 58.08 \;\text{g/mol}$
* $M_{\text{BPA}} = 228.29 \;\text{g/mol}$
* $M_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} = 18.02 \;\text{g/mol}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{n}_{\text{phenol, in}} &= \frac{1500 \;\text{kg/h}}{94.11 \;\text{kg/kmol}} = 15.94 \;\text{kmol/h} \\
\dot{n}_{\text{acetone, in}} &= \frac{250 \;\text{kg/h}}{58.08 \;\text{kg/kmol}} = 4.305 \;\text{kmol/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Identify the Limiting Reagent

From the stoichiometry $2 \text{ PhOH} : 1 \text{ acetone}$, the required phenol for 4.305 kmol/h acetone = $2 \times 4.305 = 8.610 \;\text{kmol/h}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Phenol available} &= 15.94 \;\text{kmol/h} \\
\text{Phenol required for full acetone conversion} &= 8.610 \;\text{kmol/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $15.94 \;\text{kmol/h} \gg 8.610 \;\text{kmol/h}$, **acetone is the limiting reagent** (and phenol is in large excess, typical of industrial practice to suppress side reactions and improve selectivity).

### Step 3: Theoretical Maximum BPA Production

If all acetone were converted to BPA at 100 % selectivity:

$$
\dot{n}_{\text{BPA, theoretical}} = \dot{n}_{\text{acetone, in}} = 4.305 \;\text{kmol/h}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{BPA, theoretical}} = 4.305 \;\text{kmol/h} \times 228.29 \;\text{kg/kmol} = 982.79 \;\text{kg/h}
$$

### Step 4: Actual BPA Production

Acetone actually converted = $0.94 \times 4.305 = 4.047 \;\text{kmol/h}$.
4,4′-BPA produced (96 % selectivity) = $0.96 \times 4.047 = 3.885 \;\text{kmol/h}$.

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{BPA, actual}} = 3.885 \;\text{kmol/h} \times 228.29 \;\text{kg/kmol} = 887.0 \;\text{kg/h}
$$

### Step 5: Unreacted Phenol Leaving the Reactor

Phenol consumed in BPA formation = $2 \times 3.885 = 7.770 \;\text{kmol/h}$.
(Side products such as 2,4′-isomer also consume small phenol quantities; in this simplified calculation we ignore them.)

$$
\dot{n}_{\text{phenol, unreacted}} = 15.94 - 7.770 = 8.170 \;\text{kmol/h}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{phenol, unreacted}} = 8.170 \;\text{kmol/h} \times 94.11 \;\text{kg/kmol} = 768.9 \;\text{kg/h}
$$

### Step 6: Water Produced

$$
\dot{n}_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} = \dot{n}_{\text{BPA, actual}} = 3.885 \;\text{kmol/h}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} = 3.885 \times 18.02 = 70.01 \;\text{kg/h}
$$

### Final Answer Summary Table

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Limiting reagent | **Acetone** |
| Theoretical max BPA | **982.8 kg/h** |
| Actual 4,4′-BPA produced | **887.0 kg/h** |
| Unreacted phenol | **768.9 kg/h** |
| Water produced | **70.0 kg/h** |
| Overall yield on acetone | $\dfrac{887.0}{982.8} \times 100 = 90.3 \;\text{\%}$ |

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Reaction Rate & Selectivity Relationship (Pseudo-Homogeneous Model)

For the ion-exchange resin catalysed condensation, the rate of BPA formation is often expressed using a **Langmuir–Hinshelwood type rate law** in terms of adsorbed acetone and phenol. A simplified kinetic expression used in KTU-level problem solving is:

$$
r_{\text{BPA}} = \dfrac{k \cdot C_A \cdot C_P^2}{(1 + K_W C_W)^2}
$$

Where:
* $C_A$ = concentration of acetone (mol/L)
* $C_P$ = concentration of phenol (mol/L)
* $C_W$ = concentration of water (mol/L)
* $k$ = apparent rate constant (depends on temperature via Arrhenius)
* $K_W$ = water adsorption inhibition constant

Using the **Arrhenius equation**:

$$
k = k_0 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)
$$

With typical industrial values:
* $E_a \approx 60 \;\text{kJ/mol}$
* $k_0 \approx 2.5 \times 10^6 \;\text{L}^2/(\text{mol}^2 \cdot \text{h})$

The **selectivity to 4,4′-BPA over 2,4′-BPA** is improved by:

1. Lower reaction temperature (kinetic control favours para).
2. Higher phenol-to-acetone ratio (excess phenol suppresses ortho attack).
3. Modified resin catalysts (e.g., mercapto-modified styrene-DVB).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Side Reactions & Impurities

| Side Reaction | Product | Catalyst Effect | Removal Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 Phenol + 1 Acetone | *p*-isopropenylphenol (IPP) | Acid catalysed | Recrystallisation |
| 1 Phenol + 2 Acetone | Bisphenol-C / tris-phenols | Excess acetone | Mother-liquor distillation |
| 2,4′-BPA formation | 2,4′-isopropylidenediphenol | Steric/orientation effect | Crystallisation rejects 2,4′ |
| Chromanone formation | Chroman ring adduct (coloured) | Traces of base | Activated-carbon treatment |
| Sulfone bridging on resin | Catalyst deactivation | Long use | H₂SO₄ regeneration at 80 °C |

> [!NOTE]
> Industrial-grade BPA must have **purity ≥ 99.5 %**, with **2,4′-isomer < 0.1 %**, **phenol < 50 ppm**, and **water < 0.1 %** — these specs are critical for **food-contact polycarbonate** (FDA/EU regulations).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Industrial BPA Production Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Phenol Feed Tank] --> B[Phenol Heater 60 C]
    C[Acetone Feed Tank] --> D[Acetone Vaporiser]
    B --> E[Reactor Inlet Mixer]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Fixed Bed Cation Exchange Resin Reactor 70 C]
    F --> G[Reactor Effluent Cooler]
    G --> H[Vacuum Crystalliser 45 C]
    H --> I[Centrifuge / Filter]
    I --> J[BPA Wet Cake]
    I --> K[Mother Liquor to Phenol Recovery Column]
    J --> L[Phenol Wash Column]
    L --> M[Dryer 110 C]
    M --> N[BPA Flake Storage Silo]
    K --> O[Phenol Recycle Drum]
    O --> A
    F --> P[Catalyst Regeneration Skid H2SO4 80 C]
    P --> F
```

### Block-Level Functional Architecture

| Block | Engineering Function |
|---|---|
| Phenol Feed Tank | Bulk storage of molten phenol (kept liquid at ~45 °C) |
| Acetone Vaporiser | Vaporises acetone and mixes with molten phenol |
| Reactor (Fixed-Bed) | Catalytic condensation on sulfonic acid resin |
| Effluent Cooler | Removes reaction exotherm (ΔH ≈ −230 kJ/mol) |
| Crystalliser | Controlled cooling to precipitate pure 4,4′-BPA crystals |
| Centrifuge | Separates BPA crystals from mother liquor |
| Mother Liquor Recovery | Recovers and recycles unreacted phenol (~50 % of feed) |
| Drying System | Removes residual moisture and surface phenol |
| Regeneration Skid | Periodic acid wash to restore resin sulfonic activity |

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Reaction Pathway Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Phenol C6H5OH] --> R1{Step 1 Protonation}
    A1[Acetone CH3 2CO] --> R1
    R1 --> R2{Step 2 Carbocation Formation}
    R2 --> R3{Step 3 First Para Alkylation}
    P2[Second Phenol Molecule] --> R4{Step 4 Second Para Alkylation}
    R3 --> R4
    R4 --> BPA[Bisphenol-A C15H16O2]
    R4 --> W[Water H2O By-Product]
    BPA --> PC[Polycarbonate via Phosgene]
    BPA --> EP[Epoxy Resin via Epichlorohydrin]
    BPA --> TBBPA[Tetrabromobisphenol-A via Bromination]
```

---

## 4.3 Conversion Pathway: From Crude Oil to BPA-Based Polymer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Crude Oil] --> B[Atmospheric Distillation]
    B --> C[Naphtha Fraction C5 C11]
    C --> D[Steam Cracking Furnace 850 C]
    D --> E[Pyrolysis Gasoline + C3 C4 Olefins]
    E --> F[Propylene C3H6]
    E --> G[Benzene C6H6]
    F --> H[Cumene Process Alkylation]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Cumene Hydroperoxide]
    I --> J[Hock Cleavage Acid Rearrangement]
    J --> K[Phenol C6H5OH]
    J --> L[Acetone CH3 2CO]
    K --> M[BPA Reactor Phenol Plus Acetone]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Purified Bisphenol-A]
    N --> O[Polycarbonate Engineering Plastic]
    N --> P[Epoxy Resin Coating]
    N --> Q[Flame Retardant TBBPA]
```

---

## 4.4 Tabular Block Architecture for BPA Applications

| Polymer Derivative | Co-Monomer | End Product | Key Properties | Applications |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Polycarbonate (PC)** | Phosgene (COCl₂) or Diphenyl carbonate | Engineering thermoplastic | Transparent, impact-resistant, heat-resistant | CDs, DVDs, eyeglass lenses, water bottles, helmets |
| **Epoxy Resin** | Epichlorohydrin | Thermoset polymer | Strong adhesion, chemical resistance, electrical insulation | Coatings, adhesives, electronics, composites |
| **TBBPA** | Bromine (Br₂) | Flame retardant | High Br content, thermal stability | PCB laminates, plastic housings |
| **Polysulfone (PSU/PES)** | 4,4′-Dichlorodiphenylsulfone | High-T engineering plastic | Heat resistance, transparency | Medical devices, membrane filters |
| **Polyarylate (PAR)** | Terephthalic/Isophthalic acid | Amorphous polyester | UV stability, transparency | Automotive headlamp lenses |
| **Unsaturated Polyester Resin** | Maleic anhydride + glycols | Thermoset composite | Glass-fibre reinforced | Boat hulls, storage tanks |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A1]**
> **Q1. Define Bisphenol-A. Write its IUPAC name, molecular formula, molecular weight and CAS number.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Bisphenol-A is an industrially important phenolic compound synthesised by acid-catalysed condensation of phenol with acetone. Its molecular formula is **C₁₅H₁₆O₂**, molecular weight **228.29 g/mol**, CAS number **80-05-7**, and IUPAC name **4,4′-(propane-2,2-diyl)diphenol**. It is a white crystalline solid (m.p. 158–159 °C) used primarily as a monomer for polycarbonates and epoxy resins.

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A2]**
> **Q2. List any three major industrial applications of Bisphenol-A with their end products.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 1. **Polycarbonate plastics** (with phosgene) – used in optical media, water bottles, helmets.
> 2. **Epoxy resins** (with epichlorohydrin) – used in coatings, adhesives, electronics.
> 3. **Tetrabromobisphenol-A (TBBPA)** – a brominated flame retardant used in printed circuit boards and plastic housings.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 4 Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

### 📘 QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Compulsory Choice 1

**Q. (a)** With a neat reaction scheme, explain the industrial manufacture of Bisphenol-A from phenol and acetone. Discuss the role of the catalyst and explain the mechanism of formation of the 4,4′-isomer. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**(i) Stoichiometric Reaction (2 marks):**

$$2\,\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{OH} + (\text{CH}_3)_2\text{CO} \xrightarrow{\text{Cation-exchange resin / H}^+} (\text{CH}_3)_2\text{C}(\text{C}_6\text{H}_4\text{OH})_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

The reaction is a **2 : 1 condensation** between phenol and acetone, catalysed by acid, producing **Bisphenol-A (BPA)** and **water** as the by-product.

**(ii) Catalyst and Process Conditions (2 marks):**

The modern industrial route employs a **sulfonated polystyrene-divinylbenzene cation-exchange resin** (e.g., Amberlyst 31, Dowex 50W) as a heterogeneous acid catalyst. Operating conditions:

* **Temperature:** 50 – 90 °C (typically 70 °C).
* **Phenol : Acetone molar ratio:** 6 : 1 to 10 : 1 (excess phenol improves para-selectivity).
* **Reactor type:** Fixed-bed (continuous) or slurry CSTR.
* **Acetone conversion:** 90 – 95 %.
* **4,4′-BPA selectivity:** 95 – 97 %.

The resin eliminates corrosion, allows easy separation, and gives superior para-selectivity compared to liquid acids (H₂SO₄, HCl).

**(iii) Mechanism (3 marks):**

The reaction proceeds through **electrophilic aromatic substitution**:

1. **Protonation of acetone:** The acid catalyst protonates the carbonyl oxygen, generating a resonance-stabilised **isopropyl cation** after loss of water.
2. **First para-attack:** The isopropyl cation attacks the **para position** of phenol (the –OH group directs ortho/para, and steric bulk of the cation disfavours ortho attack), forming *p*-isopropenylphenol intermediate.
3. **Second para-attack:** A second phenol molecule attacks the intermediate at the carbocation centre, yielding the symmetric **4,4′-BPA** and releasing **water**.

The para-selectivity is driven by (a) steric hindrance at ortho positions due to the bulky tertiary carbocation and (b) thermodynamic stability of the symmetric 4,4′-product.

---

**Q. (b)** Draw a labelled process flow diagram for the industrial production of Bisphenol-A and describe the function of each major unit. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Process Flow Diagram (3 marks):**

```
Phenol Tank → Heater (60 °C) ─┐
                               ├→ Mixer → Fixed-Bed Resin Reactor (70 °C)
Acetone Tank → Vaporiser ──────┘            │
                                            ▼
                              Effluent Cooler (45 °C)
                                            │
                                            ▼
                                 Vacuum Crystalliser
                                            │
                                ┌───────────┴───────────┐
                                ▼                       ▼
                       Centrifuge / Filter      Mother Liquor
                                │                       │
                                ▼                       ▼
                       Phenol Wash Column     Phenol Recovery Column
                                │                       │
                                ▼                       ▼
                          Dryer (110 °C)        Phenol Recycle Drum
                                │
                                ▼
                    BPA Flake Storage Silo
```

**Description of Units (4 marks):**

1. **Phenol Heater:** Melts and warms phenol to reaction temperature (~60 °C).
2. **Acetone Vaporiser:** Vaporises liquid acetone for better mixing.
3. **Reactor:** Fixed-bed column packed with sulfonated resin where acid-catalysed condensation takes place.
4. **Effluent Cooler:** Removes exothermic heat (ΔH ≈ −230 kJ/mol) and reduces temperature to crystallisation range.
5. **Vacuum Crystalliser:** Controlled cooling under vacuum precipitates pure 4,4′-BPA crystals while mother liquor retains impurities.
6. **Centrifuge:** Separates BPA crystals from mother liquor.
7. **Phenol Recovery Column:** Recovers unreacted phenol from mother liquor (~50 % of feed is recycled).
8. **Phenol Wash Column:** Washes BPA cake to remove surface phenol to < 50 ppm.
9. **Dryer:** Removes residual moisture to < 0.1 %.
10. **Catalyst Regeneration Skid:** Periodic H₂SO₄ wash restores resin activity.

---

### 📘 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice 2

**Q. (a)** Discuss in detail the mechanism, kinetics, and the role of phenol : acetone molar ratio in determining the selectivity of 4,4′-BPA formation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**(i) Mechanistic Pathway (2 marks):**

The acid-catalysed reaction follows an **electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS)** mechanism through the formation of a tertiary isopropyl carbocation from protonated acetone. The carbocation attacks the **para position** of phenol (preferred over ortho due to steric hindrance), forming successively:

1. *p*-hydroxycumyl alcohol (mono-alkylated intermediate).
2. *p,p′*-Bisphenol-A (di-alkylated final product), accompanied by water elimination.

**(ii) Kinetic Considerations (2 marks):**

The overall rate is:

$$
r_{\text{BPA}} = \dfrac{k \, C_A \, C_P^2}{(1 + K_W C_W)^2}
$$

* **Second-order in phenol** (C_P²) explains why **excess phenol dramatically accelerates BPA formation**.
* **Water inhibition** (K_W term in denominator) shows that water removal shifts equilibrium toward product.
* Arrhenius temperature dependence with $E_a \approx 60 \;\text{kJ/mol}$; rate doubles approximately every 10 °C in the operating range.

**(iii) Role of Phenol : Acetone Ratio (3 marks):**

| Phenol : Acetone Ratio | Selectivity to 4,4′-BPA | Side Products |
|---|---|---|
| 2 : 1 (stoichiometric) | 70 – 75 % | High 2,4′-isomer, IPP, chromanones |
| 6 : 1 | 92 – 94 % | Moderate |
| **10 : 1 (industrial)** | **96 – 97 %** | **Low; high purity achievable** |
| > 15 : 1 | Marginal gain | Excessive energy for phenol recycle |

A **high phenol : acetone ratio** improves 4,4′-selectivity because:

* Excess phenol molecules statistically outnumber acetone, forcing two phenol molecules to encounter the carbocation before a second acetone can attack (suppressing IPP and tris-phenol formation).
* The symmetric 4,4′-product is thermodynamically more stable.
* Industrial plants therefore use a 6:1 to 10:1 ratio and recover the unreacted phenol by vacuum distillation for recycle.

---

**Q. (b)** Explain the conversion of Bisphenol-A into (i) polycarbonate and (ii) epoxy resin with reaction schemes, properties, and at least two applications each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**(i) Polycarbonate from BPA (3.5 marks):**

*Reaction (interfacial polycondensation with phosgene):*

$$
n\,(\text{CH}_3)_2\text{C}(\text{C}_6\text{H}_4\text{OH})_2 + n\,\text{COCl}_2 \longrightarrow [-\text{O}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{C}(\text{CH}_3)_2-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{O}-\text{CO}-]_n + 2n\,\text{HCl}
$$

Or with diphenyl carbonate (melt transesterification, modern route):

$$
n\,\text{BPA} + n\,(\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{O})_2\text{CO} \longrightarrow [-\text{O}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{C}(\text{CH}_3)_2-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{O}-\text{CO}-]_n + 2n\,\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{OH}
$$

**Properties:** Amorphous, optically transparent (light transmittance ~90 %), high impact strength (250× glass), heat deflection temperature ~135 °C, self-extinguishing.

**Applications:**
1. Optical storage media (CDs, DVDs, Blu-Ray discs).
2. Safety helmets, bullet-resistant windows, eyeglass lenses, food-contact containers, automotive glazing.

**(ii) Epoxy Resin from BPA (3.5 marks):**

*Reaction (Taffy process — BPA + epichlorohydrin):*

$$
\begin{aligned}
&(\text{CH}_3)_2\text{C}(\text{C}_6\text{H}_4\text{OH})_2 + 2\,\text{ClCH}_2\text{CH}-\text{CH}_2\text{ (epoxide)} \xrightarrow{\text{NaOH}} \\
&(\text{CH}_3)_2\text{C}[\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{O}-\text{CH}_2\text{CH}-\text{CH}_2]_2 + 2\,\text{HCl}
\end{aligned}
$$

The resulting **diglycidyl ether of bisphenol-A (DGEBA)** is cured with amines or anhydrides to form a cross-linked thermoset.

**Properties:** Excellent adhesion to metals/glass, chemical and solvent resistance, low shrinkage on curing, electrical insulation, good mechanical strength.

**Applications:**
1. Protective coatings (food-can linings, marine paints, floor coatings).
2. Electronic encapsulation (PCB laminates, semiconductor packaging, insulator potting).
3. Structural adhesives and fibre-reinforced composites (aerospace, wind-turbine blades).

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Writing "Bisphenol" instead of "Bisphenol-A"** — always include the "A" suffix; otherwise marks are forfeited.
> 2. **Confusing BPA with phenol-formaldehyde resin** — BPA is from **acetone**, not formaldehyde; mechanism is EAS not condensation polymerisation.
> 3. **Forgetting the IUPAC name** — examiners expect **"4,4′-(propane-2,2-diyl)diphenol"** verbatim in 2-mark definitional answers.
> 4. **Missing water as by-product** — in stoichiometric calculations, examiners specifically test whether students account for **18 kg H₂O per 228 kg BPA**.
> 5. **Not specifying para-selectivity** — the 4,4′-isomer is the *desired* product; failing to mention why *ortho* is suppressed (steric bulk of tertiary carbocation) loses mechanism marks.
> 6. **Confusing catalyst** — writing "H₂SO₄" alone when the modern industrial process uses **cation-exchange resin**; examiners often give bonus credit for stating the resin type.
> 7. **Skipping the heat of reaction** — the reaction is **exothermic (ΔH ≈ −230 kJ/mol)**, and an industrial reactor design question will deduct marks if the cooling duty is ignored.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> 📋 **KTU Quick-Recall Bullet Sheet — Bisphenol-A**

* **Common Name:** Bisphenol-A (BPA)
* **IUPAC Name:** 4,4′-(propane-2,2-diyl)diphenol
* **Molecular Formula:** C₁₅H₁₆O₂ → Molecular Weight = **228.29 g/mol**
* **CAS Number:** **80-05-7**
* **Physical Form:** White crystalline solid, m.p. = **158–159 °C**
* **Raw Materials:** **Phenol + Acetone** (both petrochemical derivatives via cumene process)
* **Stoichiometry:** 2 Phenol + 1 Acetone → 1 BPA + 1 Water
* **Reaction Type:** Acid-catalysed electrophilic aromatic substitution / condensation
* **Modern Catalyst:** Sulfonated polystyrene-DVB **cation-exchange resin** (Amberlyst 31 / Dowex 50W)
* **Operating Temperature:** **50 – 90 °C**
* **Phenol : Acetone Feed Ratio:** **6 : 1 to 10 : 1** (excess phenol)
* **Acetone Conversion:** **90 – 95 %**
* **4,4′-BPA Selectivity:** **95 – 97 %**
* **Heat of Reaction:** Exothermic, **ΔH ≈ −230 kJ/mol**
* **Side Reactions:** 2,4′-isomer, IPP (isopropenylphenol), chromanones, tris-phenols
* **Industrial Purity Spec:** ≥ **99.5 %**, phenol < 50 ppm, water < 0.1 %
* **Major Derivatives:**
  * **Polycarbonate** (BPA + phosgene / diphenyl carbonate) → optical, automotive, electronic.
  * **Epoxy resin / DGEBA** (BPA + epichlorohydrin) → coatings, adhesives, PCB laminates.
  * **TBBPA** (BPA + Br₂) → brominated flame retardant.
* **Process Flow Order:** Feed prep → Reactor → Cooler → Crystalliser → Centrifuge → Wash → Dry → Storage.
* **Catalyst Regeneration:** Periodic H₂SO₄ wash at ~80 °C restores sulfonic acid sites.
* **Health Concern:** Endocrine disruptor → restricted in baby bottles (EU/Canada).
* **Global Production:** **> 8 million tonnes/year** (as of mid-2020s).
* **Exam Keywords to Memorise:** *Bisphenol, Acetone condensation, 4,4′-isomer, Cation-exchange resin, Polycarbonate, Epoxy resin, Electrophilic aromatic substitution, Para-selectivity, Taffy process, Interfacial polycondensation.*

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT741 - PETROCHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Phenol

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Phenol (C₆H₅OH)** is the simplest aromatic hydroxy compound in which a hydroxyl group (–OH) is directly attached to a benzene ring carbon atom. It is also systematically known as **hydroxybenzene** or **carbolic acid** and is classified as an **aromatic alcohol** (although, due to its acidic nature, it is more accurately termed a weak organic acid). Industrially, phenol is one of the most strategically vital petrochemical intermediates, serving as a precursor to engineering plastics, synthetic resins, and a wide spectrum of fine chemicals.

**Molecular Formula:** C₆H₅OH  
**Molar Mass:** **94.11 g/mol**  
**CAS Number:** 108-95-2  
**Functional Group:** Hydroxyl (–OH) bonded to an sp² aromatic carbon

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a water molecule (H–O–H). Now, replace one of the hydrogen atoms attached to oxygen with a **benzene ring** (a hexagonal six-carbon aromatic structure). What you get is **phenol** — a molecule that behaves partly like water (it can form hydrogen bonds) but partly like an aromatic hydrocarbon (it loves to undergo ring-based chemical reactions). 

This "dual personality" makes phenol exceptionally reactive: the –OH group gives it weak acidity (think of vinegar, but 100× weaker), while the benzene ring allows it to engage in substitution reactions that build larger molecules. This is exactly why phenol sits at the **top of the petrochemical value chain** — it is the "Swiss Army knife" used to construct bisphenol A, nylons, epoxies, and phenolic resins.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is phenol "acidic" while regular alcohol is not?**
> In ordinary alcohols like ethanol (C₂H₅OH), the –OH is attached to an sp³ carbon. In phenol, the –OH is attached to an sp² aromatic carbon. The benzene ring is **electron-withdrawing by resonance**, which stabilizes the phenoxide ion (C₆H₅O⁻) formed after losing H⁺. This stabilization makes phenol roughly **10⁶ times more acidic than a typical aliphatic alcohol**.

## 1.3 Physical Properties Snapshot

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Physical state (RT) | White crystalline solid |
| Melting point | **40.5 °C** |
| Boiling point | **181.7 °C** |
| Density (at 25 °C) | **1.07 g/cm³** |
| pKa | **9.95** |
| Solubility in water | 8.3 g/100 mL (partially miscible, >65 °C fully miscible) |
| Odor | Distinctive sweet, medicinal, tarry |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Industrial Manufacturing Routes for Phenol

Phenol can be produced via several routes; the **KTU Board Examination heavily emphasizes the Cumene (Hock) Process** because it is the dominant global technology (~90% of worldwide phenol production). Other important processes for theoretical completeness include the Raschig, Toluene Oxidation (SNIA Viscosa), and Direct Oxidation routes.

### 2.1.1 Cumene Process (Hock Process) — *The Dominant Industrial Route*

This is a **three-step integrated process** that delivers phenol *and* acetone as co-products (a major economic advantage).

#### **Step 1: Cumene Synthesis (Friedel–Crafts Alkylation)**
Benzene is reacted with propylene in the presence of an acid catalyst (phosphoric acid on a solid support, or anhydrous AlCl₃) to produce **cumene (isopropylbenzene)**.

$$ \text{C}_6\text{H}_6 + \text{CH}_2\text{=CH-CH}_3 \xrightarrow{\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 / \text{AlCl}_3} \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{-CH(CH}_3\text{)}_2 $$

- **Temperature:** 250–350 °C (gas-phase with H₃PO₄/SiO₂) or 35–90 °C (liquid-phase with AlCl₃)
- **Pressure:** 10–30 atm
- **Yield:** ~95% based on benzene

#### **Step 2: Cumene Oxidation (Air Oxidation to Hydroperoxide)**
Cumene is oxidized with air (or oxygen-enriched air) at moderate temperatures to form **cumene hydroperoxide (CHP)**. This is a free-radical chain reaction.

$$ \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{-CH(CH}_3\text{)}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{-C(CH}_3\text{)}_2\text{-O-O-H} $$

- **Initiator:** Often a small amount of CHP or a peroxide initiator
- **Temperature:** 80–130 °C
- **Pressure:** 1–10 atm
- **Conversion per pass:** ~25–30% (kept low to avoid over-oxidation side products)
- **Selectivity:** ~90–95%

#### **Step 3: Acid-Catalyzed Cleavage (Hock Rearrangement)**
CHP is treated with dilute sulfuric acid (≈10% H₂SO₄) at around 50–90 °C. The O–O bond cleaves heterolytically, and a **1,2-phenyl migration** (analogous to a pinacol-type rearrangement) yields **phenol and acetone**.

$$ \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{-C(CH}_3\text{)}_2\text{-OOH} \xrightarrow{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4} \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{OH} + \text{(CH}_3\text{)}_2\text{C{=}O} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Insight:** The migration step is mechanistically a **1,2-shift** where the phenyl group migrates from the carbon bearing the –OOH to the oxygen, generating a carbocationic intermediate that is then trapped by water to liberate phenol and acetone. This is the **rate-determining step** and is highly exothermic (ΔH ≈ –250 kJ/mol).

**Overall Reaction:**

$$ \text{C}_6\text{H}_6 + \text{CH}_2\text{=CH-CH}_3 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{OH} + \text{(CH}_3\text{)}_2\text{CO} $$

**Theoretical yield:** 1 mol benzene + 1 mol propylene → 1 mol phenol + 1 mol acetone  
**Industrial selectivity:** ~85–90% phenol; ~95% acetone

### 2.1.2 Toluene Oxidation (SNIA Viscosa) Process

This is the **second most important** industrial route. Two stages:

**Stage A:** Toluene → Benzoic Acid (liquid-phase oxidation with air, Co/Mn catalyst, 140–170 °C, 5–10 atm)

$$ \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{-CH}_3 + \tfrac{3}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{-COOH} + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$

**Stage B:** Benzoic Acid → Phenol (oxidative decarboxylation using air/steam over a Cu(II) catalyst — benzoate complex — at 200–250 °C)

$$ \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{-COOH} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{OH} + \text{CO}_2 $$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** When comparing processes in an exam, highlight that the **Toluene route yields only phenol (no co-product)**, eliminating the acetone market dependency — a strategic advantage when acetone prices crash.

### 2.1.3 Raschig Process (Historical / Legacy)

Two steps:
1. Benzene + HCl + O₂ → Chlorobenzene + H₂O (oxychlorination over CuCl₂/Al₂O₃ catalyst at ~240 °C)
2. Chlorobenzene + H₂O → Phenol + HCl (hydrolysis at 400–500 °C over Ca₃(PO₄)₂ or SiO₂ catalyst)

$$ \text{C}_6\text{H}_6 + \text{HCl} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{Cl} + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$
$$ \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{Cl} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{OH} + \text{HCl} $$

> [!WARNING]
> The Raschig process is **obsolete in most modern plants** due to high energy demand and corrosive HCl handling. However, KTU examiners occasionally ask about it for comparison-type questions.

### 2.1.4 Direct Oxidation of Benzene (Emerging Technology)

Benzene is oxidized to phenol in **one step** using:
- N₂O as oxidant (Boreskov–Panov process, used in Russia, with Fe-ZSM-5 zeolite catalyst, 400–500 °C)
- H₂O₂ over Ti-silicalite (TS-1) catalysts
- O₂/H₂ mixtures over Pd or Pt catalysts

$$ \text{C}_6\text{H}_6 + \text{N}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{Fe-ZSM-5}} \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{OH} + \text{N}_2 $$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Process | Key Reaction (Net) | Catalyst | Temp (°C) | Major By-product | Economic Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cumene (Hock)** | Benzene + Propylene + O₂ → Phenol + Acetone | H₃PO₄ (alkylation), H₂SO₄ (cleavage) | 80–350 | **Acetone** (valuable) | Dominant; ~90% world capacity |
| **Toluene (SNIA)** | Toluene + O₂ → Phenol + CO₂ | Co/Mn then Cu-benzoate | 140–250 | CO₂ | No co-product; favoured in low-acetone markets |
| **Raschig** | Benzene + HCl + O₂ → Phenol (via chlorobenzene) | CuCl₂; then Ca₃(PO₄)₂ | 240–500 | HCl | Obsolete; high energy |
| **Direct (Boreskov)** | Benzene + N₂O → Phenol + N₂ | Fe-ZSM-5 zeolite | 400–500 | N₂ | Emerging; “green” if N₂O sourced from adipic acid plant |

## 2.3 Engineering Utility of Phenol

Phenol is a **strategic petrochemical feedstock** used in manufacturing:
- **Bisphenol A (BPA)** → polycarbonates and epoxy resins
- **Phenol-formaldehyde (PF) resins** → Bakelite, circuit boards, adhesives
- **Caprolactam** → Nylon-6 fibres and engineering plastics
- **Adipic acid** → Nylon-6,6 (via cyclohexanol/cyclohexanone routes)
- **Alkylphenols** (nonylphenol, octylphenol) → detergents, antioxidants
- **Salicylic acid** → Aspirin (acetylsalicylic acid) and pharmaceutical intermediates
- **Chlorophenols, nitrophenols, aminophenols** → dyes, drugs, herbicides (e.g., 2,4-D)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Stoichiometric Mass Balance for the Cumene Process

### 3.1.1 Molar and Mass Balance (per 1000 kg of phenol produced)

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Determine the theoretical feed requirements for producing 1000 kg of pure phenol by the Cumene Process, assuming 85% overall yield.

**Step 1: Define molar masses (MM)**

$$ \begin{aligned}
\text{MM}_{\text{benzene}} &= 78.11 \text{ g/mol} \\
\text{MM}_{\text{propylene}} &= 42.08 \text{ g/mol} \\
\text{MM}_{\text{cumene}} &= 120.19 \text{ g/mol} \\
\text{MM}_{\text{phenol}} &= 94.11 \text{ g/mol} \\
\text{MM}_{\text{acetone}} &= 58.08 \text{ g/mol}
\end{aligned} $$

**Step 2: Moles of phenol required**

$$ \begin{aligned}
n_{\text{phenol}} &= \frac{1000 \text{ kg}}{94.11 \text{ kg/kmol}} \\
&= 10.625 \text{ kmol}
\end{aligned} $$

**Step 3: Apply 85% overall yield (reverse-correct)**

$$ \begin{aligned}
n_{\text{phenol, theoretical}} &= \frac{n_{\text{phenol}}}{\eta_{\text{overall}}} \\
&= \frac{10.625}{0.85} \\
&= 12.500 \text{ kmol}
\end{aligned} $$

**Step 4: Stoichiometric equivalence (1:1)**

Per the overall Cumene reaction, **1 kmol cumene yields 1 kmol phenol** at 100% conversion. Hence the required theoretical cumene is:

$$ n_{\text{cumene, theoretical}} = 12.500 \text{ kmol} $$

**Step 5: Mass of cumene required**

$$ \begin{aligned}
m_{\text{cumene}} &= 12.500 \text{ kmol} \times 120.19 \text{ kg/kmol} \\
&= 1502.4 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned} $$

**Step 6: Benzene requirement (1:1 mol)**

$$ \begin{aligned}
m_{\text{benzene}} &= 12.500 \text{ kmol} \times 78.11 \text{ kg/kmol} \\
&= 976.4 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned} $$

**Step 7: Propylene requirement (1:1 mol)**

$$ \begin{aligned}
m_{\text{propylene}} &= 12.500 \text{ kmol} \times 42.08 \text{ kg/kmol} \\
&= 526.0 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned} $$

**Step 8: Oxygen requirement for CHP formation (1:1 mol)**

$$ \begin{aligned}
V_{\text{O}_2,\text{ STP}} &= 12.500 \text{ kmol} \times 22.414 \text{ m}^3/\text{kmol} \\
&= 280.2 \text{ m}^3 \text{ at STP}
\end{aligned} $$

**Step 9: Acetone co-produced (1:1 mol)**

$$ \begin{aligned}
m_{\text{acetone, theoretical}} &= 12.500 \text{ kmol} \times 58.08 \text{ kg/kmol} \\
&= 726.0 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned} $$

With ~95% acetone selectivity in industrial practice, actual acetone ≈ **690 kg per 1000 kg phenol**.

### 3.1.2 Summary Table of Mass Balance

| Stream | Required (kg / 1000 kg phenol) | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Benzene | 976.4 | Stoichiometric; assume 99% alkylation selectivity → ~986 kg |
| Propylene | 526.0 | Stoichiometric |
| Cumene (intermediate) | 1502.4 | Theoretical |
| Acetone (co-product) | ~690–726 | Saleable; revenue offset |
| Air / O₂ | ~280 m³ STP (pure O₂) | Industrial: large excess air used |

## 3.2 Selectivity vs. Conversion Trade-off in CHP Formation

In the oxidation reactor, the operator must balance **conversion** of cumene against **selectivity** to CHP. Over-oxidation produces acetophenone and dimethylbenzyl alcohol (DMBA) by-products.

**Empirical rate equations (simplified):**

$$ \begin{aligned}
\text{Rate of CHP formation: } r_1 &= k_1 [\text{cumene}] [\text{ROO}^\bullet] \\
\text{Rate of CHP decomposition: } r_2 &= k_2 [\text{CHP}] \\
\text{Selectivity } S &= \frac{r_1}{r_1 + r_2}
\end{aligned} $$

Industrial practice: operate at **25–30% conversion per pass** with ~92% selectivity to CHP. Unreacted cumene is recycled after vacuum distillation.

## 3.3 Comparison Matrix of Phenol Manufacturing Routes

| Criterion | Cumene Process | Toluene (SNIA) | Raschig | Direct Oxidation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Number of stages | 3 | 2 | 2 | 1 |
| Co-product | Acetone | None | HCl (recycled) | None |
| Catalyst cost | Low (H₃PO₄ / H₂SO₄) | Moderate (Cu) | High (corrosion) | High (zeolite) |
| Capital cost | Moderate | Moderate–High | High | Very high |
| Energy demand | Moderate | Moderate | High | Moderate |
| By-product waste | Low | CO₂ only | HCl corrosion | Minimal |
| Global share | **~90%** | ~8% | <1% | <2% (emerging) |
| Sustainability | Good (acetone useable) | Excellent | Poor | Excellent |

## 4.4 Engineering Reactor Block — Cleavage Reactor Safety Architecture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Hock rearrangement is **strongly exothermic** (ΔH ≈ **–250 kJ/mol**). Industrial cleavage reactors use:
> - CSTR cascades (2–3 stages) with intense cooling
> - Acetone solvent as heat sink (CHP dissolved in acetone at 25–40 wt%)
> - Online FTIR/RI monitoring of CHP concentration
> - Emergency quench with dilute alkali (NaOH)

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1:** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**State the various industrial manufacturing routes for phenol. Identify the dominant process.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The major industrial routes for phenol manufacture are:
1. **Cumene (Hock) Process** — most dominant (~90% of global capacity)
2. **Toluene Oxidation (SNIA Viscosa) Process**
3. **Raschig Process** — via chlorobenzene hydrolysis
4. **Direct Oxidation of Benzene** — with N₂O or H₂O₂ over zeolites

The **Cumene Process** dominates because it produces **two saleable products (phenol + acetone)** in a single integrated plant, has moderate capital cost, uses inexpensive catalysts (H₃PO₄ / H₂SO₄), and has well-established safety protocols.

*(Mark split: 2 marks for listing processes, 1 mark for identifying Cumene as dominant and justification.)*

---

### **Question 2:** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Explain why phenol is more acidic than aliphatic alcohols like ethanol.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Phenol (pKa ≈ 9.95) is significantly more acidic than ethanol (pKa ≈ 16) because:
1. **Resonance stabilization of the conjugate base:** When phenol loses H⁺, the resulting phenoxide ion (C₆H₅O⁻) is stabilized by delocalization of the negative charge into the aromatic ring (resonance structures distribute charge to the ortho and para positions).
2. **sp² hybridization of the C–O bond:** The carbon bearing the –OH in phenol is sp²-hybridized and more electronegative than sp³ carbon in ethanol, helping to dissipate negative charge.
3. **No such stabilization in ethoxide:** Ethoxide (C₂H₅O⁻) has no resonance stabilization; the negative charge remains localized on oxygen.

This makes phenol about **10⁶ times more acidic** than aliphatic alcohols.

*(Mark split: 1 mark for stating pKa values, 2 marks for resonance explanation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**"The Cumene Process is the backbone of global phenol production." Critically evaluate this statement with reference to:**
**(a)** The reaction scheme, operating conditions, and catalysts used.
**(b)** The mass balance and economic significance of the acetone co-product.

---

#### **Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Reaction Scheme (3-step integrated process):**

**Step 1 — Alkylation of benzene with propylene to form cumene:**

$$ \text{C}_6\text{H}_6 + \text{CH}_3\text{-CH{=}CH}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 / \text{SiO}_2} \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{-CH(CH}_3\text{)}_2 $$

- **Catalyst:** Phosphoric acid supported on silica gel (modern) or anhydrous AlCl₃ (older)
- **Temperature:** 250–350 °C (gas-phase, fixed-bed) or 35–90 °C (liquid-phase)
- **Pressure:** 10–30 atm
- **Selectivity:** ~95% to cumene; main side-products are di-isopropylbenzene and n-propylbenzene
- **Excess benzene** is used to suppress polyalkylation

**Step 2 — Oxidation of cumene to cumene hydroperoxide (CHP):**

$$ \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{-CH(CH}_3\text{)}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{-C(CH}_3\text{)}_2\text{-OOH} $$

- **Mechanism:** Free-radical chain (initiation → propagation → termination)
- **Catalyst:** None homogeneous; initiator may be a small amount of pre-formed CHP or azo-bis(isobutyronitrile) (AIBN)
- **Temperature:** 80–130 °C
- **Pressure:** 1–10 atm air
- **Conversion per pass:** 25–30% (deliberately low to maintain ~92% selectivity)
- **Product concentration:** ~25–40 wt% CHP; concentrated by vacuum evaporation to 65–80 wt% before cleavage

**Step 3 — Acid-catalyzed cleavage of CHP (Hock rearrangement):**

$$ \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{-C(CH}_3\text{)}_2\text{-OOH} \xrightarrow{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4} \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{OH} + \text{(CH}_3\text{)}_2\text{CO} $$

- **Catalyst:** 10% H₂SO₄ (or sometimes H₃PO₄, BF₃, or acidic ion-exchange resins)
- **Temperature:** 50–90 °C
- **Mechanism:** (i) protonation of OOH; (ii) loss of H₂O to form carboxonium ion; (iii) **1,2-phenyl migration** to electron-deficient oxygen; (iv) generation of tertiary carbocation; (v) water attack; (vi) fragmentation to phenol + acetone
- **Exotherm:** Highly exothermic; ΔH ≈ –250 kJ/mol; reactor requires intense cooling (acetone solvent acts as heat sink)

**Overall:** Benzene + Propylene + O₂ → Phenol + Acetone (theoretical molar ratio 1:1:1).

---

#### **Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Mass Balance (per 1000 kg phenol at 85% overall yield):**

- Benzene required: **976.4 kg** (theoretical)
- Propylene required: **526.0 kg** (theoretical)
- Cumene intermediate: **1502.4 kg** (theoretical)
- Acetone co-produced: **~690–726 kg** (~95% acetone selectivity)

**Step-by-step calculation shown in Section 3.1.**

**Economic Significance of Acetone Co-product:**

1. **Revenue offset:** Acetone sales typically offset 25–40% of the total plant revenue. Phenol plants are essentially **dual-product facilities**.
2. **Market interdependency:** When acetone demand is high (e.g., for MMA, BPA, solvents), Cumene plants thrive. When acetone prices slump, plants suffer (this drives alternative routes like SNIA Viscosa).
3. **Acetone applications:** Solvent for paints, nail polish removers, pharmaceuticals; feedstock for methyl methacrylate (MMA), bisphenol A (via an alternative route), and methyl isobutyl ketone (MIBK).
4. **Process integration:** Acetone is recycled internally as solvent in the cleavage reactor, reducing net external demand.
5. **Comparative economics:**

| Factor | Cumene Process | SNIA Toluene Process |
|---|---|---|
| Co-product | Acetone (1 mol/mol phenol) | None |
| Revenue diversification | High | Low |
| Market risk | Exposed to acetone cycle | Independent of acetone |
| Typical plant size | 200,000–500,000 t/y phenol | 100,000–200,000 t/y phenol |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to mention that **acetone is internally recycled as solvent** in the cleavage reactor. Failing to note this in the mass balance loses **1–2 marks**. Also, do not confuse **per-pass conversion (~25%)** with **overall selectivity (~85%)** — these are different concepts.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Compare the major industrial processes for the manufacture of phenol with respect to:**
**(a)** Process chemistry and operating conditions of the Cumene and Toluene Oxidation routes.
**(b)** Engineering advantages, limitations, and sustainability aspects.

---

#### **Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Cumene (Hock) Process — Chemistry:**

Already detailed in Question A. Key points:
- Three-step route: alkylation → oxidation → cleavage
- Net reaction: C₆H₆ + C₃H₆ + O₂ → C₆H₅OH + (CH₃)₂CO
- Operates at moderate temperature (250–350 °C for alkylation, 80–130 °C for oxidation, 50–90 °C for cleavage)
- Catalysts: H₃PO₄/SiO₂, then H₂SO₄

**Toluene Oxidation (SNIA Viscosa) — Chemistry:**

**Step 1:** Liquid-phase oxidation of toluene with air to benzoic acid

$$ \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{-CH}_3 + \tfrac{3}{2}\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Co/Mn acetate, 140–170 °C, 5–10 atm}} \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{-COOH} + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$

**Step 2:** Vapor-phase oxidative decarboxylation of benzoic acid to phenol

$$ \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{-COOH} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Cu-benzoate, Mg benzoate promoter, 200–250 °C}} \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{OH} + \text{CO}_2 $$

- The copper catalyst works through a **redox cycle**: Cu²⁺ + benzoate → Cu⁺ + phenyl radical + CO₂ → Cu⁺ + ½ O₂ → Cu²⁺
- The magnesium benzoate acts as a **promoter** to maintain catalyst activity and selectivity
- No co-product; phenol selectivity ~85–90% based on toluene

**Comparative Operating Conditions Table:**

| Parameter | Cumene Process | Toluene (SNIA) Process |
|---|---|---|
| Number of reactors | 3 (alkylation, oxidation, cleavage) | 2 (oxidation, decarboxylation) |
| Highest temperature | 350 °C (alkylation) | 250 °C (decarboxylation) |
| Pressure range | 1–30 atm | 1–10 atm |
| Catalyst cost | Low (H₃PO₄, H₂SO₄) | Moderate (Co, Mn, Cu salts) |
| Co-product | Acetone | None |
| By-product | Acetone + small α-methylstyrene | CO₂ + water |

---

#### **Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Cumene Process — Advantages:**
1. Mature, well-understood technology with high selectivity (>85%)
2. Co-production of acetone provides significant revenue diversification
3. Uses inexpensive, non-noble-metal catalysts
4. Established safety protocols despite the exothermic cleavage step
5. Large economies of scale (world-scale plants > 400,000 t/y)

**Cumene Process — Limitations:**
1. Market dependency on acetone; acetone-glut conditions can erode margins
2. Three reaction steps require careful integration and material recycling
3. Highly exothermic cleavage requires sophisticated reactor design (CSTR cascades)
4. Production of α-methylstyrene as side-product reduces yield

**Toluene (SNIA) Process — Advantages:**
1. **No co-product dependency** — pure phenol focus reduces market risk
2. Toluene is generally cheaper than benzene (price differential ~10–20%)
3. Only two reaction steps — simpler plant layout
4. Lower maximum temperature (250 °C vs. 350 °C)
5. By-products are environmentally benign (CO₂ + H₂O)

**Toluene (SNIA) Process — Limitations:**
1. Catalyst deactivation due to tar formation; periodic regeneration required
2. Lower selectivity (~85%) compared to Cumene (~90%)
3. Higher utility consumption due to vapor-phase decarboxylation
4. Limited global adoption (<10% capacity) — fewer licensors, less operational data

**Sustainability Comparison:**

| Aspect | Cumene | SNIA |
|---|---|---|
| Atom economy | Moderate (phenol + acetone) | Higher (only phenol + CO₂ + H₂O) |
| E-factor (kg waste/kg product) | ~0.3 | ~0.5 |
| CO₂ footprint | Lower (valuable co-product) | Higher (CO₂ emitted) |
| Catalyst sustainability | Excellent (cheap, recyclable) | Moderate (metal recovery needed) |
| Feedstock renewability | Both use fossil-derived feed | Toluene from fossil or bio-route |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Question B):** Students frequently confuse **SNIA Viscosa** (toluene-based) with **Raschig** (chlorobenzene-based) routes. Always state the **two-step nature** of SNIA explicitly. Also, do not claim SNIA uses "no catalyst" — it uses Cu-benzoate with Mg-benzoate promoter. Skipping the catalyst costs you 1 mark.

---

- [ ] **Net Cumene Reaction:** Benzene + Propylene + O₂ → Phenol + Acetone (1:1:1:1:1 stoichiometry)
- [ ] **Catalyst for alkylation:** H₃PO₄ on SiO₂ (modern) or AlCl₃ (older)
- [ ] **Catalyst for cleavage:** Dilute (10%) H₂SO₄
- [ ] **Mechanism of cleavage:** 1,2-phenyl migration (Hock rearrangement)
- [ ] **Cleavage is highly exothermic:** ΔH ≈ –250 kJ/mol — requires CSTR cascade with acetone solvent cooling
- [ ] **Toluene (SNIA) route:** 2 steps — toluene → benzoic acid → phenol; uses Cu-benzoate catalyst; no co-product

- [ ] **Direct oxidation route:** benzene + N₂O → phenol + N₂; Fe-ZSM-5 zeolite catalyst; emerging

- [ ] **Safety note:** Acetone solvent in cleavage reactor serves as both solvent and heat sink
